### [1] [Layout-Aware Representation Learning for Open-Set ID Fraud Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05215)
*Jinxing Li,Nicholas Ren,Cathy Chang,Hongkai Pan,Daniel George*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Layout-aware, open-set fraud discovery for identity docs using DINOv3-based embeddings; achieves high layout classification accuracy and reveals campaign-scale adaptive fraud beyond incumbent detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraudsters adapt templates and pipelines, making historical labels stale; need non-stationary detection and open-set discovery using document layouts to identify campaigns under distribution shift.

Method: Adapt DINOv3 to document domain via context-aware SimMIM fine-tuning; supervised metric learning with composite loss promoting inter-class separability and intra-class compactness; train on US IDs; inference with lightweight MLP and softmax; embedding-based analysis on 20,448 Canadian IDs.

Result: 99.83% layout classification accuracy on Canadian layouts; 276 adaptive physical-fraud cases surfaced, including 222 not surfaced by incumbent detectors; supports similarity-based expansion from a seed to related cases beyond metadata graphs.

Conclusion: Layout-aware embeddings provide a production-aligned basis for discovering novel, campaign-scale fraud under distribution shift.

Abstract: Identity-document fraud detection is not a stationary binary classification problem. Adaptive attackers modify templates and fabrication pipelines, making historical fraud labels stale, and successful forgeries recur at scale as coherent campaigns. We therefore study layout-aware representation learning for open-set fraud discovery rather than only closed-set classification. We adapt DINOv3 to the document domain via context-aware SimMIM fine-tuning and supervised metric learning with composite loss that encourages inter-class separability and intra-class compactness. The model is trained with U.S. IDs only. With a lightweight MLP and softmax classifier, the embedding achieves 99.83% layout classification accuracy on Canadian layouts. Moreover, on a dataset of 20,448 Canadian IDs, embedding-space analysis surfaces 276 adaptive physical-fraud cases, including 222 not surfaced by incumbent detectors. The embedding supports similarity-based expansion from a single confirmed seed to additional related cases not linked by conventional metadata graphs. The layout-aware document embeddings provide a production-aligned basis for discovering novel and campaign-scale fraud under distribution shift.

</details>


### [2] [Seeing What Shouldn't Be There: Counterfactual GANs for Medical Image Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05283)
*Shakeeb Murtaza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a GAN-based counterfactual explanation framework for image classification that yields class-oriented attributions and plausible counterfactual images, with a cycle-consistent loss; evaluates on synthetic, tuberculosis, and BraTS datasets; highlights limitations of existing CX methods and offers an CI generation and evaluation technique, with BraTS baseline results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discriminative, minimal-feature explanations may miss relevant objects; need plausible, self-explanatory counterfactuals to support radiologists and improve interpretability.

Method: GAN with cycle-consistent loss to produce counterfactual explanations; class-oriented feature attribution; additional CI generation method; novel evaluation metric for CI quality.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic, TB, BraTS datasets confirms efficacy of the CX method; baseline BraTS results reported; demonstrates limitations of prior CX techniques and improved plausibility through CI generation and evaluation.

Conclusion: CXs with believable counterfactuals enhance interpretability for medical imaging; the proposed CI generation and evaluation address gaps in existing CX methods; BraTS baseline establishes comparative performance.

Abstract: Ascription of an image gives insights into the objects that influence the classification of the whole image or its pixels towards a specific category. These insights help radiologists to visualize deformities in medical imaging. Most of the existing visualization techniques are based on discriminative models and highlight regions of the input image participating in the decision-making of a classifier. However, these approaches do not take all noticeable objects into account as their objective is to classify the input by using a minimal set of discriminative features. To overcome the issue, a counterfactual explanation (CX) based class-oriented feature attribution method is proposed. A counterfactual explanation (CX) explicates a causal reasoning process of the form: "if X had not happened, then Y would not have happened". The method is built on generative adversarial networks (GANs) with a cyclical-consistent loss function. We evaluate our method on three datasets: synthetic, tuberculosis and BraTS. All experiments confirm the efficacy of the proposed method. This study also highlighted the limitations of existing counterfactual explanation techniques in producing plausible counterfactual instances (CIs). Accompanying CXs with believable CIs thus provides self-explanatory analogy-based explanations. To this end, a CI generation method is proposed. Also, a novel technique is used to evaluate the quality of CI. The baseline results are produced on the BraTS dataset.

</details>


### [3] [Query2Uncertainty: Robust Uncertainty Quantification and Calibration for 3D Object Detection under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05328)
*Till Beemelmanns,Alexey Nekrasov,Stefan Vilceanu,Jonas Steinhaus,Timo Woopen,Bastian Leibe,Lutz Eckstein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Density-aware calibration for 3D detectors: fits a density estimator on DETR-style query features to recalibrate classification and bbox uncertainties, improving calibration under distribution shift and in-distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable uncertainty estimation for 3D object detection degrades under distribution shifts; post-hoc calibrators lack adaptation to shifted data; a density-aware approach using latent query features aims to bridge this gap.

Method: Couple post-hoc calibration with a density estimator trained on latent object query features from DETR-style 3D detectors; use query-density to adjust confidences, jointly recalibrating classification and bounding box regression uncertainties.

Result: Empirical results on both multi-view camera and LiDAR detectors show consistent improvement over standard post-hoc calibration in both in-distribution and distribution-shifted scenarios; code released.

Conclusion: Density-aware calibration via query-feature density estimation improves calibration and robust uncertainty estimation for 3D object detection under distribution shifts; a promising direction for safe perception.

Abstract: Reliable uncertainty estimation for 3D object detection is critical for deploying safe autonomous systems, yet modern detectors remain poorly calibrated, especially under distribution shifts. Although post-hoc calibration methods address this issue and provide improved calibration for in-distribution tests, they fail to adapt in distribution-shifted scenarios. In this work, we address this issue and introduce a density-aware calibration method that couples post-hoc calibrators with the feature density of latent object queries from DETR-style 3D object detectors. These queries form a compact, location and class-aware feature, ideal for density estimation, allowing our approach to adjust model confidences in distribution-shift scenarios. By fitting a density estimator on these query features, our approach jointly recalibrates both classification and bounding box regression uncertainties. On both a multi-view camera and LiDAR-based detector, our approach consistently outperforms standard post-hoc methods in both in-distribution and distribution-shifted scenarios. Code available https://tillbeemelmanns.github.io/query2uncertainty/ .

</details>


### [4] [ViTok-v2: Scaling Native Resolution Auto-Encoders to 5 Billion Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05331)
*Philippe Hansen-Estruch,Jiahui Chen,Vivek Ramanujan,Orr Zohar,Yan Ping,Animesh Sinha,Markos Georgopoulos,Edgar Schoenfeld,Ji Hou,Felix Juefei-Xu,Sriram Vishwanath,Ali Thabet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViTok-v2 extends ViT-based image autoencoders with native-resolution generalization via NaFlex and a DINOv3 perceptual loss; scales to 5B parameters; achieves state-of-the-art reconstructions at high resolutions and improves the Pareto trade-off when paired with flow-matching generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: address resolution sensitivity of ViT tokenizers and instability from adversarial losses; improve reconstruction quality to enable more Pareto-efficient tokenizers.

Method: NaFlex-based native-resolution support; DINOv3 perceptual loss replacing LPIPS and GAN objectives; large-scale training (~2B images, scaling to 5B params); joint experiments with flow-matching generators.

Result: matches/exceeds state-of-the-art reconstruction at 256p; outperforms baselines at 512p+; demonstrates Pareto frontier improvement when co-scaling tokenizer and generator.

Conclusion: native-resolution ViT autoencoders with stable perceptual loss can scale to very large models, achieving superior reconstructions and Pareto efficiency; ViTok-v2 is the largest image autoencoder to date.

Abstract: Vision Transformer (ViT) autoencoders have emerged as compelling tokenizers for images, offering improved reconstruction over convolutional tokenizers. However, existing ViT tokenizers cannot explore this landscape as performance degrades outside training resolutions, and reliance on adversarial losses prevents stable scaling. ViTok (Hansen-Estruch et al., 2025) found that the compression ratio r mediates a reconstruction-generation trade-off where lower r means better reconstructions but harder generations, so improving tokenizer reconstruction is key to more Pareto-optimal tokenizers. We introduce ViTok-v2, which addresses these limitations with native resolution support via NaFlex for generalization across resolutions and aspect ratios, and a novel DINOv3 perceptual loss that replaces both LPIPS and GAN objectives for stable training at any scale. ViTok-v2 is trained on about 2B images and scaled to 5B parameters, the largest image autoencoder to date. ViTok-v2 matches or exceeds state-of-the-art reconstruction at 256p and outperforms all baselines at 512p and above. In joint scaling experiments with flow matching generators, we show that scaling both the autoencoder and the generator advances the Pareto frontier of this trade-off.

</details>


### [5] [Open-SAT: LLM-Guided Query Embedding Refinement for Open-Vocabulary Object Retrieval in Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05344)
*Md Adnan Arefeen,Biplob Debnath,Ravi K. Rajendran,Murugan Sankaradas,Srimat T. Chakradhar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Open-SAT is a training-free, inference-time refinement for open-vocabulary satellite image retrieval. It uses VLMs to encode image tiles, stores them in a vector DB, and employs LLMs at query time to refine text embeddings with contextual object and surroundings information, coupled with a threshold-free retrieval mechanism. It achieves up to 16.04% F1 gain on three public benchmarks with a comparable number of retrieved tiles, showing effectiveness without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Satellite image retrieval under open-ended natural-language queries faces generalization challenges; fine-tuned vision-language models often fail to align with unseen objects or concepts. A training-free, inference-time refinement approach can improve retrieval accuracy without requiring labeled data or model fine-tuning.

Method: (1) Encode image tiles with a vision-language model and store embeddings in a vector database. (2) At query time, use a large language model to refine the text embedding by injecting contextual information about target objects and their surroundings. (3) Apply a threshold-free retrieval mechanism to select relevant tiles efficiently and accurately. (4) No additional training or supervision is required.

Result: Experimental evaluation on three public benchmarks shows that Open-SAT improves the F1 score by up to 16.04% while retrieving a comparable number of image tiles, demonstrating improved open-vocabulary satellite image retrieval without training.

Conclusion: Open-SAT demonstrates that LLM-guided, training-free query refinement at inference time can significantly improve open-vocabulary satellite image retrieval, leveraging existing VLMs and vector databases. The approach offers practical benefits in deployment but may hinge on LLM quality, inference cost, and domain coverage; future work could explore robustness, efficiency, and broader benchmark evaluation.

Abstract: In satellite applications, user queries often take the form of open-ended natural language, extending beyond a fixed set of predefined categories. This open-vocabulary nature poses significant challenges for retrieving relevant image tiles, as the retrieval system must generalize to a wide range of unseen objects and concepts. While vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP are widely used for text-image retrieval, even fine-tuned variants often struggle to accurately align such queries with satellite imagery. To address this, we propose Open-SAT, a training-free query embedding refinement algorithm that operates at inference time to improve alignment between user queries and satellite image content. Open-SAT uses VLMs to compute embeddings for image tiles, which are stored in a vector database for efficient retrieval. At query time, it leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to refine the text embeddings by incorporating contextual information about objects of interest and their surroundings. A threshold-free retrieval mechanism further enhances accuracy and efficiency. Experimental results in three public benchmarks demonstrate that Open-SAT improves the F1 score by up to 16.04%, while retrieving a comparable number of image tiles. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of Open-SAT in open-vocabulary satellite image retrieval, leveraging LLM guidance without the need for additional training or supervision.

</details>


### [6] [egenioussBench: A New Dataset for Geospatial Visual Localisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05351)
*Phillipp Fanta-Jende,Francesco Vultaggio,Alexander Kern,Yasmin Loeper,Markus Gerke*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces egenioussBench, a scalable city-scale visual localisation benchmark using geospatial data (airborne mesh + CityGML LoD2), cm-accurate ground truth, non-co-visible test subset, and a public leaderboard for cross-domain evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable realistic, scalable large-scale visual localisation research beyond SfM by providing deployable-mapping assets and challenging, cross-view/cross-domain evaluation scenarios.

Method: Create benchmark from city-scale airborne 3D mesh and CityGML LoD2; acquire smartphone imagery with cm-accurate ground truth via PPK and GCP/CP adjustment; estimate full co-visibility from rendered depth, select maximum independent set to obtain non-co-visible subset; provide 42 non-co-visible test images with withheld ground truth and 412 sequential validation images with poses; public leaderboard with binning pose-error thresholds; release evaluation code and data.

Result: Benchmark enables fair, like-for-like comparisons between mesh- and LoD2-based methods; demonstrates cross-view and cross-domain challenges; publicly available data/code to support scalable evaluation.

Conclusion: egenioussBench offers a rigorous, scalable platform for advancing large-scale visual localisation research, bridging deployable mapping assets with pose estimation challenges and standardized evaluation.

Abstract: We present egenioussBench, a visual localisation benchmark built on geospatial reference data: a city-scale airborne 3D mesh and a CityGML LoD2 model. This pairing reflects deployable mapping assets and supports true scalability beyond traditional SfM-based approaches. The query data comprise smartphone images with centimetre-accurate, map-independent ground truth obtained via PPK and GCP/CP-aided adjustment. From 2,709 images, we derive a non-co-visible subset by estimating the full co-visibility matrix from rendered depth and selecting a maximum independent set; the released data include a test split of 42 non-co-visible images with withheld ground truth and a validation split of 412 sequential images with poses, e.g. for training of pose regressors and self-validation. The benchmark features a public leaderboard evaluated with binning metrics at multiple pose-error thresholds alongside global statistics (median, RMSE, outlier ratio), ensuring fair, like-for-like comparison across mesh- and LoD2-based methods. Together, these design choices expose realistic cross-view and cross-domain challenges while providing a rigorous, scalable path for advancing large-scale visual localisation. We make the evaluation code and data availeable at https://github.com/fratopa/egenioussBench and https://www.egeniouss.eu/

</details>


### [7] [Two Steps Are All You Need: Efficient 3D Point Cloud Anomaly Detection with Consistency Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05372)
*Pranav A,Shashank B,Pranav Siddappa,Dominik Seuss,Minal Moharir,Subramanya KN*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-model-based reconstruction anomaly detection for 3D point clouds using consistency learning to predict anomaly-free geometry in one or two evaluations, with a new hybrid loss; achieves up to 80x faster inference than SOTA (no GPU) while maintaining strong detection performance (Anomaly-ShapeNet: 76.20% I-AUROC; Real3DAD: 72.80% I-AUROC).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The demand for reliable, low-latency anomaly detection in 3D point clouds on resource-constrained, latency-critical systems (e.g., drones, industrial cameras). Diffusion-based pipelines are bottlenecked by iterative denoising and can be computationally prohibitive or unreliable in unmasked regions.

Method: Reformulate reconstruction-based anomaly detection via consistency learning to directly predict anomaly-free geometry in a single or two network evaluations; introduce a novel hybrid loss that explicitly enforces reconstruction toward clean data.

Result: Substantial inference-cost reduction, up to 80x faster than the current state-of-the-art without GPU acceleration, while preserving performance. Outperforms R3D-AD on Anomaly-ShapeNet (I-AUROC 76.20%) and remains competitive on Real3DAD (I-AUROC 72.80%).

Conclusion: Enables efficient, low-latency 3D anomaly detection on resource-constrained edge devices (e.g., drones, smart cameras) by removing diffusion bottlenecks and leveraging consistency learning and a hybrid loss.

Abstract: Diffusion models are rapidly redefining 3D anomaly detection in point cloud data. As 3D sensing becomes integral to modern manufacturing, reliable anomaly detection is essential for high-throughput quality assurance and process control. Yet practical deployment on resource-constrained, latency-critical systems remains limited. Existing methods are often computationally prohibitive or unreliable in complex, unmasked regions, and diffusion pipelines are inherently bottlenecked by iterative denoising. In this work, we address this bottleneck by reformulating reconstructionbased anomaly detection through consistency learning, enabling direct prediction of anomaly-free geometry in one or two network evaluations. We further introduce a novel hybrid loss formulation that explicitly enforces reconstruction toward clean data. This design substantially reduces inference cost, achieving up to 80x faster runtime than the current state-of-the-art method, without GPU acceleration, while preserving strong detection performance. It outperforms R3D-AD on Anomaly-ShapeNet with 76.20% I-AUROC and remains competitive on Real3DAD with 72.80% I-AUROC, enabling efficient, low-latency anomaly detection on resource-constrained platforms, including drones, smart industrial cameras, and other edge devices.

</details>


### [8] [LAMP: Localization Aware Multi-camera People Tracking in Metric 3D World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05390)
*Nan Yang,Julian Straub,Fan Zhang,Richard Newcombe,Jakob Engel,Lingni Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lift-then-fit: convert 2D keypoints from multiple moving cameras into a common 3D world frame using known device motion, then apply a spatio-temporal transformer to estimate 3D motion from the 3D ray cloud.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robust 3D human motion tracking from egocentric, moving, multi-camera setups where traditional monocular methods fail due to egomotion, occlusions, and limited training data.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) use device 6 DoF motion and calibration to back-project 2D keypoints into a unified 3D world frame over time; (2) train an end-to-end spatio-temporal transformer to map the resulting 3D ray cloud to 3D human motion (lift-then-fit).

Result: Claims state-of-the-art performance on monocular benchmarks and significantly better performance than baselines in the proposed egocentric multi-camera setting.

Conclusion: Disentangling observer motion from target motion enables leveraging a world-space human motion prior and flexible multi-camera fusion, yielding robust 3D motion tracking in dynamic egocentric scenarios.

Abstract: Tracking 3D human motion from egocentric multi-camera headset is challenged by severe egomotion, partial visibility or occlusions and lack of training data. Existing methods designed for monocular video often require static or slowly-moving cameras and cannot efficiently leverage multi-view, calibrated and localized input. This makes them brittle and prone to fail on dynamic egocentric captures. We propose LAMP (Localization Aware Multi-camera People Tracking): a novel, simple framework to solve this via early disentanglement of observer and target motion. LAMP introduces a two-step process. First, we leverage the known device 6 DoF motion and calibration to convert detected 2D body keypoints from all cameras over a temporal window into a unified 3D world reference frame. Second, an end-to-end-trained spatio-temporal transformer fits 3D human motion directly to this 3D ray cloud. This "lift-then-fit" approach allows LAMP to learn and leverage a natural human motion prior in the world-space, as well as providing an elegant framework to flexibly incorporate information from multiple temporally asynchronous, partially observing and moving cameras. LAMP achieves state-of-the-art results on monocular benchmarks, while significantly outperforming baselines for our targeted egocentric setting.

</details>


### [9] [Zero-Shot Satellite Image Retrieval through Joint Embeddings: Application to Crisis Response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05405)
*James Walsh,William Fawcett,Grace Colvard,Raúl Ramos-Pollán*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoQuery uses prompt-aligned text proxies to bridge text and visual embeddings for zero-shot retrieval in Earth observation; achieves 31.6% accuracy within 50 km across 76 disaster queries, strongest on floods; deployed in crisis system to identify vulnerable areas during Cyclone Alfred.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for natural-language grounded retrieval in Earth observation when full joint contrastive training of a CLIP-style model is impractical due to lack of paired data and global-scale compute.

Method: Generate language descriptions for a 100k proxy subset of Sentinel-2 tiles and optimize the description-generation prompt so that distances in the text-embedding space correlate with distances in the frozen CLAY visual-embedding space. Retrieval is two-stage: (1) text-similarity search over the proxy subset, then (2) visual nearest-neighbour search over worldwide CLAY embeddings.

Result: On 76 disaster-location queries (UK floods, US wildfires, US droughts), GeoQuery achieves 31.6% accuracy within 50 km, strongest performance on floods (50% within 50 km). Deployed within ECHO crisis system, identified vulnerable areas during Brisbane 2025 Cyclone Alfred, with flood simulations reproducing historical patterns.

Conclusion: Prompt-aligned proxies provide a practical bridge between Earth-observation foundation models and operational retrieval when full contrastive training is not feasible.

Abstract: Semantic search of Earth observation archives remains challenging. Visual foundation models such as CLAY produce rich embeddings of satellite imagery but lack the natural-language grounding needed for intuitive query, and full contrastive training of a remote-sensing CLIP-style model requires paired data and compute that are unavailable at global scale. We present GeoQuery, a zero-shot retrieval system that sidesteps this constraint through prompt-aligned text proxies. Rather than training a joint encoder, we generate language descriptions for a 100k proxy subset of global Sentinel-2 tiles and optimise the description-generation prompt so that distances in the resulting text-embedding space correlate with distances in the frozen CLAY visual-embedding space. Queries are resolved in two stages, with a text-similarity search over the proxy subset followed by a visual nearest-neighbour search over worldwide CLAY embeddings. On 76 disaster-location queries covering UK floods, US wildfires, and US droughts, GeoQuery achieves 31.6% accuracy within 50 km, with the strongest performance on floods (50% within 50 km) where terrain features are well captured by RGB embeddings. Deployed within ECHO, a crisis response system using Agentic Action Graphs, GeoQuery identified vulnerable areas during Brisbane's 2025 Cyclone Alfred, with downstream flood simulations reproducing historical patterns. Prompt-aligned proxies offer a practical bridge between EO foundation models and operational retrieval when full contrastive training is out of reach.

</details>


### [10] [Safety-Critical Camera Reliability Monitoring for ADAS via Degradation-Aware Uncertainty Pattern Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05439)
*Shiva Aher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proactive camera reliability score (GSHI) estimates degradation severity and uncertainty from a single RGB image, enabling early risk warning ahead of perception failures via a multi-task network trained with physics- and geometry-aware synthetic supervision across 12 degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical ADAS perception requires timely detection of camera degradation to prevent downstream failures; traditional monitoring detects issues only after perception performance degrades, leaving little room for intervention.

Method: Global Sensor Health Index (GSHI) is a risk-aware multiplicative aggregation of per-degradation severities. A lightweight multi-task network predicts degradation type, severity, GSHI, and spatial uncertainty maps from a single RGB image without downstream task feedback. Training uses physics- and geometry-aware synthetic supervision over twelve camera degradation modes.

Result: On KITTI-derived degradations, GSHI decreases monotonically with severity, achieves a health-estimation MAE of 0.064, and provides an early-warning lead time of 0.47 ± 0.25 severity units before YOLOv8 detection failure. GSHI outperforms IQA, detector-confidence, and clean-feature OOD baselines and transfers zero-shot to real adverse-weather driving data.

Conclusion: Degradation-aware uncertainty analysis is a practical direction for proactive camera reliability monitoring in intelligent vehicles; GSHI offers timely risk estimation and robust cross-domain performance.

Abstract: Reliable camera input is essential for safety-critical ADAS perception, but most monitoring approaches detect sensor failures only after downstream performance has degraded. We propose a proactive camera reliability monitoring framework that estimates perception risk from degradation-induced uncertainty patterns before downstream failure becomes observable. The method introduces a Global Sensor Health Index (GSHI), a continuous reliability score that aggregates per-degradation severities using a risk-aware multiplicative formulation, allowing severe single-mode failures such as lens occlusion or motion blur to dominate the health estimate. A lightweight multi-task network predicts degradation type, severity, GSHI, and spatial uncertainty maps from a single RGB image without downstream task feedback. Training uses physics- and geometry-aware synthetic supervision over twelve camera degradation modes. Experiments on KITTI-derived degradations show that GSHI decreases monotonically with severity, achieves a health-estimation MAE of 0.064, and provides positive early-warning lead time of 0.47 $\pm$ 0.25 severity units before YOLOv8 detection failure. GSHI also outperforms IQA, detector-confidence, and clean-feature OOD baselines, and transfers zero-shot to real adverse-weather driving data. These results support degradation-aware uncertainty analysis as a practical direction for proactive camera reliability monitoring in intelligent vehicles.

</details>


### [11] [EchoXFlow: A Beamspace Echocardiography Dataset for Cardiac Motion, Flow, and Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05447)
*Elias Stenhede,Joanna Sulkowska,Eivind Bjørkan Orstad,Henrik Schirmer,Arian Ranjbar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EchoXFlow introduces a large-scale native-geometry echocardiography dataset with separable modality streams (1D/2D/3D), multiple Doppler modalities, and synchronized ECG, preserving timing and geometry to enable cross-modal, acquisition-aware learning. Includes dense clinical annotations and open-source tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public echocardiography datasets typically rely on scan-converted videos, often omitting Doppler data or overlaying it, and lose native acquisition geometry. A dataset that preserves modality relationships and geometry is needed to study physically grounded relationships among anatomy, motion, and blood flow.

Method: Collect 666 routine-care exams, yielding 37,125 recordings. Store each recording as separable modality streams (temporally resolved 1D, 2D, 3D data) plus multiple Doppler modalities, all synchronized with ECG. Provide clinical annotations (guideline measurements, dense 2D contours, 3D LV endocardial meshes) and open-source tooling.

Result: Establishes a testbed for cross-modal, acquisition-aware learning in echocardiography and 4D vision. The abstract emphasizes potential tasks enabled by the dataset, rather than reporting quantitative results.

Conclusion: EchoXFlow fills a gap by preserving native acquisition geometry and modality relationships, enabling acquisition-aware multi-modal learning and cross-modal tasks not feasible with conventional scan-converted videos; anticipated to advance 4D echocardiography and physically grounded learning.

Abstract: We introduce EchoXFlow, a clinical echocardiography dataset for learning from ultrasound in its native acquisition geometry rather than from scan-converted Cartesian videos. Existing public datasets offer limited opportunities to study cross-modal relationships between cardiac anatomy, myocardial motion, and blood flow, as Doppler is typically absent or fused as RGB overlays, and acquisitions are released after lossy vendor display processing. EchoXFlow comprises 37125 recordings from 666 routine-care examinations, preserving the timing, geometry, and modality relationships needed for physically grounded echo learning. Each recording is retained as separable modality-specific streams: temporally resolved 1D, 2D, and 3D data alongside multiple Doppler modalities, paired with a synchronized ECG. Clinical annotations span guideline-based measurements to dense 2D myocardial contours and 3D left-ventricular endocardial meshes. With its associated open-source tooling, EchoXFlow enables cross-modal, acquisition-aware learning tasks that cannot be formulated from conventional scan-converted videos alone, and serves as a testbed for 4D vision and physically grounded multi-modal learning more broadly.

</details>


### [12] [The First Controllable Bokeh Rendering Challenge at NTIRE 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05510)
*Tim Seizinger,Florin-Alexandru Vasluianu,Jeffrey Chen,Zhuyun Zhou,Zongwei Wu,Radu Timofte,Dafeng Zhang,Yipeng Lin,Qi Yan,Junhao Chen,Yang Yang,Divyavardhan Singh,Hariom Thacker,Hammad Mohammad,Aanchal Maurya,Kishor Upla,Kiran Raja,Wei Zhou,Hongyu Huang,Yujin Cho,Grigory Malivenko,Jiachen Tu,Yaokun Shi,Guoyi Xu,Yaoxin Jiang,Jiajia Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First NTIRE Controllable Bokeh Rendering Challenge evaluates 8 accepted submissions from 44 registered participants on unseen portrait-like images, using two tracks: (i) objective fidelity metrics and (ii) perceptual expert-based evaluation. Most entrants built on or extended the Bokehlicious baseline; the study establishes benchmarks and highlights effective approaches for future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Establish benchmarks and benchmarks-driven progress for controllable bokeh rendering by combining objective fidelity metrics with perceptual evaluation, addressing the lack of standardized tests for this niche.

Method: An open challenge with 44 registered participants and 8 valid submissions. Submissions were evaluated on unseen images focusing on portraits and complex bokeh. Track 1 used established quantitative fidelity metrics; Track 2 conducted a qualitative perceptual study with expert evaluators. The Bokehlicious baseline served as the common reference point that many teams refined or extended.

Result: Eight teams submitted valid solutions that were evaluated on unseen images. The competition highlighted effective strategies, with many participants refining or extending the Bokehlicious baseline. A qualitative expert-based perceptual track complemented the quantitative metrics.

Conclusion: This inaugural contest demonstrates the feasibility of benchmarking controllable bokeh rendering, provides both objective and perceptual evaluation data, and establishes a baseline (Bokehlicious) for future NTIRE challenges while encouraging methodological innovations beyond the baseline.

Abstract: This study presents the outcomes of the first Controllable Bokeh Rendering Challenge at NTIRE and highlights the most effective submitted methodologies. In total, 44 participants registered for the competition, of which 8 teams submitted valid solutions after the conclusion of the final test phase. All submissions were evaluated on unseen images, focusing on portraits and intricate subjects with complex and visually appealing bokeh phenomena. In addition to the first track focusing on established quantitative fidelity metrics, we conducted a qualitative user study with a panel of experts for a second track focusing on perceptual assessment. As this was the inaugural challenge on this topic, most of the participants focused on refining and extending the Bokehlicious baseline method.

</details>


### [13] [Characterizing Brazilian Atlantic Forest Restoration Outcomes with Geospatial AlphaEarth Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05547)
*Alice Heiman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Large-scale restoration monitoring via satellite embeddings with a Reference Trajectory Embedding; reveals LULC-driven clustering and potential for early restoration assessment, but signals are noisy and require further fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional ground-based monitoring and NDVI metrics are inadequate for large-scale, gradual restoration; seek scalable, sensitive metrics to quantify progress using satellite-derived embeddings.

Method: Analyzed 1,729 restoration sites in São Paulo using AlphaEarth Foundation satellite embeddings; defined a Reference Trajectory Embedding as cosine similarity to reference mature secondary forest; identified change vectors and clustering by land use/land cover (LULC); assessed for capturing metadata beyond LULC.

Result: Embeddings show distinct clusters corresponding to LULC types; some sites exhibit clear change vectors; overall signal is noisy; embeddings may require further fine-tuning to better capture site metadata beyond LULC.

Conclusion: Embedding-based approaches hold promise for large-scale restoration monitoring and early success assessment but need refinement to improve robustness and capture of non-LULC site metadata.

Abstract: The Atlantic Forest in Brazil is a critical biodiversity hotspot, yet less than 12-15% of its original cover remains. Although monitoring forest restoration on a large scale is essential, traditional methods are limited by the impracticality of on-the-ground reporting on such a scale and by the saturation of remote-sensing indices such as NDVI. Furthermore, reforestation is a gradual process as opposed to the rapid spectral changes caused by deforestation. In this study, we examine 1,729 restoration sites in São Paulo, using satellite embeddings from the AlphaEarth Foundation's model to evaluate their effectiveness in characterising early restoration success. We introduce the concept of a 'Reference Trajectory Embedding', defining a metric of restoration success based on cosine similarity to reference sites of mature secondary forest. We observe distinct clusters in embedding space according to different land use and land cover (LULC) types, and we can identify sites with clear change vectors. However, the signal can be noisy, and embeddings may require further fine-tuning to capture and predict site metadata beyond LULC.

</details>


### [14] [A Novel Graph-Regulated Disentangling Mamba Model with Sparse Tokens for Enhanced Tree Species Classification from MODIS Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05549)
*Motasem Alkayid,Zhengsen Xu,Saeid Taleghanidoozdoozan,Yimin Zhu,Megan Greenwood,Quinn Ledingham,Zack Dewis,Mabel Heffring,Naser El-Sheimy,Lincoln Linlin Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Graph-regulated Disentangled Sparse Mamba (GDS-Mamba) for MODIS time-series tree species classification, combining a mini-batch graph-based context module, a disentangled Mamba architecture for spatial-spectral-temporal features, and sparse token selection to improve efficiency. Achieves 93.94% accuracy in Alberta and 80.19% in cross-provincial evaluation, outperforming 12 state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome subtle inter-species signature differences and complex spatial-spectral-temporal coupling in MODIS data; improve large-scale context modeling and efficient feature learning for tree species classification.

Method: Introduce a mini-batch graph-regulated approach to capture topological correlations among input images; develop a disentangled Mamba architecture to separately model spatial patterns, spectral signatures, and temporal phenology in MODIS time series; implement sparse token strategies to learn an optimal subset of tokens and mitigate correlation decay in standard Mamba models; evaluate on MOD13Q1 data across Alberta and Saskatchewan.

Result: Extensive experiments on large-scale annual MOD13Q1 data show overall accuracy of 93.94% in Alberta and 80.19% in cross-provincial evaluations, outperforming twelve state-of-the-art classification models.

Conclusion: GDS-Mamba effectively integrates graph-based context, disentangled feature extraction, and sparsity to enhance tree species classification from MODIS time series, delivering strong local performance and reasonable cross-domain generalization; observed cross-provincial drop suggests domain shift and potential for further generalization.

Abstract: Although tree species classification from Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) time series data is critical for supporting various environmental applications, it is a challenging task due to several key difficulties: the subtle signature differences among tree species, strong spatial-spectral-temporal information coupling, and the difficulty of modeling large-scale topological context information. To better address these challenges, this paper presents a novel Graph-regulated Disentangled Sparse Mamba model (GDS-Mamba) for enhanced tree species classification, with the following contributions. (1) First, to improve large-scale context modeling, we design a mini-batch graph-regulated approach that explicitly explores topological correlation effects among input images. (2) Second, to disentangle the high-dimensional spatial-spectral-temporal information coupling for improved feature extraction, we propose a novel disentangling Mamba architecture tailored for capturing independent spatial patterns, spectral signatures, and temporal phenology behaviors in MODIS time series. (3) Third, to improve efficiency and subtle feature learning, we design novel sparse token approaches that adaptively learn the optimum subset of tokens to better address the correlation decay problem that bottlenecks standard Mamba models. Extensive experiments using large-scale annual MOD13Q1 data across two Canadian provinces (i.e., Alberta and Saskatchewan) achieved an overall accuracy of 93.94\% in Alberta and 80.19\% in cross-provincial evaluations, outperforming twelve state-of-the-art classification models.

</details>


### [15] [An extremely coarse feedback signal is sufficient for learning human-aligned visual representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05556)
*Yash Mehta,Michael F. Bonner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Coarse supervision with as few as 8 categories yields brain-aligned representations rivaling or exceeding fine-grained and self-supervised models, and aligns best with human perceptual similarity judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question how supervisory signal granularity shapes representational alignment with human and primate vision, challenging the assumption that finer supervision yields better brain alignment.

Method: Partition a set of training images into 2,4,8,16,...,64 categories using PCA-based splits of pretrained embeddings to create coarse labels. Train hundreds of CNN and transformer models on these coarse classification tasks. Evaluate representations against macaque electrophysiology and human fMRI, and compare to other models including fine-grained supervised, self-supervised, and large-scale vision models.

Result: 8-category trained networks achieve neural alignment that matches or exceeds 1,000-class models. They also align more closely with human perceptual similarity judgments than all other models evaluated, including fine-grained supervision or self-supervision and large models.

Conclusion: Human-like visual representations can emerge from remarkably coarse feedback, suggesting learning signals for vision can be simpler than widely assumed and enabling more human-aligned AI systems.

Abstract: Artificial neural networks trained on visual tasks develop internal representations resembling those of the primate visual system, a discovery that has guided a decade of computational neuroscience. Research on building brain-aligned models has progressively embraced finer-grained supervisory signals, from object classification to contrastive self-supervised objectives that maximize distinctions among individual images, yet the role of supervisory signal granularity on brain alignment remains largely unexamined. Here we systematically investigate how the coarseness of a learning signal shapes representational alignment with human vision. We parametrically vary the level of signal granularity using a data-driven approach that partitions a set of training images into varied numbers of categories (2, 4, 8, 16, ..., 64) via PCA-based splits of pretrained embeddings. We train hundreds of neural networks across convolutional and transformer architectures on these coarse classification tasks and compare their representations to macaque electrophysiology recordings and human fMRI responses. We find that networks trained to distinguish as few as 8 broad categories learn representations that match or exceed the neural alignment of models distinguishing 1,000-classes. Even more strikingly, these coarsely trained networks align more closely with human perceptual similarity judgments than all other models evaluated, including networks trained with fine-grained supervision or self-supervision as well as leading large-scale vision models. These results demonstrate that human-like visual representations emerge from remarkably coarse feedback, reframing what learning signals vision may require and opening a path toward building AI systems that are more aligned with human perception.

</details>


### [16] [Text-to-CAD Retrieval: a Strong Baseline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05572)
*Honghu Pan,Zibo Du,Daxiang Liu,Chengliang Liu,Xiaoling Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cross-modal text-to-CAD retrieval framework that learns joint embeddings of CAD procedural sequences, geometry point clouds, and natural language. It uses a sequence encoder, a point encoder, and a text encoder, with a novel cross-attention feature decoder during training to align modalities; inference uses concatenated sequence-point features for efficiency, establishing Text2CAD as a benchmark and baseline for retrieval-augmented CAD generation. Code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CAD repositories are poorly searchable when relying on filenames/directories, hindering semantic reuse of legacy designs. There is a need for scalable, accurate retrieval of semantically relevant CAD models from natural language queries over large databases.

Method: A unified multi-modal framework: (1) sequence encoder captures construction logic from procedural CAD sequences; (2) point encoder extracts geometric features from point clouds; (3) text encoder encodes natural language queries. A feature decoder reconstructs masked sequence features via cross-attention with text and point features during training to enforce cross-modal alignment. At inference, the decoder is removed, and retrieval uses concatenated sequence-point features. Trained on paired text-CAD annotations from the Text2CAD dataset.

Result: Positioned as a strong baseline for text-to-CAD retrieval with a practical benchmark via Text2CAD; emphasizes cross-modal alignment and efficient retrieval; no quantitative results in the abstract; code release planned.

Conclusion: The work establishes a practical cross-modal retrieval framework for text-to-CAD, enabling retrieval-augmented generation and serving as a foundation for downstream CAD generation tasks, with a public code release and the Text2CAD benchmark.

Abstract: Text-based retrieval of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models is a critical yet underexplored task for the reuse of legacy industrial designs. Existing CAD repositories are typically searched using filenames or directories, which limits the efficiency, scalability, and accuracy of design retrieval. In this paper, we formally introduce text-to-CAD retrieval as a new cross-modal retrieval task, aiming to retrieve semantically relevant CAD models from large-scale databases given natural language queries. Leveraging paired text-CAD annotations from the Text2CAD dataset, we establish a practical benchmark for this task. To achieve text-based retrieval, we propose a unified framework that learns multi-modal CAD embeddings from both procedural sequences and geometric point clouds. Specifically, a sequence encoder captures the construction logic of CAD models, while a point encoder extracts explicit geometric features. A text encoder is used to learn semantic representations of textual queries. During training, we introduce a novel feature decoder that reconstructs masked sequence features via cross-attention with text and point features, encouraging implicit multi-modal alignment. At inference time, we remove this auxiliary decoder to enable efficient retrieval using concatenated sequence-point features. Our framework serves as a strong baseline for text-to-CAD retrieval and lays the foundation for downstream CAD generation paradigms, such as retrieval-augmented generation. The source code will be released.

</details>


### [17] [Uncertainty-Guided Edge Learning for Deep Image Regression in Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05590)
*Anh Vu Nguyen,Dino Sejdinovic,Tat-Jun Chin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UGEL uses deep beta regression to estimate predictive uncertainty in a single forward pass for onboard deep image regression, enabling uncertainty-guided data prioritization that speeds up edge learning on satellites; outperforms active/semi-supervised baselines. Code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge devices have strict computation and energy limits, degrading both model training and uncertainty estimation for incoming unlabeled data. In remote-sensing regression (e.g., cloud coverage percentage), efficient on-board training requires fast, flexible uncertainty estimates to prioritize informative samples and accelerate convergence.

Method: Propose uncertainty-guided edge learning (UGEL). A deep network outputs the parameters of a Beta distribution for the regression target y (normalized to [0,1]); a Beta regression loss is used to train the model in a single forward pass. Predictive uncertainty is computed from the Beta distribution, enabling data prioritization. This approach is more computable on edge devices than Monte Carlo or Gaussian-assumption methods and is compared against active/semi-supervised baselines.

Result: UGEL achieves faster convergence of on-board regression training than active or semi-supervised learning baselines in the reported experiments.

Conclusion: Deep beta regression provides a flexible, single-pass uncertainty estimation suitable for edge learning, enabling efficient data prioritization and faster on-board convergence; code and models are publicly available for replication and extension.

Abstract: Edge learning refers to training machine learning models deployed on edge platforms, typically using new data accumulated onboard. The computational limitations on edge devices affect not only model optimisation, but also calculation of the predictive uncertainty of the current model on the unlabelled data, which is vital for informing model updating. In this paper, we investigate edge learning in the context of performing deep image regression on a remote sensing satellite, where a deep network is executed by an onboard computer to regress a scalar $y$ from an input image, e.g., $y$ is the percentage of pixels indicating cloud coverage or land use. We propose an uncertainty-guided edge learning (UGEL) algorithm that can accurately prioritise the data to speed up training convergence of the on-board regression model. Underpinning UGEL is the calculation of predictive uncertainty based on deep beta regression, where a deep network is used to estimate the parameters of a beta distribution for which the target $y$ for an input image has a high likelihood. Compared to established methods for uncertainty estimation that are either too costly on edge devices (e.g., require many forward passes per sample) or make strict assumptions on the predictive distribution (e.g., Gaussian), deep beta regression is computable in a single forward pass and allows more general predictive distributions. Results show that UGEL delivers faster-converging edge learning than active or semi-supervised learning. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/anh-vunguyen/UGEL.

</details>


### [18] [Leveraging Image Generators to Address Training Data Scarcity: The Gen4Regen Dataset for Forest Regeneration Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05627)
*Gabriel Jeanson,David-Alexandre Duclos,William Larrivée-Hardy,Noé Cochet,Matěj Boxan,Anthony Deschênes,François Pomerleau,Philippe Giguère*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Generative vision-language models are used to synthesize high-fidelity images and pixel-perfect semantic masks to address data scarcity and extreme class imbalance in fine-grained forest regeneration species segmentation. The approach combines real and synthetic data (WilDReF-Q-V2: 13,977 unlabelled + 50 labelled real images; Gen4Regen: 2,101 synthetic image–mask pairs) and shows >15 percentage point F1 gains over purely supervised baselines, with up to ~30 pp gains for underrepresented species. The work argues that prompt-generated data augments scarce expert labels and that unified training with AI-generated data is beneficial. Datasets, code, and models will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome scarcity of expert-annotated high-resolution UAV imagery for fine-grained forest regeneration species and extreme class imbalance in semantic segmentation; enable scalable, label-efficient learning in niche forestry domains.

Method: Leverage a vision-language model (Nano Banana Pro) to generate high-fidelity imagery and pixel-aligned semantic masks from prompts; construct WilDReF-Q-V2 (9-real labels: 50 labelled; 13,977 unlabelled real images) and Gen4Regen (2,101 synthetic image–mask pairs); train with a combination of real and AI-generated data in a unified framework; evaluate F1 score across species, with emphasis on underrepresented classes.

Result: Unified training with AI-generated data improves F1 by >15 percentage points over purely supervised baselines; even small amounts of prompt-generated data markedly boost performance for underrepresented species, with some gains up to ~30 pp.

Conclusion: Vision-language models can serve as flexible data generators to bootstrap perception tasks in domains with scarce expert labels; the proposed datasets and pipelines enable scalable, label-efficient semantic segmentation for forest regeneration species.

Abstract: Sustainable forest management relies on precise species composition mapping, yet traditional ground surveys are labour-intensive and geographically constrained. While Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) offer scalable data collection, the transition to deep learning-based interpretation is bottlenecked by the severe scarcity of expert-annotated imagery, particularly in complex, visually heterogeneous regeneration zones. This paper addresses the dual challenges of data scarcity and extreme class imbalance in the semantic segmentation of fine-grained forest regeneration species by providing a scalable framework that reduces reliance on manual photo-interpretation for high-resolution, millimetre-level aerial imagery. Importantly, we leverage the large-scale vision-language Nano Banana Pro model to simultaneously generate high-fidelity images and their corresponding pixel-aligned semantic masks from prompts. We introduce WilDReF-Q-V2, an expansion of a natural forest dataset with 13 977 new unlabelled and 50 labelled real images, as well as the Gen4Regen dataset, featuring 2101 pairs of synthetic images and semantic masks. Our methodology integrates real-world data with AI-generated images, highlighting that AI-generated data is highly complementary to real-world data, with unified training yielding an F1 score improvement of over 15 %pt compared to purely supervised baselines. Furthermore, we demonstrate that even small quantities of prompt-generated data significantly improve performance for underrepresented species, some of which saw per-species F1 score gains of up to 30 %pt. We conclude that vision-language models can serve as agile data generators, effectively bootstrapping perception tasks for niche AI domains where expert labels are scarce or unavailable. Our datasets, source code, and models will be available at https://norlab-ulaval.github.io/gen4regen.

</details>


### [19] [Learning a Delighting Prior for Facial Appearance Capture in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05636)
*Yuxuan Han,Xin Ming,Tianxiao Li,Zhuofan Shen,Qixuan Zhang,Lan Xu,Feng Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a dataset-aware prior for reflectance/illumination disentanglement in facial capture, via Dataset Latent Modulation; yields automatic, high-quality reflectance from casual video and 4K relightable scans; open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-based inverse rendering struggles to disentangle reflectance from unknown illumination in in-the-wild data; a powerful, learnable prior is needed to regularize optimization.

Method: Train a 'delighting' prior using OLAT and Light Stage data, with Dataset Latent Modulation (DLM) that uses learnable source-aware tokens to decouple dataset-specific styles from physical delighting principles; integrate heterogeneous data to form a robust prior.

Result: Outperforms existing proprietary models; enables a simple, automatic appearance capture pipeline yielding high-quality reflectance from casual video; transforms NeRSemble into NeRSemble-Scan (4K relightable scans); open-sourced model and dataset.

Conclusion: Democratizes high-end facial capture and provides a solid foundation for photorealistic digital humans, enabling broader research and applications.

Abstract: High-quality facial appearance capture has traditionally required costly studio recording. Recent works consider an in-the-wild smartphone-based setup; however, their model-based inverse rendering paradigm struggles with the complex disentanglement of reflectance from unknown illumination. To bridge this gap, we propose to shift the paradigm into training a powerful delighting network as a prior to constrain the optimization. We leverage the OLAT dataset and the rendered Light Stage scans for training, and propose Dataset Latent Modulation (DLM) to seamlessly integrate these heterogeneous data sources. Specifically, by conditioning the core network on learnable source-aware tokens, we decouple dataset-specific styles from physical delighting principles, enabling the emergence of a delighting prior that outperforms existing proprietary models. This powerful delighting prior enables a simple and automatic appearance capture pipeline that achieves high-quality reflectance estimation from casual video inputs, outperforming prior arts by a large margin. Furthermore, we leverage our appearance capture method to transform the multi-view NeRSemble dataset into NeRSemble-Scan, a large-scale collection of 4K-resolution relightable scans. By open-sourcing our model and the NeRSemble-Scan dataset, we democratize high-end facial capture and provide a new foundation for the research community to build photorealistic digital humans.

</details>


### [20] [AffectSeek: Agentic Affective Understanding in Long Videos under Vague User Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05640)
*Zhen Zhang,Yuhang Yang,Yunxiang Jiang,Yuhuan Lu,Haifeng Lu,Zheng Lian,Runhao Zeng,Xiping Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces VQAU, a task for vague-query driven affective understanding in long videos; proposes VQAU-Bench benchmark and AffectSeek agentic framework; shows existing models struggle and that AffectSeek is effective for multi-step reasoning and rationale generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap between traditional clip-based or passive video affective understanding and real-world scenarios where users pose vague natural-language queries to long videos, requiring semantic-temporal-affective alignment, moment localization, emotion classification, and rationale generation.

Method: Define the VQAU task and construct VQAU-Bench, a unified evaluation framework containing long videos, vague queries, temporal clip annotations, emotion labels, and rationales. Propose AffectSeek, an agentic framework that decomposes the task into intent interpretation, candidate localization, clip verification, emotion reasoning, and rationale generation, with cross-stage verification and role-specialized reasoning.

Result: VQAU proves challenging for existing affective recognition and single-step vision-language models. AffectSeek offers a simple yet effective agentic framework that improves long-video affective understanding by iteratively localizing, verifying, and reasoning about affective moments and generating rationales.

Conclusion: The work establishes a new task, benchmark, and agentic framework for holistic long-video affective understanding under vague queries, highlighting the need for multi-step reasoning and providing a foundation for future research in semantic-temporal-affective alignment and explainable affective reasoning.

Abstract: Existing affective understanding studies have mainly focused on recognizing emotions from images, audio signals, or pre-cliped video clips, where the affective evidence is already given. This passive and clip-centered setting does not fully reflect real-world scenarios, in which users often interact with long videos and express their needs through natural-language queries. In this paper, we study \textbf{Vague-Query-driven video Affective Understanding (VQAU)}, a new task that requires models to localize affective moments in long videos, predict their emotion categories, and generate evidence-grounded rationales under vague user queries. To support this task, we construct \textbf{VQAU-Bench}, a benchmark that integrates long videos, vague affective queries, temporal clip annotations, emotion labels, and rationale explanations into a unified evaluation framework. VQAU-Bench enables systematic assessment of semantic-temporal-affective alignment, affective moment localization, emotion classification, and rationale generation. To address the multi-step reasoning challenges of VQAU, we further propose \textbf{AffectSeek}, an agentic framework that actively seeks, verifies, and explains affective moments in long videos. AffectSeek decomposes VQAU into intent interpretation, candidate localization, clip verification, emotion reasoning, and rationale generation, and progressively aligns vague user intent with long-video evidence through role-specialized reasoning and cross-stage verification. Experiments show that VQAU remains challenging for existing affective recognition models and single-step vision-language models, while AffectSeek provides a simple yet effective framework for agentic long-video affective understanding.

</details>


### [21] [MUSE: Resolving Manifold Misalignment in Visual Tokenization via Topological Orthogonality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05646)
*Panqi Yang,Haodong Jing,Jiahao Chao,Tingyan Xiang,Li Lin,Yao Hu,Yang Luo,Yongqiang Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MUSE decouples structural and semantic optimization in unified visual tokenization via Topological Orthogonality, breaking the reconstruction-perception trade-off and achieving state-of-the-art generation and linear probing


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified tokenization creates a conflict between high-fidelity reconstruction (spatial equivariance) and semantic abstraction (conceptual invariance) due to manifold misalignment and opposing gradients in joint optimization.

Method: Introduce MUSE, decoupling optimization with Structure as an orthogonal bridge. Structural gradients refine attention topology within Transformers, semantic gradients update feature values; topology-guided separation converts destructive interference into mutual reinforcement.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art generation quality (gFID 3.08) and improved linear probing (85.2% vs 82.5% for InternViT-300M) compared to a teacher model.

Conclusion: Structural alignment between reconstruction and semantic perception enhances both generation and downstream representation; topology-aware decoupling can overcome the previously observed trade-off in unified visual tokenization.

Abstract: Unified visual tokenization faces a fundamental trade-off between high-fidelity pixel reconstruction (spatial equivariance) and semantic abstraction (conceptual invariance). We attribute this conflict to Manifold Misalignment: naive joint optimization induces opposing gradients, creating a zero-sum game between reconstruction and perception. To address this, we propose MUSE, a framework based on Topological Orthogonality. By treating Structure as an orthogonal bridge, MUSE decouples optimization within Transformers: structural gradients refine attention topology, while semantic gradients update feature values. This turns destructive interference into Mutual Reinforcement. Experiments show that MUSE breaks the trade-off, achieving state-of-the-art generation quality (gFID 3.08) and surpassing its teacher InternViT-300M in linear probing (85.2\% vs. 82.5\%), demonstrating that structurally aligned reconstruction can enhance semantic perception. Code is available at https://github.com/PanqiYang1/MUSE.

</details>


### [22] [Sparse-to-Complete: From Sparse Image Captures to Complete 3D Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05664)
*Yiyang Shen,Yin Yang,Kun Zhou,Tianjia Shao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: S2C-3D introduces a sparse-view 3D reconstruction framework that achieves high-fidelity, complete scene reconstructions from as few as six to eight images, using (1) a scene-specific diffusion model for image restoration, (2) view-consistency conditioned sampling to enforce cross-view coherence without retraining, and (3) a trajectory planning scheme to ensure full scene coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust 3D reconstruction from very few images by addressing missing regions and artifacts common in sparse-view methods, leveraging diffusion-based restoration and a training-free consistency mechanism, combined with strategic camera trajectory planning.

Method: Three components: (1) fine-tuning a pretrained diffusion model on input views and their degraded counterparts to yield a scene-specific restoration model; (2) a view-consistency conditioned sampling process that injects cross-view consistency as a condition into a frozen diffusion model to produce coherent multi-view restorations without additional training; (3) a camera trajectory planning scheme that links each new camera to its two nearest neighbors and retains paths that significantly improve visibility, iteratively building a trajectory that covers the scene; the resultant high-fidelity 3D Gaussians are robust to artifacts.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows S2C-3D outperforms state-of-the-art methods, delivering high-quality, artifact-free reconstructions from very sparse inputs; source code and dataset are publicly available.

Conclusion: The combination of scene-specific diffusion-based restoration, view-consistency conditioning during sampling, and trajectory planning yields robust sparse-view 3D reconstruction, enabling complete scenes with six to eight views and reducing missing regions and artifacts.

Abstract: We introduce S2C-3D, a novel sparse-view 3D reconstruction framework for high-fidelity and complete scene reconstruction from as few as six to eight images. Our framework features three components: a specialized diffusion model for scene-specific image restoration, a training-free view-consistency conditioned sampling process in the diffusion model for refined Gaussian optimization, and a camera trajectory planning scheme to ensure comprehensive scene coverage. The specialized diffusion model is developed by finetuning a pretrained architecture on the input views and their corresponding degraded counterparts. The adaptation to the scene distribution allows the model to repair Gaussian renderings while effectively eliminating domain gaps. Meanwhile, the trajectory planning scheme optimizes scene coverage by connecting each newly sampled camera to its two nearest neighbors. By iteratively constructing paths and retaining only those that significantly enhance visibility, the scheme establishes a trajectory that covers the entire scene. To address multi-view conflicts, the view-consistency conditioned sampling process quantifies the consistency between neighboring repaired images. This information is injected as a condition into the sampling process of the frozen diffusion model, facilitating the generation of view-consistent images without additional training. Consequently, our approach produces high-fidelity 3D Gaussians that are robust to artifacts. Experimental results demonstrate that S2C-3D outperforms state-of-the-art methods, constructing high-quality scenes that are free from missing regions, blurring, or other artifacts with very sparse inputs. The source code and data are available at https://gapszju.github.io/S2C-3D.

</details>


### [23] [EGA: Adapting Frozen Encoders for Vector Search with Bounded Out-of-Distribution Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05674)
*Dongfang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EGA is a residual adapter for frozen vision encoders that uses zero initialization, a local triplet loss, and hypersphere projection to create a self-limiting training dynamic. It yields high worst-case Label Precision on OOD benchmarks by inducing gradient sparsity, with most triplets gradient-free at convergence and unseen-class regions largely preserved while refining seen classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address performance collapse of high-capacity adapters when adapting frozen vision encoders to unseen (OOD) classes. The goal is to refine seen-class geometry without perturbing unseen-class regions, maintaining robust out-of-distribution generalization.

Method: Introduce Euclidean Geodesic Alignment (EGA), a residual adapter built on three principles: (1) zero initialization so no initial disturbance; (2) local triplet loss on the embedding space to shape local geometry; (3) hypersphere projection to keep representations on the unit sphere. These together create a self-limiting dynamic where triplets that already satisfy a small margin stop producing gradients, effectively freezing updates in already-correct regions while enabling full-capacity refinement of seen classes.

Result: Empirical evaluation across five OOD benchmarks shows EGA achieves the highest worst-case Label Precision on four primary splits and consistent gains on the fifth. At convergence, about 96.5% of triplets are gradient-free, indicating targeted updating with minimal disruption to unseen-class regions. The method transfers to stronger backbones and CLIP, with an analytical justification connecting gradient sparsity to bounded OOD perturbation.

Conclusion: EGA delivers robust OOD robustness by preserving unseen-class geometry while allowing seen-class refinement, driven by gradient-sparse dynamics. The work also provides theoretical intuition for how gradient sparsity bounds perturbations in OOD regions and demonstrates transferability to stronger models.

Abstract: Vector search systems built on frozen vision encoders face queries from unseen classes at deployment, yet existing adapter training collapses under this shift: high-capacity adapters with global contrastive losses silently reassign unseen-class samples to wrong seen-class clusters, dropping worst-case Label Precision by over 40 points below the frozen baseline in our tests. We propose Euclidean Geodesic Alignment (EGA), a residual adapter that couples three principles: zero initialization, local triplet loss, and hypersphere projection. These collectively induce a self-limiting dynamic: triplets that already satisfy a small margin stop producing gradients, so the adapter automatically stops updating where the local geometry is already correct. Our experiments show that at convergence $96.5\%$ of triplets are gradient-free, leaving unseen-class regions largely untouched while still enabling full-capacity refinement of seen classes. Across five diverse out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks, EGA achieves the highest worst-case Label Precision on the four primary splits and a consistent improvement on the fifth. The design also transfers to stronger backbones in addition to CLIP, and we provide an analytical justification linking gradient sparsity to bounded OOD perturbation.

</details>


### [24] [MotionGRPO: Overcoming Low Intra-Group Diversity in GRPO-Based Egocentric Motion Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05680)
*Nanjie Yao,Junlong Ren,Wenhao Shen,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based 3D full-body motion recovery from HMD signals is enhanced by a reinforcement-learning post-training stage (MotionGRPO) that injects fine-grained guidance into diffusion via Group Relative Policy Optimization, with a hybrid reward and a noise-injection strategy to boost sample diversity; achieving state-of-the-art visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based full-body motion reconstruction often relies on global distribution alignment, causing local joint inaccuracies in poses derived from head-mounted device signals. There is a need for fine-grained control to improve local joint precision and stabilize learning given low intra-group diversity.

Method: Treat diffusion sampling as a Markov decision process and optimize with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Use a hybrid reward: a learned conditioned perceptual model for global visual plausibility and explicit constraints for local joint precision. Introduce a noise-injection strategy to increase sample variance and mitigate vanishing gradients.

Result: Experimental results show state-of-the-art performance with superior visual fidelity in full-body motion recovery from HMD signals.

Conclusion: MotionGRPO demonstrates that post-training reinforcement learning can inject fine-grained guidance into diffusion processes, addressing local-joint errors and training instability, leading to improved full-body motion recovery from head-mounted device signals.

Abstract: This paper studies full-body 3D human motion recovery from head-mounted device signals. Existing diffusion-based methods often rely on global distribution matching, leading to local joint reconstruction errors. We propose MotionGRPO, a novel framework leveraging reinforcement learning post-training to inject fine-grained guidance into the diffusion process. Technically, we model diffusion sampling as a Markov decision process optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). To this end, we introduce a hybrid reward mechanism that combines a learned conditioned perceptual model for global visual plausibility and explicit constraints for local joint precision. Our key technical insight is that policy optimization in diffusion-based recovery suffers from vanishing gradients due to limited intra-group sample diversity. To address this, we further introduce a noise-injection strategy that explicitly increases sample variance and stabilizes learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MotionGRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance with superior visual fidelity

</details>


### [25] [R2H-Diff: Guided Spectral Diffusion Model for RGB-to-Hyperspectral Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05688)
*Songyu Ding,Ronggiang Zhao,Mingchun Sun,Jie Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An efficient conditional diffusion framework for RGB-to-HSI reconstruction with RGB-guided refinement and spectral-aware attention, achieving high fidelity with a lightweight model and a 5-step denoising schedule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the ill-posed nature of RGB-to-HSI reconstruction and the limitations of regression-based methods, leveraging diffusion modeling for uncertain, multi-modal spectral recovery while handling high spectral dimensionality and fidelity constraints.

Method: R2H-Diff frames spectral recovery as a conditional iterative refinement guided by RGB input. It introduces a Guided Spectral Refinement Module for RGB-conditioned feature fusion and a Hyperspectral-Adaptive Transposed Attention module for efficient spatial–spectral dependency modeling. A normalization-free denoising backbone preserves spectral amplitudes, and a task-adapted linear noise schedule enables high-quality reconstruction with only five denoising steps.

Result: Empirical results on NTIRE2022, CAVE, and Harvard show favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. On NTIRE2022, it achieves 35.37 dB PSNR with a compact 0.58M-parameter model and 12.25G FLOPs, the lowest model complexity among evaluated methods while maintaining strong fidelity.

Conclusion: R2H-Diff delivers accurate RGB-to-HSI reconstruction with diffusion-based probabilistic modeling in a lightweight framework. The proposed modules effectively fuse RGB cues and capture spatial-spectral dependencies, and the normalization-free backbone plus tailored noise schedule enable efficient high-quality reconstruction with few denoising steps.

Abstract: RGB-to-hyperspectral image reconstruction is a highly ill-posed inverse problem, since multiple plausible spectral distributions may correspond to the same RGB observation. Existing regression-based methods usually learn a deterministic mapping, which limits their ability to model reconstruction uncertainty and often leads to over-smoothed spectral responses. Although diffusion models provide strong distribution modeling capability, their direct application to hyperspectral reconstruction remains challenging due to the high spectral dimensionality, strong inter-band correlations, and strict requirement for spectral fidelity. To this end, we propose R2H-Diff, an efficient diffusion-based framework tailored for RGB-to-HSI reconstruction. Specifically, R2H-Diff formulates spectral recovery as a conditional iterative refinement process, enabling progressive reconstruction under RGB guidance. We proposed a Guided Spectral Refinement Module for RGB-conditioned feature fusion and a Hyperspectral-Adaptive Transposed Attention module for efficient spatial--spectral dependency modeling. Furthermore, a normalization-free denoising backbone is adopted to preserve spectral amplitude consistency, while a task-adapted linear noise schedule enables high-quality reconstruction with only five denoising steps. Extensive experiments on NTIRE2022, CAVE, and Harvard demonstrate that R2H-Diff achieves a favorable balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. Notably, on NTIRE2022, R2H-Diff obtains 35.37 dB PSNR with a sub-million-parameter model of 0.58M parameters and 12.25G FLOPs, achieving the lowest model complexity among the evaluated methods while maintaining strong reconstruction fidelity.

</details>


### [26] [CFE-PPAR: Compression-friendly encryption for privacy-preserving action recognition leveraging video transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05692)
*Haiwei Lin,Shoko Imaizumi,Hitoshi Kiya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CFE-PPAR, a compression-friendly encryption method for privacy-preserving action recognition that enables direct recognition on encrypted videos using a video transformer whose parameters are transformed with the same keys.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Encryption-based PPAR methods offer strong privacy but suffer catastrophic performance drops when encrypted videos are compressed; a practical solution requires compression-friendly design.
