Conclusion: LipB-ViT offers an architecture-agnostic, uncertainty-calibrated framework for mitigating structured label noise in vision transformers, suitable for high-stakes applications with variable annotation reliability. Its main trade-off is higher inference cost due to MC sampling, but it provides practical tools for dataset quality and label-noise quantification.

Abstract: Label noise remains a critical bottleneck for the generalization of supervised deep learning models, particularly when errors are structured rather than random. Standard robust training methods often fail in the presence of such semantically proximal classification errors. This work presents an architecture-agnostic Lipschitz-constant Bayesian header that can be integrated into feature extractors such as vision transformers, yielding the bi-Lipschitz-constrained Bayesian Vision Transformer (LipB-ViT). In contrast to conventional Bayesian layers, our approach enforces spectral normalization on both the mean and log-variance of the variational weights, which promotes calibrated predictive uncertainty and mitigates noise amplification. We further propose a novel metric to jointly capture uncertainty and confidence across misclassification rates, as well as an adaptive arithmetic-mean fusion scheme that combines feature-space proximity with predictive uncertainty to detect corrupted labels outperforming the state of the art k-nearest neighbor based identification methods by more than 7% reaching a recall of more than 0.93 at 15% semantically misclassified labels. Although computational costs increase due to Monte Carlo sampling, the method offers plug-and-play compatibility with pre-trained backbones and consistent hyperparameters across domains, suggesting strong utility for high-stakes applications with variable annotation reliability. The stabilized confidence estimates serve as the foundation for an analysis pipeline that jointly assesses dataset quality and label noise, yielding a second novel metric for their combined quantification. Lastly, we systematically evaluate LipB-ViT under both structured (adversarial) and unstructured noise at inference time, demonstrating its robustness in realistic high-noise and attack scenarios. We compare its performance against baseline methods.

</details>


### [52] [Plug-and-play Class-aware Knowledge Injection for Prompt Learning with Visual-Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05910)
*Junhui Yin,Nan Pu,Xinyu Zhang,Lingfeng Yang,Lin Wu,Xiaojie Wang,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAKI introduces a plug-and-play, class-aware knowledge injection framework for prompt learning in vision-language models. It builds a class-specific knowledge bank from few-shot samples and retrieves it at test time to refine predictions, improving performance on base and novel classes; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-learning approaches for VLMs either use class-shared prompts or instance-specific prompts, but both neglect class-specific knowledge that can provide finer supervision and prevent cross-class misclassification. Leveraging class-level information across instances can improve discrimination and generalization, especially for base and novel classes.

Method: Two components: (1) class-specific prompt generation, which encodes class-specific knowledge from few-shot samples into prompts and stores them in a class-level knowledge bank; (2) query-key prompt matching, which retrieves relevant class-level knowledge at test time and injects it to refine predictions, enabling plug-and-play integration with existing methods.

Result: Extensive experiments show CAKI improves performance of existing prompt-learning methods on base and novel classes across benchmarks, demonstrating effective integration of class-specific knowledge; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: CAKI provides a practical, plug-and-play mechanism to inject class-specific knowledge into prompt-based VLMs, enhancing discrimination and generalization without requiring full retraining.

Abstract: Prompt learning has become an effective and widely used technique in enhancing vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP for various downstream tasks, particularly in zero-shot classification within specific domains. Existing methods typically focus on either learning class-shared prompts for a given domain or generating instance-specific prompts through conditional prompt learning. While these methods have achieved promising performance, they often overlook class-specific knowledge in prompt design, leading to suboptimal outcomes. The underlying reasons are: 1) class-specific prompts offer more fine-grained supervision compared to coarse class-shared prompts, which helps prevent misclassification of data from different classes into a single class; 2) compared to class-specific prompts, instance-specific prompts neglect the richer class-level information across multiple instances, potentially causing data from the same class to be divided into multiple classes. To effectively supplement the class-specific knowledge into existing methods, we propose a plug-and-play Class-Aware Knowledge Injection (CAKI) framework. CAKI comprises two key components, i.e., class-specific prompt generation and query-key prompt matching. The former encodes class-specific knowledge into prompts from few-shot samples that belong to the same class and stores the learned prompts in a class-level knowledge bank. The latter provides a plug-and-play mechanism for each test instance to retrieve relevant class-level knowledge from the knowledge bank and inject such knowledge to refine model predictions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our CAKI effectively improves the performance of existing methods on base and novel classes. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/yjh576/CAKI}{this https URL}.

</details>


### [53] [Think, then Score: Decoupled Reasoning and Scoring for Video Reward Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05922)
*Yuan Wang,Ouxiang Li,Yulong Xu,Borui Liao,Jiajun Liang,Jinghan Li,Meng Wang,Xintao Wang,Pengfei Wang,Kuien Liu,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A decoupled think-then-score video reward model (DeScore) that separates chain-of-thought generation from reward scoring to enable efficient training and better generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discriminative reward models learn rewards from MLLM features and risk shortcut learning; generative RMs with chain-of-thought (CoT) offer interpretability but suffer from optimization bottlenecks due to coupling CoT and scoring. A decoupled approach aims to combine CoT benefits with stable training and generalization.

Method: DeScore uses a two-stage, decoupled pipeline: (1) a discriminative cold start with a random mask to bootstrap robust scoring from MLLM-generated CoT; (2) a dual-objective reinforcement learning stage that separately refines CoT reasoning quality and calibrates the final reward via a learnable query token and a regression head that predicts the reward.

Result: The approach is claimed to improve training efficiency, interpretability, and generalization by decoupling reasoning from scoring, with the two-stage training enabling robust CoT generation and reliable reward calibration.

Conclusion: Decoupled think-then-score with a dedicated scoring module and staged optimization offers a scalable, interpretable, and robust video reward modeling paradigm that mitigates the training instability of coupled CoT and scoring.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative video models are increasingly driven by post-training and test-time scaling, both of which critically depend on the quality of video reward models (RMs). An ideal reward model should predict accurate rewards that align with human preferences across diverse scenarios. However, existing paradigms face a fundamental dilemma: \textit{Discriminative RMs} regress rewards directly on features extracted by multimodal large language models (MLLMs) without explicit reasoning, making them prone to shortcut learning and heavily reliant on massive data scaling for generalization. In contrast, \textit{Generative RMs} with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning exhibit superior interpretability and generalization potential, as they leverage fine-grained semantic supervision to internalize the rationales behind human preferences. However, they suffer from inherent optimization bottlenecks due to the coupling of reasoning and scoring within a single autoregressive inference chain. To harness the generalization benefits of CoT reasoning while mitigating the training instability of coupled reasoning and scoring, we introduce DeScore, a training-efficient and generalizable video reward model. DeScore employs a decoupled ``think-then-score'' paradigm: an MLLM first generates an explicit CoT, followed by a dedicated discriminative scoring module consisting of a learnable query token and a regression head that predicts the final reward. DeScore is optimized via a two-stage framework: (1) a discriminative cold start incorporating a random mask mechanism to ensure robust scoring capabilities, and (2) a dual-objective reinforcement learning stage that independently refines CoT reasoning quality and calibrates the final reward, ensuring that higher-quality reasoning directly translates to superior model performance.

</details>


### [54] [Backdoor Mitigation in Object Detection via Adversarial Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05928)
*Kealan Dunnett,Reza Arablouei,Dimity Miller,Volkan Dedeoglu,Raja Jurdak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Detection-aware adversarial fine-tuning for object detectors to mitigate backdoors, using soft-branch minimisation and dual-objective losses; effective across CNN/Transformer detectors with limited clean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in object detection are underexplored; classification-based defenses do not transfer well to detection; need attack-objective-agnostic mitigation and targeted repair under data constraints.

Method: Propose detection-aware adversarial fine-tuning. Soft-branch minimisation combines misclassification and disappearance objectives via a soft gate; detection-aware classification-loss maximisation; dual-objective fine-tuning loss targets predictions aligned with the backdoor attack.

Result: Empirical results show reduced attack success and preserved true detections, outperforming classification-based baselines; clean detection performance remains competitive across CNN/Transformer detectors.

Conclusion: The framework enables effective backdoor mitigation in object detection with limited clean data, without requiring knowledge of the attack objective.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks can implant malicious behaviours into deep models while preserving performance on clean data, posing a serious threat to safety-critical vision systems. Although backdoor mitigation has been studied extensively for image classification, defenses for object detection remain comparatively underdeveloped. Adversarial fine-tuning is a common backdoor mitigation approach in classification, but adapting it to detection is nontrivial as classification-oriented adversarial generation does not match the detection attack space, where attacks may cause object misclassification or disappearance, and standard detection losses can dilute the repair signal across many predictions. We address these challenges through a detection-aware adversarial fine-tuning framework for mitigating object-detection backdoors when the defender has access only to a compromised detector and a small clean dataset, without knowing the attack objective. For adversarial generation that does not require knowledge of the attack objective, we introduce soft-branch minimisation, which uses a soft gate to combine objectives aligned with misclassification and disappearance attacks, together with a detection-aware classification-loss maximisation. For targeted repair, we introduce a dual-objective fine-tuning loss applied to target-matched predictions, concentrating the defensive update on predictions most relevant to the backdoor behaviour. Experiments across CNN- and Transformer-based detectors show that our approach more effectively reduces attack success while preserving true detections, compared with classification-oriented baselines, and maintains competitive clean detection performance.

</details>


### [55] [Whole-body CT attenuation and volume charts from routine clinical scans via evidence-grounded LLM report filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05933)
*Christian Wachinger,Bernhard Renger,Christopher Späth,Jan Kirschke,Marcus Makowski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ensemble LLM filtering to create pathology-free CT cohorts and develop comprehensive reference charts for 106 structures using GAMLSS, enabling covariate-adjusted centile scoring for standardized quantitative CT phenotyping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical CT datasets are heavily enriched with pathology, complicating the construction of healthy reference distributions needed for quantitative biomarkers.

Method: Five-stage LLM pipeline: (i) flag structure-level abnormality candidates grounded in verbatim report evidence; (ii) cross-verification to resolve disagreements; apply to >350,000 CT exams to build pathology-reduced cohorts. Then, use distribution-aware generalized additive models for location, scale, and shape (GAMLSS) to model 106 anatomical structures (volumes and attenuation) across adulthood, accounting for age, sex, contrast enhancement, and acquisition parameters. Longitudinal analyses assess structure- and contrast-dependent changes distinct from cross-sectional trends. Generate covariate-adjusted centile reference charts enabling standardized phenotyping, multi-site studies, and scalable opportunistic screening.

Result: Created comprehensive whole-body reference charts for 106 structures with covariate-adjusted centiles; observed longitudinal changes that depend on structure and contrast and diverge from cross-sectional trends; provides a scalable resource for standardized quantitative CT phenotyping and multi-site imaging studies.

Conclusion: A scalable framework to derive healthy reference distributions from routine clinical CT data by filtering pathology with an LLM ensemble, enabling robust, covariate-aware centile scoring for quantitative anatomy across the adult body.

Abstract: Interpreting quantitative CT biomarkers, such as organ volume and tissue attenuation, requires large-scale healthy reference distributions. However, creating these is challenging because clinical datasets are often heavily enriched with pathology. Here, we develop an evidence-grounded, cross-verified large language model (LLM) ensemble to filter pathological findings from radiology reports, enabling the construction of pathology-reduced cohorts from over 350,000 CT examinations. Five LLMs, first, flag structure-level abnormality candidates grounded in verbatim report evidence and, second, resolve disagreements via cross-verification. Using distribution-aware generalized additive models for location, scale, and shape, we establish comprehensive whole-body reference charts for 106 anatomical structures (volumes and attenuation) across adulthood, accounting for age, sex, contrast enhancement, and acquisition parameters. Longitudinal analyses reveal structure- and contrast-dependent changes distinct from cross-sectional trends. These resources facilitate covariate-adjusted centile scoring from routine CT, supporting standardized quantitative phenotyping, multi-site imaging studies, and scalable opportunistic screening research.

</details>


### [56] [RAWild: Sensor-Agnostic RAW Object Detection via Physics-Guided Curve and Grid Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05941)
*Shuhong Liu,Gengjia Chang,Jun Liu,Xuangeng Chu,Yinqiang Zheng,Tatsuya Harada,Ziteng Cui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAWild introduces a physics-guided global-local tone mapping for sensor-agnostic RAW object detection, plus a RAW sensor simulation pipeline; achieves SOTA across datasets and bit depths (10-24).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain gaps across camera sensors in RAW data due to varying exposure, spectral sensitivities, and bit depths exceed those in sRGB; RAW preserves information and avoids ISP distortions, but cross-sensor generalization remains challenging.

Method: A global tonal correction and a spatially adaptive local color adjustment, both driven by RAW distribution priors, enabling a single network to train across heterogeneous sensors. A physics-based RAW simulation pipeline synthesizes realistic sensor outputs across diverse spectral sensitivities, illuminants, and non-idealities to support cross-sensor generalization.

Result: Extensive experiments on RAW benchmarks spanning bit depths from 10 to 24 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance under single-dataset, mixed-dataset, and robustness settings.

Conclusion: The framework and simulation pipeline together enhance sensor-agnostic RAW object detection, offering improved cross-device generalization and practical robustness across diverse RAW imaging conditions.

Abstract: Camera sensor RAW data offers intrinsic advantages for object detection, including deeper bit depth, preserved physical information, and freedom from image signal processor (ISP) distortions. However, varying exposure conditions, spectral sensitivities, and bit depths across devices introduce substantially larger domain gaps than sRGB, making sensor-agnostic generalization a fundamental challenge. In this study, we present \textbf{RAWild}, a physics-guided global-local tone mapping framework for sensor-agnostic RAW object detection. By factoring sensor-induced variations into a global tonal correction and a spatially adaptive local color adjustment, both driven by RAW distribution priors, our framework enables a single network to train jointly across heterogeneous sensors. To further support cross-sensor generalization, we construct a physics-based RAW simulation pipeline that synthesizes realistic sensor outputs spanning diverse spectral sensitivities, illuminants, and sensor non-idealities. Extensive experiments across multiple RAW benchmarks covering bit depths from 10 to 24 demonstrate state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance under single-dataset, mixed-dataset, and challenging robustness settings.

</details>


### [57] [MobileEgo Anywhere: Open Infrastructure for long horizon egocentric data on commodity hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05945)
*Senthil Palanisamy,Abhishek Anand,Satpal Singh Rathor,Pratyush Patnaik,Shubhanshu Khatana*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MobileEgo Anywhere enables hour-plus egocentric data collection on commodity smartphones, releasing a 200-hour dataset, a recording app, and a processing pipeline to standardize data for Vision Language Action and foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon temporal data is essential for learning robust robotic policies; current datasets are too short; democratize data collection to lower hardware barriers and enable global, diverse data.

Method: Framework leveraging smartphone sensors for persistent camera pose tracking; collects hours of egocentric data; provides mobile app and processing pipeline; ensures training-ready formats; includes persistent state tracking.

Result: Release of 200 hours dataset; open-source app; processing pipeline; enables large-scale long-horizon data collection across diverse environments.

Conclusion: Democratizing data collection accelerates development of generalizable robotic policies and VLA/foundation model research, addressing the long-horizon data gap.

Abstract: The recent advancement of Vision Language Action (VLA) models has driven a critical demand for large scale egocentric datasets. However, existing datasets are often limited by short episode durations, typically spanning only a few minutes, which fails to capture the long horizon temporal dependencies necessary for complex robotic task execution. To bridge this gap, we present MobileEgo Anywhere, a framework designed to facilitate the collection of robust, hour plus egocentric trajectories using commodity mobile hardware. We leverage the ubiquitous sensor suites of modern smartphones to provide high fidelity, long term camera pose tracking, effectively removing the high hardware barriers associated with traditional robotics data collection. Our contributions are three fold: (1) we release a novel dataset comprising 200 hours of diverse, long form egocentric data with persistent state tracking; (2) we open source a mobile application that enables any user to record egocentric data, and (3) we provide a comprehensive processing pipeline to convert raw mobile captures into standardized, training ready formats for Vision Language Action model and foundation model research. By democratizing the data collection process, this work enables the massive scale acquisition of long horizon data across varied global environments, accelerating the development of generalizable robotic policies.

</details>


### [58] [Prompt-Free and Efficient SAM2 Adaptation for Biomedical Semantic Segmentation via Dual Adapters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05979)
*Hinako Mitsuoka,Kazuhiro Hotta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prompt-free, parameter-efficient SAM2 adaptation for biomedical multi-class segmentation using a convolutional positional encoding generator and dual adapters (high-performance deformable convs; lightweight reparameterized convs). Achieves up to 19.66% accuracy gain over vanilla SAM2 and ~87% reduction in compute versus heavyweight medical SAM adaptations on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM2 excels in natural images but suffers from domain shift and prompt dependence in biomedical segmentation, necessitating an efficient, prompt-free fine-tuning approach that handles multi-class, variable-sized inputs.

Method: Introduce a convolutional positional encoding generator to adapt to arbitrary aspect ratios. Employ a dual-adapter scheme: a High-Performance Adapter with deformable convolutions for precise boundary modeling, and a Lightweight Adapter with structural re-parameterization to reduce inference latency, forming a prompt-free, parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for multi-class segmentation.

Result: On ISBI 2012, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC, the method outperforms strong baselines, achieving up to 19.66% higher segmentation accuracy than vanilla SAM2 and ~87% lower computational costs than heavyweight medical SAM adaptations.

Conclusion: The proposed prompt-free, parameter-efficient framework delivers a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off for biomedical multi-class segmentation under variable input sizes, with strong empirical validation across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) demonstrated impressive zero-shot capabilities on natural images but faces challenges in biomedical segmentation due to significant domain shifts and prompt dependency. To address these limitations, we propose a prompt-free, parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework designed for multi-class segmentation on variable-sized inputs. We introduce a convolutional Positional Encoding Generator to adapt effectively to arbitrary aspect ratios and present a dual-adapter strategy: High-Performance Adapter utilizing deformable convolutions for precise boundary modeling and Lightweight Adapter employing structural re-parameterization to minimize inference latency. Experiments on ISBI 2012, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms strong adaptation baselines. Specifically, our method improved segmentation accuracy by up to 19.66\% over the vanilla SAM2, while reducing computational costs by approximately 87\% compared to heavyweight medical SAM adaptations, establishing a superior trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

</details>


### [59] [iPhoneBlur: A Difficulty-Stratified Benchmark for Consumer Device Motion Deblurring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05990)
*Abdullah Al Shafi,Kazi Saeed Alam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A difficulty-stratified, ISP-aware motion blur benchmark (iPhoneBlur) for mobile devices, using 7,400 image pairs from iPhone 17 Pro videos; reveals large performance gaps across Easy/Medium/Hard obscured by aggregate metrics; shows a domain gap between professional and consumer cameras; includes metadata for deployment-aware evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregate blur benchmarks obscure how restoration models perform under real deployment conditions. iPhoneBlur aims to expose reliability and failure modes by stratifying blur difficulty and providing deployment-relevant metadata.

Method: Synthesize blur from high-framerate videos; partition samples into Easy/Medium/Hard via PSNR-guided adaptive temporal windowing; validate stratification by monotonic 2.2x increase in optical-flow magnitude across tiers; analyze spectral properties to confirm realism; evaluate six architectures; assess domain gap between professional and consumer cameras; provide metadata enabling ISP-aware and difficulty-adaptive restoration strategies.

Result: Found consistent 7–9 dB degradation from Easy to Hard across six architectures, revealing a substantial performance gap hidden by aggregate reporting; domain gap exists with pro vs. consumer cameras, with targeted fine-tuning partially recovering performance; spectral analysis shows realistic high-frequency suppression; iPhoneBlur enables systematic reliability and failure-mode assessment for edge deployment.

Conclusion: iPhoneBlur provides a deployment-focused benchmark to measure model reliability on resource-constrained devices, enabling systematic investigation of failure modes and design choices beyond aggregate metrics.

Abstract: Motion blur restoration on consumer mobile devices is typically evaluated using aggregate metrics that obscure performance variation across blur difficulty, masking model behavior under real deployment conditions. This work introduces iPhoneBlur, a difficulty-stratified benchmark of 7,400 image pairs synthesized from high-framerate iPhone 17 Pro videos captured in diverse real-world scenarios. Samples are partitioned into Easy, Medium, and Hard categories through PSNR-guided adaptive temporal windowing, with stratification validated by monotonic 2.2x increase in optical flow magnitude across tiers. Each sample includes comprehensive metadata enabling investigation of ISP-aware and difficulty-adaptive restoration strategies. Spectral analysis confirms synthesized blur exhibits high-frequency suppression patterns consistent with authentic motion degradation. Evaluation of six architectures reveals consistent 7-9 dB performance degradation from Easy to Hard subsets, a substantial gap entirely hidden by aggregate reporting. The benchmark further exposes a domain gap between professional and consumer cameras which targeted fine-tuning substantially recovers. By coupling difficulty stratification with deployment-critical metadata, iPhoneBlur enables systematic assessment of model reliability and failure modes for resource-constrained edge systems.

</details>


### [60] [4DThinker: Thinking with 4D Imagery for Dynamic Spatial Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05997)
*Zhangquan Chen,Manyuan Zhang,Xinlei Yu,Xiang An,Bo Li,Xin Xie,ZiDong Wang,Mingze Sun,Shuang Chen,Hongyu Li,Xiaobin Hu,Ruqi Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework (4DThinker) enables vision-language models to perform dynamic 4D spatial reasoning via internal latent imagery, with an annotation-free data pipeline, Dynamic-Imagery Fine-Tuning (DIFT) grounding 4D latents to text, and 4D Reinforcement Learning (4DRL) for stable, token-centered policy optimization; shows superior results on dynamic spatial reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome two shortcomings in existing VLM dynamic reasoning: (1) reliance on verbose, imprecise text-only reasoning, and (2) external geometric modules that add inference cost without improving intrinsic model capabilities. Introduce internal 4D mental imagery to model dynamics directly within the hidden space.

Method: 1) Build a scalable annotation-free data generation pipeline that creates 4D reasoning data from raw videos. 2) Dynamic-Imagery Fine-Tuning (DIFT) jointly supervises textual tokens and 4D latent representations to ground dynamic visual semantics. 3) 4D Reinforcement Learning (4DRL) uses outcome-based rewards and constrains policy gradients to text tokens to stabilize optimization.

Result: Empirical results on multiple dynamic spatial reasoning benchmarks show that 4DThinker consistently outperforms strong baselines, validating the effectiveness of internal 4D latent imagery for dynamic reasoning. Code is released at the provided repository link.

Conclusion: 4DThinker offers a new paradigm for 4D reasoning in VLMs by integrating dynamic latent imagery with text-grounded supervision and token-focused RL, reducing reliance on external modules and enabling scalable dynamic reasoning.

Abstract: Dynamic spatial reasoning from monocular video is essential for bridging visual intelligence and the physical world, yet remains challenging for vision-language models (VLMs). Prior approaches either verbalize spatial-temporal reasoning entirely as text, which is inherently verbose and imprecise for complex dynamics, or rely on external geometric modules that increase inference complexity without fostering intrinsic model capability. In this paper, we present 4DThinker, the first framework that enables VLMs to "think with 4D" through dynamic latent mental imagery, i.e., internally simulating how scenes evolve within the continuous hidden space. Specifically, we first introduce a scalable, annotation-free data generation pipeline that synthesizes 4D reasoning data from raw videos. We then propose Dynamic-Imagery Fine-Tuning (DIFT), which jointly supervises textual tokens and 4D latents to ground the model in dynamic visual semantics. Building on this, 4D Reinforcement Learning (4DRL) further tackles complex reasoning tasks via outcome-based rewards, restricting policy gradients to text tokens to ensure stable optimization. Extensive experiments across multiple dynamic spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that 4DThinker consistently outperforms strong baselines and offers a new perspective toward 4D reasoning in VLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/zhangquanchen/4DThinker.

</details>


### [61] [Neuromorphic visual attention for Sign-language recognition on SpiNNaker](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06005)
*Sarka Liskova,Olha Vedmedenko,Mazdak Fatahi,Matej Hoffmann,P. Michael Furlong,Giulia D Angelo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An end-to-end neuromorphic ASL fingerspelling recognizer using a spiking visual attention module and a compact SNN on SpiNNaker; demonstrates competitive accuracy and ultra-low energy for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address latency and power constraints of conventional sign-language recognition methods and exploit event-driven neuromorphic sensing for real-time perception.

Method: Integrates a spiking visual attention mechanism for online ROI extraction with a compact spiking neural network deployed on the SpiNNaker neuromorphic platform; evaluated on synthetic event-based Sign Language MNIST and ASL-DVS datasets.

Result: Simulation accuracy 92.27%; hardware deployment accuracy 83.1%; energy 0.565 mW; latency 3 ms; demonstrates energy-efficient, edge-deployable performance.

Conclusion: Task-dependent visual attention plus neuromorphic SNNs on SpiNNaker is a viable approach for fast, low-power sign-language recognition, enabling edge deployment with competitive accuracy.

Abstract: Sign-language recognition has achieved substantial gains in classification accuracy in recent years; however, the latency and power requirements of most existing methods limit their suitability for real-time deployment. Neuromorphic sensing and processing offer an alternative paradigm based on sparse, event-driven computation that supports low-latency and energy-efficient perception. In this work, we introduce an end-to-end neuromorphic architecture for American Sign Language (ASL) fingerspelling recognition that integrates a spiking visual attention mechanism for online region-of-interest extraction with a compact spiking neural network deployed on the SpiNNaker neuromorphic platform. We benchmark the proposed system against two datasets: a synthetically generated event-based version of the Sign Language MNIST dataset and a natively recorded ASL-DVS dataset, whilst providing a comprehensive overview of Sign-language recognition and related work. This work yields competitive performance in simulation (92.27%) and comparable performance on neuromorphic hardware deployment (83.1%), while achieving the most energy-efficient architecture (0.565 mW) and low latency (3 ms) across all benchmarked approaches. Despite its compact design, the system demonstrates the suitability of task-dependent visual attention applications for edge deployment.

</details>


### [62] [Adding Thermal Awareness to Visual Systems in Real-Time via Distilled Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06010)
*Yuchen Guo,Junli Gong,Wenjun Dong,Yiuming Cheung,Weifeng Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A plug-and-play, real-time RGB-IR image fusion module (FusionProxy) that uses teacher-ensemble variance cues to guide pixel-level and feature-level supervision, achieving diffusion-level fusion quality with real-time speed and improved static recognition and dynamic robustness (e.g., autonomous driving).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable all-day, robust perception by fusing infrared information with RGB data while meeting real-time constraints for edge deployment, addressing limitations of purely RGB models under adverse conditions.

Method: FusionProxy leverages two statistics from a teacher sample ensemble: (1) per-pixel variance in raw image space to weight pixel-level supervision, and (2) per-pixel variance inside frozen foundation backbones to route feature-level alignment spatially. It is trained as a separate fusion proxy and can be plugged into existing perception systems without joint optimization at deployment.

Result: Demonstrates superior performance on static recognition tasks and notably improved robustness in dynamic tasks (e.g., closed-loop autonomous driving). Achieves real-time inference speeds across platforms from high-end GPUs to commodity hardware, indicating practical edge deployment potential.

Conclusion: FusionProxy offers a general, flexible, plug-and-play fusion component with diffusion-level quality that enhances all-day perception without requiring joint optimization, suitable for real-time, resource-constrained environments.

Abstract: Purely RGB-based vision models often fail to provide reliable cues in challenging scenarios such as nighttime and fog, leading to degraded performance and safety risks. Infrared imaging captures heat-emitting sources and provides critical complementary information, but existing high-fidelity fusion methods suffer from prohibitive latency, rendering them impractical for real-time edge deployment. To address this, we propose FusionProxy, a real-time image fusion module designed as a fully independent, plug-and-play component with diffusion level quality. FusionProxy exploits two complementary statistics of a teacher sample ensemble: per-pixel variance in raw image space, used to weight pixel-level supervision, and per-pixel variance inside frozen foundation backbones, used to route feature-level alignment spatially. Once trained, FusionProxy can be directly integrated into any visual perception system without joint optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on static recognition tasks and significantly enhances robustness in dynamic tasks, including closed-loop autonomous driving. Crucially, FusionProxy achieves real-time inference speeds on diverse platforms, from high-end GPUs to commodity hardware, providing a flexible and generalizable solution for all-day perception.

</details>


### [63] [T2I-VeRW: Part-level Fine-grained Perception for Text-to-Image Vehicle Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06012)
*Xiao Wang,Ziwen Wang,Weizhe Kong,Wentao Wu,Yuehang Li,Aihua Zheng,Chenglong Li,Jin Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PFCVR is a text-to-image vehicle re-ID model that uses part-level cross-modal alignment with learnable part-query tokens and a bi-directional mask-recovery module, plus a new large-scale part-annotated dataset (T2I-VeRW). It achieves state-of-the-art Rank-1 on both the text-to-image VeRI/W benchmarks, outperforming baselines by notable margins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable realistic text-based vehicle re-identification by bridging fine-grained, part-level visual cues with witness descriptions; address the lack of large, annotated datasets for text-to-image vehicle Re-ID.

Method: Constructs locally paired images and texts at the part level with learnable part-query tokens that fuse part-specific and full-sentence context for alignment with visual part features. Introduces a bi-directional mask-recovery module where each modality reconstructs masked content guided by the other, promoting global alignment from local correspondences. Builds T2I-VeRW, a large-scale dataset with 14,668 images and 1,796 identities with fine-grained part annotations.

Result: On the T2I-VeRI dataset, Rank-1 accuracy is 29.2%, up by 3.7 percentage points over the best competitor. On the T2I-VeRW benchmark, Rank-1 accuracy is 55.2%, outperforming recent state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that explicit part-level cross-modal alignment combined with cross-modal mask recovery effectively improves text-to-image vehicle Re-ID. The new dataset mitigates data scarcity and enables robust evaluation. Code will be made available.

Abstract: Vehicle Re-identification (Re-ID) aims to retrieve the most similar image to a given query from images captured by non-overlapping cameras. Extending vehicle Re-ID from image-only queries to text-based queries enables retrieval in real-world scenarios where only a witness description of the target vehicle is available. In this paper, we propose PFCVR, a Part-level Fine-grained Cross-modal Vehicle Retrieval model for text-to-image vehicle re-identification. PFCVR constructs locally paired images and texts at the part level and introduces learnable part-query tokens that aggregate both part-specific and full-sentence context before aligning with visual part features. On top of this explicit local alignment, a bi-directional mask recovery module lets each modality reconstruct its masked content under the guidance of the other, implicitly bridging local correspondences into global feature alignment. Furthermore, we construct a new large-scale dataset called T2I-VeRW, which contains 14,668 images covering 1,796 vehicle identities with fine-grained part-level annotations. Experimental results on the T2I-VeRI dataset show that PFCVR achieves 29.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, improving over the best competing method by +3.7\% percentage points. On the newly proposed T2I-VeRW benchmark, PFCVR achieves 55.2\% Rank-1 accuracy, outperforming a comprehensive set of recent state-of-the-art methods. Source code will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/Neuromorphic_ReID

</details>


### [64] [PlotPick: AI-powered batch extraction of numerical data from scientific figures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06021)
*Tommy Carstensen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PlotPick is an open-source tool that uses vision-language models to batch-extract structured tabular data from scientific figures, outperforming the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot on two benchmarks (ChartX and PlotQA), especially for chart types not seen in DePlot's training data (e.g., box plots).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematic reviews require numerical data from figures that are often only in image form; manual digitisation is slow and not scalable. Existing dedicated chart-to-table models underperform on charts outside their training distribution, motivating a more generalizable, flexible approach using vision-language models.

Method: Evaluate six vision-language models from three providers on two standard benchmarks (ChartX with 300 items across bar, line, box plots, histograms; PlotQA with 529 items) and compare to the specialized model DePlot. Measure recall on ChartX and RMSF1 on PlotQA to assess extraction quality and generalization; analyze performance gaps, notably on chart types absent from DePlot's training data.

Result: VLMs outperform DePlot on both benchmarks. ChartX recall: 88-96% (VLMs) vs 71% (DePlot). PlotQA RMSF1: 86-99% (VLMs) vs 94% (DePlot). The largest gap is on box plots, where DePlot attains 24% RMSF1 while VLMs reach 83-97%. PlotPick is open-source and available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: Vision-language models enable robust, scalable extraction of chart data across diverse chart types, including those not seen in training, substantially surpassing a domain-specialist model on standard benchmarks. PlotPick offers a practical, open-source solution for digitizing figures in large-scale meta-analyses.

Abstract: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses frequently require numerical data that authors report only as figures, yet manual digitisation is slow and does not scale. We present PlotPick, an open-source tool that uses vision-language models (VLMs) to batch-extract structured tabular data from scientific figures. We evaluate six VLMs from three providers on two established chart-to-table benchmarks (ChartX and PlotQA) and compare against the dedicated chart-to-table model DePlot. All six VLMs outperform DePlot on both benchmarks. On ChartX (restricted to bar charts, line charts, box plots, and histograms; n=300), VLMs achieve 88-96% recall versus 71% for DePlot. On PlotQA (n=529), VLMs achieve 86-99% RMSF1 versus 94% for DePlot. The gap is largest on chart types absent from the dedicated models' training data: on box plots, DePlot achieves 24% RMSF1 while VLMs achieve 83-97%. PlotPick is available at https://plotpick.streamlit.app.

</details>


### [65] [Domain Generalization through Spatial Relation Induction over Visual Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06043)
*Dat Nguyen,Duc-Duy Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PARSE introduces a primitive-aware relational scheme for domain generalization, factoring recognition into visual primitives and their spatial relationships, learned end-to-end with differentiable predicates; yields improved DG performance, notably on CUB-DG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DG methods often improve stability through training tricks but do not explicitly model the visual compositional structure, which limits compositional generalization. PARSE aims to learn stable primitive representations and their relations as the core of recognition.

Method: An end-to-end architecture with three components: (1) CNN backbone for general features, (2) concept bottleneck mapping features to primitive heatmaps with differentiable coordinates, (3) a structural scoring layer evaluating candidate spatial relations among detected primitives using soft binary/ternary/quaternary predicates, yielding differentiable measures of spatial alignment; class probability is inferred from joint evidence of relational compositions.

Result: On CUB-DG, PARSE improves accuracy by >4.5 percentage points; on DomainBed it remains competitive with existing DG methods.

Conclusion: Explicit, differentiable modeling of primitive visual components and their relations enhances compositional domain generalization, enabling end-to-end learning of structure-informed representations and achieving robust DG performance.

Abstract: Domain generalization requires identifying stable representations that support reliable classification across domains. Most existing methods seek such stability through improving the training process, for example, through model selection strategies, data augmentation, or feature-alignment objectives. Although these strategies can be effective, they leave the representation learning of structural composition implicit, which may limit performance on compositional domain generalization benchmarks.
  In this work, we propose Primitive-Aware Relational Structure for domain gEneralization (PARSE), an image classification framework that factors visual recognition into visual primitives and their relational composition. We represent these compositions using soft binary, ternary, and quaternary predicates over primitive locations, yielding differentiable measures of spatial alignment that can be learned end-to-end. To learn primitives and relational structures jointly, we design an end-to-end architecture with three components: (1) a convolutional neural network (CNN) backbone that extracts general visual features, (2) a concept bottleneck layer that maps these features to primitive heatmaps with differentiable spatial coordinates, and (3) a structural scoring layer that evaluates candidate spatial relations among the detected primitives. We then compute class probability from the joint evidence of its class-specific relational compositions.
  Across CUB-DG and the DomainBed benchmark suite,PARSE improves accuracy by over 4.5 percentage points on CUB-DG and remains competitive with existing DG methods on DomainBed.

</details>


### [66] [Fusion in Your Way: Aligning Image Fusion with Heterogeneous Demands via Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06049)
*Weijian Su,Songqian Zhang,Yuqi Han,Jian Zhuang,Yongdong Huang,Qiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DPOFusion introduces a two-stage diffusion-based IVIF framework that uses PALDM and PCLDM with IDPO to achieve task-guided, preference-controlled infrared-visible fusion adaptable to human and machine vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for adaptive IVIF that flexibly accommodates heterogeneous human and machine preferences, which current methods fail to satisfy.

Method: Propose DPOFusion, integrating property-aligned latent diffusion model (PALDM) and preference-controllable latent diffusion model (PCLDM). PALDM uses latent fusion priors and a joint conditional loss to produce diverse fusion candidates; PCLDM is fine-tuned with instance direct preference optimization (IDPO) to enable direct control of final fusion results. The framework relies on direct preference optimization (DPO) in a latent diffusion setting.

Result: Empirical results show precise preference alignment among humans, vision-language models, and task-driven networks, and establish a new benchmark for adaptive fusion quality and task-oriented transferability.

Conclusion: DPOFusion enables task-guided, preference-adaptive IVIF for both human and machine vision, with strong preference alignment and transferability.

Abstract: As a key technique in multi-modal processing, infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) plays a crucial role in integrating complementary spectral information for visual enhancement and downstream vision tasks. Despite remarkable progress, existing methods struggle to flexibly accommodate heterogeneous demands. Achieving adaptive fusion that aligns with various preferences from both human and machine vision remains an open and challenging problem. To address this challenge, we propose DPOFusion, a direct preference optimization (DPO) framework integrating the property-aligned latent diffusion model (PALDM) and the preference-controllable latent diffusion model (PCLDM), enabling task-guided, preference-adaptive IVIF for both human and machine vision. The PALDM leverages a latent fusion prior and a joint conditional loss to generate diverse candidate fusion results with various properties. PCLDM is subsequently fine-tuned via instance direct preference optimization (IDPO), enabling direct control of the final fusion results with heterogeneous preference signals. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework not only attains precise preference alignment among humans, vision-language models, and task-driven networks, but also sets a new benchmark for adaptive fusion quality and task-oriented transferability.

</details>


### [67] [RealCam: Real-Time Novel-View Video Generation with Interactive Camera Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06051)
*Youcan Xu,Jiaxin Shi,Zhen Wang,Wensong Song,Feifei Shao,Chen Liang,Jun Xiao,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RealCam delivers autoregressive, real-time camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation by distilling a cross-frame in-context learning teacher into a causal student and mitigating loop artifacts via LoopAug, achieving real-time, length-agnostic, high-fidelity V2V with strong temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address latency and scalability limitations of non-causal, full-sequence V2V methods that rely on bidirectional attention with quadratic complexity and are ill-suited for real-time streaming or variable-length inputs; also tackle loop inconsistency in closed-loop trajectories.

Method: 1) Train a high-fidelity teacher using Cross-frame In-context Learning (interleaving source and target frames into synchronized contextual pairs to enable length-agnostic and causal adaptation). 2) Distill into a few-step causal student via Self-Forcing with Distribution Matching Distillation for efficient streaming. 3) Introduce Loop-Closed Data Augmentation (LoopAug) to generate globally consistent loop sequences from multiview datasets to reduce loop artifacts.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency; orders-of-magnitude faster inference than existing methods; supports truly interactive camera control in real-time.

Conclusion: RealCam enables interactive camera-controlled V2V generation with real-time performance, addressing latency, scalability, and loop-consistency limitations of prior methods; suitable for interactive filmmaking and live broadcasting.

Abstract: Camera-controlled video-to-video (V2V) generation enables dynamic viewpoint synthesis from monocular footage, holding immense potential for interactive filmmaking and live broadcasting. However, existing implicit synthesis methods fundamentally rely on non-causal, full-sequence processing and rigid prefix-style temporal concatenation. This architectural paradigm mandates bidirectional attention, resulting in prohibitive computational latency, quadratic complexity scaling, and inherent incompatibility with real-time streaming or variable-length inputs. To overcome these limitations, we introduce \texttt{RealCam}, a novel autoregressive framework for interactive, real-time camera-controlled V2V generation. We first design a high-fidelity teacher model grounded in a \textbf{Cross-frame In-context Learning} paradigm. By interleaving source and target frames into synchronized contextual pairs, our design inherently enables length-agnostic generalization and naturally facilitates causal adaptation, breaking the rigid prefix bottleneck. We then distill this teacher into a few-step causal student via Self-Forcing with Distribution Matching Distillation, enabling efficient, on-the-fly streaming synthesis. Furthermore, to mitigate severe loop inconsistency in closed-loop trajectories, we propose \textbf{Loop-Closed Data Augmentation (LoopAug)}, a novel paradigm that synthesizes globally consistent loop sequences from existing multiview datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \texttt{RealCam} achieves state-of-the-art visual fidelity and temporal consistency while enabling truly interactive camera control with orders-of-magnitude faster inference than existing paradigms. Our project page is at https://xyc-fly.github.io/RealCam/.

</details>


### [68] [PersonaGesture: Single-Reference Co-Speech Gesture Personalization for Unseen Speakers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06064)
*Xiangyue Zhang,Yiyi Cai,Kunhang Li,Kaixing Yang,You Zhou,Zhengqing Li,Xuangeng Chu,Jiaxu Zhang,Haiyang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based framework for unseen-speaker co-speech gesture personalization using Adaptive Style Infusion and Implicit Distribution Rectification to separate temporal identity from residual corrections, achieving better personalization without per-speaker finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To synthesize gestures that follow a target utterance while preserving speaker-specific pose choices, using a single reference clip for unseen speakers. The challenge is that the reference blends stable speaker habits with utterance-specific trajectories, making per-speaker optimization undesirable.

Method: Two key components: Adaptive Style Infusion (ASI) with a Style Perceiver that encodes the variable-length reference into compact speaker-memory tokens and injects them into denoising via zero-initialized residual cross-attention to bias motion formation without altering the pretrained speech-to-motion prior. Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR) applies a length-aware diagonal affine map in latent space to correct residual channel-wise moments estimated from the same reference. Evaluated on BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS with quantitative metrics, reference-identity controls, same-audio diagnostics, qualitative comparisons, and human preference studies.

Result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations indicate improved unseen-speaker personalization over baselines such as collapsed style codes, full-reference attention, and one-clip finetuning, across BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS datasets.

Conclusion: Separating denoising-time speaker memory from post-generation moment correction improves unseen-speaker gesture personalization without per-speaker optimization; the approach achieves better retention of speaker identity while adapting to utterances. Project page: https://xiangyue-zhang.github.io/PersonaGesture.

Abstract: We propose PersonaGesture, a diffusion-based pipeline for single-reference co-speech gesture personalization of unseen speakers. Given target speech and one motion clip from a new speaker, the model must synthesize gestures that follow the new utterance while retaining speaker-specific pose choices, without per-speaker optimization. This setting is useful for avatars and virtual agents, but it is hard because the reference mixes stable speaker habits with utterance-specific trajectories. PersonaGesture consists of two key components, Adaptive Style Infusion (ASI) and Implicit Distribution Rectification (IDR), to separate temporal identity evidence from residual statistic correction. A Style Perceiver first encodes the variable-length reference into compact speaker-memory tokens. ASI injects these tokens into denoising through zero-initialized residual cross-attention, enabling style evidence to affect motion formation without replacing the pretrained speech-to-motion prior. Building on this, IDR applies a length-aware diagonal affine map in latent space to correct residual channel-wise moments estimated from the same reference. Across BEAT2 and ZeroEGGS, we evaluate quantitative metrics, reference-identity controls, same-audio diagnostics, qualitative comparisons, and human preference. Experiments show that separating denoising-time speaker memory from conservative post-generation moment correction improves unseen-speaker personalization over collapsed style codes, full-reference attention, and one-clip finetuning. Project: https://xiangyue-zhang.github.io/PersonaGesture.

</details>


### [69] [Arena as Offline Reward: Efficient Fine-Grained Preference Optimization for Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06070)
*Zhikai Li,Yue Zhao,Edward Zhongwei Zhang,Xuewen Liu,Jing Zhang,Qingyi Gu,Zhen Dong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ArenaPO introduces offline, fine-grained rewards for text-to-image diffusion via Arena-based Gaussian capability modeling and latent-variable inference from pairwise preferences, achieving efficiency and improved alignment over DPO/RLHF baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPO uses coarse binary feedback, limiting optimization granularity and performance; there is a need for efficient, fine-grained rewards without explicit reward models.

Method: Build Arena to model each model's capability as a Gaussian; infer from annotated pairwise preferences; treat outputs as samples; for a pair, estimate absolute quality gap with truncated-normal latent-variable inference; use offline Arena-derived feedback for training without a reward model.

Result: ArenaPO trained on Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPD v3 consistently outperforms existing baselines.

Conclusion: Combines rich RLHF-style rewards with DPO efficiency by offline, fine-grained feedback; avoids reward modeling overhead while improving alignment on benchmark datasets.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) effectively promotes preference alignment of text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models. To improve computational efficiency, direct preference optimization (DPO), which avoids explicit reward modeling, has been widely studied. However, its reliance on binary feedback limits it to coarse-grained modeling on chosen-rejected pairs, resulting in suboptimal optimization. In this paper, we propose ArenaPO, which leverages Arena scores as offline rewards to provide refined feedback, thus achieving efficient and fine-grained optimization without a reward model. This enables ArenaPO to benefit from both the rich rewards of traditional RLHF and the efficiency of DPO. Specifically, we first construct a model Arena in which each model's capability is represented as a Gaussian distribution, and infer these capabilities by traversing the annotated pairwise preferences. Each output image is treated as a sample from the corresponding capability distribution. Then, for a image pair, conditioned on the two capability distributions and the observed pairwise preference, the absolute quality gap is estimated using latent-variable inference based on truncated normal distribution, which serves as fine-grained feedback during training. It does not require a reward model and can be computed offline, thus introducing no additional training overhead. We conduct ArenaPO training on Pick-a-Pic v2 and HPD v3 datasets, showing that ArenaPO consistently outperforms existing baselines.

</details>


### [70] [MSD-Score: Multi-Scale Distributional Scoring for Reference-Free Image Caption Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06080)
*Shichao Kan,Xuyang Zhang,Haojie Zhang,Zhe Zhu,Yigang Cen,Yixiong Liang,Lianlei Shan,Linna Zhang,Zhe Qu,Jiazhi Xia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSD-Score is a reference-free image-caption metric using von Mises-Fisher mixtures on the unit sphere to model image patches and text tokens, applying multi-scale distributional scoring with a weighted bi-directional KL divergence, achieving state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments among reference-free metrics and offering decomposable diagnostics of grounding errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of global embedding similarity in reference-free caption evaluation, particularly for fine-grained mismatches, hallucinations, and grounding errors.

Method: Represent image patches and text tokens as von Mises-Fisher mixtures on the unit hypersphere. Treat image-text matching as a multi-scale distributional problem. Compute semantic discrepancies via a weighted bi-directional KL divergence between the two mixture distributions and combine with global similarity in a multi-scale framework. Supports single- and multi-candidate evaluations and provides probabilistic, decomposable diagnostics.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments among reference-free metrics. Provides transparent diagnostics of local grounding errors, offering a complementary signal to holistic similarity metrics and judge-based evaluators.

Conclusion: MSD-Score advances reference-free caption evaluation by delivering fine-grained, probabilistic, and interpretable scoring, with decomposable diagnostics, applicable to single- or multi-candidate caption evaluation.

Abstract: Evaluating image captions without references remains challenging because global embedding similarity often misses fine-grained mismatches such as hallucinated objects, missing attributes, or incorrect relations. We propose MSD-Score, a reference-free metric that models image patch and text token embeddings as von Mises-Fisher mixtures on the unit hypersphere. Instead of treating each modality as a single point, MSD-Score formulates image-text matching as a multi-scale distributional scoring problem. Semantic discrepancies are quantified via a weighted bi-directional KL divergence and combined with global similarity in a multi-scale framework for both single- and multi-candidate evaluations. Extensive experiments show that MSD-Score achieves state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments among reference-free metrics. Beyond accuracy, its probabilistic formulation yields transparent and decomposable diagnostics of local grounding errors, providing a deterministic complementary signal to holistic similarity metrics and judge-based evaluators.

</details>


### [71] [Revisiting Uncertainty: On Evidential Learning for Partially Relevant Video Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06083)
*Jun Li,Peifeng Lai,Xuhang Lou,Jinpeng Wang,Yuting Wang,Ke Chen,Yaowei Wang,Shu-Tao Xia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Holmes is a hierarchical evidential learning framework for partially relevant video retrieval that explicitly models uncertainty and aggregates multi-granular cross-modal evidence to improve matching under sparse supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partial queries over rich, untrimmed videos cause semantic uncertainty and ambiguity; sparse temporal supervision yields limited evidence for reliable retrieval.

Method: Three-fold principle: (1) inter-video evidential modeling using a Dirichlet distribution to interpret similarity as evidential support; (2) fine-grained query identification guiding query-adaptive calibrated learning; (3) intra-video soft query-clip alignment via flexible optimal transport with an adaptive dustbin to accumulate dense evidence and suppress spurious local responses.

Result: Empirical results show Holmes achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware hierarchical evidential learning with multi-granular cross-modal evidence and adaptive transport-based alignment improves partially relevant video retrieval under sparse supervision.

Abstract: Partially relevant video retrieval aims to retrieve untrimmed videos using text queries that describe only partial content. However, the inherent asymmetry between brief queries and rich video content inevitably introduces uncertainty into the retrieval process. In this setting, vague queries often induce semantic ambiguity across videos, a challenge that is further exacerbated by the sparse temporal supervision within videos, which fails to provide sufficient matching evidence. To address this, we propose Holmes, a hierarchical evidential learning framework that aggregates multi-granular cross-modal evidence to quantify and model uncertainty explicitly. At the inter-video level, similarity scores are interpreted as evidential support and modeled via a Dirichlet distribution. Based on the proposed three-fold principle, we perform fine-grained query identification, which then guides query-adaptive calibrated learning. At the intra-video level, to accumulate denser evidence, we formulate a soft query-clip alignment via flexible optimal transport with an adaptive dustbin, which alleviates sparse temporal supervision while suppressing spurious local responses. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Holmes outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is released at https://github.com/lijun2005/ICML26-Holmes.

</details>


### [72] [LARGO: Low-Rank Hypernetwork for Handling Missing Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06086)
*Niels Vyncke,Pooya Ashtari,Aleksandra Pižurica*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LARGO is a weight-space approach that uses a CP-decomposed hypernetwork to compress all 2^N-1 missing-modality models into a single network, achieving state-of-the-art performance across configurations on BraTS 2018 and ISLES 2022 with Dice gains (BraTS +0.68 pp, ISLES +2.53 pp) and showing potential extension to non-medical modalities (avMNIST).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Missing modalities pose a core challenge in multimodal image analysis. Existing methods primarily operate in feature space and require architecture/hyperparameter tweaks to transfer between datasets. A weight-space, model-level solution can enable unified handling of all modality configurations and improve cross-dataset transferability.

Method: Introduce LARGO, a hypernetwork that models convolutional weights via Canonical Polyadic (CP) tensor decomposition to represent the 2^N-1 dedicated missing-modality models within a single network. This CP-based weight factorization enables joint training and fast adaptation across all modality combinations. Evaluations conducted on BraTS 2018 (4 modalities, 15 scenarios) and ISLES 2022 (3 modalities, 7 scenarios) compare against mmFormer, M3AE, ShaSpec, and SimMLM.

Result: LARGO ranks first in 47 of 52 configurations. It delivers average Dice improvements of +0.68 percentage points on BraTS 2018 and +2.53 percentage points on ISLES 2022 over state-of-the-art baselines. A proof-of-concept avMNIST experiment suggests potential applicability beyond medical imaging to heterogeneous non-medical modalities.

Conclusion: Weight-space modeling via CP-decomposed hypernetworks can effectively address missing-modality scenarios, yielding strong, transferable performance across datasets. The approach shows promise for broader applicability beyond medical imaging as indicated by avMNIST.

Abstract: Addressing missing modalities is an important challenge in multimodal image analysis and often relies on complex architectures that do not transfer easily to different datasets without architectural modifications or hyperparameter tuning. While most existing methods tackle this problem in feature space by engineering representations that are robust to missing inputs, we instead operate in weight space. We propose LARGO, a hypernetwork that compresses the $2^N-1$ dedicated missing-modality models into a single network by modelling the convolutional weights using the Canonical Polyadic (CP) tensor decomposition. Extensive experimental validation on BraTS 2018 (4 modalities, 15 scenarios) and ISLES 2022 (3 modalities, 7 scenarios) shows that our method ranks first in 47 out of 52 configurations, achieving average Dice improvements of +0.68$\%$ and +2.53$\%$ over state-of-the-art baselines (mmFormer, M$^{3}$AE, ShaSpec, SimMLM). A proof-of-concept experiment on avMNIST suggests that LARGO may extend beyond medical imaging to heterogeneous non-medical modalities.

</details>


### [73] [OpenGaFF: Open-Vocabulary Gaussian Feature Field with Codebook Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06088)
*Kunyi Li,Michael Niemeyer,Sen Wang,Stefano Gasperini,Nassir Navab,Federico Tombari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OpenGaFF introduces a Gaussian-based, open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding framework that ties semantic predictions to geometry via a Gaussian Feature Field, uses a structured semantic codebook with codebook-guided attention, and achieves superior 3D semantic coherence and segmentation on 2D/3D open-vocabulary benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding suffers from fragmented, spatially inconsistent semantics across views; a stronger coupling between geometry and semantics and an interpretable semantic primitive library are needed for coherent 3D reasoning.

Method: Utilize 3D Gaussian Splatting with a Gaussian Feature Field that models semantics as a continuous function of geometry and appearance, conditioned on geometric structure. Introduce a structured codebook of semantic primitives and a codebook-guided attention mechanism to match query embeddings with codebook entries for open-vocabulary reasoning.

Result: Extensive experiments on standard 2D and 3D open-vocabulary benchmarks show consistent improvements over prior methods in segmentation quality, 3D semantic consistency, and provide an interpretable codebook.

Conclusion: By enforcing geometry-informed semantic predictions and object-level consistency via a shared semantic codebook and attention mechanism, the approach yields more coherent 3D semantics and actionable insights into learned representations.

Abstract: Understanding open-vocabulary 3D scenes with Gaussian-based representations remains challenging due to fragmented and spatially inconsistent semantic predictions across multi-view observations. In this paper, we present OpenGaFF, a novel framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding built upon 3D Gaussian Splatting. At the core of our method is a Gaussian Feature Field that models semantics as a continuous function of Gaussian geometry and appearance. By explicitly conditioning semantic predictions on geometric structure, this formulation strengthens the coupling between geometry and semantics, leading to improved spatial coherence across similar structures in 3D space. To further enforce object-level semantic consistency, we introduce a structured codebook that serves as a set of shared semantic primitives. Furthermore, a codebook-guided attention mechanism is proposed to retrieve language features via similarity matching between query embeddings and learned codebook entries, enabling robust open-vocabulary reasoning while reducing intra-object feature variance. Extensive experiments on standard 2D and 3D open-vocabulary benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms prior approaches, achieving improved segmentation quality, stronger 3D semantic consistency and a semantically interpretable codebook that provides insight into the learned representation.

</details>


### [74] [Boosting Self-Supervised Tracking with Contextual Prompts and Noise Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06092)
*Yaozong Zheng,Qihua Liang,Bineng Zhong,Shuimu Zeng,Yuanliang Xue,Ning Li,Shuxiang Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised tracking framework named Tracker (\tracker) uses a dual-modal context association with semantic prompts and contextual noise to learn robust tracking representations from unlabeled videos. It follows an easy-to-hard two-stage training: early in training, instance patch tokens (prompts) guide forward/backward tracking branches; later, contextual noise perturbs features to foster robustness, with training-time only augmentation to preserve inference efficiency. Extensive experiments indicate superior performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of effective context modeling in self-supervised trackers and the difficulty of leveraging non-semantic context associations in unlabeled tracking; propose a dual-modal mechanism that combines semantic prompts and contextual noise to learn robust contextual knowledge.

Method: Introduce a dual-modal context association mechanism with two stages: (1) early stage assigns instance patch tokens (prompts) to both forward and backward tracking branches to acquire tracking knowledge; (2) later stage injects contextual noise to perturb features, encouraging robustness in a more complex feature space; training-time only context association to preserve efficient inference.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method's superiority, likely showing improved tracking accuracy/robustness and effective context modeling over baselines with ablations validating the role of prompts and noise scheduling.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-modal contextual association enables learning high-quality tracking representations from unlabeled videos while keeping inference efficient, representing a novel approach for self-supervised tracking.

Abstract: Learning robust contextual knowledge from unlabeled videos is essential for advancing self-supervised tracking. However, conventional self-supervised trackers lack effective context modeling, while existing context association methods based on non-semantic queries struggle to adapt to unlabeled tracking scenarios, making it difficult to learn reliable contextual cues. In this work, we propose a novel self-supervised tracking framework, named \textbf{\tracker}, which introduces a dual-modal context association mechanism that jointly leverages fine-grained semantic prompts and contextual noise to drive the model toward learning robust tracking representations. Adherent to the easy-to-hard learning principle, our contextual association mechanism operates based on two stages. During early training, instance patch tokens (prompts) are assigned to both forward and backward tracking branches to facilitate the acquisition of tracking knowledge. As training progresses, contextual noise is gradually injected into the model to perturb feature, encouraging the tracker to learn robust tracking representations in a more complex feature space. Thus, this novel contextual association mechanism enables our self-supervised model to learn high-quality tracking representations from unlabeled videos, while being applied exclusively during training to preserve efficient inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method.

</details>


### [75] [VISD: Enhancing Video Reasoning via Structured Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06094)
*Hao Lin,Kunyang Lv,Xu Jiang,Jingqi Tian,Zhongjing Du,Jiayu Ding,Qiaoman Zhang,Hongbo Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISD is a structured self-distillation framework for VideoLLMs that combines a video-aware judge with teacher-guided token-level supervision and a direction magnitude decoupling mechanism to align dense supervision with RL, yielding better reasoning fidelity and faster convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle sparse sequence-level rewards and poor credit assignment in long, temporally grounded video reasoning; overcome inefficiencies of RLVR and instability of prior self-distillation when used with reinforcement learning.

Method: Introduce a video-aware judge that decomposes reasoning quality into dimensions such as answer correctness, logical consistency, and spatio-temporal grounding; use a teacher policy for token-level supervision guided by these structured signals; apply a direction magnitude decoupling technique to separate rollout-level advantage directions (from rewards) from token-level update magnitudes (modulated by privileged signals); incorporate curriculum scheduling and EMA-based teacher stabilization to ensure robust optimization over long video sequences.

Result: VISD yields consistent improvements in answer accuracy and spatio-temporal grounding across diverse benchmarks, with nearly 2x faster convergence in optimization steps and enhanced training efficiency compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: Structured self-supervision with diagnostically meaningful privileged information enables fine-grained credit assignment and faithful reasoning in VideoLLMs, improving both performance and sample efficiency.

Abstract: Training VideoLLMs for complex reasoning remains challenging due to sparse sequence level rewards and the lack of fine grained credit assignment over long, temporally grounded reasoning trajectories. While reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) provides reliable supervision, it fails to capture token level contributions, leading to inefficient learning. Conversely, existing self distillation methods offer dense supervision but lack structure and diagnostic specificity, and often interact unstably with reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose VISD, a structured self distillation framework that introduces diagnostically meaningful privileged information for video reasoning. VISD employs a video aware judge model to decompose reasoning quality into multiple dimensions, including answer correctness, logical consistency, and spatio-temporal grounding, and uses this structured feedback to guide a teacher policy for token level supervision. To stably integrate dense supervision with RL, we introduce a direction magnitude decoupling mechanism, where rollout level advantages computed from rewards determine update direction, while structured privileged signals modulate token level update magnitudes. This design enables semantically aligned and fine grained credit assignment, improving both reasoning faithfulness and training efficiency. Additionally, VISD incorporates curriculum scheduling and EMA based teacher stabilization to support robust optimization over long video sequences. Experiments on diverse benchmarks show that VISD consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving answer accuracy and spatio temporal grounding quality. Notably, VISD reaches these gains with nearly 2x faster convergence in optimization steps, highlighting the effectiveness of structured self supervision in improving both performance and sample efficiency for VideoLLMs.

</details>


### [76] [Metonymy in vision models undermines attention-based interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06095)
*Ananthu Aniraj,Cassio F. Dantas,Dino Ienco,Massimiliano Mancini,Diego Marcos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision transformers exhibit intra-object leakage; part-based explanations are not faithful due to spreading information across the whole object; a two-stage disentangled feature extraction approach can prevent leakage and improve attribute-driven part discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate the locality assumption of part-based reasoning by quantifying intra-object leakage in pretrained vision transformers using part-level attribute annotations and assess its impact on interpretability.

Method: Quantitative analysis of leakage using part-based attribute annotations on pretrained vision transformers; comprehensive experiments; introduce a two-stage approach to enforce disentangled features that prevent leakage; evaluate on tasks requiring attribute-driven part discovery.

Result: Vision transformers show strong intra-object leakage, undermining locality and the interpretability of attention-based part explanations; the proposed two-stage method prevents leakage by design and enhances attribute-driven part discovery.

Conclusion: Intra-object leakage challenges the interpretability of part-based models; two-stage disentangled feature extraction provides a viable mitigation with practical benefits for part-based reasoning.

Abstract: Part-based reasoning is a classical strategy to make a computer vision model directly focus on the object parts that are relevant to the downstream task.
