Code is available at https://github.com/QuantitativeImagingLaboratory/GeoStack.

</details>


### [103] [3D MRI Image Pretraining via Controllable 2D Slice Navigation Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06487)
*Yu Wang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-supervised MRI pretraining via action-conditioned rendering of 3D volumes into controllable 2D sequences; tokenizer + latent dynamics model to predict feature evolution; outperforms static baselines and suggests a complementary pretraining signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard self-supervised MRI pretraining treats scans as static volumes; propose intrinsic supervision from dynamic navigation of 3D volumes into 2D sequences to capture spatial/pose cues and improve representations.

Method: Render slices at continuous positions, orientations, and scales to produce dense video-like sequences; use an action-conditioned objective where a tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts the evolution of latent features; compare against static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions.

Result: Indicates that controllable MRI slice navigation provides a useful complementary pretraining interface, with improvements observed across representative anatomical and spatial downstream tasks when compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Controllable MRI slice navigation offers a new self-supervised pretraining signal distinct from patch reconstruction, enabling learning of anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections.

Abstract: Self-supervised pretraining has become the mainstream approach for learning MRI representations from unlabeled scans. However, most existing objectives still treat each scan primarily as static aggregations of slices, patches or volumes. We ask whether there exists an intrinsic form of self-supervision signal that is different from reconstructing the masked patches, through transforming the 3D volumes into controllable 2D rendered sequences: by rendering slices at continuous positions, orientations, and scales, a 3D volume can be converted into dense video-action sequences whose controls are the action trajectories. We study this formulation with an action-conditioned pretraining objective, where a tokenizer encodes slice observations and a latent dynamics model predicts the evolution of latent features. Across representative anatomical and spatial downstream tasks, the proposed pretraining is evaluated against standard static-volume baselines, tokenizer-only pretraining, and dynamics variants without aligned actions. These results suggest that controllable MRI slice navigation provides a useful complementary pretraining interface for learning anatomical and spatial representations from large unlabeled MRI collections.

</details>


### [104] [MARBLE: Multi-Aspect Reward Balance for Diffusion RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06507)
*Canyu Zhao,Hao Chen,Yunze Tong,Yu Qiao,Jiacheng Li,Chunhua Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MARBLE introduces a gradient-space, multi-reward optimization for RL fine-tuning of diffusion models. It maintains independent advantage estimators per reward, solves a quadratic program to combine gradients, and uses an amortized optimization to reduce cost; achieves simultaneous improvements across five rewards with near-baseline training speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-dimensional image evaluation for diffusion-model alignment requires optimizing multiple rewards simultaneously. Naive weighted-sum aggregation leads to sample-level mismatch and diluted supervision, hindering unified multi-reward models.

Method: Maintain independent advantage estimators for each reward, compute per-reward policy gradients, and harmonize them via a Quadratic Programming problem to produce a single update direction. An amortized version exploits the affine structure of the DiffusionNFT loss to reduce backward passes from K+1 to ~1, and applies EMA smoothing on balancing coefficients to stabilize updates.

Result: On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all reward dimensions simultaneously; the worst-aligned reward's gradient cosine becomes consistently positive in 80% of mini-batches (vs negative under naive weighting); training runs at 0.97x the baseline speed.

Conclusion: Gradient-space optimization via MARBLE enables a unified, multi-reward RL fine-tuning without manually tuned reward weights, yielding simultaneous improvements across multiple reward criteria with stable, near-baseline efficiency.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning has become the dominant approach for aligning diffusion models with human preferences. However, assessing images is intrinsically a multi-dimensional task, and multiple evaluation criteria need to be optimized simultaneously. Existing practice deal with multiple rewards by training one specialist model per reward, optimizing a weighted-sum reward $R(x)=\sum_k w_k R_k(x)$, or sequentially fine-tuning with a hand-crafted stage schedule. These approaches either fail to produce a unified model that can be jointly trained on all rewards or necessitates heavy manually tuned sequential training. We find that the failure stems from using a naive weighted-sum reward aggregation. This approach suffers from a sample-level mismatch because most rollouts are specialist samples, highly informative for certain reward dimensions but irrelevant for others; consequently, weighted summation dilutes their supervision. To address this issue, we propose MARBLE (Multi-Aspect Reward BaLancE), a gradient-space optimization framework that maintains independent advantage estimators for each reward, computes per-reward policy gradients, and harmonizes them into a single update direction without manually-tuned reward weighting, by solving a Quadratic Programming problem. We further propose an amortized formulation that exploits the affine structure of the loss used in DiffusionNFT, to reduce the per-step cost from K+1 backward passes to near single-reward baseline cost, together with EMA smoothing on the balancing coefficients to stabilize updates against transient single-batch fluctuations. On SD3.5 Medium with five rewards, MARBLE improves all five reward dimensions simultaneously, turns the worst-aligned reward's gradient cosine from negative under weighted summation in 80% of mini-batches to consistently positive, and runs at 0.97X the training speed of baseline training.

</details>


### [105] [FreeSpec: Training-Free Long Video Generation via Singular-Spectrum Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06509)
*Fangda Chen,Shanshan Zhao,Longrong Yang,Chuanfu Xu,Zhigang Luo,Long Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FreeSpec presents a training-free spectral reconstruction approach for long-video generation in diffusion models. It uses singular value decomposition to separate global (low-rank) and local (high-rank) features, enabling spectrum-level fusion that preserves long-range consistency and temporal dynamics without rigid feature partitioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-video diffusion models suffer from content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothed dynamics when extended to longer sequences. Existing global/local decomposition methods rely on predefined criteria that fail when appearance and motion are tightly coupled (e.g., camera motion, sequential motion). A spectral perspective may reveal more flexible fusion strategies.

Method: Analyze and leverage enlarged self-attention windows that induce spectral concentration. Decompose features with SVD into low-rank (global) and high-rank (local) components. Use the global branch as low-rank spectral guidance and the local branch as a high-rank reconstruction basis, enabling spectrum-level fusion without rigid decomposition rules. Training-free framework; demonstrates on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video; includes a project demo.

Result: FreeSpec improves long-video generation with notable gains in temporal dynamics while preserving visual quality and temporal consistency, according to experiments on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video.

Conclusion: Spectrum-level fusion via FreeSpec provides a robust, training-free pathway to maintain long-range temporal coherence and rich spatial-temporal details in long-video diffusion, avoiding the drawbacks of fixed appearance/dynamics partitioning.

Abstract: Video diffusion models perform well in short-video synthesis, but their training-free extension to long videos often suffers from content drift, temporal inconsistency, and over-smoothed dynamics. Existing methods improve temporal consistency by combining a global branch with a local branch, but they often further decompose appearance consistency and temporal dynamics within each branch using predefined criteria. This assignment is unreliable when appearance and action progression are tightly coupled, such as in camera motion and sequential motion. We analyze the video temporal extension issue from a singular-spectrum perspective and show that enlarged self-attention windows induce spectral concentration: spectral energy becomes dominated by a few low-rank singular directions, preserving coarse structure but suppressing high-rank spatial details and motion-rich temporal variations. To mitigate this problem, we propose FreeSpec, a training-free spectral reconstruction framework for long-video generation. FreeSpec decomposes global and local features with singular value decomposition, and uses the global branch as low-rank spectral guidance and the local branch as a high-rank reconstruction basis. This spectrum-level fusion avoids the rigid feature partitioning of previous decomposition rules, preserving long-range consistency while better retaining spatial details and temporal dynamics. Experiments on Wan2.1 and LTX-Video demonstrate that FreeSpec improves long-video generation, especially for temporal dynamics, while maintaining strong visual quality and temporal consistency. Project demo: https://fdchen24.github.io/FreeSpec-Website/.

</details>


### [106] [DCR: Counterfactual Attractor Guidance for Rare Compositional Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06512)
*Taewon Kang,Matthias Zwicker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Default Completion Repulsion (DCR), a training-free method to mitigate default completion bias in diffusion models, improving rare compositional prompts without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models tend to default to common configurations when prompted with rare but plausible compositions; need to model and suppress this bias.

Method: Construct a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare factor while preserving surrounding semantics; define counterfactual drift between target and attractor; apply projection-based repulsion within the denoising guidance to remove components along the drift; operates within standard sampling without retraining.

Result: Improved compositional fidelity on rare prompts while preserving visual quality; reveals intrinsic biases and provides a new controllable generation perspective.

Conclusion: DCR provides a training-free mechanism to counter default completion, offering a new lens on controllable generation beyond explicit constraints.

Abstract: Diffusion models generate realistic visual content, yet often fail to produce rare but plausible compositions. When prompted with combinations that are valid but underrepresented in training data, such as a snowy beach or a rainbow at night, the generation process frequently collapses toward more common alternatives. We identify this failure mode as default completion bias, where denoising trajectories are implicitly attracted toward high-frequency semantic configurations. Existing guidance mechanisms do not explicitly model this competing tendency and therefore struggle to prevent such collapse. We introduce Default Completion Repulsion (DCR), a training-free framework that explicitly models and suppresses default completion behavior. DCR constructs a counterfactual attractor by relaxing the rare compositional factor while preserving surrounding semantics, inducing an alternative denoising trajectory reflecting the model's preferred completion. We define the discrepancy between target and attractor trajectories as a counterfactual drift, and propose a projection-based repulsion mechanism that removes guidance components aligned with this drift direction. This suppresses undesired frequent completions while preserving other semantic components. DCR operates entirely within the standard diffusion sampling process without retraining or architectural modification. Experiments on rare compositional prompts show that DCR improves compositional fidelity while maintaining visual quality. Our analysis further shows that the framework exposes and counteracts intrinsic model biases, offering a new perspective on controllable generation beyond explicit constraint enforcement.

</details>


### [107] [Sparkle: Realizing Lively Instruction-Guided Video Background Replacement via Decoupled Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06535)
*Ziyun Zeng,Yiqi Lin,Guoqiang Liang,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sparkle introduces a scalable, decoupled foreground/background guidance pipeline for background replacement, yielding a ~140k video-pair dataset and Sparkle-Bench benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art results on OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench; open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address poor background realism and interaction in background replacement data, caused by lack of precise background guidance and high-quality data; current datasets (OpenVE-3M, OpenVE-Bench) lead to static, unnatural backgrounds and weak models.

Method: A two-stage decoupled pipeline generating foreground and background guidance with strict quality filtering; constructs Sparkle dataset (~140k video pairs across five themes) and Sparkle-Bench benchmark.

Result: Models trained on Sparkle show substantially better performance than all baselines on both OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench.

Conclusion: Sparkle closes data-quality gap for background replacement, enabling more realistic temporally-consistent editing; release is open-source.

Abstract: In recent years, open-source efforts like Senorita-2M have propelled video editing toward natural language instruction. However, current publicly available datasets predominantly focus on local editing or style transfer, which largely preserve the original scene structure and are easier to scale. In contrast, Background Replacement, a task central to creative applications such as film production and advertising, requires synthesizing entirely new, temporally consistent scenes while maintaining accurate foreground-background interactions, making large-scale data generation significantly more challenging. Consequently, this complex task remains largely underexplored due to a scarcity of high-quality training data. This gap is evident in poorly performing state-of-the-art models, e.g., Kiwi-Edit, because the primary open-source dataset that contains this task, i.e., OpenVE-3M, frequently produces static, unnatural backgrounds. In this paper, we trace this quality degradation to a lack of precise background guidance during data synthesis. Accordingly, we design a scalable pipeline that generates foreground and background guidance in a decoupled manner with strict quality filtering. Building on this pipeline, we introduce Sparkle, a dataset of ~140K video pairs spanning five common background-change themes, alongside Sparkle-Bench, the largest evaluation benchmark tailored for background replacement to date. Experiments demonstrate that our dataset and the model trained on it achieve substantially better performance than all existing baselines on both OpenVE-Bench and Sparkle-Bench. Our proposed dataset, benchmark, and model are fully open-sourced at https://showlab.github.io/Sparkle/.

</details>


### [108] [MedHorizon: Towards Long-context Medical Video Understanding in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06537)
*Bodong Du,Bowen Liu,Yang Yu,Xinpeng Ding,Zhiheng Wu,Shuning Wang,Shuo Nie,Naiming Liu,Qifeng Chen,Yangqiu Song,Xiaomeng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedHorizon is a long-context medical video benchmark that tests retrieval-before-reasoning in full-length clinical procedures with sparse evidence. It exposes a large gap: current MLLMs achieve only 41.1% accuracy and struggle with sparse evidence, re-emphasizing retrieval and procedural reasoning as bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to evaluate true clinical video understanding beyond short clips and pre-localized evidence. Existing benchmarks assume localized evidence or segmented inputs, neglecting the retrieval step essential for full-procedure understanding in noisy, redundant streams.

Method: Compile 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures, create 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questions that require sparse-evidence retrieval and multi-hop reasoning across procedures. Evaluate representative general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs on this retrieval-before-reasoning task.

Result: Best model achieves 41.1% accuracy. Increasing frame count does not reliably improve performance. Primary bottlenecks are evidence retrieval and clinical interpretation, rooted in weak procedural reasoning and attention drift due to redundancy; generic sampling partially balances local detail with global coverage.

Conclusion: MedHorizon provides a rigorous, real-world testbed for MLLMs to retrieve sparse evidence and reason over complete clinical workflows, highlighting substantial gaps in current models and guiding future research toward improved retrieval, long-context modeling, and robust procedural reasoning.

Abstract: Medical multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced image understanding and short-video analysis, but real clinical review often requires full-procedure video understanding. Unlike general long videos, medical procedures contain highly redundant anatomical views, while decisive evidence is temporally sparse, spatially subtle, and context dependent. Existing benchmarks often assume this evidence has already been localized through images, short clips, or pre-segmented videos, leaving the retrieval-before-reasoning problem under-tested. We introduce MedHorizon, an in-the-wild benchmark for long-context medical video understanding. MedHorizon preserves 759 hours of full-length clinical procedures and provides 1,253 evidence-grounded multiple-choice questionsthat jointly evaluate sparse evidence understanding and multi-hop clinical reasoning. Its evidence is extremely sparse, with only 0.166% evidence frames on average, requiring models to search noisy procedural streams before interpreting and aggregating findings. We evaluate representative general-domain, medical-domain, and long-video MLLMs. The best model reaches only 41.1% accuracy, showing that current systems remain far from robust full-procedure understanding. Further analysis yields four key findings: performance does not scale reliably with more frames, evidence retrieval and clinical interpretation remain primary bottlenecks; these bottlenecks are rooted in weak procedural reasoning and attention drift under redundancy, and generic sampling methods only partially balances local detail with global coverage. MedHorizon provides a rigorous testbed for MLLMs that retrieve sparse evidence and reason over complete clinical workflows.

</details>


### [109] [Solving Minimal Problems Without Matrix Inversion Using FFT-Based Interpolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06572)
*Haidong Wu,Snehal Bhayani,Janne Heikkilä*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A sampling-based, matrix-inversion-free solver for minimal problems in camera geometry using sparse hidden-variable resultants and FFT-based determinant reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome computational bottlenecks of Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers (matrix inversion, symbolic expansions) and improve robustness and speed for small-scale minimal problems.

Method: Construct solvers from sparse hidden-variable resultants; reconstruct the determinant polynomial in the hidden variable via inverse FFT interpolation from sampled evaluations; solve the polynomial to recover the hidden variable; recover remaining unknowns via rank-1 deficient submatrices and Cramer's rule; employ gcd-based criterion for robust submatrix identification under noise.

Result: Experiments on diverse minimal problems show strong numerical stability and competitive runtime, especially for small-scale problems; solver provides a practical alternative to traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers.

Conclusion: The approach delivers a stable, efficient, and matrix-inversion-free solver framework for camera-geometry minimal problems, offering a viable alternative to standard algebraic solvers and potentially enabling faster online estimation.

Abstract: Estimating camera geometry typically involves solving minimal problems formulated as systems of multivariate polynomial equations, which often pose computational challenges when using existing Gröbner-basis or resultant-based methods due to matrix inversion needed in the online solver. Here we propose a sampling-based, matrix inversion-free method that constructs the solvers using sparse hidden-variable resultants. The determinant polynomial in the hidden variable is efficiently reconstructed via inverse fast Fourier transform interpolation from sampled evaluations, avoiding symbolic expansion. Solving this polynomial yields the hidden variable, and the remaining unknowns are recovered by identifying rank-1 deficient submatrices and applying Cramer's rule. A greatest common divisor-based criterion ensures robust submatrix identification under noise. Experiments on diverse minimal problems demonstrate that the proposed solver achieves strong numerical stability and competitive runtime, particularly for small-scale problems, providing a practical alternative to traditional Gröbner-basis and resultant-based solvers.

</details>


### [110] [DINORANKCLIP: DINOv3 Distillation and Injection for Vision-Language Pretraining with High-Order Ranking Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06592)
*Shuyang Jiang,Nan Yu,Yiming Zhang,Zenghui Ding,Zhenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DINORANKCLIP introduces a dual-branch student guided by a frozen DINOv3 teacher and a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking to jointly fix CLIP's symmetry and global-pooling bottlenecks, achieving strong fine-grained and OOD gains with an optimal order of 3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP's symmetric InfoNCE loss hides relative ordering among unmatched in-batch samples and global pooling collapses visual representations, reducing sensitivity to local structure. Prior RANKCLIP offers some ordering but is limited to first-order and leaves the global-structure weakness unaddressed. This work aims to tackle both deficiencies jointly with a high-order ranking and teacher-guided representation. 

Method: 1) Inject a frozen DINOv3 teacher into the contrastive trunk via a dual-branch lightweight student and a multi-scale fusion module composed of channel-spatial attention, a self-attention refiner, and a conflict-aware gate to preserve cross-modal alignment up to first order. 2) Propose a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model in which per-position utility includes attention-parameterised pairwise and tuple-wise transition terms; CLIP and RANKCLIP are special cases of zero- and first-order, respectively, with an optimal order R* = 3. 3) Extensive empirical study: order sweep, Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, four-node Modality-Gap analysis, six-variant Fusion ablation; trained on Conceptual Captions 3M. 

Result: DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute, with the largest relative gains on fine-grained and out-of-distribution evaluations that stress local structural reasoning.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses both the symmetric loss and global-pooling weaknesses of CLIP by combining teacher-guided representation learning with high-order cross-modal ranking, yielding robust improvements across diverse evaluations and establishing R*=3 as the optimal ordering in the tested settings.

Abstract: Contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) suffers from two structural weaknesses: the symmetric InfoNCE loss discards the relative ordering among unmatched in-batch pairs, and global pooling collapses the visual representation into a semantic bottleneck that is poorly sensitive to fine-grained local structure. RANKCLIP partially addresses the first issue with a list-wise Plackett-Luce ranking-consistency loss, but its model is strictly first-order and inherits the second weakness untouched. We propose DINORANKCLIP, a pretraining framework that addresses both jointly. Our principal contribution is injecting a frozen DINOv3 teacher into the contrastive trunk through a dual-branch lightweight student and a multi-scale fusion module with channel-spatial attention, a self-attention refiner, and a conflict-aware gate that preserves the cross-modal alignment up to first order. Complementarily, we introduce a high-order Plackett-Luce ranking model in which the per-position utility is augmented with attention-parameterised pairwise and tuple-wise transition terms; the family contains CLIP and RANKCLIP as nested zero-order and first-order special cases, and the optimal order on every benchmark is $R^*=3$. The full empirical study -- order sweep, Fine-grained Probe on five datasets, four-node Modality-Gap analysis, six-variant Fusion ablation -- fits in 72 hours on a single eight-GPU H100 node and trains entirely on Conceptual Captions 3M. DINORANKCLIP consistently outperforms CLIP, CyCLIP, ALIP, and RANKCLIP under matched compute, with the largest relative gains on the fine-grained and out-of-distribution evaluations that most directly stress local structural reasoning.

</details>


### [111] [DPM++: Dynamic Masked Metric Learning for Occluded Person Re-identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06637)
*Lei Tan,Yingshi Luan,Pincong Zou,Pingyang Dai,Liujuan Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DPM++ is a dynamic masked metric learning framework for occluded person re-identification that uses input-adaptive masking, CLIP-based supervision, and saliency-guided patch transfer to train robust, visibility-consistent matchers. It achieves state-of-the-art results on both occluded and holistic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion in re-ID breaks the alignment between occluded observations and holistic identity representations, corrupts discriminative cues, and degrades global metric learning. Existing solutions rely on extra pre-trained models or synthetic augmentations and lack a unified framework for robust visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion patterns.

Method: DPM++ introduces an input-adaptive masked metric that selects reliable identity subspaces per occluded instance within a classifier-prototype space. It employs a CLIP-based two-stage supervision: ID-level semantic priors learned from a text branch are transferred into the classifier-prototype space to enable dynamic masked matching. A saliency-guided patch transfer strategy synthesizes controllable, photo-realistic occluded samples using real scene priors, providing realistic partial observations. Additional occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization enhance training stability and effectiveness.

Result: Experiments on occluded and holistic person re-ID benchmarks show that DPM++ consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in both holistic and occlusion scenarios.

Conclusion: DPM++ offers a unified, robust approach to occluded person re-ID by combining dynamic masked metric learning, cross-modal CLIP supervision, and realistic occlusion augmentation, achieving superior performance and reliability across diverse occlusion conditions.

Abstract: Although person re-identification has made impressive progress, occlusion caused by obstacles remains an unsettled issue in real applications. The difficulty lies in the mismatch between incomplete occluded samples and holistic identity representations. Severe occlusion removes discriminative body cues and introduces interference from background clutter and occluders, making global metric learning unreliable. Existing methods mainly rely on extra pre-trained models to estimate visible parts for alignment or construct occluded samples via data augmentation, but still lack a unified framework that learns robust visibility-consistent matching under realistic occlusion patterns. In this paper, we propose DPM++, a Dynamic Masked Metric Learning framework for occluded person re-identification. DPM++ learns an input-adaptive masked metric that dynamically selects reliable identity subspaces for each occluded instance, enabling matching to emphasize visibility-consistent evidence while suppressing unreliable components. Built upon the classifier-prototype space, DPM++ introduces a CLIP-based two-stage supervision scheme, where ID-level semantic priors are learned from the text branch and transferred into the classifier-prototype space for dynamic masked matching. To strengthen the masked metric, we introduce a saliency-guided patch transfer strategy to synthesize controllable and photo-realistic occluded samples during training. Exploiting real scene priors, this strategy exposes the model to realistic partial observations and provides richer supervision than random erasing. In addition, occlusion-aware sample pairing and mask-guided optimization improve the stability and effectiveness of the framework. Experiments on occluded and holistic person re-identification benchmarks show that DPM++ consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods in both holistic and occlusion scenarios.

</details>


### [112] [Are We Making Progress in Multimodal Domain Generalization? A Comprehensive Benchmark Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06643)
*Hao Dong,Hongzhao Li,Shupan Li,Muhammad Haris Khan,Eleni Chatzi,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified MMDG benchmark reveals marginal gains from specialized methods and robustness gaps across modalities and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inconsistent evaluation protocols in Multimodal Domain Generalization hinder reliable progress; a standardized, comprehensive benchmark is needed to compare methods fairly and assess robustness and trustworthiness.

Method: Introduce MMDG-Bench: six datasets across three tasks (action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, sentiment analysis), six modality combinations, nine representative methods, and multiple evaluation settings. Evaluate not only accuracy but also corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, and out-of-distribution detection. Large-scale evaluation with 7,402 trained networks across 95 cross-domain tasks.

Result: Under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods offer only marginal improvements over ERM baselines; no single method consistently outperforms others; substantial gap to upper-bound performance persists; trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform strongest bimodal configurations; all evaluated methods degrade under corruption and missing-modality scenarios, with some methods compromising model trustworthiness.

Conclusion: Progress in MMDG remains far from solved; standardized, robust benchmarking is essential to accurately assess advances and guide future research.

Abstract: Despite the growing popularity of Multimodal Domain Generalization (MMDG) for enhancing model robustness, it remains unclear whether reported performance gains reflect genuine algorithmic progress or are artifacts of inconsistent evaluation protocols. Current research is fragmented, with studies varying significantly across datasets, modality configurations, and experimental settings. Furthermore, existing benchmarks focus predominantly on action recognition, often neglecting critical real-world challenges such as input corruptions, missing modalities, and model trustworthiness. This lack of standardization obscures a reliable assessment of the field's advancement. To address this issue, we introduce MMDG-Bench, the first unified and comprehensive benchmark for MMDG, which standardizes evaluation across six datasets spanning three diverse tasks: action recognition, mechanical fault diagnosis, and sentiment analysis. MMDG-Bench encompasses six modality combinations, nine representative methods, and multiple evaluation settings. Beyond standard accuracy, it systematically assesses corruption robustness, missing-modality generalization, misclassification detection, and out-of-distribution detection. With 7, 402 neural networks trained in total across 95 unique cross-domain tasks, MMDG-Bench yields five key findings: (1) under fair comparisons, recent specialized MMDG methods offer only marginal improvements over ERM baseline; (2) no single method consistently outperforms others across datasets or modality combinations; (3) a substantial gap to upper-bound performance persists, indicating that MMDG remains far from solved; (4) trimodal fusion does not consistently outperform the strongest bimodal configurations; and (5) all evaluated methods exhibit significant degradation under corruption and missing-modality scenarios, with some methods further compromising model trustworthiness.

</details>


### [113] [Relit-LiVE: Relight Video by Jointly Learning Environment Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06658)
*Weiqing Xiao,Hong Li,Xiuyu Yang,Houyuan Chen,Wenyi Li,Tianqi Liu,Shaocong Xu,Chongjie Ye,Hao Zhao,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Relit-LiVE introduces raw reference images and a joint environment-map plus relit video diffusion framework to achieve physically consistent, temporally stable video relighting without requiring known per-frame camera poses, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on synthetic and real data and enabling broad downstream applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intrinsic decomposition-based video relighting often yields artifacts on real-world videos due to unreliable intrinsic decomposition and missing camera pose information, motivating a physically-consistent, pose-free relighting framework.

Method: Two core components: (1) insert raw reference images into the rendering process to recover lost or corrupted scene cues; (2) propose an environment video prediction formulation that jointly generates relit videos and per-frame environment maps aligned with each camera viewpoint in a single diffusion process, enforcing geometric-illumination alignment and accommodating dynamic lighting and camera motion.

Result: Extensive experiments show Relit-LiVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, with improved physical consistency and temporal stability; supports downstream tasks such as scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming relighting; code available at the project repository.

Conclusion: Relit-LiVE provides a pose-free, physically coherent video relighting framework by integrating raw references and a joint environment-map–relit-video diffusion process, enabling broader applications and robust relighting across dynamic scenes.

Abstract: Recent advances have shown that large-scale video diffusion models can be repurposed as neural renderers by first decomposing videos into intrinsic scene representations and then performing forward rendering under novel illumination. While promising, this paradigm fundamentally relies on accurate intrinsic decomposition, which remains highly unreliable for real-world videos and often leads to distorted appearances, broken materials, and accumulated temporal artifacts during relighting. In this work, we present Relit-LiVE, a novel video relighting framework that produces physically consistent, temporally stable results without requiring prior knowledge of camera pose. Our key insight is to explicitly introduce raw reference images into the rendering process, enabling the model to recover critical scene cues that are inevitably lost or corrupted in intrinsic representations. Furthermore, we propose a novel environment video prediction formulation that simultaneously generates relit videos and per-frame environment maps aligned with each camera viewpoint in a single diffusion process. This joint prediction enforces strong geometric-illumination alignment and naturally supports dynamic lighting and camera motion, significantly improving physical consistency in video relighting while easing the requirement of known per-frame camera pose. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Relit-LiVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video relighting and neural rendering methods across synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Beyond relighting, our framework naturally supports a wide range of downstream applications, including scene-level rendering, material editing, object insertion, and streaming video relighting. The Project is available at https://github.com/zhuxing0/Relit-LiVE.

</details>


### [114] [BAMI: Training-Free Bias Mitigation in GUI Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06664)
*Borui Zhang,Bo Zhang,Bo Wang,Wenzhao Zheng,Yuhao Cheng,Liang Tang,Yiqiang Yan,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BAMI mitigates GUI grounding biases (precision due to high-resolution images and ambiguity from complex UI) using MPD attribution and two manipulations—coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection—achieving training-free improvements (e.g., TianXi-Action-7B on ScreenSpot-Pro: 51.9%→57.8%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI grounding for agents is essential but challenging in complex interfaces; existing models struggle on benchmarks like ScreenSpot-Pro due to precision and ambiguity biases. The work aims to diagnose and mitigate these biases to improve non-retrained (training-free) performance.

Method: Introduce Masked Prediction Distribution (MPD) attribution to identify error sources; propose Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI) with two components: coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection to reduce precision and ambiguity biases.

Result: BAMI improves accuracy of GUI grounding models in a training-free setting; demonstrated on TianXi-Action-7B with ScreenSpot-Pro (51.9%→57.8%); ablation studies show robustness across parameter configurations; code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: BAMI effectively mitigates precision and ambiguity biases in GUI grounding without retraining, offering a robust, training-free improvement with demonstrated stability across configurations.

Abstract: GUI grounding is a critical capability for enabling GUI agents to execute tasks such as clicking and dragging. However, in complex scenarios like the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark, existing models often suffer from suboptimal performance. Utilizing the proposed \textbf{Masked Prediction Distribution (MPD)} attribution method, we identify that the primary sources of errors are twofold: high image resolution (leading to precision bias) and intricate interface elements (resulting in ambiguity bias). To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{Bias-Aware Manipulation Inference (BAMI)}, which incorporates two key manipulations, coarse-to-fine focus and candidate selection, to effectively mitigate these biases. Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that BAMI significantly enhances the accuracy of various GUI grounding models in a training-free setting. For instance, applying our method to the TianXi-Action-7B model boosts its accuracy on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark from 51.9\% to 57.8\%. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the robustness of the BAMI approach across diverse parameter configurations, highlighting its stability and effectiveness. Code is available at https://github.com/Neur-IO/BAMI.

</details>


### [115] [ActCam: Zero-Shot Joint Camera and 3D Motion Control for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06667)
*Omar El Khalifi,Thomas Rossi,Oscar Fossey,Thibault Fouque,Ulysse Mizrahi,Philip Torr,Ivan Laptev,Fabio Pizzati,Baptiste Bellot-Gurlet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ActCam is a zero-shot video synthesis method that jointly transfers character motion from a driving video into a new scene and enables per-frame camera parameter control by conditioning a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model on pose and depth with a two-phase denoising schedule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve fine-grained, geometry-consistent joint control of actor motion and camera trajectories in video generation without additional training.

Method: Use a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model that accepts pose and depth conditioning. From a source video and target camera motion, synthesize pose and sparse depth that remain geometrically consistent. Apply a two-phase conditioning schedule: early denoising steps condition on both pose and sparse depth to enforce scene structure; later steps drop depth and use pose-only guidance to refine high-frequency details without over-constraining.

Result: ActCam yields improved camera adherence and motion fidelity compared to pose-only controls and other pose+camera methods, with favorable human evaluations, especially under large viewpoint changes. Demonstrations across diverse motions and viewpoints show the approach effectively enforces geometry while preserving detail.

Conclusion: Careful camera-consistent conditioning combined with staged guidance enables strong joint camera and motion control in video generation without training, expanding zero-shot capabilities for cinematography-aware synthesis.

Abstract: For artistic applications, video generation requires fine-grained control over both performance and cinematography, i.e., the actor's motion and the camera trajectory. We present ActCam, a zero-shot method for video generation that jointly transfers character motion from a driving video into a new scene and enables per-frame control of intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters. ActCam builds on any pretrained image-to-video diffusion model that accepts conditioning in terms of scene depth and character pose. Given a source video with a moving character and a target camera motion, ActCam generates pose and depth conditions that remain geometrically consistent across frames. We then run a single sampling process with a two-phase conditioning schedule: early denoising steps condition on both pose and sparse depth to enforce scene structure, after which depth is dropped and pose-only guidance refines high-frequency details without over-constraining the generation. We evaluate ActCam on multiple benchmarks spanning diverse character motions and challenging viewpoint changes. We find that, compared to pose-only control and other pose and camera methods, ActCam improves camera adherence and motion fidelity, and is preferred in human evaluations, especially under large viewpoint changes. Our results highlight that careful camera-consistent conditioning and staged guidance can enable strong joint camera and motion control without training. Project page: https://elkhomar.github.io/actcam/.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [116] [Are Flat Minima an Illusion?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05209)
*Michael Timothy Bennett*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that generalization is driven by the network's weakness—the volume of completions compatible with the learned function (in the network’s embodied language)—not by flat minima or Hessian-based sharpness. Reparameterisation can inflate the Hessian without changing predictions, so weight-space geometry cannot be the cause. Weakness is invariant to reparameterisation, minimax-optimal under exchangeable demands, and correlates with PAC-Bayes bounds. Empirically, weakness predicts generalisation on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, while sharpness is less predictive or negative; simplicity is dataset-dependent. Large-batch generalisation advantages shrink with more data. Flat minima are not the answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reassess what controls generalization in neural networks by separating parameter-space geometry from function-space behavior, challenging Hessian/sharpness explanations and linking generalization to a reparameterisation-invariant notion of weakness.

Method: Theoretical: define weakness as the volume of function-preserving completions and show minimax-optimality under exchangeable demands; relate to PAC-Bayes bounds. Empirical: train ~100 networks with identical architectures on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, measure weakness, sharpness, and simplicity, and correlate with generalisation across varying data sizes.

Result: Weakness shows positive correlation with generalisation (e.g., MNIST ρ ≈ +0.374, p ≈ 1.2e-4; Fashion-MNIST ρ ≈ +0.384, p ≈ 8.15e-5); sharpness shows negative or weaker correlation (e.g., ρ ≈ -0.226); simplicity largely uninformative on MNIST (p = 0.848) but somewhat predictive on Fashion-MNIST; large-batch generalisation advantage on MNIST shrinks as data increases (from +1.6% at 2k to +0.02% at 60k); results suggest weakness is dataset-invariant while simplicity is dataset-dependent; flat minima did not explain the results.

Conclusion: Weight-space geometry and flat minima are not causal for generalisation. The proposed notion of weakness—invariant to reparameterisation and tied to the learned function—drives generalisation and aligns with PAC-Bayes bounds; data scale modulates the observed benefits of large-batch training; future work should focus on function-space analyses and reparameterisation-invariant metrics.

Abstract: Neural networks that land in flat regions of the loss landscape tend to generalise better than those in sharp regions. Sharpness-Aware Minimisation exploits this to improve generalisation. But function-preserving reparameterisation can inflate the Hessian of any minimum by two orders of magnitude without changing a single prediction. If the geometry of weight space can be manufactured from nothing, it cannot be the cause of anything. In other words, flat is simple and simplicity depends on encoding. Here I show that the actual driver is weakness, the volume of completions compatible with the learned function in the learner's embodied language. Weakness is reparameterisation-invariant because it is defined over what the network \emph{does}, not how it is parameterised. I prove weakness is minimax-optimal under exchangeable demands, and that PAC-Bayes bounds work because they correlate with it. On MNIST, the large-batch generalisation advantage \emph{vanishes} as training data grows, from $+1.6\%$ at $n = 2{,}000$ to $+0.02\%$ at $n = 60{,}000$. A quantity whose predictive power depends on how much data you have is not a cause but a confounder. I run head-to-heads on 100 networks with identical architecture and training. For MNIST weakness predicts generalisation ($ρ= +0.374$, $p = 0.00012$), sharpness anticorrelates ($ρ= -0.226$) and simplicity predicts nothing ($p = 0.848$). For Fashion-MNIST ($ρ= +0.384$, $p = 8.15 \times 10^{-5}$), though simplicity is at least somewhat predictive there. Simplicity is dataset dependent, whereas weakness is invariant. Flat minima were never the answer.

</details>


### [117] [Geometry-Aware Simplicial Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06061)
*Elena Xinyi Wang,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GSWL extends WL/SWL to geometric simplicial complexes by integrating vertex coordinates into color refinement; it provides an upper bound for geometry-aware simplex-message-passing expressivity, shows parameterized matching of discriminative power on finite families, and, with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), yields a complete geometric invariant plus an approximation framework, with empirical validation demonstrating a hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WL/SWL capture combinatorial structure but ignore embeddings. There is a need for geometry-aware expressivity and a unifying completeness/approximation framework for geometric simplicial complexes.

Method: Define Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler–Lehman (GSWL) by augmenting color refinement with vertex coordinates in geometric simplicial complexes. Prove: (i) geometry-aware simplicial MP expressivity ≤ GSWL; (ii) for any fixed finite family, there exist parameters making GSWL’s discriminative power match that of geometry-aware MP schemes. Combine GSWL with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) to obtain a complete invariant and an approximation framework.

Result: Establishes an upper bound on the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial MP by GSWL; shows parameterized equivalence to geometry-aware MP discriminative power on finite families; integrates with ECT to form a complete geometric invariant and approximation framework; empirical experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets validate the theoretical hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

Conclusion: GSWL provides a geometric expressivity characterization for geometric simplicial complexes and enables practical approximation via ECT; theory is corroborated by experiments demonstrating a hierarchy from purely combinatorial models to geometry-aware variants.

Abstract: The Weisfeiler--Lehman (WL) test and its simplicial extension (SWL) characterize the combinatorial expressivity of message passing networks, but they are blind to geometry, i.e., meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings are indistinguishable. We introduce the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler--Lehman (GSWL) test, which incorporates vertex coordinates into color refinement for geometric simplicial complexes. In addition, we show that (i) the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes is bounded above by GSWL, and (ii) that there exist parameters such that the discriminating power of GSWL is matched by these schemes on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes. Combined with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes, this yields a geometric expressivity characterization together with an approximation framework. Experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets serve to validate our theory, showing a clear hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

</details>


### [118] [Nationwide EHR-Based Chronic Rhinosinusitis Prediction Using Demographic-Stratified Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05213)
*Sicong Chang,Yidan Shen,Justina Varghese,Akshay R Prabhakar,Sebastian Guadarrama-Sistos-Vazquez,Jiefu Chen,Masayoshi Takashima,Omar G. Ahmed,Renjie Hu,Xin Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A predictive study for chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) using nationwide longitudinal EHR data from All of Us; a two-year pre-diagnostic window; a hybrid feature-selection reduces ~110k codes to 100 features; six demographic subgroups with tailored hyperparameters; achieved AUC 0.8461, with small improvement over baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CRS diagnosis is hard to identify early due to overlapping symptoms and heterogeneous phenotypes; prior studies lack population-level generalizability due to single-institution cohorts. The study aims to build generalizable risk stratification using large-scale, diverse EHR data.

Method: Hybrid feature selection combining prevalence-based screening with model-based importance ranking to compress ~110k codes into 100 interpretable features. Models trained separately for six adult sex/life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameters. Evaluation uses AUC (0.8461 overall; improvement of 0.0168 over best baseline).

Result: AUC of 0.8461 achieved; performance improvement over baseline 0.0168. Demonstrates feasibility of CRS risk stratification using routinely collected EHR data and potential for earlier triage in primary care.

Conclusion: Population-representative CRS risk stratification from EHR data is feasible and may inform earlier referral in primary care. The subgrouped modeling approach suggests demographic heterogeneity can be leveraged for improved discrimination.

Abstract: Chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) is a common heterogeneous inflammatory disorder that causes substantial morbidity and healthcare costs. CRS is difficult to identify early from routine encounters, as symptom presentations overlap with common conditions such as allergic rhinitis, and heterogeneous phenotypes further obscure risk patterns. Prior predictive studies often rely on single-institutional cohorts , which reduce population-level generalizability. To overcome this, we leveraged nationwide longitudinal EHR data from the \textit{All of Us} Research Program to predict CRS diagnosis using two years of pre-diagnostic history. To address extreme feature sparsity and dimensionality in coded EHR data, we implemented a hybrid feature-selection pipeline that combines prevalence-based statistical screening with model-based importance ranking, compressing approximately 110,000 candidate codes into 100 interpretable features. To capture demographic heterogeneity, we trained demographic stratified models across six adult sex and life-stage subgroups with subgroup-specific hyperparameter tuning. Our framework achieved an overall AUC of 0.8461, improving discrimination by 0.0168 over the best baseline. These results demonstrate that routinely collected EHR data may support population-representative CRS risk stratification and inform earlier triage and referral prioritization in primary care.

</details>


### [119] [SAT: Sequential Agent Tuning for Coordinator Free Plug and Play Multi-LLM Training with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05216)
*Yi Xie,Yangyang Xu,Yi Fan,Bo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential Agent Tuning (SAT) offers a coordinator-free, decentralized training method for multi-agent LLM teams via factorized policies and block-coordinate updates. It introduces a sequence-aware on-policy advantage estimator conditioned on the evolving team policy and per-agent KL trust regions to limit occupancy drift. The framework guarantees monotonic improvement and plug-and-play invariance, enabling upgrading individual agents without retraining others. Empirically, a 12B total model team (three 4B agents) surpasses a 32B single model on AIME24/25 by 3.9% on average, and swapping in two 8B agents yields an additional 10.4% boost. Code is provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jointly updating multiple agents causes distribution shifts and coordination difficulties; there is a need for scalable, decentralized training that can leverage smaller models to match or exceed large models while ensuring stable, provable improvements.

Method: Represent the team as a factorized policy and perform block-coordinate updates over agents (coordinator-free). Develop a sequence-aware, on-policy advantage estimator that conditions on the evolving team policy. Enforce per-agent KL trust regions to bound occupancy drift. Provide theoretical guarantees of monotonic improvement and plug-and-play invariance (upgrading an agent yields a provable non-worsening bound on performance).

Result: The approach yields monotonic improvement guarantees and plug-and-play invariance, with empirical results showing a 12B-total-team (three 4B agents) surpassing Qwen3-32B on AIME24/25 by 3.9% average; swapping in two 8B agents improves composite score by 10.4%. Code and proofs are provided.

Conclusion: SAT enables scalable, decentralized multi-agent LLM training with strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains, validated on benchmark tasks; the method supports turnkey upgrades of individual agents without retraining the entire team.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) with a large number of parameters achieve strong performance but are often prohibitively expensive to deploy. Recent work explores using teams of smaller, more efficient LLMs that collectively match or even outperform a single large model. However, jointly updating multiple agents introduces compounding distribution shifts, making coordination and stability during training difficult. We address this by introducing Sequential Agent Tuning (SAT), a coordinator-free training paradigm. SAT represents the team as a factorized policy and employs block-coordinate updates over agents, enabling scalable, decentralized training without a central controller. Specifically, we develop a sequence-aware, on-policy advantage estimator that conditions on the evolving team policy, coupled with per-agent KL trust regions that isolate occupancy drift. Theoretically, this framework provides two critical guarantees. First, it ensures monotonic improvement, stabilizing the training process. Second, it establishes provable plug-and-play invariance: any agent can be upgraded to a stronger model without retraining the rest of the team, with a formal guarantee that the performance bound improves. Empirically, a team of three 4B agents (12B total) trained with SAT surpasses the much larger Qwen3-32B on AIME24/25 benchmarks by 3.9\% on average. We validate our plug-and-play theory by swapping in two 8B agents, which boosts the composite score by 10.4\%. We provide code and appendix of proof at https://github.com/Yydc/SAT-AAMAS

</details>


### [120] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks with Learnable Loss Balancing and Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05217)
*Reza Pirayeshshirazinezhad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive physics-informed neural network (PINN) with a learnable blending neuron that dynamically weighs physics residuals versus data losses using uncertainties, plus transfer learning for data-scarce problems; validated on heat transfer in liquid-metal micro heat sinks with 87 CFD datapoints, achieving <8% error and outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In scientific ML, data are scarce and fixed or heuristic loss weighting in PINNs can hinder training stability and generalization. A mechanism that adaptively balances physics and data supervision can improve robustness and performance.

Method: Introduce a blending neuron that learns to combine physics residual loss and data loss, with weighting guided by their uncertainties for stable training. Integrate transfer learning to reuse representations from related domains and adapt them to new physical systems with limited data.

Result: On predicting heat transfer in liquid-metal miniature heat sinks using 87 CFD datapoints, the adaptive PINN achieves error <8%, outperforming shallow neural networks, kernel methods, and physics-only baselines.

Conclusion: The framework offers a general recipe for embedding physics adaptively into neural networks, providing a robust and reproducible approach for data-scarce problems across domains such as fluid dynamics and material modeling.

Abstract: We propose a self-supervised physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework that adaptively balances physics-based and data-driven supervision for scientific machine learning under data scarcity. Unlike prior PINNs that rely on fixed or heuristic weighting of physics residuals and data loss, our approach introduces a learnable blending neuron that dynamically adjusts the relative contribution of each term based on their uncertainties. This mechanism enables stable training and improved generalization without manual tuning. To further enhance efficiency, we integrate a transfer learning strategy that reuses representations from related domains and adapts them to new physical systems with limited data. We validate the framework for the prediction of heat transfer in liquid-metal miniature heat sinks using only 87 CFD datapoints, where the adaptive PINN achieves an error <8%, outperforming shallow neural networks, kernel methods, and physics-only baselines. Our framework provides a general recipe for embedding physics adaptively into neural networks, offering a robust and reproducible approach for data-scarce problems across various scientific domains, including fluid dynamics and material modeling.

</details>


### [121] [Horizon-Constrained Rashomon Sets for Chaotic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05218)
*Gauri Kale,Rahul Vishwakarma,Holly Diamond,Ava Hedayatipour,Amin Rezaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Horizon-constrained Rashomon sets link predictive multiplicity to chaotic prediction horizons, showing that in chaotic systems the set contracts exponentially with lead time, governed by the maximum Lyapunov exponent; introduces Lyapunov-weighted metrics and decision-aligned model selection to improve downstream utility while preserving predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive multiplicity and chaotic dynamics have evolved independently, but their intersection remains poorly understood. A principled framework is needed to understand how model equivalence degrades as forecast lead time grows in chaotic systems and to select models based on downstream utility rather than pure forecast accuracy, especially in safety-critical settings.

Method: Define horizon-constrained Rashomon sets to capture model equivalence over a prediction horizon in chaotic systems. Prove exponential contraction of the effective Rashomon set with lead time, with rate determined by the maximum Lyapunov exponent. Introduce Lyapunov-weighted metrics to tighten bounds on predictive disagreement. Develop decision-aligned selection algorithms that optimize downstream utility using near-optimal models instead of accuracy alone. Validate via experiments on synthetic chaotic systems (Lorenz-96, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky) and real-world domains (wind power, traffic, weather).

Result: Theoretically, the effective Rashomon set contracts exponentially with lead time at a rate set by the maximum Lyapunov exponent. Lyapunov-weighted metrics yield tighter predictive-disagreement bounds. Practically, decision-aligned selection improves downstream decision quality by 18–34% while maintaining competitive predictive performance across diverse chaotic domains.

Conclusion: Establishes the first rigorous link between chaos theory and predictive multiplicity, offering principled guidance for deploying ML in safety-critical chaotic domains and enabling decisions that depend on downstream utility rather than forecast accuracy alone.

Abstract: Predictive multiplicity and chaotic dynamics represent two fundamental challenges in machine learning that have evolved independently despite their conceptual connections. We bridge this gap by introducing horizon-constrained Rashomon sets, a theoretical framework that characterizes how model multiplicity evolves with prediction horizon in chaotic systems. Unlike static prediction tasks where the Rashomon set remains fixed, chaos induces exponential divergence among initially similar models, fundamentally transforming the nature of predictive equivalence. We prove that the effective Rashomon set contracts exponentially with lead time at a rate determined by the maximum Lyapunov exponent and introduce Lyapunov-weighted metrics that provide tighter bounds on predictive disagreement. Leveraging these insights, we develop decision-aligned selection algorithms that choose among near-optimal models based on downstream utility rather than forecast accuracy alone. Extensive experiments on synthetic chaotic systems (Lorenz-96, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky) and real-world applications (wind power, traffic, weather) demonstrate that our framework improves decision quality by 18-34\% while maintaining competitive predictive performance. This work establishes the first rigorous connection between chaos theory and predictive multiplicity, providing principled guidance for deploying machine learning in safety-critical chaotic domains.

</details>


### [122] [MidSteer: Optimal Affine Framework for Steering Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05220)
*Tatiana Gaintseva,Andrew Stepanov,Ziquan Liu,Martin Benning,Gregory Slabaugh,Jiankang Deng,Ismail Elezi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework for concept steering in generative models that connects standard erasure to LEACE, introduces LEACE-Switch and MidSteer for affine manipulation under optimality/minimal-disturbance guarantees, and demonstrates effectiveness across vision diffusion models and large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirically successful concept steering lacks a rigorous theory; a principled framework is needed to understand when and how steering operations are optimal, generalizable, and minimally disruptive.

Method: Formally relates steering/affine erasure to LEACE (a closed-form affine erasure method); develops LEACE-Switch to characterize when affine switching is optimal; introduces MidSteer (Minimal Disturbance concept Steering) as a general affine framework with relaxed assumptions; provides theoretical analyses and broad empirical validation.

Result: Shows that standard deletions/removals of unwanted behaviors equal a special case of LEACE; derives conditions under which LEACE-Switch yields an optimal affine solution; demonstrates MidSteer achieves favorable performance across tasks, modalities, and architectures, including vision diffusion models and LLMs.

Conclusion: Provides a principled theory of concept steering, unifying prior methods under an affine framework and offering practical methods (LEACE-Switch, MidSteer) with demonstrated versatility and minimal disturbance across diverse model families.

Abstract: Steering intermediate representations has emerged as a powerful strategy for controlling generative models, particularly in post-deployment alignment and safety settings. However, despite its empirical success, it currently lacks a comprehensive theoretical framework. In this paper, we bridge this gap by formalizing the theory of concept steering. First, we establish a link between steering and affine concept erasure, proving that the standard approach for removing unwanted behaviors is a special case of LEACE (a closed-form method for affine erasure). Next, we formulate a principled theoretical framework for concept switching, LEACE-Switch, and characterize the assumptions under which it provides an optimal affine solution. Building on this analysis, we then introduce MidSteer (Minimal Disturbance concept Steering), a more general affine framework for concept manipulation that relaxes these assumptions and enables directed, minimal-disturbance transformations. We demonstrate that MidSteer performs favorably across a range of tasks, modalities, and architectures, including vision diffusion models and large language models.

</details>


### [123] [Data-Driven Variational Basis Learning Beyond Neural Networks: A Non-Neural Framework for Adaptive Basis Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05221)
*Andrew Kiruluta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DVBL is a non-neural, variational framework that learns basis atoms directly from data, jointly with sample-specific coefficients and an optional latent linear evolution operator, yielding an interpretable, data-adaptive dictionary with rigorous analysis (existence of minimizers, blockwise alternating minimization, coefficient recovery and basis identifiability) and compatibility with manifold and dynamical regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical representations (Fourier, wavelets, fixed dictionaries) are analytically tractable but not tailored to high-dimensional empirical data; neural networks learn features but are often opaque and lack explicit basis structure. DVBL aims to provide a data-adaptive, interpretable, non-neural alternative with mathematical transparency.

Method: Formulate a variational optimization where basis atoms are optimization variables learned jointly with per-sample coefficients and, when suitable, a latent linear operator governing evolution. Prove existence of minimizers, establish blockwise descent properties for an alternating minimization algorithm, derive conditions for coefficient recovery and basis identifiability, and show how to incorporate manifold and dynamical regularization without neural architectures. Compare conceptually to classical dictionary learning, spectral methods, Koopman operator methods, and deep representation learning.

Result: Mathematical foundations are established: existence of minimizers; blockwise descent for an alternating minimization scheme; conditions under which coefficients can be recovered and bases identified; integration of manifold and dynamical regularization is discussed; positions DVBL relative to classical and modern approaches with a focus on interpretability and analytical tractability.

Conclusion: DVBL delivers an explicit, interpretable data-adaptive basis learning framework that unifies ideas from dictionary learning, spectral methods, and dynamical systems while avoiding neural nets; it offers rigorous guarantees and practical regularization avenues, highlighting conceptual novelty over the compared paradigms.

Abstract: Classical representation systems such as Fourier series, wavelets, and fixed dictionaries provide analytically tractable basis expansions, but they are not intrinsically adapted to the empirical structure of modern high-dimensional data. Neural networks overcome this limitation by learning features from data, yet they do so through layered nonlinear parameterizations that often sacrifice interpretability, explicit control over basis structure, and mathematical transparency. In this manuscript we develop a non-neural alternative that learns basis functions directly from data through variational optimization. The proposed framework, termed Data Driven Variational Basis Learning (DVBL), treats basis atoms as primary optimization variables and learns them jointly with sample-specific coefficients and, when appropriate, a latent linear evolution operator. This yields a data-adaptive basis expansion that remains explicit, interpretable, and amenable to rigorous analysis. We formulate the model, establish existence of minimizers, prove blockwise descent properties for an alternating minimization algorithm, give conditions for coefficient recovery and basis identifiability, and show how manifold and dynamical regularization can be integrated without invoking neural architectures. We also discuss the conceptual novelty of the framework relative to classical dictionary learning, spectral methods, Koopman operator methods, and deep representation learning.

</details>


### [124] [Adaptive Computation Depth via Learned Token Routing in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05222)
*Ahmed Abdelmuniem Abdalla Mohammed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Token-Selective Attention (TSA): per-token learned gates on residual updates in transformers to dynamically skip token-layer computations with minimal overhead, yielding significant compute savings while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of applying a uniform depth to all tokens in transformer stacks; enable dynamic, content-dependent depth to reduce compute without sacrificing performance.

Method: Introduce a per-token gate after each transformer block that modulates the residual update. The gate is a lightweight two-layer MLP producing a continuous halting probability, enabling end-to-end differentiable routing with ~1.7% parameter overhead and no changes to the base architecture. Evaluate under lambda=0 (no depth regularization) and on character-level language modeling.

Result: TSA reduces token-layer operations by 14–23% on Tiny Shakespeare and enwik8 with <0.5% quality loss. At matched efficiency, TSA achieves 0.7% lower validation loss than early exit, and the learned routing transfers to inference-time sparse execution for real wall-clock speedup.

Conclusion: Per-token dynamic depth via TSA enables efficient transformer computation with minimal overhead and transfers to sparse, fast inference; the approach shows promise for content-adaptive computation in sequence models.

Abstract: Standard transformer architectures apply the same number of layers to every token regardless of contextual difficulty. We present Token-Selective Attention (TSA), a learned per-token gate on residual updates between consecutive transformer blocks. Each gate is a lightweight two-layer multi-layer perceptron (MLP) that produces a continuous halting probability, making the mechanism end-to-end differentiable with 1.7% parameter overhead and no changes to the base architecture. Notably, TSA learns difficulty-proportional routing without any explicit depth pressure: even at $λ=0$ (no depth regularisation), the task-loss gradient alone drives the router to skip 20% of token-layer operations. On character-level language modeling, TSA saved 14-23% of token-layer operations (TLOps) across Tiny-Shakespeare and enwik8 at <0.5% quality loss. At matched efficiency, TSA achieved 0.7% lower validation loss than early exit, and the learned routing transfers directly to inference-time sparse execution for real wall-clock speedup.

</details>


### [125] [Structural Instability of Feature Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05223)
*Yunpeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A geometric framework for compositional steering in Sparse Autoencoders is developed, modeling activation space as a high-dimensional sparse cone manifold. It derives an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold under a spherical dictionary model via Gaussian mean width, and shows ReLU-induced drift in high-bias regimes leads to interference growth with composition (ratchet effect). Empirical validation on CLEVR features confirms accelerated transitions with structured semantics, highlighting geometric constraints on union-based steering and the need for explicit interference-management in composition mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the theoretical foundations and limits of compositional steering in sparse autoencoders, addressing non-linear interference missed by linear representations and overcomplete dictionaries.

Method: Construct a geometric model of activation space as a high-dimensional sparse cone manifold under a spherical dictionary. Derive an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold using Gaussian mean width (statistical dimension) of the signal cone. Analyze ReLU rectification in the high-bias regime, showing drift that accumulates under composition and yields interference growth. Validate scaling predictions on structured semantic features extracted from CLEVR to compare hierarchical correlations against random baselines.

Result: Derivation of an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold governed by the Gaussian mean width of the sparse-cone signal set under a spherical dictionary. Identification of a ratchet-like drift due to ReLU in the high-bias regime, leading to increasing interference with composition. Empirical validation showing faster transitions for structured CLEVR features (due to hierarchical correlations) compared to random baselines, supporting the predicted scaling trends.

Conclusion: Union-based steering in sparse autoencoders faces geometric constraints that limit scalability; linear superposition is insufficient to capture interference. The work motivates designing composition mechanisms that explicitly manage non-linear interference beyond naive linear aggregation.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for disentangling feature superposition in transformer-based architectures, enabling precise control via activation steering. However, the theoretical foundations of compositional steering -- the simultaneous activation of distinct semantic latents -- remain under-explored. The prevailing Linear Representation Hypothesis often abstracts away non-linear interference effects that arise in overcomplete dictionaries. We present a geometric framework for analyzing the instability of feature unions. Modeling the activation space as a high-dimensional sparse cone manifold, we derive an asymptotic compositional-collapse threshold under a spherical dictionary model, characterized by the Gaussian mean width (statistical dimension) of the signal cone. We further show that, in the high-bias regime, ReLU rectification converts microscopic correlation-induced variance fluctuations into a systematic drift that accumulates under composition, yielding interference growth consistent with a ratchet effect. We validate the predicted scaling trends on structured semantic features extracted from CLEVR, where hierarchical correlations accelerate the transition relative to random baselines. Together, our results highlight geometric constraints on the scalability of union-based steering and motivate composition mechanisms that explicitly manage interference beyond naive linear superposition.

</details>


### [126] [Channel-Level Semantic Perturbations: Unlearnable Examples for Diverse Training Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05224)
*Bo Wang,Jia Ni,Mengnan Zhao,Zhan Qin,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In pretrain-finetune (PF) settings, standard unlearnable examples (UEs) lose effectiveness; the paper introduces Shallow Semantic Camouflage (SSC) to keep data unlearnability by restricting perturbations to a semantically valid subspace, showing robustness under shallow-layer freezing and SF-pretrain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unauthorized use of personal data in model training poses privacy risks; prior UE work evaluated mainly from-scratch training, leaving PF paradigms unexamined.

Method: Systematically analyze unlearnable examples across diverse training paradigms; explain the observed degradation via semantic filtering; propose a hierarchical deception strategy, SSC, constraining perturbations to a semantically valid subspace; evaluate under challenging PF conditions.

Result: Loading and freezing pretrained weights substantially weaken existing UEs; frozen shallow layers tend to preserve semantics and filter out unlearnable noise; SSC preserves data unlearnability even under shallow-layer freezing and semantic-focused pretraining, bridging the gap for pretrain-based UE learning.

Conclusion: SSC offers a robust mechanism for maintaining unlearnability in pretrain-based pipelines, highlighting the role of semantic subspace constraints in perturbation design and suggesting further exploration of semantically aware defenses in training paradigms.

Abstract: The unauthorized use of personal data in model training has emerged as a growing privacy threat. Unlearnable examples (UEs) address this issue by embedding imperceptible perturbations into benign examples to obstruct feature learning. However, existing studies mainly evaluate UEs under from-scratch training settings, leaving their behavior under the widely adopted pretraining-finetuning (PF) paradigm largely unexplored.
