In this work, we provide the first systematic investigation of unlearnable examples across diverse training paradigms. Our analysis reveals that loading and freezing pretrained weights significantly weakens the effectiveness of existing UEs methods. We further explain these findings through semantic filtering: while UEs tend to induce models to overfit non-semantic noise, thereby weakening their semantic extraction capabilities, under the PF paradigm, frozen shallow layers preserve data semantics, effectively filtering out distracting information like unlearnable noise. Guided by these insights, we propose a hierarchical deception strategy, Shallow Semantic Camouflage (SSC), that confines the generation process to a semantically valid subspace, aiming to bypass the semantic suppression introduced by pretrained weights. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently preserves data unlearnability even under challenging training paradigms, such as shallow-layer freezing and semantic-focused pretraining (SF-Pretrain), bridging the critical gap in pretrain-based unlearnable learning.

</details>


### [127] [MACS: Modality-Aware Capacity Scaling for Efficient Multimodal MoE Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05225)
*Bo Li,Chuan Wu,shaolin Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MACS—a training-free inference framework for mixture-of-experts multimodal LLMs—mitigates straggler effects in Expert Parallelism by addressing information heterogeneity and modality dynamics through Entropy-Weighted Load and Dynamic Modality-Adaptive Capacity, yielding significant efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In MoE MLLMs with Expert Parallelism, inference bottlenecks arise from stragglers. In multimodal settings, simple token-count load balancing fails due to information heterogeneity (redundant visual tokens) and modality dynamics (varying visual/text ratios), leading to poor resource allocation.

Method: MACS introduces two components: (1) Entropy-Weighted Load to quantify the semantic value of visual tokens, mitigating information heterogeneity; (2) Dynamic Modality-Adaptive Capacity to allocate expert resources based on the real-time modal composition of the input; both training-free and applicable during EP inference.

Result: MACS significantly outperforms existing methods on various multimodal benchmarks, demonstrating robust efficiency gains for EP inference in MoE MLLMs.

Conclusion: MACS provides a novel, training-free framework for efficient deployment of MoE MLLMs in EP inference, effectively handling information heterogeneity and modality dynamics through entropy-weighted token processing and dynamic capacity scaling.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts Multimodal Large Language Models (MoE MLLMs) suffer from a significant efficiency bottleneck during Expert Parallelism (EP) inference due to the straggler effect. This issue is worsened in the multimodal context, as existing token-count-based load balancing methods fail to address two unique challenges: (1) Information Heterogeneity, where numerous redundant visual tokens are treated equally to semantically critical ones, and (2) Modality Dynamics, where varying visual to text ratios across tasks lead to resource misallocation. To address these challenges, we propose MACS (Modality-Aware Capacity Scaling), a training-free inference framework. Specifically, MACS introduces an Entropy-Weighted Load mechanism to quantify the semantic value of visual tokens, addressing information heterogeneity. Additionally, the Dynamic Modality-Adaptive Capacity mechanism allocates expert resources based on the real-time modal composition of the input. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MACS significantly outperforms existing methods on various multimodal benchmarks, providing a novel and robust solution for the efficient deployment of MoE MLLMs in EP inference.

</details>


### [128] [Internalizing Outcome Supervision into Process Supervision: A New Paradigm for Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05226)
*Fei Ding,Yongkang Zhang,Runhao Liu,Yuhao Liao,Zijian Zeng,Sibo wang,Huiming Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conceptual RL-for-reasoning approach that internalizes outcome supervision into internal process supervision, enabling automatic extraction of fine-grained learning signals from failed reasoning trajectories and a self-refining training paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-level rewards and externally constructed process supervision hinder precise credit assignment and scalable learning in reasoning tasks; there is a need for fine-grained, scalable supervision signals derived from the model's own reasoning process.

Method: Introduce a supervision-internalization method that identifies, corrects, and reuses failed reasoning trajectories to extract process-level learning signals; the model continually generates and refines its own internal process supervision during RL, enabling finer-grained policy optimization under outcome-only supervision.

Result: The abstract proposes a conceptual framework and training paradigm rather than reporting empirical results; it claims improved credit assignment via internal process supervision but does not provide experimental validation in the abstract.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning for reasoning can be reframed as internalizing outcome supervision into process supervision, enabling a self-generated, continual supervision signal and a new path for fine-grained credit assignment beyond externally provided supervision.

Abstract: The central challenge of reinforcement learning for reasoning lies not only in the sparsity of outcome-level supervision, but more fundamentally in how to transform feedback provided only at the end of a sequence into fine-grained learning signals that can guide intermediate reasoning steps. Existing approaches either rely on outcome-level rewards for sequence-level optimization, which makes precise credit assignment difficult, or depend on externally constructed process supervision, which is costly and difficult to scale sustainably. To address this, we propose a new perspective: reinforcement learning for reasoning can be understood as the problem of internalizing outcome supervision into process supervision. From this perspective, we introduce a supervision-internalization method for reinforcement learning for reasoning, enabling the model to automatically extract process-level learning signals through identifying, correcting, and reusing failed reasoning trajectories, thereby achieving finer-grained policy optimization under outcome-only supervision. We further abstract this idea into a new training paradigm, in which the model continually generates and refines its own internal process supervision during reinforcement learning, opening a new path for fine-grained credit assignment in reinforcement learning for reasoning that differs from externally provided process supervision.

</details>


### [129] [Rethinking Data Curation in LLM Training: Online Reweighting Offers Better Generalization than Offline Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05227)
*Wanru Zhao,Yihong Chen,Yuzhi Tang,Wentao Ma,Shengchao Hu,Shell Xu Hu,Alex Iacob,Abhinav Mehrotra,Nicholas D. Lane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADAPT reframes data curation as online reweighting during training, using adaptive per-sample learning rates guided by similarity-based quality signals to dynamically emphasize helpful samples without changing dataset size. It positions itself as an implicit curriculum learner that shifts from coarse to fine semantic distinctions, and reports consistent improvements over offline selection/mixing and prior online methods under equal FLOPs in instruction tuning and large-scale pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional offline data curation creates brittle pipelines and can degrade generalization due to hard filtering/resampling and fixed data distributions. There is a need for a dynamic approach that adapts data emphasis during training to maintain diversity while improving generalization.

Method: Introduce ADAPT (Adaptive Data reweighting for Pretraining and FineTuning), an online framework that reweights training samples with adaptive per-sample learning rates. The reweighting is guided by similarity-based quality signals to assess sample usefulness, without altering the total number of training examples. It functions as an implicit curriculum, progressively focusing on finer semantic distinctions as the model evolves.

Result: Empirical results on instruction tuning and large-scale pretraining show ADAPT outperforms offline data selection/mixing and existing online methods, delivering stronger cross-benchmark generalization under the same computational budget (equal FLOPs).

Conclusion: Online data reweighting can effectively replace static offline curation by maintaining dataset size, enabling dynamic curriculum-like progression, and achieving better generalization without extra data or reprocessing.

Abstract: Data curation is a critical yet under-explored area in large language model (LLM) training. Existing methods, such as data selection and mixing, operate in an offline paradigm, detaching themselves from training. This separation introduces engineering overhead and makes the curation brittle: the entire pipeline must be re-run under model/task shifts. Moreover, offline methods alter data size through hard filtering or resampling, often sacrificing data diversity and harming generalization. We propose to rethink data curation as an online reweighting problem, where sample importance is dynamically adjusted during training via loss weighting rather than static pre-processing. Specifically, we introduce ADAPT (Adaptive Data reweighting for Pretraining and FineTuning), a dynamic online framework that reweights training samples with adaptive per-sample learning rates guided by similarity-based quality signals, without changing the number of training samples. Unlike offline methods that enforce a static data distribution, ADAPT acts as an implicit curriculum learner, progressively shifting focus from coarse-grained patterns to fine-grained semantic distinctions as the model evolves. Experiments on both instruction tuning and large-scale pretraining show that ADAPT consistently outperforms offline selection/mixing and prior online methods, achieving stronger cross-benchmark generalization under equal FLOPs.

</details>


### [130] [Evolutionary fine tuning of quantized convolution-based deep learning models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05228)
*Marcin Pietroń*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evolution-strategy-based quantization refinement improves accuracy of pretrained quantized models beyond nearest-neighbor rounding, evaluated on VGG/ResNet and Autoencoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization reduces model size but often relies on suboptimal nearest-neighbor rounding; ES exploration can discover better quantization states for a small subset of weights, boosting accuracy with minimal changes.

Method: Apply evolution strategy to pretrained quantized networks; in each iteration, mutate a small percentage of weights, adjusting their quantization state; use a specific set of operators/parameters; no full retraining; tested on image classifications (VGG, ResNet) and autoencoder.

Result: Demonstrates accuracy improvements for quantized models; cited tasks include image classification and detection; autoencoder simulations; faster improvement in accuracy with appropriate ES configuration.

Conclusion: ES-guided quantization refinement can surpass standard nearest-neighbor quantization, offering a practical post-quantization optimization path for pretrained models.

Abstract: Deep learning models are the most efficient models in many machine learning tasks. The main disadvantage when using them in IoT, mobile devices, independent autonomous or real-time systems is their complexity and memory size. Therefore, much research has concentrated on compression techniques of deep learning architectures. One of the most popular technique is quantization. In most of the works, the quantization is done based on the nearest neighbour quantization technique. This work focuses on improving the quantization efficiency in pretrained and quantized models. This approach has the potential to improve the final accuracy of quantized models. The main postulate of the work is that final quantization states of the network based on nearest neighbour rounding does not guarantee optimal accuracy. In the presented work, the evolution strategy is used as an optimization approach. The evolution in each iteration changes the values of the small percentage of weights. It shifts theirs values to different quantization states. The work shows that proposed evolution with an appropriate set of operators and parameters can fast improve the accuracy of the quantized models. The results are presented for popular architectures such as VGG and Resnet for image classification and detection. Additionally, simulations were carried out for the autoencoder architecture.

</details>


### [131] [Expert Routing for Communication-Efficient MoE via Finite Expert Banks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05278)
*Mohammad Reza Deylam Salehi,Ali Khalesi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Treats MoE gating as a stochastic channel and analyzes routing information via information-theoretic quantities. Develops a finite-bank MNIST setup with pretrained CNN experts and a discrete data-dependent selection rule to enable tractable estimations of mutual information and rate–distortion-like metrics for resource-aware MoE inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide practical, tractable information-theoretic diagnostics for resource-aware sparse MoEs by constraining the model to a finite candidate set and enabling closed-form estimators of key quantities, linking routing decisions to generalization and efficiency.

Method: Construct a finite-bank MNIST experiment with pretrained CNN experts and a discrete, data-dependent selection rule. Treat the gate as a stochastic channel and estimate I(X;T). Use a closed-form estimator for I(S;W) from the discrete posterior q(W|S) since S selects from a finite candidate W. Sweep a data-dependence parameter α to observe how I(S;W) tracks generalization. Compare Xu–Raginsky bound and a uniform-union bound baseline. Provide an empirical estimator of I(X;T) and apply a Blahut–Arimoto procedure to trace an accuracy-rate curve over the expert bank.

Result: I(S;W) monotonically tracks the generalization gap as α varies; the Xu–Raginsky bound is loose (as expected). The uniform union-bound baseline is reported for comparison. An empirical estimator of I(X;T) is developed together with a Blahut–Arimoto method to map accuracy versus routing rate across the expert bank.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical tool for analyzing resource-aware MoE inference systems and supports interpreting I(X;T) and D(R_g) as design proxies for efficient expert routing.

Abstract: Resource-efficient machine learning increasingly uses sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, where the gate acts as both a learning component and a routing interface controlling computation, communication, and accuracy. Motivated by finite-rate interpretations of MoE gating, we treat the gate as a stochastic channel and use $I(X;T)$ to quantify the routing information available to the selected expert. To make the associated information quantities tractable beyond synthetic examples, we develop a finite-bank MNIST construction using pretrained CNN experts and a discrete, data-dependent selection rule. Since the selected model belongs to a finite candidate set, the algorithmic mutual information $I(S;W)$ admits a closed-form discrete-entropy estimator from the empirical posterior $q(W|S)$. Sweeping a data-dependence parameter $α$, we observe that $\widehat I(S;W)$ monotonically tracks the generalization gap, while the Xu-Raginsky bound exhibits the expected looseness. We also compare with a uniform union-bound baseline and introduce an empirical estimator of $I(X;T)$ together with a Blahut-Arimoto procedure for tracing an accuracy-rate curve over the expert bank. The proposed framework provides a practical tool for analyzing resource-aware MoE inference systems and for interpreting $I(X;T)$ and $D(R_g)$ as design proxies for efficient expert routing.

</details>


### [132] [Forecasting Green Skill Demand in the Automotive Industry: Evidence from Online Job Postings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05280)
*Sabur Butt,Joshua N. Arrazola E.,Hector G. Ceballos,Patricia Caratozzolo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A computational framework identifies and forecasts green skill demand from online job postings in Mexico's automotive sector; uses multilingual embeddings and ESCO validation to detect 274 green skills from 8,576 occurrences; transformer-based time-series models forecast demand with high accuracy; classifies skills by growth; current demand focuses on sustainability practices; fastest growth in renewable energy, recycling, and hydrogen technologies; enables data-driven workforce planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As economies transition to sustainability, labor-market tools for identifying and forecasting green skills are limited; this study provides scalable, data-driven measurement and forecasting using online job postings in a key industry and ESCO validation.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) multilingual embeddings plus ESCO validation to extract green skills from job ads; (2) 15 time-series models with rolling-origin evaluation; classification framework for absolute/relative growth; dataset from Indeed Mexico, OCC Mundial, LinkedIn (July 2024–July 2025).

Result: Identified 274 green skills across 8,576 occurrences (4.22% of all skills); best forecast models are transformer-based (FEDformer, Reformer, Informer) with MAE ~ 2.5e-5 and relative RMSE < 15; framework categorizes skills as stable, emerging, high-impact; current demand centered on operational sustainability; fastest growth in renewable energy, recycling, hydrogen.

Conclusion: The pipeline enables data-driven workforce planning to support the green transition in the automotive sector and similar contexts.

Abstract: The global transition toward sustainable economies is reshaping labor markets, yet systematic methods for identifying and forecasting green skills remain limited. This study presents a computational framework to measure and predict green skill demand using online job postings from Mexico's automotive industry, which contributes about 4% of national GDP. We compile a dataset of job advertisements from Indeed Mexico, OCC Mundial, and LinkedIn (July 2024 to July 2025), yielding 204,373 skill records. A two-stage pipeline combining multilingual embeddings and ESCO validation identifies 274 unique green skills across 8,576 occurrences (4.22% of all skills). We benchmark 15 time series forecasting models using a rolling origin evaluation. Transformer-based models, especially FEDformer, Reformer, and Informer, achieve the best performance, with MAE around 2.5e-5 and relative RMSE below 15. We further propose a framework to classify skills by absolute and relative growth, identifying stable, emerging, and high-impact competencies. Results show current demand is concentrated in operational sustainability practices, while the fastest-growing skills relate to renewable energy, recycling, and hydrogen technologies. This pipeline supports data-driven workforce planning in the green transition.

</details>


### [133] [Attribution-Guided Continual Learning for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05285)
*Yazheng Liu,Yuxuan Wan,Rui Xu,Xi Zhang,Sihong Xie,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An attribution-guided fine-tuning framework estimates per-parameter, per-layer task importance to modulate gradients, reducing catastrophic forgetting in LLMs during continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in sequential task learning with LLMs; existing strategies (replay, freezing, regularization) ignore the semantic distribution of internal knowledge, limiting which parameters should be preserved versus updated.

Method: Estimate task-specific, element-wise parameter importance for each Transformer layer (via attribution) and use these scores to modulate gradients during fine-tuning—high-importance parameters receive smaller updates while low-importance ones remain plastic.

Result: Empirical results on continual learning benchmarks show consistent improvement over baselines, achieving better retention of old tasks while maintaining competitive performance on new tasks.

Conclusion: Attribution-guided gradient modulation creates a semantic-aware balance between stability and plasticity, enabling more effective continual learning in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often suffer from catastrophic forgetting in continual learning: after learning new tasks sequentially, they perform worse on earlier tasks. Existing methods mitigate catastrophic forgetting by data replay, parameter freezing, or regularization. However, these methods lack semantic awareness of internal knowledge distribution in LLMs. As a result, they cannot distinguish parameters that should be preserved or updated. We propose an attribution-guided continual fine-tuning framework for LLMs. Our method estimates task-specific, element-wise parameter importance in each Transformer layer and uses these scores to modulate gradients. Parameters important to previous tasks receive smaller updates, while less relevant ones remain plastic for learning new tasks. Experiments on continual learning benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms baselines, achieving better retention of old tasks while maintaining competitive performance on new tasks.

</details>


### [134] [Graph Normalization: Fast Binarizing Dynamics for Differentiable MWIS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05330)
*Laurent Guigues*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph Normalization (GN) is a differentiable dynamical system on graphs that exactly converges to a maximum weight independent set (MWIS) by embedding the problem in a Majorization-Minimization framework and linking it to replicator dynamics; it serves as a fast binarization engine and extends to assignment problems via a Sinkhorn-like process, enabling differentiable hard decisions in structured optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, differentiable engine for solving NP-hard MWIS and related combinatorial problems, enabling integration with deep learning and differentiable optimization.

Method: GN defines a dynamical system on graph nodes, performs an exact Majorization-Minimization step for MWIS, and shows equivalence to nonlinear replicator dynamics; establishes a weighted Motzkin-Straus generalization; extends to assignment via a Sinkhorn-like process; demonstrates convergence to binary MIS and practical performance on large graphs.

Result: GN converges to a binary MIS indicator, acts as a fast quasi-Newton descent improving the relaxed primal objective, and can be used as a binarization engine for MWIS solvers; achieves near-best solutions on real-world graphs up to 1M edges in seconds on CPU; offers a differentiable framework for hard decisions with constraints.

Conclusion: GN provides a theoretically grounded, efficient, differentiable method for exact MIS identification and related constrained optimization tasks, with broad applicability in deep learning, structured optimization, and resource allocation.

Abstract: We introduce Graph Normalization (GN), a principled dynamical system on graphs that serves as a differentiable approximation engine for the NP-hard Maximum Weight Independent Set (MWIS) problem. MWIS encompasses many combinatorial challenges, including optimal assignment, scheduling, set packing, and MAP inference in discrete Markov Random Fields. Unlike Belief Propagation, we prove GN always converges to a binary indicator of a Maximum Independent Set. GN realizes a fast quasi-Newton descent through an exact Majorization-Minimization step, systematically improving the MWIS relaxed primal objective. We establish an equivalence between GN and the Replicator Dynamics of a nonlinear evolutionary game, where vertices compete for inclusion in an independent set. While a non-potential game, the GN game follows Fisher's Fundamental Theorem of Natural Selection, where the average fitness equals the MWIS primal objective and strictly increases. This connection leads to a weighted extension of the Motzkin-Straus theorem, showing MISes are in bijection with the local minima of a quadratic form over a tilted simplex. For the Assignment Problem, GN acts as a variant of the Sinkhorn algorithm that naturally converges to a hard assignment while generalizing to arbitrary constraint graphs. We demonstrate GN's performance as a fast binarization engine for the state-of-the-art Bregman-Sinkhorn relaxed MWIS solver. On real-world benchmarks with up to 1M edges, GN identifies solutions within 1% of the best known results in seconds on a CPU. GN opens new avenues for deep learning architectures requiring differentiable, "hard" decisions under constraints, with applications in structured sparse attention, dynamic network pruning, and Mixture-of-Experts. Beyond core AI, the GN framework enables end-to-end learning of constrained optimization in computer vision, computational biology, and resource allocation.

</details>


### [135] [Feature Starvation as Geometric Instability in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05341)
*Faris Chaudhry,Keisuke Yano,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive elastic net sparse autoencoders (AEN-SAEs) solve feature starvation and shrinkage bias in overcomplete, l1-regularized SAEs by adding an l2 structural term and adaptive l1 reweighting, yielding a Lipschitz, differentiable sparse coding map with global feature support recovery; demonstrated on synthetic data and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature starvation (dead neurons) and shrinkage bias in standard l1-regularized SAEs arise from optimization-geometric issues in overcomplete dictionaries; a stable, differentiable architecture is needed to align sparse coding with shallow encoders.

Method: Propose adaptive elastic net SAEs (AEN-SAEs) combining an l2 structural term to enforce strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive l1 reweighting to reduce shrinkage bias and suppress spurious features; provide theoretical results showing Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and global support recovery under mild assumptions; empirically validate on synthetic data and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B).

Result: AEN-SAEs mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while preserving competitive reconstruction performance.

Conclusion: The adaptive elastic net framework yields a stable, differentiable sparse coding mechanism for overcomplete dictionaries, addressing fundamental issues in l1-based sparse coding, with theoretical guarantees and empirical effectiveness on LLM representations.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are used to disentangle the dense, polysemantic internal representations of large language models (LLMs) into interpretable, monosemantic concepts. However, standard $\ell_1$-regularized SAEs suffer from feature starvation (dead neurons) and shrinkage bias, often requiring computationally expensive heuristic resampling and nondifferentiable hard-masking methods to bypass these challenges. We argue that feature starvation is not merely an empirical artifact of poor data diversity, but a fundamental optimization-geometric pathology of overcomplete dictionaries: the $\ell_1$-induced sparse coding map is unstable and fundamentally misaligned with shallow, amortized encoders. To address this structural instability, we introduce adaptive elastic net SAEs (AEN-SAEs), a fully differentiable architecture grounded in classical sparse regression. AEN-SAEs combine an $\ell_2$ structural term that enforces strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive $\ell_1$ reweighting that eliminates shrinkage bias and suppresses spurious features, thereby jointly controlling the curvature and interaction structure of the induced polyhedral geometry. Theoretically, we show that AEN-SAEs yield a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and recover the global feature support under mild assumptions. Empirically, across synthetic settings and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B), AEN-SAEs mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while maintaining competitive reconstruction abilities.

</details>


### [136] [Balancing Stability and Plasticity in Sequentially Trained Early-Exiting Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05358)
*Alaa Zniber,Ouassim Karrakchou,Mounir Ghogho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method for incremental training of early-exit neural networks that mitigates interference from new exits by regularizing important weights (EW C) and preserving earlier exits' outputs (LwF), improving accuracy and enabling faster inference at low budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address interference and catastrophic forgetting when progressively adding exit classifiers to a shared backbone, preserving performance of earlier exits while enabling new exits.

Method: Two continual-learning-inspired strategies applied during incremental exit addition: (1) Elastic Weight Consolidation (EW C) to protect weights crucial for existing exits; (2) Learning without Forgetting (LwF) to preserve the output distributions of earlier exits by distillation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on standard benchmarks shows consistent gains in early-exit accuracy over sequential training baselines and substantial computational speedups at low budgets.

Conclusion: The approaches effectively balance stability and plasticity in incremental exit networks, enabling better early-exit performance and efficient inference by mitigating interference among exits.

Abstract: Early-exiting neural networks enable adaptive inference by allowing inputs to exit at intermediate classifiers, reducing computation for easy samples while maintaining high accuracy. In practice, exits can be trained sequentially by incrementally adding them to a shared backbone; however, this sequential training can cause newly introduced exits to interfere with previously learned ones, degrading the performance of earlier classifiers. We address this problem by retaining the knowledge embedded in existing exits while allowing new ones to specialize. We propose two alternative approaches that operate at different levels of the model. The first constrains learning by protecting parameters that are important for previously trained exits, while the second preserves the output distributions of earlier exits as the network adapts. These alternatives directly reflect the stability-plasticity trade-off studied in continual learning. Accordingly, we leverage \textit{Elastic Weight Consolidation} to constrain critical weights and \textit{Learning without Forgetting} to preserve output distributions. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that our approaches consistently improve early-exit performance, achieving higher accuracy over existing sequential training methods and significant performance speedups at low computational budgets.

</details>


### [137] [COPYCOP: Ownership Verification for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05360)
*Rahul Nandakumar,Deepayan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CopyCop detects copycat GNNs even when adversaries align architectures, embed dimensions, and apply output transformations; provides theoretical guarantees and strong empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two GNNs could be trained independently but one adversary tries to imitate the other's embeddings. Existing watermarking/fingerprinting methods are insufficient; a robust detector is needed that tolerates architecture/weight/embedding differences and embedding transformations.

Method: Propose CopyCop algorithm with theoretical guarantees. Handles differing architectures, weights, and embedding dimensions; robust to adversarial transformations of embeddings; validated on 14 datasets and 5 GNN architectures; code released.

Result: CopyCop achieves high accuracy and robustness across a broad class of attacks and transformations; outperforms existing watermarking and fingerprinting approaches.

Conclusion: CopyCop provides a reliable, theory-backed solution for graph ownership verification, with strong empirical support and practical availability of code.

Abstract: Given two GNNs that output node embeddings, how can we determine if they were trained independently? An adversary could have trained one GNN specifically to mimic the other GNN's embeddings. To obscure this relationship between the GNNs, the adversarial GNN might then transform its output embeddings. The two GNNs could have different architectures, weights, and embedding dimensions, and the adversary can transform the embeddings. Despite these stringent conditions, our algorithm (named CopyCop) can identify such copycat GNNs, unlike existing watermarking and fingerprinting methods. We also provide theoretical guarantees for CopyCop. Finally, experiments on 14 datasets and 5 GNN architectures demonstrate that CopyCop is accurate and robust against a broad class of adversarial attacks and transformations. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/CopyCop-Graph-Ownership-Verification-8143/README.md

</details>


### [138] [SPADE: Faster Drug Discovery by Learning from Sparse Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05370)
*Rahul Nandakumar,Ben Fauber,Deepayan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPADE is a data-efficient ligand selection algorithm for drug discovery that finds 10 high-quality ligands with about 40 tests on average, outperforming deep learning and Bayesian optimization baselines in one-vs-one comparisons across multiple proteins, and scoring candidates roughly 10x faster than the nearest competitor. Dataset and code are provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drug discovery is expensive and data-scarce, especially for novel proteins with no prior data. An algorithm that reliably identifies high-quality ligands with minimal experiments can accelerate early-stage discovery.

Method: Introducing SPADE, a novel approach that learns from sparse data to guide iterative ligand selection (one-vs-one comparisons). The abstract emphasizes data efficiency and speed but does not provide detailed algorithmic steps.

Result: SPADE achieves median improvements of 7–32% in sample efficiency across more proteins in one-vs-one tests, requires ~40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands, and scores candidate drugs about 10x faster than its closest competitor.

Conclusion: SPADE presents a practical, data-efficient solution for ligand discovery under data scarcity and for novel targets. The availability of code and data supports reproducibility, though further validation across broader targets and detailed ablation studies would strengthen the claims.

Abstract: Drug discovery seeks molecules (ligands) that bind strongly and selectively to a target protein. However, fewer than 5% of candidate ligands pass the bar for even the early stages of drug discovery. Furthermore, we want methods that work for novel proteins for which we have no prior data. Starting from scratch, we have to iteratively select and test candidate ligands such that we find enough ligands of the desired quality in as few tests as possible. Our proposed algorithm, named SPADE, introduces a novel approach to ligand selection that requires only 40 tests on average to find 10 high-quality ligands. In one-vs-one comparisons, SPADE outperforms deep learning and Bayesian optimization methods on more proteins, achieving median improvements of 7%-32% in sample efficiency. SPADE is also 10x faster than its closest competitor at scoring candidate drugs. Dataset and code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SPADE_Fast_Drug_Discovery_by_Learning_from_Sparse_Data-F028/README.md

</details>


### [139] [Neural Co-state Policies: Structuring Hidden States in Recurrent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05373)
*David Leeftink,Max Hinne,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recurrent policies under partial observability align with Pontryagin co-states; a PMP-based co-state loss yields interpretable, robust control with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpret latent dynamics of memory-based RL under partial observability and provide a principled, robust design via optimal-control theory (PMP).

Method: Demonstrate that latent states in standard RNNs map to PMP co-states; readout performs Hamiltonian minimization; introduce a PMP-derived co-state loss; evaluate on partially observable DMControl tasks with sensor masking to test robustness.

Result: Achieves comparable or improved performance relative to baselines and shows robustness to zero-shot sensor masking; offers an interpretable dynamical perspective by linking RNNs to PMP.

Conclusion: Framing recurrent networks as PMP-governed dynamical systems enables principled, robust design for continuous control under partial observability.

Abstract: A key capability of intelligent agents is operating under partial observability: reasoning and acting effectively despite missing or incomplete state observations. While recurrent (memory-based) policies learned via reinforcement learning address this by encoding history into latent state representations, their internal dynamics remain uninterpretable black boxes. This paper establishes a formal link between these hidden states and the Pontryagin minimum principle (PMP) from optimal control. We demonstrate that for standard recurrent architectures, latent representations map directly to PMP co-states, which allows the readout layer to be interpreted as performing Hamiltonian minimization. Because standard reward maximization does not naturally discover this alignment, we introduce a PMP-derived co-state loss to explicitly structure the internal dynamics. Empirically, this approach matches or improves performance on partially observable DMControl tasks, and is robust against zero-shot out-of-distribution sensor masking. By framing recurrent networks as dynamic processes governed by the minimum principle, we provide a principled approach to designing robust continuous control policies.

</details>


### [140] [Conditional Diffusion Under Linear Constraints: Langevin Mixing and Information-Theoretic Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05387)
*Ahmad Aghapour,Erhan Bayraktar,Asaf Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theory and method for zero-shot conditional sampling with diffusion models in linear inverse problems; introduces a normal-tangent score decomposition, a dimension-free bound via conditional mutual information, and a projected-Langevin init with guided reverse denoising that improves inpainting and super-resolution over projection-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Projection-based samplers enforce measurement consistency but do not fix how probability mass is distributed along feasible directions, leading to biased conditional samples in underdetermined inverse problems like inpainting and super-resolution.

Method: Normal-tangent decomposition of the score under Gaussian noise: the observed-direction score is determined by the measurement, while the tangent conditional score is unknown. Proves that replacing the tangent score with the unconditional tangent score incurs error bounded by a dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. Decomposes total error into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch terms. Proposes a practical algorithm: projected-Langevin initialization followed by guided reverse denoising.

Result: The authors derive a dimension-free bound on the conditional sampling error and demonstrate, via experiments on inpainting and super-resolution, that their projected-Langevin initialization with guided reverse denoising outperforms a strong projection-based baseline.

Conclusion: There is a principled separation between initialization and pathwise score-mismatch in conditional diffusion sampling for linear inverse problems; the proposed method leverages this theory to achieve more faithful conditional samples in practice.

Abstract: We study zero-shot conditional sampling with pretrained diffusion models for linear inverse problems, including inpainting and super-resolution. In these problems, the observation determines only part of the unknown signal. The remaining degrees of freedom must be sampled according to the correct conditional data distribution. Existing projection-based samplers enforce measurement consistency by correcting the observed component during reverse diffusion. However, measurement consistency alone does not determine how probability mass should be distributed along the feasible set, and this can lead to biased conditional samples.
  We analyze this issue through a normal--tangent decomposition of the score function. For Gaussian noising, the observed-direction score is exactly determined by the measurement; only the tangent conditional score is unknown. We prove that the error from replacing this score by the unconditional tangent score is upper bounded by a dimension-free conditional mutual information between observed and unobserved components. This gives an information-theoretic decomposition into initialization and pathwise score-mismatch errors. Motivated by the theory, we propose a projected-Langevin initialization followed by guided reverse denoising, which outperforms a strong projection-based baseline in inpainting and super-resolution experiments.

</details>


### [141] [Two-Stage Learned Decomposition for Scalable Routing on Multigraphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05389)
*Filip Rydin,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani,Balázs Kulcsár*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) to tackle multigraph VRPs by decomposing routing into node permutation and edge selection stages, with pre-encoding edge aggregation and a non-autoregressive edge stage, trained via hierarchical RL; achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results with significant speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and limitations of existing neural VRP methods on multigraphs, where parallel edges represent diverse travel options. Aims to efficiently utilize multigraph representations while maintaining solution quality.

Method: Proposes Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) that splits the routing policy into a node permutation stage and an edge selection stage. Introduces a pre-encoding edge aggregation to enable decomposition, a non-autoregressive architecture for the edge stage, and hierarchical reinforcement learning to train the stages jointly.

Result: Empirical evaluation across six VRP variants shows NEPF matches or surpasses state-of-the-art in solution quality, while significantly reducing training and inference time.

Conclusion: NEPF provides a scalable and efficient framework for multigraph VRPs by factorizing policy across nodes and edges, with architectural and training innovations enabling faster yet high-quality solutions.

Abstract: Most neural methods for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs) are limited to Euclidean settings or simple graphs. In this work, we instead consider multigraphs, where parallel edges represent distinct travel options with varying trade-offs (e.g., distance vs time). Few methods are designed for such formulations and those that do exist face major scalability issues. We mitigate these scalability issues via a Node-Edge Policy Factorization (NEPF) approach, which splits the routing policy into a node permutation stage and an edge selection stage. To enable the decomposition, we introduce a pre-encoding edge aggregation scheme and a non-autoregressive architecture for the edge stage, as well as a hierarchical reinforcement learning method to train the stages jointly. Our experiments across six VRP variants demonstrate that NEPF matches or outperforms the state-of-the-art in terms of solution quality, while being significantly faster in training and inference.

</details>


### [142] [Differentiable Parameter Optimization for DAEs with State-Dependent Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05395)
*Ion Matei,Maksym Zhenirovskyy,Anthony Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two gradient methods for learning in semi-explicit DAEs with state-dependent events: automatic differentiation through simulation and an explicit discrete adjoint, both yielding gradients for the forward-selected event path under fixed event ordering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges of differentiable parameter learning in DAEs with state-dependent events due to implicit algebraic variables, event-time dependence on parameters, and resets.

Method: 1) AD-through-simulation: solve algebraic variables inside vector field; differentiate the algebraic solve via implicit function theorem; segmented differentiable integration to handle events. 2) Explicit discrete adjoint: forward simulation as event-split residual system; compute gradients by solving for Lagrange multipliers of smooth and event residuals; residuals interpreted as equality constraints.

Result: Both methods yield gradients for the event path selected by forward simulation; valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings; provide complementary perspectives on gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity.

Conclusion: The residual-formulation clarifies equality-constraint nature of adjoint residuals; the two strategies offer complementary tools for gradient-based learning in DAEs with events; choice depends on desired interpretation and computational trade-offs.

Abstract: Differential-algebraic equations (DAEs) with state-dependent events arise in systems whose continuous dynamics are constrained by algebraic equations and interrupted by mode changes, switching logic, impacts, or state reinitializations. Gradient-based parameter learning for such systems is challenging because algebraic variables are implicitly defined, event times depend on the parameters, and reset maps introduce discontinuities. This paper studies differentiable parameter optimization for semi-explicit DAEs with events. We formulate the learning problem as a constrained least-squares problem with DAE dynamics, algebraic constraints, guard equations, and reset maps. We then develop two complementary gradient-computation strategies. The first is an automatic-differentiation-through-simulation method that solves algebraic variables inside the vector field, differentiates the algebraic solve using the implicit function theorem, and handles events through segmented differentiable integration. The second is an explicit discrete-adjoint method that represents the forward simulation as an event-split residual system and computes gradients by solving for the Lagrange multipliers of smooth-segment and event residuals. The formulation clarifies that residual terms in the adjoint method are equality constraints, not heuristic penalties. We compare the two approaches in terms of gradient interpretation, event-time handling, implementation complexity, and local validity. Both methods provide gradients for the event path selected by the forward simulation and are valid under fixed event ordering and transversal guard crossings.

</details>


### [143] [Information Theoretic Adversarial Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05415)
*Yiwei Zhang,Jeremiah Birrell,Reza Ebrahimi,Rouzbeh Behnia,Jason Pacheco,Elisa Bertino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WARDEN is a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs that uses an f-divergence ambiguity set around the training distribution to reweight adversarial examples, converging to a log-sum-exp objective under KL divergence to reduce attack success while preserving utility, with costs comparable to CAT/CAPO/MixAT baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial prompting remains a vulnerability in LLMs despite alignment/safety progress. Existing robust methods (CAT, CAPO) improve robustness but are computationally expensive and hard to scale. A scalable, principled approach is needed to emphasize harder adversarial examples without excessive compute.

Method: Formulates a worst-case adversarial loss within an f-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Uses convex duality to transform the objective into a log-sum-exp form (under KL divergence), with a dynamical parameter controlling the reweighting strength. Optimizes a distributionally robust objective during training, and evaluates against different LLMs and attack settings against CAT-, CAPO-, and MixAT baselines.

Result: WARDEN substantially reduces attack success rates across multiple LLMs and attack scenarios, while incurring similar computational and utility costs to existing baselines (CAT, CAPO, MixAT). Demonstrates practicality for scalable robust alignment.

Conclusion: A practical, scalable robust alignment method: distributionally robust adversarial training with adaptive reweighting yields strong defense against adversarial prompts without sacrificing model utility, aligning with or surpassing state-of-the-art baselines.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to adversarial prompting despite advances in alignment and safety, often exhibiting harmful behaviors under novel attack strategies. While adversarial training can improve robustness, existing approaches are computationally expensive and difficult to scale. Recent continuous adversarial training methods, such as Continuous adversarial training (CAT) and Continuous Adversarial Preference Optimization (CAPO), address this challenge by leveraging gradient-based perturbations in the embedding space, enabling more efficient and expressive attacks. Building on this paradigm, we propose WARDEN, a distributionally robust adversarial training framework for LLMs that dynamically reweights adversarial examples through an f -divergence ambiguity set around the empirical training distribution. Our method optimizes the worst-case adversarial loss within a divergence ball around the empirical data distribution, automatically emphasizing harder adversarial examples. Using the convex dual formulation, the objective reduces to a log-sum-exp form under the KL divergence, with a dynamical parameter controlling the strength of reweighting. This study leads to a new class of information-theoretic objectives that significantly reduce attack success rates while maintaining model utility. Across multiple LLMs and attack settings, WARDEN substantially reduces attack success rates with computational and utility costs comparable to CAT-, CAPO-, and MixAT-based baselines, making it a practical approach for scalable robust alignment.

</details>


### [144] [Active Learning for Conditional Generative Compressed Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05435)
*Alexander DeLise,Nick Dexter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conditional generative compressed sensing with prompt-conditioned sampling and recovery; stability bounds for ReLU/Lipschitz generators; prompt-matched Christoffel sampling preserves Christoffel complexity, while prompt mismatch incurs a compatibility penalty; experiments with Stable Diffusion show prompts reshape sampling distributions and influence recovery; prompts act as design variables affecting sensing, approximation, and recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend generative compressed sensing to a conditional setting where prompts govern both the sensing distribution and the recovery model, enabling explicit control over sampling and reconstruction for subsampled Fourier measurements.

Method: Theoretical analysis for ReLU and Lipschitz conditional generators to derive stable recovery bounds; study of Christoffel sampling under prompt matching/mismatch; empirical validation using Stable Diffusion to modulate the sampling distribution via prompts.

Result: Proves stability bounds showing prompt-matched sampling maintains the same Christoffel complexity constant as in near-optimal theory; prompt mismatch introduces a compatibility penalty; experiments demonstrate that prompts meaningfully reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and affect image recovery.

Conclusion: Prompts should be treated as distinct design variables with separate effects on sensing, approximation, and recovery; careful prompt design can influence both the sampling process and reconstruction quality in conditional generative compressed sensing.

Abstract: Generative compressed sensing uses the range of a pretrained generator as a nonlinear model for recovering structured signals from limited measurements. We study a conditional version of this problem for image recovery from subsampled Fourier measurements using prompt-conditioned generative models. Our framework separates two roles of conditioning: the prompt used to design the sampling distribution and the prompt used to define the recovery model. For ReLU and Lipschitz conditional generators, we prove stable recovery bounds showing that prompt-matched Christoffel sampling retains the same Christoffel complexity constant as existing near-optimal generative compressed sensing theory, while prompt mismatch incurs an explicit compatibility penalty. Experiments with Stable Diffusion show that prompts meaningfully reshape Christoffel sampling distributions and influence image recovery. Overall, our results suggest that prompts should be treated as design variables with distinct effects on sensing, approximation, and recovery.

</details>


### [145] [Adaptive Q-Chunking for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05544)
*Nandiraju Gireesh,Yuanliang Ju,He Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC) selects action chunk sizes adaptively in offline-to-online RL by comparing chunk-size advantages relative to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by the discount factor, enabling unbiased decisions when signals are weak and better planning when a scale helps. Demonstrates state-of-the-art results on OGBench, Robomimic, and RoboCasa-GR1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed chunk sizes in offline-to-online RL introduce a bias toward the smallest chunk and fail to capture context-dependent planning needs (reactive control vs long-horizon credit assignment). A per-horizon baseline with discount normalization aims to provide unbiased, discriminative selection of chunk sizes.

Method: Train critics for multiple chunk sizes; compute the advantage of each chunk size relative to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by the discount factor; select the chunk size with the highest (normalized) advantage at each state; provide theoretical bounds on noise immunity and on value dominance; empirically validate on standard benchmarks and large-scale action-sequence models.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art offline and online performance on OGBench and Robomimic; improves RoboCasa-GR1 tasks when integrated with large-scale VLA models; provides theoretical guarantees for noise immunity and dominance of adaptive chunking over fixed chunk sizes.

Conclusion: AQC resolves bias and noise issues in adaptive chunking by using a per-horizon baseline and discount normalization, enabling near-random choices when no signal exists and discriminative decisions when a particular chunk size offers planning benefits; demonstrates strong empirical performance and theoretical properties, with broad applicability to RL with action sequences.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning with action chunking eliminates multi-step off-policy bias and enables temporally coherent exploration, but all existing methods use a fixed chunk size across every state. This is suboptimal: near contact events the agent needs short chunks for reactive control, while during free-space motion long chunks provide better credit assignment. The natural solution is to train critics for several chunk sizes and select the best one at each state, but naive comparison of learned critic values systematically collapses to the shortest chunk due to discount-scale mismatch, and degrades to noise in low-value states. We propose Adaptive Q-Chunking (AQC), which resolves both failures by comparing the advantage of each chunk size relative to a per-horizon baseline, normalized by the discount factor. This criterion converts biased wrong answers into unbiased near-random choices when no genuine signal exists, and becomes discriminative when a particular scale enables better planning. We prove theoretical bounds on the advantage selector's noise immunity and on the value dominance of adaptive chunking over any fixed chunk size. We demonstrate that AQC achieves state-of-the-art offline and online success rates on OGBench and Robomimic, and can be applied to enhance the performance of large-scale VLA models that predict action sequences, significantly boosting performance on RoboCasa-GR1 tasks.

</details>


### [146] [On Semantic Loss Fine-Tuning Approach for Preventing Model Collapse in Causal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05438)
*Pratik Deshmukh,Atirek Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning transformers on causal reasoning tasks without semantic constraints causes catastrophic collapse (trivial Yes/No solutions); a semantic loss with graph-based constraints and dynamic lambda prevents collapse, yielding stable, context-dependent reasoning and strong gains on transitivity and d-separation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle catastrophic collapse during fine-tuning for causal reasoning and preserve genuine reasoning capabilities by enforcing semantic structure.

Method: Introduce a semantic loss function built on graph-based logical constraints with a dynamic lambda schedule during training; compare against standard fine-tuning; evaluate on transitivity and d-separation tasks; conduct adversarial evaluation on 1,000 samples; benchmark across five model variants on 200k+ evaluation samples.

Result: Semantic loss achieves stable context-dependent predictions: 70.4% accuracy on transitivity and 68.6% on d-separation; ~42.7% improvement over collapsed baselines; adversarial evaluation shows 67–70% accuracy for semantic models vs 43–71% for collapsed; broad benchmarking across >200k samples and five model variants supports necessity of semantic loss for stable causal reasoning.

Conclusion: Semantic loss with graph-based constraints and dynamic scheduling is essential, not optional, for enabling robust, context-aware causal reasoning in transformer models.

Abstract: Standard fine-tuning of transformer models on causal reasoning tasks leads to catastrophic model collapse, where models learn trivial solutions such as always predicting "Yes" or "No" regardless of input structure. We demonstrate that fine-tuning Gemma 270M on transitivity and d-separation tasks without semantic loss results in 100% collapse rate, with models achieving misleadingly high accuracy (73.9%) while learning no causal reasoning. We propose a semantic loss function with graph-based logical constraints and dynamic lambda scheduling that prevents this collapse. Our approach achieves 70.4% accuracy on transitivity tasks and 68.6% on d-separation tasks with stable, context-dependent predictions, representing a 42.7% improvement over collapsed baselines. Adversarial evaluation on 1,000 structural reasoning samples shows semantic models achieve 67-70% accuracy while collapsed models fail catastrophically at 43-71%. We validate our findings through comprehensive benchmarking on 200,000+ evaluation samples across five model variants, demonstrating that semantic loss is essential and not optional, for stable causal reasoning in transformers.

</details>


### [147] [Robustness of Graph Self-Supervised Learning to Real-World Noise: A Case Study on Text-Driven Biomedical Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05463)
*Othmane Kabal,Mounira Harzallah,Fabrice Guillet,Hideaki Takeda,Ryutaro Ichise*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NATD-GSSL provides a unified framework to evaluate GSSL on noisy text-driven graphs; robustness depends on task and GNN; offers practical guidelines and shows up to ~7% gains over LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the prevalence of noise in automatically extracted knowledge graphs from text and the lack of comprehensive robustness studies for GSSL under such noise; aims to evaluate and guide GSSL deployment on real-world, noisy text-driven graphs.

Method: Proposes NATD-GSSL, combining automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and graph self-supervised learning. Uses a dual-graph protocol comparing a noisy MedMentions-derived graph with a clean UMLS reference, aligned by a gold standard. Evaluates across pretext tasks (relation reconstruction, feature reconstruction, contrastive objectives) and multiple GNN architectures, emphasizing bidirectional vs unidirectional relational message passing.

Result: Robustness to noise varies across tasks and architectures: relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from clear schemas; feature reconstruction is more robust and can approach clean-graph performance; contrastive objectives are relatively noise-tolerant but depend on downstream task alignment. GNN choice matters: bidirectional relational message passing fares better on noisy graphs, while unidirectional performs best on clean graphs. NATD-GSSL yields up to 7% improvement over pretrained language model baselines.

Conclusion: NATD-GSSL offers practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs and provides benchmarks and code to facilitate robust graph-based NLP on noisy, text-driven graphs.

Abstract: Graph Self-Supervised Learning (GSSL) offers a powerful paradigm for learning graph representations without labeled data. However, existing work assumes clean, manually curated graphs. Recent advances in NLP enable the large-scale automatic extraction of knowledge graphs from text, opening new opportunities for GSSL while introducing substantial real-world noise. This type of noise remains largely unexplored, as prior robustness studies typically rely on synthetic perturbations. To address this gap, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of GSSL methods on text-driven graphs for unsupervised term typing. We introduce Noise-Aware Text-Driven Graph GSSL (NATD-GSSL), a unified framework that combines automatic graph construction, graph refinement, and GSSL. Our evaluation follows a dual-graph protocol that contrasts a noisy graph derived from MedMentions with a clean Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) reference graph, aligned through a shared gold standard. Our results reveal variability in robustness across both pretext tasks and Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures. Relation reconstruction is highly sensitive to noise and benefits from well-defined schemas, whereas feature reconstruction is considerably more robust, achieving performance comparable to clean-graph settings. Contrastive objectives are generally less affected by noise but depend strongly on alignment with downstream tasks. GNN architecture also plays a critical role: bidirectional relational message-passing designs are better suited to noisy, text-driven graphs, while unidirectional relational ones perform best on clean graphs. Overall, NATD-GSSL provides practical guidance for applying GSSL to real-world, noisy graphs and achieves up to a 7\% improvement over pretrained language model baselines. All code and benchmarks are publicly available at https://github.com/OthmaneKabal/MC2GAE.

</details>


### [148] [A Unified Benchmark for Evaluating Knowledge Graph Construction Methods and Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05476)
*Othmane Kabal,Mounira Harzallah,Fabrice Guillet,Hideaki Takeda,Ryutaro Ichise*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-purpose benchmark for evaluating GNNs on noisy text-derived graphs and the quality of graph construction methods, using two automatic graphs and a high-quality reference graph from a single biomedical corpus, plus a reproducible evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how graph noise/fragmentation and construction quality impact GNN performance, separating model vs graph effects; enable controlled, reproducible benchmarking in biomedicine.

Method: From a single textual corpus, generate two automatically constructed graphs using distinct extraction methods; curate a high-quality expert reference graph; set up semi-supervised node classification tasks; provide standardized evaluation framework to compare methods and robustness.

Result: Showcase the benchmark's utility: enables controlled comparisons of construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness; provides upper-bound performance via the expert graph; enables extension with new methods/models.

Conclusion: The benchmark offers a standardized, reproducible, extensible platform for joint evaluation of graph construction and GNNs, facilitating robust research in biomedical knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs automatically constructed from text are increasingly used in real-world applications. However, their inherent noise, fragmentation, and semantic inconsistencies significantly affect the performance of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on downstream tasks. Assessing their performance and robustness remains difficult, as it is often unclear whether observed results stem from the learning model or from the quality of the constructed graph itself. In this work, we introduce a dual-purpose benchmark designed to jointly evaluate (i) the performance of GNNs on noisy, text-derived graphs and (ii) the effectiveness of graph construction methods on a downstream task. The benchmark is built in the biomedical domain from a single textual corpus and includes two automatically constructed graphs generated using different extraction methods, alongside a high-quality reference graph curated by experts that serves as an upper performance bound. This design enables controlled comparison of construction methods and systematic evaluation of GNN robustness through semi-supervised node classification. We further provide a standardized, reproducible, and extensible evaluation framework, facilitating the integration of new graph extraction methods and learning models.

</details>


### [149] [GRALIS: A Unified Canonical Framework for Linear Attribution Methods via Riesz Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05480)
*Raimondo Fanale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRALIS provides a canonical, representation-theory-based framework for attributions in deep nets, unifying SHAP, IG, LIME, and linearized GradCAM under a Riesz-representation, with seven formal guarantees and multi-scale extension; preliminary validation on histology data suggests improved faithfulness and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a formal, additive, linear, continuous attribution framework on L^2(Q, mu) that subsumes existing XAI methods, provides completeness, interaction values, sensitivity analysis, and scalable multi-scale aggregation.

Method: Define a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta) per the Riesz Representation Theorem for additive, linear, continuous attributions on L^2. Derive seven theorems guaranteeing completeness, convergence, Shapley interactions, Sobol generalization, and multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS). Provide algebraic justification via Mobius transform for GRALIS-SIV; implement and compare with baseline XAI methods; preliminary validation on BreaKHis (DenseNet-121) with histology images.

Result: GRALIS satisfies ~13.5/14 axioms against 2.5-6/14 for individual methods; guarantees exact completeness, Monte Carlo convergence, exact Shapley interaction values, Hoeffding ANOVA, Sobol generalization, multi-scale extension with minimum-variance weights. Preliminary metrics: deletion AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL 0.762±0.109, sparsity 0.39.

Conclusion: GRALIS offers a unified, axiomatic, scalable attribution framework that outperforms individual methods on several guarantees; a companion paper will provide extended empirical comparisons.

Abstract: The main XAI attribution methods for deep neural networks -- GradCAM, SHAP, LIME, Integrated Gradients -- operate on separate theoretical foundations and are not formally comparable. We present GRALIS (Gradient-Riesz Averaged Locally-Integrated Shapley), a mathematical framework establishing a representation theory for attributions: every additive, linear, and continuous attribution functional on L^2(Q,mu) admits a unique canonical representation (Q, w, Delta), proved necessary by the Riesz Representation Theorem. This class encompasses SHAP, IG, LIME and linearized GradCAM, but excludes nonlinear functionals such as standard GradCAM or attention maps. Seven formal theorems provide simultaneous guarantees absent in any individual method: (T1) necessary canonical form; (T2) exact completeness; (T3) Monte Carlo convergence O(1/sqrt(m))+O(1/k); (T4) exact Shapley Interaction Values; (T5) Hoeffding ANOVA decomposition; (T6) Sobol sensitivity generalization; (T7) multi-scale extension (MS-GRALIS) with minimum-variance weights. An algebraic appendix justifies the GRALIS-SIV correspondence via the Mobius transform without circularity. GRALIS satisfies 13.5/14 axiomatic properties vs. 2.5-6/14 for individual methods, including completeness, sensitivity, locality, order-k interactions and optimal multi-scale aggregation simultaneously. Preliminary validation on BreaKHis (1,187 histology images, DenseNet-121) reports deletion faithfulness AUC +0.015 (malignant), 96% class-conditional consistency, SAL = 0.762+/-0.109 and sparsity index 0.39. Extended comparison with baseline XAI methods is planned for a companion paper.

</details>


### [150] [Approximate Next Policy Sampling: Replacing Conservative Target Policy Updates in Deep RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05481)
*Dillon Sandhu,Ronald Parr*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS) to align training data with the next policy, via Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API) and Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), enabling safe, larger policy updates without conservatism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the 'chicken-and-egg' issue in RL where value functions must be accurate on the updated policy's state-visitation distribution, which is unknown and hard to sample. Conservative updates fix safety but slow learning; ANPS shifts distribution alignment to the training data.

Method: Introduce SV-API: keep target policy fixed while a behavioral policy collects data and only adopt a new target after convergence; provide safety guarantees under stability; if stability fails, updates are no less safe than standard API. Apply to PPO to obtain SV-PPO.

Result: SV-API enables stable policy updates; SV-PPO matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control tasks, while allowing significantly larger target policy updates.

Conclusion: ANPS is a viable new solution to safe policy improvement in RL, demonstrated by SV-API and SV-PPO achieving stable, effective learning.

Abstract: We revisit a classic "chicken-and-egg" problem in reinforcement learning: to safely improve a policy, the value function must be accurate on the state-visitation distribution of the updated policy. That distribution over states is unknown and cannot be sampled for the purposes of training the value function. Conservative updates solve this problem, but at the cost of shrinking the policy update. This paper explores an alternative solution, Approximate Next Policy Sampling (ANPS), which addresses the problem by modifying the training distribution rather than constraining the policy update. ANPS is satisfied if the distribution of the training data approximates that of the next policy. To demonstrate the feasibility and efficacy of ANPS, we introduce Stable Value Approximate Policy Iteration (SV-API). SV-API modifies the standard approximate policy iteration loop to hold the target policy fixed while an iteratively updated behavioral policy gathers relevant experience. It only commits to a new policy once a convergence criterion has been met. If certain stability criteria are met, the update is guaranteed to be safe; otherwise, it remains no less safe than standard approximate policy iteration. Applying SV-API to PPO yields Stable Value PPO (SV-PPO), which matches or improves performance on high-dimensional discrete (Atari) and continuous control benchmarks while executing substantially larger target policy updates. These results demonstrate the viability of ANPS as a new solution to this classic challenge in RL.

</details>


### [151] [Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06246)
*Jan-Hendrik Ewering,Kathrin Flaßkamp,Niklas Wahlström,Thomas B. Schön,Thomas Seel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) that learn dynamics from sparse position data while preserving Lagrangian structure via discrete forced Euler–Lagrange equations, enabling physically consistent, uncertainty-aware, long-term predictions; validated on synthetic and real-world problems including a hysteretic soft robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need data-efficient, physically consistent dynamical models that avoid energy drift and require only position measurements in many applications (motion capture, visual servoing).

Method: Develop LGPs with linear operators for GP conditioning derived from discrete forced Euler–Lagrange equations and variational discretization; learn dynamics from position snapshots without velocities.

Result: Demonstrates data-efficiency, generalization, and uncertainty quantification; stable long-term predictions; successful real-world case with a soft robot with hysteresis.

Conclusion: LGPs enable learning physically consistent dynamics from sparse positional data with stable long-term performance; broadly applicable to energy-drift-prone systems and scenarios lacking velocity measurements.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

</details>


### [152] [A Robust Foundation Model for Conservation Laws: Injecting Context into Flux Neural Operators via Recurrent Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05488)
*Taeyoung Kim,Joon-Hyuk Ko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hypernetworked Flux NO with ViT context injection enables solving conservation laws without explicit PDEs, preserving Flux NO’s robustness and generalization, including unseen fluxes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance Flux Neural Operator by integrating temporal context via recurrent ViT to infer governing dynamics and flux coefficients without explicit equations.

Method: Solution dynamics over a finite window are encoded by a recurrent Vision Transformer; a hypernetwork then generates parameters for a context-conditioned neural operator within a Flux NO framework that couples finite-volume methods.

Result: Maintains robustness, generalization, and long-time predictive ability; reliable numerical solutions across broad conservative systems, including unseen fluxes; code release.

Conclusion: Demonstrates data-driven discovery of conservation-law dynamics without PDE coefficients and expands Flux NO to handle varying fluxes and coefficients.

Abstract: We propose an architecture that augments the Flux Neural Operator (Flux NO), which combines the classical finite volume method (FVM) with neural operators, with ViT-based context injection. Our model is formulated as a hypernetwork: it extracts solution dynamics over a finite temporal window, encodes them with a recurrent Vision Transformer, and generates the parameters of a context-conditioned neural operator. This enables the model to infer and solve conservation laws without explicit access to the governing equation or PDE coefficients. Experimentally, we show that the proposed method preserves the robustness, generalization ability, and long-time prediction advantages of Flux NO over standard neural operators, while delivering reliable numerical solutions across a broad range of conservative systems, including previously unseen fluxes. Our code is available at https://github.com/xx257xx/CONTEXT_FLUX_NO.

</details>


### [153] [MEMOA: Massive Mixtures of Online Agents via Mean-Field Decentralized Nash Equilibria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05492)
*Xuwei Yang,David B. Emerson,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Anastasis Kratsios*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives a unique closed-form decentralized policy for large-scale federated learning under a minimax (worst-client) criterion, showing asymptotic convergence to the Nash-optimal centralized policy; introduces an online weighting mechanism to improve both worst-client and mean predictions, with empirical validation that the decentralized policy outperforms greedy baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In the era of large-scale AI, federated learning incurs prohibitive computational and communication costs as the number of agents grows.
