Decentralized, agent-centric strategies that rely on minimal ensemble summaries (mean-field) can offer scalable solutions; the paper seeks a provably optimal decentralized policy and its practical enhancements.

Method: Derives a unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form under a worst-client regret (minimax) objective, proves convergence to the Nash-optimal centralized policy in the large-population limit, and introduces an online weighting mechanism to optimally blend client predictions at the server to improve both the weakest-agent and the mean predictions.

Result: Establishes a unique closed-form decentralized policy that minimizes the maximum online cost among agents, proves asymptotic convergence to the centralized Nash policy as population grows, and shows that online weighting improves the mean prediction; numerical experiments corroborate theory and indicate the decentralized policy often outperforms natural greedy baselines.

Conclusion: A scalable, theoretically grounded decentralized control policy for federated learning is attainable: it achieves near-centralized optimality in large populations and benefits from server-side online weighting to improve overall ensemble performance.

Abstract: In the modern age of large-scale AI, federated learning has become an increasingly important tool for training large populations of AI agents; however, its computational and communication costs can rapidly fail to scale with the number of agents. This is precisely where decentralized agentic strategies shine: each agent acts autonomously, using only its own state together with a minimal summary of the ensemble, namely the mean-field. We derive the unique optimal decentralized policy in closed form. Optimality is characterized through a worst-client/minimax criterion: minimizing the under-performer regret, namely the maximal online cost incurred by the weakest agent in the ensemble. We further prove that the resulting decentralized policy asymptotically converges, in the large-population limit, to the Nash-optimal centralized policy, whose direct computation is not scalable. We use an online weighting mechanism to optimize the server-computed mixture of client predictions, thereby improving the mean prediction in addition to the previously optimized weakest-client prediction. Numerical experiments verify our theoretical guarantees and demonstrate that our decentralized policy typically outperforms natural greedy decentralized baselines.

</details>


### [154] [Shortcut Solutions Learned by Transformers Impair Continual Compositional Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05495)
*William T. Redman,Erik C. Johnson,Brian Robinson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends LEGO to continual learning (continual LEGO) and compares Transformer variants (BERT vs ALBERT) on CL performance. Finds BERT prone to shortcut solutions limiting transfer; ALBERT exhibits a For loop–like strategy with stronger CL, but both fail on cross-experience composition. Data-sharing across experiences rescues ALBERT but not BERT. Suggests recurrence in ALBERT imparts inductive biases favorable for CL; calls for deeper study of architecture–solution interactions in CL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Transformer architectures and their representations support continual learning and compositional reasoning, extending prior LEGO work to a continual setting and comparing feedforward (BERT) vs recurrent (ALBERT) transformers.

Method: Apply continual LEGO paradigm to BERT (feedforward) and ALBERT (recurrent) models within a continual learning setup. Assess performance on forward transfer, composition across experiences, and the impact of cross-experience data sharing on CL.

Result: BERT tends to learn shortcut solutions that hurt generalization and forward transfer. ALBERT appears to learn a For loop–like solution leading to better CL. When tasks require composition across experiences, both fail. Training strategies that mix data across experiences can rescue ALBERT performance but not BERT; BERT's shortcut solution becomes entrenched.

Conclusion: Architecture matters for CL performance: recurrent ALBERT shows an inductive bias more suitable for continual learning, motivating further work on how Transformer design shapes emergent computational strategies in CL.

Abstract: Identifying and exploiting common features across domains is at the heart of the human ability to make analogies, and is believed to be crucial for the ability to continually learn. To do this successfully, general and flexible computational strategies must be developed. While the extent to which Transformer neural network models can perform compositional reasoning has been the subject of intensive recent investigation, little work has been done to systematically understand how well these models can leverage their representations to learn new, related experiences. To address this gap, we expand the previously developed Learning Equality and Group Operations (LEGO) framework to a continual learning (CL) setting ("continual LEGO"). Using this continual LEGO experimental paradigm, we study the capability of feedforward and recurrent Transformer models to perform CL. We find that BERT, a canonical feedforward Transformer model, learns shortcut solutions that limits its ability to generalize and prevents strong forward transfer to new experiences. In contrast, we find evidence supporting the hypothesis that ALBERT, a recurrent version of BERT, learns a For loop-esque solution, which leads to better CL performance. When applying BERT and ALBERT models to a CL setting that requires composition across experiences, we find that both model families fail. Our investigation suggests that ALBERT models can have their performance drop rescued by use of training strategies that combine data across experiences, but this is not true for BERT models, where a detrimental shortcut solution becomes entrenched with initial training. Our results demonstrate that the recurrent ALBERT model may have an inductive bias better suited for CL and motivate future investigation of the interplay between Transformer architecture and computational solutions that emerge in modern models and tasks.

</details>


### [155] [Online Localized Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05497)
*Yuheng Lai,Garvesh Raskutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP) and OLCP-Hedge extend conformal prediction to online/time-series with covariate-dependent localization and online bandwidth selection, yielding valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In online/time-series settings, exchangeability fails and global calibration is inefficient under covariate heterogeneity. There is a need for locally adaptive, valid uncertainty quantification.

Method: Introduce OLCP that combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization; develop OLCP-Hedge to select localization bandwidth online via constrained online convex optimization and expert aggregation.

Result: Both methods come with coverage guarantees; simulations and real-data experiments show valid long-run coverage and narrower prediction sets compared with existing baselines.

Conclusion: Localization and online adaptation improve efficiency while preserving validity; OLCP-Hedge reduces sensitivity to bandwidth, offering practical tools for valid predictive uncertainty in non-exchangeable online settings.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a framework that provides valid uncertainty quantification for general models with exchangeable data. However, in the online learning and time-series settings, exchangeability is not satisfied. Existing online conformal methods, such as adaptive conformal inference (ACI), can achieve long-run validity, yet they remain inefficient under covariate heterogeneity because they rely on global calibration. We propose \emph{Online Localized Conformal Prediction (OLCP)}, which combines online adaptation with covariate-dependent localization to better reflect heterogeneity. To reduce sensitivity to the localization bandwidth, we further develop \emph{OLCP-Hedge}, which performs bandwidth selection as an online expert aggregation problem using a constrained online convex optimization framework. Importantly, we provide coverage guarantees for both algorithms and demonstrate through simulations and real-data experiments that the proposed methods attain valid long-run coverage with narrower prediction sets than existing baselines.

</details>


### [156] [Non-Myopic Active Feature Acquisition via Pathwise Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05511)
*Linus Aronsson,Morteza Haghir Chehreghani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-myopic pathwise policy gradients for active feature acquisition using a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process and a straight-through rollout, enabling end-to-end optimization with lower gradient variance and improved performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active feature acquisition seeks cost-efficient prediction by adaptively acquiring feature values; standard policy-gradient methods suffer high variance and short-sighted decisions. AFA framed as a POMDP allows sequential decision making. There is a need for non-myopic, end-to-end optimization that respects training-deployment alignment.

Method: Introduce NM-PPG with a continuous relaxation of the acquisition trajectory enabling pathwise (reparameterized) gradients. Use straight-through rollout: hard feature choices in the forward pass, backpropagating through soft relaxation. Apply entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening to stabilize optimization.

Result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows NM-PPG outperforms state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

Conclusion: Non-myopic pathwise gradients with a continuous relaxation and straight-through training improve AFA performance and training stability, enabling effective end-to-end optimization of cost-aware feature acquisition.

Abstract: Active feature acquisition (AFA) considers prediction problems in which features are costly to obtain and the learner adaptively decides which feature values to acquire for each instance and when to stop and predict. AFA can be formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), which naturally admits a sequential decision-making perspective. In this paper, we present non-myopic pathwise policy gradients (NM-PPG), a new AFA method built around this formulation. We introduce a continuous relaxation of the acquisition process that enables pathwise gradients through the full acquisition trajectory, avoiding the high variance of standard score-function policy gradients while allowing end-to-end optimization of a non-myopic acquisition policy. To better align training with deployment, we further develop a straight-through rollout scheme that follows hard feature acquisitions in the forward pass while backpropagating through the corresponding soft relaxation in the backward pass. We stabilize optimization with entropy regularization and staged temperature sharpening. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that NM-PPG yields superior performance relative to state-of-the-art AFA baselines.

</details>


### [157] [OpenG2G: A Simulation Platform for AI Datacenter-Grid Runtime Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05519)
*Jae-Won Chung,Zhirui Liang,Yanyong Mao,Jiasi Chen,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Vladimir Dvorkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenG2G is a modular simulation platform linking AI datacenters with grid models to study, compare, and optimize runtime coordination under flexible power envelopes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing compute demand and multiyear datacenter interconnection delays strain electricity grids, creating reliability and capacity challenges. Rapid, controllable power flexibility in datacenters can mitigate grid stress, necessitating tools to study coordination between AI workloads and grid responses.

Method: OpenG2G combines a production-backed datacenter backend (using real AI service measurements), a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface that enables classic, optimization-, and learning-based controllers to close the loop. It supports realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads to compare coordination strategies.

Result: The platform demonstrates versatility in answering a wide range of coordination questions, enabling quantitative assessment of how AI model choices and deployment strategies affect datacenter flexibility and grid coordination outcomes.

Conclusion: OpenG2G enables flexible, realistic evaluation of AI datacenter–grid coordination, supporting the design of effective control strategies and deeper understanding of how workload and deployment decisions impact grid reliability and efficiency.

Abstract: AI's growing compute demand and new datacenter buildouts present major capacity and reliability challenges for the electricity grid, leading to multi-year interconnection delays for new datacenters and bottlenecking AI growth. To ease this strain, datacenters increasingly offer rapid power flexibility in response to grid signals, where the datacenter can increase or decrease its power consumption by adapting its workload in real time.
  In order to understand the impact of large datacenters on the grid and to facilitate the design of effective coordination strategies, we build OpenG2G, a simulation platform for AI datacenter-grid runtime coordination. We show that OpenG2G is capable of answering a wide range of coordination questions by allowing users to implement and compare various control paradigms (including classic, optimization, and learning-based controllers), and quantify how AI model and deployment choices affect datacenter flexibility and coordination outcomes. This versatility is enabled by OpenG2G's modular and extensible architecture: a datacenter backend driven by real measurements of production-grade AI services, a grid backend built on high-fidelity grid simulators, and a generic controller interface that closes the loop between them. We describe the design of OpenG2G and demonstrate its usefulness through realistic grid scenarios and AI workloads.

</details>


### [158] [Bayesian Rain Field Reconstruction using Commercial Microwave Links and Diffusion Model Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05520)
*Badr Moufad,Albina Ilina,Hai Victor Habi,Salem Lahlou,Yazid Janati,Hagit Messer,Eric Moulines*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-model priors improve rainfall-field reconstruction from Commercial Microwave Link data by treating it as a Bayesian inverse problem and enabling training-free posterior sampling, outperforming censored Gaussian-process baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CMLs offer dense spatial coverage but produce path-integrated measurements; ground-level rainfall reconstruction is challenging due to line integration and heterogeneity. Existing methods oversimplify CMLs as point sensors, leading to degraded performance.

Method: Model rainfall estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem with diffusion models (DMs) as high-fidelity spatial priors. Compare DM priors to censored Gaussian processes for rainfall statistics. Enable training-free posterior sampling via Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show consistent improvements over established CML-based reconstruction baselines.

Conclusion: Diffusion-model priors provide better preservation of rainfall statistics and enable effective, training-free posterior sampling for CML-based rainfall field reconstruction, yielding improved accuracy in heterogeneous precipitation scenarios.

Abstract: Commercial Microwave Links (CMLs) offer dense spatial coverage for rainfall sensing but produce path-integrated measurements that make accurate ground-level reconstruction challenging. Existing methods typically oversimplify CMLs as point sensors and neglect line integration relating rainfall to signal attenuation, resulting in degraded performance under heterogeneous precipitation. In this work, we view rain field reconstruction as a Bayesian inverse problem with Diffusion Models (DMs) as high-fidelity spatial priors. We show that diffusion models better preserve key rainfall statistics compared to censored Gaussian processes. Framing rainfall estimation as a Bayesian inverse problem with a DM prior enables training-free posterior sampling using a broad family of methods, including Plug-and-Play, Sequential Monte Carlo, and Replica Exchange methods. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over established CML-based reconstruction baselines.

</details>


### [159] [MOSAIC: Module Discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal Learning for Scientific Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05524)
*Shicheng Fan,Nour Elhendawy,Jianle Sun,Ke Fang,Kun Zhang,Yihang Wang,Lu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a sparse temporal VAE that links identifiable latent factors to semantically meaningful observed variables, enabling module-level interpretability in causal representation learning for scientific time series, with theoretical guarantees and broad empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the gap between identifiability and interpretability in causal representation learning for scientific time series by enabling latent factors to align with known observations, thereby discovering interpretable, semantically meaningful latent modules.

Method: MOSAIC integrates regime-conditioned temporal variation for identifiability with a sparse additive decoder that assigns each latent factor a sparse subset of observed variables. The approach provides identifiability of ANOVA main-effect supports under smooth nonlinear mixing and offers finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant.

Result: Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across diverse scientific domains (RNA dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, Tennessee Eastman process, synthetic tokamak), enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

Conclusion: MOSAIC enables interpretable causal representations by tying latent structure to observed variables while preserving identifiability, supported by theoretical guarantees and broad empirical validation across multiple scientific domains.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) seeks to recover latent variables with identifiability guarantees, typically up to permutation and component-wise reparameterization under appropriate assumptions. However, identifiability does not imply interpretability: latent semantics are typically assigned post hoc by alignment with known ground-truth factors. This limitation is particularly acute in scientific time series, where underlying mechanisms are unknown and discovering interpretable structure is a primary goal. In contrast, scientific observations (such as residue-pair distances, climate indices, or process sensors) are inherently semantic, as they correspond to named physical quantities. This raises a key question: can the interpretability of observations be transferred to the identifiable latent space? We propose MOSAIC (Module discovery via Sparse Additive Identifiable Causal learning), a sparse temporal VAE that integrates temporal CRL identifiability with support recovery over observed variables. MOSAIC identifies latent variables via regime-conditioned temporal variation, and recovers for each latent a sparse set of associated observations through an additive decoder, yielding module-level interpretability. We show that ANOVA main-effect supports are identifiable under general smooth mixing functions, and provide finite-sample recovery guarantees for a tractable sparse-additive variant. Empirically, MOSAIC recovers domain-consistent variable groups across RNA molecular dynamics, solar wind, ENSO climate, the Tennessee Eastman process, and a synthetic tokamak benchmark, enabling interpretable discovery of latent mechanisms in scientific time series.

</details>


### [160] [Energy Generative Modeling: A Lyapunov-based Energy Matching Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05530)
*Yixuan Wang,Wenqian Xue,Warren E. Dixon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies training and sampling in static scalar energy generative models via density transport on Wasserstein space, using KL divergence as a Lyapunov function; yields a finite-step Langevin stopping criterion, shows no Lyapunov certificate for deterministic gradient flow, and proves that additive energy composition preserves the Gibbs invariant measure and Lyapunov certificate, connecting to nonlinear control tools with implications for barriers and contraction-based acceleration; validated on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the separation between training and sampling in static energy-based generators by recasting the problem as a single master dynamics on Wasserstein space and leveraging Lyapunov and control-theoretic tools to obtain guarantees and new capabilities.

Method: Formulate the generation process as a nonlinear control problem with KL divergence as a Lyapunov function. Treat training and sampling as two instances of the same autonomous dynamics with different initial conditions. Prove a finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and the nonexistence of a Lyapunov certificate for deterministic gradient flow. Show that additive composition of trained scalar energies preserves a Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Discuss extensions to barrier functions and contraction metrics.

Result: Two analytic results: (1) a finite-step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling; no Lyapunov certificate exists for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy. (2) Additive energy composition retains an explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the Lyapunov certificate. The framework enables application of nonlinear control tools to static energy models and is supported by experiments on synthetic distributions.

Conclusion: The reformulation bridges static scalar energy generative models with the nonlinear control toolkit, enabling barrier-based constrained generation and contraction-based acceleration, while unifying training and sampling under a single master dynamics. This offers theoretical guarantees and practical pathways for improved generation and sampling efficiency.

Abstract: Generative models based on static scalar energy functions represent an emerging paradigm in which a single time independent potential drives sample generation through its gradient field, eliminating the need for time conditioning entirely. We unify the training and sampling phases of this paradigm, conventionally treated as separate procedures, within a single framework: density transport on the Wasserstein space, cast as a nonlinear control problem in which the Kullback Leibler (KL) divergence serves as a Lyapunov function. Training and sampling are then two instances of this same master dynamics, differing only in initial condition. Within this autonomous framework we develop two analytic results. First, since the Lyapunov certificate is asymptotic, we derive a finite step stopping criterion for Langevin sampling and prove that no Lyapunov certificate exists for the deterministic gradient flow on the same energy landscape. Second, the reformulation brings the toolkit of nonlinear control theory to bear on static scalar energy generative modeling, that is, we show that additive composition of trained scalar energies retains an explicit Gibbs invariant measure and inherits the closed-loop Lyapunov certificate. Beyond these immediate results, this reformulation bridges static scalar energy generative models with the full toolkit of nonlinear control theory, opening the door to barrier functions for constrained generation and contraction metrics for accelerated sampling. Experiments on synthetic distributions validate the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [161] [Adversarial Graph Neural Network Benchmarks: Towards Practical and Fair Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05534)
*Tran Gia Bao Ngo,Zulfikar Alom,Federico Errica,Murat Kantarcioglu,Cuneyt Gurcan Akcora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Benchmark study shows that adversarial GNN evaluations are highly sensitive to experimental choices; standardized protocols across attacks, defenses, datasets are essential to avoid distorted conclusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inconsistent experimental settings in adversarial GNN research and establish fair, robust benchmarks.

Method: Re-evaluated 7 attacks and 8 defenses under poisoning and evasion across 6 datasets (453k experiments) in a unified framework; examined effects of target node selection and training regime on attack effectiveness.

Result: Notable differences in attack performance under fair evaluation; previously overlooked factors can distort performance insights; call for standardized evaluation to obtain reliable comparisons.

Conclusion: Standardized evaluation protocols are urgently needed in adversarial graph ML; provide guidelines for target selection, training setups, and reporting to ensure credible, comparable results.

Abstract: Adversarial learning and the robustness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are topics of widespread interest in the machine learning community, as documented by the number of adversarial attacks and defenses designed for these purposes. While a rigorous evaluation of these adversarial methods is necessary to understand the robustness of GNNs in real-world applications, we posit that many works in the literature do not share the same experimental settings, leading to ambiguous and potentially contradictory scientific conclusions. In this benchmark, we demonstrate the importance of adopting fair, robust, and standardized evaluation protocols in adversarial GNN research. We perform a comprehensive re-evaluation of seven widely used attacks and eight recent defenses under both poisoning and evasion scenarios, across six popular graph datasets. Our study spans over 453,000 experiments conducted within a unified framework. We observe substantial differences in adversarial attack performance when evaluated under a fair and robust procedure. Our findings reveal that previously overlooked factors, such as target node selection and the training process of the attacked model, have a profound impact on attack effectiveness, to the extent of completely distorting performance insights. These results underscore the urgent need for standardized evaluations in adversarial graph machine learning.

</details>


### [162] [Towards Scalable One-Step Generative Modeling for Autoregressive Dynamical System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05540)
*Tianyue Yang,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MeLISA offers a latent-free autoregressive surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow that uses blockwise transitions and two consistency losses to deliver efficient long-horizon rollouts with preserved statistics, outperforming neural-operator baselines on high-res flow benchmarks while maintaining fast inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need fast, accurate surrogates for high-dimensional physical dynamics that maintain temporal statistics over long horizons; current neural operators drift in turbulent regimes; diffusion-based and latent surrogates incur denoising and design overhead; aim for a latent-free, efficient autoregressive model with good long-horizon statistics.

Method: Develop Pixel-space MeanFlow-based autoregressive generator MeLISA with blockwise stochastic transition kernel; single model evaluation per forecast block; no latent encoders or diffusion solvers at inference; Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective for learning conditional spatiotemporal generation from partial temporal windows; Time Increment Consistency loss to constrain multi-lag increments and capture temporal correlations; evaluate with compact UNet and DiT backbones on 256x256 Kolmogorov flow and 192x192 turbulent channel-flow slice.

Result: MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term accuracy and long-horizon statistics (energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, mixing-rate dynamics); inference speed comparable or faster; parameter-efficient variants (3.7-5.7M) and scalable to 150M parameters with DiT; improved rollout efficiency and long-horizon accuracy.

Conclusion: Latent-free, mean-flow-based autoregressive surrogates with consistency losses enable efficient, accurate long-horizon forecasts for high-dimensional flows; practical inference speed and scalability; promising direction for surrogate modeling.

Abstract: Fast surrogate modeling for high-dimensional physical dynamics requires more than low short-term error: useful models must roll out efficiently while preserving the statistical structure of long trajectories. Neural operators provide inexpensive autoregressive forecasts but can drift in turbulent regimes, whereas rolling diffusion and latent generative surrogates can represent stochastic transitions at the cost of multi-step denoising, noise-schedule design, or auxiliary compression models. We propose MeanFlow Long-term Invariant Spatiotemporal Consistency Autoregressive Models (MeLISA), a latent-free autoregressive generative surrogate built on pixel-space MeanFlow. MeLISA defines a blockwise stochastic transition kernel that generates each forecast block with a single model evaluation, avoiding latent encoders and iterative diffusion solvers at inference time. To stabilize long-horizon rollouts, MeLISA combines a Window-Consistency MeanFlow objective that learns conditional spatiotemporal generation from partially observed temporal windows with a Time Increment Consistency loss that constrains multi-lag finite increments and targets temporal-correlation structure. We evaluate MeLISA with compact UNet and scalable DiT backbones on two high-resolution benchmarks, extended 2D Kolmogorov flow at $256 \times 256$ and turbulent channel-flow slice at $192 \times 192$. MeLISA outperforms neural-operator baselines on short-term forecasting accuracy and long-horizon statistical metrics, including energy spectra, turbulent kinetic energy, and mixing-rate-related dynamics, while achieving inference speeds comparable to, and in some cases faster than, neural operators. Compact 3.7-5.7M-parameter variants already deliver strong parameter efficiency, and DiT variants provide a scalable path up to 150M parameters. Overall, MeLISA benefits both rollout efficiency and long-horizon statistical accuracy.

</details>


### [163] [FedeKD: Energy-Based Gating for Robust Federated Knowledge Distillation under Heterogeneous Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05553)
*Quang-Huy Nguyen,Jiaqi Wang,Wei-shinn Ku*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reliability-aware federated knowledge distillation framework, FedeKD, uses a private high-capacity model and a lightweight proxy model. Proxies are aggregated server-side to form a global proxy that guides updates of private models. An energy-based gating mechanism yields sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation, reducing negative transfer in heterogeneous FL without public data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning with heterogeneous data distributions and asymmetric models often leads to negative transfer. Existing FKD methods mitigate parameter sharing but rely on public data or assume uniform trust in transferred knowledge, which hurts robustness. A mechanism for sample-wise, data-driven reliability estimation without public data is needed.

Method: Each client maintains a high-capacity private model and a lightweight shared proxy model. Proxy models are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy. The global proxy guides updates of private models via backward distillation. An energy-based gating mechanism converts private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights, enabling adaptive weighting of knowledge transfer during distillation. No public data is used.

Result: Experiments on six real-world datasets show that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance, outperforming baselines that rely on uniform trust or public data.

Conclusion: FedeKD introduces a reliability-aware FKD framework with sample-wise trust estimation and energy-based gating, improving robustness to data heterogeneity without public data and preserving predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) operates in heterogeneous environments, where variations in data distributions and asymmetric model design often result in negative transfer. While federated knowledge distillation (FKD) avoids direct model parameter sharing, existing methods typically rely on public datasets or assume that transferred knowledge is uniformly reliable, which limits their robustness in practice. This paper presents FedeKD, a reliability-aware FKD framework that makes sample-wise trust estimation an explicit component of knowledge transfer, without relying on additional public data. Each client maintains a high-capacity private model for local learning and a lightweight shared proxy model for cross-client knowledge exchange. During training, proxy models are aggregated on the server to form a global proxy, which is then used to guide updates of the private models. At the core of FedeKD is an energy-based gating mechanism that converts task-specific private-proxy disagreement into sample-wise trust weights for backward distillation. This mechanism enables sample-wise weighting of knowledge transfer, where the proxy model contributes more to reliable samples while down-weighting unreliable ones. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that FedeKD significantly reduces negative transfer under heterogeneous settings while maintaining strong predictive performance.

</details>


### [164] [Accelerating LMO-Based Optimization via Implicit Gradient Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05577)
*Won-Jun Jang,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces LMO-IGT, a stochastic LMO framework using implicit gradient transport with a unified RSF-based stationarity measure; achieves improved iteration complexity (ε^-3.5) using a single gradient per iteration, with Muon-IGT giving strongest empirical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce variance-reduction overhead in LMO-based optimizers, unify unconstrained and constrained analyses, and accelerate convergence while preserving a simple single-gradient-per-iteration structure.

Method: Propose LMO-IGT by transporting gradients via implicit gradient transport within stochastic LMO updates; develop a unified stochastic LMO framework around the regularized support function (RSF) to connect gradient-norm and Frank–Wolfe gap notions; derive iteration complexity results and instantiate Muon-IGT; provide empirical evaluations.

Result: Theoretical: standard stochastic LMO: O(ε^-4); variance-reduced LMO: O(ε^-3) with extra gradient costs; LMO-IGT: O(ε^-3.5) with one stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirical: LMO-IGT and Muon-IGT outperform baselines with negligible overhead; Muon-IGT shows strongest performance across tested settings.

Conclusion: Implicit gradient transport is an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for LMO-based optimization; RSF provides a unifying framework linking competing stationarity notions; single-gradient-per-iteration architectures can achieve strong convergence with IGT; the results encourage broader adoption and extension to related constrained/unconstrained problems.

Abstract: Recent optimizers such as Lion and Muon have demonstrated strong empirical performance by normalizing gradient momentum via linear minimization oracles (LMOs). While variance reduction has been explored to accelerate LMO-based methods, it typically incurs substantial computational overhead due to additional gradient evaluations. At the same time, the theoretical understanding of LMO-based methods remains fragmented across unconstrained and constrained formulations. Motivated by these limitations, we propose \emph{LMO-IGT}, a new class of stochastic LMO-based methods leveraging implicit gradient transport (IGT). We further introduce a unified framework for stochastic LMO-based optimization together with a new stationarity measure, the \emph{regularized support function} (RSF), which bridges gradient-norm and Frank--Wolfe-gap notions within a common framework. By evaluating stochastic gradients at transported points, LMO-IGT accelerates convergence while retaining the single-gradient-per-iteration structure of standard stochastic LMO. Our analysis establishes that stochastic LMO achieves an iteration complexity of $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-4})$, variance-reduced LMO achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$ at the cost of additional gradient evaluations, and LMO-IGT achieves $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3.5})$ using only a single stochastic gradient per iteration. Empirically, LMO-IGT consistently improves over stochastic LMO counterparts with negligible overhead. Among its instantiations, Muon-IGT achieves the strongest overall performance across evaluated settings, demonstrating that IGT provides an effective and practical acceleration mechanism for modern LMO-based optimization.

</details>


### [165] [AeroJEPA: Learning Semantic Latent Representations for Scalable 3D Aerodynamic Field Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05586)
*Francisco Giral,Abhijeet Vishwasrao,Andrea Arroyo Ramo,Mahmoud Golestanian,Federica Tonti,Adrian Lozano-Duran,Steven L. Brunton,Sergio Hoyas,Hector Gomez,Soledad Le Clainche,Ricardo Vinuesa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AeroJEPA introduces a latent-space surrogate for aerodynamic fields that predicts a flow latent from a geometry/operating-conditions latent, enabling scalable high-resolution outputs and semantically meaningful representations; validated on HiLiftAeroML and SuperWing with latent-interpolation, probing, vector arithmetic, and design-optimization demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current aerodynamic surrogates struggle with (i) scaling to very large 3D fields and (ii) latent representations that are not readily usable for analysis and design. A latent-prediction framework could decouple field resolution from latent prediction and yield semantically organized representations.

Method: A joint-embedding predictive architecture that encodes geometry and operating conditions into a context latent, predicts a target latent representation of the flow, and optionally reconstructs the field via a continuous implicit decoder. This decouples latent prediction from field resolution and encourages semantic organization of the latent space. Evaluated on HiLiftAeroML (high-fidelity, large boundary layers) and SuperWing (large-scale generalization and latent-space optimization for transonic wings).

Result: AeroJEPA competitive as a continuous aerodynamic-field surrogate, naturally scalable to high-resolution outputs. The learned latent representations encode geometry and aerodynamic quantities not explicitly supervised. Demonstrated latent-space utilities: controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and a constrained design latent-optimization experiment.

Conclusion: Predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

Abstract: Aerodynamic surrogate models are increasingly used to replace repeated high-fidelity CFD evaluations in many-query design settings, but current approaches still face two important limitations: they often scale poorly to the very large fields arising in realistic 3D aerodynamics, and they rarely produce latent representations that are directly useful for analysis and design. We introduce AeroJEPA, a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for aerodynamic field modeling that addresses both issues. Rather than predicting the full flow field directly from geometry, AeroJEPA predicts a target latent representation of the flow from a context latent representation of the geometry and operating conditions, and optionally reconstructs the field through a continuous implicit decoder. This formulation decouples latent prediction from field resolution while encouraging the latent space to organize semantically. We evaluate AeroJEPA on two complementary datasets: HiLiftAeroML, which stresses the method in a high-fidelity regime with extremely large boundary-layer fields, and SuperWing, which tests large-scale generalization and latent-space optimization over a broad family of transonic wings. Across these benchmarks, AeroJEPA is competitive as a continuous surrogate for aerodynamic fields, scales naturally to high-resolution outputs, and learns context and predicted latents that encode geometry and aerodynamic quantities not used directly as supervision. We further show that the resulting latent space supports controlled interpolation, linear probing, concept-vector arithmetic, and a constrained design latent-optimization experiment. These results suggest that predictive latent learning is a promising direction for scalable and design-meaningful aerodynamic surrogate modeling.

</details>


### [166] [When Can Voting Help, Hurt, or Change Course? Exact Structure of Binary Test-Time Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05592)
*Yi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Voting under exchangeable repeated predictions is governed by latent per-example correctness distributions, producing nonmonotone and complex curves. The observed curve encodes a signed voting signature, and there is an equivalence: the odd-budget curve and the signature are two views of the same latent structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge the classical monotone view of majority voting under fixed competence; understand what the observed voting curve reveals about latent per-example correctness probabilities when predictions are exchangeable.

Method: Apply the de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness; analyze latent probability mixtures; construct explicit nonmonotone curves with potentially infinitely many trend changes; define a signed voting signature; prove that the odd-budget curve and the signature are equivalent via signed Hausdorff moments; discuss identifiability, realizability, and estimation regimes.

Result: Latent mixtures can yield nonmonotone voting curves; the latent law does not get identified uniquely from the curve alone; the curve’s increments are signed Hausdorff moments and the full curve recovers the signed voting signature; separates estimation regimes into per-example success-probability information (full signature) versus grouped-label information (finite prefix).

Conclusion: A latent-probability view is essential to understand voting behavior. The full latent law is recoverable from the curve via the signed signature, but the curve alone does not determine the latent law; this framework explains shape phenomena, nonidentifiability, and informs estimation strategies.

Abstract: Majority voting is one of the few black-box interventions that can improve a fixed stochastic predictor: repeated access can be cheaper than changing a high-capability model. Classical fixed-competence theory makes this intervention look monotone -- more votes help above the majority threshold and hurt below it. We show that this picture is fundamentally incomplete. Under the de Finetti representation for exchangeable repeated correctness, voting is governed by a latent distribution of per-example correctness probabilities. Even simple latent mixtures can generate sharply different voting curves, including nonmonotone behavior and, in an explicit construction, infinitely many trend changes. The full latent law determines the curve, but the curve does not determine the law. The exact object recovered by voting is a signed voting signature: at each binomial variance scale, it records excess latent mass above rather than below the majority threshold. Our main theorem proves that the complete odd-budget curve and this signature are equivalent: the curve increments are signed Hausdorff moments, and the full curve recovers the signature uniquely. This viewpoint explains shape phenomena, branch-symmetric nonidentifiability, realizability, variation, and endpoint rates. It also separates estimation regimes: direct per-example success-probability information targets the full signature, whereas fixed-depth grouped labels reveal only a finite prefix.

</details>


### [167] [Optimal Contextual Pricing under Agnostic Non-Lipschitz Demand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05609)
*Jianyu Xu,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Near-optimal contextual dynamic pricing algorithm for linear valuations with bounded-support agnostic noise, achieving Õ(T^{2/3}) regret and closing the gap to the lower bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discontinuous, non-Lipschitz demand due to atoms breaks smooth interpolation; previous Õ(T^{3/4}) regret; need tighter regret bound under agnostic noise.

Method: Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing: randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection.

Result: Regret bound Õ(T^{2/3}); matches Kleinberg & Leighton (2003) lower bound up to logs; improves over Xu & Wang (2022).

Conclusion: Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, resolves the open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing with agnostic non-Lipschitz noise; polynomial-time algorithm.

Abstract: We study contextual dynamic pricing with linear valuations and bounded-support agnostic noise, whose induced demand curve may be non-Lipschitz with arbitrary jumps and atoms. Such discontinuities break the cross-context interpolation arguments used by smooth-demand pricing algorithms, while the best previous method achieved only $\tilde O(T^{3/4})$ regret. We propose Conservative-Markdown Redirect-UCB Pricing, a polynomial-time algorithm that combines randomized parameter estimation, conservative residual-grid probing, and confidence-based one-step redirection. Our algorithm achieves $\tilde O(T^{2/3})$ optimal regret, matching the known lower bounds of Kleinberg and Leighton (2003) up to logarithmic factors and improving over the previous upper bound of Xu and Wang (2022). Under stochastic well-conditioned contexts, this closes the long-existing open regret gap in linear-valuation contextual pricing under agnostic non-Lipschitz noise distribution.

</details>


### [168] [LLMSpace: Carbon Footprint Modeling for Large Language Model Inference on LEO Satellites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05615)
*Lei Jiang,Adrian Ildefonso,Daniel Loveless,Fan Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMSpace offers a carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites, integrating operational and embodied carbon, radiation-hardened hardware, and workload dynamics to illuminate trade-offs between carbon footprint, latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the growing energy and carbon footprint of large language models by evaluating a space-based inference paradigm using solar-powered LEO satellites and hardened hardware.

Method: Develop a comprehensive carbon model (LLMSpace) that jointly accounts for operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics (pre-fill, decode behavior, and token generation). Employ realistic satellite and GPU configurations to simulate trade-offs.

Result: Demonstrates key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference, providing quantitative insights and guiding design choices.

Conclusion: LLMSpace is a first-of-its-kind framework enabling lifecycle-aware assessment of space-based LLM inference, highlighting the feasibility and critical factors for reducing carbon impact; source code is available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) impose rapidly growing energy demands, creating an emerging energy and carbon crisis driven by large-scale inference. Solar-powered, AI-enabled low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites have been proposed to mitigate terrestrial electricity consumption, but their lifecycle carbon footprint remains poorly understood due to launch emissions, satellite manufacturing, and radiation-hardened hardware requirements. This paper presents \textit{LLMSpace}, the first carbon modeling framework for LLM inference on AI-enabled LEO satellites. LLMSpace jointly models operational and embodied carbon, peripheral subsystems, radiation-hardened accelerators and memories, and LLM-specific workload characteristics such as prefill-decode behavior and token generation. Using realistic satellite and GPU configurations, LLMSpace reveals key trade-offs among carbon footprint, inference latency, hardware design, and operational lifetime for sustainable space-based LLM inference. Source code: https://github.com/UnchartedRLab/LLMSpace.

</details>


### [169] [Region-adaptable retrieval of coastal biogeochemical parameters from near-surface hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance using physics-aware meta-learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05623)
*Yiqing Guo,Nagur R. C. Cherukuru,Eric A. Lehmann,S. L. Kesav Unnithan,Tim J. Malthus,Gemma Kerrisk,Xiubin Qi,Faisal Islam,Tisham Dhar,Mark J. Doubell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework uses synthetic data from a bio-optical forward model to enable region-agnostic pretraining and region-specific finetuning for coastal BGC parameter retrieval from near-surface Rrs; tested on five Australian sites; outperforms baselines and captures temporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization challenges in retrieving coastal BGC parameters from Rrs due to regional variation in bio-optical properties; aims to achieve scalable, accurate retrieval by combining physics-based data with meta-learning.

Method: Stage 1: generate a large synthetic dataset using a bio-optical forward model based on a comprehensive Australian coastal spectral library; pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning. Stage 2: fine-tune the pretrained base model for specific regions using local samples. In situ data collected from five geographically distinct Australian sites; evaluation includes comparisons to five benchmark models and time-series validation.

Result: (1) BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures show clear regional distinctions across sites; (2) the synthetic data are physically plausible and closely align with real samples in parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperforms five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; (4) time series of measured vs. model-predicted BGC parameters agree well in magnitude and temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: A two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework improves generalization for coastal BGC retrieval from Rrs by leveraging physics-based synthetic data and region-specific fine-tuning, demonstrated across diverse Australian coastal environments.

Abstract: Hyperspectral in situ sensing has shown promise in retrieving aquatic biogeochemical (BGC) parameters, such as total suspended solids, dissolved organic carbon, and total chlorophyll-a, for cost-effective monitoring of coastal water quality. However, generalising such retrieval algorithms across water bodies remains challenging, as the relationship between remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) and BGC parameters can vary considerably from one region to another due to regional distinctions in environmental conditions and biogeochemistry that lead to different BGC ranges and bio-optical properties. In this study, we propose a two-stage physics-aware meta-learning framework for retrieving coastal BGC parameters from near-surface Rrs observations. In the first stage, a bio-optical forward model is used to generate a large synthetic dataset based on an in situ bio-optical spectral library with broad representativeness of Australian coastal waters. This dataset is then used to pretrain a region-agnostic base model with meta-learning, allowing the model to learn fundamental physical relationships. In the second stage, the pretrained base model is fine-tuned for specific regions with local samples. We collected in situ hyperspectral Rrs and BGC measurements from five geographically distinct sites in Australian coastal waters. Our experimental results suggest: (1) the BGC parameters and their corresponding hyperspectral Rrs signatures exhibited clear regional distinctions among the experimental sites; (2) the synthetic dataset was physically plausible and closely aligned with real-world samples in both parameter distributions and inter-parameter correlations; (3) the proposed approach outperformed five benchmark models in BGC retrieval; and (4) time series of in situ measured and model-predicted BGC parameters showed good agreement in both magnitude and temporal dynamics.

</details>


### [170] [Scaling Pretrained Representations Enables Label-Free Out-of-Distribution Detection Without Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05638)
*Brett Barkley,Preston Culbertson,David Fridovich-Keil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Frozen pretrained representations inherently encode geometry supporting label-free OOD detection; two detectors—global Mahalanobis on unlabeled features and local diffusion-based ReSCOPED—achieve comparable performance, with detector differences vanishing as representation quality improves, enabling efficient deployment on frozen models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether modern pretrained backbones (without fine-tuning or class-conditional training) can support effective label-free OOD detection under distribution shift.

Method: Empirically compare two label-free detectors across 59 backbone-task pairs in vision and language: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a local diffusion-based typicality estimator applied to the same features.

Result: Both detectors exhibit improved performance with higher-quality representations; the performance gap between the two detectors narrows as representations scale; the geometry exposed by frozen backbones strongly supports OOD detection.

Conclusion: Label-free OOD detection relies on the geometry of frozen pretrained representations; as backbone scale increases, detector choice becomes less critical, enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models without task-specific fine-tuning.

Abstract: Models trained with deep learning often fail to signal when inputs fall outside their training data manifold, leading to unreliable predictions under distribution shift. Prior work suggests that effective out-of-distribution (OOD) detection often requires class-conditional modeling or specialized models obtained through supervised fine-tuning. We revisit this assumption in modern pretrained models and show that their frozen representations already encode sufficient geometric structure for accurate label-free OOD detection. Across 59 backbone-task pairings spanning vision and language, we compare two complementary label-free detectors: a global Mahalanobis estimator fit on unlabeled latent representations, and ReSCOPED, a lightweight, diffusion-based typicality estimator operating on the same features at a local level. Despite their different detection mechanisms, representation scaling reveals a consistent regime-dependent pattern: both local and global detectors' absolute performance improves with better representation quality, and performance gaps between the two detectors disappear across both language and vision tasks as representations scale. These results suggest that label-free OOD detection depends strongly on the geometry exposed by frozen pretrained backbones, reducing the importance of detector choice as backbone scale increases and enabling efficient deployment directly on frozen models.

</details>


### [171] [Information-Preserving Domain Transfer with Unlabeled Data in Misspecified Simulation-Based Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05652)
*Joon Jang,Eunho Jeong,Kyu Sung Choi,Hyeonjin Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPIN is an SBI framework that learns parameter-relevant domain transfer between simulator and real data, enabling posterior inference under misspecification without labeled real parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SBI performance degrades when the simulator is misspecified; aligning only marginal distributions between simulated and real data does not preserve parameter-relevant information needed for accurate posterior inference.

Method: During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the simulator labels to encourage transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information; at test time, real observations are mapped into the simulator domain for posterior inference without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired data.

Result: Empirically improves real-world posterior inference on controlled synthetic and physical benchmarks, with greater gains as misspecification increases.

Conclusion: Parameter-relevant domain transfer in SBI can mitigate misspecification effects, enabling more accurate posterior inference without labeled real-world parameters.

Abstract: Simulation-based inference (SBI) provides amortized Bayesian parameter inference from simulator-generated data without requiring explicit likelihood evaluation. Its reliability can degrade under model misspecification, where real-world observations are not well represented by the simulator used for training. Existing methods using unlabeled real-world data often align simulated and real-world data distributions, but marginal alignment alone does not directly preserve parameter-relevant information needed for posterior inference. We propose SPIN, an SBI framework with parameter-relevant information-preserving domain transfer using unlabeled, unpaired real-world observations. During training, SPIN translates labeled simulator observations toward the real-world domain and back to the simulator domain, using the original simulator labels to encourage domain transfer that preserves parameter-relevant mutual information. At test time, the learned real-to-simulator transport maps real-world observations into the simulator domain for posterior inference, without requiring real-world parameter labels or paired real--simulator observations. Across controlled synthetic and physical real-world benchmarks, SPIN improves real-world posterior inference, with the improvement becoming clearer as misspecification increases.

</details>


### [172] [Structural Correspondence and Universal Approximation in Diagonal plus Low-Rank Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05659)
*Ying Chen,Aoxi Li,Jihun Kim,Javad Lavaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified Structural Correspondence framework shows that Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) architectures restore universal approximation in parameter-efficient networks. Rank-1 layers can interpolate scalars but fail for general function approximation; DLoR enables exact reconstruction of full-rank mappings via additive width or multiplicative depth, with multiplicative depth offering superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling. Dense matrices and certain activations are not required for universal expressivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of theoretical guarantees for the expressive power of low-rank neural networks without pretrained priors; identify whether universal approximation can be achieved under minimalist low-rank constraints.

Method: 1) Analyze the limits of networks constrained to low-rank manifolds without dense priors (rank-1 failure for function approximation). 2) Propose Structural Correspondence and introduce Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure. 3) Prove exact reconstruction of any full-rank transform via additive (width) or multiplicative (depth) decompositions. 4) Use asymptotic Taylor remainder analysis to show universal approximation for general activations. 5) Compare additive vs multiplicative depth in parameter-efficiency. 

Result: With DLoR, universal approximation is achieved for general activations; any full-rank transformation can be reconstructed from DLoR components by adjusting width or depth. The multiplicative depth yields superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Dense matrices and specific activation priors are not prerequisites for universal expressivity.

Conclusion: Dense matrices or fixed activation constraints are unnecessary for universal expressivity in parameter-efficient networks; a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) design with appropriate depth or width suffices to realize full expressivity.

Abstract: The massive computational costs of scaling modern deep learning architectures have driven the widespread use of parameter-efficient low-rank structures, such as LoRA and low-rank factorization. However, theoretical guarantees for their expressive power are less explored, often relying on restrictive priors like a pretrained base matrix, ReLU activations or non-verifiable singularity conditions. We first investigate the limits of neural networks constrained strictly to low-rank manifolds without pretrained dense priors. We demonstrate a theoretical paradox: while purely rank-1 layers can exactly interpolate arbitrary scalar datasets, they collapse for function approximations. To overcome this bottleneck without surrendering parameter efficiency, we introduce a unified \textit{Structural Correspondence} framework. We prove that augmenting low-rank layers with only a minimal sparse diagonal component, say a Diagonal plus Low-Rank (DLoR) structure, is sufficient to reach Universal Approximation. We show that any full-rank transformation can be exactly reconstructed using these DLoR components by trading off network width (additive decomposition) or depth (multiplicative decomposition). By tracking asymptotic Taylor remainders, we prove that DLoR neural networks fully restore the Universal Approximation Theorem for general activation functions. Finally, we establish that multiplicative depth provides superior parameter-to-expressivity scaling compared to additive width. Our results show that dense matrices and specific activation functions are not topological prerequisites for universal expressivity.

</details>


### [173] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Objective Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05660)
*Yufeng Yang,Fangning Zhuo,Ziyi Chen,Heng Huang,Yi Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DR-MOO introduces distributionally robust multi-objective optimization with Pareto-type solutions, and MGDA-based algorithms enhanced by a Lagrangian dual reformulation. It achieves a large improvement in sample complexity via gradient clipping, moving from a double-loop O(ε^{-12}) to a single-loop O(ε^{-4}) while handling generalized-smooth and biased gradients in nonconvex settings; experiments show competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address distributional shifts in multi-objective learning, where each objective is optimized under its worst-case distribution, a gap exists for robust Pareto-optimal solutions in MOO.

Method: A Lagrangian dual reformulation is used to derive two MGDA variants. The first is a double-loop MGDA with an inner loop to estimate dual variables. The second introduces gradient clipping to manage biased gradient estimates and generalized smoothness, enabling a single-loop double-clip MGDA without double sampling.

Result: The double-loop approach achieves a total sample complexity of O(ε^{-12}) to reach an ε-Pareto-stationary point. The single-loop double-clip MGDA improves this to O(ε^{-4}). The theory covers nonconvex settings and does not require objective or gradient boundedness.

Conclusion: The proposed DR-MOO framework and MGDA algorithms provide theoretically grounded, scalable solutions for distributionally robust Pareto optimization in nonconvex settings, with empirical results indicating competitiveness against state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

Abstract: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) has received growing attention in applications that require learning under multiple criteria. However, the existing MOO formulations do not explicitly account for distributional shifts in the data. We introduce distributionally robust multi-objective optimization (DR-MOO), which minimizes multiple objectives under their respective worst-case distributions. We propose Pareto-type solution concepts for DR-MOO and develop multi-gradient descent algorithms (MGDA) with provable guarantees. Leveraging a Lagrangian dual reformulation, we first design a double-loop MGDA that uses an inner loop to estimate dual variables and achieves a total sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-12})$ for reaching an $ε$-Pareto-stationary point. To further improve efficiency, we incorporate gradient clipping to handle generalized-smooth and biased gradient estimates, removing the need for double sampling. This yields a single-loop double-clip MGDA with substantially improved sample complexity $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$. Our theory applies to the nonconvex setting and does not require bounded objectives or gradients. Experiments demonstrate that our methods are competitive with state-of-the-art MGDA baselines.

</details>


### [174] [Temporal Functional Circuits: From Spline Plots to Faithful Explanations in KAN Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05685)
*Naveen Mysore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Functional Circuits provide faithful, temporally grounded explanations for Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) by turning edge functions into interpretable, input-lag-based attributions and validating them with targeted interventions. The gated residual KAN adapts its edge activation with signal complexity, achieving strong performance and interpretability across synthetic regimes and eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of mechanistic, edge-level explanations in time-series forecasting with powerful nonlinear models like MLPs; leverage KANs' explicit edge functions to obtain faithful, interpretable explanations.

Method: Introduce Temporal Functional Circuits built on a gated residual KAN. Map each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, rank edges by learned activation range, and validate faithfulness through edge-level interventions (zeroing, spline removal). Evaluate by ablations (removing the B-spline component) and experiments on synthetic regimes (including regime-switching) and eight benchmarks.

Result: Gated KAN reduces MSE by 59% vs linear-only models on regime-switching signals. Across eight benchmarks, the architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP baselines and yields edge-function explanations that MLP corrections cannot provide.

Conclusion: Temporal Functional Circuits enable faithful, temporally grounded explanations for KANs by translating edge functions into input-lag attributions, with gating that scales to signal complexity. The approach delivers competitive predictive performance while offering interpretable, edge-level insights beyond MLP-based corrections.

Abstract: Unlike MLPs, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) expose explicit learnable edge functions on every connection, enabling mechanistic explanation in time-series forecasting. This paper introduces Temporal Functional Circuits, a framework that transforms KAN edge functions from latent visualizations into faithful, temporally grounded explanations. Built on a gated residual KAN that decomposes forecasts into a linear base and a sparsely activated KAN correction, the framework (i) maps each edge to input lags via output-aware attribution, (ii) ranks edges by learned activation range, and (iii) validates faithfulness through edge-level interventions including zeroing and spline removal. Removing the learned B-spline component while retaining the base SiLU term degrades forecasts, providing evidence that the spline shape itself carries predictive value beyond the base activation. On four synthetic regimes of increasing complexity, the learned gate opens progressively wider as signal complexity grows. On regime-switching signals, gated KAN achieves 59% lower MSE than linear-only models. Across eight benchmarks, the gated architecture is competitive with linear, attention, and MLP alternatives, while providing interpretable edge functions that MLP-based corrections cannot offer.

</details>


### [175] [Budgeted Attention Allocation: Cost-Conditioned Compute Control for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05697)
*Amrit Nidhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feasibility study of budgeted attention gating across budgets in transformers, showing tradeoffs between accuracy and attention cost with reproducible CPU-speedups on small benchmarks; no universal dominance, but practical controllable checkpoints across budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a single trained model capable of operating at multiple cost–quality points by learning monotone gating of attention, enabling deployment under varying resource constraints.

Method: Introduce Budgeted Attention Allocation with a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget, aided by dense warm-starts for stability. Evaluate on: synthetic sequence task (robust), AG News with a custom word-level transformer (budgeted gates vs hard-gate adaptation), pretrained BERT-Mini with budgeted structural pruning and post-hoc baselines, and DBpedia14. Compare against dense full attention and static fixed-budget gates; use per-budget specialists and recovery epochs to improve performance.

Result: On synthetic task: 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 cost and 100.0% at 0.504 cost. AG News: 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. BERT-Mini AG News: 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; zero-shot dense post-hoc baseline at 86.1%, improved to 87.9% after a recovery epoch. DBpedia14: 97.4% at 0.50 vs 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain competitive.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates reproducible feasibility of a controllable checkpoint across budgets that trades attention cost for accuracy and translates into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks; it does not claim universal dominance across tasks or models.

Abstract: Transformers usually expose one inference cost per trained model, while deployed systems often need multiple cost-quality operating points. We study Budgeted Attention Allocation, a monotone head-gating mechanism conditioned on a requested attention budget. Dense warm-starting is important for stability: on a robust synthetic sequence task, one budgeted model reaches 99.7% accuracy at 0.303 estimated attention cost and 100.0% accuracy at 0.504 cost. On held-out AG News with a custom word-level transformer, hard-gate adaptation turns soft cost control into measured single-thread CPU speed, reaching 82.1% accuracy with 1.28x speedup at budget 0.50. In pretrained BERT-Mini AG News, budgeted structural pruning reaches 87.6% accuracy with 1.20x speedup at budget 0.50; a validation-ranked zero-shot dense post-hoc structural baseline reaches 86.1%, and one recovery epoch raises that per-budget specialist to 87.9%. On DBpedia14, BERT-Mini budgeted gates reach 97.4% at exact budget 0.50 versus 96.6% for dense full attention. Static fixed-budget gates and recovered dense specialists remain strong. The contribution is therefore not universal dominance, but a reproducible feasibility study of one controllable checkpoint across budgets that can trade attention cost for accuracy and be converted into measured structural speedups on small CPU benchmarks.

</details>


### [176] [On the Blessing of Pre-training in Weak-to-Strong Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05710)
*Wei Yao,Wang Zhaoyang,Gengze Xu,Chen Qian,Dongrui Liu,Ziqiao Wang,Yong Liu,Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: W2SG (weak-to-strong generalization) hinges on pre-training as a necessary warm-start. The paper formalizes W2SG in a high-dimensional single-index model with spiked Gaussian data, using spectral initialization, proving that without a good initialization learning may fail, whereas a warm start placing the model in an effectively strongly convex region enables W2SG. It derives a generalization bound reflecting an initial gain followed by a saturation bottleneck from the weak supervisor's bias, and validates via synthetic experiments and a large-scale, phase-transition-like empirical study across many pre-training checkpoints of LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify and theoretically justify the essential role of pre-training in enabling Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG), addressing gaps where prior work emphasized improvement but not the prerequisites or dynamics.

Method: Theoretical: model W2SG in a high-dimensional single-index framework with spiked Gaussian data; treat pre-training as a spectral initialization that places the model in an 'effective region' with perturbed strong-convexity. Derive a generalization bound that captures optimization dynamics (initial gains then saturation). Empirical: synthetic simulations to validate assumptions; large-scale analysis across hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models to observe phase-transition-like emergence of W2SG.

Result: Formalization of W2SG within the proposed model; proof that pre-training provides a geometric warm start enabling W2SG in the effective region; a generalization bound reflecting the optimization trajectory; empirical validation showing a phase-transition linked to pre-training progress.

Conclusion: Pre-training is a necessary prerequisite for W2SG; the ability to generalize from weak to strong supervision is not innate but emerges through a pre-training-driven phase transition that moves the model into a region with favorable geometry for learning.

Abstract: The paradigm of Weak-to-Strong Generalization (W2SG) suggests that a pre-trained strong model can surpass its weak supervisor, yet the decisive role of pre-training remains theoretically and empirically under-explored. In this work, we identify pre-training as the essential prerequisite for the emergence of W2SG. Theoretically, we formalize the W2SG problem within a high-dimensional single-index model framework using spiked Gaussian data, modeling pre-training as a spectral initialization step. Building upon prior impossibility results regarding the failure of learning under random initialization, we prove that W2SG is achievable when pre-training provides a geometric warm start that places the model within an "effective region" characterized by a perturbed strong-convexity geometry. Within this region, we derive a rigorous generalization bound that naturally captures the optimization dynamics: an initial performance improvement followed by a saturation bottleneck dictated by the weak supervisor's bias. Empirically, we first validate all our assumptions and theoretical insights through controlled synthetic simulations. Finally, through a massive-scale evaluation of hundreds of intermediate pre-training checkpoints from large language models, we demonstrate that W2SG is not an innate capability but emerges via a phase transition tightly coupled with the progression of pre-training.

</details>


### [177] [Enabling Federated Inference via Unsupervised Consensus Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05718)
*Yui Hashimoto,Takayuki Nishio,Yuichi Kitagawa,Takahito Tanimura*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CE-FI enables cooperative inference among pretrained models without sharing inputs or parameters or requiring a common encoder, via a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer trained on unlabeled data, achieving strong results on CIFAR-10/100 under non-IID conditions and showing cross-domain applicability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings where multiple independently deployed models should cooperate at inference time without exposing raw data, model parameters, or requiring a shared encoder.

Method: Introduce two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that generates predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, enabling cooperative inference without sharing inputs or parameters or a common encoder.

Result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under diverse non-IID partitions shows CE-FI consistently improves over solo inference and matches conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional experiments on text and time-series tasks indicate broader applicability, though performance depends on ensemble strategy. Analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

Conclusion: CE-FI provides a privacy-preserving, encoder-agnostic framework for cooperative inference across independently deployed models. Its effectiveness hinges on effective representation alignment; the approach broadens cooperative inference beyond image data but necessitates careful ensemble design and potentially improved alignment mechanisms.

Abstract: Cooperative inference across independently deployed machine learning models is increasingly desirable in distributed environments, as there is a growing need to leverage multiple models while keeping their data and model parameters private. However, existing cooperative frameworks typically rely on sharing input data, model parameters, or a common encoder, which limits their applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings. To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data.
