Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

</details>


### [178] [WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05728)
*Dhruv Suri,Helgi Hilmarsson,Shourya Bose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reassesses ML-based warm-starts for AC-OPF with interior-point solvers, showing primal-only warm starts are ineffective against a correct baseline. Full primal-dual-barrier state predictions dramatically reduce iterations; introduces dual-labeled benchmarks and WARP model achieving large gains and handling topology changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critically evaluate reported iteration reductions from ML warm-starts in AC-OPF and provide a rigorous, fair benchmarking setup to enable robust comparison across solvers and methods.

Method: 1) Analyze evaluation baselines used in prior work and identify misalignment with IPOPT's default interior-point state. 2) Conduct oracle experiments with full primal-dual-barrier state. 3) Develop a dual-labeled AC-OPF benchmark suite with IPOPT-extracted solutions. 4) Propose and evaluate WARP, a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode network predicting the full interior-point state on constraint graphs.

Result: Primal-only warm-starts do not outperform the corrected baseline; the full primal-dual-barrier state reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction). WARP achieves 76% iteration reduction and generalizes to N-1 contingencies without retraining.

Conclusion: Reliable assessment of warm-start methods requires dual-labeled data and a baseline aligned with the solver’s interior-point state. Access to dual variables and barrier multipliers is critical; the complete primal-dual-barrier state yields substantial gains that primal-only methods cannot match. The proposed benchmark and WARP provide a rigorous framework for future study.

Abstract: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

</details>


### [179] [CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05732)
*Md Anwar Hossen,Fatema Siddika,Juan Pablo Munoz,Tanya Roosta,Ali Jannesari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CRAFT is a KL-guided, low-rank representation intervention framework for continual learning in LLMs that routes tasks by output-distribution similarity, updates via KL regularization without full weight updates, and merges changes into shared representations, yielding reduced forgetting and strong performance across scales and task orders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual fine-tuning of LLMs risks catastrophic forgetting; there is a need for scalable, weight-efficient continual learning methods that operate in representation space and leverage task-similarity signals.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) route each task to a group of similar tasks using output-distribution divergence; (2) apply low-rank interventions by fine-tuning with a KL divergence objective against the group's prior state to control forgetting and determine convergence; (3) merge the updated task's interventions into the shared representation using the same KL signal; all stages unified under a single KL-based objective.

Result: Empirically improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based baselines across multiple benchmarks and model scales; robust to task ordering.

Conclusion: Controlling adaptation in representation space guided by output-space divergence provides a scalable, principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

</details>


### [180] [CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05738)
*Mei Wu,Wenchao Weng,Wenxin Su,Wenjie Tang,Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-branch continual learning framework (CoMemNet) for dynamic traffic graphs with an online predictor and a momentum target using Wasserstein-based dynamic sampling and a Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer, achieving SOTA on three datasets with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static graph-based models fail to capture evolving, streaming traffic patterns in non-Euclidean spaces. A continual learning approach is needed to adapt to changing network dynamics while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

Method: 1) An Online branch performs rapid predictions. 2) A momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to form a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler) that selects nodes with significant dynamic changes for training. 3) A lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) stores and replays old knowledge to prevent memory explosion. 4) Two new open-source datasets are provided.

Result: CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art performance across three large-scale real-world datasets. The paper provides code at the given GitHub link.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-branch continual learning framework effectively handles evolving dynamic graphs for traffic prediction, mitigating forgetting via DC sampling and memory replay, and offering practical datasets and code for reproducibility.

Abstract: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

</details>


### [181] [Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05739)
*Mohammad Al Ridhawi,Mahtab Haj Ali,Hussein Al Osman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A behavioral evaluation framework for agentic stock prediction systems uses ensemble LLM judges to score sequential decision behavior across six dimensions, validates with perturbations, and links per-dimension penalties to SAC rewards. Offline testing yields improved MAPE, directional accuracy, and Sharpe, especially in high-volatility episodes; live deployment effects remain untested.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional aggregate metrics (e.g., MAPE, directional accuracy) obscure the quality of interdependent, per-decision actions in agentic trading systems. A per-dimension behavioral evaluation aims to diagnose deficiencies, align behavior with finance goals, and link behavior to risk-adjusted returns.

Method: Collect traces from autonomous decisions, partition into five-day episodes. Have an ensemble of three LLM judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro) score six domain-specific dimensions: regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery. Validate via perturbations on 420 episodes to measure score drops. Compute cross-model agreement (Krippendorff's α up to 0.85). Build a composite behavioral score for cross-episode reporting and correlate with realized 20-day Sharpe (ρ=0.72). Convert deficient per-dimension scores into a penalty term added to Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward, then perform three short fine-tuning cycles within the validation period. Test on held-out 2017–2025 period with offline backtesting: MAPE 0.61%→0.54% (11.5% rel, p<0.001, d=0.31), directional accuracy 71%→74%, Sharpe ratio up 18% (bootstrap CI 8.2%–27.4%). Gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes; results are offline only.

Result: -1.6 to -2.4 point declines on intended dimensions in perturbation tests vs -0.32 on other dimensions (420 episodes). Krippendorff’s α up to 0.85 indicates strong cross-model agreement. Composite behavioral score correlates ρ=0.72 with 20-day Sharpe. After three SAC fine-tuning cycles, held-out period shows MAPE improvement from 0.61% to 0.54% (11.5% rel, p<0.001, d=0.31), directional accuracy 71%→74%, Sharpe ratio up 18% (CI [8.2%, 27.4%]). Improvements are concentrated in high-volatility episodes. All results are from offline backtesting; live deployment effects are not assessed.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that per-dimension behavioral penalties can meaningfully align autonomous trading policies with risk-adjusted performance, yielding measurable offline improvements in predictive accuracy and risk-adjusted return, especially under volatility. However, evidence in live deployment remains needed.

Abstract: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

</details>


### [182] [Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05742)
*Scott Geng,Dutch Hansen,Jerry Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weak-to-strong generalization can occur in standard linear logistic regression under mild data assumptions; the student often surpasses the teacher even without greater capacity, making the phenomenon nearly inevitable for most teacher–student pairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether weak-to-strong generalization extends beyond frontier language models and to challenge the belief that capacity mismatch between student and teacher is required for this phenomenon.

Method: Theoretical analysis of a teacher–student setup using linear logistic regression under mild distributional assumptions, showing that, for most teacher–student pairs, the student generalizes better than the teacher after training with feedback from the teacher alone.

Result: Weak-to-strong generalization holds in this basic setting for the majority of teacher–student pairs, implying that the phenomenon is almost inevitable and does not require increased student expressivity or capacity.

Conclusion: The phenomenon is robust even in simple models, challenging the notion that capacity mismatch is central; suggests fundamental mechanisms allow a weaker teacher to enable a stronger student under feedback, with implications for distillation and learning with feedback.

Abstract: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

</details>


### [183] [RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05750)
*Ivan Montero,Tomasz Jurczyk,Bhuwan Dhingra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RVPO uses a variance-aware reward aggregation with a SoftMin (LogSumExp) penalty to address constraint neglect in multi-objective RLHF, improving safety/formatting adherence while preserving performance across model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fix the tendency of simple arithmetic-mean aggregation to overlook hard bottleneck objectives (e.g., safety, formatting) in multi-objective RLHF by promoting consistency among rewards.

Method: Introduce Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO) that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation via a LogSumExp-based variance penalty, effectively shifting the objective from maximizing the sum to maximizing consistency across rewards. Evaluation on rubric-based medical/scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged signals and tool-calling with rule constraints across Qwen models.

Result: RVPO yields higher overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, p<0.001) and preserves GPQA-Diamond accuracy without late-stage degradation seen in other multi-reward methods, indicating variance regularization reduces constraint neglect across model scales while maintaining general capabilities.

Conclusion: Variance-regularized multi-reward optimization via a soft-min variance penalty improves robust multi-objective alignment, mitigating constraint neglect without sacrificing general performance across model sizes.

Abstract: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

</details>


### [184] [Full-Spectrum Graph Neural Network: Expressive and Scalable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05759)
*Xiaohan Wang,Deyu Bo,Longlong Li,Kelin Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSpecGNN generalizes spectral GNNs to second-order node-pair signals using a bi-variate eigenvalue filter, extending expressivity beyond 1-WL while maintaining scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical spectral GNNs primarily operate on node signals and are limited by the 1-WL expressivity barrier, hindering performance on heterophilic graphs and higher-order relations. A second-order, node-pair aware framework is pursued to surpass these limitations.

Method: Introduce Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN) that (1) lifts signals from the node domain to the node-pair domain and (2) replaces univariate eigenvalue filtering with a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. Demonstrate that standard spectral GNNs are a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN. Establish that FSpecGNN is at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals. Propose scalable implementations avoiding explicit node-pair computations and apply a low-rank approximation to reduce full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters.

Result: The framework achieves the predicted expressivity bound (no more than Local 2-GNN) and universal approximation for node-pair signals. Empirically, it delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks, validating both theory and practical scalability.

Conclusion: FSpecGNN extends spectral GNNs beyond the 1-WL limit by enabling node-pair level processing and bi-variate spectral filtering, achieving universality for node-pair signals, and offering scalable, large-graph applicability with competitive results on heterophilic tasks.

Abstract: It is well established that spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) can universally approximate node signals; however, their expressive power remains bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test, which is mirrored in their lack of universality for higher-order signals. To go beyond this bound, we propose the Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN), a second-order generalization of classical spectral GNNs. FSpecGNN advances spectral filtering in two perspectives: (1) it lifts the signal from the node domain to the node-pair domain; and (2) it extends the univariate spectral filter over eigenvalues to a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. We show that classical spectral GNNs arise as a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN, and prove that FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals, the latter being particularly beneficial for heterophilic graph learning. Moreover, FSpecGNN admits scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair-level computations; combined with a low-rank approximation that reduces full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters, it enables learning on large graphs. Empirically, FSpecGNN validates the predicted expressivity and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

</details>


### [185] [Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05769)
*Myoungjun Kim,Sangwoo Park,Yoseob Han,Jin-Hyun Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AS-LoRA adapts LoRA-based DP federated fine-tuning along three axes (layer-wise, round-wise, and curvature-aware scoring) to mitigate LoRA multiplicative aggregation errors, achieving faster convergence and higher accuracy with no additional privacy cost and lower aggregation overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP federated fine-tuning with LoRA suffers from aggregation errors due to LoRA's multiplicative structure, which are amplified by DP noise; existing methods use a uniform update mode across layers/rounds, ignoring layer asymmetry and round dynamics.

Method: AS-LoRA introduces (i) layer-wise freedom (each layer selects an active component independently), (ii) round-wise adaptivity (selection updates across rounds), and (iii) a curvature-aware score from a second-order loss approximation to guide updates; aims to reduce reconstruction error and improve convergence under DP constraints.

Result: The approach eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, biases solutions toward flatter minima, and does not increase privacy cost. Empirically, it improves results over federated LoRA baselines by up to 7.5 percentage points on GLUE and 12.5 points on MNLI-mm, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation at 33–180× lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead.

Conclusion: AS-LoRA provides a principled, adaptive framework for DP federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA, delivering both theoretical and practical gains over existing aggregation strategies; code is available.

Abstract: Differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, which is further amplified by DP noise and degrades both stability and accuracy. Existing remedies apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and all communication rounds (or alternate them on a fixed schedule), ignoring both the structural asymmetry between the two LoRA factors and the round-wise dynamics of training. We propose AS-LoRA, an adaptive framework defined by three axes (i) layer-wise freedom, in which each layer independently selects its active component, (ii) round-wise adaptivity, in which the selection updates over communication rounds, and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases solutions toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost. Across GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions, AS-LoRA improves over the federated LoRA baselines by up to $+7.5$ pp on GLUE and $+12.5$ pp on MNLI-mm for example, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation methods at $33\text{--}180 \times$ lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead. Code for the proposed method is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

</details>


### [186] [A Measure-Theoretic Finite-Sample Theory for Adaptive-Data Fitted Q-Iteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05791)
*Manuel Haussmann,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified finite-sample analysis of fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable spaces, bridging measure-theoretic MDP foundations, error propagation, and PAC-style bounds to yield online regret guarantees for continuous-space RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Close the gap between successful model-free deep RL in practice and theory, which is hampered by isolated traditions (measure-theoretic MDPs, deterministic error propagation with concentrability, and PAC generalization on simplified topologies).

Method: Develop a unified FQI framework on general Borel spaces; derive finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bounds by chaining measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces; show sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data; extend to cumulative, pathwise online regret for FQI in continuous spaces.

Result: Finite-sample generalization bound for FQI; a first cumulative online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces; demonstration that sequential Rademacher complexity governs Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data.

Conclusion: The results provide foundational theoretical tools bridging measure-theoretic MDPs, error propagation analyses, and PAC-style bounds, enabling formal analysis of modern deep RL algorithms.

Abstract: While reinforcement learning (RL) promises to revolutionize the control of complex nonlinear robotic systems, a profound gap persists between the heuristic success of model-free off-policy deep RL and the underlying theory, which remains largely confined to tabular or linearizable settings. We identify the cause of this gap as an emergent isolation of three traditions: (i) measure-theoretic MDP foundations on general spaces limit their analysis to exact dynamic programming and ignore all error sources of a learning process; (ii) deterministic error propagation analysis addresses the approximation error via concentrability coefficients without a finite-sample analysis of the estimation error; and (iii) PAC generalization bounds characterize the estimation errors of simplified topologies. We bridge these traditions with a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable Borel spaces. Our main result provides a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound by chaining measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces. We prove that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection. We further extend this analysis to provide the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces. These results lay the necessary foundations for the formal analysis of many modern deep RL algorithms.

</details>


### [187] [Revealing Modular Gradient Noise Imbalance in LLMs: Calibrating Adam via Signal-to-Noise Ratio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05794)
*Ziqing Wen,Zhouyang Liu,Jiahuan Wang,Ping Luo,Li Shen,Dongsheng Li,Tao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoLS introduces module-wise learning-rate scaling using module-level SNR estimates to adapt Adam updates, enabling automatic, per-module LR allocation that improves convergence and generalization in LLM training and is compatible with memory-efficient methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit substantial module-level gradient heterogeneity and noise. Existing adaptive optimizers and manually tuned module-specific LRs either neglect module heterogeneity or require costly manual tuning, reducing convergence speed and generalization.

Method: The approach analyzes Adam's noise-damping behavior in high-noise modules and introduces Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS). It estimates module-level signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) during optimization to scale updates, enabling automated per-module LR scaling without manual tuning.

Result: Empirical evaluations on multiple LLM benchmarks show faster convergence and improved generalization with MoLS, achieving performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific LRs and remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.

Conclusion: MoLS provides a principled, automated, module-aware LR scaling method that mitigates module-level gradient heterogeneity effects, reduces manual tuning, and enhances convergence and generalization in large-scale model training.

Abstract: The impressive performance of large language models (LLMs) arises from their massive scale and heterogeneous module composition. However, this structural heterogeneity introduces additional optimization challenges. While adaptive optimizers such as Adam(W) provide per-parameter adaptivity, they do not explicitly account for module-level gradient heterogeneity, resulting in slower convergence, suboptimal performance, or training instability. Existing approaches typically rely on manually tuned module-specific learning rates or specific optimization strategies, which are computationally costly and difficult to generalize across tasks or models. To establish a more principled approach, we first analyze the noise-damping behavior of Adam in high-noise modules and introduce \textbf{Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS)}. MoLS estimates module-level SNRs to scale Adam updates, allowing automated module-wise learning rate allocation without manual tuning. Empirical results through multiple LLM training benchmarks demonstrate that MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization, achieving performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific learning rates, while remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.

</details>


### [188] [Selective Rollout: Mid-Trajectory Termination for Multi-Sample Agent RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05802)
*Zhiyuan Zhai,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gating mechanism for selective rollouts in Group-relative RL training (GRPO) stops groups early based on convergence of partial trajectories, reducing wasted compute and improving sample efficiency; achieved 10.7% faster wall-clock and +2.5 pp held-out success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In agentic RL with long, multi-turn dialogues, many parallel rollout groups end with the same reward, yielding zero within-group variance and wasted computation. Existing prompt-level filters miss intra-rollout signals that could predict zero-variance early.

Method: Introduce a one-parameter gate that stops a group when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between its partial action sequences falls below a threshold. Evaluated on 60 on-policy GRPO iterations in ALFWorld using Qwen2.5-7B across four seeds.

Result: The gated approach finishes 10.7% faster in wall-clock time (95% bootstrap CI excludes 0) and increases held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 percentage points. The improvement is linked to reduced zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution.

Conclusion: In-group divergence of partial trajectories can predict zero-variance groups early, enabling selective rollouts that save compute and improve generalization; code is available for replication.

Abstract: Group-relative RL training (GRPO) samples a small group of parallel rollouts for every training prompt and uses their within-group reward spread to compute per-trajectory advantages. In agentic environments each rollout is a long multi-turn dialogue with one LLM call per step, so this multi-sample multiplier dominates the total training cost. When every rollout of a prompt ends with the same reward, the group has zero reward variance and contributes no gradient, so the extra rollouts add no information; such groups are common in practice (typically around 40% of all groups), so the wasted-compute fraction is substantial rather than marginal. Existing methods filter such groups at the prompt level, either after their rollouts are paid for or before any rollout begins, but both decide without using information that becomes available during the rollout itself. We instead ask whether the in-group divergence between the partial trajectories at an intermediate step can already predict that the group will be zero-variance: when the parallel rollouts have already converged on the same action prefix, the group is on track to produce a single reward, and we can stop early. We propose a one-parameter gate that stops a group when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between its partial action sequences falls below a threshold. On a 60-iteration on-policy GRPO run on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, averaged over four random seeds, the gated arm finishes 10.7% faster in wall-clock (bootstrap 95% CI excludes 0) and shifts held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 pp, with the held-out gain tracing to a measurable reduction in zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution. Code is available at https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/selective-rollout.

</details>


### [189] [Retrieval from Within: An Intrinsic Capability of Attention-Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05806)
*Elad Hoffer,Yochai Blau,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A decoder-attention based retrieval within a single model (INTRA) unifies retrieval and generation by scoring pre-encoded evidence chunks with decoder attention, and outperforms engineered pipelines on QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To remove the retriever-generator mismatch in RAG and to leverage latent retrieval in encoder–decoder models by retrieving from internal representations.

Method: Introduce INTRA: pre-encode evidence chunks with an encoder; during decoding, the decoder's attention queries score these chunks and reuse them as context; no external retriever; encoder states are reused across queries to amortize encoding cost.

Result: On QA benchmarks, INTRA surpasses strong engineered retrieval pipelines in both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality.

Conclusion: Attention-based models inherently possess a retriever mechanism that can be elicited, enabling a unified, amortized retrieval-and-generation architecture without external retrieval modules.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) typically treats retrieval and generation as separate systems. We ask whether an attention-based encoder-decoder can instead retrieve directly from its own internal representations. We introduce INTRA (INTrinsic Retrieval via Attention), a framework where decoder attention queries score pre-encoded evidence chunks that are then directly reused as context for generation. By construction, INTRA unifies retrieval and generation, eliminating the retriever-generator mismatch typical of RAG pipelines. This design also amortizes context encoding by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries. On question-answering benchmarks, INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality. Our results demonstrate that attention-based models already possess a retrieval mechanism that can be elicited, rather than added as an external module.

</details>


### [190] [A Testable Certificate for Constant Collapse in Teacher-Guided VAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05813)
*Zegu Zhang,Jianhua Peng,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: For a fixed nonconstant teacher, the best constant student is the dataset-average teacher; its alignment cost equals the teacher mutual information I_T(X;T). If a latent-only witness achieves alignment loss below this threshold (with margin), the witness cannot be constant in the input, certifying a boundary for input-independent constant collapse in VAEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond symptom-based diagnosis of posterior collapse to a concrete, certifiable boundary. Address the specific failure mode of input-independent constant collapse with an exact threshold.

Method: Derive an exact threshold by comparing the best constant student to a nonconstant teacher distribution, expressing alignment cost as the teacher mutual information I_T(X;T). Evaluate with CIFAR-100 and Tiny-ImageNet-200 experiments, using per-seed teacher search and fixed-target runs to observe prevention–collapse–rescue dynamics; compare with standard VAE baselines.

Result: The analysis yields a precise boundary: if a latent-only witness attains alignment loss below I_T(X;T) (with safety margin), the witness must vary with input. Empirically, preventing alignment drives the witness into the constant-student regime; restoring alignment from a collapsed checkpoint rescues the certificate. Across datasets and multiple teachers, the boundary remains intact. The standard VAE baselines remain negative under the raw certificate.

Conclusion: The work converts a qualitative failure mode into a measurable certificate specific to input-dependent variation through the latent pathway. The guarantee is intentionally narrow: it certifies that the matched nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway rather than ruling out all forms of posterior collapse.

Abstract: Posterior collapse in variational autoencoders is often diagnosed by its symptoms: a small KL term, a strong decoder, or weak use of the latent code. These signals are useful, but they do not define a collapse boundary. We study a concrete failure mode, input-independent constant collapse, and show that this case admits an exact threshold. For any fixed nonconstant teacher distribution \(T(\cdot\mid x)\), the best constant student is the dataset-average teacher distribution, and its alignment cost is the teacher mutual information \(I_T(X;T)\). Therefore, if a strictly latent-only raw witness achieves alignment loss below this value, with a safety margin, the witness cannot be constant in the input.
  This identity turns a qualitative failure mode into a measurable one. In CIFAR-100 experiments with per-seed teacher search, full training stays on the certified side of the boundary, removing alignment drives the raw witness into the constant-student regime, and restarting from a collapsed checkpoint with alignment enabled restores the certificate. Tiny-ImageNet-200 fixed-target runs show the same prevention--collapse--rescue pattern across three independently searched teachers. Standard VAE-style baselines, including methods that preserve reconstruction quality or post-hoc predictability, remain negative under the raw certificate. The guarantee is intentionally narrow: it certifies that the matched nonconstant teacher-relative variation passes through the latent pathway, rather than claiming that all forms of posterior collapse have been ruled out.

</details>


### [191] [HCInfer: An Efficient Inference System via Error Compensation for Resource-Constrained Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05819)
*Shen Xu,Xiangwen Zhuge,Zhe Xu,Yingkun Hu,Zheng Yang,Yunhao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCInfer offloads residual error-compensation to CPU while running the compressed backbone on GPU, using asynchronous compensation and dynamic rank allocation to improve accuracy and speed for memory-constrained LLM deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs on consumer hardware face memory constraints due to large parameter counts. Existing compression/offloading methods either degrade accuracy or create throughput bottlenecks. Error compensation branches (LoRA-style) are storage-heavy but only intermittently accessed, making them good candidates for offloading.

Method: HCInfer is a heterogeneous inference system that runs the compressed backbone on GPU and offloads residual compensation to CPU. It includes an asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide overhead and maximize accuracy recovery.

Result: Maximum accuracy improvement of 5.2% on downstream tasks compared to the compression-only model, and up to 10.4x speedup compared to the full-precision model.

Conclusion: HCInfer demonstrates that offloading residual compensation to CPU with asynchronous processing and adaptive resource allocation can significantly improve both accuracy and throughput for memory-constrained LLM deployment.

Abstract: LLMs often struggle with memory-constrained deployment on consumer-grade hardware due to their massive parameter sizes. While existing solutions such as model compression and offloading improve deployment feasibility, they often suffer from substantial accuracy degradation or severe throughput bottlenecks. Recent error compensation methods recover accuracy through auxiliary LoRA-style branches, and we observe that these branches are inherently amenable to offloading: they require substantial parameter storage but access only a small subset of compensation parameters during each inference step. Motivated by this opportunity, we propose HCInfer, a heterogeneous inference system that offloads residual compensation to the CPU while executing the compressed backbone on the GPU, and further introduces an asynchronous compensation pipeline and sensitivity-aware dynamic rank allocation to hide compensation overhead and maximize accuracy recovery. Experimental results show that HCInfer achieves a maximum accuracy improvement of 5.2% on downstream tasks compared to compression model and sustaining a maximum speedup of 10.4x compared to full-precision model.

</details>


### [192] [MDN: Parallelizing Stepwise Momentum for Delta Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05838)
*Yulong Huang,Xiang Liu,Hongxiang Huang,Xiaopeng Lin,Zunchang Liu,Xiaowen Chu,Zeke Xie,Bojun Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Momentum DeltaNet (MDN) introduces a momentum-based, chunkwise parallel linear attention with geometrically reordered updates to stabilize online SGD, analyzed as a second-order dynamical system; achieves competitive throughput and improved accuracy over strong LA baselines on 400M and 1.3B parameter LMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale linear attention to long sequences without quadratic complexity while addressing instability and suboptimal convergence of naive SGD in LA; momentum helps but standard approaches conflict with training efficiency; MDN aims to deliver stable, efficient momentum-based LA.

Method: Chunkwise parallel linear attention with a stepwise momentum rule implemented by geometrically reordering update coefficients; dynamical systems view of the momentum recurrence as a second-order system with complex conjugate eigenvalues to guide stable gating constraints; Triton-based kernels for performance; open-source code at provided URL.

Result: MDN achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines (Transformers, Mamba2, GDN) on 400M and 1.3B parameter models across diverse downstream benchmarks; training throughput is comparable to Mamba2 and KDA, indicating practical efficiency.

Conclusion: MDN provides a stable, efficient momentum-based LA with competitive throughput and improved performance relative to key LA baselines; dynamical analysis informs gating to ensure stability; code released for replication.

Abstract: Linear Attention (LA) offers a promising paradigm for scaling large language models (LLMs) to long sequences by avoiding the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Recent LA models such as Mamba2 and GDN interpret linear recurrences as closed-form online stochastic gradient descent (SGD), but naive SGD updates suffer from rapid information decay and suboptimal convergence in optimization. While momentum-based optimizers provide a natural remedy, they pose challenges in simultaneously achieving training efficiency and effectiveness. To address this, we develop a chunkwise parallel algorithm for LA with a stepwise momentum rule by geometrically reordering the update coefficients. Further, from a dynamical systems perspective, we analyze the momentum-based recurrence as a second-order system that introduces complex conjugate eigenvalues. This analysis guides the design of stable gating constraints. The resulting model, Momentum DeltaNet (MDN), leverages Triton kernels to achieve comparable training throughput with competitive linear models such as Mamba2 and KDA. Extensive experiments on the 400M and 1.3B parameter models demonstrate consistent performance improvements over strong baselines, including Transformers, Mamba2 and GDN, across diverse downstream evaluation benchmarks. Code: https://github.com/HuuYuLong/MomentumDeltaNet .

</details>


### [193] [Hypothesis generation and updating in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05851)
*Hua-Dong Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can be modeled by a two-parameter Bayesian fit in a controlled number-game setting but exhibit systematic biases and a generation/evaluation gap, limiting their utility for general scientific inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify whether LLMs perform Bayesian-like inference, how biases arise, and how results compare to optimal Bayesian reasoning and human performance across different inference probes.

Method: Study a 'number game' where a learner infers a rule from a few positive integers. Evaluate posterior via three probes (posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, hypothesis generation). Compare LLMs to an optimal Bayesian model and humans, test posterior consistency across probes, and fit a two-parameter Bayesian model to the LLMs. Analyze biases and generalization. 

Result: LLMs’ behavior is largely captured by a two-parameter Bayesian fit but with systematic deviations. A strong-sampling bias induces an implicit Occam’s razor, preferring narrower hypotheses; a think-mode increases reliance on priors. There is a robust evaluation–generation gap: LLMs pick more correct hypotheses during evaluation but generate simpler, rule-like hypotheses. The Bayesian-with-bias pattern does not extrapolate well: models can appear to hold rule-like hypotheses over observed examples yet generalize poorly to unobserved parts of the domain.

Conclusion: LLMs are not yet reliable general problem solvers for scientific inference. Their inference is biased and context-dependent, with poor generalization beyond observed data. This calls for careful interpretation of LLM-based hypothesis formation and suggests limitations in using LLMs to autonomously conduct scientific inference without addressing these biases.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly help people solve problems, from debugging code to repairing machinery. This process requires generating plausible hypotheses from partial descriptions, then updating them as more information arrives. Yet how LLMs perform this form of inference, and how close it is to optimal, remains unclear. We study this question in the number game, a controlled setting in which a learner infers the hypothesis supported by a few positive integers, such as $\{16, 8, 2, 64\}$: a rule like powers of 2 or an interval like numbers near 20. We measure the posterior over hypotheses using three complementary probes: posterior prediction, hypothesis evaluation, and hypothesis generation. We then compare LLM behavior with an optimal Bayesian model and human behavior, and test whether the same posterior is expressed across probes. LLMs are often well described by a two-parameter Bayesian fit, but with systematic offsets: by default they show a strong-sampling assumption that creates an implicit Occam's razor, favoring narrower hypotheses, while thinking mode shifts them toward greater prior reliance. We also find a robust evaluation--generation gap: LLMs select more correct hypotheses during hypothesis evaluation but generate simpler, more rule-like hypotheses. Finally, this Bayesian-with-bias pattern does not extrapolate. Models can behave as if they hold rule-like hypotheses over observed examples, yet generalize poorly to parts of the hypothesis domain not covered by those examples. Our results highlight a limitation of LLMs as general problem solvers, especially for scientific inference, where hypotheses must go beyond the data.

</details>


### [194] [Measuring Learning Progress via Gradient-Momentum Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05856)
*Samuel Blad,Martin Längkvist,Amy Loutfi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC) provides a learning-signal derived from optimization dynamics that measures a sample's usefulness for ongoing learning by the per-parameter normalized absolute product of the current gradient with the momentum from previous gradients. It robustly filters noise, enabling emergent curriculum that prioritizes learning speed over difficulty; replacing prediction error with GMC in curiosity-driven RL improves robustness to observation noise (demonstrated on MiniGrid).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction-error-based signals often conflate meaningful patterns with random noise and fail to reflect which samples truly drive ongoing learning. There is a need for a signal that captures gradient usefulness for continual learning, especially under noisy observations, to guide intrinsic curiosity-driven exploration.

Method: Introduce Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC): for each parameter, compute the normalized absolute product of the current gradient g_i and the momentum m_i (e.g., an exponential moving average of past gradients). Normalize across parameters to produce a per-sample GMC score, which aggregates into a sample-level signal for intrinsic motivation. The momentum term acts as a filter for noise and oscillations, highlighting samples that contribute to ongoing parameter updates. Integrate GMC as a replacement for prediction error within curiosity-driven architectures and evaluate on controlled experiments and MiniGrid.

Result: GMC demonstrates noise robustness and induces emergent curriculum effects, with the signal prioritizing samples by learning speed rather than difficulty. In MiniGrid experiments, substituting GMC for prediction error improves robustness to observation noise when used in existing curiosity-driven RL frameworks.

Conclusion: GMC provides a robust, optimization-dynamics–based intrinsic signal for curiosity-driven exploration that better differentiates informative samples from noise, enabling faster learning and resilient exploration. It can serve as a drop-in replacement for prediction error in existing architectures, yielding emergent curricula and improved performance under observation noise.

Abstract: Measuring learning progress is essential for curiosity-driven exploration in reinforcement learning, but widely used signals such as prediction error often fail to distinguish meaningful, learnable patterns from random noise. This paper proposes Gradient-Momentum Coupling (GMC), a signal derived from optimization dynamics that quantifies how useful each sample's gradient is for ongoing learning by measuring its per-parameter normalized absolute product with the momentum from previous gradients. By leveraging momentum's natural filtering of noise and oscillations, GMC identifies samples that contribute to ongoing parameter updates. Controlled experiments demonstrate noise robustness and emergent curriculum learning, with the signal prioritizing tasks by learning speed rather than difficulty. Experiments on MiniGrid suggest that replacing prediction error with GMC within existing curiosity-driven architectures can improve robustness to observation noise.

</details>


### [195] [Offline Reinforcement Learning for Rotation Profile Control in Tokamaks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05857)
*Rohit Sonker,Hiro Josep Farre Kaga,Jiayu Chen,Andrew Rothstein,Ian Char,Ricardo Shousha,Egemen Kolemen,Jeff Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline RL and offline model-based RL trained on historical DIII-D data to control MRI rotation-profile in tokamaks; probabilistic dynamics models generate rollouts; deployed on DIII-D with promising real-world results; highlights data efficiency and deployment challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Control of the plasma rotation profile is high-dimensional and affected by multiple actuators and plasma conditions. RL can model complex, nonlinear interactions and enable multi-input multi-output control, but accurate simulators are scarce, motivating offline data-driven approaches.

Method: Train offline RL and offline model-based RL policies using historical DIII-D data. Use probabilistic plasma-dynamics models to generate rollouts for RL training. Deploy the resulting policy on the DIII-D tokamak to evaluate real-world performance.

Result: Policy demonstrated promising real-world results on the DIII-D tokamak. The work provides insights into training and deploying RL for complex physical devices using limited past data.

Conclusion: Offline data-driven RL and MBRL show potential for rotation-profile control in tokamaks, but face challenges related to data coverage, model fidelity, safety, and transfer from historical data to real-time operation.

Abstract: Tokamaks remain leading candidates for achieving practical fusion energy, yet many important control problems inside these devices are still difficult or unsolved. One such challenge is controlling the plasma rotation profile, which strongly influences stability, confinement, and transport. While the average rotation can be controlled, controlling the full profile is challenging due to high dimensionality, response to multiple actuators and dependence on plasma condition. Learning-based control methods, such as reinforcement learning (RL), provide a potential solution to this challenging problem with ability to model complex interactions leading to effective multi-input multi-output control. However, learning such policies is challenging due to the lack of accurate simulators that can model the rotation profile dynamics. In this work, we investigate the use of offline RL and offline model-based RL algorithms for rotation profile control, training them solely on historical data from the DIII-D tokamak. Our final method uses probabilistic models of plasma dynamics to generate rollouts for RL training. We deploy this policy on the DIII-D Tokamak and observe promising real-world results. We conclude by highlighting key challenges and insights from training and deploying an RL policy on a complex physical device while using only limited past data.

</details>


### [196] [Do Neural Operators Forget Geometry? The Forgetting Hypothesis in Deep Operator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05862)
*Yanming Xia,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Depth-driven geometric forgetting in neural operators; a lightweight geometry memory injection mitigates forgetting and reveals geometry retention as a structural necessity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate why neural operators struggle with irregular geometries and whether the issue is due to depth-based loss of geometric information; propose a hypothesis and empirical probe.

Method: Layer-wise geometric probing for spectral and attention-based operators; design a geometry memory injection mechanism with minimal overhead; evaluate on accuracy, stability, generalization.

Result: Geometric fidelity decays with depth across operator types; forgetting degrades performance; memory injection mitigates forgetting and uncovers a geometry shortcut instability in transformers.

Conclusion: Geometric retention is essential; simple injection suffices to restore geometric constraints; geometry-aware design improves robustness on irregular domains.

Abstract: Neural operators perform well on structured domains, yet their behaviour on irregular geometries remains poorly understood. We show that this limitation is not merely an encoding issue, but a depth-wise failure mode inherent to deep operator architectures.
  We formalise the Geometric Forgetting Hypothesis: due to the Markovian structure of operator layers and their reliance on global mixing mechanisms, neural operators progressively lose access to domain geometry as depth increases. Using layer-wise geometric probing, we demonstrate that both spectral and attention-based operators systematically lose geometric fidelity.
  We show that this geometric forgetting degrades accuracy, stability, and generalisation. To counteract it, we introduce a lightweight geometry memory injection mechanism that restores geometric constraints at intermediate depths with minimal architectural overhead. This simple intervention consistently mitigates forgetting and exposes a geometric shortcut instability in transformer-based operators, revealing that geometric retention is a structural requirement rather than a design choice.

</details>


### [197] [SOPE: Stabilizing Off-Policy Evaluation for Online RL with Prior Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05863)
*Carlo Romeo,Girolamo Macaluso,Alessandro Sestini,Andrew D. Bagdanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOPE automates early stopping of offline training phases using an actor-aligned OPE signal, removing manual schedule tuning and yielding better performance with lower compute in online RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-length or manually-tuned offline training schedules in RL can be inefficient: they either waste prior data or overfit, while incurring high computational costs.

Method: SOPE evaluates the critic on a held-out validation set under the current policy’s action distribution to obtain an OPE signal and halts gradient updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, thereby dynamically controlling offline-training length.

Result: Across 25 Minari benchmark continuous control tasks, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% and reduces TFLOPs by up to 22x, demonstrating improved sample and computational efficiency with adaptive schedules.

Conclusion: Adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules outperform static, exhaustive ones, providing a practical method to balance sample efficiency and computation in online RL with prior data.

Abstract: Incorporating prior data into online reinforcement learning accelerates training but typically forces a difficult trade-off between high computational costs and long, multi-stage training pipelines. While fixed-length stabilization phases are significantly more computationally efficient than static update schedules, they require task-dependent manual tuning, risking either the waste of prior knowledge or severe overfitting. To address this, we propose SOPE, an algorithm that uses an actor-aligned Off-Policy Policy Evaluation (OPE) signal as an automated early-stopping mechanism to dynamically control the length of offline training phases. By evaluating the critic on a held-out validation split under the current policy's action distribution, SOPE halts gradient updates exactly when out-of-distribution benefits saturate, eliminating the need for manual schedule tuning. Evaluated on 25 continuous control tasks from the Minari benchmark suite, SOPE improves baseline performance by up to 45.6% while reducing the required TFLOPs by up to 22x, thus balancing the tradeoff between sample and computational efficiency. These findings demonstrate that adaptive, evaluation-driven update schedules are more effective than relying on static, exhaustive update schedules.

</details>


### [198] [QuadraSHAP: Stable and Scalable Shapley Values for Product Games via Gauss-Legendre Quadrature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05870)
*Majid Mohammadi,Grigory Reznikov,Pavel Sinitcyn,Krikamol Muandet,Siu Lun Chau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shapley values in product games admit a one-dimensional integral form; exact with sufficient quadrature nodes; efficient Gauss-Legendre quadrature and parallel implementation lead to fast, stable computation (QuadraSHAP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficient Shapley-value computation when the coalition value factorizes multiplicatively, common in ML models with product kernels or tree ensembles, enabling scalable explanations.

Method: Derives exact integral representation of each player's Shapley value as an integral over [0,1] of a degree-(d-1) polynomial; uses Gauss-Legendre quadrature with m_q nodes to compute the integral. Proves exactness when m_q >= ceil(d/2); otherwise provides near-exact approximations with geometric error decay. Implements numerically in log-space and parallelizes via associative scan with O(d m_q) work and O(log d) time.

Result: Quadrature-based Shapley computation (QuadraSHAP) achieves highly precise estimates; experimentally the fastest numerically stable method across configurations; handles thousands of features efficiently.

Conclusion: The approach offers provable guarantees for exactness/approximation and scalable, stable computation of Shapley values in multiplicative (product) games, with practical impact on ML explainability for models with product structures.

Abstract: We study the efficient computation of Shapley values for \emph{product games} -- cooperative games in which the coalition value factorizes as a product of per-player terms. Such games arise in machine learning explainability whenever the value function inherits a multiplicative structure from the underlying model, as in kernel methods with product kernels and tree-based models. Our key result is that the Shapley value of each player in a product game admits an exact one-dimensional integral representation: the weighted sum over exponentially many feature coalitions collapses to the integral of a degree-$(d-1)$ polynomial over $[0,1]$, where $d$ is the total number of features. This yields a Gauss--Legendre quadrature scheme that is \emph{provably exact} whenever the number of nodes satisfies $m_q \geq \lceil d/2 \rceil$, and otherwise provides a \emph{near-exact} approximation with error provably decaying geometrically in $m_q$. In practice, a few hundred nodes can achieve highly precise estimates even with thousands of features. Building on this formulation, we derive a numerically stable implementation via log-space evaluation, together with an efficient parallel implementation based on associative scan primitives that achieves $O(d\,m_q)$ total work and $O(\log d)$ parallel time. Experiments show that \textsc{QuadraSHAP} is the fastest numerically stable method across all tested configurations.

</details>


### [199] [Retain-Neutral Surrogates for Min-Max Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05871)
*Junhao Cai,Dohun Kim,Dowon Kim,Sung Il Choi,Chengjun Jin,Juhyun Park,Changhee Joo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROSU (Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning) offers a lightweight, theory-backed method for machine unlearning that improves forgetting while preserving retained performance, by constraining surrogate updates to be orthogonal to retain gradients and maximizing forget gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragility of approximate/min-max unlearning when forget and retain gradients align, which can cause perturbations to harm retention even as forgetting occurs.

Method: Formulate unlearning as a constrained surrogate optimization: maximize first-order forget gain while enforcing zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, a transported outer update, and amplification along the retain-neutral direction.

Result: Theoretical contributions include a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, a positive-alignment regime where ROSU reduces surrogate retain loss relative to standard min-max perturbations, and near-equivalence when forget/retain gradients are nearly orthogonal. Empirically, on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP, ROSU provides the clearest gains in high-coupling regimes and remains competitive elsewhere.

Conclusion: ROSU delivers a principled, efficient strategy for robust machine unlearning under gradient coupling, with both theoretical guarantees and empirically favorable performance in regimes of strong forget-retain coupling.

Abstract: Machine unlearning seeks to remove the influence of designated training data while preserving performance on the remaining data. Approximate unlearning can be viewed as a local editing problem; in min-max unlearning, the key local object is the surrogate point at which the retain objective is evaluated. When forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned, an unconstrained forget-maximizing perturbation can move to a surrogate point that increases retain loss. We propose Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU), which constrains the inner surrogate construction by maximizing first-order forget gain subject to zero first-order retain change under a fixed perturbation budget. This yields a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation, a lightweight transported outer update, and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. Our analysis establishes (i) a curvature-controlled second-order bound on retain damage, (ii) a positive-alignment regime in which ROSU strictly reduces surrogate retain loss relative to standard min-max perturbations, and (iii) near-equivalence when the two gradients are nearly orthogonal. Across vision and language benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, TOFU, WMDP), the empirical pattern follows this geometry: ROSU gives its clearest gains in high-coupling regimes while remaining competitive elsewhere.

</details>


### [200] [RepFlow: Representation Enhanced Flow Matching for Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05890)
*Yifei Xie,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RepFlow: joint representation learning and Conditional Flow Matching for causal effect estimation from observational data; uses entropically regularized Wasserstein distance to balance treated/control, adds L2 normalization for stability; achieves improved point and distributional causal effect estimation across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal inference with observational data suffers from missing counterfactuals and selection bias; existing methods focus on point estimates and struggle to model distributions of potential outcomes.

Method: Joint optimization of representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM); minimize entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations to reduce selection bias; apply L2 normalization on latent representations for numerical stability; use a flow model to capture the distribution of potential outcomes given balanced representations.

Result: Extensive experiments on benchmarks show RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

Conclusion: RepFlow provides a distribution-aware causal inference framework by learning balanced representations and employing conditional flow matching, yielding superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data has become increasingly critical in diverse fields including healthcare, economics, and social policy. The fundamental challenge in causal inference arises from the missing counterfactuals and the selection bias. Existing methods are largely limited to point estimates and lack the capacity for distribution modeling. In this work, we propose RepFlow, a novel framework that formulates causal effect estimation as a joint optimization problem integrating representation learning with Conditional Flow Matching (CFM).
  RepFlow mitigates selection bias by minimizing the entropically regularized Wasserstein distance between treated and control representations.
  To enhance numerical stability, we further introduce an $L_2$ normalization constraint on latent representations.
  This balanced representation enables the flow model to accurately capture the distribution of potential outcomes. Extensive experiments across a wide range of benchmarks demonstrate that RepFlow consistently outperforms existing methods in both point and distributional causal effect estimation.

</details>


### [201] [VARS-FL: Validation-Aligned Client Selection for Non-IID Federated Learning in IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05896)
*Mohamed Lakas,Mohamed Amine Ferrag*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VARS-FL introduces a validation-aligned reputation scoring mechanism for client selection in Federated Learning. By measuring the reduction in server-side validation loss caused by each client's update, it aggregates signals into a history-aware Reputation score (sliding window of contributions + logarithmic participation term). It remains compatible with FedAvg and requires no changes to local training. Empirically, on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion task with 100 clients, VARS-FL improves accuracy, F1-Macro, loss, and reduces rounds to convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy) compared to FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FL with non-IID data, stateless client selection based on local proxies can misalign with global objectives, causing slow convergence and unstable training. This is exacerbated in IoT/IIoT settings with highly heterogeneous data. A history-aware, validation-aligned selection strategy could better capture client quality and improve convergence and robustness.

Method: Compute per-round contribution as the reduction in server-side validation loss attributed to a client’s update. Aggregate contributions into a Reputation score using a sliding-window average of recent contributions and a logarithmically scaled participation term to balance exploration-exploitation. Use this Reputation score for client selection in each round while remaining compatible with FedAvg (no changes to local training or aggregation).

Result: Experimental evaluation on Edge-IIoTset with 100 clients across multiple seeds shows VARS-FL yields higher accuracy, higher F1-Macro, and lower loss than FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice, with up to 36% fewer rounds to achieve 80% accuracy, demonstrating improved convergence reliability in heterogeneous IoT environments.

Conclusion: Validation-aligned, history-aware client selection enhances FL performance in non-IID IoT/IIoT scenarios without modifying local training or aggregation, offering faster convergence and more reliable training.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) systems typically employ stateless client selection, treating each communication round independently and ignoring accumulated evidence of client contribution quality. Under non-IID data, this leads to slow convergence and unstable training, particularly when selection relies on local proxies (e.g., training loss) that are misaligned with the global optimization objective. These challenges are especially pronounced in Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) environments, where data is highly heterogeneous and distributed across devices observing different traffic patterns. In this paper, we propose VARS-FL (Validation-Aligned Reputation Scoring for Federated Learning), a client selection framework that quantifies each client's contribution using the reduction in server-side validation loss induced by its update. These per-round signals are aggregated into a Reputation score that combines a sliding-window average of recent contributions with a logarithmically scaled participation term, enabling robust exploration-exploitation selection. VARS-FL requires no changes to local training or aggregation and remains fully compatible with standard FedAvg. We evaluate VARS-FL on a 15-class non-IID IoT intrusion detection task using the Edge-IIoTset dataset, with 100 clients across multiple seeds, and compare it against FedAvg, Oort, and Power-of-Choice. VARS-FL consistently improves accuracy, F1-Macro, and loss, while accelerating convergence (up to 36% fewer rounds to reach 80% accuracy). These results demonstrate that validation-aligned, history-aware client selection provides a more reliable and efficient training process for federated learning in heterogeneous IoT environments.

</details>


### [202] [VisMMOE: Exploiting Visual-Expert Affinity for Efficient Visual-Language MoE Offloading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05899)
*Cheng Xu,Xiaofeng Hou,Jiacheng Liu,Chao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VisMMoE prunes visual tokens to increase expert locality in VL-MoE models, enabling faster offloading on memory-limited devices with preserved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VL-MoE models suffer high memory and irregular expert accesses due to many visual tokens; existing offloading methods tuned for text are ineffective for visual-heavy workloads.

Method: Introduce visual-expert affinity; combine affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration; prune redundant visual tokens to concentrate expert demand; implement across multiple frameworks; evaluate on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks.

Result: End-to-end inference performance improves up to 2.68x and 1.61x over strong baselines; maintains competitive accuracy; improved expert locality and prefetch effectiveness demonstrated.

Conclusion: Pruning visual tokens to shape expert demand enables more efficient VL-MoE offloading on memory-constrained platforms; VisMMoE offers a practical, framework-agnostic solution with substantial speedups.

Abstract: Large-scale vision-language mixture-of-experts (VL-MoE) models provide strong multimodal capability, but efficient deployment on memory-constrained platforms remains difficult. Existing MoE offloading systems are largely designed for text-centric workloads and become much less effective for visual-heavy inputs, where large numbers of visual tokens induce broader and less predictable expert accesses. We present VisMMoE, a VL-MoE offloading system built on a single systems insight: pruning redundant visual tokens can improve offloading not only by reducing computation, but also by reshaping expert demand. We refer to this effect as \textit{visual-expert affinity}: token pruning makes expert accesses more concentrated within layers and more stable across layers, producing a smaller and more predictable expert working set. Guided by this insight, VisMMoE combines affinity-aware token compression, lookahead expert prediction, and cache/pipeline orchestration to improve expert locality and prefetch effectiveness under tight memory budgets. We implement VisMMoE on multiple frameworks and evaluate it on representative VL-MoE models and benchmarks. VisMMoE improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 2.68x and 1.61x, respectively, over strong baselines for today's VL-MoE deployments while maintaining competitive accuracy.

</details>


### [203] [Quadratic Objective Perturbation: Curvature-Based Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05905)
*Daniel Cortild,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP) for DP-ERM, replacing linear perturbation with a random quadratic form to induce strong convexity via curvature; enables DP under weaker assumptions in the interpolation regime, extends to inexact solves, and offers empirical excess risk guarantees with favorable comparison to LOP and efficient optimization via splitting methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gradient-boundedness limitation of Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) by leveraging curvature-based perturbations to achieve privacy, enabling models with unbounded gradients.

Method: Perturb the empirical objective with a random quadratic term to induce strong convexity through curvature; analyze differential privacy guarantees (ε, δ) in the interpolation regime; extend to approximate solutions; derive excess risk guarantees; compare theoretically and numerically to LOP; discuss algorithmic implementation using splitting schemes.

Result: Establishes (ε, δ)-DP under weaker assumptions in the interpolation regime; privacy guarantees hold for inexact solves; provides utility guarantees via empirical excess risk; shows theoretical and numerical advantages over LOP; outlines efficient algorithmic solutions via modern splitting methods.

Conclusion: Curvature-based (quadratic) objective perturbation offers a viable DP-ERM approach that relaxes gradient boundedness requirements, enforces stability through curvature, and enables sensitivity control via spectral properties. It yields efficient solvable formulations and favorable comparisons to LOP.

Abstract: Objective perturbation is a standard mechanism in differentially private empirical risk minimization. In particular, Linear Objective Perturbation (LOP) enforces privacy by adding a random linear term, while strong convexity and stability are ensured by an additional deterministic quadratic term.
