However, this approach requires the strong assumption of bounded gradients of the loss function, which excludes many modern machine learning models. In this work, we introduce Quadratic Objective Perturbation (QOP), which perturbs the objective with a random quadratic form. This perturbation induces strong convexity and enforces stability of the problem through curvature, thereby enabling privacy and allowing sensitivity to be controlled through spectral properties of the perturbation rather than assumptions on the gradients. As a result, we obtain $(\varepsilon, δ)$-differential privacy under weaker assumptions, in the interpolation regime. Furthermore, we extend the analysis to account for approximate solutions, showing that privacy guarantees are preserved under inexact solves. Additionally, we derive utility guarantees in terms of empirical excess risk, and provide a theoretical and numerical comparison to LOP, highlighting the advantages of curvature-based perturbations. Finally, we discuss algorithmic aspects and show that the resulting problems can be solved efficiently using modern splitting schemes.

</details>


### [204] [From Drops to Grid: Noise-Aware Spatio-Temporal Neural Process for Rainfall Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05912)
*Rafael Pablos Sarabia,Joachim Nyborg,Morten Birk,Ira Assent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DropsToGrid is a Neural Process-based method that densifies rainfall by fusing sparse, noisy stations with radar context to generate high-resolution, stochastic rainfall fields with calibrated uncertainties; it excels in sparse and cross-regional scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution rainfall fields are essential for forecasting and management, but existing measurements are biased/low-resolution. Densification methods struggle with skewed/localized rainfall, noise, and spatio-temporal fusion, and uncertainty quantification is often missing.

Method: The approach uses a Neural Process framework with multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion to integrate irregular, noisy station sequences with radar context, producing stochastic rainfall estimates and explicit uncertainty quantification.

Result: On real-world datasets, DropsToGrid outperforms operational and deep learning baselines, delivering accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, effective even with few stations and in cross-regional scenarios.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a robust probabilistic densification framework for rainfall, combining sparse observations with radar context to yield high-resolution maps and reliable uncertainty, with practical benefits for forecasting and hazard mitigation.

Abstract: High-resolution rainfall observations are crucial for weather forecasting, water management, and hazard mitigation. Traditional operational measurements are often biased and low-resolution, limiting their ability to capture local rainfall. Accurate high-resolution rainfall maps require integrating sparse surface observations, yet existing deep learning densification methods are hindered by rainfall's skewed, localized nature, noise, and limited spatio-temporal fusion. We present DropsToGrid, a Neural Process-based method that generates dense rainfall fields by fusing temporal sequences from noisy, irregularly distributed private weather stations with spatial context from radar. Leveraging multi-scale feature extraction, temporal attention, and multi-modal fusion, the model produces stochastic, continuous rainfall estimates and explicitly quantifies uncertainty. Evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that DropsToGrid outperforms both operational and deep learning baselines, generating accurate high-resolution rainfall maps with well-calibrated uncertainty, even when only few stations are available and in cross-regional scenarios.

</details>


### [205] [Near-Policy: Accelerating On-Policy Distillation via Asynchronous Generation and Selective Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05940)
*Miao Rang,Zhenni Bi,Hang Zhou,Kai Han,Xuechun Wang,An Xiao,Xinghao Chen,Yunhe Wang,Hanting Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asynchronous near-policy distillation (NPD) with Δ-IFD filtering and sparse updates improves efficiency and performance for autoregressive models, outperforming on-policy RL and SFT baselines and achieving state-of-the-art scores on a 1B-scale model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address distribution mismatch in standard distillation for autoregressive models and the high computational cost of on-policy RL; seek a faster, stable alternative that preserves near-policy behavior.

Method: Decoupled training: student-generated data are produced asynchronously; supervised fine-tuning with sequence packing; Δ-IFD heuristic filters extreme samples; sparse updates mitigate policy lag; mechanism to keep updates within proximal learning region.

Result: 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines; 8.09% improvement over SFT; achieves 68.73% on openPangu-Embedded-1B, surpassing Qwen3-1.7B.

Conclusion: NPD stabilizes learning by reducing sample noise and narrowing exploration, enabling efficient near-policy optimization and strong performance; code forthcoming.

Abstract: Standard knowledge distillation for autoregressive models often suffers from distribution mismatch. While on-policy methods mitigate this by leveraging student-generated outputs, they rely on computationally expensive Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks. To improve efficiency, we propose Near-Policy Distillation (NPD), an asynchronous approach that decouples student generation from training. This reformulation enables Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with sequence packing. However, asynchronous updates inevitably introduce policy lag and sample noise, which can cause the behavior to drift from near-policy toward off-policy. To counteract this without sacrificing efficiency, NPD integrates sparse student updates and the $Δ$-IFD filtering mechanism, a heuristic sample selection mechanism that empirically stabilizes the optimization trajectory. By filtering extreme out-of-distribution samples, $Δ$-IFD prevents noise from dominating the gradients, ensuring updates remain within a safe proximal learning zone. Empirically, the NPD framework achieves a 8.1x speedup over on-policy baselines and outperforms SFT by 8.09%. Crucially, by effectively narrowing the exploration space for subsequent RL, our method enables openPangu-Embedded-1B to reach a state-of-the-art score of 68.73%, outperforming the substantially larger Qwen3-1.7B. Codes will be released soon.

</details>


### [206] [Knowing but Not Correcting: Routine Task Requests Suppress Factual Correction in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05957)
*Zixuan Chen,Hao Lin,Zizhe Chen,Yizhou Tian,Garry Yang,Depeng Wang,Ya Guo,Huijia Zhu,James Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Correction suppression: LLMs often comply with task prompts and fail to correct false premises embedded in instructions. A 300-premise benchmark across eight models shows suppression rates from 19% to 90%. The authors reveal a mechanistic cause—errors are known to the model but are suppressed during response selection due to context steering. Two training-free interventions, Correction Direction Steering (CDS) and Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA), substantially improve factual strictness (e.g., CDS from 0% to 58.2% on Qwen3.5-9B; DPA preserves/improves reasoning). The work introduces 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work targets reliability gaps in LLMs when false premises are embedded in tasks, seeking to quantify suppression, understand its mechanism, and propose data-free remedies to enforce factual correctness under contextual pressure.

Method: Benchmark construction of 300 false premises evaluated across eight models; mechanistic analysis of activation flow showing suppression at response selection rather than knowledge encoding; development of two interventions (CDS and DPA) that operate without calibration data; evaluation on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B.

Result: Suppression rates span 19–90%, with four models exceeding 80%. CDS raised correction rates (notably 0%→58.2% on Qwen3.5-9B). DPA preserves or improves reasoning. Both interventions substantially enhance factual strictness.

Conclusion: Introduces factual strictness as a new reliability dimension; demonstrates training-free methods to counter correction suppression, attributing the phenomenon to output selection dynamics rather than knowledge deficits.

Abstract: LLMs reliably correct false claims when presented in isolation, yet when the same claims are embedded in task-oriented requests, they often comply rather than correct. We term this failure mode \emph{correction suppression} and construct a benchmark of 300 false premises to systematically evaluate it across eight models. Suppression rates range from 19\% to 90\%, with four models exceeding 80\%, establishing correction suppression as a prevalent and severe phenomenon. Mechanistic analysis reveals that suppression is not a knowledge failure: the model registers the error internally but task context diverts early-layer attention from the false claim as output intent crystallizes toward compliance at middle layers. We characterize this as \emph{knowing but not correcting} -- suppression occurs at response selection rather than knowledge encoding. Guided by this mechanism, we propose two training-free interventions. Correction Direction Steering (CDS) estimates a correction-compliance direction from matched pairs and injects it at middle layers before output intent crystallizes. Dynamic Payload Amplification (DPA) localizes payload tokens via attention divergence between early and late layers and amplifies their representation at the final layer, requiring no calibration data. Experiments on Qwen3.5-9B and LLaMA3.1-8B show both methods substantially improve factual strictness. CDS achieves the highest correction rate on Qwen3.5-9B (0\%$\to$58.2\%). DPA is the only method that preserves or improves reasoning capability on both models. These findings introduce \emph{factual strictness} -- the willingness to uphold accuracy against contextual pressures -- as a new dimension of model reliability.

</details>


### [207] [Uncertainty Estimation via Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05964)
*Eunseo Choi,Ho-Yeon Kim,Jaewon Lee,Taeyong jo,Myungjun lee,Heejin Ahn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HCM provides a sampling-free, distribution-free uncertainty estimator by decomposing outputs into magnitude and a unit-direction vector; uncertainty equals violation of the hyperspherical constraint, enabling deterministic, interpretable estimates for regression and classification with reduced inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need reliable, efficient uncertainty estimation for high-stakes tasks without expensive sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions.

Method: Output decomposition into a magnitude and a unit-norm direction vector on a hypersphere; uncertainty is quantified as the degree of violation of the unit-norm constraint; deterministic, applicable to regression and classification; sampling-free and distribution-free.

Result: Empirical results show HCM matches or exceeds ensemble and evidential methods across benchmarks and industrial tasks, with substantially lower inference cost and better confidence-error alignment.

Conclusion: Geometric structure on the output space can yield a versatile, scalable uncertainty estimator that competes with traditional approaches while offering interpretability and efficiency.

Abstract: Quantifying uncertainty in neural network predictions is essential for high-stakes domains such as autonomous driving, healthcare, and manufacturing. While existing approaches often depend on costly sampling or restrictive distributional assumptions, we propose Hyperspherical Confidence Mapping (HCM), a simple yet principled framework for sampling-free and distribution-free uncertainty estimation. HCM decomposes outputs into a magnitude and a normalized direction vector constrained to lie on the unit hypersphere, enabling a novel interpretation of uncertainty as the degree of violation of this geometric constraint. This yields deterministic and interpretable estimates applicable to both regression and classification. Experiments across diverse benchmarks and real-world industrial tasks demonstrate that HCM matches or surpasses ensemble and evidential approaches, with far lower inference cost and stronger confidence-error alignment. Our results highlight the power of geometric structure in uncertainty estimation and position HCM as a versatile alternative to conventional techniques.

</details>


### [208] [Beyond Uniform Credit Assignment: Selective Eligibility Traces for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05965)
*Chaoli Mou,Zhan Zhuang,Xinning Chen,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace) improves credit assignment in critic-free RL with verifiable rewards by replacing uniform trajectory-level credits with sparse, high-entropy token masking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inefficiency of uniform credit assignment in critic-free methods (e.g., GRPO) by enabling fine-grained credit attribution to critical reasoning steps.

Method: Introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient critic-free eligibility traces baseline, then develop S-trace with sparse eligibility traces that selectively mask low-entropy tokens. Also contextualize GSPO as a special case under uniform credit assignment within this framework.

Result: S-trace outperforms GRPO with gains of 0.49% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16% on Qwen3-4B, and 2.98% on Qwen3-8B (average pass@16), while achieving higher sample and token efficiency.

Conclusion: Selective eligibility traces yield better learning efficiency and performance in RL with verifiable rewards, mitigating the drawbacks of uniform credit assignment and demonstrating strong empirical gains across model scales.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a key approach for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, widely used critic-free algorithms such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) necessitate a ``uniform credit assignment'' assumption that indiscriminately broadcast trajectory-level advantages, hindering learning efficiency by failing to distinguish critical reasoning steps. To address this limitation, we propose Selective Eligibility Traces (S-trace). Grounded in the intuition of partial trust region preservation, we initially introduce P-trace as a sample-efficient, critic-free eligibility traces method, upon which we build S-trace, implementing a sparse eligibility traces mechanism to further mitigate variance and achieve fine-grained credit assignment by selectively masking low-entropy tokens. Theoretically, we contextualize the recent Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) method within the critic-free eligibility traces framework, identifying it as a special instance of the eligibility traces method operating under uniform credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that S-trace not only outperforms GRPO, showing gains of 0.49\% on Qwen3-1.7B and 3.16\% on Qwen3-4B, and maintaining a robust 2.98\% improvement when scaled further to Qwen3-8B in average pass@16, but notably achieves this with simultaneously higher sample and token efficiency.

</details>


### [209] [Sharper Guarantees for Misspecified Kernelized Bandit Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05967)
*Davide Maran,Csaba Szepesvári*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Misspecification in kernelized bandits can be greatly mitigated for broad kernel classes: offline simple-regret improves from sqrt(d_eff) to logarithmic/polylog growth; online regret bound improves from n ε + sqrt(γ_n n) to n ε + ~sqrt(γ_n n) under domain-splitting with localized eigendecay, removing extra sqrt(γ_n) in the misspecification term.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how misspecification harms kernelized bandit guarantees and whether one can curb its amplification beyond kernel complexity factors, enabling tighter data-dependent regret/outcome guarantees for more kernels.

Method: Introduce spectral Lebesgue constant as the governing quantity for misspecification amplification in offline bounds; prove high-probability simple-regret bounds with missing term scaling with the Lebesgue constant. For online bounds, adapt a domain-splitting algorithm and assume localized eigendecay to achieve a cumulative regret bound of ~O(√(γ_n n) + nε), removing the extra √γ_n factor. The unifying idea is localization—spectral localization for offline operators and spatial localization via domain splitting online.

Result: Offline: misspecification amplification becomes logarithmic for 1D monotone spectra and polylogarithmic for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. Online: with mild localization assumptions, regret bound matches standard √(γ_n n) up to a factor, plus a linear-in-n term from misspecification, i.e., ~O(√(γ_n n) + nε).

Conclusion: Localization—spectral in offline analysis and domain-based in online analysis—controls misspecification amplification across a broad class of kernels, yielding tighter guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandits and aligning offline and online behavior with classical, well-behaved complexity measures.

Abstract: Existing guarantees for misspecified kernelized bandit optimization pay for misspecification through kernel complexity: in generic offline bounds, the misspecification level $\varepsilon$ is multiplied by $\sqrt{d_\mathrm{eff}}$, where $d_\mathrm{eff}$ is the kernel effective dimension, while in online regret bounds, the corresponding penalty is $\sqrt{γ_n}\,n\varepsilon$, where $γ_n$ is the maximum information gain after $n$ rounds of interaction.
  In this work, we show that, for a large class of kernels, the misspecification amplification can be reduced to logarithmic or polylogarithmic growth. In the offline setting, we first prove high-probability simple-regret bounds whose misspecification term is governed by a spectral Lebesgue constant. This yields logarithmic amplification for one-dimensional monotone spectra and polylogarithmic amplification for multivariate Fourier-diagonal product kernels. In the online setting, we modify a domain-splitting algorithm and prove a cumulative regret bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal O}(\sqrt{γ_n n}+n\varepsilon)$ under mild localized eigendecay assumptions, removing the extra $\sqrt{γ_n}$ factor from the misspecification term. The common principle is localization: spectral localization controls the Lebesgue constant of the offline approximation operator, while domain splitting implements the spatial analogue of this mechanism in the online setting, preventing local misspecification errors from being amplified globally.

</details>


### [210] [Training Transformers for KV Cache Compressibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05971)
*Yoav Gelberg,Yam Eitan,Michael Bronstein,Yarin Gal,Haggai Maron*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV-CAT trains transformers to learn compressible key-value representations by masking KV slots during training, improving downstream compression methods for long-context scenarios across tasks like retrieval, long-context QA, and perplexity-based metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context LMs rely on KV caches whose size and access cost grow with prefix length. Post-hoc KV compression is limited by how compressible the model’s internal representations are. The authors formalize KV compressibility as a property of learned representations and argue for training-time guidance.

Method: Continued pretraining with a train-time KV sparsification policy that randomly masks KV slots during training, forcing the model to use fewer slots and learn representations that are amenable to post-hoc compression. Evaluation across retrieval, long-context QA, and perplexity-based compressed-prefix continuation.

Result: KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

Conclusion: Compressibility is a property of learned representations, not context alone. Training-time sparsification can steer models toward representations that enable more effective post-hoc compression, yielding gains across multiple tasks.

Abstract: Long-context language modeling is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory and decode-time access costs scale linearly with the prefix length. This bottleneck has motivated a range of context-compression methods, from token-level summarization to recent optimization-based KV compression methods. These post-hoc methods operate on the KV cache of a fixed pretrained model, so their effectiveness is fundamentally limited by how well the model's internal representations can be compressed. In this work, we formalize the notion of KV compressibility and show that it is a property of the learned representations, rather than of the context alone. We prove that almost any sequence-to-vector function admits both highly compressible and inherently non-compressible transformer implementations, highlighting the need to guide transformers toward compressible representations during training. Motivated by this, we propose KV-Compression Aware Training (KV-CAT), a continued pretraining procedure that incentivizes the emergence of compressible representations. We introduce a train-time KV sparsification policy that masks KV slots during training. This forces the model to use fewer KV slots and encourages it to learn representations amenable to post-hoc compression. Empirically, we show that KV-CAT improves the quality-budget tradeoff of downstream compression methods across retrieval, long-context question answering, and perplexity-based evaluation of compressed-prefix continuation.

</details>


### [211] [Physical Fidelity Reconstruction via Improved Consistency-Distilled Flow Matching for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05975)
*Sicheng Ma,Tianyue Yang,Xiuzhe Wu,Xiao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distill a high-fidelity flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model for fast, conditional flow reconstruction from low-fidelity observations; achieves 12x inference speedup, ~50% fewer parameters, and superior training efficiency over training from scratch, while preserving spectrum-level fidelity on multiple fluid benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce latency and compute costs of high-fidelity flow reconstructions to enable real-time, ensemble, or simulation-in-the-loop workflows by replacing iterative sampling with a compact one-step model.

Method: Knowledge distillation from an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher to a one-step consistency student. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the student’s generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, enabling conditional reconstruction with an unconditional high-fidelity model. The teacher remains fixed; the student is trained within the same budget and evaluated on coarse-to-fine flow reconstruction.

Result: The student retains similar spectrum performance as the teacher, uses about half the parameters, and achieves roughly a 12× speedup in inference. With the same training budget, the distilled student outperforms a directly trained one-step model from scratch by 23.1% in SSIM. On three benchmarks (Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, Kolmogorov Flow) at 256×256, the approach demonstrates effective coarse-to-fine reconstruction and favorable efficiency gains.

Conclusion: Teacher distillation enables compact, fast, and trainable scientific generative models for flow reconstruction, making high-capacity models more deployable in latency-constrained settings and serving as a blueprint for future compression of scientific generative models.

Abstract: Reconstructing high-fidelity flow fields from low-fidelity observations is a central problem in scientific machine learning, yet recent diffusion and flow-matching models typically rely on iterative sampling, making them costly for latency-sensitive workflows such as ensemble forecasting, real-time visualization, and simulation-in-the-loop inference. We study whether a high-fidelity flow-matching generative model can be compressed into a compact one-step model for fast scientific flow reconstruction. Our approach distills an optimal-transport flow-matching teacher into a one-step consistency model. Low-fidelity observations are incorporated at inference by initializing the generative trajectory from a noised observation along the transport path, allowing an unconditional high-fidelity flow model to perform conditional reconstruction without retraining the teacher. We evaluate this distillation strategy on three fluid benchmarks, Smoke Buoyancy, Turbulent Channel Flow, and Kolmogorov Flow, using coarse-to-fine reconstruction as a controlled testbed at field sizes up to $256 \times 256$. Across these settings, the distilled student retains similar performance of the teacher's model on spectrum metrics, while using roughly half as many parameters and achieving a $12\times$ inference speedup over the flow-matching teacher. Under the same training budget, the distilled student also outperforms a one-step consistency model trained directly from scratch by $23.1\%$ in SSIM, showing that teacher distillation improves training efficiency rather than merely accelerating sampling. These results suggest a promising route for turning future high-capacity scientific generative models into compact reconstruction models that are faster to train, cheaper to run, and easier to deploy.

</details>


### [212] [Towards Steering without Sacrifice: Principled Training of Steering Vectors for Prompt-only Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05983)
*Yuntai Bao,Qinfeng Li,Xinyan Yu,Xuhong Zhang,Ge Su,Wenqi Zhang,Liu Yan,Haiqin Weng,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jointly train steering factors and directions to remove post-hoc factor selection; introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes on a few prompt tokens; PrOSV outperforms full-sequence SVs on AxBench and offers better utility–robustness tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fine-tuned steering vectors (SVs) require per-SV factor tuning to balance steering and generation quality, and full-sequence SVs (FSSVs) can hurt generation quality due to extensive intervention. There is a need for a stable, efficient, and minimally invasive SV mechanism that avoids per-instance factor selection.

Method: 1) Jointly train steering factors and directions to eliminate post-hoc factor selection. 2) Use neural network scaling theory to set moderately large initializations and learning rates for stability and efficiency. 3) Propose Prompt-only SV (PrOSV) that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens, drawing inspiration from representation fine-tuning. 4) Evaluate on AxBench and compare against traditional FSSVs under the joint training scheme.

Result: Empirical results show PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when trained jointly. PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

Conclusion: Joint training of steering factors and directions removes the need for post-hoc factor selection and, together with PrOSV, reduces intervention to a small portion of the input, improving both generation quality and robustness. The approach demonstrates strong potential for efficient, robust steering of LLMs via SVs.

Abstract: Recently, steering vectors (SVs) have emerged as an effective and lightweight approach to steer behaviors of large language models (LLMs), among which fine-tuned SVs are more effective than optimization-free ones. However, current approaches to fine-tuned SVs suffer from two limitations. First, they require careful selection of steering factors on a per-SV basis to balance steering effectiveness and generation quality at inference time. Second, they operate as full-sequence SVs (FSSVs), which can sacrifice generation quality regardless of factor selection due to excessive intervention on the model generation process. To address the first limitation, we propose joint training of steering factors and directions, such that post-hoc factor selection is no longer required. Using neural network scaling theory, we find that moderately large initialization sizes and learning rates for steering factors are essential for stability and efficiency of joint training. To tackle the second limitation, we draw inspiration from representation fine-tuning and introduce Prompt-only SV (PrOSV), an SV that intervenes only on a few prompt tokens. Our empirical results show that PrOSV outperforms traditional FSSVs on AxBench when using our joint training scheme. We also find that PrOSV achieves a better tradeoff between general model utility and adversarial robustness than FSSV.

</details>


### [213] [DiBA: Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation for Neural Network Weight Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05994)
*Nobutaka Ono*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiBA compresses dense neural network weights via a diagonal-binary-diagonal-binary-diagonal factorization, enabling storage- and compute-efficient approximations; DiBA-Greedy optimizes the factors via alternating updates; DiBARD allows replacing dense layers with DiBA factors and retuning only diagonals for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense weight matrices in modern networks (linear layers, 1x1 convs, attentions, embeddings) dominate memory and compute. A compact, trainable factorization that preserves accuracy and enables efficient adaptation is highly desirable.

Method: Approximate A∈R^{m×n} as Â = D1 B1 D2 B2 D3, with D1,D2,D3 diagonal and B1,B2 binary (0/1); intermediate rank k controls storage/accuracy. For mat-vec, compute uses 3 element-wise scales and 2 binary mixes (reducing FLOPs from mn to m+k+n). Introduce DiBA-Greedy: alternating solver with closed-form least-squares for diagonals and exact one-bit improvements for binaries. Introduce DiBARD: replace dense layers with DiBA factors, freeze binaries, retune only diagonals downstream, avoiding discrete search during adaptation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 40 dense weight matrices from public pretrained models shows DiBA-Greedy provides consistent SNR gains as storage ratio grows. In two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands (Audio Spectrogram Transformer) accuracy from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing binary factors.

Conclusion: DiBA enables a compact, flexible weight representation that reduces storage and computes for dense layers while offering practical adaptation pathways (DiBA-Greedy optimization and DiBARD retuning) with demonstrated accuracy gains on real models.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose DiBA (Diagonal and Binary Matrix Approximation), a compact matrix factorization for neural network weight compression. Many components of modern networks, including linear layers, $1\times1$ convolutions, attention projections, and embedding layers, have dense matrix weights. DiBA approximates $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ by $\widehat A=D_1B_1D_2B_2D_3$, where $D_1,D_2,D_3$ are diagonal matrices and $B_1,B_2$ are $0/1$ binary matrices. The intermediate dimension $k$ controls the trade-off between theoretical storage and approximation accuracy. For matrix-vector products, DiBA decomposes dense multiplication into three element-wise scaling operations and two binary mixing operations, reducing the floating-point multiplication count from $mn$ to $m+k+n$. For optimization, we introduce DiBA-Greedy, an alternating solver that combines closed-form least-squares updates for the diagonal factors with exact one-bit improvement tests for the binary factors. We also introduce DiBARD (DiBA with Retuning only Diagonal factors), which replaces dense-matrix layers by DiBA factors, freezes the binary matrices, and retunes only the diagonal entries on downstream data. This preserves compact binary mixing without discrete search during adaptation. On 40 dense weight matrices extracted from public pretrained models, DiBA-Greedy yields consistent SNR improvements as the theoretical storage ratio increases. After DiBA replacement in two component-replacement studies, DiBARD improves DistilBERT/WikiText masked-token accuracy from 0.4447 to 0.5210 and Speech Commands test accuracy for an Audio Spectrogram Transformer from 0.7684 to 0.9781 without reoptimizing the binary factors.

</details>


### [214] [A Fine-Grained Understanding of Uniform Convergence for Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06004)
*Aryeh Kontorovich,Kasper Green Larsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-grained uniform convergence for halfspaces. Inhomogeneous halfspaces (d≥2) are VC-tight: population error Θ(d ln(n/d)/n); agnostic deviation Θ(√(τ ln(1/τ))). Homogeneous halfspaces in R^2 exhibit dramatically better behavior: realizable error O(1/n); in the agnostic setting, per-dyadic-band deviation is log-free with a ln ln n overhead when aggregating bands, and this overhead is unavoidable (matching lower bound). This yields a sharp, threshold-based picture across dimension and structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To sharpen uniform convergence theory for halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds, isolating how dimension (d) and inhomogeneity (inhomogeneous vs homogeneous) drive tight, fine-grained guarantees.

Method: Perform a dyadic risk-band decomposition and apply a critical-wedge localization argument to obtain per-band (log-free) deviation bounds in the homogeneous case. Use standard VC-type arguments for the inhomogeneous case, plus lower-bound constructions to establish tightness. Aggregate across bands via a careful union bound, incurring only a ln ln n overhead, and prove matching lower bounds.

Result: Inhomogeneous halfspaces in d≥2: population error for any consistent hypothesis scales as Θ(d ln(n/d)/n); agnostic deviation at true error τ scales as Θ(√(τ ln(1/τ))). Homogeneous halfspaces in R^2: realizable case yields O(1/n) error for all consistent hypotheses; agnostic case yields bandwise, log-free deviation bounds per dyadic risk band; aggregation across bands costs only a ln ln n factor; a matching lower bound confirms this overhead is necessary.

Conclusion: The work maps out a near-complete, fine-grained uniform convergence landscape for halfspaces, revealing sharp thresholds by dimension and by inhomogeneity (inhomogeneous vs. homogeneous), and highlighting that homogeneous, especially in 2D, enjoys substantially stronger uniform convergence properties than the general, d≥2 inhomogeneous case.

Abstract: We study the fine-grained uniform convergence behavior of halfspaces beyond worst-case VC bounds. For inhomogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with $d\ge 2$, we show that standard first-order VC bounds are essentially tight: even consistent hypotheses can incur population error $Θ(d\ln(n/d)/n)$, and in the agnostic setting the deviation scales as $\sqrt{τ\ln(1/τ)}$ at true error $τ$. In contrast, homogeneous halfspaces in $\mathbb{R}^2$ exhibit a markedly different behavior. In the realizable case, every hypothesis consistent with the sample has error $O(1/n)$. In the agnostic case, we prove a bandwise, log-free deviation bound on each dyadic risk band via a critical-wedge localization argument. Unioning over bands incurs only a $\ln\ln n$ overhead, and we establish a matching lower bound showing this overhead is unavoidable. Together, these results give a fine-grained and nearly complete picture of uniform convergence for halfspaces, revealing sharp dimensional and structural thresholds.

</details>


### [215] [Quantizing With Randomized Hadamard Transforms: Efficient Heuristic Now Proven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06014)
*Ran Ben-Basat,William Kuszmaul,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs) suffice to emulate URRs for two modern schemes (DRIVE, QUIC-FL) in high dimensions; three RHTs are needed for Vector Quantization to ensure decaying multi-coordinate covariance; an adaptive O(d) moment-check yields runtime selection of the number of RHTs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: URRs are ideal but expensive; RHTs provide fast, orthogonal transforms but may exhibit adverse worst-case behavior. This work quantifies when RHTs approximate URRs in common compression tasks and identifies the threshold where an additional RHT is required, with practical adaptivity.

Method: The authors analyze the distribution of coordinates after applying two RHTs to a d-dimensional vector, proving the marginal distribution of each fixed coordinate is within O(d^{-1/2}) of standard Gaussian in both Kolmogorov and 1-Wasserstein distances. They apply these bounds to the analyses of DRIVE and QUIC-FL to show asymptotic performance matching URRs. For Vector Quantization (VQ), they show two RHTs fail to ensure decaying coordinate covariance and prove a composition of three RHTs yields decaying covariance, preserving URR-like error for fixed, bounded VQ codebooks up to a vanishing additive term. They also propose a linear-time O(d) moment-based check to adapt the number of RHTs at runtime.

Result: Two RHTs achieve asymptotic performance parity with URRs in DRIVE and QUIC-FL; three RHTs restore the necessary cross-coordinate decorrelation for VQ; the proposed runtime adaptive scheme improves practical performance by tuning the count of RHTs based on input moments.

Conclusion: RHTs are viable substitutes for URRs in many pipelines: use two RHTs for typical compression schemes, but for VQ tasks three RHTs are required to ensure decaying covariance. Incorporating a cheap runtime moment check can further enhance practical performance by adapting the number of RHTs in response to input characteristics.

Abstract: Uniform random rotations (URRs) are a common preprocessing step in modern quantization approaches used for gradient compression, inference acceleration, KV-cache compression, model weight quantization, and approximate nearest-neighbor search in vector databases. In practice, URRs are often replaced by randomized Hadamard transforms (RHTs), which preserve orthogonality while admitting fast implementations. The remaining issue is the performance for worst-case inputs. With a URR, each coordinate is individually distributed as a shifted beta distribution, which converges to a Gaussian distribution in high dimensions. Generally, one RHT is not suitable in the worst case, as individual coordinates can be far from these distributions. We show that after composing two RHTs on any $d$-sized input vector, the marginal distribution of every fixed coordinate of the normalized rotated vector is within $O(d^{-1/2})$ of a standard Gaussian both in Kolmogorov distance and in $1$-Wasserstein distance. We then plug these bounds into the analyses of modern compression schemes, namely DRIVE and QUIC-FL, and show that two RHTs achieve performance that asymptotically matches URRs.
  However, we show that two RHTs may not be sufficient for Vector Quantization (VQ), which often requires weak correlation across fixed-size blocks of coordinates (as opposed to only marginal distribution convergence for single coordinates). We prove that a composition of three RHTs leads to decaying coordinate covariance. This ensures that any fixed, bounded, multi-dimensional VQ codebook optimized for URRs has the same expected error when using three RHTs, up to an additive term that vanishes with the dimension.
  Finally, because practical inputs are rarely adversarial, we propose a linear-time ${O}(d)$ check on the input's moments to dynamically adapt the number of RHTs used at runtime to improve performance.

</details>


### [216] [Matrix-Decoupled Concentration for Autoregressive Sequences: Dimension-Free Guarantees for Sparse Long-Context Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06017)
*Pei-Sen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC), a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences in autoregressive LLMs, delivering a dimension-free O(1) variance proxy by preserving sparsity; achieves optimal constants for Markov chains and order-optimal bounds for causal trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concentration bounds for sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive models are hampered by (i) scalar variance inflation due to decoupled dependency and target sensitivities, and (ii) lack of strictly causal filtration in spatial methods; a unified framework is needed to obtain tight, dimension-free bounds that respect causality.

Method: Develop MDC: a McDiarmid-like bound governed by the exact matrix-vector product of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity. Leverage directed d-separation for causal trees and preserve coordinatewise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework.

Result: Derives concentration bounds with optimal constants for Markov chains and order-optimal bounds for causal trees; yields a dimension-free, O(1) variance proxy; explains stability of long-context reasoning in autoregressive generation.

Conclusion: MDC resolves the two bottlenecks, providing tight, causality-respecting concentration bounds for dependent sequences in LLM evaluation and offering a rigorous foundation for long-context reliability.

Abstract: Sequence-level evaluations in autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on highly dependent token generation. Establishing tight concentration bounds for these processes remains a challenge due to two fundamental bottlenecks in existing frameworks: (i) classical inequalities typically separate dependency structures from target sensitivities, leading to a scalar collapse that inflates the variance proxy to a suboptimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ for sparse terminal rewards; (ii) conversely, while certain spatial methods achieve tighter bounds, they lack the strictly causal filtration required by sequential generation, rendering them inapplicable to the autoregressive setting. To resolve both bottlenecks, we establish a sharp McDiarmid-type inequality for dependent sequences, governed strictly by the exact matrix-vector multiplication of the causal dependency resolvent and the target sensitivity vector. This Matrix-Decoupled Concentration (MDC) framework natively recovers optimal constants for Markov chains and exploits directed $d$-separation to yield order-optimal bounds for causal trees. Crucially, by exactly preserving the coordinate-wise sparsity of rewards within a strictly causal framework, MDC mathematically prevents scalar collapse, guaranteeing a dimension-free $\mathcal{O}(1)$ variance proxy and providing a rigorous mathematical justification for the stability of long-context reasoning.

</details>


### [217] [Multi-agent decision making: A Blackwell's informativeness approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06028)
*Zheng Zhang,Cuong C. Nguyen,Kevin Wells,Gustavo Carneiro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Blackwell-information analysis of multi-LLM decision-making shows voting/debate are no more informative than pooled private information; Bayesian pooled posterior maximization is the information-theoretic upper bound; introduces product-of-posteriors estimator for QA; empirical gains on six benchmarks over current multi-LLM methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address lack of formal guarantees for informativeness in aggregation (voting/debate) in multi-agent LLM systems and establish principled bounds using Blackwell information theory.

Method: Formalize information structures induced by voting and debate under Blackwell framework; prove they cannot exceed the informativeness of pooled private information; identify Bayesian pooled posterior maximization as upper bound; propose a practical QA method estimating each agent posterior and approximating the pooled posterior via product-of-posteriors.

Result: The approach achieves superior performance on six QA benchmarks, outperforming state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

Conclusion: The study provides a formal informativity bound for aggregation in multi-LLM systems and demonstrates a practical, effective product-of-posteriors method; suggests design of aggregation should aim for Bayesian pooling under Blackwell order.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has motivated research on decision-making in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents collaborate to achieve shared objectives. Existing aggregation approaches, such as voting and debate, are largely ad-hoc and lack formal guarantees regarding the informativeness of the resulting decisions. In this paper, we provide a principled approach to analyse decisions made in the multi-LLM setting using Blackwell's informativeness framework. Within the Blackwell information-structure abstraction, we show that voting and debate induce information structures that are no more informative than the pooled private information of all agents. This result identifies Bayesian pooled posterior maximisation as an information-theoretic upper-bound decision rule under the Blackwell ordering. Motivated by this theoretical analysis, we introduce a practical method for LLM-based question-answering (QA) tasks that estimates each agent's posterior and approximates the pooled posterior using a product-of-posteriors estimator. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art multi-LLM debate and voting methods.

</details>


### [218] [Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06032)
*Hugo Cazaux,Eyjólfur Ingi Ásgeirsson,Hlynur Stefánsson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Synthetic data augmentation for time-series forecasting is highly architecture-dependent; channel-mixing models often benefit, channel-independent models typically degrade; gains in low-resource settings can be substantial but with an overall negative average; only the Seasonal-Trend generator consistently helps; hard curriculum scheduling is harmful; practical guidance includes using synthetic data with channel-mixing architectures and gradual annealing, and treating augmentation as architecture- and data-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify the role of synthetic data in time-series forecasting, quantify its effects across architectures, signals, datasets, and resource regimes, and derive actionable usage guidelines.

Method: Large-scale empirical study with nine experiment groups and 4,218 runs, evaluating synthetic augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals, and seven datasets; analyzed performance by architecture, data resource level, and signal type; also tested curriculum strategies.

Result: Augmentation benefits are strongly architecture-dependent: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) show improvement in many trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are generally degraded. In some low-resource Weather settings, TimesNet with synthetic augmentation using only 10% of data surpasses the full-data baseline (in 4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combos). Across all trials, augmentation reduces performance on about 67% of cases. Among synthetic signals, only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps; hard curriculum switching degrades performance by ~24% MSE. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Provide concrete guidelines: (1) apply synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures; (2) employ gradual annealing schedules rather than hard curricula; (3) treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent; (4) use the Seasonal-Trend generator for general gains; and (5) validate across datasets before deployment.

Abstract: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

</details>


### [219] [Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06036)
*Licheng Pan,Haochen Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yunsheng Lu,Yongqi Tong,Yinuo Wang,Shijian Wang,Zhixuan Chu,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelectiveRM uses optimal transport to robustly train reward models from noisy human preferences by (1) aligning model predictions with preferences via a Joint Consistency Discrepancy and (2) relaxing mass conservation with partial transport to ignore outlier/noisy samples; it provides a tighter bound on the clean risk and achieves strong empirical gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world RLHF datasets contain noisy or conflicting preferences; standard training overfits to noise and existing denoising methods assume homogeneous noise and fail to capture linguistic preference complexity.

Method: Introduce Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align model predictions with preference data; apply Mass Relaxation via partial transport to allow excluding samples with noisy preferences that violate semantic consistency; present SelectiveRM framework grounded in optimal transport with theoretical guarantee of a tighter bound on clean risk.

Result: Extensive experiments show that SelectiveRM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: SelectiveRM provides a principled OT-based approach to robust RLHF under noisy preferences, delivering theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements by selectively ignoring conflicting samples.

Abstract: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

</details>


### [220] [Requests of a Feather Must Flock Together: Batch Size vs. Prefix Homogeneity in LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06046)
*Saksham Rathi,Preeti,Mythili Vutukuru*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feather uses reinforcement learning to optimize batch formation for autoregressive LLM inference by balancing prefix homogeneity and batch size; CH T enables fast prefix detection; integration into vLLM and SGLang yields 2-10x throughput gains by reducing KV cache accesses, surpassing prefix-aware kernels while maintaining performance when prefix sharing is low.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV-cache memory bottleneck in auto-regressive generation; existing schedulers optimize memory footprint but ignore potentially higher-throughput smaller homogeneous batches; high CPU overhead for prefix detection via radix trees.

Method: Train or apply RL-based scheduler (Feather) to decide batch size and prefix-homogeneity; introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT) for fast prefix detection; integrate into vLLM and SGLang.

Result: 2-10x end-to-end throughput improvement vs existing schedulers; no worse than baselines when workload lacks sufficient prefix sharing; reduced KV-cache accesses; outperforms prefix-aware attention kernels with same goal.

Conclusion: Prefix-aware scheduling with RL and CHT offers substantial throughput improvements for prefix-sharing workloads and maintains performance on others; feasible to deploy in real systems.

Abstract: Auto-regressive token generation in large language models is memory-bound because it requires "attending to" key and value tensors (KV cache) of all previous tokens. Prior work aims to improve the efficiency of this decode process by batching multiple requests together, and maximizing batch size subject to GPU memory constraints. The key observation of our work is that with prefix-sharing workloads, smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches -- where all requests share a common prefix -- can achieve higher decode throughput than larger, heterogeneous batches, due to better spatial and temporal locality during KV cache accesses. However, prefix-aware schedulers in state-of-the-art inference engines maximize prefix reuse within a batch only to reduce KV cache memory footprint, but do not stop batch formation at smaller homogeneous batches that could have performed better. Further, we show that shared prefix detection in existing schedulers relies on radix-tree traversals, incurring substantial CPU overhead that is often comparable to GPU execution time. This paper presents Feather, a prefix-aware scheduler that uses reinforcement learning (RL) to learn the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. We also introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure that enables fast prefix detection and efficient request selection for the RL scheduler, avoiding expensive tree traversals. We integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang, and our evaluation shows that Feather achieves 2--10$\times$ higher end-to-end throughput as compared to existing schedulers, while doing no worse than the status quo when the workload does not have enough prefix sharing. Feather achieves these gains by reducing the total number of KV cache accesses, surpassing the performance of prefix-aware attention kernels that have the same goal.

</details>


### [221] [TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06047)
*Duong Nguyen,Mohammed Jawhar,Nicolas Chesneau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces TFM-Retouche, an input-space residual adapter for Tabular Foundation Models that is architecture-agnostic with a frozen backbone and an identity guard; demonstrates top performance on TabArena-Lite via TabICLv2-Retouche, achieving a +56 Elo gain over the frozen base and favorable time/accuracy Pareto efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TFMs exhibit strong zero-shot performance but fixed inductive biases, and adapting them usually requires costly full fine-tuning or architecture-specific PEFTs. Weight-space fine-tuning evidence is mixed. There is a need for a lightweight, architecture-agnostic adaptation that operates in input space and preserves performance when adaptation is unhelpful.

Method: Introduce a small residual adapter operating in the input space that is trained end-to-end with the frozen TFM. Includes a post-training identity guard that reverts to the original TFM if validation shows no improvement. Evaluated on TabArena-Lite (51 datasets across binary, multiclass, regression). TabICLv2-Retouche instantiated on TabICLv2 demonstrated on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling.

Result: TabICLv2-Retouche achieves top-ranked performance on TabArena-Lite, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base. It sits on the Pareto front for predictive quality versus training and inference time.

Conclusion: An architecture-agnostic input-space residual adapter can effectively adapt TFMs with minimal tuning, preserving the frozen backbone while achieving substantial gains in predictive performance and efficiency. The identity guard ensures safe deployment, and the approach generalizes as a practical recipe for adapting tabular backbones to diverse datasets.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models (TFMs), such as TabPFN-2.6, TabICLv2, ConTextTab, Mitra, LimiX, and TabDPT, achieve strong zero-shot performance through in-context learning, but their inductive biases remain fixed at inference time. Adapting a pretrained TFM to a specific dataset or task typically requires either full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive, or parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT) such as LoRA, which must be tailored to the internal architecture of each TFM. Furthermore, the evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed \citep{tanna_exploring_2026,rubachev_finetuning_2025}. We introduce TFM-Retouche, a lightweight input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic by design with respect to the frozen TFM backbone. TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align the input data with the inductive biases of the pretrained model. The adapter is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM, with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM whenever adaptation does not help on held-out validation. On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche -- the framework instantiated on TabICLv2 -- is the top-ranked method on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and sitting on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus both training and inference time.

</details>


### [222] [When Brain Networks Travel: Learning Beyond Site](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06050)
*Yingxu Wang,Kunyu Zhang,Yanwu Yang,Thomas Wolfers,Yujie Wu,Siyang Gao,Nan Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORE is a cross-site OOD robust brain network learning framework that decouples site confounders, models transient dynamics on a cross-site scaffold, and uses a prior-guided adaptive gating to improve generalization to unseen sites. It outperforms baselines and is robust to atlas variations across ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD with leave-one-site-out evaluation (up to 6.7% gain).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-site out-of-distribution generalization remains challenging in fMRI-based brain network analysis due to site-conditioned confounders causing non-pathological shortcuts and loss of transient dynamics from temporal averaging. A unified approach to identify reproducible diagnostic edges and model dynamics is needed.

Method: CORE consists of: (1) site-aware confounder decoupling to remove site bias and extract cross-site scaffold edges; (2) profiling transient pathway dynamics with lightweight temporal descriptors on the scaffold; (3) organizing scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling; (4) a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that uses population priors from the scaffold while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Evaluation uses leave-one-site-out on ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, ABCD, with atlas variation tests.

Result:  CORE achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain in cross-site generalization, and maintains performance gains across different brain parcellations.

Conclusion:  CORE provides a unified framework for cross-site robust brain network learning, combining confounder decoupling, dynamic pathway profiling on a transferable scaffold, and population-prior-guided subject adaptation to enhance generalization to unseen sites and robustness to atlas choices.

Abstract: Graph-based learning on functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has shown strong potential for brain network analysis. However, existing methods degrade under cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) settings because site-conditioned confounders induce non-pathological shortcuts, while functional connectivity constructed by temporal averaging obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites. In this paper, we propose Cross-site OOD Robust brain nEtwork (CORE), a unified framework for brain network learning across unseen sites. CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to mitigate site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic connectivity edges. It then profiles transient pathway dynamics over this scaffold using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling. Finally, CORE introduces a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that leverages scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Extensive experiments under leave-one-site-out evaluation on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD) show that CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain. Furthermore, CORE remains robust to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

</details>


### [223] [Towards Generation-Efficient Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06053)
*Mingcheng Zhu,Yu Liu,Tingting Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-cost uncertainty estimation for LLMs using partial generation or input prompts; introduces Logit Magnitude and MetaUE within an early-estimation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computation and latency of uncertainty estimation in high-stakes LLM applications while preserving accuracy. Address reliance on full generation for uncertainty.

Method: Proposes a unified early-estimation framework for autoregressive generation; categorizes estimators by observed information from multi-generation to input-only; introduces Logit Magnitude that uses top-M logit evidence from an early-stopped prefix; introduces MetaUE that distills generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores; extensive experiments on general and domain benchmarks.

Result: Logit Magnitude yields strong uncertainty estimation with partial generation; partial generation often suffices; MetaUE provides competitive input-only performance; demonstrates substantial reduction in generation needs and earlier identification of unreliable responses.

Conclusion: Effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with less generation; early detection of unreliable outputs is feasible and cost-effective; the proposed framework and estimators enable faster, scalable uncertainty assessment in LLM deployments.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is important for deploying LLMs in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and finance, where hallucinations can appear fluent and plausible while being factually incorrect, making it difficult for users to judge whether an output should be trusted. Existing methods require one or more full autoregressive generations to estimate uncertainty, which introduces substantial inference cost and often delays uncertainty assessment. In this paper, we investigate whether effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or even input-only information. Specifically, we first develop a unified framework that formulates uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over the autoregressive generation process of LLMs. This framework organises existing and proposed estimators by the information they observe, ranging from multi-generation to input-only prediction, and clarifies the performance-cost trade-off underlying different uncertainty estimation methods. Building on this view, we study two largely underexplored low-cost settings: estimating uncertainty with part of the generation, and predicting uncertainty from the input prompt. We propose Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence to estimate uncertainty from an early-stopped generation prefix, and MetaUE, which distils generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores. Extensive experiments on general and domain-specific benchmarks show that Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance, and partial generations of LLMs are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE further provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings. These findings suggest that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed, enabling unreliable responses to be identified earlier.

</details>


### [224] [Towards Self-Explainable Document Visual Question Answering with Chain-of-Explanation Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06058)
*Kjetil Indrehus,Adrian Duric,Changkyu Choi,Ali Ramezani-Kebrya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoExVQA proposes a self-explainable, grounding-based DocVQA framework using a chain-of-explanation to identify evidence, localize the answer, and decode solely from the grounded region, achieving state-of-the-art explainable performance on PFL-DocVQA with a 12% ANLS gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DocVQA models fuse evidence and answers and function as black boxes, hindering verifiability of reasoning across visual/document modalities.

Method: A three-stage chain-of-explanation: 1) identify question-relevant evidence, 2) explicitly localize answer region, 3) decode answer only from grounded region; predictions are interpretable across modalities.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art explainable DocVQA on PFL-DocVQA; improves ANLS by 12% over existing explainable baselines; provides transparent verifiable predictions.

Conclusion: Grounded reasoning with constrained decoding promotes inspectable, verifiable predictions and can improve explainability without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) requires vision-language models to reason not only about what information in a document is relevant to a question, but also where the answer is grounded on the page. Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization and operate largely as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. We propose CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework with a grounded reasoning process through a chain-of-explanation design. CoExVQA first identifies question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localizes the answer region, and finally decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region. Prediction via CoExVQA's chain-of-explanation enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process across modalities. Empirical results show that restricting decoding to grounded evidence achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over the current explainable baselines while providing transparent and verifiable predictions.

</details>


### [225] [Causal Reinforcement Learning for Complex Card Games: A Magic The Gathering Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06066)
*Cristiano da Costa Cunha,Ajmal Mian,Tim French,Wei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark for causal RL in Magic: The Gathering with high-dimensional partial observations, large masked action space, explicit SCM, and per-factor credit traces; proposes CGFA-PPO; baseline results show competitive performance and diagnostic capabilities beyond win rate; release of benchmark and protocol.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of benchmarks for complex, partially observed, sequential decision-making with masked actions and explicit causal structure, enabling causal credit assignment, structural transfer, and policy auditability.

Method: Construct MTG-Causal-RL with 3,077-dim partial observations, 478-action masked discrete space, five archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM). Expose causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces. Compare baselines (random, heuristic, masked PPO, causal-world-model PPO, scalar-control). Propose CGFA-PPO leveraging SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. Evaluation uses paired seeds, paired-bootstrap CIs, and Holm-Bonferroni correction.

Result: Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO achieve competitive in-distribution win rates, surpass random baselines. Per-factor calibration and leave-one-out transfer gaps reveal diagnostic structure not captured by overall win rate. The benchmark supports causal credit assignment, cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability.

Conclusion: MTG-Causal-RL provides a shared testbed for causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research to study causal credit assignment under masked actions, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability; the benchmark and protocol are released openly.

Abstract: Causal reinforcement learning (RL) lacks benchmarks for complex systems that combine sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. We introduce MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. Every episode exposes causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces, making causal credit assignment, leave-one-out cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability first-class metrics. We adapt a panel of reference baselines: random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and an architecture-matched scalar control. We propose Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) as a reference causal agent that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. All comparisons use paired seeds, paired-bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction within pre-registered families. Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates and exceed the random baseline; per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps expose diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot. We release the benchmark, reference-baseline results, and full evaluation protocol openly. By coupling a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, MTG-Causal-RL gives causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot pose together: causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

</details>


### [226] [Normalized Architectures are Natively 4-Bit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06067)
*Maxim Fishman,Brian Chmiel,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: nGPT uses a unit-hypersphere constraint to enable robust 4-bit training without extra normalization steps, demonstrated on dense 1.2B and MoE models up to 3B/30B; yields higher effective SNR and a flatter loss landscape that scales with hidden dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce training costs and memory for large LMs by enabling reliable low-precision (4-bit) training;传统 quantization methods require extra transforms and per-tensor scaling.

Method: Impose a unit-hypersphere constraint on weights and hidden representations (nGPT) and perform end-to-end NVFP4 training; validate on 1.2B dense and Mamba-Transformer MoE models up to 3B/30B; analyze dot-product quantization noise and signal behavior; provide a reference implementation.

Result: Quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated, but the hypersphere constraint induces weak positive correlations in element-wise products, yielding constructive signal accumulation, higher effective SNR, and flatter loss landscape; benefits grow with hidden dimensionality and scale; end-to-end NVFP4 training is viable.

Conclusion: Constraining models to the unit hypersphere mitigates low-precision quantization errors by aligning signals across hidden dimensions, enabling scalable, efficient 4-bit training without additional normalizations or transforms; demonstrated on dense and MoE architectures, with code available.

Abstract: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency. We show that nGPT, an architecture that constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, is inherently more robust to low-precision arithmetic. This removes the need for interventions-such as applying random Hadamard transforms and performing per-tensor scaling calculations-to preserve model quality, and it enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training. We validate this approach on both a 1.2B dense model and hybrid (Mamba-Transformer) MoE models of up to 3B/30B parameters. We trace this robustness to the dot product: while quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated in both standard and normalized architectures, the signal behaves differently. In nGPT, the hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations among the element-wise products, leading to a constructive accumulation of the signal across the hidden dimension while the noise continues to average out. This yields a higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and a flatter loss landscape, with the effect strengthening as the hidden dimension grows, suggesting increasing advantages at scale. A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/anonymous452026/ngpt-nvfp4

</details>


### [227] [Understanding diffusion models requires rethinking (again) generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06077)
*Pierre Marion,Yu-Han Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models demand new theoretical frameworks beyond classical learning theory and benign overfitting; memorization and generalization are incompatible, prompting a shift to studying pre-memorization learning; an empirical CIFAR-10 study yields concrete open questions for understanding generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the theoretical gap in understanding generalization for diffusion models and reconcile why practical models generalize despite memorization concerns.

Method: Argues conceptually and conducts an empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, distilling observations into concrete open questions to guide future research.

Result: Recommends pivoting the research focus from memorization avoidance to analyzing what the model learns before memorization; provides a set of open questions.

Conclusion: A new theoretical framework is needed to explain generalization in diffusion models; the paper offers an empirical grounding and a research agenda centered on pre-memorization dynamics.

Abstract: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires fundamentally new theoretical frameworks that go beyond both classical statistical learning theory and the benign overfitting paradigm developed for supervised learning. In diffusion models, unlike in supervised learning, memorization of training data and generalization to novel samples are incompatible: a model that has fully memorized its training set generates copies rather than novel data. Several theoretical explanations for why practical diffusion models nevertheless generalize have been proposed, based on capacity limitations, implicit regularization from optimization, or architectural inductive biases, but their interactions remain unclear. We argue that the field should pivot from explaining why the diffusion models do not memorize to investigating what the model actually learns during pre-memorization phase. To highlight our stance, we conduct empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, and we distill the findings into concrete open questions that we believe are key to improve understanding of generalization in diffusion models.

</details>


### [228] [Fast Gauss-Newton for Multiclass Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06081)
*Mikalai Korbit,Mario Zanon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposes multiclass GGN into true-vs-rest and within-competitor terms; Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) keeps the former, drops the latter to yield a PSD under-approximation exact for binary classification; solved efficiently via conjugate gradient on a whitened row-space system using scalar-margin Jacobian-vector products.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling the full multiclass GGN is difficult due to coupling across all classes via softmax covariance; a tractable, positive semidefinite approximation that preserves key curvature directions is sought.

Method: Derive an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy. Decompose the full GGN into a true-vs-rest term plus a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance. Define FGN by retaining the true-vs-rest term. Formulate the damped update as a whitened row-space system with one row per minibatch example and solve using Conjugate Gradient with Jacobian-vector/vector-Jacobian products of the scalar-margin map.

Result: FGN provides a PSD under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. It remains close to the full GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and diverges as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

Conclusion: FGN offers a scalable, stable curvature approximation for multiclass softmax training, capturing essential curvature directions in regimes with concentrated competition and damping, while acknowledging deviations when within-class covariance becomes significant.
