Abstract: In multiclass softmax cross-entropy, the full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature couples all output logits through the softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products harder to scale as the number of classes grows. We show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed exactly into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term. Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) retains the first term and drops the second, yielding a positive semidefinite under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. The derivation uses an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy: the loss and gradient are unchanged, and the approximation enters only at the curvature level. Exploiting the FGN curvature structure, the damped update can be written as an equivalent whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch example. We solve this system matrix-free by conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map. Targeted mechanism experiments and an evaluation on a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predictions from the decomposition: FGN stays closest to the full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

</details>


### [229] [Beyond Autoregressive RTG: Conditioning via Injection Outside Sequential Modeling in Decision Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06104)
*Yongyi Wang,Hanyu Liu,Lingfeng Li,Bozhou Chen,Ang Li,Qirui Zheng,Xionghui Yang,Chucai Wang,Wenxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SlimDT removes the Return-to-Go token from the autoregressive sequence and injects RTG into the state representation, reducing sequence length and improving inference efficiency while maintaining or improving performance on D4RL, competitive with SOTA methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DT uses a scalar RTG token that adds computational overhead and lacks rich information compared to state/action vectors. Reducing sequence length and decoupling the conditioning signal can improve both efficiency and performance.

Method: Modify the Decision Transformer by embedding RTG information into state representations prior to sequence modeling, so the Transformer processes only (state, action) tokens (no separate RTG token). This shortens the sequence by about one third and leverages RTG as part of state conditioning.

Result: SlimDT outperforms standard DT on the D4RL benchmark across various tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Decoupling a sparse, scalar conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence yields computational gains and potential performance improvements in offline RL.

Abstract: Decision Transformer (DT) formulates offline reinforcement learning as autoregressive sequence modeling, achieving promising results by predicting actions from a sequence of Return-to-Go (RTG), state, and action tokens. However, RTG is a scalar that summarizes future rewards, containing far less information than typical state or action vectors, yet it consumes the same computational budget per token. Worse, the self-attention cost of Transformers grows quadratically with sequence length, so including RTG as a separate token adds unnecessary overhead. We propose SlimDT, which removes RTG from the autoregressive sequence. Instead, we inject RTG information into the state representations before the sequential modeling step, allowing the Transformer to process only a compact (state, action) sequence. This reduces the sequence length by one-third, directly improving inference efficiency. On the D4RL benchmark, SlimDT surpasses standard DT across various tasks and achieves performance comparable to existing state-of-the-art methods. Decoupling a sparse conditioning signal from an information-rich sequence thus yields both computational gains and higher task performance.

</details>


### [230] [BoostLLM: Boosting-inspired LLM Fine-tuning for Few-shot Tabular Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06117)
*Yi-Siang Wang,Kuan-Yu Chen,Yu-Chen Den,Darby Tien-Hao Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BoostLLM reframes PEFT fine-tuning as multi-round boosting with sequential residual adapters, integrating a tabular inductive bias via decision-tree paths as a second input view. This yields consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning and competitive performance with tree-based methods across data regimes, scaling with stronger trees and longer boosting horizons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with tabular prediction in low-data settings; standard language-model finetuning underperforms dedicated tree ensembles. The work seeks a general, principled training paradigm for LLM fine-tuning that leverages boosting and structured inductive biases.

Method: Transform parameter-efficient fine-tuning into iterative residual boosting by training sequential PEFT adapters as weak learners. Incorporate a second input view consisting of decision-tree paths alongside raw features. Early training uses the path view as a structured teacher, with the model gradually shifting toward feature-driven representations. Evaluate across multiple LLM backbones and datasets, compare to XGBoost and GPT-4o-based methods, and explore scaling with stronger tree models and extended boosting horizons.

Result: BoostLLM delivers consistent gains over standard fine-tuning across various backbones and datasets, matching or surpassing XGBoost over a wide range of shot counts and outperforming GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model. The approach scales further when paired with stronger tree models and longer boosting horizons, indicating boosted training can benefit LLM fine-tuning for structured data in low-data regimes.

Conclusion: Boosting can serve as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning, particularly for structured data in low-data settings. The combination of sequential PEFT adapters and a tabular-path view provides a data-efficient, scalable pathway to leverage LLMs alongside tree-ensemble inductive biases.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adapted to tabular prediction by serializing structured features into natural language, but their performance in low-data regimes remains limited compared to gradient-boosted decision trees (GBDTs). In this work, we revisit the boosting paradigm, traditionally associated with tree ensembles, and ask whether it can be applied as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning. We propose BoostLLM, a framework that transforms parameter-efficient fine-tuning into a multi-round residual optimization process by training sequential PEFT adapters as weak learners. To incorporate tabular inductive bias, BoostLLM integrates decision-tree paths as a second input view alongside raw features; analysis reveals that the path view acts as a structured teacher in early training steps before the model shifts toward feature-driven representations. Empirically, BoostLLM achieves consistent improvements over standard fine-tuning across multiple LLM backbones and datasets, matching or surpassing XGBoost across a wide range of shot counts and outperforming GPT-4o-based methods with a 4B model. We further show that the framework scales: pairing with stronger tree models and extended boosting horizons yields additional gains under appropriate stabilization. These results suggest that boosting can serve as a general training principle for LLM fine-tuning, particularly in low-data regimes for structured data.

</details>


### [231] [Listwise Policy Optimization: Group-based RLVR as Target-Projection on the LLM Response Simplex](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06139)
*Yun Qu,Qi Wang,Yixiu Mao,Heming Zou,Yuhang Jiang,Yingyue Li,Wutong Xu,Lizhou Cai,Weijie Liu,Clive Bai,Kai Yang,Yangkun Chen,Saiyong Yang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) for RL with verifiable rewards in LLMs, deriving a target-projection framework that explicitly minimizes divergence on the response simplex, yielding monotonic improvement, stability, and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group-based policy gradients implicitly define a target distribution on the response simplex; there is a unifying geometric structure. The work aims to demystify the implicit target and optimize toward it more explicitly.

Method: Restrict the proximal RL objective to the response simplex and perform exact divergence minimization for the projection step; decouple the projection from the policy update; introduce LPO and analyze its properties such as monotonic improvement and flexibility in divergence choices.

Result: Empirically, LPO improves training performance over standard policy gradient baselines under matched targets, while preserving optimization stability and response diversity across diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones.

Conclusion: Explicit target-projection via LPO clarifies the optimization geometry in RL with verifiable rewards, enabling stable, diverse, and improved policy learning; the decoupled projection step provides flexibility through different divergences.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for large language models (LLMs) post-training to incentivize reasoning capacity. Among existing recipes, group-based policy gradient is prevalent, which samples a group of responses per prompt and updates the policy via group-relative advantage signals. This work reveals that these optimization strategies share a common geometric structure: each implicitly defines a target distribution on the response simplex and projects toward it via first-order approximation. Building on this insight, we propose Listwise Policy Optimization (LPO) to explicitly conduct the target-projection, which demystifies the implicit target by restricting the proximal RL objective to the response simplex, and then projects the policy via exact divergence minimization. This framework provides (i) monotonic improvement on the listwise objective with bounded, zero-sum, and self-correcting projection gradients, and (ii) flexibility in divergence selection with distinct structural properties through the decoupled projection step. On diverse reasoning tasks and LLM backbones, LPO consistently improves training performance over typical policy gradient baselines under matched targets, while intrinsically preserving optimization stability and response diversity.

</details>


### [232] [SymDrift: One-Shot Generative Modeling under Symmetries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06140)
*Samir Darouich,Vinh Tong,Lluís Pastor-Pérez,Tanja Bien,Loay Mualem,Mathias Niepert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymDrift makes drifting diffusion models symmetry-aware for 3D molecular generation, enabling single-shot inference with two strategies (symmetrized coordinate drift via alignment and a G-invariant embedding), achieving strong performance and large speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D physical systems (e.g., molecules) require distributions invariant to global symmetries (rotations). While equivariant diffusion/flow models handle this, they rely on costly multi-step sampling. Drifting models offer single-step generation but face symmetry-specific challenges that typically require expensive distribution symmetrization.

Method: Introduce SymDrift with two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment, and (ii) a G-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. The approach makes the drifting field itself symmetry-aware without symmetrizing the empirical distribution.

Result: Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation and remains competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches. One-shot inference reduces computational overhead by up to 40x relative to baselines, enabling high-throughput applications like virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration.

Conclusion: SymDrift provides a symmetry-aware drifting framework that enables efficient one-shot generation for 3D molecular tasks, offering substantial speedups and competitive performance compared to multi-step methods, with strong applicability to high-throughput settings.

Abstract: Generative modeling of physical systems, such as molecules, requires learning distributions that are invariant under global symmetries, such as rotations in three-dimensional space. Equivariant diffusion and flow matching models can incorporate such invariances effectively, even when trained on a non-invariant empirical distribution, but they typically rely on costly multi-step sampling. Recently, drifting models have emerged as an efficient alternative, enabling single-step generation and achieving state-of-the-art performance in generative modeling tasks. However, we show that drifting models face a symmetry-specific challenge, since an equivariant generator does not generally produce the same drifting field as the one obtained from the symmetrized target distribution. Addressing this issue would require expensive symmetrization of the empirical distribution. To avoid this cost, we propose SymDrift, a framework that makes the drifting field itself symmetry-aware. We introduce two complementary strategies: (i) a symmetrized drift in coordinate space based on optimal alignment, and (ii) a $G$-invariant embedding that removes symmetry ambiguity by construction. Empirically, SymDrift outperforms existing one-shot methods on standard benchmarks for conformer and transition state generation, while remaining competitive with significantly more expensive multi-step approaches. By enabling one-shot inference, SymDrift reduces computational overhead by up to 40$\times$ compared to existing baselines, making it promising for high-throughput applications such as virtual drug screening and large-scale reaction network exploration.

</details>


### [233] [Matrix-Valued Optimism is Matrix-Valued Augmentation: Additive Hybrid Designs for Constrained Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06141)
*Jiayi Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends equivalence between augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal-dual methods from scalar to matrix-valued corrections, proving an additivity principle for symmetric matrices and deriving a closed-form hybrid rule that optimally splits a correction between channels and sets steps via spectral weights; experiments show improved finite-step feasibility and robustness under ill-conditioning, with a limitation that exact cancellation requires large corrections as conditioning worsens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify and extend the understanding of two popular constraint-handling mechanisms (augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal–dual) to matrix corrections, to uncover design freedoms and improve finite-step feasibility, especially under ill-conditioning.

Method: Prove an additivity principle: for symmetric matrix corrections, the ideal primal trajectory depends only on the sum of the corrections, not their distribution. Derive a closed-form hybrid rule that (i) selects a correction matrix, (ii) splits it between augmented and optimistic channels, and (iii) sets primal/dual step sizes using local spectral weights. Analyze a step-size limit and study ill-conditioning effects. Validate via experiments on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled constraint-Jacobian conditioning.

Result: The ideal primal trajectory is determined solely by the total correction; different decompositions with the same sum yield the same trajectory in theory. The hybrid rule closely tracks a grid-search oracle and outperforms pure augmented or pure optimistic endpoints, while remaining competitive with first-order baselines under moderate ill-conditioning. The experiments reveal a practical limitation: exact cancellation requires increasingly large corrections as the constraint Jacobian becomes ill-conditioned.

Conclusion: There exists a design freedom in distributing a symmetric matrix correction between augmented and optimistic channels; a principled hybrid rule can exploit this to improve finite-step feasibility and robustness. The main limitation is deterioration under severe ill-conditioning, where large corrections are needed for exact cancellation.

Abstract: Augmented Lagrangian and optimistic primal--dual methods stabilize equality-constrained optimization through seemingly different mechanisms: the former adds constraint-dependent primal curvature, while the latter adds dual memory. Recent work has shown that these mechanisms are equivalent for scalar parameters. We extend this equivalence to matrix-valued correction. We prove an additivity principle: for symmetric matrix parameters, the ideal primal trajectory depends only on the summed correction matrix, not on how it is split between augmented and optimistic channels. This exposes a design freedom: algebraically equivalent decompositions can have different finite-step feasibility because augmented correction affects primal curvature, whereas optimistic correction affects the scale of the dual memory correction. We formulate the resulting step-size-limited design problem and derive a closed-form hybrid rule that selects a matrix correction, splits it between the two channels, and chooses primal and dual steps using local spectral weights. Experiments on nonlinear equality-constrained problems with controlled constraint-Jacobian conditioning show that the hybrid design improves over pure augmented and pure optimistic endpoints, closely tracks a grid-search hybrid oracle, and is competitive with first-order primal--dual baselines under mild-to-moderate ill-conditioning. The experiments also identify the expected limitation: exact cancellation requires increasingly large matrix corrections as the constraint Jacobian becomes ill-conditioned.

</details>


### [234] [Unifying Goal-Conditioned RL and Unsupervised Skill Learning via Control-Maximization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06145)
*Alireza Modirshanechi,Benjamin Eysenbach,Peter Dayan,Eric Schulz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies GCRL and MISL under control maximization, shows three canonical GCRL formulations are inequivalent but share a view of goal-sensitivity; MISL corresponds to skill-sensitivity and is bounded by downstream goal-sensitivity, enabling a mapping from MISL objectives to downstream tasks and guiding pretraining choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why MISL-based pretraining can benefit diverse downstream goal-reaching tasks and reconcile competing GCRL and MISL definitions.

Method: Formalize three canonical GCRL formulations, prove their fundamental inequivalence, reinterpret MISL as measures of skill-sensitivity, derive bounds that link MISL objectives to downstream goal-sensitivity, and establish a correspondence between MISL objectives and downstream GCRL tasks.

Result: Three GCRL formulations are inequivalent even in the same environment; MISL objectives bound by formulation-specific goal-sensitivities; there exists a matching MISL objective for each GCRL formulation where greater skill diversity yields greater downstream sensitivity; provides a theoretical foundation linking pretraining objectives to downstream tasks.

Conclusion: The work provides a theoretical framework for RL pretraining by clarifying how MISL-based pretraining relates to downstream GCRL objectives and offering guidance on selecting pretraining objectives aligned with targeted downstream tasks.

Abstract: Unsupervised pretraining has driven empirical advances in goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), but its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. In particular, an influential class of methods, mutual information skill learning (MISL), discovers behaviorally diverse skills that can later be used for downstream goal-reaching. However, it remains a theoretical mystery why skills learned through MISL should support goal-reaching. A subtle challenge is that both GCRL and MISL are umbrella terms: different GCRL tasks use distinct criteria for measuring goal-reaching performance, while different MISL methods optimize distinct notions of behavioral diversity. We address this challenge and unify GCRL and MISL as instances of control maximization. We identify three canonical GCRL formulations and prove that they are fundamentally inequivalent: they can induce incompatible optimal policies even in the same environment. Nevertheless, they all share a common interpretation: a well-performing goal-conditioned policy is one whose future trajectory is highly sensitive to the commanded goal, with the precise notion of sensitivity determined by the GCRL formulation. Noting that MISL objectives can be understood as measures of skill-sensitivity akin to goal-sensitivity, we show that MISL objectives are bounded by formulation-specific downstream goal-sensitivities. These bounds establish a precise correspondence between MISL methods and downstream GCRL tasks: for every GCRL formulation, there exists a matching MISL objective for which more diverse skills afford greater downstream goal sensitivity. Our results thus lay a theoretical foundation for RL pretraining and have important practical implications, such as suggesting which pretraining objectives to use when a user cares about a specific class of downstream tasks.

</details>


### [235] [AdaGamma: State-Dependent Discounting for Temporal Adaptation in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06149)
*Yaomin Wang,Jianting Pan,Ran Tian,Xiaoyang Li,Yu Zhang,Hengle Qin,Tianshu YU*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaGamma introduces state-dependent discounting in deep actor-critic RL with a return-consistency regularizer, achieving improvements across SAC/PPO and in an online JD Logistics test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The discount factor governs planning horizon and bootstrapping; fixed state-agnostic discounts ignore state-specific uncertainty and dynamics. State-dependent discounting could improve sample efficiency and performance but can destabilize training without regularization.

Method: Propose AdaGamma: jointly learn a state-dependent discount function and a return-consistency objective that stabilizes the backup structure. The paper analyzes the Bellman operator under state-dependent discounting for basic well-posedness and demonstrates integration into SAC and PPO, with empirical validation on continuous-control tasks and an online A/B test.

Result: Consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks when integrated with SAC/PPO and statistically significant gains in an online JD Logistics A/B test.

Conclusion: State-dependent discounting is effective in deep RL when paired with a return-consistency regularizer that prevents degenerate target manipulation and stabilizes learning.

Abstract: The discount factor in reinforcement learning controls both the effective planning horizon and the strength of bootstrapping, yet most deep RL methods use a single fixed value across all states. While state-dependent discounting is conceptually appealing, naive deep actor--critic implementations can become unstable and degenerate toward TD-error collapse. We propose AdaGamma, a practical deep actor--critic method for state-dependent discounting that learns a state-dependent discount function together with a return-consistency objective to regularize the induced backup structure. On the theory side, we analyze the Bellman operator induced by state-dependent discounting and establish its basic well-posedness properties under suitable conditions. Empirically, AdaGamma integrates into both SAC and PPO, yielding consistent improvements on continuous-control benchmarks, and achieves statistically significant gains in an online A/B test on the JD Logistics platform. These results suggest that state-dependent discounting can be made effective in deep RL when coupled with a return-consistency objective that prevents degenerate target manipulation.

</details>


### [236] [Grokking or Glitching? How Low-Precision Drives Slingshot Loss Spikes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06152)
*Liu Hanqing,Jianjun Cao,Yuanze Li,Zijian Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI) due to finite-precision arithmetic explains Slingshot loss spikes: gradient rounding breaks the zero-sum constraint, triggering a positive feedback that exponentially inflates classifier and feature means and mimics late-stage training anomalies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify the true trigger of the Slingshot mechanism and determine whether floating-point precision limits, not intrinsic optimization dynamics, drive the observed spikes and parameter growth.

Method: Theoretical analysis of backpropagation under finite precision, introducing an absorption-error threshold where the correct-class logit separation exceeds machine epsilon; show that the gradient for the correct class rounds to zero while incorrect classes remain, breaking gradient zero-sum and creating a positive feedback loop that inflates means.

Result: Demonstrates that NFI can cause rapid norm growth and logit divergence, explaining Slingshot spikes as a numerical dynamic; in practical tasks, partial absorption may occur without visible spikes but still breaks zero-sum and drives parameter growth.

Conclusion: Slingshot can be understood as a finite-precision numerical phenomenon (NFI); provides a testable explanation for abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence in late-stage training and calls for reevaluation of such dynamics beyond purely optimization-driven narratives.

Abstract: Deep neural networks exhibit periodic loss spikes during unregularized long-term training, a phenomenon known as the "Slingshot Mechanism." Existing work usually attributes this to intrinsic optimization dynamics, but its triggering mechanism remains unclear. This paper proves that this phenomenon is a result of floating-point arithmetic precision limits. As training enters a high-confidence stage, the difference between the correct-class logit and the other logits may exceed the absorption-error threshold. Then during backpropagation, the gradient of the correct class is rounded exactly to zero, while the gradients of the incorrect classes remain nonzero. This breaks the zero-sum constraint of gradients across classes and introduces a systematic drift in the parameter update of the classifier layer. We prove that this drift forms a positive feedback loop with the feature, causing the global classifier mean and the global feature mean to grow exponentially. We call this mechanism Numerical Feature Inflation (NFI). This mechanism explains the rapid norm growth before a Slingshot spike, the subsequent reappearance of gradients, and the resulting loss spike. We further show that NFI is not equivalent to an observed loss spike: in more practical tasks, partial absorption may not produce visible spikes, but it can still break the zero-sum constraint and drive rapid growth of parameter norms. Our results reinterpret Slingshot as a numerical dynamic of finite-precision training, and provide a testable explanation for abnormal parameter growth and logit divergence in late-stage training.

</details>


### [237] [Entropy-Regularized Adjoint Matching for Offline RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06156)
*Abdelghani Ghanem,Mounir Ghogho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ME-AM: a maximum-entropy adjoint matching framework for offline RL with flow-based policies that mitigates popularity bias and expands support via Mirror Descent entropy maximization and a Mixture Behavior Prior, yielding strong performance on sparse-reward continuous control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) stabilizes optimization but remains tied to a fixed behavior distribution, causing popularity bias (suppresses high-reward actions in low-density regions) and support binding (limits off-manifold exploration). Residual Gaussian policies reintroduce expressivity bottlenecks. A framework that preserves expressive flow-based policies while enabling robust off-distribution exploration is needed.

Method: Two mechanisms are proposed within the continuous flow formulation: (1) a Mirror Descent entropy maximization objective to increase policy entropy and reduce popularity bias, aiding extraction of optimal policies from offline data; (2) a Mixture Behavior Prior that broadens the geometric support to cover out-of-distribution high-reward regions, while maintaining absolute continuity of the generative vector field.

Result: Empirically, ME-AM is competitive or superior to prior state-of-the-art methods across a diverse suite of sparse-reward continuous control environments.

Conclusion: ME-AM provides a unified, principled framework that addresses popularity bias and restricted exploration in flow-based offline RL by combining entropy-regularized optimization with a broadened, yet continuous, behavior prior, enabling robust high-reward action selection from offline data.

Abstract: Integrating expressive generative policies, such as flow-matching models, into offline reinforcement learning (RL) allows agents to capture complex, multi-modal behaviors. While Q-learning with Adjoint Matching (QAM) stabilizes policy optimization via the continuous adjoint method, it remains inherently bound to the fixed behavior distribution. This dependence induces a \textit{popularity bias} that can suppress high-reward actions in low-density regions, and creates a \textit{support binding} that restricts off-manifold exploration. Existing workarounds, such as appending \textit{residual} Gaussian policies, often re-introduce the expressivity bottlenecks associated with unimodal distributions. In this work, we propose \textit{Maximum Entropy Adjoint Matching} (ME-AM), a unified framework that addresses these limitations within the continuous flow formulation. ME-AM incorporates two mechanisms: (1) a Mirror Descent entropy maximization objective that mitigates the popularity bias to facilitate the extraction of optimal policies from offline datasets, and (2) a \textit{Mixture Behavior Prior} that mathematically broadens the geometric support to encompass out-of-distribution high-reward regions. By exploring this extended geometry, ME-AM identifies robust actions while preserving the absolute continuity of the generative vector field. Empirically, ME-AM demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to prior state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods across a diverse suite of sparse-reward continuous control environments.

</details>


### [238] [One Algorithm, Two Goals: Dual Scoring for Parameter and Data Selection in LLM Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06166)
*Xinrui Chen,Liu Yang,Ou Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualSFT jointly selects a parameter mask and data subset from shared gradient statistics to enable efficient, coordinated fine-tuning of large language models; it outperforms sequential baselines under matched budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce fine-tuning cost and coordination overhead by integrating parameter and data selection into a single framework via bilevel optimization and a unified gradient-based scoring rule.

Method: Formulates parameter and data selection as two bilevel optimization problems with a shared validation objective. Derives a local response-surrogate scoring rule from first- and second-order validation-improvement approximations, yielding a gradient interaction matrix whose column-wise and row-wise aggregations correspond to parameter importance and data utility. Establishes a closed-form row-column correspondence for co-extracting both signals and proposes DualSFT, a one-shot dual-scoring algorithm that outputs a parameter mask and data subset using shared gradient statistics.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 3B-9B LLMs shows that single-axis DualSFT variants improve target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within their comparison groups. Full DualSFT achieves a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets.

Conclusion: The approach delivers a unified, efficient mechanism for simultaneous parameter and data selection, enabling coordinated fine-tuning with improved trade-offs. The framework may generalize to other model scales and fine-tuning objectives.

Abstract: In Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning, parameter and data selection are common strategies for reducing fine-tuning cost, yet they are typically driven by separate scoring mechanisms. When a parameter mask and data subset jointly determine restricted fine-tuning, this separation incurs redundant overhead and makes coordinated selection difficult. We cast parameter and data selection as two bilevel selection problems under a common validation objective and derive a shared local response-surrogate scoring rule. Under first- and second-order validation-improvement approximations, parameter importance and data utility emerge as column-wise and row-wise aggregations of a single gradient interaction matrix, yielding a closed-form row-column correspondence for co-extracting both signals. Building on this structure, we propose DualSFT (Dual-Selection Fine-Tuning), a one-shot dual-scoring algorithm that produces a parameter mask and data subset from shared gradient statistics. On 3B-9B LLMs, single-axis DualSFT variants strengthen target-task performance and stability-plasticity trade-offs within their comparison groups, while full DualSFT yields a more favorable joint-constrained trade-off than sequential hybrid baselines under matched budgets.

</details>


### [239] [Mean Mode Screaming: Mean--Variance Split Residuals for 1000-Layer Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06169)
*Pengqi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep diffusion transformers (DiTs) face a silent, mean-dominated collapse when scaled to hundreds of layers. The authors diagnose this as Mean Mode Screaming (MMS), root in mean-coherent backward shocks and gradient decomposition, exacerbated by Softmax Jacobian nullspace. They propose Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) residuals to separate centered updates from trunk means, yielding stability up to 1000 layers in experiments (400-layer relief and a 1000-layer scale validation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and mitigate depth-induced instability in ultra-deep diffusion transformers. The work identifies a mechanistic failure mode (MMS), links it to a gradient decomposition into mean-coherent and centered components, and uncovers how Softmax Jacobian nullspace suppresses attention-gradients when token representations homogenize.

Method: Mechanistic auditing of gradients; exact decomposition into mean-coherent and centered components; analysis of residual writers; design of MV-Split residuals combining a centered update with leaky trunk-mean replacement; empirical evaluation on a 400-layer single-stream DiT and a 1000-layer DiT for scale validation; comparison to LayerScale and token-isotropic gating.

Result: Identification of MMS as the trigger for collapse; clear gradient decomposition explains instability; MV-Split prevents divergent collapse, tracking near pre-crash baseline and outperforming LayerScale; demonstrates stable training at extreme depth (up to 1000 layers).

Conclusion: MV-Split provides a robust remedy for ultra-deep DiTs, highlighting the critical role of managing mean and centered gradient components and the Softmax Jacobian effect. The work establishes practical depth limits and opens avenues for further stabilizing extremely deep diffusion architectures.

Abstract: Scaling Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to hundreds of layers introduces a structural vulnerability: networks can enter a silent, mean-dominated collapse state that homogenizes token representations and suppresses centered variation. Through mechanistic auditing, we isolate the trigger event of this collapse as Mean Mode Screaming (MMS). MMS can occur even when training appears stable, with a mean-coherent backward shock on residual writers that opens deep residual branches and drives the network into a mean-dominated state. We show this behavior is driven by an exact decomposition of these gradients into mean-coherent and centered components, compounded by the structural suppression of attention-logit gradients through the null space of the Softmax Jacobian once values homogenize.
  To address this, we propose Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) Residuals, which combine a separately gained centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement. On a 400-layer single-stream DiT, MV-Split prevents the divergent collapse that crashes the un-stabilized baseline; it tracks close to the baseline's pre-crash trajectory while remaining substantially better than token-isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale across the full schedule. Finally, we present a 1000-layer DiT as a scale-validation run at boundary scales, establishing that the architecture remains stably trainable at extreme depth.

</details>


### [240] [In-Context Black-Box Optimization with Unreliable Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06187)
*Nicolas Samuel Blumer,Julien Martinelli,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A feedback-aware in-context optimizer for black-box optimization that leverages multiple sources of feedback via a structured prior and a pretrained transformer, improving query selection and robustness across diverse, potentially biased signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the practical scenario where multiple feedback sources (experts, simulators, predictors, heuristics) can guide search but may be biased, input-dependent, or misleading. Extends beyond single-task feedback handling and test-time history-only signals by enabling cross-task adaptation to diverse feedback.

Method: Pretrain a feedback-aware transformer using a structured feedback prior that encodes how feedback sources vary in access, relevance, and distortion relative to the true objective. At test time, the model estimates source reliability in context by comparing observed objective values with auxiliary signals and then performs query selection within the FICBO framework.

Result: Empirically, FICBO outperforms baselines on synthetic and real-world tasks by effectively leveraging informative feedback while remaining robust to weak or misleading sources. The experiments also illustrate interpretability insights into how the model perceives test-time feedback sources and makes decisions.

Conclusion: FICBO demonstrates robust cross-source feedback integration for black-box optimization, enabling improved query selection and offering interpretable insights into how auxiliary signals influence decisions. The approach highlights the value of explicit reliability modeling of feedback sources in in-context optimization.

Abstract: Black-box optimization in science and engineering often comes with side information: experts, simulators, pretrained predictors, or heuristics can suggest which candidates look promising. This information can accelerate search, but it can also be biased, input-dependent, or misleading. Feedback-aware BO methods typically handle one task at a time, limiting their ability to generalize over multiple sources of feedback. In-context optimizers address cross-task adaptation, but usually assume that optimization history is the only available signal at test time. We study feedback-informed in-context black-box optimization (FICBO), where a pretrained optimizer conditions on both the observed history and cheap auxiliary feedback for the current candidate set. We introduce a structured feedback prior that models how feedback sources vary in their access, relevance, and distortion relative to the true objective, and use it to pretrain a feedback-aware transformer. At test time, the model estimates source reliability in context by comparing observed objective values with auxiliary signals, improving query selection. On synthetic and real-world tasks, FICBO effectively exploits informative feedback while remaining robust to weak or misleading sources, improving over other baselines. Empirical investigations further illustrate how the model perceives test-time sources, offering insights into its interpretability and decision-making process.

</details>


### [241] [Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06190)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular reduction framework for budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts using online regression oracles to transform constrained learning into an unconstrained contextual bandit problem via adaptive surrogate rewards; extends SquareCB and yields improved guarantees in adversarial settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address budget-constrained decision making under adversarial contexts in the continuing horizon, where context is potentially non-stationary and standard stochastic-context guarantees do not apply. Aims to unify constrained learning with online regression tools and to improve robustness and guarantees.

Method: Builds on the SquareCB framework; introduces a simple, modular reduction that uses online regression oracles to construct adaptively defined surrogate reward functions, converting the constrained problem into a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem over the same horizon with continuation after budget exhaustion.

Result: Provides improved theoretical guarantees for adversarial contexts, together with an efficient algorithm and a compact, transparent analysis.

Conclusion: The proposed reduction offers a practical and robust approach to budget-constrained contextual bandits under adversarial contexts, extending SquareCB to a more general setting and delivering solid performance guarantees with a streamlined analysis.

Abstract: We study budget-constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We focus on the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget for cumulative cost is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and the violation of the budget constraint. Building on the seminal $\mathsf{SquareCB}$ framework of Foster et al. [2018], we propose a simple and modular framework that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to prior works, which focus on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for more general adversarial contexts, together with an efficient algorithm with a compact and transparent analysis.

</details>


### [242] [Playing the network backward: A Game Theoretic Attribution Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06212)
*Jakob Paul Zimmermann,Jim Berend,Georg Loho,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified game-theoretic framework recasting backward attribution (gradients, LRP) as a two-player game on an extended network graph, yielding attribution maps as trajectory projections and enabling principled adaptations; an alpha-beta-LRP variant shows state-of-the-art localisation on ViT-B/16.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backward attribution methods (gradients, LRP, transformer-specific rules) lack a common framework for comparison and principled adaptation. A game-theoretic formulation provides a unified, flexible foundation to analyze, compare, and tailor explanations.

Method: Recast backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Show that gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family correspond to integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria. Treat explanation properties as game-theoretic concepts (policy regularization, risk aversion, extended action sets), enabling novel adaptations of backward rules.

Result: On ViT-B/16, a selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

Conclusion: The framework translates explanation goals into game-theoretic constructs, enabling principled, novel adaptations of backward attribution methods and demonstrated practical gains on a transformer model.

Abstract: Attribution methods explain which input features drive a model's prediction, making them central to model debugging and mechanistic interpretability. Yet backward attribution methods, including gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules, lack a shared framework in which to compare the underlying backward calculations. We introduce such a framework by recasting backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, so attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions rather than the primary object. Desired explanation properties, such as localisation focus, robustness to input noise, or stable attention routing, can be specified as game-theoretic concepts, including policy regularization, risk aversion, and extended action sets, and translate directly into novel adaptations of the well-known backward rules. On ViT-B/16, one such selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

</details>


### [243] [Bandit Learning in General Open Multi-agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06202)
*Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general open-system bandit framework for arriving/departing agents, introducing pre-training degree, stability, and global dynamic regret; presents certified global-UCB algorithms with regret bounds that separate entry uncertainty from learning dynamics, and establishes tight lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open digital platforms exhibit non-stationarity due to agent arrivals/departures. Existing open-system bandit work relies on restrictive assumptions; a general model with heterogeneous rewards and arbitrary agent patterns is needed to understand learning performance.

Method: Formulate a unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics. Define pre-training degree (information carried by new agents), stability (impact of new arrivals), and global dynamic regret (cumulative reward compared to a time-varying optimum). Develop certified global-UCB algorithms with theoretical guarantees; derive upper bounds showing linear dependence on pre-training degree and regime-dependent terms; prove lower bounds for hard instances.

Result: Regret decomposes into entry uncertainty (linear in pre-training degree) and learning-time terms. In stable regimes, regret is governed by the time to identify a persistent optimal arm and the agent-pattern complexity. Lower bounds match the upper bounds, demonstrating tightness.

Conclusion: Provides a general, provable framework for open-system bandits, clarifying how onboarding information and agent dynamics shape regret; introduces certified global-UCB methods and tight bounds, with potential for extension to broader dynamic settings.

Abstract: Recent developments in digital platforms have highlighted the prevalence of open systems, where agents can arrive and depart over time. While bandit learning in open systems has recently received initial attention, existing work imposes structural assumptions that are frequently violated in practice. A learning paradigm for general open systems creates fresh challenges: newly arriving agents induce endogenous non-stationarity; agent patterns determine how quickly information accumulates; and new agents make regret scale further with the time horizon. To this end, we formulate a unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics, including heterogeneous rewards and general agent patterns. We introduce new concepts to capture the inherent complexities: the \emph{pre-training degree} of new agents quantifies how much information an agent carries upon entry, \emph{stability} measures the impact of new agents on the system, and \emph{global dynamic regret} compares the cumulative expected reward of all active agents with that of the varying optimal arms. We develop certified global-UCB learning methodologies with provable guarantees. Our regret bounds reveal that entry uncertainty enters linearly via the pre-training degree, while in stable regimes, regret is governed by the time needed to identify a persistent optimal arm, as well as by the agent patterns. We further show that these dependencies are tight via lower bounds in hard instances.

</details>


### [244] [When Labels Have Structure: Improving Image Classification with Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06274)
*April Chan,Davide D'Ascenzo,Sebastiano Cultrera di Montesano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE) introduces a drop-in loss that leverages class hierarchies to better reflect semantic distances. It combines prediction aggregation (propagating probability mass up the hierarchy) and ancestral label smoothing (distributing the true label signal along the root path). Empirically, HACE improves accuracy across multiple datasets, architectures, and evaluation regimes, with notable gains in end-to-end training (mean +4.66%) and linear probing (mean +2.18%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cross-entropy treats all misclassifications equally and ignores semantic distances encoded in a class hierarchy. A hierarchy-aware objective can penalize near-misses more appropriately and leverage hierarchical structure to guide learning.

Method: Introduce Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE) as a drop-in replacement for cross-entropy. Two components: (1) prediction aggregation that propagates predicted probability mass upward through the class hierarchy so parent nodes accumulate confidence from children; (2) ancestral label smoothing that distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. Evaluated on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, NABirds; end-to-end across six architectures and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features.

Result: In end-to-end training, HACE improves accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 of 18 architecture–dataset pairs, mean gain 4.66%. In linear probing with frozen features, HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, mean improvement 2.18% over the next best baseline.

Conclusion: Hierarchy-aware training yields robust gains across tasks and regimes, validating HACE as an effective drop-in loss that respects semantic class structure and improves predictive performance.

Abstract: Standard cross-entropy is the default classification loss across virtually all of machine learning, yet it treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring the semantic distances that a class hierarchy encodes. We propose Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE), a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy that incorporates a known class hierarchy directly into the loss. HACE combines two components: prediction aggregation, which propagates the model's probability mass upward through the class hierarchy to ensure that parent nodes accumulate the confidence of their children; and ancestral label smoothing, which distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. We evaluate HACE on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, and NABirds in two regimes: end-to-end training across six architectures spanning convolutional and attention-based designs, and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features. In end-to-end training, HACE improves accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 out of 18 architecture--dataset pairs, with a mean gain of 4.66\%. In linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features, HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, with a mean improvement of 2.18\% over the next best baseline.

</details>


### [245] [Memory Efficient Full-gradient Attacks (MEFA) Framework for Adversarial Defense Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06357)
*Yuan Du,Mitchel Hill,HanQin Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation framework for iterative stochastic purification defenses, enabling exact-gradient white-box attacks via gradient checkpointing, applied to diffusion-based purification and Langevin EBMs; reveals vulnerabilities ignored by approximate gradients and underscores the need for exact-gradient benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory bottlenecks in backprop through long purification trajectories hinder accurate robustness assessment; stochasticity in iterative purification causes large variability in reported robustness, risking unreliable conclusions.

Method: Use gradient checkpointing to trade recomputation for memory, enabling full backpropagation through purification trajectories; pair with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability; apply to diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs).

Result: Achieves stronger state-of-the-art ℓ∞ and ℓ2 white-box attacks; uncovers vulnerabilities not exposed by approximate-gradient methods; supports probing out-of-distribution robustness; demonstrates that exact-gradient evaluation improves reliability of robustness benchmarking.

Conclusion: Exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses; the proposed memory-efficient framework enables practical, precise attacks and more trustworthy robustness assessments.

Abstract: This work studies the robust evaluation of iterative stochastic purification defenses under white-box adversarial attacks. Our key technical insight is that gradient checkpointing makes exact end-to-end gradient computation through long purification trajectories practical by trading additional recomputation for substantially lower memory usage. This enables full-gradient adaptive attacks against diffusion- and Langevin-based purification defenses, where prior evaluations often resort to approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints. These approximations can weaken the attack signal and risk overestimating robustness. In parallel, stochasticity in iterative purification is frequently under-controlled, even though different purification trajectories can substantially change reported robustness metrics. Building on this insight, we introduce a memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation framework for stochastic purification defenses. The framework combines checkpointed backpropagation with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability, thereby reducing memory bottlenecks while preserving exact gradients. We evaluate diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs), demonstrating that full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities missed by approximate-gradient evaluations. Our framework yields stronger state-of-the-art $\ell_{\infty}$ and $\ell_{2}$ white-box attacks and further supports probing out-of-distribution robustness. Overall, our results show that exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses.

</details>


### [246] [Contrastive Identification and Generation in the Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06211)
*Xiaoyu Li,Andi Han,Jiaojiao Jiang,Junbin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work initiates contrastive identification and generation in the limit with relational (contrastive) supervision, providing exact characterizations, a new combinatorial dimension, tight sample complexity results, a hierarchy with existing models, and corruption-resilience results unified by a crossing-graph construct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend identification in the limit from singleton/positive signals to relational supervision signals. Address how contrastive data (unordered pairs with opposite labels) affects learnability and generation, and how robustness to corruption can differ from positive-data settings.

Method: (i) Define contrastive identifiable classes as a geometric refinement of Angluin’s tell-tale condition; (ii) introduce the contrastive closure dimension, an analogue of closure dimension; (iii) exactly characterize uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity; (iv) establish a strict hierarchy showing contrastive generation and positive-identification (text identification) are mutually incomparable; (v) prove a reversal under finite adversarial corruption: contrastive methods can be budget-robust, while positive-data methods fail; (vi) develop the crossing graph as a unifying object capturing ambiguity, generation obstructions, and corruption defects.

Result: Exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes; introduction of the contrastive closure dimension; tight-sample characterization of uniform contrastive generation; a mutual incomparability hierarchy with positive-identification and generation; a sharp corruption-reversal result; a unifying crossing-graph framework.

Conclusion: Relational (contrastive) supervision fundamentally shifts identifiability and generation in the limit, enabling robustness to finite corruption and offering a unified analytical framework via the crossing graph that encodes ambiguity and obstructions across family-level generation and corruption.

Abstract: In the classical identification in the limit model of Gold [1967], a stream of positive examples is presented round by round, and the learner must eventually recover the target hypothesis. Recently, Kleinberg and Mullainathan [2024] introduced generation in the limit, where the learner instead must eventually output novel elements of the target's support. Both lines of work focus on positive-only or fully labeled data. Yet many natural supervision signals are inherently relational rather than singleton, which encode relationships between examples rather than labels of individual ones. We initiate the study of contrastive identification and generation in the limit, where the learner observes a contrastive presentation of data: a stream of unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$ satisfying $h(x)\ne h(y)$ for an unknown target binary hypothesis $h$, but which element is positive is hidden from the learner. We first present three results in the noiseless setting: an exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes (a one-line geometric refinement of Angluin [1980]'s tell-tale condition), a combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension (a contrasitive analogue of the closure dimension in Raman et al. [2025]) and exactly characterizing uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity, and a strict hierarchy in which contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. We then prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation. The unifying technical object is the common crossing graph, which encodes pairwise ambiguity, family-level generation obstructions, and corruption defects in a single coverage-and-incidence language.

</details>


### [247] [E = T*H/(O+B): A Dimensionless Control Parameter for Mixture-of-Experts Ecology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06415)
*Qingjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes E = T*H/(O+B) as a single, dimensionless diagnostic for MoE training health. It predicts whether Mixture-of-Experts will maintain a healthy ecosystem or collapse into dead experts. With E >= 0.5, dead experts are avoided without handcrafted load-balancing losses; validated cross-modally and across vision and language tasks. Also uncovers several robust phenomena and argues E behaves like a Reynolds number for MoE ecology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models are prone to emergent maladaptive regimes where experts die or become underutilized. A unified, quantitative diagnostic could replace ad hoc auxiliary losses and guide hyperparameter tuning across domains.

Method: Define E = T*H/(O+B). Conduct 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling >11k training epochs; systematically vary routing temperature T, routing entropy weight H, oracle weight O, and balance weight B. Evaluate dead vs healthy experts across CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet-200, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. Investigate mechanisms such as resuscitation, dataset dependence of orthogonality toxicity, task complexity effects on the E threshold, the relationship between overfitting and ecological health, spontaneous collapse of three-tier MoE to two-tier, and temperature invariance over a 50x range.

Result: Empirical evidence that E >= 0.5 suffices to guarantee zero dead experts, removing need for handcrafted load-balancing losses. E's threshold shifts with task complexity; across tasks, cross-modal validation holds. Dead experts can be resuscitated when balance loss drives router re-exploration. Ortho toxicity is dataset-dependent rather than universal. Three-tier MoE often collapses into a two-tier functional structure. Ecological structure is temperature-invariant over a 50x range. E acts as a unified diagnostic for MoE training.

Conclusion: E is proposed as a unified diagnostic for MoE training, analogous to a Reynolds number for fluid dynamics. It enables predicting and steering expert ecology, reducing reliance on task-specific auxiliary losses and providing a cross-domain, interpretable metric for MoE health.

Abstract: We introduce E = T*H/(O+B), a dimensionless control parameter that predicts whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models will develop a healthy expert ecology or collapse into dead experts. E combines four hyperparameters -- routing temperature T, routing entropy weight H, oracle weight O, and balance weight B -- into a single quantity. Through 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling over 11,000 training epochs, we establish that E >= 0.5 alone is sufficient to guarantee zero dead experts, removing the necessity for handcrafted load-balancing auxiliary losses. We validate this cross-modally on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet-200, WikiText-2, and WikiText-103. Six additional findings emerge: (1) dead experts can resuscitate -- triggered by balance loss driving router re-exploration; (2) ortho toxicity is dataset-dependent, not universal; (3) task complexity shifts the critical E threshold; (4) model overfitting is decoupled from expert ecological health; (5) three-tier MoE spontaneously collapses into a two-tier functional structure; (6) ecological structure is temperature-invariant across a 50x range. We propose that E serves as a unified diagnostic for MoE training, analogous to the Reynolds number in fluid dynamics.

</details>


### [248] [Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06440)
*Daniel Uyterlinde,Swasti Shreya Mishra,Pascal Mettes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HypCBM reframes concept bottlenecks in hyperbolic space to align with hierarchical concept structures, using entailment cones as natural activation signals and an adaptive scaling rule for corrections. It achieves data-efficient interpretability, stronger hierarchical consistency, and robustness, rivaling Euclidean models trained with much more data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CBMs use flat Euclidean embeddings, treating concepts as independent; this ignores semantic hierarchies and entailed relationships among concepts, limiting interpretability and robustness, especially in data-scarce regimes.

Method: Reformulate concept activations as asymmetric geometric containment in hyperbolic space. Use vertices’ entailment cones as a test-time activation signal (margin of inclusion yields sparse, hierarchy-aware activations). Introduce an adaptive scaling law to propagate user-corrections coherently through the concept tree. Operates post-hoc without extra supervision or modules.

Result: HypCBM achieves performance on par with post-hoc Euclidean CBMs trained on ~20x more data in sparse interpretability settings, with stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic grounding of CBMs enables hierarchy-aware interpretability and data efficiency, with a practical mechanism for user-guided corrections; suggests a promising direction for incorporating semantic structure into interpretable neural models.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have become a popular approach to enable interpretability in neural networks by constraining classifier inputs to a set of human-understandable concepts. While effective, current models embed concepts in flat Euclidean space, treating them as independent, orthogonal dimensions. Concepts, however, are highly structured and organized in semantic hierarchies. To resolve this mismatch, we propose Hyperbolic Concept Bottleneck Models (HypCBM), a post-hoc framework that grounds the bottleneck in this structure by reformulating concept activation as asymmetric geometric containment in hyperbolic space. Rather than treating entailment cones as a pre-training penalty, we show they encode a natural test-time activation signal: the margin of inclusion within a concept's entailment cone yields sparse, hierarchy-aware activations without any additional supervision or learned modules. We further introduce an adaptive scaling law for hierarchically faithful interventions, propagating user corrections coherently through the concept tree. Empirically, HypCBM rivals post-hoc Euclidean models trained on 20$\times$ more data in sparse regimes required for human interpretability, with stronger hierarchical consistency and improved robustness to input corruptions.

</details>


### [249] [AffineLens: Capturing the Continuous Piecewise Affine Functions of Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06218)
*Yi Wei,Xuan Qi,Furao shen,Jian Zhao,Vittorio Murino,Cigdem Beyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AffineLens provides exact enumeration and visualization of the region partitioning in piecewise affine neural networks by computing neuron-induced hyperplane arrangements within a bounded input domain, enabling direct expressivity analysis across modern architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on indirect proxies for interpretability and lack of practical tools to enumerate CPA regions in realistic networks with bounded inputs.

Method: Compute hyperplane arrangements induced by neurons; intersect with input domain; enumerate affine subregions layer-wise; identify non-empty maximal CPA regions with interior representatives; support for BN, pooling, residuals, MLPs, conv; provide visualizations.

Result: A practical framework with provably non-empty regions and interior representatives; enables region count visualizations; provides quantitative metrics; applied to empirical study showing how architecture affects region complexity.

Conclusion: AffineLens enables qualitative and quantitative analysis of PANNs expressivity and geometry; broadly applicable to modern architectures; helps understand how design choices shape learned functions.

Abstract: Piecewise affine neural networks (PANNs) provide a principled geometric perspective on neural network expressivity by characterizing the input--output map as a continuous piecewise affine (CPA) function whose complexity is governed by the number, arrangement, and shapes of its affine regions. However, existing interpretability and expressivity analyses often rely on indirect proxies (e.g., activation statistics or theoretical upper bounds) and rarely offer practical, accurate tools for enumerating and visualizing the induced region partition under realistic architectures and bounded input domains. In this work, we present AffineLens, a unified framework for computing the hyperplane arrangements and polyhedral structures underlying PANNs. Given a calibrated (bounded) input polytope, AffineLens identifies the subset of neuron-induced hyperplanes that intersect the domain, enumerates the resulting affine sub-regions in a layer-wise manner, and returns provably non-empty maximal CPA regions together with interior representatives. The framework further provides visualizations of region partitioning and decision boundaries, enabling qualitative inspection alongside quantitative region counts. By exploiting the affine restriction property of CPA networks under fixed activation patterns, AffineLens supports a broad class of modern components, including batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, multilayer perceptrons, and convolutional layers. Finally, we use AffineLens to perform a systematic empirical study of architectural expressivity, comparing networks through region complexity metrics and revealing how design choices influence the geometry of learned functions.

</details>


### [250] [Agentic AIs Are the Missing Paradigm for Out-of-Distribution Generalization in Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06522)
*Xin Wang,Haibo Chen,Wenxuan Liu,Wenwu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Argues OOD for foundation models is structurally distinct from prior OOD problems and cannot be solved by model-centric methods alone; introduces an agentic paradigm with a stage-aware formalization, a parameter-coverage ceiling for model-centric approaches, four defining properties of agentic OOD systems, and a complementary research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models face open-world distribution shifts that are multi-stage and partially observed; current OOD research (model-centric) cannot fully address these shifts, necessitating an agentic, decision-and-action oriented paradigm.

Method: Stage-aware formalization of OOD for partially observed multi-stage distributions; theoretical proof of a parameter-coverage ceiling for model-centric methods; characterization of agentic OOD systems by four structural properties (perception, strategy selection, external action, closed-loop verification); critical responses to seven counterarguments and a proposed research agenda.

Result: Proves existence of practically relevant inputs beyond the reach of model-centric methods within a tolerance ε; shows that agentic systems extend the reachable set beyond this ceiling; acknowledges some counterarguments and advocates complementary integration of agentic and model-centric approaches; outlines a roadmap for future research.

Conclusion: Agentic paradigm is necessary as a first-class direction to address FM-OOD; progress requires explicit recognition and development of agentic methods alongside model-centric approaches.

Abstract: Foundation models (FMs) are increasingly deployed in open-world settings where distribution shift is the rule rather than the exception. The out-of-distribution (OOD) phenomena they face -- knowledge boundaries, capability ceilings, compositional shifts, and open-ended task variation -- differ in kind from the settings that have shaped prior OOD research, and are further complicated because the pretraining and post-training distributions of modern FMs are often only partially observed. Our position is that OOD for foundation models is a structurally distinct problem that cannot be solved within the prevailing model-centric paradigm, and that agentic systems constitute the missing paradigm required to address it. We defend this claim through four steps. First, we give a stage-aware formalization of OOD that accommodates partially observed multi-stage training distributions. Second, we prove a parameter coverage ceiling: there exist practically relevant inputs that no model-centric method (training-time or test-time) can handle within tolerance $\varepsilon$, for reasons intrinsic to parameter-based representation. Third, we characterize agentic OOD systems by four structural properties -- perception, strategy selection, external action, and closed-loop verification -- and show that they strictly extend the reachable set beyond the ceiling. Fourth, we respond to seven counterarguments, conceding two, and outline a research agenda. We do not claim that agentic methods subsume model-centric ones; we argue that the two are complementary, and that progress on FM-OOD requires explicit recognition of the agentic paradigm as a first-class research direction.

</details>


### [251] [Memory Inception: Latent-Space KV Cache Manipulation for Steering LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06225)
*Andy Zeyi Liu,Michael Zhang,Ilana Greenberg,Adam Alnasser,Lucas Baker,John Sous*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory Inception (MI) is a training-free method that steers LLMs by inserting text-derived key-value banks at selected layers, enabling selective latent KV allocation for improved control with reduced prompt clutter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Steering LLMs faces a trade-off: prompting offers strong control but burdens prompt caches; activation steering is compact but weaker and cannot manage large structured reminders. MI aims to combine persistence, structure, and efficiency.

Method: Insert text-derived KV banks at selected transformer layers in latent attention space, treating steering as selective KV allocation rather than propagating content through the visible prompt. It is training-free.

Result: MI achieves best drift-control trade-off on matched personality-steering tasks, competitive with prompting and better than CAA; enables mid-conversation updates without rewriting transcripts (highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3); outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS for structured reasoning (10/12 cells); reduces content-matched KV storage by up to 118x.

Conclusion: MI is a powerful steering technique for persistent, structured, or storage-expensive guidance, offering strong control with lower visible content, and enabling dynamic updates without prompt rewrites.

Abstract: Steering large language models (LLMs) is usually done by either instruction prompting or activation steering. Prompting often gives strong control, but caches guidance tokens at every layer and can clutter long interactions; activation steering is compact but typically weaker and does not support large structured reminders. We introduce memory inception (MI), a training-free method that steers in latent attention space by inserting text-derived key-value (KV) banks only at selected layers. Rather than materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache, MI treats steering as selective KV allocation, injecting latent slots only where the model routes to them. On matched personality-steering tasks, MI gives the best overall control--drift trade-off, remaining competitive with prompting while consistently outperforming CAA. On updateable guidance, MI supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without rewriting the visible transcript, achieving the highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3. On structured reasoning, MI outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject$\times$mode cells), serving as proxies for structured reasoning in verifiable domains, while cutting content-matched KV storage by up to 118$\times$. These results position MI as a powerful steering method when guidance is persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript.

</details>


### [252] [SoftSAE: Dynamic Top-K Selection for Adaptive Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06610)
*Jakub Stępień,Marcin Mazur,Jacek Tabor,Przemysław Spurek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SoftSAE introduces a differentiable Soft Top-K to adaptively choose the sparsity level per input, improving interpretability and representation quality over fixed-K SAEs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed sparsity in TopK SAEs is suboptimal for data lying on manifolds with varying local intrinsic dimensionality; some inputs need few features, others more, leading to noise or missed structure. An input-dependent sparsity can better match data complexity and yield more informative explanations in mechanistic interpretability.

Method: Incorporate a differentiable Soft Top-K operator into a sparse autoencoder to learn an input-dependent sparsity level k. This dynamic Top-K selects the number of active features per input and is trained end-to-end, enabling per-sample sparsity control. Source code available at the provided anonymous link.

Result: Experiments show that SoftSAE discovers meaningful, interpretable features and automatically selects an appropriate number of features for each concept, aligning sparsity with input complexity.

Conclusion: Dynamic, data-adaptive sparsity via Soft Top-K enhances interpretability and representation quality in sparse autoencoders, addressing limitations of fixed-K architectures.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have become an important tool in mechanistic interpretability, helping to analyze internal representations in both Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). By decomposing polysemantic activations into sparse sets of monosemantic features, SAEs aim to translate neural network computations into human-understandable concepts. However, common architectures such as TopK SAEs rely on a fixed sparsity level. They enforce the same number of active features (K) across all inputs, ignoring the varying complexity of real-world data. Natural data often lies on manifolds with varying local intrinsic dimensionality, meaning the number of relevant factors can change significantly across samples. This suggests that a fixed sparsity level is not optimal. Simple inputs may require only a few features, while more complex ones need more expressive representations. Using a constant K can therefore introduce noise in simple cases or miss important structure in more complex ones. To address this issue, we propose SoftSAE, a sparse autoencoder with a Dynamic Top-K selection mechanism. Our method uses a differentiable Soft Top-K operator to learn an input-dependent sparsity level k. This allows the model to adjust the number of active features based on the complexity of each input. As a result, the representation better matches the structure of the data, and the explanation length reflects the amount of information in the input. Experimental results confirm that SoftSAE not only finds meaningful features, but also selects the right number of features for each concept. The source code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SoftSAE-8F71/.

</details>
