### [253] [Soft Deterministic Policy Gradient with Gaussian Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06228)
*Hyunjun Na,Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Soft DDPG: a Gaussian-smoothed Bellman framework that removes reliance on critic action-gradients, yielding a well-defined gradient for non-smooth Q-functions; shows competitive performance in dense rewards and gains in discretized reward settings compared to standard DDPG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPG relies on differentiability of the critic with respect to action; this breaks with sparse or discrete rewards, causing ill-defined gradients and unstable learning. A principled approach that remains well-defined for non-smooth Q-functions is needed.

Method: Introduce a smoothed Bellman equation via Gaussian smoothing to define a new action-value function; derive the Soft Deterministic Policy Gradient; instantiate as Soft DDPG; eliminate explicit dependence on critic action-gradients.

Result: Empirical evaluations on standard continuous control benchmarks and discretized-reward variants show Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and yields clear gains in discretized-reward environments where standard DDPG is sensitive to irregular critic landscapes.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled, stable alternative to DPG for non-smooth Q-functions and discretized rewards, with solid empirical performance and broad applicability.

Abstract: Deterministic policy gradient (DPG) is widely utilized for continuous control; however, it inherently relies on the differentiability of the critic with respect to the action during policy updates. This assumption is violated in practical control problems involving sparse or discrete rewards, leading to ill-defined policy gradients and unstable learning. To address these challenges, we propose a principled alternative based on a smoothed Bellman equation formulated via Gaussian smoothing. Specifically, we define a novel action-value function based on a smoothed Bellman equation and derive the soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG). Our formulation eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients and ensures that the gradient remains well-defined even for non-smooth Q-functions. We instantiate this framework into a deep reinforcement learning algorithm, which we call soft deep deterministic policy gradient (Soft DDPG). Empirical evaluations on standard continuous control benchmarks and their discretized-reward variants show that Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments, where standard DDPG is more sensitive to irregular critic landscapes.

</details>


### [254] [Band Together: Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination against Evasion-based Promotion Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06238)
*Guanmeng Xian,Ning Yang,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: They identify cross-modal gradient mismatch in multimodal recommender systems under evasion-based promotion attacks and propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC) to align gradients across visual and textual modalities by treating all items as potential targets; results show improved robustness with acceptable performance trade-offs; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To defend multimodal recommenders against evasion-based promotion attacks, addressing the gap that existing defenses focus on single-modal settings and poisoning threats; gradient mismatch arises from conflicting perturbations driven by different user groups, reducing attack strength and underestimating risks during robust training.

Method: Develop UAT-MC: untargeted adversarial training with a gradient alignment mechanism and multimodal coordination; treat all items as potential targets (unknown targeted items); synchronize perturbations across modalities to maximize adversarial strength for robust training.

Result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate improved robustness to promotion attacks and acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: UAT-MC effectively mitigates cross-modal gradient mismatch in evasion-based settings, enabling synchronized multimodal perturbations during training and yielding stronger defense without excessive loss in utility; supports practical deployment.

Abstract: Multimodal recommender systems exploit visual and textual signals to alleviate data sparsity, but this also makes them more vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks. Existing defenses are largely limited to single-modal settings and mainly focus on poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored. In this work, we first identify a cross-modal gradient mismatch under the multi-user promotion setting, where visual and textual perturbations are optimized in inconsistent directions due to the dominance of distinct user groups. This phenomenon dilutes the attack effectiveness and leads robust training to underestimate worst-case risks. To address this issue, we propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). UAT-MC tackles the challenge of unknown targeted items in evasion-based attacks (as opposed to poisoning-based attacks) by treating all items as potential targets, and introduces a gradient alignment mechanism to explicitly correct this mismatch. This design ensures synchronized perturbations across modalities, thereby maximizing adversarial strength for robust training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/gmXian/UAT-MC.

</details>


### [255] [When Graph Language Models Go Beyond Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06239)
*Masatsugu Yamada,Mahito Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibrated diagnostic protocol disentangles memorization from structural learning in graph language models, combining frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification. At scale these models exhibit structural regularities beyond memorization, primarily in high-frequency patterns; at small scale memorization dominates. With 3.75M graphs, memorization drops while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Novel-only subgraph mining tracks all-generation correlation, suggesting true alignment not solely due to verbatim recall. High-frequency patterns are well reproduced; rare patterns remain under-covered, with marginal improvement as capacity grows. Results robust to two graph serializations (canonical DFS code and action sequences).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether graph language models learn structural regularities or merely memorize training graphs; current aggregate fidelity metrics conflate the two and are insufficient to resolve this.

Method: Calibrated diagnostic protocol combining (i) frequent subgraph mining, (ii) a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and (iii) three-level frequency stratification to separate memorization from structural alignment. Analyses include fixed-subsample comparison, Spearman correlation (subgraph-rank) between generated and reference graphs, and cross-serialization validation using canonical DFS code and action sequences.

Result: Across five TU benchmarks, high subgraph-rank correlation is observed, but alignment is often matched or exceeded by the memorization bootstrap at most scales. At small scale fidelity resembles verbatim recall; at 3.75M graphs memorization declines while rank correlation stays near ceiling. Frequent subgraph mining on the novel-only subset tracks the all-generation correlation, indicating genuine structural alignment beyond memorization. High-frequency patterns are well reproduced; rare patterns remain poorly covered, with only marginal gains from increased capacity. Findings are robust to both serializations.

Conclusion: Graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, particularly for high-frequency patterns; however, a substantial portion of alignment at smaller scales is explainable by memorization. The proposed diagnostic framework robustly disentangles memorization from structural learning across scales and representations.

Abstract: It remains unclear whether graph language models learn structural regularities or merely memorize training graphs; this cannot be resolved by current aggregate fidelity metrics alone. We develop a calibrated diagnostic protocol that combines frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment. Using this framework, we show that graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, primarily in the high-frequency regime. This is supported by the following empirical evidence: On five TU benchmarks, LLaMA-style graph language models reach high subgraph-rank correlation, yet their alignment is matched or exceeded by the memorization bootstrap in most cases. At small scale, under our bootstrap diagnostic, fidelity is largely indistinguishable from verbatim recall. In contrast, at large scale with 3.75M graphs, verbatim memorization drops sharply while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Crucially, in a separate fixed-subsample analysis, frequent subgraph mining restricted to the novel-only subset closely tracks the corresponding all-generation Spearman correlation, providing evidence that the alignment is not driven solely by verbatim recall. Across all scales, high-frequency patterns are well reproduced, while rare patterns remain poorly covered, and this deficit narrows only marginally as capacity increases. We observe the same scale-dependent crossover under two distinct graph serializations (canonical DFS code and action sequences), providing evidence of robustness in our analysis.

</details>


### [256] [Cumulative-Goodness Free-Riding in Forward-Forward Networks: Real, Repairable, but Not Accuracy-Dominant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06240)
*Amirhossein Yousefiramandi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Forward-Forward training suffers from layer free-riding under cumulative-goodness criteria, where later layers inherit partially separated representations and the class-discrimination gradient decays with prior margins. Local remedies (per-block, hardness-gated, depth-scaled) restore layer separation without backprop. They yield large improvements in layer-health metrics (4x–45x in deeper layers) with minimal accuracy impact on CIFAR-10/100; Tiny ImageNet confirms the qualitative gap between diagnostics and accuracy. Architecture/augmentation choices dominate final accuracy over the studied training-rule tweaks. Overall, cumulative free-riding is real but not the primary accuracy bottleneck for the studied settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To diagnose and mitigate layer-wise optimization pathologies (layer free-riding) in Forward-Forward (FF) training, and to assess whether local remedies can restore layer health without backpropagated gradients, across multiple datasets and training configurations.

Method: Formalize the decay of the class-discrimination signal in a softplus FF setting as a function of preceding margins. Propose and evaluate three local remedies (per-block, hardness-gated, depth-scaled) that aim to preserve current-layer separation without relying on backpropagation. Empirically test on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet, using layer-separation diagnostics and final accuracy; perform calibration experiments to assess the influence of architecture and augmentation.

Result: Local remedies markedly improve layer-separation metrics, especially in deeper layers (4x–45x gains). Non-degenerate training procedures see accuracy change by <1 percentage point. Tiny ImageNet shows the same qualitative gap between layer-health metrics and final accuracy. Calibration indicates architecture/augmentation impact on final accuracy surpasses the effects of the proposed training-rule modifications.

Conclusion: Cumulative free-riding is a real, repairable optimization pathology in FF training. However, within the studied architectures, datasets, and FF rules, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy; architecture and augmentation play a larger role in final performance.

Abstract: Forward-Forward (FF) training allows each layer to learn from a local goodness criterion. In cumulative-goodness variants, however, later layers can inherit a task that earlier layers have already partially separated. We formalize this phenomenon as layer free-riding: under the softplus FF criterion, the class-discrimination gradient reaching block $d$ decays exponentially with the positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks. We then study three local remedies -- per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled -- that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics, with $4\times$--$45\times$ gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point for non-degenerate training procedures. Tiny ImageNet provides a tougher cross-dataset check for our selected block-wise configuration and reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments further show that architecture and augmentation choices have a larger effect on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications studied here. Cumulative free-riding is therefore a real and repairable optimization pathology. Nonetheless, for the FF training rules, architectures, and datasets we study, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

</details>


### [257] [A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18555)
*Ran Ben-Basat,Yaniv Ben-Itzhak,Gal Mendelson,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TurboQuant is a constrained instantiation of EDEN; TurboQuant_mse is EDEN with S=1, which is suboptimal; TurboQuant_prod combines biased EDEN (b-1 bits) with a 1-bit residual quantization and is also suboptimal for three reasons; EDEN’s biased and unbiased variants generally outperform TurboQuant in both theory and experiments; EDEN outperforms TurboQuant across all tested setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how TurboQuant relates to DRIVE/EDEN, quantify the performance gaps, and derive design insights (choice of S, bias/unbiased quantization, and residual quantization) that explain the observed accuracy differences.

Method: Analytical mapping of TurboQuant components to EDEN; show that TurboQuant_mse corresponds to EDEN with S=1; dissect TurboQuant_prod into a biased (b−1)-bit EDEN step plus a 1-bit unbiased residual step; discuss the role of S optimization, the superiority of unbiased EDEN, and the use of random rotations/Lloyd-Max/shifted Beta; support claims with experiments that re-run TurboQuant benchmarks.

Result: 1) S=1 is suboptimal for biased EDEN, though optimal S tends to 1 as dimension grows; 2) unbiased EDEN outperforms TurboQuant_prod; 3) biased EDEN with optimized S outperforms TurboQuant_mse; 4) EDEN consistently outperforms TurboQuant across all tested setups.

Conclusion: EDEN subsumes TurboQuant; use EDEN with optimized S (for biased) or use unbiased EDEN for best accuracy; TurboQuant_mse and TurboQuant_prod are suboptimal due to their fixed S and residual quantization strategy, respectively.

Abstract: This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

</details>


### [258] [The Role of Node Features in Graph Pooling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06250)
*Jan von Pichowski,Alžbeta Hrabošová,Ingo Scholtes,Christopher Blöcker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pooling can outperform WL-1 GNNs when node features are well-aligned with graph topology; otherwise gains are marginal. The work formalizes requirements for feature quality and introduces a metric to quantify it, with empirical evidence across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why graph pooling often yields only marginal gains relative to strong WL-1 expressive GNNs, by studying the interaction between node features and topology and its impact on pooling objectives.

Method: Theoretical analysis of pooling operators, derivation of fundamental feature-quality requirements, proposal of a quantitative feature-quality measure, and empirical evaluation across datasets to validate the conditions under which pooling helps.

Result: Pooling provides improvements when the node features satisfy the proposed alignment requirements; when features fail to meet them, pooling gains are limited or inconsistent.

Conclusion: Effective pooling hinges on feature-topology alignment; the authors formalize essential feature requirements and introduce a metric to assess feature quality, guiding when pooling is likely to help and informing dataset/feature design.

Abstract: Graph pooling is commonly applied in graph classification, yet its empirical gains over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs are often marginal or inconsistent. We study this gap by analysing the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. Our analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology -- a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. We formalise fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. Our empirical evaluation shows that, when these requirements are satisfied, pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets.

</details>


### [259] [The Weight Gram Matrix Captures Sequential Feature Linearization in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06258)
*Taehun Cha,Daniel Beaglehole,Adityanarayanan Radhakrishnan,Donghun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A feature-centric framework for understanding neural network training, linking weight updates to evolving features via the Feature Learning Equation and a Weight Gram matrix, introducing Virtual Covariance and Target Linearity to quantify feature-target alignment; shows deep networks progressively morph representations toward target-linear structure, offering a unifying view of Neural Collapse and linear interpolation phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how deep networks learn representations by connecting gradient-based weight updates to an implicit evolution of features, and to unify observed empirical phenomena under a common linearization perspective.

Method: Derive the Feature Learning Equation that identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object for feature dynamics; interpret gradient descent as a hypothetical feature evolution with covariance (Virtual Covariance); define Target Linearity as a measure of alignment between features and targets; perform layer-wise and training dynamics analyses to show sequential transformation toward target-linear structure.

Result: A cohesive theoretical framework that explains how representations evolve during training, showing networks move representations toward target-linear structures; provides a unified explanation for Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models through the lens of feature dynamics and weight-driven updates.

Conclusion: A linearization view of training dynamics offers a unified interpretation of representation learning in deep networks and accounts for several empirical phenomena, suggesting that controlling feature alignment could guide training and generalization.

Abstract: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. In this work, we propose a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution. We introduce a simple identity, the Feature Learning Equation, which identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics. This enables us to interpret gradient descent as implicitly inducing a hypothetical evolution of features, whose covariance structure - termed the Virtual Covariance - characterizes how representations evolve during training. Building on this perspective, we introduce Target Linearity, a measure quantifying the linear alignment between features and targets. By analyzing the training and layer-wise dynamics, we show that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of several empirical phenomena, including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

</details>


### [260] [Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06259)
*Marten van Dijk,Murat Bilgehan Ertan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tight, transparent f-DP analysis for DP-SGD with random-shuffling subsampling; provides closed-form bounds in a regime σ ≥ sqrt(3/ln M) using Berry-Esseen; shows concrete single-epoch and multi-epoch parameter regimes; contrasts with Poisson subsampling; introduces LLN-based asymptotic diagonal-limit; open questions on rates beyond Berry-Esseen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offer transparent, interpretable DP-utility trade-offs for DP-SGD with random-shuffling subsampling within the f-DP framework, addressing non-closed forms in Poisson subsampling and enabling practical parameter tuning.

Method: Model DP-SGD with random-shuffling subsampling in f-DP, derive Berry-Esseen-based bounds yielding closed-form trade-off functions; provide concrete single-epoch settings; compose across epochs to obtain delta-c scaling; introduce a generalization of the law of large numbers to obtain asymptotic diagonal-limit results; contrast with Poisson subsampling asymptotics.

Result: Obtains tight, closed-form bounds for the trade-off function under random shuffling; concrete example: δ≈0.01, σ=1 yields M≈1.14×10^6 rounds and N≈1.14×10^7 samples per epoch; composition across E epochs yields δ growing linearly in E (implying E=O(√M) for control); introduces an asymptotic diagonal-limit where f^{⊗E}(a) → 1−a with δ≈O(√E) when E=c_M^2 M and c_M→0; shows contrast with Poisson-subsampling asymptotics and flags open questions on convergence rates beyond Berry-Esseen.

Conclusion: Random-shuffling subsampling within the f-DP framework yields transparent, near-tight DP-SGD trade-off bounds with interpretable closed forms; Berry-Esseen-based results are tight up to constants in the stated regime; multi-epoch composition is feasible but imposes E=O(√M); an LLN-based asymptotic approach provides a diagonal-limit illustrating asymptotic behavior as E grows; the Poisson-subsampling analogue remains less transparent, with open questions on convergence rates beyond Berry-Esseen.

Abstract: We derive a tight analysis of the trade-off function for Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) with subsampling based on random shuffling within the $f$-DP framework. Our analysis covers the regime $σ\geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$, where $σ$ is the noise multiplier and $M$ is the number of rounds within a single epoch. Unlike $f$-DP analyses for Poisson subsampling, which yield non-closed implicit formulas that can be machine computed but are non-transparent, random shuffling admits a tight analysis yielding transparent and interpretable closed-form bounds. Our concrete bounds, derived via the Berry-Esseen theorem, are tight up to constant factors within the proof framework. We demonstrate worked parameter settings for a single epoch ($E=1$) with a corresponding trade-off function $\geq 1-a-δ$, that is, only $δ$ below the ideal random guessing diagonal $1-a$: For $δ= 1/100$ and $σ= 1$, roughly $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ training samples suffice to achieve meaningful differential privacy. This is in contrast to recent negative results for the regime $σ\leq 1/\sqrt{2 \ln M}$. Our concrete bounds can be composed over multiple epochs leading to $δ$ having a linear in $E$ dependency, which restricts $E=O(\sqrt{M})$. To go beyond Berry--Esseen, we introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers that yields an asymptotic random guessing diagonal-limit result: if $E=c_M^2M$ with $c_M\to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function satisfies $f^{\otimes E}(a)\to 1-a$ uniformly in $a\in[0,1]$ with $δ$ having only an $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency. We compare this asymptotic regime with the corresponding Poisson subsampling asymptotic, and highlight the characterization of explicit convergence rates as an open question.

</details>


### [261] [Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06260)
*Wentao Yu,Bo Han,Jie Yang,Chen Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedGMC introduces dual manifold calibration for graph federated learning to address semantic and structural heterogeneity. The server builds a semantic manifold using equidistant anchors and a structural manifold using global templates, guiding local calibration, with dynamic refinement via aggregation of local manifolds. It achieves superior performance on 11 homophilic/heterophilic graphs by balancing global commonality and local personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneity across distributed subgraphs in Graph Federated Learning leads to performance degradation when using rigid, globally linear alignment. There is a need to preserve diverse local distributions while maintaining some global coherence.

Method: FedGMC constructs two complementary manifolds at the server: a semantic manifold calibrated by equidistant semantic anchors and a structural manifold calibrated by global structural templates. Local clients calibrate their semantic and structural manifolds accordingly. The server aggregates local manifolds to refine the global manifolds, enabling dynamic, personalized representations.

Result: Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs show FedGMC balances global commonality and local personalization and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: FedGMC effectively tackles semantic and structural heterogeneity in Graph Federated Learning by employing dual manifold calibration, yielding improved personalization without sacrificing global coherence.

Abstract: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables collaborative representation learning across distributed subgraphs while preserving privacy. However, heterogeneity remains a critical challenge, as subgraphs across clients typically differ significantly in both semantics and structures. Existing methods address heterogeneity by enforcing the rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes between clients and the server. However, these alignments implicitly rely on a restrictive global linearity assumption that summarizes local data distributions using a single and globally consistent representation space. This severely compresses the personalized representation space of clients and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC), a novel paradigm that tackles semantic heterogeneity and structural heterogeneity from a unified manifold perspective. Instead of enforcing rigid alignment, FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism that preserves global commonalities while maximizing the personalized representation space of local clients. Specifically, for semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors, so as to guide the calibration of local semantic manifolds. For structural heterogeneity, the server constructs a global structural manifold by building global structural templates, so as to guide the calibration of local structural manifolds. Finally, the server dynamically refines both global semantic manifolds and structural manifolds by aggregating local manifolds. Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, thereby significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [262] [Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06261)
*Eugenio Lomurno,Filippo Balzarini,Francesco Benelle,Francesca Pia Panaccione,Matteo Matteucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TARDIS refines a frozen tabular diffusion backbone at inference time using Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) guided by a dataset-specific search, achieving substantial gains over real-data-trained baselines across 15 datasets with practical runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although diffusion-based tabular generators approach real-data utility, they rarely exceed it, and gains have been driven mainly by training-time innovations. Inference-time refinement of pre-trained backbones remains underexplored for tabular data.

Method: Freeze a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone; per-dataset configuration via Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion. Each trial uses an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and optional soft-label distillation. The search space encodes Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): minimize the symmetric Chamfer distance between synthetic and real samples both continuously (score-level gradients) and discretely (batch-ranking post-generation). Evaluate across datasets to identify BCR-aligned configurations.

Result: On 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks, TARDIS yields median +8.6% improvement in downstream tasks over models trained on real data (CI +3.3 to +16.4, p=0.016, 11/15 wins) and +12.9% over the TabDiff backbone (p<1e-4). It matches the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement completes within 1–80 minutes on a consumer-grade GPU.

Conclusion: Inference-time refinement of a frozen tabular diffusion backbone can reach and surpass real-data utility; Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement emerges as the dominant refinement pattern, enabling dataset-adaptive improvements across diverse tabular tasks with practical runtimes.

Abstract: Diffusion-based generators set the current state of the art for synthetic tabular data. These methods approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and closing this synthetic-real gap has so far been pursued exclusively at training time, via architectural advances, scaling, and retraining of monolithic generators. The inference-time alternative, i.e., refining the outputs of a pre-trained backbone with parameters left untouched, has remained largely unexplored for tabular synthesis. We introduce TARDIS (Tabular generation through Refinement, Distillation, and Inference-time Sampling), an inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained backbone, configured per dataset by a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, with each trial's objective set by an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and an optional soft-label distillation step. The search space encodes a single mathematical pattern we name Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples is minimized both continuously, via a score-level gradient, and discretely, via batch-ranking post-generation. The per-dataset search recovers BCR-aligned configurations on most datasets, evidence for BCR as the dominant refinement pattern. Across 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over the TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), matching the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone reaches and exceeds real-data utility in 1 to 80 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU.

</details>


### [263] [Can Attribution Predict Risk? From Multi-View Attribution to Planning Risk Signals in End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06264)
*Le Yang,Ruoyu Chen,Haijun Liu,Jiawei Liang,ShangQuan Sun,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a hierarchical, region-based attribution framework for end-to-end planning in six-view autonomous driving, deriving risk signals from attribution statistics to predict planning risk and detect collisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end driving models are opaque; existing explanations are decoupled from planning or rely on auxiliary models and do not reveal visual evidence influencing trajectory generation or support risk identification.

Method: A coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy across six camera views guided by an L2-consistency objective with the original trajectory, plus three predictive attribution statistics: attribution entropy (view-space concentration), within-camera spatial variance, and cross-camera Gini coefficient.

Result: Attribution-derived signals correlate with planning risk: Spearman 0.30±0.07 with trajectory error and AUROC 0.77±0.04 for collision detection; signal generalizes to held-out scenes and is stable against alternative baselines; validated on BridgeAD, UniAD, GenAD.

Conclusion: Hierarchical attribution can reveal both critical views and regions driving trajectory generation and provide actionable risk indicators for end-to-end planning, with good generalization across scenes.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models generate future trajectories from multi-view inputs, improving system integration but introducing opaque decisions and hard-to-localize risks. Existing methods either rely on auxiliary monitoring models or generate textual explanations, but are decoupled from the planning process and fail to reveal the visual evidence underlying trajectory generation. While attribution offers a direct alternative, planning differs from image classification by taking six-view camera images as input and predicting continuous multi-step trajectories, requiring attribution to capture both critical views and regions and their influence on outputs. Moreover, whether attribution maps can support risk identification remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning. Specifically, using L2 consistency with the original trajectory as the objective, we design a coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them. We further extract three attribution statistics as predictive signals for planning risk, including attribution entropy to measure how concentrated the planner's reliance is over the joint visual space, within-camera spatial variance to characterize how spread out the attribution is within each view, and cross-camera Gini coefficient to quantify how unevenly attribution is distributed across the six cameras. Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD show that these statistics correlate with planning risk, achieving Spearman correlations of $0.30 \pm 0.07$ with trajectory error and AUROC of $0.77 \pm 0.04$ for collision detection. The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under an alternative attribution baseline.

</details>


### [264] [A Flow Matching Algorithm for Many-Shot Adaptation to Unseen Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06272)
*Tyler Ingebrand,Ruihan Zhao,Kushagra Gupta,David Fridovich-Keil,Sandeep P. Chinchali,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FP-FM learns a fixed basis of velocity fields from multiple training distributions and uses a least-squares projection to adapt to any new target distribution at inference time, enabling efficient conditional generation without retraining. It introduces variants (e.g., time-dependent coefficients) to trade expressivity and compute. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable direct conditioning of generative models on samples from a target distribution without retraining, addressing the gap between flexible conditioning and computational efficiency in flow-matching approaches.

Method: Train a basis of velocity-field functions spanning the training distributions. At inference, project a new target distribution onto this basis via least-squares to obtain coefficients, which define the conditioned generation. Variants enrich the coefficient calculation (e.g., time-dependent coefficients) to increase expressivity with additional compute.

Result: Significant improvements in precision and recall over baselines on both synthetic and image datasets, with particularly strong gains on unseen distributions, while maintaining inference efficiency by avoiding retraining.

Conclusion: FP-FM provides an efficient, flexible mechanism for adapting generative models to new target distributions without additional training, with variant-based trade-offs between expressivity and compute.

Abstract: While generative modeling has achieved remarkable success on tasks like natural language-conditioned image generation, enabling model adaptation from example data points remains a relatively underexplored and challenging problem. To this end, we propose Function Projection for Flow Matching (FP-FM), an algorithm that directly conditions generation on samples from the target distribution. FP-FM learns basis functions to span the velocity fields corresponding to a set of training distributions, and adapts to new distributions by computing a simple least-squares projection onto this basis. This enables efficient generation of samples from diverse target distributions without additional training at inference time. We further introduce multiple variants of FP-FM that provide a trade-off in expressivity and compute by enriching the coefficient calculation, e.g., by making the coefficients dependent on time. FP-FM achieves greatly improved precision and recall relative to baselines across synthetic and image-based datasets, with especially strong gains on unseen distributions.

</details>


### [265] [PACE: Prune-And-Compress Ensemble Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06278)
*Fabian Akkerman,Julien Ferry,Théo Guyard,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACE interleaves generation and pruning/compression of ensemble learners in two phases to boost diversity and efficiency, while controlling faithfulness to the original ensemble; it outperforms prior pruning/compression methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensembles reach state-of-the-art accuracy but rely on many weak learners, which hurts deployment, interpretability, and downstream tasks like robustness verification. Pruning and compression address these issues, but need principled guidance and faithfulness guarantees.

Method: A two-phase framework: (1) generate new diverse learners via a theoretically grounded procedure to enrich the ensemble; (2) prune the augmented ensemble when no further relevant learners can be found. Faithfulness to the original ensemble is controlled throughout both phases.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows PACE outperforms prior pruning and compression methods and provides principled faithfulness guarantees during both generation and pruning phases.

Conclusion: PACE offers an effective strategy to balance diversity, size, and faithfulness in ensemble models, improving deployability and robustness verification while maintaining high predictive performance.

Abstract: Ensemble models achieve state-of-the-art performance on prediction tasks, but usually require aggregating a large number of weak learners. This can hinder deployment, interpretability, and downstream tasks such as robustness verification. Remedies to this issue fall into two main camps: pruning, which discards redundant learners, and compression, which generates new ones from scratch. We introduce PACE, a framework that interleaves these paradigms in a two-phase strategy. First, new learners are actively generated via a theoretically grounded procedure to enhance the diversity of the initial ensemble. When no more relevant learners can be found, a second phase of pruning is performed on this enriched ensemble. During both operations, PACE allows fine control on the faithfulness to the original ensemble. Experiments show that our method outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled control of faithfulness guarantees.

</details>


### [266] [INEUS: Iterative Neural Solver for High-Dimensional PIDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06281)
*Jean-Loup Dupret,Davide Gallon,Patrick Cheridito*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A meshfree iterative neural solver (INEUS) for PIDEs using single-jump sampling and recursive regression to efficiently handle nonlocal terms, enabling global space-time solutions with contraction-based convergence for linear PIDEs and scalable performance on high-dimensional problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational challenge of nonlocal jump terms in PIDEs, and the limitations of differentiating full PIDE residuals in high dimensions; provide a scalable, convergent solver.

Method: Introduce INEUS, a meshfree iterative neural solver that replaces explicit nonlocal integral evaluation with single-jump sampling, reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems, and learns a global space-time solution. A contraction-based convergence proof is provided for linear PIDEs.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear PIDEs, indicating efficiency and robustness of the approach, particularly in handling nonlocal terms.

Conclusion: INEUS offers an effective, scalable framework for solving high-dimensional PIDEs, with convergence guarantees in the linear setting and strong empirical performance for nonlinear cases, outperforming traditional approaches that rely on full PIDE residual differentiation.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce INEUS, a meshfree iterative neural solver for partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs). The method replaces the explicit evaluation of nonlocal jump integrals with single-jump sampling and reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems. Like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), INEUS learns global solutions over the entire space-time domain, yet it offers a more efficient treatment of nonlocal terms and avoids the computationally expensive differentiation of full PIDE residuals. These features make INEUS particularly well suited for high-dimensional PDEs and PIDEs. Supported by a contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs, our numerical experiments show that INEUS delivers accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear examples.

</details>


### [267] [Attributions All the Way Down? The Metagame of Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06295)
*Hubert Baniecki,Przemyslaw Biecek,Fabian Fumagalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the metagame framework to quantify second-order interaction effects of model explanations using meta-attributions with Shapley-value analysis; provides hierarchical decomposition and validates across NLP, vision-language, and multimodal diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current attribution methods only capture first-order effects; there is a need to quantify how explanatory attributions themselves interact, i.e., how the attribution of feature i is influenced by feature j. The study formalizes this as a cooperative-game problem and aims to yield directional meta-attributions with theoretical guarantees.

Method: Define meta-attribution φ_{j→i}(f) as the Shapley value of the attribution method treated as a cooperative game over input features; show hierarchical decomposition of attributions into meta-attributions and position them as directional extensions of existing interaction indices; provide proofs and general framework.

Result: Empirically demonstrate the metagame across three domains: (i) token interactions in instruction-tuned LMs, (ii) cross-modal similarity explanations in vision-language encoders, (iii) interpretation of text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

Conclusion: The metagame delivers a principled, hierarchical framework for analyzing second-order effects in model explanations, enabling richer interpretability and cross-domain insights through meta-attributions.

Abstract: We introduce the metagame, a conceptual framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects of model explanations. For any first-order attribution $φ(f)$ explaining a model $f$, we measure the directional influence of feature $j$ on the attribution of feature $i$, denoted as meta-attribution $\varphi_{j \to i}(f)$, by treating the attribution method itself as a cooperative game and computing its Shapley value. Theoretically, we prove that attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions, and establish these as directional extensions of existing interaction indices. Empirically, we demonstrate that the metagame delivers insights across diverse interpretability applications: (i) quantifying token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (ii) explaining cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, and (iii) interpreting text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

</details>


### [268] [Region Seeding via Pre-Activation Regularization: A Geometric View from Piecewise Affine Nerual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06300)
*Yi Wei,Xuan Qi,Furao Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A region-seeding regularizer for ReLU-like networks that nudges neuron switching surfaces toward data points to increase local affine-region counts, enabling earlier data-driven partitioning and improved training dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between architectural capacity (potential affine partitions) and the partitions realized by optimization; provide a training-time mechanism to seed data-relevant partitions and improve generalization.

Method: The authors derive a sufficient condition for increasing the local affine-region count by moving neuron switching surfaces closer to data points so they intersect local neighborhoods; they then implement a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that promotes early partitioning and allows task-driven refinement later; evaluation uses exact enumeration of regions and tests on toy datasets and ImageNet-1k with classical models.

Result: The regularizer increases the number of realized affine regions (as counted by exact enumeration) and improves early-stage accuracy; final accuracy on ImageNet-1k is comparable or slightly improved for classical models.

Conclusion: Region-seeding regularizers provide a practical, theory-grounded approach to induce data-relevant partitions early in training without hindering later refinement, yielding tangible gains in both training dynamics and final performance.

Abstract: Deep networks with continuous piecewise affine activations induce polyhedral partitions of the input space, making the number of realized affine regions a natural measure of expressive capacity and a key determinant of how well the model can approximate nonlinear target functions. In practice, standard training realizes far fewer region refinements in data-visited neighborhoods than the architecture could in principle support, while existing region-count theory is primarily architectural and offers little guidance on how optimization shapes the realized partition near the data. Our theory provides a sufficient condition under which bringing neuron switching surfaces sufficiently close to data points ensures their intersection with local neighborhoods, which in turn implies a strict increase in the local affine-region count, yielding a principled training-time handle for seeding data-relevant partitions early in optimization. Guided by these results, we propose a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning while allowing task-driven refinement to dominate later in training. Experiments show that the regularizer increases the number of realized affine regions via exact enumeration and improves overall performance on toy datasets, while also improving early-stage accuracy and achieving comparable (or slightly improved) final accuracy on ImageNet-1k for classical models.

</details>


### [269] [Molecules Meet Language: Confound-Aware Representation Learning and Chemical Property Steering in Transformer-VAE Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06303)
*Zakaria Elabid,Jan Andrzejewski,Bartosz Brzoza,Attila Cangi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chemically meaningful latent-space steering can emerge in an autoregressive Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES, but is largely confounded by representation-level artifacts and requires decoded-molecule validation; some properties show robust global directions while others are localized.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether latent-space directions in molecular generative models reflect true chemical structure or merely sequence-level shortcuts encoded in SELFIES and related representations.

Method: Train an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE on SELFIES; freeze the model post-training; fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors; treat probe weights as candidate global steering directions; perform residualization, confound-direction alignment, and decoded-molecule traversal to control for representation-level confounds (SELFIES length, tokens, entropy).

Result: Under confound-aware evaluation, observed monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA; nonlinear probes reveal some properties have stable global directions while others are better described by local latent gradients.

Conclusion: Chemically meaningful steering can arise in entangled molecular latent spaces, but robust validation via decoded molecules and explicit confound control is essential; such steering is not uniformly present and depends on the property and evaluation approach.

Abstract: Molecular generative models often assume meaningful latent geometry, but apparent property predictability can reflect sequence-level shortcuts rather than chemical organization. We study this issue in an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES. After training, we freeze the model, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, and use the probe weights as candidate global steering directions. To separate chemical signal from SELFIES artifacts, we introduce a confound-aware evaluation based on residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal. This is necessary because SELFIES length, branch tokens, ring tokens, and token entropy are strongly encoded in the latent space. Under this confound-aware evaluation, we find robust monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes further show that some properties admit stable global directions, while others are better described by local latent gradients. Overall, our results show that chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds.

</details>


### [270] [Perceive, Route and Modulate: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06310)
*Siru Zhong,Zhao Meng,Haohuan Fu,Haoyang Li,Qingsong Wen,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DPR is a backbone-agnostic dynamic recalibration module for time-series forecasting. It uses a Perceive-Route-Modulate pipeline to compute a soft routing over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns and applies a time-aware modulation via a residual Hadamard product, improving forecasting with minimal overhead and functioning as both an adapter and a standalone model (DPRNet).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global, fixed-weight transformations in deep forecasting poorly capture shifting local temporal patterns in real-world time series, leading to suboptimal static responses and degraded performance as dynamics change.

Method: A lightweight Perceive-Route-Modulate pipeline: (1) perceive local patterns to form a soft routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns; (2) route inputs through this distribution; (3) modulate hidden states with a time-aware modulation vector via a residual Hadamard product. DPR is backbone-agnostic, functioning as an adapter with minimal overhead, and can also operate as a standalone model (DPRNet).

Result: DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with low overhead, validating dynamic recalibration as a general solution to the static-pattern bottleneck. DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, supporting the approach's effectiveness against purely scale-based improvements.

Conclusion: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration addresses a fundamental bottleneck in time-series forecasting by enabling token-level, time-aware recalibration. Its backbone-agnostic adapter design and standalone DPRNet demonstrate broad applicability and potential for improved generalization when local dynamics shift.

Abstract: Local temporal patterns in real-world time series continuously shift, rendering globally shared transformations suboptimal. Current deep forecasting models, despite their scale and complexity, rely on fixed weight matrices applied uniformly to all temporal tokens. This creates a static pattern response: models settle into a compromised average, unable to adapt to changing local dynamics. We introduce Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR), a backbone-agnostic mechanism that resolves this via token-level recalibration. Through a lightweight "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline, DPR computes a soft-routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, generating a time-aware modulation vector that recalibrates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product. As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead, confirming it addresses a general bottleneck. As a minimalist standalone model, DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, validating dynamic recalibration against macroscopic parameter scaling.

</details>


### [271] [When Does $\ell_2$-Boosting Overfit Benignly? High-Dimensional Risk Asymptotics and the $\ell_1$ Implicit Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06314)
*Ye Su,Jian Li,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Benign overfitting under l1 (greedy ensembles) analysis shows slower decay than in ℓ2; using CGMT and truncated Gaussian moments, the non-smooth ℓ1 interpolant is resolved. Pure-noise yields excess variance Θ(σ^2/log(p/n)); noise localizes to sparse active sets. In spiked designs with r2 tail dims, risk vanishes only at Θ(σ^2/log(r2/n)). A tuning-free early stopping rule from non-smooth subdifferential dynamics recovers Lasso basic inequality and achieves minimax-optimal empirical rate for ℓ1-bounded signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how greedy ℓ1 implicit bias shapes benign overfitting in high dimensions, beyond ℓ2 geometry; isolate algorithmic bias via continuous-time ℓ2-boosting and non-smooth optimization; quantify risk scaling and derive practically usable stopping rules.

Method: Analyze continuous-time ℓ2-Boosting over p features and n samples; combine Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with asymptotics of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments to handle non-smooth ℓ1 interpolation; extend to spiked-isotropic designs; study non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of boosting flow to derive stopping rules.

Result: In isotropic pure-noise, benign overfitting fails at linear rate; greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets; excess variance decays as Θ(σ^2/log(p/n)). For r2 = p−k* tail dims, risk→0 only if r2>>n but at Θ(σ^2/log(r2/n)), slower than ℓ2. The non-smooth analysis yields a tuning-free early stopping rule that, under a bounded ℓ1-path, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and attains minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for ℓ1-bounded signals.

Conclusion: ℓ1-geometry induces slower risk decay than ℓ2; localizing noise explains the lack of linear-rate benign overfitting; non-smooth boosting dynamics enable a practical, tuning-free early stopping that achieves minimax-like rates for ℓ1-bounded regimes; exact signal–noise decomposition remains open; results rely on isotropic/pure-noise and spiked designs.

Abstract: Benign overfitting is well-characterized in $\ell_2$ geometries, but its behavior under the $\ell_1$ implicit bias of greedy ensembles remains challenging. The analytical barrier stems from the non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds, which invalidates standard spectral resolvent tools. To isolate this algorithmic bias, we characterize the high-dimensional risk of continuous-time $\ell_2$-Boosting over $p$ features and $n$ samples. By coupling the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with delicate asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments, we analytically resolve the non-smooth $\ell_1$ interpolant. Under an isotropic pure-noise model, we prove that benign overfitting fails at the linear rate: greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets, and the excess variance decays at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(p/n))$ for noise variance $σ^2$. We remark that while this localization mechanism should persist in the presence of signals, the exact signal-noise decomposition remains an open problem. For spiked-isotropic designs with $k^*$ head eigenvalues and $r_2 = p - k^*$ tail dimensions, the risk converges to zero when $r_{2} \gg n$, but only at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(r_2/n))$, which is slower than the linear decay observed in $\ell_2$ geometries. To avoid this slow convergence, we analyze the non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of the boosting flow. This yields a tuning-free early stopping rule that, under a bounded $\ell_1$-path condition, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and attains the minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for $\ell_1$-bounded signals.

</details>


### [272] [Pro-KLShampoo: Projected KL-Shampoo with Whitening Recovered by Orthogonalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06316)
*Ruotong Sun,Ermin Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pro-KLShampoo exploits spike-and-flat eigenvalue structure in KL-Shampoo preconditioners by constraining a Kronecker factor to a spike-and-flat parametric form and applying orthogonalization on remaining directions, achieving consistent performance gains over KL-Shampoo across multiple model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve efficiency of gradient-based preconditioning by leveraging the spectral structure of gradients, bridging explicit Kronecker preconditioning with momentum orthogonalization.

Method: Identify spike-and-flat eigenvalue spectra under a rank-ρ signal-plus-noise model; constrain one KL-Shampoo Kronecker factor to a parametric spike-and-flat form (full spectrum on an r-dim subspace, a single eigenvalue on the remaining n−r directions); apply orthogonalization on those remaining directions; show that this orthogonalization recovers the full KL-Shampoo preconditioner; validate on GPT-2 (124M/350M) and LLaMA (134M/450M).

Result: Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every tested subspace rank in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level across all four model scales.

Conclusion: A structural, subspace-constrained KL-Shampoo coupled with orthogonalization yields practical gains and recovers the full preconditioner algebra, suggesting a productive synthesis of the two prior optimization frontiers.

Abstract: Optimizers that exploit the matrix structure of gradients are central to modern LLM pre-training, with two distinct frontiers: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning -- most recently KL-Shampoo, which estimates the preconditioner via KL divergence minimization -- and orthogonalization of the gradient momentum, exemplified by Muon and analyzed as steepest descent under the spectral norm. The two routes are typically developed in isolation. We make a structural observation about KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners: their eigenvalue spectra exhibit a \emph{spike-and-flat} shape -- a few dominant eigenvalues followed by an approximately uniform tail -- across layers and training stages, holding exactly under a rank-$ρ$ signal-plus-noise gradient model. We exploit this structure by restricting one of KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors to a parametric family aligned with the spike-and-flat shape: full spectral structure on a tracked $r$-dimensional subspace, single shared eigenvalue across the remaining $n-r$ directions. On these directions, we apply orthogonalization. An identity shows that this orthogonalization recovers the algebraic form of full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner. On four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M / 350M, LLaMA 134M / 450M), Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank we test in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

</details>


### [273] [LINC: Decoupling Local Consequence Scoring from Hidden Matching in Constructive Neural Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06332)
*Shaofeng Qin,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LINC is a decoder-side architecture that explicitly computes one-step deterministic consequences (travel, waiting, slack, capacity changes) and uses them in a local linear scorer with feasible-set modulation to improve constrained routing, achieving substantial gap reductions on CVRPTW and extensions to CVRP and TSP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current constructive neural routing solvers rely on global decoder-context matching and often fail to model deterministic one-step consequences, overloading the hidden state and hindering optimal decisions in constrained routing tasks like CVRPTW.

Method: Introduce LINC: compute centered relative consequences via a shared linear local scorer; feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context; preserves standard global matching; applicable to CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP.

Result: For CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83%/38.15% to 7.26%/14.71%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

Conclusion: LINC provides decoder-side, explicit computation of local consequences, maintaining global matching while reducing hidden-state burden and improving constrained-routing performance with cross-domain generality.

Abstract: Constructive neural routing solvers usually score the next action by matching a decoder context to candidate embeddings, hiding deterministic one-step consequences such as travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes. We propose LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison), a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that computes these consequences explicitly. LINC uses them according to their decision role: centered relative consequences are compared by a shared linear local scorer, while feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context. This preserves standard global matching and relieves the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic. The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW) serves as the main constrained-routing stress test; the same interface extends to the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) and Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). In particular, for CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83\%/38.15\% to 7.26\%/14.71\%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

</details>


### [274] [Eliciting associations between clinical variables from LLMs via comparison questions across populations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06335)
*Fabian Kabus,Kian Kordtomeikel,Thomas Brox,Heinz Wiendl,Daiana Stolz,Harald Binder*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Indirect elicitation of correlations from LLMs via structured triplet comparisons and ICP to infer robust, invariant potential causal links in clinical domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs trained on biomedical literature may encode correlations; direct elicitation of causal structure is difficult and risky; a method is needed to extract interpretable association and potential causal links without accessing internal activations.

Method: Use patient comparison triplet questions to elicit similarity judgments; model LLM representations to estimate correlations without probing internals; vary prompt environments to induce subpopulation-specific correlations; apply invariant causal prediction (ICP) to identify invariant parent links, yielding conservative causal statements.

Result: Across COPD and MS, elicited correlations are smooth, stable across environments, clinically interpretable, and vary across environments enough to support ICP, yielding a small set of candidate invariant parents.

Conclusion: Indirect elicitation through triplet comparisons can recover meaningful associations from LLMs and enable cautious, invariant-informed causal statements aligned with LLM answering patterns.

Abstract: The training data of large language models (LLMs) comprises a wide range of biomedical literature, reflecting data from many different patient populations. We investigate how it might be possible to recover information on correlation and causal links between patient characteristics, as a key building block for medical decision making. To avoid the pitfalls of direct elicitation, we propose an approach based on structured comparison questions, specifically patient comparison triplet questions. This is combined with a statistical model for the LLM representation that provides estimates of correlations without access to activations or model internals. Intuitively, we consider how similarity decisions of LLMs based on a first variable are affected by providing information on a second variable for one of the patients being assessed. We then induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain correlation estimates for different subpopulations, which enables an invariant causal prediction (ICP) approach to obtain conservative candidate parent links. We demonstrate the method in two clinical domains, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and multiple sclerosis (MS). Across prompted environments, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in a statistically significant way that supports downstream invariance testing, such that ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links. These results show that indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offer a cautious route from implicit correlations to causal statements that are congruent with LLM answering patterns.

</details>


### [275] [Is Escalation Worth It? A Decision-Theoretic Characterization of LLM Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06350)
*Dylan Bouchard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Develops a principled, decision-theoretic framework for model cascades using constrained optimization and duality; shows the cost–quality frontier is piecewise concave and can be characterized as the envelope of pairwise cascades; demonstrations reveal pairwise cascades often match or exceed full chain cascades, while lightweight routing can surpass cascade policies; main bottleneck is structural cost rather than insufficient model stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to provide principled guidance for cascade design by analyzing the geometry of the cost–quality frontier and the role of thresholds, rather than treating deferral thresholds as empirical hyperparameters.

Method: formulates a constrained optimization problem for cascaded LLMs and derives dual relationships (shadow prices); proves piecewise concavity on decreasing-benefit regions for two-model cascades; characterizes k-model frontiers as the envelope of binomial(k,2) pairwise cascades with switching points; derives first-order conditions for k-model cascades where a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost; conducts empirical validation on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) using eight models from five providers; compares deterministic threshold cascades, optimized subsequences, and a lightweight pre-generation router.

Result: Found that the cost–quality frontier is piecewise concave on certain regions; the optimal frontier for k models is the envelope of all pairwise cascades with switching points; a single shadow price equilibrates marginal quality-per-cost across stages; within deterministic threshold cascades, fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequences yield little held-out gain; a lightweight router surpasses the best cascade policy on 4 of 5 datasets by avoiding cheap-model costs on directly routed queries; overall, cascade performance is limited mainly by structural cost (paying the cheap model before escalation) rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

Conclusion: Cascades are fundamentally constrained by cost structure. The optimal policy tends to be achieved via pairwise cascades or routing strategies that bypass unnecessary cheap-model computation, rather than by adding more intermediate models or relying on fixed-threshold chains. This highlights the value of duality-based analysis to guide practical cascade design and suggests future work should focus on mitigating the early cheap-model cost or dynamic routing strategies.

Abstract: Model cascades, in which a cheap LLM defers to an expensive one on low-confidence queries, are widely used to navigate the cost-quality tradeoff at deployment. Existing approaches largely treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter, with limited guidance on the geometry of the resulting cost-quality frontier over a model pool. We develop a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For a two-model cascade, we establish piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support, with reciprocal shadow prices linking the budget- and quality-constrained formulations. Given a pool of $k$ models, we characterize the frontier achievable by deterministic two-model threshold cascades as the pointwise envelope over $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise cascades, with switching points where the optimal pair changes. For $k$-model cascades, we derive first-order conditions in which a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. We validate the framework on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) across eight models from five providers. Within the deterministic threshold-cascade class, full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequence cascades do not deliver practically meaningful held-out gains over it. A lightweight pre-generation router exceeds the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly because it avoids the cheap model's generation cost on queries sent directly to a larger model rather than because of a stronger routing signal. These results suggest that cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost, since cascades pay the cheap model before any escalation decision, rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

</details>


### [276] [Topological Signatures of Grokking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06352)
*Yifan Tang,Qiquan Wang,Inés García-Redondo,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grokking is associated with a topological transition in neural representations, revealed by persistent homology as a sharp rise in H1 maximum and total persistence and a dominant long-lived feature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand grokking through topology, linking representation learning to global geometric structure via topological data analysis.

Method: Compute embedding-based point clouds from models trained on modular arithmetic tasks (varying primes); apply persistent homology to obtain H1 features; analyze max/total persistence and diagrams; compare with Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension; ablation across data regimes to link topology with generalization.

Result: Found a consistent topological signature: a rapid increase in both maximum and total H1 persistence, emergence of a dominant long-lived H1 feature, and structured secondary features; signatures align with the task's cyclic structure; topology provides a unified view of local/global structure and generalization rather than memorization.

Conclusion: Persistent homology offers an interpretable, principled framework to analyze how networks internalize latent structure during training, complementing spectral and geometric diagnostics and capturing multi-scale representation geometry.

Abstract: We study the grokking phenomenon through the lens of topology. Using persistent homology on point clouds derived from the embedding matrices of a range of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, we identify a clear and consistent topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of first homology ($H_1$). Persistence diagrams reveal the emergence of a dominant long-lived topological feature together with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the underlying cyclic structure of the task. Compared to existing spectral and geometric diagnostics -- specifically, Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension -- persistent homology provides a unified geometric and topological characterization of representation learning, capturing both local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations across data regimes and control settings show that these topological transitions are tied to generalization rather than memorization. Our results suggest that persistent homology offers a principled and interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure during training.

</details>


### [277] [Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Modelling with Missing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06355)
*Ignacio Peis,Pablo M. Olmos,Jes Frellsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Order-Agnostic autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in deep generative modeling, yet their use in settings with incomplete data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we reinterpret them through the lens of missing data. First, we show that their standard training procedure on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under a missing completely at random mechanism, resulting in robust out-of-sample imputation performance in settings with high missingness. Second, we introduce the first principled framework for training them directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms. Third, we leverage their amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition, i.e., sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream prediction or inference. Across a suite of real-world benchmarks, our Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

</details>


### [278] [Preliminary Insights in Chronos Frequency Data Understanding and Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06361)
*Alessandro Pagani,Marco Cominelli,Liying Han,Gaofeng Dong,Sergio Benini,Francesco Gringoli,Mattia Savardi,Mani B. Srivastava,Trevor Bihl,Erik P. Blasch,Daniel O. Brigham,Kara Combs,Lance M. Kaplan,Federico Cerutti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Preliminary analysis of Chronos' encoding of frequency-domain information using online MDL probes on sinusoidal inputs to assess presence, separability, and quality of frequency representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize how temporal foundation models capture fundamental signal properties, enabling trust, interpretability, and robust transfer across signal modalities.

Method: Test with simple sinusoidal signals at fixed frequencies. Apply lightweight online minimum description length probes to the decoder to detect frequency information and its separability in internal representations under controlled conditions.
