Result: Shows how frequential content is captured across the spectrum and identifies regimes where representation quality degrades or requires care.

Conclusion: Provides practical guidance for Chronos users in signal processing and information fusion, and contributes to interpretability and evaluation of temporal foundation models.

Abstract: This paper presents a preliminary analysis of the ability of Chronos foundation model to process and internally represent frequency domain information. Foundation models that process time-series data offer practitioners a unified architecture capable of learning generic temporal representations across diverse tasks and domains, reducing the need for task-specific feature engineering and enabling transfer across signal modalities. Despite their growing adoption, the extent to which such models encode fundamental signal properties remains insufficiently characterised. We address this gap by analysing Chronos under controlled conditions, starting from the simplest class of signals: discrete sinusoids generated at fixed frequencies. Using lightweight online minimum description length probes applied to the decoder architecture, we test for the presence and separability of frequency information in the model's internal representations. The results provide insight into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum and highlight regimes in which representation quality may degrade or require particular care. These findings offer practical guidance for users of Chronos in signal processing and information fusion contexts, and contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

</details>


### [279] [Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06364)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces AuxPath-FM, a flexible flow matching framework that uses an arbitrary auxiliary variable η from any distribution to define probability paths X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η, preserving the continuity equation and the marginal objective, enabling diverse priors and tasks like label-guided generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend conditional flow matching beyond Gaussian auxiliary noise to allow arbitrary priors, enriching the geometry and expressivity of generative flows.

Method: Construct probability paths with an auxiliary variable η drawn from an arbitrary distribution, such that X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η. Prove preservation of the continuity equation and alignment of the training objective with the marginal formulation. Demonstrate with priors including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher, and discuss encoding semantic information into η for label guidance.

Result: A theoretically general and practically flexible framework for designing probability paths in flow-based models; shows that a broad class of priors can be used without breaking the core training objective or underlying dynamics; enables specialized tasks like semantic/label-guided generation by shaping the auxiliary distribution.

Conclusion: AuxPath-FM offers a principled, general foundation for probability-path design in flow-based generative modeling, combining theoretical rigor with practical flexibility across diverse priors and applications.

Abstract: We introduce a new generative modeling framework, \textbf{Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM)}, which generalizes conditional flow matching by incorporating an auxiliary variable drawn from an arbitrary distribution into the probability path. Unlike prior methods that restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, AuxPath-FM allows the variable $η$ to follow any distribution, producing trajectories of the form $X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η$. We theoretically demonstrate that this construction preserves the continuity equation and maintains a training objective consistent with the marginal formulation. This flexibility enables the design of diverse probability paths using various priors, including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher distributions, each offering unique geometric properties for generative flows. Furthermore, our framework allows for specialized tasks such as label-guided generation by encoding structured semantic information into the auxiliary distribution. Overall, AuxPath-FM provides a principled and general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

</details>


### [280] [A Unified Pair-GRPO Family: From Implicit to Explicit Preference Constraints for Stable and General RL Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06375)
*Hao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified Pair-GRPO framework for preference-based RLHF optimization, introducing Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO to stabilize gradients and reduce variance, with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical gains on standard alignment benchmarks and MuJoCo tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability, ambiguous gradient directions, interpretability, and high gradient variance in standard pairwise preference RLHF methods by unifying the approach under Pair-GRPO and leveraging clipped surrogate/KL-regularized optimization.

Method: Soft-Pair-GRPO replaces continuous rewards with binary pairwise preferences while preserving GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL regularization; proves a gradient-equivalence theorem showing the soft variant's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of GRPO's gradient. Hard-Pair-GRPO adds explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting to further reduce gradient noise and policy drift.

Result: Theoretical guarantees for both variants: monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Empirical results show Pair-GRPO improves alignment quality, human-preference win rates, training stability, and generalization on HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback, and MuJoCo HalfCheetah-v4, with ablations confirming core component contributions.

Conclusion: Pair-GRPO provides a principled, stable, and efficient framework for preference-based RL optimization in LLM alignment, combining theoretical rigor with practical performance gains and offering a pathway for improved generalization in RL tasks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) alignment via reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in mainstream pairwise preference learning paradigms. To systematically address these limitations, we establish a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization centered on the Pair-GRPO family, comprising two tightly coupled variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO is a minimal modification of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that replaces group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure. We prove a critical gradient equivalence theorem: under first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of standard GRPO's gradient, explaining its empirical stability despite discarding continuous reward magnitudes. Building on this foundation, we propose Hard-Pair-GRPO, an advanced variant introducing explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and global policy drift. We provide comprehensive theoretical guarantees for both variants--including monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Extensive experiments on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF,UltraFeedback) and the MuJoCo continuous control task HalfCheetah-v4 demonstrate that our Pair-GRPO family consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general reinforcement learning. Ablation studies validate the critical contributions of each core component.

</details>


### [281] [MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06384)
*Alessandro Ronca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) define multi-layer recurrence that is resistant to vanishing/exploding gradients, with expressivity up to all regular languages, parallelizable evaluation, bounded states/gradients, and strong empirical performance on synthetic tasks and competitive next-token results for a large model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome gradient issues and improve efficiency/expressivity of recurrent architectures; achieve finite-memory systems capable of representing regular languages with scalable training/inference.

Method: Introduce MinMax recurrence in cascaded layers; prove theoretical properties: expressivity (regular languages), parallelizable with log input length, bounded state/activations, gradient existence and boundedness, non-vanishing state gradient. Provide empirical validation on synthetic tasks and a 127M-parameter model for next-token prediction.

Result: Theoretically: MinMax RNCs can represent all regular languages, parallelizable to log input length, uniformly bounded state/activations, gradients that exist and are bounded, and state gradients do not vanish. Empirically: solve synthetic tasks better than SOTA RNNs; a 127M-parameter MinMax RNC achieves competitive performance on next-token prediction.

Conclusion: MinMax RNCs combine strong theoretical guarantees with practical performance, showing promise for real-world sequence modeling and potentially overcoming common RNN limitations.

Abstract: We show that the MinMax algebra provides a form of recurrence that is expressively powerful, efficiently implementable, and most importantly it is not affected by vanishing or exploding gradient. We call MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) the models obtained by cascading several layers of neurons that employ such recurrence. We show that MinMax RNCs enjoy many favourable theoretical properties. First, their formal expressivity includes all regular languages, arguably the maximal expressivity for a finite-memory system. Second, they can be evaluated in parallel with a runtime that is logarithmic in the input length given enough processors; and they can also be evaluated sequentially. Third, their state and activations are bounded uniformly for all input lengths. Fourth, at almost all points, their loss gradient exists and it is bounded. Fifth, they do not exhibit a vanishing state gradient: the gradient of a state w.r.t. a past state can have constant value one regardless of the time distance between the two states. Finally, we find empirical evidence that the favourable theoretical properties of MinMax RNCs are matched by their practical capabilities: they are able to perfectly solve a number of synthetic tasks, showing superior performance compared to the considered state-of-the-art recurrent neural networks; also, we train a MinMax RNC of 127M parameters on next-token prediction, and the obtained model shows competitive performance for its size, providing evidence of the potential of MinMax RNCs on real-world tasks.

</details>


### [282] [Data-Driven Covariate Selection for Nonparametric and Cycle-Agnostic Causal Effect Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06385)
*Ana Leticia Garcez Vicente,Gijs van Seeventer,Saber Salehkaleybar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Local, data-driven covariate selection via conditional independence remains sound and complete in cyclic causal models thanks to σ-acyclification invariance; cycle-agnostic approach for covariate selection and causal effect estimation, validated on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimate causal effects from observational data with latent confounding and feedback loops; acyclicity assumptions limit applicability; seek efficient, local covariate selection that works in cycles.

Method: Local conditional-independence based covariate selection; relies on invariance of CI under σ-acyclification; applies to cyclic and acyclic models without modification.

Result: Theoretical guarantees extend to cyclic models; unified framework; empirical validation on extensive synthetic data showing reliable performance in cyclic models.

Conclusion: Cycle-agnostic covariate selection enables robust causal effect estimation across acyclic and cyclic settings; supports practical use in complex systems; future work could involve real data and robustness to CI errors.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects from observational data requires identifying valid adjustment sets. This task is especially challenging in realistic settings where latent confounding and feedback loops are present. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, limiting applicability and computational efficiency. In this work, we study a local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information. While this method is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models, its validity in the presence of cycles has remained unclear. Our main contribution is to show that these guarantees extend to cyclic causal models. In particular, our result relies on the invariance of conditional independence assertions under $σ$-acyclification. These findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation, showing that the method applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, we validate this on extensive synthetic data, showing reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

</details>


### [283] [Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation: Bridging Exploitation and Imitation at the Token Level](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06387)
*Nan Jia,Haojin Yang,Xing Ma,Jiesong Lian,Shuailiang Zhang,Weipeng Zhang,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Zequn Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AOPD replaces negative reinforcement in non-positive advantage regions with localized divergence minimization, improving stability, exploration, and performance over OPD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD’s advantage-weighted policy gradient suffers from high variance, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks due to insufficient corrective signals.

Method: Introduce Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD): apply localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement within the on-policy distillation framework.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, AOPD outperforms OPD with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization; it also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

Conclusion: AOPD mitigates the core structural weaknesses of OPD by asymmetrically shaping updates to maintain positive learning signals while improving exploration and stability.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own trajectories with token-level teacher feedback and often outperforms off-policy distillation and standard reinforcement learning. However, we find that its standard advantage weighted policy gradient suffers from three structural weaknesses, including high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.We therefore propose Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization, respectively. AOPD also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

</details>


### [284] [Consistent Geometric Deep Learning via Hilbert Bundles and Cellular Sheaves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06395)
*Kartik Tandon,Julian Gould,Tanishq Bhatia,Francesca Dominici,Alejandro Ribeiro,Claudio Battiloro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces HilbNets, a convolutional framework for infinite-dimensional signals on manifolds using the connection Laplacian of a Hilbert bundle; establishes sampling-based discretization via Hilbert Cellular Sheaves, proving convergence to the manifold Laplacian and consistency across samplings; validates on synthetic/real tasks; extends geometric learning to bundle-valued signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lack of unified learning theory for infinite-dimensional signals on irregular domains; need principled convolution for signals like time series, distributions, or operators on manifolds; extend Laplacian-based geometric learning to Hilbert-bundle-valued signals.

Method: Define convolution with connection Laplacian of a Hilbert bundle; construct HilbNets; two-stage sampling: (1) sampling induces Hilbert Cellular Sheaf; prove its sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to the connection Laplacian as sampling density increases; (2) discretize signals to produce implementable HilbNets; prove discretized nets converge to continuous architecture and are transferable across samplings.

Result: Generalizes Belkin & Niyogi convergence of graph Laplacian to manifold Laplacian in infinite-dimensional bundle setting; discretization preserves convergence and transferability; empirical validation on synthetic and real-world tasks.

Conclusion: Broadens geometric learning by lifting Laplacian-based frameworks to per-point Hilbert spaces, enabling learning on more general signal types (time series, distributions, operators) on manifolds.

Abstract: Modern deep learning architectures increasingly contend with sophisticated signals that are natively infinite-dimensional, such as time series, probability distributions, or operators, and are defined over irregular domains. Yet, a unified learning theory for these settings has been lacking. To start addressing this gap, we introduce a novel convolutional learning framework for possibly infinite-dimensional signals supported on a manifold. Namely, we use the connection Laplacian associated with a Hilbert bundle as a convolutional operator, and we derive filters and neural networks, dubbed as \textit{HilbNets}. We make HilbNets and, more generally, the convolution operation, implementable via a two-stage sampling procedure. First, we show that sampling the manifold induces a Hilbert Cellular Sheaf, a generalized graph structure with Hilbert feature spaces and edge-wise coupling rules, and we prove that its sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to the underlying connection Laplacian as the sampling density increases. Notably, this result is a generalization to the infinite-dimensional bundle setting of the Belkin \& Niyogi \cite{BELKIN20081289} convergence result for the graph Laplacian to the manifold Laplacian, a theoretical cornerstone of geometric learning methods. Second, we discretize the signals and prove that the discretized (implementable) HilbNets converge to the underlying continuous architectures and are transferable across different samplings of the same bundle, providing consistency for learning. Finally, we validate our framework on synthetic and real-world tasks. Overall, our results broaden the scope of geometric learning as a whole by lifting classical Laplacian-based frameworks to settings where the signal at each point lives in its own Hilbert space.

</details>


### [285] [SparseForge: Efficient Semi-Structured LLM Sparsification via Annealing of Hessian-Guided Soft-Mask](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06402)
*Liu Hanzuo,Chaofan Lin,Weixuan Sun,Yulong Wang,Key,Rayying,Mingyu Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SparseForge optimizes the sparsity mask during post-training via Hessian-aware importance and soft-to-hard mask annealing, enabling hardware-friendly semi-structured sparsity with far fewer retraining tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the high computational cost of post-training semi-structured pruning by avoiding large-scale sparse retraining while maintaining, or improving, accuracy under structured sparsity.

Method: Use Hessian-aware importance estimation to guide mask selection, and progressively anneal soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity during post-training, with a small number of retraining tokens.

Result: For LLaMA-2-7B at 2:4 sparsity, achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with 5B retraining tokens, beating the dense baseline (56.43%) and close to a state-of-the-art method that uses 40B tokens (57.52%). The gains are reported to be consistent across model families.

Conclusion: Directly optimizing sparsity masks with Hessian guidance and soft-to-hard mask annealing yields a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off for post-training semi-structured pruning and generalizes across model families.

Abstract: Semi-structured sparsity provides a practical path to accelerate large language models (LLMs) with native hardware support, but post-training semi-structured pruning often suffers from substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling. Existing methods rely on large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, resulting in high computational cost.
  We propose SparseForge, a post-training framework that improves recovery efficiency by directly optimizing the sparsity mask rather than scaling up retraining tokens. SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery. On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only $\textbf{5B}$ retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% accuracy and approaching the 57.52% result of a state-of-the-art method using $\textbf{40B}$ tokens. Such improvements on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off from SparseForge are shown to be consistent across model families.

</details>


### [286] [FRInGe: Distribution-Space Integrated Gradients with Fisher--Rao Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06404)
*Gabriele Martino,Sebastian Tschiatschek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRInGe is a Fisher–Rao-based attribution method that uses a maximum-entropy reference and geodesic interpolation in predictive distribution space to improve calibration-oriented attribution metrics on ImageNet, while remaining competitive on other attribution flux.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrated Gradients can be brittle due to heuristic baselines, straight-line interpolation, discretization, and saturation. A distribution-space, geometry-aware approach can yield more faithful, calibrated attributions.

Method: Define both reference and interpolation in predictive distribution space using a maximum-entropy predictive reference and Fisher–Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The input-space trajectory is obtained via the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized with KL and Euclidean trust regions. Attributions are computed by integrating input gradients along this trajectory.

Result: Evaluated on six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe yields the clearest improvement in calibration-oriented attribution metrics (notably MAS scores) and remains competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

Conclusion: FRInGe improves calibration-oriented attribution and demonstrates the value of Fisher–Rao geometry for attribution in neural models, without sacrificing performance on other attribution criteria.

Abstract: Gradient-based attribution methods are model-faithful and scalable, but Integrated Gradients (IG) can be brittle because explanations depend on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. We propose Fisher--Rao Integrated Gradients (FRInGe), which defines both the reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space. FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The corresponding input-space trajectory is realized through the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions; attributions are obtained by integrating input gradients along this trajectory. Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe most clearly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics, especially MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

</details>


### [287] [Federated Cross-Client Subgraph Pattern Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06433)
*Selin Ceydeli,Rui Wang,Kubilay Atasu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated, layer-wise embedding exchange aligns distributed GNN representations with a centralized GNN for subgraph pattern detection; embeddings must be fresh per layer; under extended-subgraph and shared parameters, the approach can recover the centralized node representations; experiments on synthetic graphs show the embedding exchange plus federated aggregation nearly closes the representation gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distributed graphs prevent centralized GNN training, leading to a representation-equivalence gap for cross-partition subgraph patterns. A formalized notion of structural observability motivates a privacy-preserving, collaborative solution to restore identifiability of cross-boundary patterns.

Method: A per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework where clients share intermediate node representations at each GNN layer during the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. With shared model parameters and an extended-subgraph assumption, this is combined with federated parameter aggregation to reproduce the centralized GNN’s node representations; freshness of exchanged embeddings per-step (not per-epoch) is emphasized.

Result: Under the extended-subgraph assumption, the proposed framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN on the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated aggregation are complementary and that using fresh per-step embeddings recovers most of the representation gap.

Conclusion: Embedding exchange is essential to bridge the representation gap in distributed GNNs for subgraph pattern detection. When combined with federated parameter aggregation, it can closely match centralized performance under suitable assumptions; the freshness of per-step embeddings is crucial for gap recovery.

Abstract: Subgraph pattern detection aims to uncover complex interaction structures in graphs. However, state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN)-based solutions assume centralized access to the entire graph. When graphs are instead distributed across multiple parties, client-local GNN computations diverge from those of a centralized model, resulting in a representation-equivalence gap. We formalize this as a structural observability problem, where subgraph patterns crossing partition boundaries become locally unidentifiable. To bridge this gap, we propose a per-step, layer-wise embedding exchange framework in which clients synchronize intermediate node representations at each layer of the forward pass, without exposing raw features or labels. Under an extended-subgraph assumption and shared model parameters across clients, this framework recovers the same node representations as a centralized GNN over the full graph. Experiments on synthetic directed multigraphs with cycles, bicliques, and scatter-gather patterns show that embedding exchange and federated parameter aggregation are complementary rather than interchangeable: their combination recovers most of the representation gap, provided exchanged embeddings are fresh per-step rather than stale per-epoch.

</details>


### [288] [FedFrozen: Two-Stage Federated Optimization via Attention Kernel Freezing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06446)
*Junye Du,Zhenghao Li,Yushi Feng,Long Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedFrozen: a two-stage federated optimization for Transformers with heterogeneous clients that freezes the query/key block after a warm-up, optimizing only the value block; under linear-attention, warm-up approximates inexact descent on a kernel-profile objective; frozen stage solves restricted value-block optimization; trade-off on warm-up length; experiments show improved stability and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address client drift in heterogeneous federated learning, motivated by observed robustness of Transformer architectures. Dissect attention into kernel (Q/K) and semantic transform (V) blocks to understand how parameter components affect optimization in FL.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) full-model warm-up training across clients; (2) freeze Q/K block and continue optimizing V under a fixed attention kernel. Analysis under linear-attention shows warm-up corresponds to inexact descent on a regularized kernel-profile objective; frozen stage becomes restricted value-block optimization. Also derive a trade-off for warm-up length.

Result: Simulations validate predicted bias-drift behavior; real-data experiments show FedFrozen improves stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous FL settings.

Conclusion: FedFrozen provides a principled mechanism leveraging attention structure to mitigate client drift in FL, with a tunable warm-up length; likely beneficial for Transformer-based FL; future work could extend to non-linear attention and other modules.

Abstract: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients remains a significant challenge for deep learning, primarily due to client drift arising from inconsistent local updates. Existing federated optimization methods typically address this issue through objective-level regularization or update-correction mechanisms. Recent studies, however, suggest that Transformer-based architectures may be inherently more robust than conventional models under heterogeneous federated training. Motivated by this observation, we investigate how different parameter components within the attention mechanism influence federated optimization. Specifically, we decompose the attention module into a query/key block, which determines the attention kernel, and a value block, which performs semantic transformation under the induced kernel. Based on this perspective, we propose FedFrozen, a two-stage federated optimization framework that first performs full-model warm-up training and then freezes the query/key block while continuing to optimize the value block. Under a linear-attention formulation, we show that the warm-up stage can be interpreted as an inexact descent procedure on a regularized kernel-profile objective, while the frozen stage reduces to a restricted value-block optimization problem under a fixed attention kernel. Our analysis further reveals an explicit trade-off that governs the choice of warm-up length. Simulations validate the predicted bias-drift behavior, and real-data experiments demonstrate that FedFrozen improves both the stability and effectiveness of Transformer models in heterogeneous federated learning.

</details>


### [289] [Scene-Adaptive Continual Learning for CSI-based Human Activity Recognition with Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06447)
*Wenhan Zheng,Yuyi Mao,Ivan Wang-Hei Ho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAMoE-C: a scene-adaptive mixture of experts for cross-domain CSI-based HAR with a lightweight router and small replay buffer, achieving competitive accuracy with reduced inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CSI-based HAR performance degrades under domain shifts across physical environments. Existing continual-learning (CL) methods for CSI-HAR scale poorly, require large replay buffers, or incur linearly growing inference costs. There is a need for a scalable, efficient cross-domain adaptation framework.

Method: Proposes Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), a mixture-of-experts model where a scene-specific set of experts handles inputs. An attention-based semantic router activates only selected experts per input. Introduces a lightweight training protocol requiring only a tiny replay buffer to stabilize router-domain discrimination.

Result: Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset show SAMoE-C achieving near state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly reducing inference cost, demonstrating scalable cross-domain deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency.

Conclusion: Modular experts with selective router activation and a lightweight training protocol enable scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR with efficient inference and low overhead.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI)-based human activity recognition (HAR) is vulnerable to performance degradation under domain shifts across varying physical environments. Continual learning (CL) offers a principled way to learn new domains sequentially while preserving past knowledge, but existing CL solutions for CSI-based HAR scale poorly with accumulating domains, rely on a large replay buffer, or incur linearly growing inference cost. In this letter, we propose Scene-Adaptive Mixture of Experts with Clustered Specialists (SAMoE-C), which formulates cross-domain CSI-based HAR as a mixture-of-experts system that enables scene-specific adaptation, via an attention-based semantic router that activates only selected experts for each input. Moreover, we develop a novel training protocol, which requires only a tiny replay buffer for stabilizing domain discrimination of the router. Experimental results on a four-scene CSI dataset demonstrate that SAMoE-C approaches the state-of-the-art accuracy, while maintaining a significantly lower inference cost. By jointly combining modular experts, selective activation with router and a lightweight training protocol, SAMoE-C enables scalable cross-domain CSI-based HAR deployment with low training overhead and high computational efficiency in real-world settings.

</details>


### [290] [ORTHOBO: Orthogonal Bayesian Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06454)
*Maresa Schröder,Pascal Janetzky,Michael Klar,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrthoBO introduces a variance-reduced, orthogonal acquisition estimator for Bayesian optimization to stabilize candidate rankings under Monte Carlo noise, combining ensemble surrogates and an outer log transform, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation in neural network hyperparameter optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Noisy, finite-sample Monte Carlo estimates of acquisition functions can flip candidate rankings, causing suboptimal BO decisions; reducing variance is essential for stable optimization.

Method: Develop an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate to create an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions. Integrate this estimator into OrthoBO with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. Provide theoretical guarantees that the target is preserved and variance is reduced, plus empirical evaluation.

Result: The estimator reduces acquisition variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and improves BO performance in numerical experiments. It demonstrates strong performance in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

Conclusion: OrthoBO effectively reduces acquisition estimation variance, yields more stable rankings, and enhances Bayesian optimization performance, with demonstrated utility for neural network hyperparameter tuning.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is widely used for hyperparameter optimization when model evaluations are expensive; however, noisy acquisition estimates can lead to unstable decisions. We identify acquisition estimation noise as a failure mode that was previously overlooked: even when the surrogate model and acquisition target are correctly specified, finite-sample Monte Carlo error can perturb acquisition values. This can, in turn, flip candidate rankings and lead to suboptimal BO decisions. As a remedy, we aim at variance reduction and propose an orthogonal acquisition estimator that subtracts an optimally weighted score-function control variate, which yields an acquisition residual orthogonal to posterior score directions and which thus reduces Monte Carlo variance. We further introduce OrthoBO: a Bayesian optimization framework that combines our orthogonal acquisition estimator with ensemble surrogates and an outer log transformation. We show theoretically that our estimator preserves the target, leads to variance reduction, and improves pairwise ranking stability. We further verify the theoretical properties of OrthoBO through numerical experiments where our framework reduces acquisition estimation variance, stabilizes candidate rankings, and achieves strong performance. We also demonstrate the downstream utility of OrthoBO in hyperparameter optimization for neural network training and fine-tuning.

</details>


### [291] [Invariant Features in Language Models: Geometric Characterization and Model Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06458)
*Agnibh Dasgupta,Abdullah Tanvir,Xin Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a local geometric view of semantic invariance in language models: paraphrase-equivalent inputs form invariant subspaces with nuisance directions, introduces a contrastive subspace discovery method and applies invariant features to zero-shot model attribution; supported by empirical evidence across models and layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how semantic invariance arises in neural representations, and how to identify and exploit invariant vs. nuisance variation in latent space.

Method: (1) formalize a local geometric framework for invariant features; (2) develop a contrastive subspace discovery algorithm to separate semantic-preserving from semantic-changing variation; (3) demonstrate causal role of invariant components via representation-level interventions and apply to zero-shot attribution; cross-model, cross-layer evaluation.

Result: Invariant structure appears in specific depth regions; semantic displacement occurs largely outside the nuisance subspace; interventions show invariant components causally influence outputs; invariant representations enable accurate model attribution and reveal model-specific geometric patterns.

Conclusion: Semantic invariance behaves as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled lens on how LMs organize meaning and enabling practical tools for interpretation and attribution.

Abstract: Language models exhibit strong robustness to paraphrasing, suggesting that semantic information may be encoded through stable internal representations, yet the structure and origin of such invariance remain unclear. We propose a local geometric framework in which semantically equivalent inputs occupy structured regions in latent space, with paraphrastic variation along nuisance directions and semantic identity preserved in invariant subspaces. Building on this view, we make three contributions: (1) a geometric characterization of invariant latent features, (2) a contrastive subspace discovery method that separates semantic-changing from semantic-preserving variation, and (3) an application of invariant representations to zero-shot model attribution. Across models and layers, empirical results support these contributions. Invariant structure emerges in specific depth regions, semantic displacement lies largely outside the nuisance subspace, and representation-level interventions indicate a causal role of invariant components in model outputs. Invariant representations also capture model-specific geometric patterns, enabling accurate attribution. These findings suggest that semantic invariance can be viewed as a local geometric property of latent representations, offering a principled perspective on how language models organize meaning.

</details>


### [292] [MINER: Mining Multimodal Internal Representation for Efficient Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06460)
*Weien Li,Rui Song,Zeyu Li,Haochen Liu,Gonghao Zhang,Difan Jiao,Zhenwei Tang,Bowei He,Haolun Wu,Xue Liu,Ye Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MINER is a lightweight plug-in that mines internal transformer representations for efficient, high-quality dense single-vector visual document retrieval by probing layerwise signals and adaptively fusing them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense single-vector retrievers compress all information into one embedding and underperform compared to late-interaction models. Internal layer representations contain retrieval-relevant signals that can be exploited without changing the backbone.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing attaches probes at each transformer layer to identify retrieval-relevant dimensions; (2) Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion masks neuron-level signals across selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into a final compact embedding, preserving single-vector efficiency and without backbone modification.

Result: On ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on most benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over the backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, MINER in some settings narrows the nDCG@5 gap to 0.2, while maintaining storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

Conclusion: Exploiting cross-layer internal representations via MINER yields significant gains for dense single-vector visual document retrieval with minimal overhead, bridging the gap to late-interaction methods.

Abstract: Visual document retrieval has become essential for accessing information in visually rich documents. Existing approaches fall into two camps. Late-interaction retrievers achieve strong quality through fine-grained token-level matching but store hundreds of vectors per page, incurring large index footprints and high serving costs. By contrast, dense single-vector retrievers retain storage and latency advantages but consistently lag in quality because they compress all information into a single final-layer embedding. In this work, we first conduct a layerwise diagnostic on single-vector retrievers, revealing that retrieval-relevant signal resides in internal representations. Motivated by these findings, we propose MINER (Mining Multimodal Internal RepreseNtation for Efficient Retrieval), a lightweight plug-in module that probes and fuses internal signals across transformer layers into a single compact embedding without modifying the backbone or sacrificing single-vector efficiency. The first Retrieval-Aligned Layer Probing stage attaches a lightweight probe at each layer, surfacing which dimensions carry retrieval-relevant information. The subsequent Adaptive Sparse Multi-Layer Fusion stage applies performance-adaptive neuron-level masking to the selected layers and fuses the surviving signals into the final dense vector. Across ViDoRe V1/V2/V3, MINER outperforms existing dense single-vector retrievers on the majority of benchmarks, with up to 4.5% nDCG@5 improvement over its corresponding backbone. Compared to strong late-interaction baselines, in some settings MINER substantially narrows the nDCG@$5$ gap to $0.2$ while preserving the storage and serving advantages of dense retrieval.

</details>


### [293] [Invariant-Based Diagnostics for Graph Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06462)
*Richard von Moos,Mathieu Alain,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Permutation-invariant graph invariants are proposed as a diagnostic, task-agnostic proxy for structure in graph benchmarks. They are shown to be more expressive than standard GNNs, reveal structural heterogeneity, predict multi-task performance, and rival supervised baselines across 26 datasets, suggesting structure matters and invariant baselines should be standard for evaluating when learning on graphs is necessary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark practices conflate node features and graph structure, making it hard to determine whether models learn from connectivity or if they need it. A task-agnostic, permutation-invariant diagnostic (invariants) can separately quantify structural information and its impact on performance.

Method: Define permutation-invariant, task-agnostic graph invariants as structural descriptors. Compare their expressivity to standard GNNs, assess their ability to capture heterogeneity within and across benchmarks, evaluate their predictive power for multi-task performance, and benchmark simple invariant-based models against transformers and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets.

Result: Invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, capture structural heterogeneity within/across datasets, predict multi-task performance, and, in many cases, simple invariant-based models compete with or surpass transformer and MP baselines. Expressivity is not the sole driver of performance; non-trainable structural proxies can match trained MP models on structure-relevant tasks.

Conclusion: Invariant baselines should become a standard diagnostic for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model leverages structure, serving as a stepping stone toward graph foundation models.

Abstract: Progress on graph foundation models is hindered by benchmark practices that conflate the contributions of node features and graph structure, making it hard to tell whether a model actually learns from connectivity, or whether it even needs to. We propose addressing this using graph invariants, i.e., permutation-invariant, task-agnostic structural descriptors that serve as a diagnostic framework for graph benchmarks. We show that (i) invariants are more expressive than standard GNNs, (ii) invariants characterize structural heterogeneity within and across benchmark datasets, (iii) invariants predict multi-task performance, and (iv) simple invariant-based models are competitive with, and sometimes exceed, transformer and message-passing baselines across 26 datasets. Our results suggest that expressivity is not the main driver of predictive performance, and that on tasks where structure matters, a non-trainable structural proxy often matches trained message-passing models. We thus posit that invariant baselines should become a standard for evaluating whether structure is required for a task and whether a model picks up on it, serving as a stepping stone towards graph foundation models.

</details>


### [294] [Diversity Curves for Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06466)
*Katharina Limbeck,Nadja Häusermann,Martin Carrasco,Guy Wolf,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces diversity curves: size-aware, interpretable graph-level representations obtained by edge-contraction coarsening, capturing structural diversity across coarsening levels and enabling cross-hierarchy comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graphs of different sizes require comparable, expressive representations; current descriptors often fail to capture metric diversity and geometry, hindering unsupervised, scalable, and interpretable analysis across coarsening hierarchies.

Method: Compute the spread (diversity) of graphs across coarsening levels as an isometry-invariant measure; employ edge contraction coarsening to enhance expressivity; show that this leads to more powerful graph-level representations than using structural descriptors alone.

Result: Diversity curves enable clustering and visualization across varying sizes, distinguish geometry of single-cell graphs, compare structures of molecular graph datasets, and characterize geometric shapes; outperform baseline methods across these tasks.

Conclusion: Diversity curves offer interpretable, efficient, and cross-hierarchy graph representations; edge-contraction coarsening improves expressivity and broadens applicability to diverse graph domains.

Abstract: Graph-level representations are crucial tools for characterising structural differences between graphs. However, comparing graphs with different cardinalities, even when sampled from the same underlying distribution, remains challenging. Unsupervised tasks in particular require interpretable, scalable, and reliable size-aware graph representations. Our work addresses these issues by tracking the structural diversity of a graph across coarsening levels. The resulting graph embeddings, which we denote diversity curves, are interpretable by construction, efficient, and directly comparable across coarsening hierarchies. Specifically, we track the spread of graphs, a novel isometry invariant that is inherently well-suited for encoding the metric diversity and geometry of graphs. We utilise edge contraction coarsening and prove that this improves expressivity, thus leading to more powerful graph-level representations than structural descriptors alone. Demonstrating their utility over a range of baseline methods in practice, we use diversity curves to (i) cluster and visualise simulated graphs across varying sizes, (ii) distinguish the geometry of single-cell graphs, (iii) compare the structure of molecular graph datasets, and (iv) characterise geometric shapes.

</details>


### [295] [No Triangulation Without Representation: Generalization in Topological Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06467)
*Johannes S. Schmidt,Martin Carrasco,Ernst Röell,Guy Wolf,Nello Blaser,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends MANTRA benchmark to a wider class of manifolds; finds that with proper representation, both GNNs and higher-order message passing saturate the benchmark; introduces a new evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement; reveals lack of generalization beyond combinatorial structure, signaling need for topology-aware inductive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inconsistent evaluation of topological deep learning models and the misuse of refinement operations applicable to manifolds but not to graphs; provide a robust benchmark and protocol to study topology-aware learning.

Method: Augment the MANTRA benchmark with manifold triangulations from a broader set of manifolds (diverse homeomorphism types); evaluate GNNs and higher-order message passing methods under varying representation and feature assignment; propose an evaluation protocol emphasizing representational diversity and triangulation refinement.

Result: GNNs and HOPMs saturate the benchmark when coupled with appropriate representations and features; the models do not demonstrate generalization beyond combinatorial structure; a novel evaluation protocol is proposed and the work provides scaffolding for topology-aware inductive biases.

Conclusion: Current models struggle to learn topology independent of scale; there is a gap in developing models with true topological understanding; the work offers a framework for future evaluation and bias development for topology-aware learning.

Abstract: Despite an ever-increasing interest in topological deep learning models that target higher-order datasets, there is no consensus on how to evaluate such models. This is exacerbated by the fact that topological objects permit operations, such as structural refinements, that are not appropriate for graph data. In this work, we extend MANTRA, a benchmark dataset containing manifold triangulations, to a larger class of manifolds with more diverse homeomorphism types. We show that, unlike prior claims, both graph neural networks (GNNs) and higher-order message passing (HOMP) methods can saturate the benchmark. However, we find that this is contingent on the right representation and feature assignment, emphasizing their importance in baseline models. We thus provide a novel evaluation protocol based on representational diversity and triangulation refinement. Surprisingly, we find no indication that existing models are capable of generalizing beyond the combinatorial structure of the data. This points towards a research gap in developing models that understand topological structure independent of scale. Our work thus provides the necessary scaffolding to evaluate future models and enable the development of topology-aware inductive biases.

</details>


### [296] [Hitting Time Isomorphism for Multi-Stage Planning with Foundation Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06470)
*Magnus Victor Boock,Abdullah Akgül,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Melih Kandemir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Operator-theoretic, isomorphic embedding framework for offline reinforcement learning that learns a directed, asymmetric geometry via hitting times; introduces IEL algorithm with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results on offline maze tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of symmetric metrics and triangle-inequality violations in representation learning for decision-making; need a representation reflecting temporal progress to enable robust long-horizon planning.

Method: Develops a Hilbert-space displacement representation where hitting times are linear functionals of latent displacements; latent linear closure ensuring identifiability; finite-dimensional bounds; derives finite-sample guarantees; formulates IEL combining a consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression.

Result: Theoretically bounds global hitting-time error by one-step transition error multiplied by the environment's transient spectral radius; provides finite-sample guarantees; IEL improves state-of-the-art foundation policy learning from offline maze data; code available at the linked repository.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, scalable foundation for directional, multi-stage planning in offline RL; the framework yields robust geometry and a practical algorithm (IEL) with strong empirical performance; well-suited for long-horizon navigation tasks.

Abstract: We present a new operator-theoretic representation learning framework for offline reinforcement learning that recovers the directed temporal geometry of a controlled Markov process from hitting time observations. While prior art often produces symmetric distances or fails to satisfy the triangle inequality, our framework learns a Hilbert-space displacement geometry where expected hitting times are realized as linear functionals of latent displacements. We prove that this representation exists under latent linear closure and is uniquely identifiable up to a bounded linear isomorphism. For finite-dimensional implementations, we show that global hitting-time error is bounded by one-step transition error amplified by the environment's transient spectral radius. Furthermore, we provide finite-sample guarantees accounting for approximation, statistical complexity, and trajectory-label mismatch. Derived from this theory, we curate Isomorphic Embedding Learning (IEL) as a new goal-agnostic foundation policy learning algorithm that anchors a HILP-style consistency objective with explicit hitting-time regression to ensure that the learned geometry reflects actual decision-time progress. This asymmetric and compositional structure enables robust graph-based multi-stage planning for long-horizon navigation. Our experiments demonstrate that IEL improves the state of the art of learning foundation policy policies from offline maze locomotion data. Our code can be found on https://github.com/MagnusBoock/IEL

</details>


### [297] [Efficient Serving for Dynamic Agent Workflows with Prediction-based KV-Cache Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06472)
*Haoyu Zheng,Fangcheng Fu,Jia Wu,Binhang Yuan,Yongqiang Zhang,Hao Wang,Yuanyuan Zhu,Xiao Yan,Jiawei Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PBKV is a prediction-based KV-cache manager for dynamic LLM workflows that forecasts future agent invocations to selectively cache and prefetch entries, boosting efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based workflows involve dynamic sequences of agent invocations, creating high opportunities for cache reuse but existing KV-cache strategies fail to exploit reuse within workflows or assume static agent sequences, leading to inefficiencies.

Method: PBKV predicts upcoming agent invocations for a workflow by fusing guidance from historical workflows and the current workflow context. It then estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps high-potential ones in GPU memory, using conservative eviction and prefetching to tolerate prediction errors.

Result: Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show up to 1.85× speedup over LRU for dynamic workflows and up to 1.26× speedup over the state-of-the-art KVFlow on static workflows.

Conclusion: Prediction-based cache management can significantly improve KV-cache efficiency in dynamic LLM workflows by forecasting inter-agent reuse and applying conservative eviction/prefetching to mitigate prediction errors.

Abstract: LLM-based workflows compose specialized agents to execute complex tasks, and these agents usually share substantial context, allowing KV-Cache reuse to save computation. Existing approaches either manage KV-Cache at agent level and fail to exploit the reuse opportunities within workflows, or manage cache at the workflow level but assume that each workflow calls a static sequence of agents. However, practical workflows are typically dynamic, where the sequence of invoked agents and thus induced cache reuse opportunities depend on the context of each task. To serve such dynamic workflows efficiently, we build a system dubbed PBKV (\textbf{P}rediction-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{KV}-Cache Management). For each workflow, PBKV predicts the agent invocations in several future steps by fusing the guidance from historical workflows and context of the target workflow. Based on the predictions, PBKV estimates the reuse potential of cache entries and keeps the high-potential entries in GPU memory. To be robust to prediction errors, PBKV utilizes the predictions conservatively during both cache eviction and prefetching. Experiments on three workflow benchmarks show that PBKV achieves up to $1.85\times$ speedup over LRU on dynamic workflows, and up to $1.26\times$ speedup over the SOTA baseline KVFlow on the static workflow.

</details>


### [298] [Q-MMR: Off-Policy Evaluation via Recursive Reweighting and Moment Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06474)
*Xiang Li,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-MMR is a per-data-point weighting framework for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs with a dimension-free finite-sample guarantee under Q^π realizability, connecting to importance sampling and linear FQE and offering new insights on coverage in offline RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs is crucial but challenging due to high variance, data scarcity, and weak guarantees. A data-dependent, dimension-free bound under realizability would provide robust evaluation and deepen understanding of coverage in offline RL.

Method: Learn scalar weights for each data point via a top-down moment-matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Reweight rewards with these weights to approximate the target policy's expected return. Theoretical results include a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee under Q^π realizability with a dimension-free bound, and connections to importance sampling and linear FQE; broader analyses illuminate coverage concepts in offline RL.

Result: The work establishes a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee that is dimension-free (independent of function class complexity) under the realizability of Q^π. It also situates Q-MMR relative to importance sampling and linear FQE and provides theoretical insights into the role of coverage in offline RL.

Conclusion: Q-MMR advances off-policy evaluation for finite-horizon MDPs by delivering strong, data-driven guarantees with a scalable, data-dependent approach, clarifying connections to existing methods and offering deeper theoretical understanding of coverage in offline RL.

Abstract: We present a novel theoretical framework, Q-MMR, for off-policy evaluation in finite-horizon MDPs. Q-MMR learns a set of scalar weights, one for each data point, such that the reweighted rewards approximate the expected return under the target policy. The weights are learned inductively in a top-down manner via a moment matching objective against a value-function discriminator class. Notably, and perhaps surprisingly, a data-dependent finite-sample guarantee for general function approximation can be established under only the realizability of $Q^π$, with a dimension-free bound -- that is, the error does not depend on the statistical complexity of the function class. We also establish connections to several existing methods, such as importance sampling and linear FQE. Further theoretical analyses shed new light on the nature of coverage, a concept of fundamental importance to offline RL.

</details>


### [299] [Operator-Guided Invariance Learning for Continuous Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06500)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Fei Xu Yu,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes VPSD-RL, a framework to discover and leverage value-preserving Lie-group structures in continuous-time RL to improve data efficiency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous-time RL is data-intensive and brittle to nuisance variability; existing symmetry methods are narrow; need general nonlinear structure discovery to preserve value.

Method: Model RL as controlled diffusion; define value-preserving mappings via Lie-group actions and pullback operators; exact condition: pullback of value function and pushforward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward; approximate if HJB mismatch small; learn infinitesimal generators by solving determining-equation residuals; exponentiate via ODE flows to finite transformations; apply via transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization.

Result: The framework yields exact/approximate structures; bounded mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value along approximate orbits; empirical gains: improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

Conclusion: VPSD-RL enables discovery of value-preserving structures beyond exact symmetries, improving stability, data efficiency, and robustness; provides a general methodology for continuous RL with Lie-group-based transformations.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) with continuous time and state/action spaces is often data-intensive and brittle under nuisance variability and shift, motivating methods that exploit value-preserving structures to stabilize and improve learning. Most existing approaches focus on special cases, such as prescribed symmetries and exact equivariance, without addressing how to discover more general structures that require nonlinear operators to transform and map between continuous state/action systems with isomorphic value functions. We propose \textbf{VPSD-RL} (Value-Preserving Structure Discovery for Reinforcement Learning). It models continuous RL as a controlled diffusion with value-preserving mappings defined through Lie-group actions and associated pullback operators. We show that a value-preserving structure exists exactly when pulling back the value function and pushing forward actions commute with the controlled generator and reward functional. Further, approximate value-preserving structures with rigorous guarantees can be found when the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman mismatch is small. This framework discovers exact and approximate value-preserving structures by searching for the associated Lie group operators. VPSD-RL fits differentiable drift, diffusion, and reward models; learns infinitesimal generators via determining-equation residual minimization; exponentiates them with ODE flows to obtain finite transformations; and integrates them into continuous RL through transition augmentation and transformation-consistency regularization. We show that bounded generator/reward mismatch implies quantitative stability of the optimal value function along approximate orbits, with sensitivity governed by the effective horizon, and observe improved data efficiency and robustness on continuous-control benchmarks.

</details>


### [300] [Cubit: Token Mixer with Kernel Ridge Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06501)
*Chuanyang Zheng,Jiankai Sun,Yihang Gao,Yuehao Wang,Liangchen Tan,Mac Schwager,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka,Xiaodong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes Transformer attention as Nadaraya-Watson regression and introduces Cubit, a kernel ridge regression (KRR)–based alternative with Limited-Range Rescale to improve long-sequence modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a stronger mathematical foundation for token mixing beyond attention and address long-sequence modeling limitations.

Method: Replace attention with a closed-form KRR solution, aggregating values via kernel similarities and normalizing with the inverse kernel matrix; add Limited-Range Rescale to stabilize training.

Result: Empirical results indicate Cubit achieves stronger long-sequence modeling capability, with performance gains that increase with sequence length.

Conclusion: Cubit offers a theoretically grounded alternative to Transformers with potential advantages for long sequences; KRR-based design may outperform vanilla attention as sequence length grows.

Abstract: Since its introduction in 2017, the Transformer has become one of the most widely adopted architectures in modern deep learning. Despite extensive efforts to improve positional encoding, attention mechanisms, and feed-forward networks, the core token-mixing mechanism in Transformers remains attention. In this work, we show that the attention module in Transformers can be interpreted as performing Nadaraya-Watson regression, where it computes similarities between tokens and aggregates the corresponding values accordingly. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Cubit, a potential next-generation architecture that leverages Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR), while the vanilla Transformer relies on Nadaraya-Watson regression. Specifically, Cubit modifies the classical attention computation by incorporating the closed-form solution of KRR, combining value aggregation through kernel similarities with normalization via the inverse of the kernel matrix. To improve the training stability, we further propose the Limited-Range Rescale (LRR), which rescales the value layer within a controlled range. We argue that Cubit, as a KRR-based architecture, provides a stronger mathematical foundation than the vanilla Transformer, whose attention mechanism corresponds to Nadaraya-Watson regression. We validate this claim through comprehensive experiments. The experimental results suggest that Cubit may exhibit stronger long-sequence modeling capability. In particular, its performance gain over the Transformer appears to increase as the training sequence length grows.

</details>


### [301] [PACZero: PAC-Private Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Sign Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06505)
*Murat Bilgehan Ertan,Xiaochen Zhu,Phuong Ha Nguyen,Marten van Dijk,Srinivas Devadas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A PAC-private zeroth-order mechanism, PACZero, enables usable fine-tuning utility at I(S*; Y)=0, with two variants PACZero-MI and PACZero-ZPL, achieving near non-private performance on SST-2 with OPT models and nontrivial SQuAD results, offering a practical privacy-utility trade-off in high-privacy regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for private fine-tuning of large language models with strong MIA resistance, using PAC privacy to overcome DP limitations in high privacy settings. Leverage sign-quantized subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients to create zero-information release on unanimous steps, enabling usable utility under strict privacy.

Method: Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients; release sign only at steps with unanimity among candidate updates; mutual information cost tied to dependence on secret subset. Two variants: PACZero-MI with budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release; PACZero-ZPL with I=0 via uniform coin flip on disagreement steps. Evaluation on SST-2 and SQuAD using OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B, in both LoRA and full-parameter fine-tuning tracks.

Result: On SST-2 with OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at I=0, PACZero-ZPL attains 88.99±0.91, only 2.1 pp below non-private MeZO baseline (91.1). No prior method achieves usable utility in high-privacy ε<1 regime; PACZero-ZPL shows competitive SST-2 performance and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at I=0.

Conclusion: PACZero provides a practical PAC-private zeroth-order mechanism for LLM fine-tuning, with tunable privacy-utility via MI-budgeting or zero-information requirement. It delivers usable performance in extreme privacy regimes and demonstrates competitive results across model sizes and tasks, suggesting a viable path for MIA-resistant private fine-tuning.

Abstract: We introduce PACZero, a family of PAC-private zeroth-order mechanisms for fine-tuning large language models that delivers usable utility at $I(S^*; Y_{1:T})=0$. This privacy regime bounds the membership-inference attack (MIA) posterior success rate at the prior, an MIA-resistance level the DP framework matches only at $\varepsilon=0$ and infinite noise. All DP-ZO comparisons below are matched at the MIA posterior level. The key insight is that PAC Privacy charges mutual information only when the release depends on which candidate subset is the secret. Sign-quantizing subset-aggregated zeroth-order gradients creates frequent unanimity, steps at which every candidate subset agrees on the update direction; at these steps the released sign costs zero conditional mutual information. We propose two variants that span the privacy-utility trade-off: PACZero-MI (budgeted MI via exact calibration on the binary release) and PACZero-ZPL ($I=0$ via a uniform coin flip on disagreement steps). We evaluate on SST-2 and SQuAD with OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B in both LoRA and full-parameter tracks. On SST-2 OPT-1.3B full fine-tuning at $I=0$, PACZero-ZPL reaches ${88.99\pm0.91}$, within $2.1$pp of the non-private MeZO baseline ($91.1$ FT). No prior method produces usable utility in the high-privacy regime $\varepsilon<1$, and PACZero-ZPL obtains competitive SST-2 accuracy and nontrivial SQuAD F1 across OPT-1.3B and OPT-6.7B at $I=0$.

</details>


### [302] [Is One Layer Enough? Understanding Inference Dynamics in Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06510)
*Amir Rezaei Balef,Mykhailo Koshil,Katharina Eggensperger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based tabular foundation models exhibit depthwise redundancy with iterative refinement during inference; a looped single-layer model using ~20% of the original parameters achieves comparable performance, supported by six state-of-the-art models and publicly released code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the internal inference dynamics of TFMs on tabular data, specifically how predictions emerge across depth, and to identify potential efficiency gains.

Method: Large-scale mechanistic study across 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models to map layerwise dynamics, identify inference stages, and analyze latent-space behavior; followed by design of a proof-of-concept looped single-layer model with substantial parameter reduction; code release.

Result: Discovery of substantial depthwise redundancy and iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference; the looped single-layer model achieves comparable performance using only 20% of the original parameters.

Conclusion: Depthwise redundancy in TFMs enables parameter-efficient inference; a looped single-layer architecture can match performance on tabular tasks, indicating avenues for efficient, scalable tabular foundation models and guiding future mechanistic investigations.

Abstract: Transformer-based tabular foundation models (TFMs) dominate small to medium tabular predictive benchmark tasks, yet their inference mechanisms remain largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale mechanistic study of layerwise dynamics in 6 state-of-the-art tabular in-context learning models. We explore how predictions emerge across depth, identify distinct stages of inference and reveal latent-space dynamics that differ from those of language models. Our findings indicate substantial depthwise redundancy across multiple models, suggesting iterative refinement with overlapping computations during inference stages. Guided by these insights, we design a proof-of-concept, looped single-layer model that uses only 20% of the original model's parameters while achieving comparable performance. The code is available at https://github.com/amirbalef/is_one_layer_enough.

</details>


### [303] [Efficient Techniques for Data Reconstruction, with Finite-Width Recovery Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06519)
*Edward Tansley,Roy Makhlouf,Estelle Massart,Coralia Cartis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified optimization framework is proposed for data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks. The authors prove finite-width, PAC-style recovery guarantees in the random feature model, show that subspace structure lowers the width needed for successful reconstruction, and introduce a subspace-aware reconstruction algorithm that uses changes in first-layer weights to infer the subspace and last-layer weights for reconstruction. Empirical results on synthetic data and CIFAR-10 show the subspace-aware method outperforms full-space approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data reconstruction attacks threaten privacy by recovering training data from neural networks. The work seeks a principled, unified formulation that encompasses existing proposals, provides theoretical guarantees under finite-width regimes, and leverages data subspace structure to improve attack efficiency.

Method: 1) Formulate data reconstruction as a unified optimization problem based on initial and trained parameters. 2) In the random feature model, prove that sufficient network width yields high-probability reconstruction (PAC-style finite-width bounds). 3) Show that if data lie in a low-dimensional subspace, width requirements depend on subspace dimension. 4) Propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm for general nets that approximates the subspace via changes in first-layer weights during training and uses only last-layer weights for reconstruction, reducing search space.

Result: The unified formulation yields provable reconstruction in the random feature model with large width. Subspace structure allows relaxing width requirements in proportion to subspace dimension. The proposed subspace-aware algorithm, which operates on a reduced parameter space (first-layer change for subspace estimation and last-layer weights for reconstruction), outperforms full-space methods on synthetic data and CIFAR-10.

Conclusion: The paper provides a principled framework and practical algorithm for data reconstruction attacks, with both theoretical finite-width guarantees and empirical validation. Exploiting low-dimensional subspaces makes reconstruction more feasible with smaller networks and reduced search space.

Abstract: Data reconstruction attacks on trained neural networks aim to recover the data on which the network has been trained and pose a significant threat to privacy, especially if the training dataset contains sensitive information. Here, we propose a unified optimization formulation of the data reconstruction problem based on initial and trained parameter values, incorporating state-of-the-art proposals. We show that in the random feature model, this formulation provably leads to training data reconstruction with high probability, provided the network width is sufficiently large; this unprecedented finite-width result uses PAC-style bounds. Furthermore, when the data lies in a low-dimensional subspace, we show that the network width requirement for successful reconstruction can be relaxed, with bounds depending on the subspace dimension rather than the ambient dimension. For general neural network models and unknown data orientations, we propose an efficient reconstruction algorithm that approximates the low-dimensional data subspace through the change in the first-layer weights during training and uses only the last-layer weights for reconstruction, thus reducing the search space dimension and the required network width for high-quality reconstructions. Our numerical experiments on synthetic datasets and CIFAR-10 confirm that our subspace-aware reconstruction approach outperforms standard full-space techniques.

</details>


### [304] [On the Implicit Reward Overfitting and the Low-rank Dynamics in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06523)
*Hao Ye,Jisheng Dang,Junfeng Fang,Bimei Wang,Yizhou Zhang,Ning Lv,Wencan Zhang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR's enhanced reasoning primarily emerges from rank-1 components; periodic rank-1 substitution reveals implicit reward overfitting to training data, where test performance can be high even with low training rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how RLVR reshapes model parameters via low-rank (rank-1) structures, and how this affects training dynamics, generalization, and potential continual-learning insights.

Method: Introduce Periodic Rank-1 Substitution (PR1S) and analyze singular value spectra, focusing on left/right singular vectors and their alignment; characterize three RL training properties: (1) effective rank-1 components preserve mainly mathematical reasoning capabilities, (2) optimization of a specific singular spectrum with heavy-tailed layer spectra, (3) stronger alignment of left singular vectors for rank-1 components, implying sampling-efficiency optimization.

Result: Observation of implicit reward overfitting: models perform well on test while training rewards are low; rank-1 components govern reasoning capabilities; RLVR optimizes a particular singular spectrum; most linear layers exhibit heavy-tailed singular value distributions; left singular vectors of rank-1 components show stronger alignment during training.

Conclusion: RLVR shapes model parameters by targeting low-rank spectral structure, with implications for improving RL paradigms and continual learning; findings suggest focusing on spectral properties could enhance generalization and knowledge retention across tasks.
