Abstract: Recent extensive research has demonstrated that the enhanced reasoning capabilities acquired by models through Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) are primarily concentrated within the rank-1 components. Predicated on this observation, we employed Periodic Rank-1 Substitution and identified a counterintuitive phenomenon: RLVR may exhibit implicit reward overfitting to the training dataset. Specifically, the model can achieve satisfactory performance on the test set even when its rewards remain relatively low during the training process. Furthermore, we characterize three distinct properties of RL training: (1) The effective rank-1 component in RLVR don't maintain other model knowledge except mathematical reasoning capability. (2) RLVR fundamentally functions by optimizing a specific singular spectrum. The distribution of singular values of almost all linear layers in RLVR-trained model behaves like heavy-tailed distribution. (3) the left singular vectors associated with rank-1 components demonstrate a stronger alignment tendency during training, which echoes the discovery that RLVR is optimizing sampling efficiency in essence. Taken together, our findings and analysis further reveal how RLVR shapes model parameters and offer potential insights for improving existing RL paradigms or other training paradigms to implement continual learning.

</details>


### [305] [Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling: A Feynman-Kac Analysis of Bias and Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06538)
*Matias G. Delgadino,Sebastien Motsch,Advait Parulekar,William Porteous,Sanjay Shakkottai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tractable surrogate-path framework is introduced to quantify and correct bias in diffusion-based posterior samplers (DPS, STSL). By linking the sampler's density ratio to a parabolic PDE with a reaction term and applying a Feynman-Kac representation, the Radon-Nikodym correction is expressed as an explicit path expectation. The framework yields an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck correction for DPS, reinterprets STSL as a drift steering trajectories to low-uncertainty regions, and analyzes early guidance-stopping as a mitigation for forward-Euler instabilities. This clarifies bias sources, existing corrective mechanisms, and guides stable variant design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Theoretical understanding of diffusion-based posterior samplers is incomplete: even with exact priors, outputs are biased, and low-temperature discretizations can be unstable. A tractable framework is needed to quantify bias, understand where samplers under- or over-sample, and guide robust algorithmic variants.

Method: Introduce a surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian. Analyze the posterior–Gaussian density ratio via a parabolic PDE; interpret the reaction term as accumulated bias. Use a Feynman–Kac representation to convert the Radon–Nikodym correction into an explicit path expectation. Apply the framework to DPS and STSL; reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift; examine early guidance-stopping as a mitigation for forward-Euler instability.

Result: For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling data-conditional covariance with reward curvature, revealing under- or over-sampling regions. STSL is reframed as an auxiliary drift that pushes trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions, flattening the spatial variation of the DPS reaction term. Early guidance-stopping is characterized as a practical mitigation for low-temperature discretization bias. The framework thus identifies bias sources, explains corrective mechanisms, and guides stable design choices.

Conclusion: The proposed surrogate-path, PDE-based analytical framework clarifies where diffusion-based posterior samplers bias samples, reconciles existing corrective ideas, and provides principled guidance for developing more stable variants.

Abstract: Diffusion-based posterior samplers use pretrained diffusion priors to sample from measurement- or reward-conditioned posteriors, and are widely used for inverse problems. Yet their theoretical behavior remains poorly understood: even with exact prior scores, their outputs are biased, and in low-temperature regimes their discretizations can become unstable. We characterize this bias by introducing a tractable surrogate path connecting the true posterior to a standard Gaussian and comparing it to the sampler's path. Their density ratio satisfies a parabolic PDE whose reaction term measures the accumulated bias. A Feynman-Kac representation then expresses the Radon-Nikodym correction as an explicit path expectation, identifying which posterior regions are over- or under-sampled.
  We apply this framework to DPS and STSL, a related sampler. For DPS, the correction is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck path expectation coupling the data conditional covariance with the reward curvature, revealing where DPS over- or under-samples. Next, we reinterpret STSL as an auxiliary drift that steers trajectories toward low-uncertainty regions, flattening the spatially varying part of the DPS reaction term. Finally, we characterize early guidance-stopping, a common mitigation for low-temperature instabilities caused by forward-Euler integration of the vector field. Together, these results clarify sampler bias, explain existing correctives, and guide stable variant designs.

</details>


### [306] [Hedging Memory Horizons for Non-Stationary Prediction via Online Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06541)
*Yutong Wang,Yannig Goude,Qiwei Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MELO: a model-agnostic online forecasting method that hedges adaptation across memory scales using EWLS at multiple forgetting factors, aggregated with a parameter-free online rule (MLpol); shows oracle-like guarantees under distribution shift and strong RMSE improvements on electricity-load forecasting with low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address online prediction under distribution shift where regimes change unpredictably and memory of adaptation is unknown. Need stability in quiet periods and rapid adaptation when regimes shift without relying on regime indicators or policy covariates.

Method: MELO wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with EWLS adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors and aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts using MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule. It yields deterministic oracle inequalities relative to the best raw predictor and the best bounded time-varying affine combination, with a path-length dependent tracking term and sublinear aggregation overhead.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: deterministic oracle inequalities showing competitive performance against both the best raw predictor and the best bounded, time-varying affine combination. Empirical results on French national electricity-load forecasting during COVID-19 lockdown show a 34.7% RMSE reduction relative to base-only MLpol and better performance than a COVID-covariate-enabled TabICL reference; MELO uses lightweight per-step recursive updates without retraining.

Conclusion: MELO provides a model-agnostic, memory-hedged online forecasting framework that robustly adapts to regime shifts with provable guarantees and practical efficiency, delivering substantial RMSE improvements in real-world distribution-shift scenarios.

Abstract: We study online prediction under distribution shift, where inputs arrive chronologically and outcomes are revealed only after prediction. In this setting, predictors must remain stable in quiet regimes yet adapt when regimes shift, and the right adaptation memory is unknown in advance. We propose MELO (Memory-hedged Exponentially Weighted Least-Squares Online aggregation), a model-agnostic method that hedges across adaptation scales: it wraps any non-anticipating base-predictor pool with exponentially weighted least-squares (EWLS) adaptation experts at multiple forgetting factors, and aggregates raw and EWLS-adapted forecasts with MLpol, a parameter-free online aggregation rule. Under boundedness conditions, we establish deterministic oracle inequalities showing that it competes with both the best raw predictor and the best bounded, time-varying affine combinations of the base predictions, up to a path-length-dependent tracking cost and a sublinear aggregation overhead. We evaluate MELO on French national electricity-load forecasting through the COVID-19 lockdown using no regime indicators, lockdown dates, or policy covariates. MELO reduces overall RMSE by 34.7\% relative to base-only MLpol and achieves lower overall RMSE than a TabICL reference supplied with an external COVID policy-response covariate. Moreover, MELO requires only lightweight per-step recursive updates without model retraining.

</details>


### [307] [Sequential Design of Genetic Circuits Under Uncertainty With Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06552)
*Michal Kobiela,Diego A. Oyarzún,Michael U. Gutmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Amortized RL-driven framework for designing genetic circuits under intrinsic stochasticity and cross-lab variability, using simulator-based models (ODEs or Markov jump processes) and upfront training to adapt experiments without online parameter inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty from stochastic biomolecular reactions and varying laboratory conditions hampers genetic circuit design; Bayesian methods, while capable, incur costly iterative inference/optimization after each experiment.

Method: Simulator-based models (differential equations or Markov jump processes) integrated with a policy-based reinforcement learning approach. An amortized training phase across a distribution of uncertain parameters enables immediate, observation-driven adaptation without explicit parameter inference during the design cycle.

Result: Applied to heterologous gene expression and a repressilator circuit, demonstrating efficient handling of molecular noise and cross-lab variability and enabling rapid, adaptive experimental design.

Conclusion: The proposed amortized RL framework allows fast, observation-based design under dual uncertainties, reducing computational bottlenecks and potentially generalizing to broader synthetic biology design problems.

Abstract: The design of biological systems is hindered by uncertainty arising from both intrinsic stochasticity of biomolecular reactions and variability across laboratory or experimental conditions. In this work, we present a sequential framework to optimize genetic circuits under both forms of uncertainty. By employing simulator models based on differential equations or Markov jump processes alongside a reinforcement learning (RL) policy-based approach, our method suggests experiments that adapt to unknown laboratory conditions while accounting for inherent stochasticity. While previous Bayesian methods address uncertainty through iterative experiment-inference-optimization cycles, they typically require computationally expensive inference and optimization steps after each experimental round, leading to delays. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose an amortized approach trained up-front across a distribution of possible uncertain parameters. This strategy sidesteps the need for explicit parameter inference during the design cycle, enabling immediate, observation-based adaptation. We demonstrate our framework on models for heterologous gene expression and a repressilator circuit, showing that it efficiently handles both molecular noise and cross-laboratory variability.

</details>


### [308] [Diverse Sampling in Diffusion Models with Marginal Preserving Particle Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06553)
*Gal Vinograd,Idan Achituve,Ethan Fetaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EDD Y introduces divergence-free, marginal-preserving diversification for diffusion/flow models via kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise drift; yields higher joint diversity with maintained per-particle marginals; includes practical approximations for efficiency and shows improvements on synthetic and text-to-image tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limited diversity in generated samples and high cost of diversity-promoting guidance by exploiting Fokker-Planck symmetries to perturb particle trajectories without altering evolving marginals.

Method: Construct drift perturbations that are divergence-free using kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise fields derived from repulsive directions; preserves marginal distributions, requires no extra training; proposes practical approximations to reduce compute; evaluated on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation.

Result: Increases diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution and maintaining distributional fidelity; outperforms common baselines; approximations provide efficient practical solutions for costly perceptual embeddings.

Conclusion: Divergence-free guidance via EDDY is a principled, efficient approach to diversify samples of diffusion/flow models without sacrificing quality, with demonstrated efficacy and practical approximations for real-world tasks.

Abstract: We present EDDY (Exact-marginal Diversification via Divergence-free dYnamics), a guidance mechanism for diffusion and flow matching models that promotes diversity among samples generated while maintaining quality. EDDY exploits symmetries of the Fokker-Planck equation, using drift perturbations that change particle trajectories while preserving the evolving marginal distribution. We instantiate this principle through kernel-based anti-symmetric pairwise matrix fields, constructed from the repulsive directions. The resulting divergence-free dynamics promote diversity at the joint particle level while preserving each particle's marginal distribution without any additional training. As computing the guidance can be computationally expensive in cases such as text-to-image generation with perceptual embeddings, we propose practical approximations as an effective and efficient solution. Experiments on synthetic distributions and text-to-image generation show that EDDY improves diversity while maintaining strong distributional fidelity compared to common baselines.

</details>


### [309] [Optimal Counterfactual Search in Tree Ensembles: A Study Across Modeling and Solution Paradigms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06561)
*Awa Khouna,Youssouf Emine,Julien Ferry,Thibaut Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyses optimal counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles under plausibility and actionability constraints, comparing CP, MaxSAT, and MILP formulations across datasets; finds CP most versatile, with MaxSAT strong for hard-voting ensembles and MILP competitive in amortized settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual explanations must be minimal and actionable; suboptimal or heuristic errors can mislead users and impose uneven costs. There is a need for exact, scalable search under structural constraints of tree ensembles.

Method: Introduce CPCF: a constraint-programming formulation encoding numerical features as interval domains defined by split thresholds and discrete features as finite domains; enable multiple distance objectives without continuous boundary search. Compare CP with a MaxSAT extension for soft-voting ensembles and with MILP, evaluating scalability, anytime performance, and distance metrics across ten datasets and three tree-ensemble types.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows CP (constraint programming) achieves the best overall performance; Regimes identified where each paradigm excels: CP is most versatile; MaxSAT excels for hard-voting ensembles; MILP remains competitive for amortized inference with a moderate number of split levels.

Conclusion: Choice of optimization formalism should align with ensemble type and task: CP offers broad applicability and strong baseline performance; MaxSAT and MILP have targeted strengths in specific settings.

Abstract: Trust in counterfactual explanations depends critically on whether their recommended changes are truly minimal: suboptimal explanations may vastly overshoot the actual changes needed to alter a decision, and heuristic errors can affect individuals unevenly, giving some users relevant recourse while assigning others unnecessarily costly recommendations. Consequently, we study the problem of computing optimal counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles under plausibility and actionability constraints. This is a combinatorial problem: for a fixed model, counterfactual search boils down to selecting consistent branching decisions and threshold-defined regions under a distance objective. We exploit this structure through CPCF, a constraint programming (CP) formulation in which numerical features are encoded as interval domains induced by split thresholds, while discrete features retain native finite-domain representations. This yields a compact finite-domain formulation that supports multiple distance objectives without continuous split-boundary search. We then place CPCF in a broader comparison across mathematical programming paradigms: we extend a maximum Boolean satisfiability (MaxSAT) formulation, originally designed for hard-voting random forests, to soft-voting ensembles, and compare against the current state-of-the-art mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) optimal approach. Across ten datasets and three types of tree ensembles, we analyze scalability, anytime performance, and sensitivity to distance metrics. We observe that CP achieves the best overall performance. More importantly, our results identify regimes in which the specific strengths of each paradigm make it best suited: CP is most versatile overall, MaxSAT handles hard-voting ensembles particularly well, and MILP remains competitive in amortized inference settings with a moderate number of split levels.

</details>


### [310] [Feature Dimensionality Outweighs Model Complexity in Breast Cancer Subtype Classification Using TCGA-BRCA Gene Expression Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06562)
*Meena Al Hasani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Model complexity and feature selection markedly influence subtype-level performance in high-dimensional gene expression classification; logistic regression yields stable, balanced results across subtypes when using varying numbers of highly variable genes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how model complexity and feature dimensionality affect breast cancer subtype classification from TCGA-BRCA gene expression data in a high-dimensional, limited-sample setting.

Method: Compare logistic regression, random forest, and SVM trained with 50–20,518 highly variable genes. Use stratified 5-fold cross-validation and evaluate with accuracy and macro F1 score.

Result: All models attain high accuracy; macro F1 reveals subtype-level differences. Logistic regression is the most stable and balanced across subtypes, including rare classes. Random forest underperforms on minority subtypes despite high overall accuracy. SVM performance is sensitive to the number of features.

Conclusion: Model simplicity, appropriate evaluation metrics (macro F1), and careful feature selection are crucial for effective high-dimensional biological classification.

Abstract: Accurate classification of breast cancer subtypes from gene expression data is critical for diagnosis and treatment selection. However, such datasets are characterized by high dimensionality and limited sample size, posing challenges for machine learning models.
  In this study, we evaluate the impact of model complexity and feature selection on subtype classification performance using TCGA-BRCA gene expression data. Logistic regression, random forest, and support vector machine (SVM) models were trained using varying numbers of highly variable genes (50 to 20,518). Performance was evaluated using stratified 5-fold cross-validation and assessed with accuracy and macro F1 score. While all models achieved high accuracy, macro F1 analysis revealed substantial differences in subtype-level performance. Logistic regression demonstrated the most stable and balanced performance across subtypes, including improved detection of rare classes. Random forest underperformed on minority subtypes despite strong overall accuracy, while SVM showed sensitivity to feature dimensionality. These findings highlight the importance of model simplicity, evaluation metrics, and feature selection in high-dimensional biological classification tasks.

</details>


### [311] [Criticality and Saturation in Orthogonal Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06563)
*Max Guillen,Jan E. Gerken*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Derives explicit layer-wise finite-width recursion relations for networks with orthogonal initializations, extends i.i.d.-case Feynman diagram methods to all orders in 1/width, and explains stability of finite-width tensors at large depth, with numerical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why orthogonal weights stabilize training compared to i.i.d. Gaussian, via finite-width corrections and depth-stability observed by Day et al.

Method: Derive layer-wise recursions for finite-width tensors under orthogonal initialization; extend diagrammatic perturbation/recursion framework to all orders in 1/width; perform large-depth expansions; compare with Monte Carlo network ensembles.

Result: Recursions reproduce observed stability for activations with vanishing fixed points; numerical solutions and large-depth expansions match Monte Carlo estimates.

Conclusion: Provides theoretical explanation for stability of nonlinear finite-width networks with orthogonal initialization, filling a gap, and offers tools (recursions and diagrams) for all-orders finite-width analysis.

Abstract: It has been known for a long time that initializing weight matrices to be orthogonal instead of having i.i.d. Gaussian components can improve training performance. This phenomenon can be analyzed using finite-width corrections, where the infinite-width statistics are supplemented by a power series in $1/\mathrm{width}$. In particular, recent empirical results by Day et al. show that the tensors appearing in this treatment stabilize for large depth, as opposed to the tensors of i.i.d.-initialized networks. In this article, we derive explicit layer-wise recursion relations for the tensors appearing in the finite-width expansion of the network statistics in the case of orthogonal initializations. We also provide an extension of recently-introduced Feynman diagrams for the corresponding recursions in the i.i.d.-case which are valid to all orders in $1/\mathrm{width}$. Finally, we show explicitly that the recursions we derive reproduce the stability of the finite-width tensors which was observed for activation functions with vanishing fixed point. This work therefore provides a theoretical explanation for the stability of nonlinear networks of finite width initialized with orthogonal weights, closing a long-standing gap in the literature. We validate our theoretical results experimentally by showing that numerical solutions of our recursion relations and their analytical large-depth expansions agree excellently with Monte-Carlo estimates from network ensembles.

</details>


### [312] [SNAPO: Smooth Neural Adjoint Policy Optimization for Optimal Control via Differentiable Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06570)
*Dmitri Goloubentsev,Natalija Karpichina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SNAPO embeds a neural policy inside a differentiable simulator and uses a single adjoint backward pass to compute exact gradients for all policy parameters and inputs, enabling fast training and scalable sensitivities across domains such as gas storage, pension ALM, and pharmaceutical manufacturing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off between exact dynamic-programming solutions (high accuracy but exponential scaling) and black-box RL (scalable but lacks sensitivities). Provide exact gradient information w.r.t. policy and inputs to enable efficient optimization and sensitivity analysis.

Method: Embed a neural policy in a known differentiable simulator; replace hard constraints with smooth relaxations; compute exact gradients of the objective with respect to policy parameters and all inputs via a single adjoint (backward) pass; train policy while simultaneously obtaining sensitivities.

Result: Empirical demonstrations across three domains achieving: natural gas storage training under 1 minute with 365 forward-curve sensitivities at no extra cost per sensitivity; pension ALM achieving 6.5x–200x sensitivity speedups over bump-and-revalue, scalability with risk factors; pharmaceutical manufacturing obtaining cross-unit sensitivities through a 4-unit chain and 20 regulatory sensitivities from 5 adjoint passes in 74.5 ms. All sensitivities produced by the same backward pass; cost scales with a single reverse pass.

Conclusion: SNAPO delivers efficient, end-to-end differentiable sequential decision-making with exact gradient information, enabling fast optimization and rich sensitivity analyses. The approach hinges on differentiable simulators and smooth constraint relaxations; promising broad applicability but dependent on simulator differentiability and adjoint efficiency.

Abstract: Many real-world problems require sequential decisions under uncertainty: when to inject or withdraw gas from storage, how to rebalance a pension portfolio each month, what temperature profile to run through a pharmaceutical reactor chain. Dynamic programming solves small instances exactly but scales exponentially in state dimensions. Black-box reinforcement learning handles high-dimensional states but trains slowly and produces no sensitivities.
  We introduce SNAPO (Smooth Neural Adjoint Policy Optimization), a framework that embeds a neural policy inside a known, differentiable simulator, replaces hard constraints with smooth approximations, and computes exact gradients of the objective with respect to all policy parameters and all inputs in a single adjoint pass.
  We demonstrate SNAPO on three domains: natural gas storage (training in under a minute, 365 forward curve sensitivities at no additional cost per sensitivity), pension fund asset-liability management (6.5x-200x sensitivity speedup over bump-and-revalue, scaling with the number of risk factors), and pharmaceutical manufacturing (cross-unit sensitivities through a 4-unit process chain, with 20 ICH Q8 regulatory sensitivities from 5 adjoint passes in 74.5 milliseconds).
  All sensitivities are produced by the same backward pass that trains the policy, at a cost proportional to one reverse pass regardless of how many sensitivities are computed.

</details>


### [313] [CLAD: A Clustered Label-Agnostic Federated Learning Framework for Joint Anomaly Detection and Attack Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06571)
*Iason Ofeidis,Nikos Papadis,Randeep Bhatia,Leandros Tassiulas,TV Lakshman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified clustered federated learning framework (CLAD) with a dual-mode micro-architecture (DM^2A) for IoT/IIoT intrusion detection, combining unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised attack classification with dynamic device clustering to address heterogeneity and label scarcity; achieves ~30% relative detection gains with 80% unlabeled clients and half the communication cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT/IIoT introduce heterogeneous traffic and limited labeled data, hindering traditional FL-based IDS; need to leverage unlabeled data and manage heterogeneity while controlling communication.

Method: DM^2A: a shared encoder feeding two branches for unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised classification; CFL dynamically clusters devices with congruent traffic patterns to align local updates; integration ensures no data discarded; evaluation on realistic IoT/IIoT scenarios compare to SOTA baselines.

Result: Significant improvement over baselines; 30% relative gain in detection performance with 80% unlabeled clients; communication cost reduced by 50%; robust to device heterogeneity and label scarcity.

Conclusion: The CLAD framework effectively combines CFL and DM^2A to provide data-efficient, scalable intrusion detection for heterogeneous IoT/IIoT environments, preserving privacy while improving detection.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of the Internet of Things (IoT) and Industrial IoT (IIoT) has created a massive, heterogeneous attack surface that challenges traditional network security mechanisms. While Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative to centralized Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS), standard approaches struggle to generalize across diverse device behaviors and typically fail to utilize the vast amounts of unlabeled data present in realistic edge environments. To bridge these gaps, we propose CLAD, a holistic framework that seamlessly incorporates Clustered Federated Learning (CFL) with a novel Dual-Mode Micro-Architecture ($\text{DM}^2\text{A}$). This unified approach simultaneously tackles the two primary bottlenecks of IoT security: device heterogeneity and label scarcity. The $\text{DM}^2\text{A}$ component features a shared encoder followed by two branches, enabling joint unsupervised anomaly detection and supervised attack classification; this allows the framework to harvest intelligence from both labeled and unlabeled clients. Concurrently, the clustering component dynamically groups devices with congruent traffic patterns, preventing global model divergence. By carefully combining these elements, CLAD ensures that no data is discarded and distinct operational patterns are preserved. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that this integrated approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 30% relative improvement in detection performance in scenarios with 80% unlabeled clients, with only half the communication cost.

</details>


### [314] [Directional Consistency as a Complementary Optimization Signal: The GONO Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06575)
*Victor Daniel Gera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Directional alignment and loss convergence can be decoupled; the paper introduces GONO, a gradient-direction-aware optimizer that adapts momentum based on directional consistency (cc_t). It matches Adam's convergence rate and is competitive with AdamW on standard benchmarks, with clear oscillation detection using cc_t.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing optimizers rely on magnitude-based signals and struggle to distinguish plateaus, saddles, and true convergence. The authors propose to leverage temporal directional consistency of gradients to improve optimization efficiency and robustness.

Method: Define cc_t as the cosine similarity between consecutive gradients. Develop GONO by adapting Adam's momentum coefficient beta_1 according to cc_t: amplify momentum when directional consistency is high, suppress it when oscillations are detected. Prove that GONO achieves the same O(1/√T) convergence rate as Adam and reduces to Adam when the signal is uninformative. Provide empirical evaluation on standard benchmarks and release code.

Result: The method yields oscillation detection with cc_t achieving F1=1.00 (vs. 0.45 for gradient norm). GONO is competitive with AdamW on MNIST (98.15%), CIFAR-10 (43.14%), and ResNet-18 (75.44%). The code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Directional alignment is a theoretically grounded and practically actionable optimization signal. GONO offers a principled way to exploit gradient direction stability, preserving Adam’s convergence guarantees while adapting to temporal consistency; it collapses to Adam when the directional signal is uninformative.

Abstract: We identify and formalize an underexplored phenomenon in deep learning optimization: directional alignment and loss convergence can be decoupled. An optimizer can exhibit near-perfect directional consistency (cc_t -> 1, measured via consecutive gradient cosine similarity) while the loss remains high or decreases slowly. This observation reveals that existing optimizers such as Adam, SGD, and RMSprop lack explicit mechanisms to exploit temporal consistency in gradient directions, relying instead on magnitude-based signals that fail to distinguish plateaus, saddle points, and genuine convergence. Motivated by this, we introduce GONO (Gradient-Oriented Norm-Adaptive Optimizer), which adapts Adam's momentum coefficient beta_1 based on cc_t: amplifying momentum under directional consistency and suppressing it during oscillation. We prove GONO matches Adam's O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence rate and reduces exactly to Adam when the signal is uninformative. Empirically, cc_t achieves oscillation detection with F1=1.00 (vs. 0.45 for gradient norm), and GONO remains competitive with AdamW on MNIST (98.15%), CIFAR-10 (43.14%), and ResNet-18 (75.44%), establishing directional alignment as a theoretically grounded, practically actionable optimization signal. Code: https://github.com/victordaniel/gono-optimizer

</details>


### [315] [On the Safety of Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06576)
*Xiaoguang Guo,Zehong Wang,Ziming Li,Shawn Spitzel,Soonwoo Kwon,Tianyi Ma,Yanfang Ye,Chuxu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRL-Safety is a multi-axis benchmark evaluating 12 GRL methods across 25 datasets on five safety axes (corruption robustness, OOD generalization, class imbalance, fairness, interpretation) with per-axis reporting; shows safety depends on representation- stress interaction, axis-specific strengths of foundation-era methods, and deployment gaps remain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the reliability of graph representation learning under deployment stresses not captured by clean transfer, addressing robustness, generalization, fairness, and interpretability across varied graph factors.

Method: Introduce GRL-Safety benchmark; standardized evaluation protocol; 12 representative methods spanning topology-only embeddings, supervised GNNs, self-supervised models, GFMs; 25 graph datasets; five safety axes; per-axis/sub-condition reporting.

Result: Three cross-axis insights: (1) safety behavior is driven by interaction between representation design and stressed graph factor, not by method family; (2) foundation-era methods exhibit axis-specific strengths rather than universal safety dominance; (3) several deployment regimes remain difficult even for top methods, indicating gaps requiring new robustness/adaptation/training objectives; code released at provided URL.

Conclusion: GRL-Safety provides a granular, multi-axis safety evaluation framework and its findings guide future research toward axis-aware design, targeted robustness, and training objectives beyond model selection.

Abstract: Graph representation learning (GRL) has evolved from topology-only graph embeddings to task-specific supervised GNNs, and more recently to reusable representations and graph foundation models (GFMs). However, existing evaluations mainly measure clean transfer, adaptation, and task coverage. It remains unclear whether GRL methods stay reliable when deployment stresses affect graph signals, graph contexts, label support, structural groups, or predictive evidence. We introduce GRL-Safety, a multi-axis safety evaluation benchmark for GRL. GRL-Safety evaluates twelve representative methods, spanning topology-only embedding methods, supervised GNNs, self-supervised graph models, and GFMs, on twenty-five graph datasets under standardized evaluation conditions while preserving method-native adaptation. The evaluation covers five safety axes: corruption robustness, OOD generalization, class imbalance, fairness, and interpretation, with per-axis and sub-condition reporting rather than a single aggregate score. Our analysis yields three cross-axis insights that can inspire future research. First, safety behavior is shaped by the interaction between representation design and the stressed graph factor, rather than by method family alone. Second, foundation-era methods show axis-specific strengths rather than broad safety dominance. Third, several deployment regimes remain difficult even for the best evaluated method, revealing capability gaps that require new robustness, adaptation, or training objectives beyond model selection. The benchmark, evaluation protocols, and code are available at: https://github.com/GXG-CS/GRL-Safety.

</details>


### [316] [PairAlign: A Framework for Sequence Tokenization via Self-Alignment with Applications to Audio Tokenization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06582)
*Adhiraj Banerjee,Vipul Arora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PairAlign proposes a sequence-level, self-aligned audio tokenizer that learns compact, variable-length symbolic sequences from two content-preserving views. It optimizes cross-view likelihoods to preserve edit-distance-like properties while avoiding collapse, yielding substantial token reductions (e.g., 55% on TIMIT) and strong length control compared to local, quantization-based tokenizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete symbolic representations enable reliable operations (compare, store, retrieve, reason) on sensory data. Existing audio tokenizers optimize local reconstruction or clustering, neglecting sequence-level objectives like edit-distance, length control, and termination. PairAlign introduces a cross-view, sequence-level objective to learn token identities, order, and EOS placement.

Method: Encoder produces a continuous conditioning; autoregressive decoder emits tokens from BOS. Two content-preserving views are trained so that each view is likely under the representation of the other, using an EMA-teacher, cross-paired teacher forcing, prefix corruption, likelihood contrast, and explicit length control. Starts from VQ-style tokenization and refines it through these objectives, aiming to preserve sequence structure and discourage many-to-one collapse.

Result: On 3-second speech, PairAlign yields compact, non-degenerate token sequences with broad vocabulary usage and cross-view consistency. In TIMIT retrieval, it preserves edit-distance search while reducing archive token count by 55%. A continuous-sweep probe shows lower local overlap than dense geometric tokenizers, with improved length control and bounded edit trajectories for shifts under 100 ms.

Conclusion: PairAlign functions as a sequence-symbolic predictive learner (in the spirit of JEPA) that predicts a learned, variable-length symbolic sequence from another view rather than a continuous latent, enabling better sequence-level alignment and token efficiency.

Abstract: Many operations on sensory data -- comparison, memory, retrieval, and reasoning -- are naturally expressed over discrete symbolic structures. In language this interface is given by tokens; in audio, it must be learned. Existing audio tokenizers rely on quantization, clustering, or codec reconstruction, assigning tokens locally, so sequence consistency, compactness, length control, termination, and edit similarity are rarely optimized directly.
  We introduce PairAlign, a framework for compact audio tokenization through sequence-level self-alignment. PairAlign treats tokenization as conditional sequence generation: an encoder maps speech to a continuous condition, and an autoregressive decoder generates tokens from BOS, learning token identity, order, length, and EOS placement. Given two content-preserving views, each view's sequence is trained to be likely under the other's representation, while unrelated examples provide competing sequences. This gives a scalable surrogate for edit-distance preservation while discouraging many-to-one collapse.
  PairAlign starts from VQ-style tokenization and refines it with EMA-teacher targets, cross-paired teacher forcing, prefix corruption, likelihood contrast, and length control.
  On 3-second speech, PairAlign learns compact, non-degenerate sequences with broad vocabulary usage and strong cross-view consistency. On TIMIT retrieval, it preserves edit-distance search while reducing archive token count by 55%. A continuous-sweep probe shows lower local overlap than a dense geometric tokenizer, but stronger length control and bounded edit trajectories under 100 ms shifts. PairAlign is a sequence-symbolic predictive learner: like JEPA-style objectives, it predicts an abstract target from another view as a learned variable-length symbolic sequence, not a continuous latent.

</details>


### [317] [Distributionally-Robust Learning to Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06585)
*Vinit Ranjan,Jisun Park,Bartolomeo Stellato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A distributionally robust learning-to-optimize framework for first-order methods that unifies L2O and worst-case PEP, solved via SGD differentiating through an inner SDP, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address generalization and robustness in learned optimization, providing certifiable performance bounds across problem distributions and bridging the L2O and PEP paradigms.

Method: Formulate a Wasserstein distributionally robust version of the Performance Estimation Problem (PEP) over algorithm parameters (e.g., step sizes). The outer objective optimizes the worst-case expected performance under a Wasserstein ambiguity set; the inner SDP encodes PEP constraints and is differentiated through during SGD to update the algorithm parameters.

Result: Theory: high-probability risk bounds show the true risk of the learned algorithm is at most the in-sample L2O optimum plus a shrinking slack and no worse than the worst-case PEP bound. Empirically, on unconstrained quadratic minimization, LASSO, and linear programming, the learned algorithms achieve strong out-of-sample performance with certifiable robustness, outperforming both worst-case optimal and vanilla L2O baselines.

Conclusion: The framework creates a continuum between L2O and PEP, delivering robust learned optimizers with provable guarantees and improved empirical performance.

Abstract: We propose a distributionally robust approach to learning hyperparameters for first-order methods in convex optimization. Given a dataset of problem instances, we minimize a Wasserstein distributionally robust version of the performance estimation problem (PEP) over algorithm parameters such as step sizes. Our framework unifies two extremes: as the robustness radius vanishes, we recover classical learning to optimize (L2O); as it grows, we recover worst-case optimal algorithm design via PEP. We solve the resulting problem with stochastic gradient descent, differentiating through the solution of an inner semidefinite program at each step. We prove high-probability bounds showing that the true risk of the learned algorithm is at most the in-sample L2O optimum plus a slack that shrinks with the sample size, and is no worse than the worst-case PEP bound. On unconstrained quadratic minimization, LASSO, and linear programming benchmarks, our learned algorithms achieve strong out-of-sample performance with certifiable robustness, outperforming both worst-case optimal and vanilla L2O baselines.

</details>


### [318] [Towards Metric-Faithful Neural Graph Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06588)
*Jyotirmaya Shivottam,Subhankar Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a geometry-aware framework for neural GED, linking encoder bi-Lipschitz geometry to GED estimation quality; demonstrates improvements by using FSW-GNN and confirms via ablations and transfer experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite progress in neural GED, the role of encoder geometry in GED estimation remains unclear. Understanding and leveraging encoder geometry could improve accuracy, stability, and transferability of neural graph matching methods.

Method: Theoretical framework for two neural GED estimator classes (graph-similarity predictors and alignment-based methods). On fixed graphs where the doubly-stochastic metric d_DS approximates GED, graph-level bi-Lipschitz encoders yield controlled GED surrogates and enhanced ranking stability; for matching-based estimators, node-level bi-Lipschitz geometry propagates to encoder-induced alignment costs and the optimization objective. Instantiates the framework with FSW-GNN, a bi-Lipschitz WL-equivalent encoder, as a drop-in replacement in representative neural GED architectures. Empirical evaluation across baselines and datasets, faithfulness study of untrained encoders, ablations, and transfer experiments.

Result: Geometry-aware variants significantly improve GED prediction and ranking metrics across baselines and benchmarks. The benefits are attributed to improved representation geometry, with untrained-encoder analyses and transfers corroborating the geometry-based rationale.

Conclusion: Encoder geometry is a practical design principle for neural graph matching. Bi-Lipschitz geometric properties of encoders can enhance neural GED methods, guiding future architecture design and evaluation in graph matching tasks.

Abstract: Graph Edit Distance (GED) is a fundamental, albeit NP-hard, metric for structural graph similarity. Recent neural graph matching architectures approximate GED by first encoding graphs with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) and then applying either a graph-level regression head or a matching-based alignment module. Despite substantial architectural progress, the role of encoder geometry in neural GED estimation remains poorly understood. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework that connects encoder geometry to GED estimation quality for two broad classes of neural GED estimators: graph similarity predictors and alignment-based methods. On fixed graph collections, where the doubly-stochastic metric $d_{\mathrm{DS}}$ is comparable to GED, we show that graph-level bi-Lipschitz encoders yield controlled GED surrogates and improved ranking stability; for matching-based estimators, node-level bi-Lipschitz geometry propagates to encoder-induced alignment costs and the resulting optimized alignment objective. We instantiate this perspective using FSW-GNN, a bi-Lipschitz WL-equivalent encoder, as a drop-in replacement in representative neural GED architectures. Across representative baselines and benchmark datasets, the resulting geometry-aware variants significantly improve GED prediction and ranking metrics. A faithfulness case study of untrained encoders, together with ablations and transfer experiments, supports the view that these gains arise from improved representation geometry, positioning encoder geometry as a useful design principle for neural graph matching.

</details>


### [319] [BRICKS: Compositional Neural Markov Kernels for Zero-Shot Radiation-Matter Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06591)
*Richard Hildebrandt,Evangelos Kourlitis,Baran Hashemi,Manuel Bünstorf,Thierry Meyer,Nikola Boskov,Michael Kagan,Dan Rosenbaum,Sanmay Ganguly,Lukas Heinrich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A differentiable, compositional neural surrogate for radiation–matter interactions using a next-particle predictor with hybrid discrete-continuous transformers on product manifolds, enabling zero-shot large-scale simulations and GPU speedups, with autoregressive stability and a 20M-event dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radiation-matter interactions are central across physics and engineering, but mechanistic simulators are costly and non-differentiable. A differentiable, scalable, compositional surrogate with tractable likelihoods would enable faster simulations and downstream inference.

Method: Introduce a next-particle prediction kernel built from hybrid discrete-continuous transformer models based on Riemannian Flow Matching on product manifolds. The model outputs variable-sized typed particle sets and radiation side effects, and the kernel can be composed to simulate unseen large-scale material distributions in a zero-shot manner. The approach is differentiable with tractable likelihoods.

Result: Demonstrates a significant GPU speed-up for single-kernel execution versus CPU-bound mechanistic simulators. Evaluated at the kernel level with predictive stability across multi-round autoregressive rollouts. Also releases a 20M-event radiation-matter interaction dataset for further research.

Conclusion: The paper presents a differentiable, compositional surrogate capable of zero-shot extrapolation to unseen large-scale materials, enabling faster simulations and enabling downstream tasks; the accompanying 20M-event dataset provides a valuable resource for the community.

Abstract: We introduce a new strategy for compositional neural surrogates for radiation-matter interactions, a key task spanning domains from particle physics through nuclear and space engineering to medical physics. Exploiting the locality and the Markov nature of particle interactions, we create a \emph{next-particle prediction} kernel using hybrid discrete-continuous transformer models based on Riemannian Flow Matching on product manifolds. The model generates variable-sized typed sets of particles and radiation side effects that are the result of the interaction of an incident particle with a material volume. The resulting kernel can be composed to simulate unseen large-scale material distributions in a zero-shot manner. Unlike mechanistic simulators, our model is designed to be differentiable, provides tractable likelihoods for future downstream applications. A significant computational speed-up on GPU compared to CPU-bound mechanistic simulation is observed for single-kernel execution. We evaluate the model at the kernel level and demonstrate predictive stability over multi-round autoregressive rollouts. We additionally release a novel 20M-event radiation-matter interaction dataset for further research.

</details>


### [320] [Weight-Decay Turns Transformer Loss Landscapes Villani: Functional-Analytic Foundations for Optimization and Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06599)
*Abhijit Das,Sayantan Dutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First rigorous functional-analytic characterization of a weight-decayed Transformer objective (cross-entropy with L2 regularization) showing coercive energy properties and explicit log-Sobolev/Poincaré bounds; introduces a scalable Villani diagnostic and validates with large-model experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the mathematical role of weight decay in Transformer loss landscapes and establish conditions for fast Langevin mixing and sharp generalization guarantees.

Method: Prove that the regularized loss F satisfies Villani's coercivity criteria: smoothness, quadratic growth, Gaussian tails, and the differential growth condition -ΔF + (1/s)||∇F||^2 → ∞. Derive explicit C_LS bounds; relate to regularization λ and model dimension d. Introduce Ψ_s(θ) = -ΔF + (1/s)||∇F||^2 and estimate via Hutchinson probes. Empirically validate on GPT-Neo-125M across PTB and WikiText-103.

Result: Functional-analytic framework established; finite-time convergence guarantees for noisy SGD; PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds that improve with larger λ; a scalable diagnostic Ψ_s validated; observed quadratic growth of Ψ_s and Hessian spectral inflation; exponential convergence behavior consistent with log-Sobolev analysis.

Conclusion: Weight decay provides not only empirical generalization benefits but also a rigorous foundation for fast Langevin mixing and curvature-aware optimization in deep learning, via coercive energy structure and explicit functional-analytic constants.

Abstract: Weight decay is widely used as a regularizer in large language models, yet its precise role in shaping Transformer loss landscapes remains theoretically underexplored. This paper provides the first rigorous functional-analytic characterization of the standard Transformer objective--cross-entropy loss with $L^2$ regularization--by proving it satisfies Villani's criteria for coercive energy functions. Specifically, we show that the regularized loss $\mathcal{F}$ is infinitely differentiable, grows at least quadratically, has Gaussian-integrable tails, and satisfies the differential growth condition $-Δ\mathcal{F} + \tfrac{1}{s}\|\nabla\mathcal{F}\|^{2} \to \infty$ as $\|θ\| \to \infty$ for all $s>0$. From this structure, we derive explicit log-Sobolev and Poincaré constants $C_{\mathrm{LS}} \leq λ^{-1} + d/λ^{2}$, linking the regularization strength $λ$ and model dimension $d$ to finite-time convergence guarantees for noisy stochastic gradient descent and PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds that tighten with increasing $λ$. To validate our theory, we introduce a scalable Villani diagnostic $Ψ_s(θ) = -Δ\mathcal{F} + s^{-1}\|\nabla \mathcal{F}\|^2$ and estimate it efficiently using Hutchinson trace probes in models with over 100M parameters. Experiments on GPT-Neo-125M across Penn Treebank and WikiText-103 confirm the predicted quadratic growth of $Ψ_s$, spectral inflation of the Hessian, and exponential convergence behavior consistent with our log-Sobolev analysis. These results demonstrate that weight decay not only improves generalization empirically but also establishes the mathematical conditions required for fast Langevin mixing and theoretically grounded curvature-aware optimization in deep learning.

</details>


### [321] [How Many Iterations to Jailbreak? Dynamic Budget Allocation for Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06605)
*Shai Feldman,Yaniv Romano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces DAPRO, a dynamic budget allocation framework for time-to-event analysis in multi-turn LLM interactions that provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees and tighter bounds than prior static conformal survival methods; enables unbiased evaluation of population metrics like jailbreak rate under limited compute; demonstrated on Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5 across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate and predict LLM performance in multi-turn conversations under limited compute, where key events (e.g., jailbreaks, task success) are rare and may not be observed within a fixed budget. Prior conformal survival methods rely on static budgets and conditional independence assumptions, leading to inefficiency.

Method: Propose Dynamic Allocation via PRojected Optimization (DAPRO), a theoretically valid dynamic budget allocation scheme for bounding time-to-event in multi-turn interactions. Proves budget satisfaction and distribution-free, finite-sample coverage without requiring conditional independence between censoring and event times. Introduces a new coverage bound that scales with the square root of the mean censoring weight (instead of the worst-case weight), yielding tighter guarantees. Enables unbiased, low-variance population metric estimation under limited resources.

Result: Empirically, DAPRO achieves coverage closer to nominal with lower variance than static baselines while satisfying the budget constraint across scenarios including agentic task success, jailbreaks, toxic content generation, and RAG hallucinations, tested on Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5.

Conclusion: DAPRO is the first theoretically valid dynamic budgeting framework for time-to-event bounding in multi-turn LLM settings, delivering distribution-free, finite-sample guarantees with potentially tighter bounds and enabling unbiased population metric estimation under limited compute.

Abstract: Evaluating and predicting the performance of large language models (LLMs) in multi-turn conversational settings is critical yet computationally expensive; key events -- e.g., jailbreaks or successful task completion by an agent -- often emerge only after repeated interactions. These events might be rare, and under any feasible computational budget, remain unobserved.
  Recent conformal survival frameworks construct reliable lower predictive bounds (LPBs) on the number of iterations to trigger the event of interest, but rely on static budget allocation that is inefficient in multi-turn setups. To address this, we introduce \emph{Dynamic Allocation via PRojected Optimization} (DAPRO), the first theoretically valid dynamic budget allocation framework for bounding the time-to-event in multi-turn LLM interactions.
  We prove that DAPRO satisfies the budget constraint and provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees without requiring the conditional independence between censoring and event times assumed by prior conformal survival approaches.
  A key theoretical contribution is a novel coverage bound that scales with the square root of the mean censoring weight rather than the worst-case weight, yielding provably tighter guarantees than prior work. Furthermore, DAPRO can be employed to obtain unbiased, low-variance estimates of population-level evaluation metrics, such as the jailbreak rate, under limited computing resources.
  Comprehensive experiments across agentic task success, adversarial jailbreaks, toxic content generation, and RAG hallucinations using LLMs such as Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5 demonstrate that DAPRO consistently achieves coverage closer to the nominal level with lower variance than static baselines, while satisfying the budget constraint.

</details>


### [322] [Transformers Efficiently Perform In-Context Logistic Regression via Normalized Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06609)
*Chenyang Zhang,Yuan Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Softmax transformers can act as in-context learners by implementing gradient-descent steps for in-context logistic regression; a multi-layer setup yields per-layer descent steps, and a trained self-attention layer can be recurrently applied to form a looped model, with convergence and OOD guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge theory and practice of in-context learning by showing how standard transformer attention can internally perform optimization-like computations on context, clarifying mechanism behind ICL.

Method: 1) Construct multi-layer transformer where each layer executes one step of normalized gradient descent on an in-context loss to perform in-context logistic regression. 2) Show equivalence by training a single self-attention layer supervised by one-step gradient descent and repeatedly applying it to create a looped (recurrent) model. 3) Prove training convergence of the self-attention layer and out-of-distribution generalization of the looped model.

Result: The paper demonstrates that softmax transformers can perform in-context logistic regression via gradient-descent-like steps; the looped model generalizes OOD and the training converges under stated conditions.

Conclusion: This work advances theoretical understanding of ICL by confirming that standard transformers can serve as in-context learners through implicit optimization on the context, strengthening the interpretation of ICL as algorithm execution rather than mere pattern matching.

Abstract: Transformers have demonstrated remarkable in-context learning (ICL) capabilities. The strong ICL performance of transformers is commonly believed to arise from their ability to implicitly execute certain algorithms on the context, thereby enhancing prediction and generation. In this work, we investigate how transformers with softmax attention perform in-context learning on linear classification data. We first construct a class of multi-layer transformers that can perform in-context logistic regression, with each layer exactly performing one step of normalized gradient descent on an in-context loss. Then, we show that our constructed transformer can be obtained through (i) training a single self-attention layer supervised by one-step gradient descent, and (ii) recurrently applying the trained layer to obtain a looped model. Training convergence guarantees of the self-attention layer and out-of-distribution generalization guarantees of the looped model are provided. Our results advance the theoretical understanding of ICL mechanism by showcasing how softmax transformers can effectively act as in-context learners.

</details>


### [323] [The Structural Origin of Attention Sink: Variance Discrepancy, Super Neurons, and Dimension Disparity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06611)
*Siquan Li,Kaiqi Jiang,Jiacheng Sun,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explains attention sink in LLMs as arising from value aggregation in self-attention, amplified by FFN activations—especially channel-sparse down-projections that create first-token disparity—validated through two input-control interventions, and mitigated by a proposed head-wise RMSNorm that stabilizes aggregation and speeds pre-training convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain the structural origins of the attention sink phenomenon to enable systematic control and improved training dynamics in LLMs.

Method: The authors perform a mechanistic analysis of the value-aggregation step in self-attention, identify a variance discrepancy in token representations, and show that FFN activations amplify this via super neurons. They validate causality with two interventions: (i) masking to isolate aggregation effects, and (ii) increasing variance of targeted token representations. They then propose head-wise RMSNorm to stabilize value aggregation outputs and test its effect on pre-training convergence.

Result: They demonstrate that variance discrepancy stems from self-attention value aggregation and is amplified by FFN-induced super neurons; channel-sparse down-projections cause first-token dimension disparity leading to attention sinks; the two interventions reproduce sinks at arbitrary positions; head-wise RMSNorm stabilizes outputs, restoring parity across positions and accelerating convergence during pre-training.

Conclusion: The work provides a concrete mechanistic account for attention sinks and offers a practical normalization-based approach (head-wise RMSNorm) to control sink formation and improve training efficiency.

Abstract: Despite the prevalence of the attention sink phenomenon in Large Language Models (LLMs), where initial tokens disproportionately monopolize attention scores, its structural origins remain elusive. This work provides a \textit{mechanistic explanation} for this phenomenon. First, we trace its root to the value aggregation process inherent in self-attention, which induces a systematic variance discrepancy. We further demonstrate that this discrepancy is drastically amplified by the activation of super neurons within Feed-Forward Network (FFN) layers. Specifically, the channel-sparse down-projections trigger a dimension disparity of the first-token representation, necessitating the formation of attention sinks as a structural anchor. Then, we validate this causal chain through two controlled interventions: (i) isolating the aggregation effect via attention mask modifications and (ii) amplifying the variance of targeted token representations. Both interventions can replicate attention sinks at arbitrary positions. Our mechanistic understanding offers a foundation for the systematic control of sink formation. Finally, as a proof of concept, we propose \textit{head-wise RMSNorm}, an architectural modification that stabilizes value aggregation outputs during pre-training. Our experiments demonstrate that restoring statistical parity across positions significantly accelerates convergence.

</details>


### [324] [Online Bayesian Calibration under Gradual and Abrupt System Changes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06612)
*Yang Xu,Chiwoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BRPC is an online Bayesian calibration framework for streaming data with simulator mismatch and nonstationarity. It separates a discrepancy-free parameter update from a discrepancy GP update, and uses restart mechanisms to handle regime shifts. The approach provides theoretical guarantees and shows improved calibration accuracy under gradual evolution, plus robustness under abrupt shifts compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical Bayesian calibration suffers from parameter–discrepancy confounding, is typically offline and assumes stationarity, which is ill-suited for evolving digital twins that exhibit drift and regime changes. There is a need for online, bias-aware calibration that can adapt to nonstationarity and abrupt changes.

Method: BRPC extends projected calibration to the online setting. It performs a discrepancy-free particle update for calibration parameters and a conditional Gaussian process update for the discrepancy term, preserving identifiability. For abrupt changes, it integrates restart mechanisms to detect regime shifts and reset calibration. The authors provide theoretical guarantees for both components, including tracking under gradual evolution and false-alarm/detection behavior for restarts.

Result: Empirical studies on synthetic and plant-simulation benchmarks show BRPC improves calibration accuracy under gradual evolution. When augmented with restarts, BRPC gains robustness and predictive performance under abrupt regime shifts, outperforming sliding-window Bayesian calibration and data assimilation baselines.

Conclusion: BRPC offers a principled, bias-aware online calibration framework for nonstationary digital twins, with theoretical guarantees and demonstrated robustness to regime changes compared with existing online calibration and data assimilation methods.

Abstract: Bayesian model calibration is central to digital twins and computer experiments, as it aligns model outputs with field observations by estimating calibration parameters and correcting systematic model bias. Classical Bayesian calibration introduces latent parameters and a discrepancy function to model bias, but suffers from parameter--discrepancy confounding and is typically formulated as an offline procedure under a stationary data-generating assumption. These limitations are restrictive in modern digital twin applications, where systems evolve over time and may exhibit gradual drift and abrupt regime shifts. While data assimilation methods enable sequential updates, they generally do not explicitly model systematic bias and are less effective under abrupt changes. We propose Bayesian Recursive Projected Calibration (BRPC), an online Bayesian calibration framework for streaming data under simulator mismatch and nonstationarity. BRPC extends projected calibration to the online setting by separating a discrepancy-free particle update for calibration parameters from a conditional Gaussian process update for discrepancy, preserving identifiability while enabling bias-aware adaptation under gradual system evolution. To handle abrupt changes, BRPC is integrated with restart mechanisms that detect regime shifts and reset the calibration process. We establish theoretical guarantees for both components, including tracking performance under gradual evolution and false-alarm and detection behavior for restart mechanisms. Empirical studies on synthetic and plant-simulation benchmarks show that BRPC improves calibration accuracy under gradual changes, while restart-augmented BRPC further improves robustness and predictive performance under abrupt regime shifts compared to sliding-window Bayesian calibration and data assimilation baselines.

</details>


### [325] [When and Why SignSGD Outperforms SGD: A Theoretical Study Based on $\ell_1$-norm Lower Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06615)
*Hongyi Tao,Dingzhi Yu,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SignSGD and Muon achieve optimal lower/upper bounds under an l1-norm stationarity, linfty-smoothness, and a separable noise model, showing a dimension-dependent gain (by a factor of d) over SGD in sparse-noise scenarios, with extensions to matrices; theoretical claims align with faster GPT-2 (124M) pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard smoothness and finite-variance analyses render sign-based methods ineffective relative to SGD in minimax terms. The work posits a different geometric setting better capturing coordinate-wise updates, enabling provable advantages for sign-based optimizers.

Method: Develop an analysis in an l1-based stationarity framework with linfty-smoothness and a separable noise model. Derive matched upper bounds for SignSGD and lower bounds for SGD. Compare these bounds to identify regimes where SignSGD dominates. Extend the framework to matrices to obtain an optimal lower bound for Muon. Validate the theory by correlating predictions with empirical pretraining of a 124M GPT-2 model.

Result: The analysis yields a tight characterization: SignSGD reduces problem complexity by a factor of d under sparse noise, and Muon preserves this dimensionality-dependent scaling in the matrix domain. The upper bound for SignSGD matches the SGD lower bound in the proposed geometry, establishing provable advantage in the specified regime. Empirical pretraining results on GPT-2 corroborate the theory’s predicted speedups.

Conclusion: Sign-based optimizers are provably advantageous within the proposed l1/linfty geometric regime and separable-noise model, enabling a dimension-dependent improvement over SGD and extending to matrix problems with Muon. This work clarifies when and why sign-based methods outperform vanilla SGD and provides guidance for when to apply them in large-scale pretraining.

Abstract: Sign-based optimization algorithms, such as SignSGD and Muon, have garnered significant attention for their remarkable performance in training large foundation models. Despite this empirical success, we still lack a theoretical understanding of when and why these sign-based methods outperform vanilla SGD. The core obstacle is that under standard smoothness and finite variance conditions, SGD is known to be minimax optimal for finding stationary points measured by $\ell_2$-norms, thereby fundamentally precluding any complexity gains for sign-based methods in standard settings. To overcome this barrier, we analyze sign-based optimizers leveraging $\ell_1$-norm stationarity, $\ell_\infty$-smoothness, and a separable noise model, which can better capture the coordinate-wise nature of signed updates. Under this distinct problem geometry, we derive matched upper and lower bounds for SignSGD and explicitly characterize the problem class in which SignSGD provably dominates SGD. Specifically, we compare the \emph{upper bound of SignSGD} with the \emph{lower bound of SGD}, illustrating that SignSGD effectively reduces the complexity by a factor of $d$ under \emph{sparse noise}, where $d$ is the problem dimension. Furthermore, we elevate this framework to the matrix domain, providing an equivalent optimal lower bound for the Muon optimizer, proving that extending the sign operator to matrices preserves this optimal scaling with dimensionality. Finally, we bridge our theoretical bounds to practice, demonstrating that the theoretical superiority of SignSGD accurately predicts its faster convergence during the pretraining of a 124M parameter GPT-2 model.

</details>


### [326] [Hybrid Quantum-Classical GANs for the Generation of Adversarial Network Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06629)
*Prateek Paudel,Nitin Jha,Abhishek Parakh,Mahadevan Subramaniam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid quantum-classical GAN (QC-GAN) for generating adversarial network traffic to test classical IDS; encodes latent features as a quantum state; attacker has limited quantum power; evaluated with UNSW-NB15; stresses hardware noise impact to motivate quantum-resilient defense. No quantitative results provided in abstract.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of classical GANs in cyber-adversarial traffic generation (large high-dimensional datasets, mode collapse, high compute). Propose a quantum-encoded latent representation to improve expressiveness and reduce overhead, enabling more realistic attack traffic and robust IDS stress-testing.

Method: A variational quantum generator encodes latent features as a quantum state; a classical discriminator is trained on real UNSW-NB15 data and QC-GAN-produced flows; minimax training where generator hides real from fake and discriminator improves accuracy. Attacker model assumes limited quantum resources; IDS evaluation uses classical models (random forest, CNN); hardware-based noise is analyzed to assess defense implications.

Result: The abstract suggests potential benefits (expressive latent representations, reduced overhead, improved attack realism, quantum-noise considerations) but provides no quantitative results or benchmarks.

Conclusion: Quantum machine learning could enable more sophisticated attack simulation and drive development of quantum-resilient IDS. However, substantial work is needed to quantify benefits, assess practicality, and validate robustness against quantum noise and real-world IDS diversity.

Abstract: Classical generative adversarial networks (GANs) have been applied to generate adversarial network traffic capable of attacking intrusion detection systems, but they suffer from shortcomings such as the need for large amounts of high-dimensional datasets, mode collapse, and high computational overhead. In this work, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical GAN (QC-GAN) framework where a variational quantum generator is used to generate synthetic network traffic flows mimicking malicious traffic using latent representations. Instead of sampling classical noise vectors, we encode the latent vector (the hidden features) as a quantum state, which is the basis for claiming more expressive latent representations and reducing computational overhead. A classical discriminator will be trained on real-world datasets (UNSW-NB15) and the proposed QC-GAN-generated fake network flows. In this configuration, the generator aims to minimize the discriminator's ability to distinguish real from fake traffic, while the discriminator aims to maximize its classification accuracy, in an iterative manner. In our attack model, we assume that the attacker is a state actor with access to limited quantum computing power, whereas the discriminator is chosen to be classical, as will likely be the case for most end users and organizations. We test the generated flows using classical intrusion detection system (IDS) models, such as a random forest classifier and a convolutional neural network-based classifier, for their ability to bypass the detection process. This work aims to highlight the possibilities of quantum machine learning as a means of generating advanced attack flows and stress testing classical IDS. Lastly, we further evaluate how hardware-based noise affects these attacks to offer a new perspective on IDS, highlighting the need for a quantum resilient defense system.

</details>


### [327] [Crafting Reversible SFT Behaviors in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06632)
*Yuping Lin,Pengfei He,Yue Xing,Yingqian Cui,Jiayuan Ding,Subhabrata Mukherjee,Hui Liu,Zhen Xiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a framework to compress SFT-induced behaviors into sparse, causally necessary subnetworks (carriers) and a trigger-based soft-prompt (SFT-Eraser) to selectively reverse them without weight changes; experiments show structural necessity and robust reversibility across tasks and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SFT induces new behaviors but lacks structural constraints; post-hoc circuit attribution shows correlations, not causality. Establishing a mechanistically necessary subnetwork would enable controlled suppression of SFT-induced behaviors at inference time.

Method: Loss-Constrained Dual Descent (LCDD) jointly optimizes routing masks and model weights under a utility budget to form sparse carriers. SFT-Eraser optimizes a soft prompt via activation matching on the carrier channels to reverse the behavior when triggered.

Result: LCDD yields sparse carriers that preserve target SFT-induced behaviors while enabling strong reversal when triggered by SFT-Eraser. Across safety, fixed-response, and style behaviors on multiple model families, ablations show the sparse structure is essential; the same trigger fails on standard SFT models, indicating that the structure, not the trigger design, drives reversibility.

Conclusion: Learned carriers are causally necessary for SFT-induced behaviors, enabling systematic localization and selective suppression of such behaviors in deployed models and opening a new direction for behavior localization and control without weight modification.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) induces new behaviors in large language models, yet imposes no structural constraint on how these behaviors are distributed within the model. Existing behavior interpretation methods, such as circuit attribution approaches, identify sparse subnetworks correlated with SFT-induced behaviors post-hoc. However, such correlations do not imply *causal necessity*, limiting the ability to selectively control SFT-induced behaviors at inference time. We pursue an alternative by asking: can an SFT-induced behavior be deliberately compressed into a sparse, mechanistically necessary subnetwork, termed a *carrier*, while remaining controllable at inference time without weight modification? We propose (a) **Loss-Constrained Dual Descent (LCDD)**, which constructs such carriers by jointly optimizing routing masks and model weights under an explicit utility budget, and (b) **SFT-Eraser**, a soft prompt optimized via activation matching on extracted carrier channels, to reverse the SFT-induced behavior. Across safety, fixed-response, and style behaviors on multiple model families, LCDD yields sparse carriers that preserve target behaviors while enabling strong reversion when triggered by SFT-Eraser. Ablations further establish that the sparse structure is the key precondition for reversal: the same trigger optimization fails on standard SFT models, confirming that structure rather than trigger design is the operative factor. These results provide direct evidence that the learned carriers are causally necessary for the behaviors, pointing to a new direction for systematically localizing and selectively suppressing SFT-induced behaviors in deployed models.

</details>


### [328] [Recursive Agent Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06639)
*Apurva Gandhi,Satyaki Chakraborty,Xiangjun Wang,Aviral Kumar,Graham Neubig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive Agent Optimization (RAO) trains recursive agents that spawn and delegate subtasks to new instantiations of themselves, enabling divide-and-conquer scaling of context and task complexity; improves training efficiency, extends effective context, generalization to harder tasks, and reduces wall-clock time versus single-agent baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of single-agent context windows and planning for long-horizon or complex tasks by enabling recursive inference and delegation.

Method: Proposes RAO as a reinforcement learning framework for training recursive agents; agents learn when and how to spawn, delegate, and communicate with sub-agents; optimizes for recursive inference efficiency.
