Result: Agents trained with RAO show better training efficiency, can scale beyond the model's context window, generalize to tasks much harder than training tasks, and achieve reduced wall-clock time compared to single-agent systems.

Conclusion: Recursive agents trained via RAO can effectively leverage recursion to enhance scalability, generalization, and efficiency in RL settings, enabling divide-and-conquer problem solving for long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: We introduce Recursive Agent Optimization (RAO), a reinforcement learning approach for training recursive agents: agents that can spawn and delegate sub-tasks to new instantiations of themselves recursively. Recursive agents implement an inference-time scaling algorithm that naturally allows agents to scale to longer contexts and generalize to more difficult problems via divide-and-conquer. RAO provides a method to train models to best take advantage of such recursive inference, teaching agents when and how to delegate and communicate. We find that recursive agents trained in this way enjoy better training efficiency, can scale to tasks that go beyond the model's context window, generalize to tasks much harder than the ones the agent was trained on, and can enjoy reduced wall-clock time compared to single-agent systems.

</details>


### [329] [Concept-Based Abductive and Contrastive Explanations for Behaviors of Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06640)
*Ronaldo Canizales,Divya Gopinath,Corina Păsăreanu,Ravi Mangal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for concept-based abductive and contrastive explanations identifying minimal, causally relevant sets of high-level concepts for model outputs, using concept erasure and aggregating explanations across images and behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing concept-based explanations lack causal grounding or are restricted to single concepts; formal abductive/contrastive methods focus on low-level features. The work aims to provide high-level, causally grounded, minimal explanations that scale to collections of instances.

Method: Define concept erasure procedures to test causal impact and enumerate all minimal concept-based explanations; extend abductive/contrastive formalism to concept space; aggregate explanations across images sharing a user-specified behavior; evaluate across multiple models/datasets/behaviors.

Result: Demonstrates the ability to compute minimal, user-friendly concept explanations and to scale to collections of instances exhibiting common behavior, showing effectiveness across models and datasets.

Conclusion: Bridges concept-based explainability with formal abductive/contrastive explanations, delivering a framework and algorithms for causal, minimal, high-level concept explanations that are composable over collections of instances.

Abstract: *Concept-based explanations* offer a promising approach for explaining the predictions of deep neural networks in terms of high-level, human-understandable concepts. However, existing methods either do not establish a causal connection between the concepts and model predictions or are limited in expressivity and only able to infer causal explanations involving single concepts. At the same time, the parallel line of work on *formal abductive and contrastive explanations* computes the minimal set of input features causally relevant for model outcomes but only considers low-level features such as pixels. Merging these two threads, in this work, we propose the notion of *concept-based abductive and contrastive explanations* that capture the minimal sets of high-level concepts causally relevant for model outcomes. We then present a family of algorithms that enumerate all minimal explanations while using *concept erasure* procedures to establish causal relationships. By appropriately aggregating such explanations, we are not only able to understand model predictions on individual images but also on collections of images where the model exhibits a user-specified, common *behavior*. We evaluate our approach on multiple models, datasets, and behaviors, and demonstrate its effectiveness in computing helpful, user-friendly explanations.

</details>


### [330] [Inductive Venn-Abers and related regressors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06646)
*Ivan Petej,Vladimir Vovk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Venn-Abers predictors to unbounded regression via conformal prediction; shows modest efficiency gains for larger training sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the binary/bounded limitation of Venn-Abers predictors by enabling unbounded regression and evaluating efficiency of derived point regressors.

Method: Develop unbounded Venn-Abers regression by incorporating conformal prediction; run simulations and empirical studies to assess predictive efficiency of the derived point regressors versus standard regressors across varying training-set sizes.

Result: Point regressors derived from the unbounded Venn-Abers framework provide somewhat improved predictive efficiency compared with standard regressors, particularly for larger training sets.

Conclusion: Unbounded regression generalization is feasible and can yield modest efficiency gains; integrating conformal prediction extends Venn-Abers' applicability to broader regression tasks.

Abstract: Venn-Abers predictors are probabilistic predictors that enjoy appealing properties of validity, but their major limitation is that they are applicable only to the case of binary classification, with a recent extension to bounded regression. We generalize them to the case of unbounded regression, which requires adding an element of conformal prediction. In our simulation and empirical studies we investigate the predictive efficiency of point regressors derived from Venn-Abers regressors and argue that they somewhat improve the predictive efficiency of standard regressors for larger training sets.

</details>


### [331] [When No Benchmark Exists: Validating Comparative LLM Safety Scoring Without Ground-Truth Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06652)
*Sushant Gautam,Finn Schwall,Annika Willoch Olstad,Fernando Vallecillos Ruiz,Birk Torpmann-Hagen,Sunniva Maria Stordal Bjørklund,Leon Moonen,Klas Pettersen,Michael A. Riegler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for benchmarkless comparative safety scoring for language models, using a scenario-based audit and an instrumental-validity chain; introduces SimpleAudit; demonstrates validity on Norwegian safety pack and Petri; real-world procurement show safety depends on scenario and risk measure; argues for reporting scores with contextual factors rather than a single ranking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable comparative safety assessments for language models in the absence of labeled benchmarks, by formalizing how scenario-based audits can serve as deployment evidence and what constitutes valid scoring.

Method: Define a fixed scenario pack, rubric, auditor/judge, and rerun budget; replace ground-truth with instrumental validity components (response to safe-vs-abliterated contrast, dominance of target-driven variance, stability across reruns); instantiate SimpleAudit; evaluate on Norwegian safety pack; apply to Petri; analyze upstream claim-contract enforcement and deployment fit; illustrate with a public-sector procurement case comparing Borealis and Gemma 3.

Result: Demonstrates that safe vs abliterated targets separate with AUROC 0.89–1.00; target identity variance dominates (η^2 ≈ 0.52); severity profiles stabilize after ~10 reruns; findings differ upstream in contract enforcement; evidence shows dependency on scenario category and risk measure; requires reporting of scores, deltas, uncertainty, and roles (auditor/judge) together, not as a single ranking.

Conclusion: Benchmarkless safety scoring can provide deployment-relevant evidence if scores are reported with full context (scenario, risk measure, contract rules, auditor/judge, rerun config). Upstream governance and deployment fit critically shape results.

Abstract: Many deployments must compare candidate language models for safety before a labeled benchmark exists for the relevant language, sector, or regulatory regime. We formalize this setting as benchmarkless comparative safety scoring and specify the contract under which a scenario-based audit can be interpreted as deployment evidence. Scores are valid only under a fixed scenario pack, rubric, auditor, judge, sampling configuration, and rerun budget. Because no labels are available, we replace ground-truth agreement with an instrumental-validity chain: responsiveness to a controlled safe-versus-abliterated contrast, dominance of target-driven variance over auditor and judge artifacts, and stability across reruns.
  We instantiate the chain in SimpleAudit, a local-first scoring instrument, and validate it on a Norwegian safety pack. Safe and abliterated targets separate with AUROC values between 0.89 and 1.00, target identity is the dominant variance component ($η^2 \approx 0.52$), and severity profiles stabilize by ten reruns. Applying the same chain to Petri shows that it admits both tools. The substantial differences arise upstream of the chain, in claim-contract enforcement and deployment fit. A Norwegian public-sector procurement case comparing Borealis and Gemma 3 demonstrates the resulting evidence in practice: the safer model depends on scenario category and risk measure. Consequently, scores, matched deltas, critical rates, uncertainty, and the auditor and judge used must be reported together rather than collapsed into a single ranking.

</details>


### [332] [Optimizer-Model Consistency: Full Finetuning with the Same Optimizer as Pretraining Forgets Less](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06654)
*Yuxing Liu,Jianyu Wang,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Maintaining optimizer consistency across pretraining and supervised finetuning (SFT) improves the learning-forgetting tradeoff, often outperforming other optimizers and LoRA; Muon underperforms on reasoning tasks due to memorization tendencies, with implications rooted in activation regularization and update structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why using the same optimizer during pretraining and SFT can yield better retention of pretrained knowledge while achieving strong performance on new tasks, and to analyze the mechanisms behind this phenomenon.

Method: Controlled experiments and theoretical analysis comparing optimizers (e.g., Muon vs AdamW) across pretraining and SFT phases, including a synthetic language modeling experiment to probe memorization vs pattern learning, focusing on activation regularization and update dynamics.

Result: Full finetuning with the pretraining optimizer achieves a better learning-forgetting tradeoff than other optimizers and LoRA; optimizers influence the loss landscape around pretrained checkpoints via activation regularization; the weight update in SFT should follow structures that reduce forgetting, which is achieved by using the same optimizer; Muon tends to rote memorization and underperforms on reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Optimizer-consistency across pretraining and SFT materially affects forgetting and task performance; the choice of optimizer shapes the regularization of activations and the update geometry, with practical guidance to prefer the pretraining optimizer for SFT in many scenarios, while recognizing that Muon may hurt pattern acquisition in small-data SFT tasks.

Abstract: Optimizers play an important role in both pretraining and finetuning stages when training large language models (LLMs). In this paper, we present an observation that full finetuning with the same optimizer as in pretraining achieves a better learning-forgetting tradeoff, i.e., forgetting less while achieving the same or better performance on the new task, than other optimizers and, possibly surprisingly, LoRA, during the supervised finetuning (SFT) stage. We term this phenomenon optimizer-model consistency. To better understand it, through controlled experiments and theoretical analysis, we show that: 1) optimizers can shape the models by having regularization effects on the activations, leading to different landscapes around the pretrained checkpoints; 2) in response to this regularization effect, the weight update in SFT should follow some specific structures to lower forgetting of the knowledge learned in pretraining, which can be obtained by using the same optimizer. Moreover, we specifically compare Muon and AdamW when they are employed throughout the pretraining and SFT stages and find that Muon performs worse when finetuned for reasoning tasks. With a synthetic language modeling experiment, we demonstrate that this can come from Muon's strong tendency towards rote memorization, which may hurt pattern acquisition with a small amount of data, as for SFT.

</details>


### [333] [Verifier-Backed Hard Problem Generation for Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06660)
*Yuhang Lai,Jiazhan Feng,Yee Whye Teh,Ning Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VHG is a verifier-enhanced hard problem generation framework using three-party self-play (setter, solver, verifier) to produce valid, hard problems for LLM training. It uses two verifier variants (hard symbolic and soft LLM-based) and shows performance gains over baselines on indefinite integrals and general math reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortage of valid, challenging, and novel problems for LLM self-improvement, existing methods depend on experts or naive self-play prone to reward hacking; a scalable, robust generation framework is needed.

Method: Introduce three-party self-play with an independent verifier that constrains the setter’s reward by problem validity (via the verifier) and difficulty (via the solver). Implement two verifier variants: a Hard symbolic verifier and a Soft LLM-based verifier. Evaluate on indefinite integral tasks and general mathematical reasoning, comparing against baselines.

Result: VHG substantially outperforms all baselines, indicating higher-quality problem generation with better validity and specified difficulty.

Conclusion: Verifier-enhanced self-play with a three-party framework effectively mitigates reward hacking and yields more valid and challenging problems for LLM training; the dual verifier options offer a trade-off between rigor and scalability and the approach is demonstrated on math problem domains.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities for solving scientific and mathematical problems, yet they struggle to produce valid, challenging, and novel problems - an essential component for advancing LLM training and enabling autonomous scientific research. Existing problem generation approaches either depend on expensive human expert involvement or adopt naive self-play paradigms, which frequently yield invalid problems due to reward hacking. This work introduces VHG, a verifier-enhanced hard problem generation framework built upon three-party self-play. By integrating an independent verifier into the conventional setter-solver duality, our design constrains the setter's reward to be jointly determined by problem validity (evaluated by the verifier) and difficulty (assessed by the solver). We instantiate two verifier variants: a Hard symbolic verifier and a Soft LLM-based verifier, with evaluations conducted on indefinite integral tasks and general mathematical reasoning tasks. Experimental results show that VHG substantially outperforms all baseline methods by a clear margin.

</details>


### [334] [UniPool: A Globally Shared Expert Pool for Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06665)
*Minbin Huang,Han Shi,Chuanyang Zheng,Yimeng Wu,Guoxuan Chen,Xintong Yu,Yichun Yin,Hong Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniPool replaces per-layer expert ownership with a single shared pool accessed by per-layer routers, plus pool-level auxiliary loss and NormRouter. It yields consistent validation loss and perplexity improvements over vanilla MoE across five model scales, with pool size acting as a depth-scaling hyperparameter and potential sublinear growth of expert parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional MoE ties expert capacity to layer depth, causing linear parameter growth and assuming each layer requires isolated capacity. Observations show that random routing can degrade little, indicating redundancy in per-layer experts. A shared pool could reduce parameter growth while maintaining or improving performance.

Method: Introduce UniPool: a global shared pool of experts accessed by independent per-layer routers. Use a pool-level auxiliary loss to balance utilization and NormRouter for sparse, scale-stable routing into the shared pool. Evaluate on five LLaMA-scale models (182M–978M parameters) trained on 30B tokens from the Pile, comparing against vanilla MoE baselines. Explore reduced-pool variants using 41.6%-66.7% of the vanilla budget. Analyze pool size as a depth-scaling hyperparameter and assess compatibility with finer-grained expert decomposition.

Result: UniPool consistently improves validation loss and perplexity over matched vanilla MoE baselines across all scales. Relative validation loss reduction up to 0.0386 compared to vanilla MoE. Reduced-pool UniPool variants with 41.6%-66.7% of the vanilla expert budget match or outperform layer-wise MoE. Pool size emerges as an explicit depth-scaling hyperparameter, with sublinear expert growth yet superior efficiency and effectiveness. Benefits persist when combined with finer-grained expert decomposition.

Conclusion: A shared-expert pool with per-layer routers is a viable and efficient alternative to per-layer expert ownership. Pool-based design allows sublinear growth of expert parameters with depth, while delivering consistent performance gains and compatibility with finer decomposition strategies.

Abstract: Modern Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures allocate expert capacity through a rigid per-layer rule: each transformer layer owns a separate expert set. This convention couples depth scaling with linear expert-parameter growth and assumes that every layer needs isolated expert capacity. However, recent analyses and our routing probe challenge this allocation rule: replacing a deeper layer's learned top-k router with uniform random routing drops downstream accuracy by only 1.0-1.6 points across multiple production MoE models. Motivated by this redundancy, we propose UniPool, an MoE architecture that treats expert capacity as a global architectural budget by replacing per-layer expert ownership with a single shared pool accessed by independent per-layer routers. To enable stable and balanced training under sharing, we introduce a pool-level auxiliary loss that balances expert utilization across the entire pool, and adopt NormRouter to provide sparse and scale-stable routing into the shared expert pool. Across five LLaMA-architecture model scales (182M, 469M, 650M, 830M, and 978M parameters) trained on 30B tokens from the Pile, UniPool consistently improves validation loss and perplexity over the matched vanilla MoE baselines. Across these scales, UniPool reduces validation loss by up to 0.0386 relative to vanilla MoE. Beyond raw loss improvement, our results identify pool size as an explicit depth-scaling hyperparameter: reduced-pool UniPool variants using only 41.6%-66.7% of the vanilla expert-parameter budget match or outperform layer-wise MoE at the tested scales. This shows that, under a shared-pool design, expert parameters need not grow linearly with depth; they can grow sublinearly while remaining more efficient and effective than vanilla MoE. Further analysis shows that UniPool's benefits compose with finer-grained expert decomposition.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [335] [A Constant-Factor Approximation for Continuous Dynamic Time Warping in 2D](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05917)
*Kevin Buchin,Maike Buchin,Jan Erik Swiadek,Sampson Wong*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Introduces a 5-approximation algorithm for 2D CDTW under the L1 norm with O(n^5) time; extends to all polygonal norms to achieve (5+ε)-approx with O(n^5/ε^{1/2}) time; first constant-factor polynomial-time CDTW approximation in 2D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CDTW is a robust similarity measure for polygonal curves, but the algorithmic complexity in 2D is poorly understood. There is a need for efficient exact or strongly approximate algorithms with provable guarantees.

Method: Develop a 5-approximation algorithm for CDTW in 2D under the L1 norm with O(n^5) time. Extend the approach to general polygonal norms to obtain a (5+ε)-approximation in 2D for any fixed norm, with time O(n^5/ε^{1/2}).

Result: First constant-factor (polynomial-time) approximation for 2D CDTW. The method generalizes to all polygonal norms, yielding (5+ε)-approximation in polynomial time for fixed norms, including Euclidean.

Conclusion: Establishes polynomial-time approximability for 2D CDTW with provable guarantees and broad norm applicability; opens avenues for tighter bounds and faster algorithms.

Abstract: Continuous Dynamic Time Warping (CDTW) is a robust similarity measure for polygonal curves that has recently found a variety of applications. Despite its practical use, not much is known about the algorithmic complexity of computing it in 2D, especially when one requires either an exact solution or strong approximation guarantees. We fill this gap by introducing a $5$-approximation algorithm with running time $O(n^5)$ under the 1-norm.
  This is the first constant-factor approximation for 2D CDTW with polynomial running time. We extend our algorithm to all polygonal norms on $\mathbb{R}^2$, which we subsequently use in order to achieve a $(5+\varepsilon)$-approximation with time complexity $O(n^5 / \varepsilon^{1/2})$ for CDTW in 2D under any fixed norm. The latter result in particular includes the usual Euclidean 2-norm.

</details>


### [336] [Scalable GPU Construction of 3D Voronoi and Power Diagrams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06408)
*Bernardo Taveira,Carl Lindström,Maryam Fatemi,Lars Hammarstrand,Fredrik Kahl*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: A GPU-based, highly parallel algorithm for constructing large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams by progressively clipping an initial cell with bisecting planes from candidate neighbors; uses directional bounds and a hierarchical best-first traversal to prune the neighbor search; generalizes to power diagrams without extra assumptions; achieves competitive performance and good scalability for heterogeneous distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient construction of large-scale 3D Voronoi diagrams (and power diagrams) is critical for applications like mesh-based neural rendering, but existing CPU-based Delaunay tetrahedralization is slow for large data, and GPU methods struggle with scale or rely on homogeneous distributions; power diagrams are underexplored. A fast, scalable, and general GPU solution would broaden applicability to complex, heterogeneous datasets.

Method: A per-cell construction where each Voronoi (or power) cell is built by clipping an initial cell volume with bisecting planes induced by candidate neighboring points. Candidate neighbors are efficiently identified via directional geometric bounds of the evolving cell and a hierarchical best-first traversal of bounding volumes (BVH-like structure) to prune unlikely neighbors. The approach is highly parallel on GPU and naturally extends to weights for power diagrams without extra assumptions.

Result: The method achieves performance on par with state-of-the-art Delaunay tetrahedralization methods on small to moderate problem sizes and shows robust scalability to large point sets and diverse spatial distributions. It also generalizes naturally to power diagrams.

Conclusion: The work presents a scalable, GPU-accelerated framework for constructing large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams, with broad applicability to large-scale geometric computation and mesh-based rendering. The approach is general, efficient, and applicable to heterogeneous distributions, and it provides a public link to the publication for further details.

Abstract: Voronoi diagrams, and their more general weighted counterpart, power diagrams, are fundamental geometric constructs with wide-ranging applications. Recently, they have gained renewed attention in mesh-based neural rendering. Despite being extensively studied, the construction of 3D Voronoi diagrams for large-scale point sets remains computationally expensive, limiting their adoption in large-scale applications. Existing CPU-based approaches typically rely on computing its dual, the Delaunay tetrahedralization, but are prohibitively slow for large diagrams, while GPU-based methods either struggle to scale efficiently to large point sets or assume homogeneous point distributions. The weighted case, power diagrams, is even less explored in this context. Existing approaches are typically tailored to the application at hand, assuming homogeneous point distributions and small weight variations, making them unsuitable for general use in more complex heterogeneous data.
  In this paper, we present a highly parallelizable GPU algorithm for the fast construction of large-scale 3D Voronoi and power diagrams. Our approach constructs each convex cell from a weighted 3D point by progressively clipping an initial cell volume against bisecting planes induced by candidate neighboring points. To efficiently identify candidate neighbors under arbitrary spatial distributions, we introduce a culling criterion based on directional geometric bounds of the evolving cell, combined with a hierarchical best-first traversal of bounding volumes.
  We achieve performance on par with state-of-the-art Delaunay tetrahedralization methods on small and moderate problem sizes, while exhibiting robust scalability to large point sets and diverse spatial distributions. Moreover, our method naturally generalizes to power diagrams without additional assumptions. See https://research.zenseact.com/publications/paragram .

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [337] [A GPU-Accelerated Hybrid Method for a Class of Multi-Depot Vehicle Routing Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05208)
*Zhenyu Lei,Jin-Kao Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid algorithm for multi-depot VRPs that combines learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover with a multi-depot feasible/infeasible search guided by a multi-penalty evaluation, augmented by depot-specific local searches and a GPU-accelerated, multi-move update variant; shows strong performance on three MDVRP variants, especially for large-scale instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDVRPs are common in practice but computationally challenging due to combinatorial complexity and coordination across multiple depots; there is a need for scalable, high-quality solvers that can exploit modern hardware.

Method: A hybrid algorithm that integrates: (1) a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover; (2) a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function; (3) two depot-related local search operators; (4) an enhanced GPU-accelerated version using tensor-based computation and a novel multi-move update strategy.

Result: Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, with particular effectiveness on large-scale instances.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid framework effectively tackles MDVRPs, achieving high-quality solutions and demonstrating scalability through GPU acceleration; the approach advances the state-of-the-art for multi-depot routing problems and is especially beneficial for large-scale instances.

Abstract: Multi-depot vehicle routing problems (MDVRPs) are prevalent in a variety of practical applications. However, they are computationally challenging to solve due to their inherent complexity. This paper proposes an effective hybrid algorithm for a class of MDVRPs. The algorithm integrates a learning-driven, diversity-controlled route-exchange crossover and a multi-depot-supported feasible-and-infeasible search framework guided by a multi-penalty evaluation function. Two dedicated depot-related local search operators are incorporated to further strengthen the search capability in multi-depot settings. To improve computational efficiency and scalability, an enhanced version of the algorithm is developed that uses a tensor-based GPU acceleration combined with a novel multi-move update strategy. Extensive computational experiments on benchmark instances of three MDVRP variants show that the proposed algorithms are highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods, especially for large-scale instances.

</details>


### [338] [Topology-Driven Anti-Entanglement Control for Soft Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05236)
*Haoyang Le,Shengxuan Wang,Mohan Chen,Shuo Feng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a topology-driven multi-agent reinforcement learning (TD-MARL) framework to coordinate multiple soft robots for anti-winding in highly constrained environments. Centralized learning enables sharing of topological state while distributed execution preserves communication efficiency; a topological security layer using invariants mitigates entanglement risk. Simulation results claim superior convergence and anti-winding performance over state-of-the-art DRL methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In precision manufacturing with complex constraints, soft robots need to coordinate to avoid entanglement (winding). Existing distributed MARL struggles with observability and instability in high-density, uncertain environments, hindering learning and reliability.

Method: TD-MARL framework with: (1) a critical network employing centralized learning to share topological state among intelligent bodies; (2) distributed execution to reduce inter-robot communication; (3) an integrated topological security layer leveraging topological invariants to assess and mitigate entanglement risk; (4) simulation-based evaluation in a realistic environment.

Result: Simulation experiments show the proposed method achieves better convergence and stronger anti-winding performance than current advanced DRL methods.

Conclusion: Topology-aware coordination with centralized information sharing and invariant-based risk assessment enhances multi-robot unwinding in constrained environments, improving learning stability and anti-entanglement performance in simulation.

Abstract: In the field of precision manufacturing in complex constrained environments, the role of soft robots is increasingly prominent, and the realization of anti-winding control based on multi-intelligent body reinforcement learning has become a research hotspot. One of the core problems at present is to coordinate multiple robots to complete the unwinding operation in a highly constrained environment. The existing distributed training framework faces some observability challenges in high-density barrier and unstable environments, resulting in poor learning results. This paper proposes a topology-driven Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (TD-MARL) framework to coordinate multi-robot systems to avoid entanglement. Specifically, the critical network adopts centralized learning, so that each intelligent body can perceive the strategies of other intelligent bodies by sharing the topological state, thus alleviating the training instability caused by complex interactions; eliminating the demand for communication resources between robots through distributed execution, Upgrade system reliability; the integrated topological security layer uses topological invariants to accurately assess and mitigate the risk of entanglement to avoid the strategy from falling into local difficulties. Finally, the full simulation experiments carried out in the real simulation environment show that the method is better than the current advanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) method in terms of convergence and anti-winding effect.

</details>


### [339] [Track A*: Fast Visibility-Aware Trajectory Planning for Active Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05338)
*Hanxuan Chen,Kangli Wang,Ji Pei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TA star is an offline, beam-pruned DAG-based planner on a 4D spatio-temporal grid, delivering near-baseline visibility with large speedups over a baseline A* in CARLA-like scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide repeatable, scalable offline reference trajectories for active target tracking to benchmark online planners and build multi-modal datasets. Address computational bottlenecks of search-based planning.

Method: Constructs a layered DAG on a discretized (x, y, z, t) grid. Uses cross-time obstacle distance caching via a BVH, per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. Performs beam-pruned heuristic search on the discrete graph, with a 5e6 expansion cap and 32-worker parallelism in reported experiments.

Result: TA star converges on all 1000-scenario stress tests across eight CARLA Optimized maps, finishing in 45 s with 32 workers. In a 248-scenario comparison against an unoptimized priority-queue A* baseline, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0x and worst-case by 11.8x, and increases convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 converged subset, it changes average visibility by -0.15 percentage points (no scenario >5 pp drop).

Conclusion: TA star is a practical offline reference planner that balances near-baseline visibility with substantial computational savings; suitable for benchmarking under specified conditions, though it has known failure modes (e.g., Town07 dense vegetation) and does not guarantee strict optimality.

Abstract: Offline reference trajectories for active target tracking are needed both for building multi-modal tracking datasets and for benchmarking online tracking planners under repeatable conditions. We present Track A star (TA star), an offline search-based trajectory planner that targets the visibility-aware target tracking objective on a discretized four-dimensional spatio-temporal grid (x, y, z, t). TA star combines a layered Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) search with three engineering optimizations: cross-time obstacle distance caching against a Bounding Volume Hierarchy (BVH), per-layer beam pruning, and a configurable multi-ray visibility evaluator. TA star employs a beam-pruned heuristic search on this discrete graph to efficiently find high-quality tracking trajectories. While it trades strict theoretical optimality for practical scalability, our empirical results demonstrate robust, near-baseline visibility performance at a fraction of the computational cost. On a 1000-scenario stress test across eight CARLA Optimized maps, TA star converges on all scenarios and completes in 45 s using 32 workers; on a 248-scenario controlled comparison against an unoptimized priority-queue A star baseline (BinaryHeap implementation) under identical scenario inputs and a 5 x 10^6 expansion cap, TA star reduces mean planning time by 23.0x and worst-case planning time by 11.8x, while raising convergence from 56.9% to 100%. On the n=141 baseline-converged subset, TA star changes average visibility by only -0.15 percentage points (pp), with no scenario exceeding a 5 pp drop. We position TA star as a practical offline reference planner under these specific conditions, with limitations and failure cases discussed for environments such as Town07 dense vegetation.

</details>


### [340] [Passive Fault Tolerance through Tension-to-Thrust Feed-Forward: Hybrid Input-to-State Stability for Decentralized Multi-UAV Slung-Load Transport under Abrupt Cable Severance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05339)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Paul Schmitt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A passive, decentralized recovery strategy for multi-UAV slung-load under abrupt cable severance, using tension feed-forward to altitude control plus a PD anti-swing cascade, backed by a conditional hybrid practical ISS certificate and validated in Drake simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Abrupt cable severance redistributes load and reduces feasible control options, leaving little time for fault diagnosis; existing decentralized controllers rely on coordinated force allocation, peer-state exchange, or fixed topology and lack a certified unannounced-severance recovery mechanism.

Method: Route each vehicle's measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command; employ a surrounding proportional–derivative anti-swing and projection cascade to preserve local tracking feasibility; prove a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate that combines slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction ρ in (0,1) into an explicit recovery envelope under actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions.

Result: Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, 10 kg payload, Kelvin–Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and single/dual severance schedules: closed-loop RMSE 0.312–0.328 m, peak sag 76.1–95.2 mm, recovery within one payload-pendulum period; disabling the identity inflates cruise error by 34–39% and peak sag by 3.6×–4.0×, indicating local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism.

Conclusion: The proposed passive, decentralized tension feed-forward approach yields robust, certified recovery from unannounced severance, with a clear quantitative recovery envelope and strong evidence that tension feedback is the key passive mechanism in the tested cascade.

Abstract: Abrupt cable severance in multi-UAV slung-load transport redistributes load and changes the active constraint set, leaving limited time for fault diagnosis and reconfiguration. Existing controllers rely on coordinated force allocation, peer-state exchange, or fixed cable topology, and therefore lack a certified decentralized recovery mechanism for unannounced severance. We present a passive architecture that routes each vehicle's measured cable tension directly into its altitude thrust command, $T_i^{\mathrm{ff}}=T_i$, while a surrounding proportional-derivative, anti-swing, and projection cascade preserves local tracking feasibility. The main contribution is a conditional hybrid practical input-to-state-stability certificate that composes a slack-excursion-bounded taut-cable reduction, bounded post-severance Lyapunov jumps, inter-fault decay, and per-fault-cycle contraction $ρ\in (0,1)$ into an explicit recovery envelope under stated actuator, slack, and dwell assumptions. We validate the controller in Drake multibody simulation with five vehicles, a 10 kg payload, Kelvin-Voigt cables, Dryden wind, and single- and dual-severance schedules: the closed loop attains 0.312-0.328 m RMSE, 76.1-95.2 mm peak sag, and recovery within one payload-pendulum period. Disabling the identity inflates cruise error by 34-39% and peak sag by 3.6x-4.0x, identifying local tension feed-forward as the dominant passive recovery mechanism in the tested decentralized cascade.

</details>


### [341] [Creative Robot Tool Use by Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05411)
*M. Tuluhan Akbulut,Varun Satheesh,Ahmed Jaafar,Alper Ahmetoglu,Shane Parr,Aditya Ganeshan,Shivam Vats,George Konidaris*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use grounded in dynamics, using VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation to identify causal features, enabling better tool selection and skill transfer across novel objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robots to select and use tools beyond their primary objectives by uncovering causal relationships between tools, task goals, and physics, improving generalization and transfer to novel objects.

Method: Decouple causal discovery into two components: (1) VLM-based feature suggestion to propose relevant features; (2) counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric/physical perturbations. Reconstruct tasks in a dynamics model to ground physics. Classify novel objects by identified causal features. Transfer tool-use skill via keypoint matching conditioned on the causal features.

Result: Evidence from baseline comparisons that leveraging causal features and physics-grounded tool properties improves reliability of tool selection and strength of skill keypoint transfer. Demonstrations include reaching with sticks, scooping candies with diverse items, and using boxes/crates as stepping platforms to retrieve an object from a high shelf.

Conclusion: Grounding tool use in the physics of the problem through identified causal features enhances generalization and transferability of tool-use skills across tasks and objects.

Abstract: We propose a causal reasoning framework for creative robot tool use where a suitable tool for a task is correctly identified for use beyond its primary objectives. The proposed framework first discovers the causal relationships between the tool and the task by conducting simulated experiments in a dynamics model. We decouple the causal discovery problem into two complementary components: VLM-based feature suggestion and counterfactual tool generation via targeted geometric and physical feature perturbations. Then, novel objects are classified based on identified causal features, and the tool use skill is transferred via keypoint matching conditioned on the identified causal features. By reconstructing the task in a dynamics model, our approach grounds tool use in the physics of the problem. We illustrate our approach in reaching a distant object with different sticks, scooping candies from a bowl using diverse items, and using different boxes or crates as stepping platforms to retrieve an object from a high shelf. Our baseline comparisons show that identifying causal features and grounding them in physical tool properties leads to more reliable tool selection and stronger skill keypoint transfer.

</details>


### [342] [Contact-Free Grasp Stability Prediction with In-Hand Time-of-Flight Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05461)
*Kyle DuFrene,Cindy Grimm*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone ToF sensors on the gripper, enabling fast non-contact stability classification at 15 Hz with competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bypass the need for grasp execution for stability assessment and to speed up planning under sensor noise and variability.

Method: Deploy multi-zone time-of-flight sensors on distal gripper links; train a classifier from 2,500 real grasps across 15 objects; evaluate on six unseen objects (3 for validation/model selection, 3 for testing).

Result: Achieves 85.5% validation accuracy and 86.0% test accuracy; operates at 15 Hz; demonstrates data collection of 2,500 grasps and generalization to unseen objects.

Conclusion: Non-contact, fast stability prediction is feasible and generalizes to new objects, potentially accelerating grasp planning without object contact.

Abstract: Current approaches to grasp planning for robotics demonstrate high success rates, but degrade with noisy sensors and other factors. Previous works have proposed tactile-based grasp stability classifiers to detect failures, but these approaches rely on making contact and grasping the object to do so. We propose a contact-free grasp stability predictor using multi-zone time-of-flight sensors mounted in the distal links of a gripper. Our method, as it does not require grasping the object to make a prediction, significantly speeds up the stability classification process, cycling at 15 Hz. We collected over 2,500 real-world grasps across 15 objects to train a classifier. Additionally, we conducted grasp attempts over six additional unseen objects, three for validation and model selection, and three for model testing. Our approach demonstrated strong classification performance, with an accuracy of 85.5% on validation and 86.0% on test objects.

</details>


### [343] [Robust $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Controller Design For INDI-Controlled Quadrotor Using Online Parameter Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05483)
*Tom Aantjes,Till M. Blaha,Spilios Theodoulis,Ewoud J. J. Smeur*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Robust gain-scheduled attitude control for a quadrotor using an INDI inner loop with online parameter identification and H-infinity shaping; demonstrates stability margins and practical performance under model uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust outer-loop gain tuning for INDI-based controllers is lacking; want a real-time, gain-scheduled controller that adapts to changing model parameters.

Method: Design of a gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with an INDI-based inner loop, online identification of system parameters, a feedforward filter, and signal-based H-infinity closed-loop shaping for a symmetric quadrotor.

Result: Nonlinear simulations show effective tracking under parameter uncertainty with good stability margins; experimental flight tests confirm viability with full online parameter identification; identified parameters outside the nominal uncertainty range but acceptable performance remains for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

Conclusion: The proposed approach yields robust attitude control for quadrotors under parameter uncertainty, integrating online parameter identification with gain-scheduled INDI and H-infinity shaping, and proves effective in both simulation and real-world flight tests.

Abstract: It has recently been shown that all physical parameters of an Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller can be estimated onboard a multirotor within half a second, which is fast enough to do the full identification during a throw in the air. However, a robust method to tune outer loop gains for this feedback-linearizing INDI controller depending on the model parameters is still missing. This work presents the design of a robust gain-scheduled controller for attitude control of quadrotor, using an INDI-based inner loop with online identification of its system parameters. A gain-scheduled cascaded attitude controller with a feedforward filter is synthesized for a symmetric quadrotor using signal-based $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ closed-loop shaping. The resulting controller exhibits good stability margins, with nonlinear simulations confirming effective tracking performance under uncertainty. Experimental evaluation is also conducted through flight tests with full online parameter identification. Even though the identified parameters during these tests are far outside the defined uncertainty range, acceptable flight performance comparable to simulation results is maintained for actuator time constants below 40 ms.

</details>


### [344] [Real-world Latency Analysis of Vehicular Visible Light Communication with Multiple LED Transmitters and an Event-Based Camera](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05541)
*Ryota Soga,Tsukasa Shimizu,Shintaro Shiba,Quan Kong,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An event-camera–based VLC system for V2X addresses bandwidth, multi-transmitter reception, and latency. It uses a positive-event-only mode with a protocol to suppress events while preserving range and FOV, identifies multiple transmitters, and demonstrates simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs with end-to-end latency compatible with cooperative perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit event cameras' high temporal resolution and dynamic range to enable low-latency, robust VLC for V2X, addressing practical challenges like bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter scenarios, and latency characterization.

Method: Adopt a positive-event-only mode; design a protocol to suppress event generation without sacrificing communication range or field of view; develop a transmitter-identification method enabling simultaneous reception from multiple LEDs (up to three); conduct end-to-end latency measurements in real vehicular scenarios.

Result: Demonstrates simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs; end-to-end latency meets cooperative perception requirements in real-world vehicular settings; shows feasibility of event-camera VLC as a supplementary V2X link.

Conclusion: Event-camera-based VLC is a feasible complement to RF V2X technologies, offering high temporal resolution and low latency with the capacity to handle multiple transmitters and realistic latency performance.

Abstract: Event cameras offer high temporal resolution, low latency, and wide dynamic range, making them promising receivers for visible light communication (VLC) in vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications. This work presents an event-camera-based VLC system addressing three key challenges: bandwidth saturation, multi-transmitter reception, and latency characterization.
  We adopt a positive-event-only mode and design a protocol that suppresses event generation while maintaining communication distance and a wide field of view. We also propose a method to identify multiple transmitters and demonstrate simultaneous reception from up to three LEDs. Finally, we evaluate end-to-end latency in real vehicular scenarios and show that the system meets cooperative perception requirements. These results demonstrate that event-camera-based VLC is a feasible complement to existing V2X technologies (e.g., RF).

</details>


### [345] [On the Emergence of Pendular Structure in Multi-Contact Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05707)
*Lingxue Lyu,Zihui Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An OCP penalizing angular momentum rate yields LIPM-like optimal patterns; the resulting force distribution and feasibility depend on stance geometry and friction, aligning with ZMP/DCM but with limitations in multi-contact/trot-like configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify why LIPM appears as a natural outcome in legged locomotion control by embedding LIPM into the controller’s cost, and to understand how stance configuration and contact constraints shape the optimal policy.

Method: Start from a small centroidal OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum. Analyze the optimal solution under different stance regimes: full-rank stance, N=2 stance (trot), and with additional task terms. Derive closed-form conditions (e.g., a pendular-like force pattern, a friction-cone–induced lower bound on angular-momentum rate, a non-smooth feasibility kink). Compare to ZMP/DCM intuition and validate on a point-mass quadruped and MuJoCo Go1 (open-loop QP and torque-controlled).

Result: Full-rank stance: the optimum approaches a pendular force pattern, with a rate governed by the SVD of the moment Jacobian; the constant is geometry-determined via foot span and matches experiments within ~16%. N=2 stance: friction cone enforces a lower bound on ||Ḣ_G||, regardless of weight tuning; a closed-form non-smooth kink appears at a critical horizontal acceleration. Adding a task term that favors nonzero Ḣ_G shifts the optimum away from the pendular set in a predictable way. The analysis remains close to ZMP/DCM intuition. Tests on a point-mass quadruped and the Unitree Go1 in MuJoCo show where the asymptotic description breaks down in the closed-loop system.

Conclusion: LIPM emerges naturally as part of the optimal policy when the controller penalizes angular-momentum rate, and the resulting optimal patterns align with pendular behavior under favorable contact conditions. However, contact geometry, friction, and closed-loop dynamics introduce nontrivial deviations from the pendulum picture, especially in multi-contact or fast maneuvers. The results bridge LIPM with ZMP/DCM intuition and quantify when the classic picture is valid and when it fails.

Abstract: LIPM is everywhere in legged-locomotion control, but almost always as a modeling choice rather than as something the
  controller's cost actually prefers. This note tries to make that link more explicit. Working from a small centroidal
  OCP that penalizes the rate of angular momentum, we look at what its optimum tends to look like. Three things come
  out. With full-rank stance, the optimum drifts toward a pendular force pattern at a rate determined by the SVD of the
  moment Jacobian; the constant is set by foot-span geometry and matches the experiments to within 16%. With N=2 stance,
  as in trot, the friction cone introduces a lower bound on $\|\dot{H}_G\|$ that no amount of weight tuning fixes; we
  also see a non-smooth feasibility kink at a critical horizontal acceleration that we can write in closed form. Adding
  a task term that asks for a nonzero $\dot{H}_G$ moves the optimum off the pendular set in a predictable way. None of
  this is far from the classical ZMP/DCM picture. We test these claims on a point-mass quadruped and on the Unitree Go1
  in MuJoCo (open-loop QP and a torque-level closed-loop controller), and we note where the asymptotic story stops being
  a good description of what the closed loop actually does.

</details>


### [346] [MaMi-HOI: Harmonizing Global Kinematics and Local Geometry for Human-Object Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05756)
*Hao Wang,Shiqi Wang,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MaMi-HOI is a diffusion-based, hierarchical framework for 3D human–object interaction that balances macro-level motion fluidity with micro-level contact precision via two adapters: GAPA for dense object detail re-injection (residual snapping) and KHA for global kinematic harmony, enabling precise contact and long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the common gap in 3D HOI generation between achieving semantic alignment of pose/actions and maintaining strict object-object contact constraints, particularly as diffusion models deepen and geometric information can fade (geometric forgetting).

Method: Proposes MaMi-HOI, a two-adapter system layered on a diffusion-based HOI generator: (1) Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA) re-injects dense geometry details to enforce precise contact via residual snapping; (2) Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA) aligns whole-body pose with spatial objectives to prevent stiffness and preserve naturalness; operates within a hierarchical macro/micro framework to balance dynamics and precision; supports long-horizon, complex trajectories.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate simultaneous natural motion and precise contact, with extended generation to long-term tasks involving complex trajectories; shows improved balance between global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes; code released.

Conclusion: MaMi-HOI offers a cohesive, hierarchical solution for realistic 3D HOI generation that overcomes geometric forgetting and achieves both naturalistic motion and precise contact, enabling long-horizon, complex manipulation tasks in 3D environments.

Abstract: Generating realistic 3D Human-Object Interactions (HOI) is a fundamental task for applications ranging from embodied AI to virtual content creation, which requires harmonizing high-level semantic intent with strict low-level physical constraints. Existing methods excel at semantic alignment, however, they struggle to maintain precise object contact. We reveal a key finding termed \textit{Geometric Forgetting}: as diffusion model depth increases, semantic feature tend to overshadow object geometry feature, causing the model to lose its perception to object geometry. To address this, we propose MaMi-HOI, a hierarchical framework reconciling \textbf{Ma}cro-level kinematic fluidity with \textbf{Mi}cro-level spatial precision. First, to counteract geometric forgetting, we introduce the Geometry-Aware Proximity Adapter (GAPA), which explicitly re-injects dense object details to perform residual snapping corrections for precise contact. Nevertheless, such aggressive local enforcement can disrupt global dynamics, leading to robotic stiffness. In response, we introduce the Kinematic Harmony Adapter (KHA), which proactively aligns whole-body posture with spatial objectives, ensuring the skeleton actively accommodates constraints without compromising naturalness. Extensive experiments validate that MaMi-HOI simultaneously achieves natural motion and precise contact. Crucially, it extends generation capabilities to long-term tasks with complex trajectories, effectively bridging the gap between global navigation and high-fidelity manipulation in 3D scenes. Code is available at https://github.com/DON738110198/MaMi-HOI.git

</details>


### [347] [Resource-Constrained Robotic Planning in the face of Mixed Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05797)
*Yihao Yin,Pian Yu,Andrea Turrini,Zhiming Chi,Yong Li,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework for robust, resource-aware robot planning under uncertainty using CMDPST integrated with LTLf, offering two synthesis methods (unrolling and pruning) with empirical validation in a warehouse setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots face both quantitative and unquantifiable uncertainty and must operate without exhausting limited resources; there is a need for strategies that maximize task satisfaction probability while enforcing resource constraints.

Method: Model the system as a Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions (CMDPST) to capture nondeterminism, stochastic and unknown uncertainties, and resource consumption; combine with an LTLf specification; propose two synthesis approaches: (1) direct unrolling-based method, and (2) an optimized, state-space pruning method.

Result: Derives strategies that maximize the probability of satisfying the LTLf objective without depleting resources; the two methods are evaluated, with the optimized pruning approach showing improved performance; warehouse transportation experiments demonstrate practical effectiveness.

Conclusion: The CMDPST-LTLf framework enables robust, resource-constrained planning for robotic tasks under uncertainty; the optimized pruning method offers a scalable solution, validated on a warehouse network.

Abstract: Robots operate under significant uncertainty, from quantifiable noise to unquantifiable unknowns, and must account for strict operational constraints, such as limited resources. In this paper, we consider the problem of synthesizing robust strategies to guide a robot's actions in fulfilling a given task, while ensuring the system never exhausts its resources. To solve this problem, we first model the robotic system as a Consumption Markov Decision Process with Set-valued Transitions(CMDPST), a unified framework modelling nondeterministic actions, quantifiable and unquantifiable uncertainty, and resource consumption. Then, we combine the CMDPST with the task specification, expressed as a Linear Temporal Logic over finite traces (LTLf ) formula. Lastly, we address the resource constrained optimal robust strategy synthesis problem, which aims to synthesize a strategy that maximizes the probability of satisfying the LTLf objective without resource exhaustion. Our solution involves two techniques: a direct unrolling-based method and a more efficient, optimized approach that leverages state-space pruning for better performance. Experiments on a warehouse transportation network show the effectiveness of the proposed solutions.

</details>


### [348] [A Comparative Study of INDI and NDI with Nonlinear Disturbance Observer for Aerial Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05825)
*Benedetta Rota,Mirko Mizzoni,Amr Afifi,Pieter van Goor,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Simulation-based robustness comparison of INDI vs NDI+NDO for fully actuated aerial robots; INDI shows greater robustness to model mismatch and combined disturbances, while NDI+NDO matches nominal performance but is more sensitive under non-ideal conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess robustness of incremental INDI versus observer-based NDI+NDO for aerial robots under parameter variations, disturbances, and noise to guide controller design choices.

Method: Systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios; compare tracking performance, robustness, and control effort under param variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise for both schemes.

Result: INDI shows stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases; NDI+NDO largely matches nominal performance but is more sensitive under several non-ideal conditions.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental versus observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotics, informing controller design decisions.

Abstract: This work presents a simulation-based comparative robustness analysis of Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) and Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion augmented with a nonlinear disturbance observer (NDI+NDO) for fully actuated aerial robots. A systematic simulation campaign across representative operating scenarios is conducted, where we compare tracking performance, robustness, control effort, under parametric variations, external disturbances, and measurement noise. Results show that INDI demonstrates stronger robustness in several model-mismatch and combined-stress cases, while NDI+NDO primarily matches nominal performance but exhibits greater sensitivity under several non-ideal conditions. These findings provide practical guidance on the relative strengths and limitations of incremental and observer-based inversion strategies for aerial robotic applications.

</details>


### [349] [Cycle-resolved Cephalopod-Inspired Pulsed-Jet Robot With High-Volume Expulsion and Drag-Reduced Gliding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05875)
*Yiyuan Zhang,Anye Zhong,Junkai Chen,Wenci Xin,Cecilia Laschi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot using a rigid-soft origami mantle achieves large, actively driven body deformation to improve propulsion; cycle-resolved study of expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics with notable drag reduction and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cephalopod jet locomotion is a cycle of jet expulsion, passive gliding, and mantle refilling. Understanding and leveraging this cycle can yield more efficient, controllable pulsed-jet robots and insights into marine propulsion dynamics.

Method: Develop a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle combining rigid folding panels and compliant silicone. Implement a cycle-resolved experimental framework to separate effects of expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway on locomotion. Quantify cavity-volume reduction, drag-area reduction, and performance metrics across jetting cycles.

Result: The mantle achieves ~75% cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and ~75.7% drag-area reduction in contracted gliding. The robot reaches a peak speed of ~0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and >0.2 m/s average speed (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. Expelled-volume contraction, glide duration, and refill pathway all modulate speed and efficiency; mantle-aperture-like passive inlet valves assist refill.

Conclusion: A functional cephalopod-like pulsed-jet robot is demonstrated, offering a cycle-resolved platform to study expulsion–gliding–refilling dynamics and enabling geometry-guided deformation for enhanced propulsion.

Abstract: Cephalopod pulsed-jet locomotion is not a single isolated expulsion event, but a coordinated cycle involving jet expulsion, passive gliding, and mantle refilling. Inspired by this cycle-resolved biological strategy, this paper presents a cephalopod-inspired pulsed-jet robot with a rigid-soft hybrid origami mantle that enables large, actively driven, and geometry-guided body deformation. The proposed mantle integrates rigid folding panels with a compliant silicone framework, allowing a 75% effective cavity-volume reduction during expulsion and reducing the projected cross-sectional drag area by approximately 75.7% in the contracted gliding configuration. Using this platform, we formulate a cycle-resolved framework to separately investigate how expelled volume, glide duration, and refill pathway influence whole-cycle locomotion performance. Experiments show that the robot reaches a peak speed of approximately 0.5 m/s (3.8 BL/s) and an average speed exceeding 0.2 m/s (1.5 BL/s) within the first jetting cycle. The results further demonstrate the roles of high expelled-volume-ratio contraction in speed generation, reduced-drag-area gliding under different glide durations, and mantle-aperture-inspired passive inlet valves in assisting refill. This work provides both a robotic implementation of actively deformable cephalopod-like jet propulsion and a unified experimental platform for studying expulsion-gliding-refilling dynamics in pulsed-jet locomotion.

</details>


### [350] [Generating Roadside LiDAR Datasets from Vehicle-Side Datasets via Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05897)
*Yuhan Xia,Runxin Zhao,Hanyang Zhuang,Chunxiang Wang,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS) to generate labeled roadside LiDAR data from vehicle-side datasets via cross-view LiDAR synthesis, with point cloud completion and occupancy-based visibility constraints to bridge the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, enabling scalable data generation and improved roadside 3D object detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the shortage of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets, which limits training high-performance roadside perception models.

Method: VRS framework: (1) LiDAR novel view synthesis from vehicle-side data to create roadside viewpoints; (2) vehicle point cloud completion to recover missing geometry; (3) occupancy-based visibility constraint to account for large viewpoint changes in cross-view rendering; (4) flexible multi-view rendering for scalable roadside data generation.

Result: Extensive experiments on roadside 3D object detection show that synthesized data complements real roadside data, mitigates limitations of scarce real roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

Conclusion: VRS enables scalable generation of labeled roadside LiDAR data from vehicle-side datasets, mitigating domain gaps and enhancing generalization for roadside perception tasks.

Abstract: Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) require reliable environmental perception to support safe and efficient transportation. With the rapid development of Vehicle-to-everything (V2X), roadside perception has become an effective means to extend sensing coverage and improve traffic safety. However, the scarcity of large-scale annotated roadside LiDAR datasets poses a major challenge for training high-performance roadside perception models. In this paper, we introduce Vehicle-to-Roadside LiDAR Synthesis (VRS), a data synthesis framework that generates labeled roadside LiDAR datasets from vehicle-side datasets via LiDAR novel view synthesis. To mitigate the vehicle-to-roadside domain gap, VRS employs vehicle point cloud completion to compensate for missing geometry in vehicle-side observations, and introduces an occupancy-based visibility constraint to handle large viewpoint changes during cross-view rendering. The proposed framework enables flexible multi-view rendering for scalable roadside data generation. Extensive experiments on roadside 3D object detection demonstrate that the synthesized data effectively complements real roadside data, mitigates the limitations of limited real-world roadside data, and improves generalization to unseen roadside viewpoints.

</details>


### [351] [DexSynRefine: Synthesizing and Refining Human-Object Interaction Motion for Physically Feasible Dexterous Robot Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05925)
*Hyesung Lee,Hyunwoo Jung,Si-Hwan Heo,Sungwook Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSynRefine combines HOI-driven trajectory synthesis with a task-space residual policy and a dynamics adaptation module to enable sim-to-real dexterous manipulation, achieving consistent real-robot transfer across five tasks and substantial gains over kinematic retargeting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HOI data provide scalable supervision for dexterous manipulation, but demonstrations are sparse and kinetic-only; direct execution is hindered by embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics. The paper aims to bridge this gap by learning a manipulable reference that can be physically grounded and adapted to real dynamics.

Method: Three coupled components: (1) HOI-MMFP — a task- and initial-state-conditioned extension of motion manifold primitives synthesizing hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations; (2) a task-space residual RL policy that grounds the synthesized reference in physics while preserving the kinematic structure; (3) a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module enabling sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history.

Result: In five dexterous tasks (pick-and-place, tool use, object reorientation), the task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulation and transfers to a real robot on all tasks, delivering 50–70 percentage-point gains over kinematic retargeting.

Conclusion: DexSynRefine offers a principled, end-to-end framework for HOI-based dexterous manipulation with robust sim-to-real transfer and substantial performance gains, demonstrating the viability of using sparse HOI data to govern complex hand-object interactions across varied tasks.

Abstract: Learning dexterous manipulation from human-object interaction (HOI) data is a scalable alternative to teleoperation, but HOI demonstrations are sparse and provide only kinematic motion that is not directly executable under embodiment mismatch and contact-rich dynamics. We present DexSynRefine, a framework with three coupled components: HOI-MMFP, a task- and object-initial-state-conditioned extension of motion manifold primitives that synthesizes coordinated hand-object trajectories from sparse HOI demonstrations; a task-space residual RL policy that physically grounds the synthesized reference while inheriting its kinematic structure; and a contact-and-dynamics adaptation module that enables sim-to-real transfer from proprioceptive history. Across five dexterous manipulation tasks spanning pick-and-place, tool use, and object reorientation, our task-space residual policy outperforms prior action-representation baselines in simulations and transfers to a real robot on all five tasks, improving over kinematic retargeting by 50-70 percentage points.

</details>


### [352] [Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion for Universal Goal-Oriented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05960)
*Zhixuan Shen,Yijie Zeng,Shengxiang Luo,Tianrui Li,Haonan Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PLMD uses a DDPM-based map completion diffusion model to predict obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved BEV regions, enabling robust GOAL localization in partially observed environments and integrating with existing semantic-map navigation; it achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Goal-Oriented Navigation requires locating a specific object in unknown environments, but current map-based methods rely on complete maps and suffer from inconsistent semantic associations between known and unseen regions. A diffusion-based completion framework can coherently infer unobserved areas.

Method: Introduce Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), a DDPM-based map completion model that (1) predicts obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions, (2) enforces structural consistency between observed and unobserved obstacle layouts, (3) incorporates obstacle priors into semantic denoising, and (4) plugs into existing semantic-map-driven navigation pipelines to improve goal localization in partially observed maps.

Result: Extensive experiments show that PLMD (i) expands the exploitable area of unknown maps, (ii) integrates seamlessly with current navigation strategies leveraging semantic maps, and (iii) achieves state-of-the-art performance across three GON tasks.

Conclusion: PLMD provides a principled diffusion-based mechanism for completing BEV maps under partial observation, mitigating semantic misalignment and enabling accurate goal localization, thereby advancing GON in embodied vision.

Abstract: In embodied vision, Goal-Oriented Navigation (GON) requires robots to locate a specific goal within an unexplored environment. The primary challenge of GON arises from the need to construct a Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) map to understand the environment while simultaneously localizing an unobserved goal. Existing map-based methods typically employ self-centered semantic maps, often facing challenges such as reliance on complete maps or inconsistent semantic association. To this end, we propose Plug-and-Play Label Map Diffusion (PLMD), which defines a novel map completion diffusion model based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM). PLMD generates obstacle and semantic labels for unobserved regions through a diffusion-based completion process, thereby enabling goal localization even in partially observed environments. Moreover, it mitigates inconsistent semantic association by leveraging structural consistency between known and unknown obstacle layouts and integrating obstacle priors into the semantic denoising process. By substituting predicted labels for unobserved regions, robots can accurately localize the specified objects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLMD \textbf{(I)} effectively expands the region of unknown maps, \textbf{(II)} integrates seamlessly into existing navigation strategies that rely on semantic maps, \textbf{(III)} achieves state-of-the-art performance on three GON tasks.

</details>


### [353] [Accurate Trajectory Tracking with MPCC for Flapping-Wing MAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06042)
*Charbel Toumieh,Jack Zeng,Niel Mistry,Dario Floreano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a real-time Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) framework for bird-scale flapping-wing MAVs, using arc-length trajectory tracking with online progress optimization to handle coupled aerodynamics without predefined timing, validated on XFly with high-precision tracking and substantial performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bird-scale ornithopters exhibit tightly coupled lift, airspeed, and turning dynamics. Conventional cascaded controllers treat axes independently, causing tracking errors during complex maneuvers, and time-parameterized tracking relies on predefined speed profiles that are hard to robustly generate for these dynamics.

Method: Develop a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures dominant coupled aerodynamics and integrate it into a Model Predictive Contouring Control framework. The MPCC tracks arc-length parameterized trajectories and optimizes progress online, removing the need for predefined timing while enabling real-time nonlinear optimization.

Result: Experimental validation on the XFly ornithopter shows mean trajectory deviation of 6.5–9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s while following circular and 3D racing trajectories, representing about a 10-fold improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

Conclusion: The proposed MPCC approach with a compact coupled-dynamics model enables accurate, real-time trajectory tracking for bird-scale ornithopters without predefined timing, supporting high-speed, complex maneuvers.

Abstract: Flapping-wing micro aerial vehicles offer quieter and safer operation than rotary-wing drones, yet achieving precise autonomous control of bird-scale ornithopters remains challenging: lift, airspeed, and turning authority are tightly coupled and governed by only a few control inputs. Conventional cascaded controllers treat altitude, speed, and heading independently, producing persistent tracking errors during complex maneuvers, while time-parameterized trajectory tracking requires predefined speed profiles that existing methods cannot robustly produce for these coupled dynamics. We address both limitations simultaneously with a Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) approach that tracks arc-length-parameterized trajectories while optimizing progress online, eliminating the need for predefined timing. However, MPCC requires a dynamical model that captures the coupled aerodynamics without exceeding the computational budget of real-time nonlinear optimization. Here, we propose a compact, continuously differentiable model that captures the dominant couplings of bird-scale ornithopters, enabling real-time predictive control. We validated the method with the XFly ornithopter flying along circular and three-dimensional racing trajectories and achieved a mean deviation from the reference trajectory between 6.5 and 9 cm at speeds up to 3 m/s, which represents an almost 10-fold improvement over prior ornithopter control methods.

</details>


### [354] [Monitoring autonomous persistent surveillance missions using invariance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06062)
*Vladislav Nenchev,Prodromos Sotiriadis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable, invariant-based runtime monitor for persistent surveillance by robots with black-box autonomy, using a compositional, decentralized approach; soundness/completeness hold under independence; demonstrated on a real robot in a labyrinth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable runtime monitoring of persistent surveillance when the autonomy stack is a black box, especially in large environments where computing a full-system invariant is intractable.

Method: Model the closed-loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter-varying dynamics. Compute an offline invariant; to scale, compute low-dimensional invariants for each uncertainty region decentralarily and online check their conjunction. Under independence assumptions, this compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant.

Result: The compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full invariant under independence. A real-robot labyrinth case study demonstrates practical applicability.

Conclusion: Decentralized invariant-based monitoring offers a scalable and practically viable approach for runtime assurance in persistent surveillance with opaque autonomy stacks.

Abstract: This paper studies runtime monitoring for persistent surveillance by autonomous robots when the autonomy stack is a black box. The environment is partitioned into finitely many parts, each carrying an uncertainty state that decreases when observed and increases otherwise. We model the closed loop as a state-dependent hybrid system with linear parameter varying dynamics and design a monitor based on an invariant computed offline.
