As this invariant is typically hard to obtain for large to-be-surveyed spaces, we propose a compositional monitor obtained by decentralized computation of low-dimensional invariant sets for each uncertainty region, and checking their conjunction online. Under common independence assumptions, the compositional monitor is sound and complete with respect to the full-system invariant. The approach is applied in a case study with a real robot persistently monitoring a labyrinth, emphasizing its applicability in practice.

</details>


### [355] [VLA-GSE: Boosting Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning in VLA with Generalized and Specialized Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06175)
*Yuhua Jiang,Junjie Lu,Xinyao Qin,Xiaoyu Chen,Kaixin Wang,Feifei Gao,Li Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework for vision-language-action models, VLA-GSE, uses spectral decomposition to create generalized and specialized experts, enabling effective robotic control with ~2.5% trainable parameters and improved zero-shot, multimodal, and real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting vision-language-action (VLA) models to robotic control is challenging due to overfitting and catastrophic forgetting with full fine-tuning. Standard PEFT preserves pretrained knowledge but struggles to match transfer performance for robotics under fixed parameter budgets.

Method: Freeze the backbone and perform spectral decomposition. Allocate leading singular components to generalized (shared) experts and disjoint residual components to specialized (routed) experts. This generalized-specialized expert split yields more adaptation capacity within a fixed trainable-parameter budget; during training, only 2.51% of parameters are updated.

Result: With the same parameter budget, VLA-GSE outperforms FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pretrained VLM capabilities comparably to LoRA on multimodal benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation under multiple distribution shifts.

Conclusion: VLA-GSE provides an effective, parameter-efficient path to adapt VLA models for robotics, balancing knowledge preservation with improved generalization and practical manipulation performance; source code is available.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models inherit rich visual-semantic priors from pre-trained vision-language backbones, but adapting them to robotic control remains challenging. Full fine-tuning (FFT) is prone to overfitting on downstream robotic data and catastrophic forgetting of pretrained vision-language capabilities. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) better preserves pre-trained knowledge, yet existing PEFT methods still struggle to adapt effectively to robot control tasks. To address this gap, we propose VLA-GSE, a parameter-efficient VLA fine-tuning framework that improves control adaptation while retaining PEFT's knowledge preservation advantage. Specifically, VLA-GSE (Generalized and Specialized Experts) is initialized by spectrally decomposing the frozen backbone, assigning leading singular components to generalized experts (shared experts) and disjoint residual components to specialized experts (routed experts). This decomposition improves adaptation capacity under a fixed trainable-parameter budget. Under a comparable parameter budget, VLA-GSE updates only 2.51% of the full model parameters and consistently outperforms strong FFT and PEFT baselines. It achieves 81.2% average zero-shot success on LIBERO-Plus, preserves pre-trained VLM capability comparably to LoRA on multimodal understanding benchmarks, and improves real-world manipulation success under multiple distribution shifts. Code is available at: https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/VLA-GSE

</details>


### [356] [When to Trust Imagination: Adaptive Action Execution for World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06222)
*Rui Wang,Yue Zhang,Jiehong Lin,Kuncheng Luo,Jianan Wang,Zhongrui Wang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive WAM execution via FFDC with Mixture-of-Horizon Training; a verifier uses predicted futures, observations, and language to decide action chunk length, enabling adaptive rollout and improved efficiency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WAMs predict future observations and actions but typically use a fixed-length rollout, causing misalignment with real dynamics and reduced performance in uncertain phases.

Method: Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC) jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate trust in the remaining rollout; yields adaptive action chunking. Mixture-of-Horizon Training enhances long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution.

Result: On RoboTwin: ~69.1% fewer WAM forward passes and ~34.0% faster execution; +2.54% higher success than short-chunk baseline. Real-world tests: +35% improvement in success rate.

Conclusion: FFDC enables adaptive horizon execution with a favorable robustness-efficiency trade-off, maintaining long-horizon efficiency while improving responsiveness during difficult phases; Mixture-of-Horizon Training improves trajectory coverage for adaptive execution.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by jointly predicting future visual observations and future actions. However, current WAMs typically execute a fixed number of predicted actions after each model inference, leaving the robot blind to whether the imagined future remains consistent with the actual physical rollout. In this work, we formulate adaptive WAM execution as a future-reality verification problem: the robot should execute longer when the WAM-predicted future remains reliable, and replan earlier when reality deviates from imagination. To this end, we propose Future Forward Dynamics Causal Attention (FFDC), a lightweight verifier that jointly reasons over predicted future actions, predicted visual dynamics, real observations, and language instructions to estimate whether the remaining action rollout can still be trusted. FFDC enables adaptive action chunk sizes as an emergent consequence of prediction-observation consistency, preserving the efficiency of long-horizon execution while restoring responsiveness in contact-rich or difficult phases. We further introduce Mixture-of-Horizon Training to improve long-horizon trajectory coverage for adaptive execution. Experiments on the RoboTwin benchmark and in the real world demonstrate that our method achieves a strong robustness-efficiency trade-off: on RoboTwin, it reduces WAM forward passes by 69.10% and execution time by 34.02%, while improving success rate by 2.54% over the short-chunk baseline; in real-world experiments, it improves success rate by 35%.

</details>


### [357] [RobotEQ: Transitioning from Passive Intelligence to Active Intelligence in Embodied AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06234)
*Kuofei Fang,Xinyi Che,Haomin Ouyang,Shufan Zhang,Xuehao Wang,Qi Liu,Liyi Liu,Chenqi Zhang,Wenxi Cai,Wenyu Dai,Jinyang Wu,Fan Zhang,Haoyu Chen,Bin He,Zheng Lian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces RobotEQ, a benchmark for active intelligence in embodied AI. It includes RobotEQ-Data (1,900 egocentric images across 10 categories and 56 subcategories with 5,353 action-judgment and 1,286 spatial-grounding questions) and RobotEQ-Bench for evaluation. Findings show current models underperform, especially in spatial grounding, but retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with external social-norm knowledge improves performance. Aims to promote active social compliance in robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots must understand permissible vs. prohibited actions in real-world, uncommanded situations to integrate into human society; existing work focuses on following explicit instructions or passive guidance, lacking benchmarks for active social compliance.

Method: Create RobotEQ-Data dataset of 1,900 egocentric images across 10 embodied categories and 56 subcategories, annotated with 5,353 action-judgment and 1,286 spatial-grounding questions; establish RobotEQ-Bench for evaluation; conduct experiments with state-of-the-art models; explore RAG with external social-norm knowledge bases.

Result: Current models struggle to achieve reliable active intelligence, notably in spatial grounding; using RAG to incorporate external social-norm knowledge generally improves performance.

Conclusion: RobotEQ provides a benchmark and dataset to evaluate and advance active social compliance in embodied AI; highlights gaps in current models and shows potential of RAG-based integration of social norms.

Abstract: Embodied AI is a prominent research topic in both academia and industry. Current research centers on completing tasks based on explicit user instructions. However, for robots to integrate into human society, they must understand which actions are permissible and which are prohibited, even without explicit commands. We refer to the user-guided AI as passive intelligence and the unguided AI as active intelligence. This paper introduces RobotEQ, the first benchmark for active intelligence, aiming to assess whether existing models can comprehend and adhere to social norms in embodied scenarios. First, we construct RobotEQ-Data, a dataset consisting of 1,900 egocentric images, spanning 10 representative embodied categories and 56 subcategories. Through extensive manual annotation, we provide 5,353 action judgment questions and 1,286 spatial grounding questions, specifying appropriate robot actions across diverse scenarios. Furthermore, we establish RobotEQ-Bench to evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art models on this task. Experimental results show that current models still fall short in achieving reliable active intelligence, particularly in spatial grounding. Meanwhile, we observe that leveraging RAG techniques to incorporate external social norm knowledge bases can generally enhance performance. This work can facilitate the transition of robotics from user-guided passive manipulation to active social compliance.

</details>


### [358] [CKT-WAM: Parameter-Efficient Context Knowledge Transfer Between World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06247)
*Yuhua Jiang,Yijun Guo,Hongbing Yang,Guojun Lei,Nuo Chen,Yinuo Zhang,Shaoqiang Yan,Bo Lin,Feifei Gao,Biqing Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CKT-WAM is a parameter-efficient, context-based knowledge transfer from a teacher WAM to a student WAM using a compact text-embedding context assembled via LQCA and adapters, yielding strong zero-shot generalization and long-horizon manipulation with minimal trainable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer of knowledge across heterogeneous world action models is hampered by mismatched latent interfaces, high adaptation cost, and rigid distillation objectives; a lightweight, context-centric approach is sought.

Method: Extract intermediate teacher hidden states, compress tokens with learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), and pass them through an always-on generalized adapter, a lightweight router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters; append the resulting context to the student's conditioning text embeddings to inject knowledge.

Result: Consistent improvement in zero-shot generalization and best overall score on LIBERO-Plus (86.1% total success) with only 1.17% trainable parameters; approaches full fine-tuning; strong real-world long-horizon manipulation (average 83.3% across four tasks).

Conclusion: Proposes CKT-WAM as a parameter-efficient, context-based transfer mechanism that gracefully injects knowledge into WAMs, addressing heterogeneity and reducing adaptation cost.

Abstract: World action models (WAMs) provide a powerful generative framework for embodied control, yet transferring knowledge across heterogeneous WAMs remains challenging due to mismatched latent interfaces, high adaptation cost, and the rigidity of conventional distillation objectives. We propose \textbf{CKT-WAM}, a parameter-efficient \textbf{C}ontext \textbf{K}nowledge \textbf{T}ransfer framework that transfers teacher WAM's knowledge into a student WAM through a compact context in the text embedding space, rather than output imitation or dense hidden-state matching. Specifically, CKT-WAM extracts intermediate teacher hidden states, reduces the number of tokens via compressors' learnable-query cross attention (LQCA), and transforms them through an always-on generalized adapter, a lightweight router, and sparsely activated specialized adapters. The resulting context is then appended to the student's conditioning textual embeddings, thereby injecting the transferred knowledge into the student with minimal architectural modification. Experiments show that CKT-WAM consistently improves zero-shot generalization and achieves the best overall performance on LIBERO-Plus, reaching 86.1\% total success rate with only 1.17\% trainable parameters, while approaching full fine-tuning performance. Beyond simulation, CKT-WAM also demonstrates strong real-world long-horizon manipulation ability, achieving the best average success rate of 83.3\% across four multi-step and long-horizon tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/YuhuaJiang2002/CKT-WAM.

</details>


### [359] [Toward Visually Realistic Simulation: A Benchmark for Evaluating Robot Manipulation in Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06311)
*Yixin Zhu,Zixiong Wang,Jian Yang,Jin Xie,Jingyi Yu,Jiayuan Gu,Beibei Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VISER is a visually realistic benchmark for robot manipulation in simulation that mitigates the sim-to-real visual gap by focusing on lighting and material realism. It builds a high-fidelity 3D asset dataset with PBR materials, generates scenes with curated/layouts, and uses multimodal LLMs for material-aware segmentation and retrieval to enable scalable asset generation. It defines tasks (grasping, placing, long-horizon) and demonstrates strong real-world correlation (Pearson r = 0.92) across policies, enabling scalable VLA benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monte Carlo and systematic analyses show lighting and material critically influence geometric reasoning and spatial grounding in robots, yet current benchmarks overlook these factors. Improving visual realism is essential for reliable sim-to-real evaluation of manipulation policies.

Method: Construct a high-fidelity dataset of 1,000+ 3D assets with physically-based rendering (PBR) materials; create 3D scenes from assets via curated layouts or generative methods. Develop an automated pipeline leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to perform material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval, enabling scalable, physically plausible asset generation. Build diverse evaluation tasks (grasping, placing, long-horizon) for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) benchmarking.

Result: The VISER benchmark correlates strongly with real-world performance, achieving an average Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92 across different policies, demonstrating improved predictivity of simulation-based evaluation.

Conclusion: VISER provides a scalable, high-fidelity simulation benchmark that reduces the sim-to-real visual gap for robot manipulation, enabling reliable and reproducible evaluation of VLA models and policies.

Abstract: Reliable simulation evaluation of robot manipulation policies serves as a high-fidelity proxy for real-world performance. Although existing benchmarks cover a wide range of task categories, they lack visual realism, creating a large domain gap between simulation and reality. This undermines the reliability of simulation-based evaluation in predicting real-world performance. To mitigate the sim-to-real visual gap, we conduct a systematic analysis to isolate the effects of lighting and material. Our results show that these factors play a critical role in geometric reasoning and spatial grounding, yet are largely overlooked in existing benchmarks. Motivated by the analysis, we propose VISER, a visually realistic benchmark for evaluating robot manipulation in simulation. VISER features a high-fidelity dataset of over 1,000 3D assets with physically-based rendering (PBR) materials, along with 3D scenes created from these assets through curated layouts or generation. To this end, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for material-aware part segmentation and material retrieval, enabling scalable generation of physically plausible assets. Building on the high-fidelity 3D asset dataset, we construct diverse evaluation tasks, such as grasping, placing, and long-horizon tasks, enabling scalable and reproducible assessment of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Our benchmark shows a strong correlation between simulation and real-world performance, achieving an average Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.92 across different policies.

</details>


### [360] [AssistDLO: Assistive Teleoperation for Deformable Linear Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06323)
*Berk Guler,Simon Manschitz,Kay Pompetzki,Jan Peters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AssistDLO integrates real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance, and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller (SA-CBF) for deformable linear object manipulation. It improves naive users' performance and succeeds at preserving geometry, revealing that adaptive, user- and material-aware autonomy is required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DLO manipulation is difficult due to infinite-dimensional configuration spaces and nonlinear dynamics. Teleoperation suffers from depth uncertainty and perception challenges. A shared-autonomy framework that preserves geometry and allows operator authority could improve performance.

Method: Real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). Evaluated in a bimanual knot-untangling study (N=22) with ropes of varying length and rigidity. Compared SA-CBF against VA, examining effects across operator expertise.

Result: SA-CBF significantly boosts naive users’ success (71% to 88%) and helps with stiffer ropes; VA preferred by experts; long, highly compliant ropes benefit more from visual support than localized action assistance.

Conclusion: Fixed strategies are inadequate for DLO teleoperation. Adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy is essential to balance autonomy with operator control and to accommodate varying rope properties and operator expertise.

Abstract: Manipulating Deformable Linear Objects (DLOs) is challenging in robotics due to their infinite-dimensional configuration space and complex nonlinear dynamics. In teleoperation, depth uncertainty hinders state perception and reaction. AssistDLO addresses this challenge as an assistive teleoperation framework for DLO manipulation that combines real-time multi-view state estimation, visual assistance (VA), and a geometry-aware shared-autonomy controller based on Control Barrier Functions (SA-CBF). While traditional shared autonomy methods often rely on simple geometric attractors and may fail to preserve DLO geometry, SA-CBF acts as a geometry-aware funnel, facilitating precise grasping while preserving the operator's high-level authority. The framework is evaluated in a bimanual knot-untangling user study (N = 22) using ropes with varying length and rigidity. Results show that the effectiveness of the assistance depends strongly on operator expertise and DLO properties. SA-CBF provides the strongest gains for naive users, acting as a skill equalizer that increases task success from 71% to 88%, and is effective for stiffer ropes. Conversely, expert users prefer VA, and highly compliant, long ropes benefit more from visual support than localized action assistance. Ultimately, these findings demonstrate that effective DLO teleoperation cannot rely on a fixed strategy, highlighting the critical need for adaptive, user-aware, and material-aware shared autonomy.

</details>


### [361] [TouchDrive: Electronics-Free Tactile Sensing Interface for Assistive Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06432)
*Jing Xu,Xuezhi Niu,Didem Gurdur Broo,Klas Hjort*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TouchDrive offers an electronics-free tactile interface for assistive grasping that converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback via a passive mechanical loop, enabling precise, robust manipulation of delicate objects without electronics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce system complexity and cost, improve accessibility, and provide real-time tactile feedback to users during grasping tasks.

Method: Use a pneumatic normally closed valve, compressed air tank, sensing element, and haptic actuator to create a valve-mediated switching mechanism that directly translates contact forces into pneumatic feedback, forming a single passive loop without electronics.

Result: Validated across diverse robotic platforms; demonstrated reliable performance in assistive grasping tasks, including handling up to 20 objects such as fruits and everyday items.

Conclusion: Electronics-free tactile sensing interfaces like TouchDrive can empower users to modulate grasp forces for delicate manipulation and have broad applicability in assistive robotics, with potential for simpler, cost-effective deployment.

Abstract: Assistive robotic grasping plays an important role in enabling safe and adaptive manipulation of diverse objects. However, existing systems often rely on electronic sensing and multi-stage processing pipelines, increasing system complexity and reducing accessibility. To address these limitations, we present TouchDrive, a cost-effective, electronics-free tactile sensing interface for assistive grasping. TouchDrive directly converts contact forces into pneumatic feedback through valve-mediated switching, integrating sensing, signal generation, and feedback within a single passive mechanical loop. The system can be employed using a pneumatic normally closed valve, a compressed air tank, sensing element, and haptic feedback actuator without electronics. By delivering tactile cues, TouchDrive empowers users to modulate grasp forces, enabling precise and robust delicate manipulation of compliant and fragile objects. The interface has been validated across diverse robotic platforms, consistently demonstrating reliable performance and practical applicability in assistive grasping tasks, such as handling fruits and everyday items (up to 20 objects).

</details>


### [362] [GA3T: A Ground-Aerial Terrain Traversability Dataset for Heterogeneous Robot Teams in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06478)
*Siwei Cai,Knut Peterson,Quan Tran,Christian Ricks,Dhanush Parthasarathy,Amir Kaidarov,Neil Deshpande,Sukaina Najm,David Han,Lifeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A real-world cross-view air-ground multi-robot dataset (GA3T) for off-road perception, combining ground LiDAR/camera/IMU/GPS with aerial RGB/thermal imagery; 13k synchronized frames (~29 min) across four unstructured environments; includes SAM3 zero-shot segmentation and 8k manually labeled images; enables occlusion-aware cross-view perception and traversability research in unstructured terrain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of real-world, overlapping multi-modal data from ground and aerial platforms in unstructured terrain, enabling cross-view fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding beyond SLAM or simulated driving.

Method: Dataset collected with a ground robot (Clearpath Husky UGV) and an aerial drone (Autel EVO II). Sensors: ground – 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, GPS; aerial – RGB camera, thermal/IR, GPS. Data gathered in 4 environments with ~13,000 synchronized frames over ~29 minutes. Annotations include SAM3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images; early-spring canopy allows partial visibility for occlusion-aware collaboration.

Result: Provides a real-world, cross-modal, cross-view dataset enabling research in cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in unstructured environments; includes both automatic and manual annotations suitable for training and benchmarking.

Conclusion: GA3T fills a gap in real-world, multi-robot perception datasets by offering cross-modal and cross-view data in unstructured terrain, facilitating air-ground perception fusion and occlusion-aware collaboration to improve perception and traversability in real off-road settings.

Abstract: Heterogeneous air-ground robot teams combine complementary sensing modalities, mobility characteristics, and spatial viewpoints that can significantly enhance perception in complex outdoor environments. However, progress in multi-robot collaborative perception has been constrained by the lack of real-world datasets featuring overlapping multi-modal observations from platforms operating in unstructured terrain. We present GA3T (Ground-Aerial Team for Terrain Traversal), a real-world multi-robot collaborative perception dataset collected using a Clearpath Husky UGV and an Autel EVO~II UAV across diverse unstructured environments, including forest trails, rocky paths, muddy terrain, snow piles, and grass-covered fields. The ground platform provides 3D LiDAR, stereo camera, IMU, and GPS data, while the aerial platform contributes RGB imagery, thermal/infrared observations, and GPS from a complementary overhead viewpoint, allowing for rich cross-modal and cross-view perception. The dataset is collected in 4 unique environments, with over 13,000 synchronized frames across approximately 29 minutes of operation, and includes both SAM~3-based zero-shot segmentation and over 8,000 manually labeled images. A unique aspect of the dataset is its early-spring collection period, during which sparse tree canopies allow the aerial robot to partially observe the ground robot and terrain through the trees, allowing for occlusion-aware collaborative perception. Unlike prior multi-robot datasets that focus on SLAM or simulated cooperative driving, GA3T is specifically designed to support research on cross-view perception, air-ground viewpoint fusion, traversability estimation, and collaborative scene understanding in real off-road environments.

</details>


### [363] [OA-WAM: Object-Addressable World Action Model for Robust Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06481)
*Yushan Liu,Peibo Sun,Shoujie Li,Yifan Xie,Lingfeng Zhang,Xintao Chao,Shiyuan Dong,Fang Chen,Xiao-Ping Zhang,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OA-WAM introduces an Object-Addressable World Action Model that uses per-slot, addressable object representations to decouple identity from content, enabling robust object-grounded policy execution in dynamic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Holistic world representations in prior WAMs entangle object identity with context, making instruction grounding difficult when objects move or undergo scene changes. An object-addressable interface could improve instruction grounding, robustness to perturbations, and reactivity.

Method: Frame-wise decomposition into N+1 slots (one robot, N objects). Each slot has a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector. Slots fuse with text, image, proprioception, and past actions in a block-causal Transformer. A world head predicts next-frame slot states; a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every Transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens.

Result: OA-WAM matches or exceeds strong baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on a seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields swap-binding cosine of 0.87, far above holistic baselines (≤0.09).

Conclusion: Explicit, addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations, enabling better grounding and manipulation in dynamic environments.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) enhance Vision-Language-Action policies by jointly predicting scene evolution and robot actions, but existing methods usually represent the predicted world as holistic images, video tokens, or global latents. These representations are difficult for an action decoder to address when an instruction refers to a particular object, especially under scene shifts where object identity is entangled with context. We propose OA-WAM, an Object-Addressable World Action Model for robust robot manipulation. OA-WAM decomposes each frame into N+1 slot states, with one robot slot and N object slots. Each slot contains a persistent address vector and a time-varying content vector, and is fused with text, image, proprioception, and past-action tokens in a block-causal sequence. A world head predicts next-frame slot states, while a flow-matching action head decodes a 16-step continuous action chunk in the same forward pass. Addressability is enforced by routing cross-slot attention through address-only keys and resetting the address slice at every transformer layer, separating which object to act on from what that object currently is without adding extra tokens. OA-WAM matches strong VLA and WAM baselines on LIBERO (97.8%) and SimplerEnv (79.3%), reaches state-of-the-art performance on the most relevant LIBERO-Plus geometric axes, and remains competitive on the seven-axis aggregate. A causal slot-intervention test yields a swap-binding cosine of 0.87, versus at most 0.09 for holistic baselines. These results suggest that addressable object states provide an effective interface for robust world-action modeling under scene perturbations.

</details>


### [364] [Lie Group Formulation of Recursive Dynamics Algorithms of Higher Order for Floating-Base Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06498)
*Ahmed Ali,Chiara Gabellieri,Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Extends Lie-group Newton–Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics to compute higher-order time derivatives for floating-base trees using spatial twists; yields closed-form equations of motion, a passive Coriolis matrix, and an invariant articulated inertia; demonstrated on a 12-DoF aerial manipulator with analytic dynamics and derivatives up to the fifth order; shows quadratic scaling with derivative order vs exponential scaling for auto-differentiation baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient, stable computation of high-order time derivatives for complex floating-base robotics while preserving key physical properties (passivity, inertia invariance) and providing analytic dynamics to support control and planning.

Method: Develop recursive algorithms for higher-order derivatives of floating-base tree robots with base on SE(3) and open kinematic trees with configuration on T^{n1}×R^{n2}, employing a spatial twist representation. Derive closed-form equations of motion, identify an admissible Coriolis matrix that satisfies a passivity property, and prove that the articulated inertia tensor remains invariant across time derivatives. Obtain analytic forward/inverse dynamics and their first derivatives for a 12-DoF system; validate numerically up to the fifth derivative and benchmark computational cost against automatic differentiation (AD).

Result: Analytic expressions for geometric forward and inverse dynamics and their first derivatives; numerical validation up to fifth-order derivatives for a 12-DoF aerial manipulator; computational cost scales quadratically with derivative order, outperforming AD baselines which exhibit exponential scaling in the tested regime.

Conclusion: The work extends high-order dynamic analysis to floating-base trees with preserved inertia properties and passivity, providing efficient, scalable methods for control and planning that require high-order derivatives and demonstrating practical utility on a nontrivial aerial manipulator.

Abstract: In this paper, we describe procedures for computing higher-order time derivatives of the Lie-group Newton-Euler, Articulated-Body Inertia, and hybrid dynamics algorithms for floating-base trees, where the base configuration evolves on SE(3) and the attached mechanism is an open kinematic tree with configuration on the (n1+n2)-dimensional manifold T^{n1} \times R^{n2}, using spatial representation of twists. After presenting the algorithms, we collect the resulting recursions into closed-form equations of motion, identifying an admissible Coriolis matrix satisfying the passivity property, and showing that the articulated inertia tensor remains unchanged across all time derivatives. We then apply the developed methods to a 12-DoF aerial manipulator to derive analytical expressions for its geometric forward and inverse dynamics along with their first time derivatives whereas the numerical simulations successfully evaluate these dynamics up to fifth order. Finally, to demonstrate their practical utility, we benchmark the proposed extensions and show that, in the considered tests, their computational cost scales quadratically with the derivative order, whereas the automatic-differentiation baseline exhibits exponential scaling.

</details>


### [365] [ReActor: Reinforcement Learning for Physics-Aware Motion Retargeting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06593)
*David Müller,Agon Serifi,Sammy Christen,Ruben Grandia,Espen Knoop,Moritz Bächer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A bilevel optimization framework retargets human motion to diverse robot morphologies by jointly adapting references and training a tracking policy via RL, using approximate gradients and sparse semantic correspondences, to produce physically plausible motions integrated with physics simulation for robust imitation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retargeting human motion to robots is error-prone, often causing foot sliding, self-collisions, or dynamic infeasibility. There is a need for a method that can adapt motions to different morphologies with minimal manual tuning while enabling effective imitation learning.

Method: Joint bilevel optimization: upper level adapts reference motions to robot morphology; lower level trains a tracking policy with reinforcement learning. An approximate gradient is derived for the upper-level loss to make optimization tractable. The approach uses sparse semantic rigid-body correspondences and a parameterization that preserves motion characteristics, and integrates retargeting within physics simulation.

Result: Produces physically plausible motions across morphologies, including quadruped retargeting, validated in simulation and on hardware; enhances robustness of imitation learning by avoiding compensatory artifacts.

Conclusion: The framework enables morphology-aware retargeting with reduced manual tuning, yielding high-quality motion data for imitation learning and better sim-to-real transfer due to physics integration and a generalizable parameterization.

Abstract: Retargeting human kinematic reference motion onto a robot's morphology remains a formidable challenge. Existing methods often produce physical inconsistencies, such as foot sliding, self-collisions, or dynamically infeasible motions, which hinder downstream imitation learning. We propose a bilevel optimization framework that jointly adapts reference motions to a robot's morphology while training a tracking policy using reinforcement learning. To make the optimization tractable, we derive an approximate gradient for the upper-level loss. Our framework requires only a sparse set of semantic rigid-body correspondences and eliminates the need for manual tuning by identifying optimal values for a parameterization expressive enough to preserve characteristic motion across different embodiments. Moreover, by integrating retargeting directly with physics simulation, we produce physically plausible motions that facilitate robust imitation learning. We validate our method in simulation and on hardware, demonstrating challenging motions for morphologies that differ significantly from a human, including retargeting onto a quadruped.

</details>


### [366] [Cross-Modal Navigation with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06595)
*Shuo Liu,Xinzichen Li,Christopher Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CRONA introduces a cross-modal MARL framework with modality-specialized agents and a centralized multi-modal critic, enabling scalable, efficient visual-acoustic navigation and robust cross-modal collaboration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges of obtaining aligned multi-modal data and training a monolithic, high-capacity model; propose lightweight, modular agents that collaborate to leverage complementary modalities in a scalable MARL setting.

Method: CRONA uses multi-agent reinforcement learning with control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic that has access to the global state. The framework explores homogeneous vs. heterogeneous collaboration across agents with different modalities in visual-acoustic navigation tasks.

Result: Multi-agent methods outperform single-agent baselines in visual-acoustic navigation. Short-range navigation benefits from homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities; heterogeneous collaboration among modalities is efficient and effective for broader coverage; large, complex environments require richer multi-modal perception and greater model capacity.

Conclusion: Cross-modal MARL via CRONA offers scalable, flexible cross-modal navigation, with collaboration strategies varying by task difficulty and environmental complexity; richer modalities and larger models are warranted in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: Robust embodied navigation relies on complementary sensory cues. However, high-quality and well-aligned multi-modal data is often difficult to obtain in practice. Training a monolithic model is also challenging as rich multi-modal inputs induce complex representations and substantially enlarge the policy space. Cross-modal collaboration among lightweight modality-specialized agents offers a scalable paradigm. It enables flexible deployment and parallel execution, while preserving the strength of each modality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{CRONA}, a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for \textbf{Cro}ss-Modal \textbf{Na}vigation. CRONA improves collaboration by leveraging control-relevant auxiliary beliefs and a centralized multi-modal critic with global state. Experiments on visual-acoustic navigation tasks show that multi-agent methods significantly improve performance and efficiency over single-agent baselines. We find that homogeneous collaboration with limited modalities is sufficient for short-range navigation under salient cues; heterogeneous collaboration among agents with complementary modalities is generally efficient and effective; and navigation in large, complex environments requires both richer multi-modal perception and increased model capacity.

</details>


### [367] [Multi-Robot Coordination in V2X Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06662)
*John Pravin Arockiasamy,Alexey Vinel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A decentralized V2X framework for robot coordination in urban traffic using robot-centric services (RAS RMCS) built on RAM RMCM, enabling role-aware awareness and event-driven coordination without central infrastructure; real-world proof of concept and simulations show integration of non-V2X VRUs and reduced channel load, offering a scalable standards-aligned path for cooperative robots in C-ITS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, scalable, low-latency coordination of social robots in dense urban environments with both V2X and non-V2X VRUs, addressing the lack of centralized infrastructure and the risk of channel overload.

Method: Introduce two robot-centric facility-layer services—Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS)—implemented via Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM) within the ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination framework. The approach supports role-aware, task-oriented awareness and event-driven maneuver coordination with explicit roles, without prior pairing or centralized control. Validation includes a real-world proof-of-concept with a humanoid and a quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian at a road crossing, governed by a formal finite-state coordination model, and complementary simulations of VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments.

Result: Deterministic multi-robot coordination demonstrated in a real-world road-crossing scenario; RAM-based VRU clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs to safety-critical zones and reduces redundant transmissions from V2X VRUs, lowering overall channel load.

Conclusion: The proposed RAM/RMCM-based, ETSI-aligned facility-layer services provide a scalable foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems.

Abstract: This paper presents a Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication framework that enables decentralized cooperation among social robots operating in complex urban traffic environments. Building on ETSI Cooperative Awareness and Maneuver Coordination services, the framework introduces two robot-centric facility-layer services: the Robot Awareness Service (RAS) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Service (RMCS), realized through the Robot Awareness Message (RAM) and the Robot Maneuver Coordination Message (RMCM), respectively. RAS enables role-aware, task-oriented robot awareness while integrating externally detected Vulnerable Road Users (VRUs), including non-V2X pedestrians, into cooperative awareness. RMCS supports event-driven, low-latency coordination of robot maneuvers under explicitly established roles, without centralized infrastructure or prior pairing. A real-world proof of concept demonstrates deterministic multi-robot coordination between a humanoid robot and a quadrupedal robot assisting a pedestrian during a road-crossing scenario, governed by a formally specified finite-state coordination model. Complementary simulations evaluate robot-mediated VRU clustering in mixed V2X environments, showing that RAS-based clustering integrates non-V2X VRUs in safety-critical areas while reducing redundant transmissions from V2X-enabled VRUs, thereby lowering channel load. Together, the proposed services provide a scalable and standards-aligned foundation for integrating cooperative robots into future Connected, Cooperative, and Automated Mobility ecosystems.

</details>


### [368] [DexSim2Real: Foundation Model-Guided Sim-to-Real Transfer for Generalizable Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05241)
*Zijian Zeng,Fei Ding,Huiming Yang,Xianwei Li,Yuhao Liao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DexSim2Real presents a triad framework combining FM-DR, TVCAP, and PSC to bridge sim-to-real for dexterous manipulation, integrating vision-language guidance, visuo-tactile cross-attention, and LLM-driven curriculum; shows improved real-world success and reduced sim-to-real gap vs DrEureka/DeXtreme on six tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper targets the persistent sim-to-real gap in dexterous manipulation and the limitations of manually designed domain randomization and task-specific adaptation, aiming for better generalization across diverse manipulation scenarios.

Method: 1) FM-DR uses a vision-language foundation model as a visual realism critic to guide simulation parameter optimization via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches; 2) TVCAP applies cross-attention fusion of vision and tactile inputs for zero-shot sim-to-real RL; 3) PSC leverages LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler for contact-rich dexterous tasks.

Result: Experimental evaluation on six challenging manipulation tasks with blinded assessment shows DexSim2Real achieves 78.2% average real-world success and reduces the sim-to-real gap to 8.3%, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme.

Conclusion: Integrating foundation-model-guided visual realism, visuo-tactile cross-attention, and language-informed curriculum can substantially improve real-world transfer for dexterous manipulation, offering strong performance gains and reduced gap, albeit with potential trade-offs in computational cost and reliance on large models.

Abstract: Sim-to-real transfer remains a critical bottleneck for deploying dexterous manipulation policies learned in simulation to real-world robots. Existing approaches rely on manually designed domain randomization or task-specific adaptation, limiting their generalizability across diverse manipulation scenarios. We present DexSim2Real, an integrated framework that leverages vision-language foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap for dexterous manipulation. Our system combines three components: (1) Foundation Model-Guided Domain Randomization (FM-DR), which uses a vision-language model as a visual realism critic to optimize simulation parameters via closed-loop CMA-ES, complementing text-based approaches like DrEureka with direct visual feedback; (2) a Tactile-Visual Cross-Attention Policy (TVCAP) that adapts cross-attention visuo-tactile fusion to zero-shot sim-to-real RL; and (3) a Progressive Skill Curriculum (PSC) that builds on LLM-based task decomposition with a difficulty scheduler tailored to contact-rich dexterous tasks. Extensive experiments on six challenging manipulation tasks with blinded evaluation demonstrate that DexSim2Real achieves a 78.2% average real-world success rate, outperforming DrEureka and DeXtreme while reducing the sim-to-real performance gap to only 8.3%.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [369] [Understanding Annotator Safety Policy with Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05329)
*Alex Oesterling,Donghao Ren,Yannick Assogba,Dominik Moritz,Sunnie S. Y. Kim,Leon Gatys,Fred Hohman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APMs are interpretable models that infer annotators' internal safety policies from labeling data, enabling detection of policy ambiguity and value pluralism without extra annotation burden; they achieve >80% modeling accuracy, predict counterfactual edits, and reveal demographic differences in safety priorities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disagreement among annotators in safety labeling arises from operational failures, policy ambiguity, and value pluralism. Distinguishing these sources enables targeted policy improvements (quality control, policy clarification, or inclusive deliberation). Directly querying annotators is costly and unreliable, so a scalable, interpretable alternative is valuable.

Method: Train Annotator Policy Models (APMs) on labeling behavior to recover annotators' internal safety policies. Evaluate accuracy (>80%), counterfactual edit prediction, and recovery of known policy differences in controlled settings. Apply APMs to both LLM and human annotations to surface: (1) policy ambiguity (divergent interpretations of safety instructions) and (2) value pluralism (systematic safety-priority differences across demographics).

Result: APMs accurately model annotator safety policies (>80% accuracy). They faithfully predict responses to counterfactual edits and recover known policy differences in controlled experiments. Applied to LLM and human annotation data, APMs reveal policy ambiguity and value pluralism, enabling more targeted, transparent, and inclusive safety policy design.

Conclusion: APMs offer a scalable, interpretable lens into annotator safety reasoning, making disagreements analyzable without extra annotation cost. They support clarifying policies and incorporating diverse perspectives. Limitations may include dependence on labeling data quality, potential oversimplification of complex decision processes, and generalization across domains; future work should address robustness, interpretability, and integration into policy development workflows.

Abstract: Safety policies define what constitutes safe and unsafe AI outputs, guiding data annotation and model development. However, annotation disagreement is pervasive and can stem from multiple sources such as operational failures (annotators misunderstand or misexecute the task), policy ambiguity (policy wording leaves room for interpretation), or value pluralism (different annotators hold different perspectives on safety). Distinguishing these sources matters. For example, operational failures call for quality control, ambiguity calls for policy clarification, and pluralism calls for deliberation about incorporating diverse perspectives. Yet understanding why annotators disagree is difficult. Directly asking annotators for their reasoning is costly, substantially increasing annotation burden, and can be unreliable for both human and LLM annotators as self-reported reasoning often fails to reflect actual decision processes.
  We introduce Annotator Policy Models (APMs), interpretable models that learn annotators' internal safety policies from labeling behavior alone, making annotator reasoning visible and comparable without additional annotation effort. We validate that APMs accurately model annotator safety policy (>80% accuracy), faithfully predict responses to counterfactual edits, and recover known policy differences in controlled settings. Applying APMs to LLM and human annotations, we demonstrate two core applications: (1) surfacing policy ambiguity by revealing how annotators interpret safety instructions differently, and (2) surfacing value pluralism by uncovering systematic differences in safety priorities across demographic groups. Together, these capabilities support more targeted, transparent, and inclusive safety policy design.

</details>


### [370] [ZAYA1-8B Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05365)
*Robert Washbourne,Rishi Iyer,Tomas Figliolia,Henry Zheng,Ryan Lorig-Roach,Sungyeon Yang,Pritish Yuvraj,Quentin Anthony,Yury Tokpanov,Xiao Yang,Ganesh Nanduru,Stephen Ebert,Praneeth Medepalli,Skyler Szot,Srivatsan Rajagopal,Alex Ong,Bhavana Mehta,Beren Millidge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: 700M active / 8B total MoE model ZAYA1-8B focused on reasoning; achieves competitive math/coding performance with a four-stage RL cascade and a Markovian RSA test-time compute method, narrowing gap to larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limited reasoning in small-to-medium models and reduce reliance on extremely large models by designing an efficient, reasoning-focused architecture with staged RL training and innovative test-time compute.

Method: MoE++ with 700M active / 8B total; training from scratch with reasoning data incorporated via answer-preserving trimming; four-stage RL cascade: reasoning warmup, 400-task RLVE-Gym curriculum, math/code RL with traces and synthetic environments, behavioral RL; Markovian RSA test-time compute that aggregates parallel traces while maintaining bounded tails.

Result: In TTC, Markovian RSA yields 91.9% AIME'25 and 89.6% HMMT'25 with only 4K-token tails; matches/exceeds DeepSeek-R1-0528 on math/coding; competitive with larger open-weight models like Gemini-2.5 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2, GPT-5-High.

Conclusion: Reasoning-focused MoE with staged RL and efficient test-time tracing can achieve strong math/coding reasoning with modest active parameters, approaching larger models; Markovian RSA is a promising technique for long-horizon reasoning with bounded memory.

Abstract: We present ZAYA1-8B, a reasoning-focused mixture-of-experts (MoE) model with 700M active and 8B total parameters, built on Zyphra's MoE++ architecture. ZAYA1-8B's core pretraining, midtraining, and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) were performed on a full-stack AMD compute, networking, and software platform. With under 1B active parameters, ZAYA1-8B matches or exceeds DeepSeek-R1-0528 on several challenging mathematics and coding benchmarks, and remains competitive with substantially larger open-weight reasoning models. ZAYA1-8B was trained from scratch for reasoning, with reasoning data included from pretraining onward using an answer-preserving trimming scheme. Post-training uses a four-stage RL cascade: reasoning warmup on math and puzzles; a 400-task RLVE-Gym curriculum; math and code RL with test-time compute traces and synthetic code environments built from competitive-programming references; and behavioral RL for chat and instruction following. We also introduce Markovian RSA, a test-time compute method that recursively aggregates parallel reasoning traces while carrying forward only bounded-length reasoning tails between rounds. In TTC evaluation, Markovian RSA raises ZAYA1-8B to 91.9\% on AIME'25 and 89.6\% on HMMT'25 while carrying forward only a 4K-token tail, narrowing the gap to much larger reasoning models including Gemini-2.5 Pro, DeepSeek-V3.2, and GPT-5-High.

</details>


### [371] [Partial Evidence Bench: Benchmarking Authorization-Limited Evidence in Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05379)
*Krti Tallam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Partial Evidence Bench introduces a deterministic benchmark to quantify unsafe completeness in enterprise agents: measuring when systems return seemingly complete answers despite evidence outside authorization. It uses 72 tasks across three scenario families (due diligence, compliance audit, security incident response) with ACL-partitioned corpora and oracle facilitators, evaluating four dimensions and showing explicit fail-and-report beats silent filtering in reducing unsafe completeness; preliminary real-model runs reveal scenario- and model-dependent overclaim/underclaim behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise agents must respond under access-control constraints without leaking material evidence outside a caller’s authorization. Without a measurable benchmark, governance-critical failures (unsafe completeness) go undetected and hard to audit.

Method: Three scenario families (due diligence, compliance audit, security incident response) comprising 72 tasks. Data hyperparameters include ACL-partitioned corpora, oracle complete answers, oracle authorized-view answers, oracle completeness judgments, and structured gap-report oracles. Evaluation surfaces: answer correctness, completeness awareness, gap-report quality, unsafe completeness behavior. Checked-in baselines show silent filtering is unsafe; explicit fail-and-report demonstrates safer behavior. Preliminary real-model runs show model- and scenario-dependent variance in overclaim vs underclaim vs reporting incompleteness.

Result: Key findings: silent filtering is catastrophically unsafe across families; explicit fail-and-report eliminates unsafe completeness without forcing abstention; real-model runs show variability in completeness behavior depending on model and scenario.

Conclusion: The benchmark provides a governance-critical, judge-free, contamination-resistant framework to measure and improve safe completeness in enterprise agents, enabling systematic evaluation and safety-oriented improvements.

Abstract: Enterprise agents increasingly operate inside scoped retrieval systems, delegated workflows, and policy-constrained evidence environments. In these settings, access control can be enforced correctly while the system still produces an answer that appears complete even though material evidence lies outside the caller's authorization boundary. This paper introduces Partial Evidence Bench, a deterministic benchmark for measuring that failure mode. The benchmark ships three scenario families -- due diligence, compliance audit, and security incident response -- with 72 tasks total, ACL-partitioned corpora, oracle complete answers, oracle authorized-view answers, oracle completeness judgments, and structured gap-report oracles. It evaluates systems along four surfaces: answer correctness, completeness awareness, gap-report quality, and unsafe completeness behavior. Checked-in baselines show that silent filtering is catastrophically unsafe across all shipped families, while explicit fail-and-report behavior eliminates unsafe completeness without collapsing the task into trivial abstention. Preliminary real-model runs show model-dependent and scenario-sensitive differences in whether systems overclaim completeness, conservatively underclaim, or report incompleteness in an enterprise-usable form. The benchmark's broader contribution is to make a governance-critical agent failure measurable without human judges or contamination-prone static corpora.

</details>


### [372] [BALAR : A Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05386)
*Aymen Echarghaoui,Dongxia Wu,Emily B. Fox*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BALAR is a task-agnostic outer-loop method for LLM–user dialogue that maintains a Bayesian belief over latent states, selects clarifying questions by maximizing expected mutual information, and expands its state representation dynamically, achieving strong cross-domain gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current dialogue systems are reactive and lack a principled mechanism to identify missing information and decide what to ask next; BALAR aims to enable structured multi-turn interaction without fine-tuning.

Method: Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning (BALAR) is an outer-loop algorithm that maintains a structured belief over latent states, asks clarifying questions by maximizing expected mutual information, and dynamically expands its state representation when needed; it requires no fine-tuning.

Result: BALAR significantly outperforms baselines across three benchmarks: AR-Bench-DC (14.6% accuracy increase), AR-Bench-SP (38.5%), and iCraft-MD (30.5%).

Conclusion: BALAR provides a principled, generalizable approach to active information gathering in LLM-driven dialogues, enabling improved accuracy across diverse tasks without additional fine-tuning.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly operate in interactive settings where solving a task requires multiple rounds of information exchange with a user. However, most current systems treat dialogue reactively and lack a principled mechanism to reason about what information is missing and which question should be asked next. We propose BALAR (Bayesian Agentic Loop for Active Reasoning), a task-agnostic outer-loop algorithm that requires no fine-tuning and enables structured multi-turn interaction between an LLM agent and a user. BALAR maintains a structured belief over latent states, selects clarifying questions by maximizing expected mutual information, and dynamically expands its state representation when the current one proves insufficient. We evaluate BALAR on three diverse benchmarks: AR-Bench-DC (detective cases), AR-Bench-SP (thinking puzzles), and iCraft-MD (clinical diagnosis). BALAR significantly outperforms all baselines across all three benchmarks, with $14.6\%$ higher accuracy on AR-Bench-DC, $38.5\%$ on AR-Bench-SP, and $30.5\%$ on iCraft-MD.

</details>


### [373] [Intelligent CCTV for Urban Design: AI-Based Analysis of Soft Infrastructure at Intersections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05402)
*Vinit Katariya,Seungjin Kim,Curtis Craig,Nichole Morris,Hamed Tabkhi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-enabled CCTV analytics quantify traffic-calming effects of soft interventions; substantial reductions in speeds and pass-through at both unsignalized and signalized intersections in Minneapolis, validating low-cost, scalable policy evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess efficacy of soft infrastructure (pedestrian refuges, curb extensions) and demonstrate a scalable, low-cost method using existing CCTV and AI to measure speed and safety outcomes.

Method: Deep learning-based perspective speed estimation from CCTV; pre/post comparison with Week 1 and Week 2 monitoring; metrics include mean speed, 85th percentile speed, and pass-through traffic; locations include unsignalized and signalized intersections in Minneapolis.

Result: Mean speeds decreased up to 18.75% (unsignalized) and 20.0% (signalized); 85th-percentile speeds decreased up to 16.56% and 17.19%; pass-through traffic down as much as 12.2%; one location deviated from trend.

Conclusion: Soft interventions are effective for traffic calming; AI-powered CCTV analytics offer rapid, low-cost evaluation for transport policy and can inform deployment decisions.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) and computer vision are transforming transportation data collection. This study introduces an AI-enabled analytics framework leveraging existing CCTV infrastructure to evaluate the impact of soft interventions, such as temporary pedestrian refuges and curb extensions, on vehicle speed and safety. Using deep learning and perspective-based speed estimation, we evaluated driver behavior before and after interventions, with repeated post-installation monitoring in Week 1 and Week 2, in Minneapolis. Findings reveal that at unsignalized intersections, mean and 85th-percentile speeds fell by up to 18.75% and 16.56%, respectively, while pass-through traffic decreased by as much as 12.2%. Signalized intersections showed comparable reductions except one location, with mean and 85th-percentile speeds dropping by up to 20.0% and 17.19%. These results demonstrate the traffic-calming effectiveness of soft infrastructure and underscore the utility of AI-powered methods for rapid, low-cost, and evidence-based transport policy evaluation.

</details>


### [374] [When Helpfulness Becomes Sycophancy: Sycophancy is a Boundary Failure Between Social Alignment and Epistemic Integrity in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05403)
*Jiechen Li,Catherine A. Barry,Rishika Randev,Janet Chen,Ella Jorgensen,Brinnae Bent*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A position paper reframes sycophancy in LLMs as a boundary failure between social alignment and epistemic integrity; introduces a three-condition framework and a taxonomy (alignment targets, mechanisms, severity); discusses boundary-aware evaluation, rubrics, and mitigation strategies, plus situating these ideas with alternative views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work emphasizes overt, external manifestations of sycophancy (e.g., agreement with user beliefs) and neglects subtler boundary failures affecting epistemic integrity. The paper motivates precise delineation to improve alignment evaluation.

Method: Conceptual analysis: defines a three-condition framework for sycophancy and develops a taxonomy (alignment targets, mechanisms, severity); discusses evaluation implications and proposes structured rubrics and mitigation strategies; situates proposals relative to alternative perspectives on sycophancy.

Result: A clarified conceptual boundary for sycophancy and a taxonomy to classify it; proposed evaluation and mitigation directions through boundary-aware assessment and structured rubrics; no empirical results provided.

Conclusion: Highlights the need to separate social alignment from epistemic integrity, advocating for assessment frameworks that capture boundary-violating alignment and mitigation approaches, while acknowledging alternative views of sycophancy.

Abstract: This position paper argues that sycophancy in LLMs is a boundary failure between social alignment and epistemic integrity. Existing work often operationalizes sycophancy through external behavior such as agreement with incorrect user beliefs, position reversals, or deviation from an objective standard of correctness. These formulations capture only overt forms of the phenomenon and leave subtler boundary failures involving epistemic integrity and social alignment underspecified. We argue that sycophancy should not be understood as agreement alone, but as alignment behavior that displaces independent epistemic judgment. To clarify this boundary, we propose a three-condition framework for sycophancy. First, the user expresses a cue in the form of a belief, preference, or self-concept. Second, the model shifts toward that cue through alignment behavior. Third, this shift compromises epistemic accuracy, independent reasoning, or appropriate correction. We also introduce a taxonomy for classifying sycophancy, consisting of alignment targets, mechanisms, and severity. The paper concludes by discussing implications for alignment evaluation and argues for boundary-aware assessment, structured rubrics, and mitigation strategies, while situating these proposals alongside alternative views of sycophancy.

</details>


### [375] [Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Financial Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05409)
*Yang Shu,Yingmin Liu,Zequn Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FinAgent-RAG is an agentic RAG framework for financial QA that uses iterative retrieval-reasoning loops with self-verification and three domain-specific innovations to achieve higher numerical reasoning precision and cost efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial document QA requires complex multi-step numerical reasoning over heterogeneous sources; single-pass RAG struggles with compositional reasoning and precision; reliable arithmetic and cost control are essential for real-world deployment.

Method: Three domain-specific innovations: (1) Contrastive Financial Retriever with hard negative mining to distinguish semantically similar but numerically distinct passages; (2) Program-of-Thought reasoning module that emits executable Python code for precise arithmetic rather than relying on unverified LLM mental computation; (3) Adaptive Strategy Router that dynamically allocates computational resources based on question complexity; framework orchestrates iterative retrieval-reasoning loops with self-verification.

Result: On FinQA, ConvFinQA, and TAT-QA, FinAgent-RAG achieves 76.81%, 78.46%, and 74.96% execution accuracy respectively, outperforming the strongest baseline by 5.62–9.32 percentage points. API cost reduced by 41.3% on FinQA. Ablation studies, cross-backbone evaluation with four LLMs, and deployment cost analysis confirm robustness and practical viability.

Conclusion: FinAgent-RAG demonstrates robustness and practical viability for financial institutions by delivering precise numerical reasoning with iterative, resource-aware retrieval and computation.

Abstract: Financial document question answering (QA) demands complex multi-step numerical reasoning over heterogeneous evidence--structured tables, textual narratives, and footnotes--scattered across corporate filings. Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches adopt a single-pass retrieve-then-generate paradigm that struggles with the compositional reasoning chains prevalent in financial analysis. We propose FinAgent-RAG, an agentic RAG framework that orchestrates iterative retrieval-reasoning loops with self-verification, specifically engineered for the precision requirements of financial numerical reasoning. The framework integrates three domain-specific innovations: (1) a Contrastive Financial Retriever trained with hard negative mining to distinguish semantically similar but numerically distinct financial passages, (2) a Program-of-Thought reasoning module that generates executable Python code for precise arithmetic rather than relying on error-prone LLM-based mental computation, and (3) an Adaptive Strategy Router that dynamically allocates computational resources based on question complexity, reducing API costs by 41.3% on FinQA while preserving accuracy. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets--FinQA, ConvFinQA, and TAT-QA--demonstrate that FinAgent-RAG achieves 76.81%, 78.46%, and 74.96% execution accuracy respectively, outperforming the strongest baseline by 5.62--9.32 percentage points. Ablation studies, cross-backbone evaluation with four LLMs, and deployment cost analysis confirm the framework's robustness and practical viability for financial institutions.

</details>


### [376] [LaTA: A Drop-in, FERPA-Compliant Local-LLM Autograder for Upper-Division STEM Coursework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05410)
*Jesse A. Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-source, on-prem LLM-based autograder for LaTeX assignments (LaTA) using a local 120B model; four-stage pipeline; binary rubric scoring; runs on commodity hardware; reduces data risks and costs; improves exam performance and student confidence; AGPLv3 license.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate FERPA/data-risk issues from third-party LLM APIs; preserve LaTeX-native workflow; scale grading for large enrollments; enable regrading and expanded TA support.

Method: Four-stage pipeline: ingest, segment, grade, report. Local open-weight LLM (gpt-oss:120b) compares student solution to instructor reference; YAML rubric with per-item binary scoring; on-premises Mac Studio; ~1-3 min per submission; about $0 marginal cost; supports regrading.

Result: Grading error rate ~0.02-0.04% per rubric line item; midterm improvement ~11%; final improvement ~8%; self-reported objective mastery: Δ ≥ +1.49 Likert points; p < 1e−27; N=159 survey responses.

Conclusion: LaTA demonstrates that an on-prem, open-weight LLM autograder can be cost-effective, privacy-preserving, and improve learning outcomes and student confidence; released under AGPLv3.

Abstract: Large-language-model (LLM) graders promise to relieve the grading burden of upper-division STEM courses, but most deployments to date send student work to third-party APIs, violating FERPA and exposing institutions to data risk while requiring substantial assignment modification. We present $\textbf{LaTA}\ (\textit{LaTeX Teaching Assistant})$, a drop-in, open-source autograder that runs entirely on commodity on-premises hardware and assumes a LaTeX-native workflow already adopted by many engineering and physics courses. LaTA implements a four-stage pipeline (ingest, segment, grade, report) using a locally hosted open-weight chain-of-thought LLM grader (gpt-oss:120b) that compares student work to an instructor-authored reference solution and applies a YAML rubric with binary per-item scoring. We deployed LaTA in Winter~2026 in ME 373 (Mechanical Engineering Methods) at Oregon State University, grading every weekly assignment for approximately 200 students on a single Mac Studio at \$0 marginal cost per assignment and 1--3 minutes of wall-clock time per submission, enabling regrading of corrected assignments and greatly expanded TA office hour offerings. The instructor-confirmed grading-error rate held at roughly $0.02$--$0.04\%$ per rubric line item across the term. Relative to the same instructor's previous traditionally-graded cohort, the LaTA-graded cohort outperformed by approximately $11\%$ on the midterm exam and $8\%$ on the final exam, and reported large gains in self-assessed confidence on every stated learning objective ($N = 159$ survey responses, $Δ\geq +1.49$ Likert points, $p < 10^{-27}$ on every comparison). We release the code under AGPLv3.

</details>


### [377] [From History to State: Constant-Context Skill Learning for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05413)
*Haoyang Xie,Xinyuan Wang,Yancheng Wang,Puda Zhao,Feng Ju*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes constant-context skill learning to move procedural context from prompts into weights via reusable task-family modules and a state-tracker, enabling privacy-friendly, low-token-cost agent workflows with strong unseen-task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the privacy-cost-capability tension in personal LLM agents: cloud models offer reliability but risk exposing context; local models preserve privacy but are weaker, and long prompts accumulate cost. The work aims to reduce prompt token usage while preserving or improving performance across recurring workflows.

Method: Introduce a context-to-weights framework: learn reusable procedures as lightweight task-family modules; inference conditions on the current observation plus a compact state block produced by a deterministic tracker from task progress; train modules with step-level supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and refine via online reinforcement learning (RL). Evaluate across multiple environments (ALFWorld, WebShop, SciWorld) and models (Qwen-4B, Qwen-8B, Llama-3.1-8B).

Result: With Qwen-8B, SFT+RL achieves 89.6% unseen success on ALFWorld, 76.8% on WebShop, and 66.4% unseen success on SciWorld. Across environments, agents match or exceed strong published agent-training baselines while reducing prompt tokens per turn by 2–7x relative to ReAct prompting baselines.

Conclusion: Procedural context can be moved from prompts into model weights, enabling efficient, privacy-conscious agents that maintain or surpass state-of-the-art performance on diverse tasks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to operate browsers, files, code and tools, making personal assistants a natural deployment target. Yet personal agents face a privacy-cost-capability tension: cloud models execute multi-step workflows well but expose sensitive intermediate context to external APIs, while local models preserve privacy but remain less reliable. Both settings also pay repeatedly for long skill prompts and growing histories. We propose constant-context skill learning, a context-to-weights framework for recurring agent workflows: reusable procedures are learned in lightweight task-family modules, while inference conditions only on the current observation and a compact state block. A deterministic tracker renders this state block from task progress and supplies aligned subgoal rewards, so each module can be trained with step-level SFT and refined through online RL. Across ALFWorld, WebShop, and SciWorld, our agents achieve strong performance across Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B. With Qwen3-8B, SFT+RL reaches 89.6\% unseen success on ALFWorld, 76.8\% success on WebShop, and 66.4\% unseen success on SciWorld. They match or exceed strong published agent-training results while reducing prompt tokens per turn by 2--7$\times$ relative to controlled ReAct prompting baselines, showing that procedural context can be moved from prompts into weights.

</details>


### [378] [The Geopolitics of AI Safety: A Causal Analysis of Regional LLM Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05427)
*Alif Al Hasan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Causal auditing of LLM safety using a Probabilistic Graphical Model to isolate interventional bias from observational bias across diverse models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address confounding in fairness evaluations of LLM safety by separating contextual toxicity from demographic bias using Pearl's do-calculus, enabling causal interpretation of safety guardrails.

Method: Construct a Probabilistic Graphical Model and apply Pearl's do-operator to measure the causal effect of injecting a cultural demographic into a prompt. Conduct large-scale evaluation across seven instruction-tuned models from multiple regions using ToxiGen and BOLD datasets.

Result: Observational bias overestimates demographic bias; causal bias patterns differ by region, with Western models showing higher intervention refusals and Eastern models showing lower overall intervention but regional sensitivities. Demographic-sensitive over-triggering can unduly constrain benign discourse.

Conclusion: Causal auditing reveals gaps in traditional fairness metrics and highlights the need for causal safety guardrails in LLM deployment across global contexts; suggests directions for more nuanced, region-aware alignment and evaluation.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are integrated into global software systems, ensuring equitable safety guardrails is a critical requirement. Current fairness evaluations predominantly measure bias observationally, a methodology confounded by the inherent toxicity of topics naturally paired with specific demographics in testing datasets. This study introduces a Probabilistic Graphical Model (PGM) framework to audit LLM safety mechanisms causally. By applying Pearl's do-operator, we mathematically isolate the causal effect of injecting a cultural demographic into a prompt. We conduct a large-scale empirical analysis across seven instruction-tuned models spanning diverse origins: the United States (Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B), Europe (Mistral-7B-v0.3), the UAE (Falcon3-7B), China (Qwen2.5-7B, DeepSeek-7B), and India (Airavata-7B). Utilizing two distinct datasets (ToxiGen and BOLD), the findings reveal a disparity between observational and interventional bias, demonstrating that standard fairness metrics can overestimate demographic bias by failing to account for context toxicity. Furthermore, the causal probabilities indicate distinct alignment trends: Western models exhibit higher causal refusal rates for specific demographic groups, whereas Eastern models demonstrate low overall intervention rates with targeted sensitivities toward regional demographics. We discuss the implications of these biases, highlighting how demographic-sensitive over-triggering restricts benign discourse in downstream applications.

</details>


### [379] [Authorization Propagation in Multi-Agent AI Systems: Identity Governance as Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05440)
*Krti Tallam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes 'authorization propagation' as a distinct multi-agent AI security problem, formalizes it as a workflow-level property with three subproblems, and argues for infrastructure-level identity governance; outlines seven architectural requirements and presents preliminary evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-injection-focused security is insufficient: non-human principals propagate authorization across data, tasks, and results, creating novel invariants not captured by RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC.

Method: Formalizes the problem, decomposes it into transitive delegation, aggregation inference, and temporal validity; derives seven structural requirements for authorization architectures; surveys related work on capability tokens, task-scoped envelopes, policy enforcement, and revocation; provides preliminary production evidence.

Result: Seven structural requirements for authorization architectures in multi-agent AI are proposed; existing approaches are converging but lack a complete architecture; production evidence shows normal operation can violate the intended invariants.

Conclusion: Identity governance should be treated as infrastructure—continuous evaluation and enforcement at every interaction boundary; architecture should be designed before orchestration scales; more work needed to compose a complete, provable architecture.

Abstract: The security discussion around agentic AI focuses heavily on prompt injection.
