This paper argues that multi-agent systems also create a distinct authorization problem: maintaining authorization invariants as non-human principals retrieve data, delegate tasks, and synthesize results across changing boundaries. We call this problem authorization propagation. It is not reducible to prompt injection and is not fully addressed by classical access-control models such as RBAC, ABAC, or ReBAC. The paper formalizes authorization propagation as a workflow-level property, identifies three sub-problems (transitive delegation, aggregation inference, and temporal validity), and derives seven structural requirements for authorization architectures in multi-agent AI systems. Recent work on invocation-bound capability tokens, task-scoped authorization envelopes, dependency-graph policy enforcement, and execution-count revocation demonstrates that the field is converging on the problem, but not yet on a complete architecture. The central claim is that identity governance must be treated as infrastructure: evaluated continuously, enforced at every interaction boundary, and designed into the system before orchestration logic is allowed to scale. Preliminary implementation evidence from a production enterprise AI platform shows that ordinary system behavior, not only adversarial action, already produces the failures this model predicts.

</details>


### [380] [Agentic Discovery of Exchange-Correlation Density Functionals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05460)
*Titouan Duston,Jiashu Liang,Yuanheng Wang,Weihao Gao,Xuelan Wen,Nan Sheng,Weiluo Ren,Yang Sun,Yixiao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-guided agentic search discovers SAFS26-a, a new exchange-correlation functional that outperforms ωB97M-V by ~9% on a standard thermochemistry benchmark, highlighting both potential and risks of AI-assisted scientific discovery; emphasizes need for explicit constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate the design of accurate exchange-correlation functionals in density functional theory (DFT) by leveraging large language models (LLMs) and evolutionary history, reducing dependence on manual, intuition-driven development.

Method: Iterative plan–execute–summarize loop where an LLM proposes structured functional-form changes guided by historical results; optimize functional parameters on a standard thermochemistry dataset and evaluate on a held-out subset; identify SAFS26-a as the strongest discovered functional.

Result: The strongest discovered functional, SAFS26-a, improves upon the gold-standard ωB97M-V baseline by ~9%.

Conclusion: Demonstrates potential of AI-assisted scientific discovery in functional design while highlighting a risk of gaming benchmarks; explicit, domain-specific constraints remain essential to maintain scientific grounding.

Abstract: The development of accurate exchange-correlation (XC) functionals remains a longstanding challenge in density functional theory (DFT). The vast majority of XC functionals have been hand designed by human researchers combining physical insight, exact constraints, and empirical fitting. Recent advances in large language models enable a systematic, automated alternative to this human-driven design loop. This report presents an agentic search system in which an LLM proposes structured functional-form changes guided by evolutionary history. The system attempts to improve functional performance through an iterative plan-execute-summarize loop, where improvements are measurable by optimizing functional parameters against a standard thermochemistry dataset, then evaluating performance on a held-out subset. The strongest discovered functional, SAFS26-a (Seed Agentic Functional Search 2026), improves upon the gold-standard ωB97M-V baseline by ~9%. These results also surface a cautionary lesson for AI-assisted science: models powerful enough to discover genuine improvements are equally capable of exploiting unphysical shortcuts to game the benchmark; domain expertise translated into explicitly enforced constraints remains essential to keeping results scientifically grounded.

</details>


### [381] [Intentionality is a Design Decision: Measuring Functional Intentionality for Accountable AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05475)
*Allessia Chiappetta,Robert Mahari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Functional Intentionality Test (FIT) to quantify intentional-like behavior in AI across five dimensions, with FIT-Eval evaluation protocol; frames intentionality as design-contingent and controllable for governance and accountability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of standardized methods to detect and measure intentional-like behavior in autonomous AI, enabling governance, accountability, and controlled autonomy.

Method: Defines intentionality as a design-contingent behavioral profile and identifies five observable dimensions; introduces FIT to quantify these dimensions and FIT-Eval as a structured scoring protocol; discusses measurement as the basis for oversight.

Result: A framework and evaluation protocol (FIT and FIT-Eval) for translating intentionality into interpretable levels, enabling proportionate oversight and autonomy calibration in agentic AI.

Conclusion: FIT provides a practical tool for governance and accountability, mapping architectural design choices to intentional-like behavior and guiding safe deployment of increasingly agentic systems.

Abstract: As AI systems increasingly exhibit autonomous, goal-directed, and long-horizon behavior, users lack a standardized way to detect the degree to which a system functions like an intentional actor for governance and accountability purposes. This position paper defines intentionality not as consciousness, but as a behavioral profile characterized by purpose, foresight, volition, temporal commitment, and coherence - criteria long used in legal and philosophical contexts to infer intent. These properties are design-contingent: architectural choices such as memory persistence, planning depth, and tool autonomy shape the degree to which systems exhibit organized goal pursuit. If intentionality is design-contingent, it is in principle controllable. Yet control requires measurement.
  We introduce the Functional Intentionality Test (FIT), a multidimensional framework that quantifies intentional-like behavior across five observable dimensions, and propose FIT-Eval, a structured evaluation protocol for eliciting and scoring them. While reduced human agency can increase efficiency, rising intentional capacity heightens accountability risks. By translating intentionality into interpretable levels, FIT enables proportionate oversight and deliberate autonomy calibration in increasingly agentic systems.

</details>


### [382] [LANTERN: LLM-Augmented Neurosymbolic Transfer with Experience-Gated Reasoning Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05478)
*Mahyar Alinejad,Yue Wang,Amrit Singh Bedi,George Atia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LANTERN introduces a multi-source neurosymbolic transfer framework for RL that uses LLM-generated DFAs from natural language task descriptions, semantic-embedding aggregation of multiple source policies by cross-task similarity, and adaptive teacher-student gating based on TD error and semantic uncertainty, achieving notable sample-efficient gains and robustness across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neurosymbolic transfer methods rely on manually specified task automata, assume a single source, and use fixed integration mechanisms that do not adapt to varying source relevance. There is a need for scalable, robust multi-source transfer that can automatically align sources to tasks.

Method: Three components: (i) deterministic finite automata (DFA) generated from natural language task descriptions via large language models, (ii) aggregation of multiple source policies using semantic embeddings weighted by cross-task similarity, and (iii) adaptive teacher-student gating guided by temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty to combine policy guidance.

Result: Empirical evaluation across resource management, navigation, and control domains showing 40-60% improvements in sample efficiency over baselines; demonstrated robustness to poorly aligned sources.

Conclusion: Multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer enhances scalability and robustness in symbolic RL settings.

Abstract: Transfer learning in reinforcement learning (RL) seeks to accelerate learning in new tasks by leveraging knowledge from related sources. Existing neurosymbolic transfer methods, however, typically rely on manually specified task automata, assume a single source task, and use fixed knowledge-integration mechanisms that cannot adapt to varying source relevance. We propose LANTERN, a unified framework for multi-source neurosymbolic transfer that addresses these limitations through three components: (i) deterministic finite automata generated from natural language task descriptions using large language models, (ii) semantic embedding-based aggregation of multiple source policies weighted by cross-task similarity, and (iii) adaptive teacher-student gating based on temporal-difference error and semantic uncertainty. Across domains spanning resource management, navigation, and control, LANTERN achieves 40-60% improvements in sample efficiency over existing baselines while remaining robust to poorly aligned sources. These results demonstrate that multi-source, adaptively weighted neurosymbolic transfer can improve scalability and robustness in symbolic RL settings.

</details>


### [383] [FinRAG-12B: A Production-Validated Recipe for Grounded Question Answering in Banking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05482)
*Denys Katerenchuk,Pablo Duboue,Keelan Evanini,David Gondek,Nithin Govindugari,Olivier Allauzen,Joshua Baptiste,David J More,Joshua Schechter*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A data-efficient framework for grounded domain-specific LLMs in banking, delivering a 12B model that improves citation grounding and calibrated refusals, with end-to-end deployment at 40+ institutions, yielding faster, cheaper, and more reliable responses than GPT-4.1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Banking requires high accuracy, regulatory compliance, verifiable grounding, and controlled refusals. The goal is a data-efficient pipeline to train domain-specific LLMs that canground responses with citations, refuse unanswerable questions appropriately, and operate under real-world constraints (cost and latency).

Method: Data generation pipeline combining LLM-as-a-Judge filtering, citation annotation, and curriculum learning using 143M tokens. Train a 12B model. Develop calibrated refusal by training on 22% unanswerable examples. End-to-end process from data curation to quantized serving.

Result: The 12B model achieves high answer quality and superior citation grounding compared with GPT-4.1, with modest citation tradeoffs versus the untuned base. Calibrated refusal yields 12% 'I don’t know' answers (vs 4.3% unsafe baseline and GPT-4.1’s 20.2% over-refusal). Deployment at 40+ financial institutions leads to 7.1 percentage points better query resolution (p<0.001) and 3–5× faster responses at 20–50× lower cost than GPT-4.1.

Conclusion: A data-efficient, grounding-focused, calibrated-refusal framework can meet banking constraints, delivering practical impact and cost efficiency through end-to-end deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted across various domains. However, their adoption in banking industry faces resistance due to demands for high accuracy, regulatory compliance, and the need for verifiable and grounded responses. We present a unified, data-efficient framework for training grounded domain-specific LLMs that optimizes answer quality, citation grounding, and calibrated refusal under real-world deployment constraints. First, we describe a data generation pipeline that combines LLM-as-a-Judge filtering, citation annotation, and curriculum learning with only 143M tokens. The resulting 12B model achieves high answer quality outperforming GPT-4.1 on citation grounding, with a modest citation tradeoff versus the untuned base. Second, we propose a calibrated refusal mechanism: training on 22% unanswerable examples yield a 12% "I don't know" rate, substantially improving over the base model's unsafe 4.3% rate while avoiding GPT-4.1's over-refusal (20.2%). Third, we present an end-to-end methodology spanning from data curation to quantized serving. The system is deployed at 40+ financial institutions, achieving a 7.1 percentage point improvement in query resolution (p < 0.001). Additionally, the model delivers 3-5x faster responses at 20-50x lower cost compared to GPT-4.1.

</details>


### [384] [FoodCHA: Multi-Modal LLM Agent for Fine-Grained Food Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05499)
*Woojin Lee,Pranav Mekkoth,Ye Tian,Onat Gungor,Tajana Rosing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FoodCHA reframes food recognition as hierarchical decision-making, using high-level categories to anchor subcategory and cooking-style predictions; it leverages the Moondream-2B vision-language model for efficiency and achieves substantial gains on FoodNExTDB over a baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome high intra-class similarity, multi-item images, and lack of fine-grained attributes such as cooking style; and to address non-canonical labels from open-ended vision-language models for practical deployment.

Method: A multimodal agentic framework that progressively anchors predictions: first classify broad categories, then guide subcategory and cooking style via hierarchical decisions; uses Moondream-2B as the reasoning backbone to balance performance and efficiency; evaluated on FoodNExTDB against Food-Llama-3.2-11B.

Result: Category precision improved by 13.8%, subcategory precision by 38.2%, and cooking style precision by 153.2% compared to Food-Llama-3.2-11B.

Conclusion: FoodCHA enhances semantic consistency and attribute-level discrimination in food recognition while maintaining practical deployability through an efficient vision-language model.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of camera-equipped mobile devices and wearables has enabled convenient capture of meal images, making food recognition a key component for real time dietary monitoring. However, real-world food images present challenges due to high intra-class similarity and the frequent presence of multiple food items within a single image. While deep learning models achieve strong performance in coarse grained classification, they often struggle to capture fine-grained attributes such as cooking style. Moreover, open-ended generation in modern vision-language models can produce non-canonical labels, limiting their practical deployment. We propose FoodCHA, a multimodal agentic framework that reformulates food recognition as a hierarchical decision-making process. By progressively anchoring predictions, FoodCHA guides subcategory identification using high-level categories and guides cooking style recognition using subcategories, improving semantic consistency and attribute-level discrimination. To ensure practical deployability, FoodCHA utilizes the compact Moondream-2B vision language model, which provides strong reasoning capability while maintaining lower computational and memory overhead. Experiments on FoodNExTDB show that FoodCHA outperforms Food-Llama-3.2-11B by 13.8% and 38.2% in category and subcategory recognition precision, respectively, and achieves a striking 153.2% improvement in cooking style classification precision.

</details>


### [385] [Housing Potential Common Data Model and City Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05535)
*Megan Katsumi,Mark Fox,Anderson Wong,Divnoor Chatha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A standardized data model (HPCDM) unifies housing-potential datasets to enable integrated analysis, demonstrated via City Digital Twin and a pilot dashboard, with adoption barriers and mitigations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data silos and interoperability gaps across zoning, land use, demographics, and services data to support accurate housing-potential assessment and planning.

Method: Design and implement the Housing Potential Common Data Model (HPCDM); evaluate its performance; develop a City Digital Twin for housing; deploy a pilot dashboard; identify adoption barriers and propose mitigation strategies.

Result: A standardized, interoperable data framework enabling integrated housing-potential analysis; a demonstrator (City Digital Twin + dashboard); identified barriers and actionable mitigation strategies for practitioners.

Conclusion: HPCDM advances housing-potential analytics by standardizing data exchange and enabling practical deployment, though successful adoption requires targeted governance, validation, and stakeholder engagement.

Abstract: The evaluation of housing potential requires consideration of a location from multiple perspectives, ranging from zoning and land use to population characteristics and access to services. This research introduces the Housing Potential Common Data Model (HPCDM) to overcome existing data silos, serving as a standard to support integration and interoperability across the diverse range of datasets that are required for housing potential analysis. This report details the evaluation of the model along with the creation of a City Digital Twin for housing and a pilot dashboard application to demonstrate a practical implementation. Beyond the technical framework, this work identifies critical barriers to adoption and provides actionable mitigation strategies for urban planners and stakeholders.

</details>


### [386] [AgenticRAG: Agentic Retrieval for Enterprise Knowledge Bases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05538)
*Susheel Suresh,Hazel Mak,Shangpo Chou,Fred Kroon,Sahil Bhatnagar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgenticRAG introduces an agentic harness atop enterprise search enabling a reasoning LLM to autonomously search, navigate, and summarize evidence, yielding substantial gains on three benchmarks and close to oracle performance; designed for real-world enterprise deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the reliance on fixed candidate sets in standard RAG pipelines and alleviate grounding burden on the LM by enabling iterative tool-based retrieval and analysis within enterprise knowledge bases.

Method: Layer a lightweight agentic harness on top of existing enterprise search, exposing tools: search, find, open, summarize; enables multi-query search and in-document navigation; ablation studies highlight importance of agentic tool use.

Result: Significant gains on three benchmarks: BRIGHT recall@1 49.6% (+21.8 pp), WixQA factuality 0.96 (+13% relative), FinanceBench 92% answer correctness within 2 percentage points of oracle; 5.9x improvement from agentic tool use; design choices informed by pre-production deployments.

Conclusion: Demonstrates practicality of agentic harness for enterprise production, enabling autonomous reasoning and evidence analysis with enterprise search; near-oracle performance on evaluation benchmarks, and robust gains attributed to tool-based agentic reasoning.

Abstract: We present AgenticRAG, a practical agentic harness for retrieval and analysis over enterprise knowledge bases. Standard RAG pipelines place significant burden of grounding on the search stack, constraining the language model to a fixed candidate set chosen deep in the retrieval process. Our approach reduces this overdependence by layering a lightweight harness on top of existing enterprise search infrastructure, equipping a reasoning LLM with search, find, open, and summarize tools enabling the model to iteratively retrieve information, navigate within documents, and analyze evidence autonomously. On three open benchmarks we observe substantial gains: $49.6\%$ recall@1 on BRIGHT (+21.8 pp over the best embedding baseline), 0.96 factuality on WixQA ($+13\%$ relative improvement), and $92\%$ answer correctness on FinanceBench--within 2 pp of oracle access to true evidence. Ablation studies show that the most significant factor is the shift from single-shot retrieval to agentic tool use ($5.9\times$ improvement), while multi-query search and in-document navigation contribute to both quality and efficiency. We present various design choices in our agentic harness that were informed by pre-production deployments. Our results demonstrate its suitability for real-world enterprise production environments.

</details>


### [387] [SPARK: Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05546)
*Hyobin Park,Taeseop Kim,Dong-Geol Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPARK builds a unified knowledge graph from multi-document scientific literature to ground self-play in relational reasoning tasks. It uses KG-derived multi-hop paths to generate questions and uses KG facts to compute verifiable rewards, with a small vision-language model alternating roles against a fixed KG. It outperforms flat-corpus baselines, particularly as hop count increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-play RL performs well in domains with explicit rules, but scientific literature lacks explicit cross-document relational structure and reliable rewards. A knowledge-graph grounding approach offers a structured representation to enable relational, multi-hop reasoning with verifiable rewards.

Method: Construct a unified knowledge graph from multiple documents; extract multimodal nodes and paths to generate relational reasoning questions; derive rewards from structured KG facts for verifiability; employ a single small vision-language model that alternates between Proposer and Solver under information asymmetry against the fixed KG (self-play). Evaluate on public benchmarks and a self-constructed cross-document multi-hop QA dataset.

Result: SPARK consistently outperforms baselines that rely on flat corpora, with increasingly larger performance gaps as the required hop count grows, indicating the value of KG-structure grounding for multi-hop relational reasoning.

Conclusion: KG-structure grounding enhances relational multi-hop reasoning beyond unstructured corpora; the proposed asymmetric self-play framework shows promise and could be extended to online/adaptive settings in future work.

Abstract: Self-play reinforcement learning has shown strong performance in domains with formally verifiable structure, such as mathematics and coding, where both problem generation and reward computation can be grounded in explicit rules. Extending this paradigm to scientific literature is more challenging: the relationships among multi-modal elements within and across documents are rarely made explicit in text, which makes automatic generation of relational reasoning questions difficult and weakens the reliability of reward signals. We propose SPARK (Self-Play with Asymmetric Reward from Knowledge Graphs), a framework that automatically constructs a unified knowledge graph (KG) from multi-document scientific literature and uses it as the structural basis for self-play. KG paths over multimodal nodes serve as a source for generating relational reasoning questions, and structured facts stored in the KG provide a basis for verifiable reward computation. A single small vision-language model (sVLM) alternates between Proposer and Solver roles under information asymmetry against a fixed KG, a design that we believe can be naturally extended toward online adaptation in future work. We evaluate SPARK on public benchmarks and a self-constructed cross-document multi-hop QA dataset. Results show that SPARK consistently outperforms flat-corpus-based self-play baselines, and the performance gap widens as hop count increases, suggesting that KG-structure grounding contributes to relational multi-hop reasoning beyond what unstructured corpus grounding can provide.

</details>


### [388] [Who Prices Cognitive Labor in the Age of Agents? A Position on Compute-Anchored Wages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05558)
*Siqi Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframes AI agents as a production technology that converts compute capital into cognitive labor, deriving a Compute-Anchored Wage (CAW) bound: for substitutable tasks, human wages are bounded above by λ·k·r_c, with the price-setter shifting from the labor market to the compute capital market. Generalizes via CES and task categorization, implying a reversal in skill-biased technical change dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address a common intuition—the near-zero marginal cost of replicated AI agents would erode cognitive-labor wages. The paper argues this is incorrect in mechanism but partially correct in conclusion, and that recognizing compute as the binding margin changes theory and policy.

Method: Extend the textbook factor-pricing framework by modeling agents as a production technology that converts compute capital K_c into effective cognitive labor L_A. Define k as compute intensity per effective agent-labor unit and λ as relative human-to-agent productivity. Derive a CAW bound on wages. Generalize with CES to separate substitutable vs complementary tasks and analyze factor shares.

Result: Derivation of the Compute-Anchored Wage bound for substitutable tasks, showing that competitive human wages are bounded by λ·k·r_c and that the elastic margin migrates to the compute capital market. The framework yields an inverted direction of skill-biased technical change when separating task substitutability vs complementarity; policy and theory implications follow.

Conclusion: The price-setter for cognitive labor is the compute capital market, not the labor market; this reframes wage-setting, policy considerations, and the flow of talent and capital in AI-intensive economies.

Abstract: A natural intuition about the economics of AI agents is that, because agents can be replicated at near-zero marginal cost, they constitute a labor input in infinitely elastic supply, and therefore drive cognitive-labor wages to zero. We argue this framing is wrong in mechanism but partially correct in conclusion, and that the correction matters for both theory and policy. \textbf{Agents are not labor; they are a production technology that converts compute capital $K_c$ into effective units of cognitive labor $L_A$.} Once this is recognized, the elastic-supply margin that anchors the equilibrium wage migrates from the labor market to the compute capital market. Building on the textbook factor-pricing framework \citep{mankiw2020}, we derive a \emph{Compute-Anchored Wage} (CAW) bound stating that, on tasks where human and agent cognitive labor are substitutes, the competitive human wage is bounded above by $λ\cdot k \cdot r_c$, where $r_c$ is the rental rate of compute capital, $k$ is the compute intensity of one effective agent-labor unit, and $λ$ is the relative human-to-agent productivity. We generalize the result through CES aggregation, separate substitutable from complementary tasks (yielding a directional inversion of skill-biased technical change), and discuss factor-share consequences. The position is concise: \emph{the price-setter for cognitive labor is no longer the labor market.}

</details>


### [389] [Nonsense Helps: Prompt Space Perturbation Broadens Reasoning Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05566)
*Langlin Huang,Chengsong Huang,Jinyuan Li,Donghong Cai,Yuyi Yang,Jiaxin Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoPE (Lorem Perturbation for Exploration) introduces prompt-space perturbations using Lorem Ipsum to break the zero-advantage bottleneck in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for LLMs, improving exploration and training signals across 1.7B–7B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO often encounters a zero-advantage problem when all sampled rollouts fail, leaving no useful gradient signals. Static sampling limits reasoning exploration on hard questions. A simple method to broaden exploration without increasing computation is needed.

Method: Stochastically prepend sequences assembled from Lorem Ipsum (Latin placeholder text) to prompts before resampling, creating Latin-based perturbations with low perplexity. This perturbation aims to shift the model’s output distribution to unlock alternative reasoning paths. Experiments evaluate LoPE on 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models and compare against standard resampling.

Result: LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with original prompts. Other Latin-based random sequences with low perplexity also serve as effective perturbations, indicating the approach generalizes beyond Lorem Ipsum.

Conclusion: LoPE is a simple, effective baseline for broadening exploration in LLM reinforcement learning and can mitigate the zero-advantage problem by diversifying prompt-space inputs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, in complex tasks, GRPO frequently suffers from the ``zero-advantage problem'': when all sampled rollouts for a query fail, the relative advantage collapses to zero. Consequently, the model loses effective training signals for these questions, wasting the training data and computational budget. While simply increasing the sampling budget for these questions is a common remedy, the static sampling policy inherently constrains reasoning exploration, limiting the success rate. In this paper, we propose Lorem Perturbation for Exploration (LoPE), a simple yet effective training framework to break this exploration bottleneck. We posit that task-irrelevant prompt-space perturbations can shift the model's output distribution enough to unlock orthogonal reasoning pathways for hard questions. Specifically, LoPE prepends sequences stochastically assembled from Lorem Ipsum vocabulary (a pseudo-Latin placeholder text) to the prompts before resampling. Experiments across 1.7B, 4B, and 7B models demonstrate that LoPE significantly outperforms resampling with the original prompts. Further analysis reveals that other Latin-based random sequences with low perplexity are also effective perturbations. Our results establish LoPE as a strong baseline for broadening exploration in LLM reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [390] [Locality-aware Private Class Identification for Domain Adaptation with Extreme Label Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05567)
*Chuan-Xian Ren,Cheng-Jun Guo,Hong Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReOT: an OT-based domain adaptation method that identifies private classes via a locality-aware score on transport mass, with theoretical generalization guarantees and empirical validation under severe label shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address domain adaptation with partial/private label overlap, where private classes exist in one domain only. Traditional outlier-based assumptions fail due to large intra-class variance, necessitating reliable private-class identification and robust transfer.

Method: Introduce a locality-aware private-class identification score derived from local transport properties of optimal transport. Build ReOT (reliable OT-based domain adaptation) that minimizes classification risk while enforcing a separated cluster structure between identified shared and private classes, thereby avoiding mismatched cross-domain pairs and enabling reliable intra-class transport.

Result: The score function can theoretically distinguish shared vs private samples. A generalization upper bound for target risk under extreme label shift is established and minimized by ReOT. Empirical results on benchmark datasets demonstrate effectiveness in severe label shift scenarios.

Conclusion: ReOT provides a principled OT-based framework for handling private classes in domain adaptation, achieving reliable private-class identification, separation of shared/private clusters, bounded target risk, and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Domain adaptation aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain with different distributions. In real-world scenarios, the label spaces of the two domains often have an inclusion relationship, where some classes exist only in one domain but not the other. These non-overlapping classes are referred to as private classes. Identifying private class samples and mitigating their adverse effects is critical in the literature. Existing methods rely on the assumption that shifts in private classes are large enough to be considered outliers. However, the variance within a single shared class can be significantly larger than the difference between a private class and another shared class, challenging this assumption. Consequently, private classes substantially increase the difficulty of cross-domain classification. To address these issues, based on local transportation and metric properties of optimal transport (OT), a locality-aware private class identification approach is proposed in the form of a score function on transport mass. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is theoretically proven, highlighting the score function's strong ability to distinguish between shared and private class samples. Building on this, we introduce a reliable OT-based method (ReOT) for domain adaptation under severe label shift. ReOT minimizes classification risk while learning the separated cluster structure between the identified shared classes and private classes, effectively avoiding mismatch between shared-private sample pairs, thus ensuring that important knowledge is reliably transported intra-class to mitigate class-conditional discrepancy. Furthermore, a generalization upper bound of the target risk is provided for extreme label shift scenarios, which can be minimized by ReOT. Extensive experiments on benchmarks validate the effectiveness of ReOT.

</details>


### [391] [AlphaCrafter: A Full-Stack Multi-Agent Framework for Cross-Sectional Quantitative Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05580)
*Yishuo Yuan,Jiayi Sheng,Sirui Zeng,Jiaqi Wang,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A tri-agent full-stack framework AlphaCrafter combines continuous factor mining, regime-aware screening, and risk-controlled execution to achieve robust, adaptive cross-sectional trading; it outperforms baselines on the CSI 300 and S&P 500.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial markets are non-stationary due to evolving macro regimes, microstructural frictions, and behavioral dynamics. Effective strategies require continuous factor discovery, regime-sensitive selection, and disciplined execution, not static, siloed components.

Method: Three specialized agents: (1) Miner uses LLM-guided search to continually expand the factor pool; (2) Screener evaluates current market conditions to construct regime-conditioned ensembles of factors; (3) Trader translates ensembles into quantitative strategies with explicit risk constraints; the agents operate in a closed-loop cross-sectional trading framework adapting to evolving market dynamics.

Result: Empirical experiments on CSI 300 and S&P 500 show AlphaCrafter achieves higher risk-adjusted returns and lower cross-trial variance compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: An integrated, adaptive factor-to-execution pipeline can yield robust trading performance in non-stationary markets, illustrating the value of end-to-end automation with rational multi-agent orchestration.

Abstract: Financial markets are inherently non-stationary, driven by complex interactions among macroeconomic regimes, microstructural frictions, and behavioral dynamics. Building quantitative strategies that remain profitable demands the continuous coupling of factor discovery, regime-adaptive selection, and risk-constrained execution. Prevailing approaches, however, optimize these components under static or isolated assumptions. Factor mining frameworks typically treat alpha discovery as a one-time search process, implicitly assuming that factor efficacy persists across market regimes. Execution-oriented systems often adopt role-playing agent architectures that simulate anthropomorphic trading committees, introducing behavioral noise rather than systematic rationality. Consequently, a fully automated, rationality-driven framework unifying a coherent quantitative pipeline remains absent. We introduce AlphaCrafter, a full-stack multi-agent framework that closes this gap through a continuously adaptive factor-to-execution pipeline, designed to track and respond to evolving market conditions without manual intervention. AlphaCrafter operates via three specialized agents: a Miner that continuously expands the factor pool via LLM-guided search, a Screener that assesses prevailing market conditions to construct regime-conditioned factor ensembles, and a Trader that translates these ensembles into quantitative strategies under explicit risk constraints. Together, these three agents form a closed-loop cross-sectional trading system that adapts holistically to evolving market dynamics. Extensive experiments on CSI 300 and S&P 500 demonstrate that AlphaCrafter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in risk-adjusted returns while exhibiting the lowest cross-trial variance, confirming that integrated and adaptive factor-to-execution design yields robust trading performance.

</details>


### [392] [Belief Memory: Agent Memory Under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05583)
*Junfeng Liao,Qizhou Wang,Jianing Zhu,Bo Du,Rui Yan,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BeliefMem introduces a probabilistic memory for LLM agents by storing multiple candidate conclusions per observation with associated probabilities, updated via Noisy-OR rules. It preserves uncertainty, surfaces all candidates during retrieval, and empirically improves performance on LoCoMo and ALFWorld with limited data, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory paradigms for long-context LLM agents commit to a single, deterministic interpretation of observations, which creates self-reinforcing errors and discards useful uncertainty. A probabilistic memory that retains multiple competing conclusions can prevent overconfidence and improve robustness in partially observable environments.

Method: BeliefMem stores, for each observation, several candidate conclusions as separate memory entries, each with a probability. When new observations arrive, probabilities are updated using Noisy-OR aggregation across evidence. Retrieval presents all candidates with their probabilities, preserving uncertainty and allowing the agent to act with high confidence on well-supported conclusions while remaining adaptable to new evidence.

Result: Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld show that BeliefMem achieves the best average performance, even with limited data, and substantially outperforms established baselines.

Conclusion: Probabilistic memory with multi-candidate beliefs preserves uncertainty and enables more robust decision-making in partially observable environments, representing a promising direction for future agent memory architectures.

Abstract: LLM agents that operate over long context depend on external memory to accumulate knowledge over time. However, existing methods typically store each observation as a single deterministic conclusion (e.g., inferring "API~X failed" from temporary errors), even though such observations are inherently partial and potentially ambiguous. By committing to one conclusion and discarding uncertainty, these methods introduce self-reinforcing error: the agent acts on the stored conclusion, never revisits alternatives, and reinforces the conclusion over time. To address this issue, we propose BeliefMem, which shifts the memory paradigm from committing to a single conclusion per observation to retaining multiple candidate conclusions with their probabilities. Concretely, BeliefMem stores the candidate conclusions as separate memory entries, each carrying a probability that is updated via Noisy-OR rules as new observations arrive. At retrieval, all candidates surface together with their probabilities, keeping alternatives visible to the agent. Since each conclusion in memory retains its probability, BeliefMem preserves the uncertainty that the deterministic paradigm discards, enabling the agent to act with high confidence on well-evidenced knowledge while retaining the capacity to update its confidence when new evidence arrives. Empirical evaluations on LoCoMo and ALFWorld benchmarks show that, even with limited data, BeliefMem achieves the best average performance, remarkably outperforming well-known baselines. More broadly, such probabilistic memory produces substantial gains and explores a new direction for agent memory in partially observable environments.

</details>


### [393] [Causal Probing for Internal Visual Representations in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05593)
*Zehao Deng,Tianjie Ju,Zheng Wu,Liangbo He,Jun Lan,Huijia Zhu,Weiqiang Wang,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Activation-steering causal interventions reveal distinct encoding patterns in MLLMs: entities are locally memorized while abstract concepts are globally distributed; model depth is needed to encode distributed/complex abstractions, whereas entity representations are scale-invariant. Reverse steering shows perception-generation compensation, and visual reasoning reveals a gap between perception and procedural execution for abstract problem solving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the internal mechanisms by which Multimodal LLMs encode, ground, and reason about visual concepts, and to explain observed scaling behaviors and perceptual-reasoning gaps.

Method: Introduce a causal framework based on activation steering to perturb internal visual representations. Conduct systematic interventions across four visual concept categories, and perform reverse steering (blocking explicit output) to study latent activations. Extend analysis to visual reasoning tasks to compare perception with procedural execution.

Result: Entities exhibit localized memorization; abstract concepts are globally distributed across the network. Model depth scaling is indispensable for encoding distributed/complex abstract concepts, while entity localization remains invariant to depth. Reverse steering triggers a surge in latent activations when explicit output is blocked, indicating a perception-generation compensation. In visual reasoning, MLLMs recognize geometric relations but treat them as static features, failing to trigger procedural execution for abstract problem solving.

Conclusion: There is a mechanistic driver of scaling laws: depth is required to encode distributed and complex abstract concepts; local concepts remain depth-invariant. The findings reveal a compensatory balance between perception and generation and identify a perception–reasoning disconnect that hampers abstract problem solving, suggesting directions to enhance grounding and procedural reasoning in MLLMs.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable success of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across diverse tasks, the internal mechanisms governing how they encode and ground distinct visual concepts remain poorly understood. To bridge this gap, we propose a causal framework based on activation steering to actively probe and manipulate internal visual representations. Through systematic intervention across four visual concept categories, our results reveal a divergence in concept encoding: entities exhibit distinct localized memorization, whereas abstract concepts are globally distributed across the network. Critically, this divergence uncovers a mechanistic driver of scaling laws: increasing model depth is indispensable for encoding distributed and complex abstract concepts, whereas entity localization remains remarkably invariant to scale. Furthermore, reverse steering uncovers that blocking explicit output triggers a surge in latent activations, exposing a compensatory mechanism between perception and generation. Finally, extending our analysis to visual reasoning, we expose a disconnect between perception and reasoning although MLLMs successfully recognize geometric relations, they treat them merely as static visual features, failing to trigger the procedural execution necessary for abstract problem-solving.

</details>


### [394] [Text-Graph Synergy: A Bidirectional Verification and Completion Framework for RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05643)
*Jiarui Zhong,Hong Cai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A bidirectional Text-Graph RAG framework (TGS-RAG) that jointly leverages a Graph-to-Text and Text-to-Graph channel to address information islands, improving multi-hop factual grounding while maintaining efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RAG struggles with irrelevant pseudo-evidence and pruning-induced loss of valid reasoning paths; there is a need for symmetric, bidirectional integration to mitigate asymmetric information flows between text and graphs.

Method: Graph-to-Text uses a Global Voting mechanism from visited graph nodes to re-rank/refine textual evidence, filtering semantic noise. Text-to-Graph employs a Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging algorithm that resurrects valid but previously pruned reasoning paths from search history using textual cues, without extra database overhead.

Result: On multiple multi-hop reasoning benchmarks, TGS-RAG outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a better balance between retrieval precision and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: TGS-RAG provides a unified, bidirectional synergistic framework that mitigates information islands and yields robust gains across benchmarks; the approach demonstrates strong potential for broader applicability and can be complemented with targeted ablations and efficiency analyses.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a core paradigm for enhancing factual grounding and multi-hop reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). Traditional text-based RAG often retrieves logically irrelevant pseudo-evidence, while graph-based RAG is frequently hindered by search-time pruning, which may discard potentially valid reasoning paths. Existing hybrid approaches primarily adopt simple evidence concatenation or unidirectional enhancement, which fails to address the fundamental "Information Island" problem caused by asymmetric reasoning flows between unstructured text and structured graphs. We propose \textbf{TGS-RAG}, a unified framework for \textbf{T}ext-\textbf{G}raph \textbf{S}ynergistic enhancement. TGS-RAG introduces a bidirectional mechanism: (i) a \textbf{Graph-to-Text} channel that employs a Global Voting strategy from visited graph nodes to re-rank and refine textual evidence, filtering out semantic noise; and (ii) a \textbf{Text-to-Graph} channel that utilizes the \textbf{Memory-based Orphan Entity Bridging} algorithm. This algorithm utilizes textual cues to proactively resurrect valid but previously pruned reasoning paths from the search history without additional database overhead. Experimental results on multiple multi-hop reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TGS-RAG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a superior balance between retrieval precision and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [395] [Retrieval-Conditioned Topology Selection with Provable Budget Conservation for Multi-Agent Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05657)
*Abhijit Talluri,Pujith Anne,Bhagavan Choudary Pendiyala,Raghavendra Chilukuri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RGAO integrates complexity-conditioned routing with formal resource algebras for code-generation multi-agent systems, using a hierarchical code retrieval to adapt topology; achieves provable budget conservation, reduces misrouting, and enables fast DAG construction with linear scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fundamental routing problem in multi-agent LLM code-generation: the optimal orchestration topology should depend on the code's structural complexity, but existing systems pick topologies without consulting the codebase; combine complexity-aware routing with formal budget guarantees to align topology with structure.

Method: Introduce Retrieval-Guided Adaptive Orchestration (RGAO) within Code-Agent. Extract a structural complexity vector from a hierarchical code index and select an orchestration topology accordingly. Sub-agents operate under six-dimensional budget vectors with formal contracts. Combine two prior lines of work—complexity-conditioned LLM routing and formal resource algebras—to yield budget conservation under retrieval-conditioned dynamic topology selection; include a complexity-conditioned topology router, budget algebra with a structural-induction conservation theorem, and a hierarchical code retrieval engine.

Result: Misrouting proxy reduced from 30.1% to 8.2%; sub-millisecond DAG construction; linear scalability of tree-index; empirical evaluation supports efficient, retrieval-guided topology selection with provable budget properties.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that integrating complexity-aware routing with formal budget constraints is viable, delivering provable budget conservation and efficient, scalable retrieval-guided orchestration for multi-agent LLM code-generation systems; highlights the practical benefits and potential limitations needing further investigation.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems for code generation face a fundamental routing problem: the optimal orchestration topology depends on the structural complexity of the code under modification, yet existing systems select topologies without consulting the codebase. We present Retrieval-Guided Adaptive Orchestration (RGAO), an architecture that closes this loop by extracting a structural complexity vector from a hierarchical code index before selecting the orchestration topology. RGAO operates within Code-Agent, a multi-agent framework whose sub-agents are governed by formal contracts with six-dimensional budget vectors. Our headline contribution is the composition of two previously separate lines of work -- complexity-conditioned LLM routing and formal resource algebras -- yielding a property neither admits alone: provable budget conservation under retrieval-conditioned dynamic topology selection. Concretely we contribute: (1) a complexity-conditioned topology router that reduces proxy-measured misrouting from 30.1% to 8.2%; (2) a budget algebra with a structural-induction conservation theorem; and (3) a hierarchical code retrieval engine. Empirical evaluation demonstrates sub-millisecond DAG construction and linear tree-index scalability.

</details>


### [396] [Large Vision-Language Models Get Lost in Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05668)
*Gongli Xi,Ye Tian,Mengyu Yang,Huahui Yi,Liang Lin,Xiaoshuai Hao,Kun Wang,Wendong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified information-theoretic and geometric framework analyzes LVLM decoder modules, uncovering a functional decoupling: attention preserves subspaces and reconfigures; FFNs expand subspaces and drive semantic innovation. Empirically, replacing learned attention with fixed weights (e.g., Gaussian noise) matches or surpasses vanilla models on many datasets, suggesting misallocation and redundancy in current LVLMs and that they may rely too heavily on attention rather than visual context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a unified theoretical basis for attributing the roles of internal modules in LVLM decoders, bridging information-theoretic and geometric perspectives and informing architectural optimization.

Method: Develop a framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify geometric and entropic properties of residual updates in Transformer-based decoders. Use this framework to characterize attention and FFN updates as subspace-preserving vs subspace-expanding operators. Conduct experiments by replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) and evaluate performance across multiple datasets to assess reliance on attention versus context.

Result: The framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving, reconfigurative operator; FFNs function as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Replacing learned attention weights with fixed values yields comparable or superior performance in the majority of datasets, indicating misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms and that LVLMs may over-rely on attention rather than effectively leveraging visual context.

Conclusion: There is a misallocation in current LVLM decoder design, with attention not efficiently exploiting visual context. A theoretical framework based on information theory and geometry explains the decoupled roles and motivates architectural revisions, potentially improving efficiency by reducing reliance on learned attention or rethinking how attention is integrated.

Abstract: Despite the rapid evolution of training paradigms, the decoder backbone of large vision--language models (LVLMs) remains fundamentally rooted in the residual-connection Transformer architecture. Therefore, deciphering the distinct roles of internal modules is critical for understanding model mechanics and guiding architectural optimization. While prior statistical approaches have provided valuable attribution-based insights, they often lack a unified theoretical basis. To bridge this gap, we propose a unified framework grounded in information theory and geometry to quantify the geometric and entropic nature of residual updates. Applying this unified framework reveals a fundamental functional decoupling: Attention acts as a subspace-preserving operator focused on reconfiguration, whereas FFNs serve as subspace-expanding operators driving semantic innovation. Strikingly, further experiments demonstrate that replacing learned attention weights with predefined values (e.g., Gaussian noise) yields comparable or even superior performance across a majority of datasets relative to vanilla models. These results expose severe misallocation and redundancy in current mechanisms, suggesting that state-of-the-art LVLMs effectively ``get lost in attention'' rather than efficiently leveraging visual context.

</details>


### [397] [Attractor Geometry of Transformer Memory: From Conflict Arbitration to Confident Hallucination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05686)
*Qiyao Liang,Risto Miikkulainen,Ila Fiete*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two LM failure modes—conflict between parametric memory (PM) and working memory (WM) and hallucination when facts are never learned—are explained by a geometric attractor framework in hidden state space. A margin to memorized basins predicts recall vs hallucination far better than output entropy; a scaling law governs confident hallucinations, and freezing the LM head erases epistemic state, worsening with scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why language models produce confident but incorrect outputs and to develop better monitoring by examining internal geometry, not just output behavior.

Method: Construct a controlled synthetic task: map entity identifiers to unique codes, implant PM via LoRA adapters, and causally isolate components with targeted adapter placements. Analyze hidden-state dynamics of autoregressive generation, define a geometric margin (distance to the nearest memorized attractor basin), compare margin-based detection to output-entropy-based methods, and validate on natural-language factual queries without adaptation. Demonstrate attractor basins, basin competition (conflict) and basin absence (hallucination), and derive scaling laws.

Result: Evidence for a unified attractor-based account: correct recall corresponds to convergence toward the nearest memorized basin, while conflict or absence leads to confident but incorrect outputs. Margin-based separation outperforms entropy for detecting hallucinations, with zero false refusals in the tested regime. The separation generalizes to natural-language factual queries without fine-tuning. A scaling law, C = exp(-c/Δ̄), describes the fraction of confident hallucinations and increases with model scale. Hidden states encode epistemic state; the frozen output head erases this information, and the erasure worsens as scale grows.

Conclusion: Attractor geometry is structural to language models; output-head monitoring is fundamentally limited because it cannot distinguish when PM and WM disagree or when no memorized basin exists. Margin-based internal diagnostics offer a robust, scalable way to detect epistemic failures and motivate architectural or training adjustments to preserve and utilize hidden-state epistemic representations.

Abstract: Language models draw on two knowledge sources: facts baked into weights (parametric memory, PM) and information in context (working memory, WM). We study two mechanistically distinct failure modes--conflict, when PM and WM disagree and interfere; and hallucination, when the queried fact was never learned. Both produce confident output regardless, making output-based monitoring blind by design. We show both failures share a unified geometric account. In the hidden-state space of autoregressive generation, learned facts form attractor basins. Conflict is basin competition: WM disrupts convergence to the correct basin without raising output entropy. Hallucination is basin absence: the hidden state drifts freely when no memorized basin exists. The frozen LM head, designed for next-token prediction, cannot distinguish these cases and fires confidently either way. We verify this account in a controlled synthetic task--entity identifiers mapped to unique codes with PM installed via LoRA adapters--where ground truth is exact and component roles can be causally isolated through targeted adapter placement. Geometric margin--the hidden state's distance to the nearest memorized basin--reads this geometry directly and separates correct recall from hallucination far more cleanly than output entropy, with zero false refusals where entropy-based detection cannot avoid rejecting the vast majority of correct outputs. The separation holds on natural-language factual queries from the pretrained model with no adaptation, confirming attractor geometry is structural rather than a fine-tuning artifact. The fraction of confident hallucinations follows a scaling law $C = \exp(-c/\barΔ)$, growing with scale even as overall error rates fall. Hidden states reliably encode epistemic state; the frozen output head systematically erases it--and this erasure worsens with scale.

</details>


### [398] [DataDignity: Training Data Attribution for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05687)
*Xiaomin Li,Andrzej Banburski-Fahey,Jaron Lanier*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces FakeWiki, a controlled benchmark for pinpoint provenance in LLM outputs, and two retrieval approaches—SteerFuse (activation-space steering) and ScoringModel (contrastive provenance ranker). Across 9 instruction-tuned LLMs and 5 query types, ScoringModel substantially boosts Recall@10, outperforming baselines and SteerFuse; results highlight the necessity of evaluation settings that separate true answer support from surface similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditors require precise source attributions for model outputs, not just correctness. Existing retrieval baselines risk exploiting lexical cues or topical similarity rather than true provenance. A robust benchmark with transformed queries is needed to stress-test provenance ranking and ensure evaluation reflects true support.

Method: Create FakeWiki with 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles preserving provenance but weakening lexical shortcuts. Include QA probes, source-preserving paraphrases, retro-generated variants, hard anti-documents, and five query conditions (clean prompting plus four jailbreak-inspired transformations). Evaluate seven retrieval baselines, SteerFuse, and ScoringModel. ScoringModel trains a cross-modal embedding space with InfoNCE using in-batch, retrieval-mined, and anti-document negatives.

Result: ScoringModel raises mean Recall@10 from 35.0 (best baseline) to 52.2, without inference-time fusion; wins 41/45 model-by-condition cells. SteerFuse is usually second-best without training. On jailbreak-inspired queries, ScoringModel improves Recall@10 by 15.7 points on average over the best baseline.

Conclusion: Robust provenance attribution requires evaluation setups that separate true support from topical or lexical resemblance. Activation-space evidence combined with contrastive training can substantially improve provenance ranking, and FakeWiki provides a stress-test to reveal these gains.

Abstract: Auditing language-model outputs often requires more than judging correctness: an auditor may need to identify which source document most likely supports the knowledge expressed in a response. We study this as pinpoint provenance: given a prompt, a target-model response, and a candidate corpus, rank the documents that best support the response. We introduce FakeWiki, a controlled benchmark of 3,537 fabricated Wikipedia-style articles designed to preserve ground-truth provenance while weakening lexical shortcuts. FakeWiki includes QA probes, source-preserving paraphrases, retro-generated variants, hard anti-documents that remain topically similar while removing answer-critical facts, and five query conditions: clean prompting plus four jailbreak-inspired transformations. We evaluate seven retrieval baselines, a training-free activation-steering retrieval-fusion method, SteerFuse, and a supervised contrastive provenance ranker, ScoringModel. ScoringModel maps response and document features into a shared space and is trained with InfoNCE using in-batch, retrieval-mined, and anti-document negatives. Across nine open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs and five query conditions, ScoringModel improves mean Recall@10 from 35.0 for the strongest retrieval baseline to 52.2, without inference-time fusion, and wins 41/45 model-by-condition cells. SteerFuse is usually second-best despite requiring no supervised training, showing that activation-space evidence can efficiently complement text retrieval. On jailbreak-inspired transformed queries, ScoringModel improves Recall@10 by 15.7 points on average over the best baseline. Overall, our work shows that robust training data attribution requires evaluation settings that separate true answer support from topical or lexical resemblance.

</details>


### [399] [Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment for Ambiguous User Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06223)
*Junhyuk Kwon,Seungjoon Lee,Hyejin Park,Kyle Min,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProCompNav: a two-stage, pool-level comparative-judgment approach for interactive instance navigation that asks discriminative binary questions to prune distractors; achieves higher success rates and shorter responses on CoIN-Bench and TextNav.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ambiguity in natural-language instance navigation where the initial request does not uniquely identify the target. The agent should actively ask only necessary questions to distinguish the target from distractors, reducing user burden and avoiding premature decisions.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) build a candidate pool; (2) identify the target via comparative judgment. Each round extracts an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, asks a binary yes/no question, and prunes all inconsistent candidates at once, converting disambiguation into pool-level discriminative questioning.

Result: On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while reducing Response Length. It achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, suggesting broad usefulness of comparative judgment for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

Conclusion: Comparative judgment and pool-level question selection effectively disambiguate targets among distractors, reducing user burden and improving reliability across navigation benchmarks; potential for broader applicability to NL navigation tasks.

Abstract: Natural-language instance navigation becomes challenging when the initial user request does not uniquely specify the target instance. A practical agent should reduce the user's burden by actively asking only the information needed to distinguish the target from similar distractors, rather than requiring a detailed description upfront. Existing approaches often fall short of this goal: they may stop at the first plausible candidate before sufficiently exploring alternatives, or, even after collecting multiple candidates, ask about the target's attributes derived from individual candidates rather than questions selected to distinguish candidates in the pool. As a result, despite the dialogue, the agent may still fail to distinguish the target from distractors, leading to premature decisions and lengthy user responses. We propose Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment (ProCompNav), a two-stage framework that first constructs a candidate pool and then identifies the target through comparative judgment. At each round, ProCompNav extracts an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, asks a binary yes/no question, and prunes all inconsistent candidates at once. This reframes disambiguation from open-ended target description to pool-level discriminative questioning, where each question is chosen to narrow the candidate set. On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. ProCompNav also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, suggesting that comparative judgment is broadly useful for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

</details>


### [400] [GCCM: Enhancing Generative Graph Prediction via Contrastive Consistency Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05689)
*Shaozhen Ma,Wei Huang,Hanchen Wang,Dong Wen,Wenjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GCCM, a graph diffusion model with contrastive consistency that uses negative pairs and feature perturbations to prevent shortcut collapse, achieving improved graph prediction performance over deterministic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based conditional graph generation faces costly inference and unstable sampling; existing consistency-training methods risk shortcut solutions where the model ignores the noisy target, effectively becoming deterministic.

Method: GCCM adds negative pairs into a contrastive consistency objective and applies input feature perturbations to break identical conditioning across noise levels, enforcing both self-consistency and separation from negative targets.

Result: Empirical results on benchmark graph datasets show GCCM mitigates shortcut behavior and yields consistent improvements over deterministic predictors.

Conclusion: GCCM provides a robust training framework for diffusion-based graph prediction by combining contrastive consistency with feature perturbations to avoid shortcut solutions and improve performance.

Abstract: Conditional generative models, particularly diffusion-based methods, have recently been applied to graph prediction by modeling the target as a conditional distribution given the input graph, yielding competitive results compared to deterministic predictor. However, existing diffusion-based prediction methods typically require expensive iterative denoising at inference and often suffer from unstable sampling, which motivates recent efforts to reduce inference denoising steps and enable stable sampling via techniques such as consistency training. Despite this progress, we find that existing consistency training methods for graph prediction could potentially fall into a shortcut solution: the model may attempt to satisfy the self-consistency constraint by ignoring the noisy target (i.e., assigning it negligible weight), ultimately collapsing into a purely deterministic predictor. To mitigate such shortcut solution, we propose GCCM, a graph contrastive consistency model that goes beyond isolated pairwise matching between the same target at different noise levels by introducing negative pairs into a contrastive consistency objective. This adds an additional separation requirement, making the shortcut solution no longer trivially sufficient to satisfy the proposed objective. Moreover, we apply feature perturbation to the input node/edge features to break identical conditioning on the input graph, so that the shortcut no longer yields the same predictions across noise levels and becomes less attractive. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that GCCM mitigates the shortcut solution and yields consistent performance improvements in graph prediction compared to deterministic predictors.

</details>


### [401] [Saliency-Aware Regularized Quantization Calibration for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05693)
*Yanlong Zhao,Xiaoyuan Cheng,Huihang Liu,Baihua He,Xinyu Zhang,Harrison Bo Hua Zhu,Wenlong Chen,Li Zeng,Zhuo Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Saliency-Aware Regularized Quantization Calibration (SARQC) improves PTQ by regularizing quantized weights toward their full-precision counterparts using saliency cues, yielding better generalization with no inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration-only optimization on limited data can drive quantized weights away from original weights, increasing generalization risk. A regularizer tethering quantized weights improves downstream performance.

Method: Introduce a saliency-aware regularization term into the PTQ objective. The framework unifies scale-search and Gram-based PTQ under a common formulation; can be integrated into existing pipelines.

Result: On dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs, SARQC yields consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot accuracy, without extra inference cost.

Conclusion: SARQC provides a simple, effective, and broadly applicable enhancement to PTQ, improving generalization while maintaining inference efficiency; it can be adopted across PTQ calibration strategies.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is an effective approach for deploying large language models (LLMs) under memory and latency constraints. Most existing PTQ methods determine quantization parameters by minimizing a layer-wise reconstruction error on a predetermined calibration dataset, usually optimized via either scale search or Gram-based methods. However, from the perspective of generalization risk, existing calibration objectives of PTQ based only on empirical reconstruction error on limited or unrepresentative calibration data could move the quantized weights away from the original weights. This may cause the generalization risk to diverge, potentially degrading downstream performance. To address this issue, we propose \emph{Saliency-Aware Regularized Quantization Calibration} (SARQC) a unified framework that augments the standard PTQ objective with a saliency-aware regularization term. This term encourages quantized weights to stay close to the original weights during calibration, leading to improved generalization during inference. SARQC integrates seamlessly into existing PTQ pipelines, enhancing both scale search and Gram-based methods under a unified formulation. Extensive experiments on dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs demonstrate consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot accuracy, without additional computational overhead during inference.

</details>


### [402] [Inference-Time Budget Control for LLM Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05701)
*Zhengru Fang,Senkang Forest Hu,Zhonghao Chang,Yu Guo,Yihang Tao,Hongyao Liu,Mengzhe Ruan,Jun Huang,Yuguang Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two-stage inference-time budget control for LLM search agents in multi-hop QA using VOI-based action scoring to allocate budget across retrieval, decomposition, and answer commitment, plus an evidence-grounded finalizer; achieves gains over baselines under dual-budget constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual constraints on tool usage and token generation limit this setting; there is a need to selectively allocate search actions and determine when evidence suffices to commit an answer.

Method: At search time, assign each feasible action a task-level Value-of-Information (VOI) score that estimates marginal task value per unit budget given the current state and remaining budgets; select among retrieval, decomposition, and answer commitment based on VOI. After search, apply a selective finalizer that compares the trajectory answer with a refined candidate and rewrites only if a low-risk residual error is detected.

Result: Experiments across four multi-hop QA benchmarks, three LLM backbones, and four budget levels show positive gains over four audited baselines under the same dual-budget protocol. Ablations indicate search-time budget control (especially budget-dependent penalty) yields the main improvements; answer-time control helps mainly when the retrieval path is already adequate.

Conclusion: Inference-time budget control should govern both budget spending during search and the decision of when to commit the final answer; VOI-based budget allocation is effective for balancing retrieval, decomposition, and finalization under dual-budget constraints.

Abstract: LLM search agents increasingly rely on tools at inference time, but their trajectories are often constrained by hard limits on both tool calls and generated tokens. Under such dual budgets, better answers require not only stronger models, but also explicit control over which search action should receive the next budget unit and when the accumulated evidence is sufficient to commit a final answer. We study this problem in multi-hop question answering (QA) and formulate it as two-stage inference-time budget control. At search time, our controller assigns each feasible action a task-level Value-of-Information (VOI) score, defined as an operational estimate of marginal task value per unit budget under the current search state and remaining dual budget, and uses this score to choose among retrieval, decomposition, and answer commitment. After search, a selective evidence-grounded finalizer compares the trajectory answer with a refined candidate and rewrites only when the residual error appears to be a low-risk answer-form error. Across four multi-hop QA benchmarks, three LLM backbones, and four budget levels, the method yields positive aggregate gains over four audited baselines under the same hard dual-budget protocol. Ablations show that search-time budget control, especially budget-dependent penalty, provides the main performance gain, while answer-time control helps mainly when the retrieval path is already adequate. These results suggest that inference-time budget control for LLM search agents should govern both how budget is spent during search and how the final answer is committed.

</details>


### [403] [Knowledge-Graph Paths as Intermediate Supervision for Self-Evolving Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05702)
*Huyu Wu,Jun Liu,Xiaochi Wei,Yan Gao,Yi Wu,Yao Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Knowledge-graph guided question construction plus Waypoint Coverage Reward (WCR) improve SSP, yielding consistent gains across QA benchmarks, especially multi-hop tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two SSP bottlenecks: (1) Proposer forms questions from isolated entities with limited relational context, causing many invalid/unverifiable questions; (2) Solver gets only a binary reward, losing information from partially successful search trajectories.

Method: Two-part approach: (1) ground question construction on LLM-guided knowledge-graph subgraphs to provide relational context for the Proposer; (2) introduce Waypoint Coverage Reward (WCR) that grants graded partial credit to solver trajectories based on coverage of entities along the construction path, with full credit for correct answers. Evaluation across seven QA benchmarks and nine model configurations.

Result: Across seven QA benchmarks and nine model configurations, the method improves the average score over standard SSP in all configurations, with notable gains on multi-hop QA tasks.

Conclusion: Knowledge-graph paths serve as lightweight intermediate supervision, offering relational guidance for question construction and process feedback via WCR without additional task-specific human annotations.

Abstract: Self-evolving search agents reduce reliance on human-written training questions by generating and solving their own search tasks. We build on Search Self-Play (SSP), a representative Proposer and Solver framework in which questions are generated and answered via multi-step search and reasoning. In practice, however, SSP faces two bottlenecks: the Proposer constructs questions from isolated answer entities without relational context, yielding many invalid or unverifiable questions in early self-play training, while the Solver receives only a binary outcome reward that discards useful signal from partially on-track search trajectories. We address both bottlenecks by reusing knowledge-graph paths as construction-derived intermediate supervision for both question construction and reward shaping. First, we ground question construction in LLM-guided knowledge-graph subgraphs, providing relational context for the Proposer. Second, we observe that constructing and solving a multi-hop question can involve overlapping intermediate entities: the factual bridges used to formulate the question may provide approximate waypoints for answering it. Exploiting this overlap, we introduce Waypoint Coverage Reward (WCR), which grants graded partial credit to incorrect Solver trajectories according to their coverage of entities on the construction path, while preserving full reward for correct answers. Across seven QA benchmarks and nine model configurations, our approach improves the average score over standard SSP in all configurations, including notable gains on multi-hop QA tasks. These results suggest that knowledge-graph paths can be reused as lightweight intermediate supervision, providing both relational guidance and process feedback without additional task-specific human annotations or manually labeled process steps.

</details>


### [404] [Resolving the bias-precision paradox with stochastic causal representation learning for personalized medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05706)
*Peisong Zhang,Manqiang Peng,Yuxuan Wu,Pawit Phadungsaksawasdi,Wesley Yeung,Ye Zhang,Trang Nguyen,Qiang Zhang,Nan Liu,Meng Wang,Kee Yuan Ngiam,Yih-Chung Tham,Ching-Yu Cheng,Tianfan Fu,Qingyu Chen,Rosemary Ke,Chang Li,Wenzhuo Yang,Zhenghao Lu,Chunyou Lai,Yu Zhang,Sheng Zhong,Hao Deng,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI
