TL;DR: Introduces sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD) to address the bias-precision paradox in causal representation learning for longitudinal, observational data. It replaces global adversarial balancing with subset-level, stochastic alignment to preserve informative heterogeneity while reducing confounding bias, enabling accurate, interpretable counterfactual predictions in ICU data and strong human–AI performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard confounding-bias reduction often suppresses clinically informative heterogeneity, degrading patient-specific predictions. The work frames this as a bias-precision paradox in causal representation learning and seeks to maintain heterogeneity without sacrificing bias reduction.

Method: Propose sMMD, a stochastic, subset-level maximum mean discrepancy alignment method that replaces global adversarial balancing. Integrate into a counterfactual outcome predictor with attribution-grounded interpretability, evaluated on two large ICU cohorts (n=27,783).

Result: sMMD improves accuracy under distribution shift (up to 11.5% error reduction) and increases recall in high-risk tasks. Mechanistic analyses show selective preservation of decisive clinical variables. In human–AI evaluation, the method outperforms clinicians-in-training and large language models, increasing clinician accuracy by 14.7% and reducing decision time.

Conclusion: sMMD offers a practical balance between reducing confounding bias and preserving clinically informative heterogeneity, enabling accurate, interpretable real-time decision support in critical care and robust performance under distribution shift.

Abstract: Estimating individualized treatment effects from longitudinal observational data is central to data-driven medicine, yet existing methods face a fundamental limitation: reducing confounding bias often suppresses clinically informative heterogeneity, degrading patient-specific predictions. Here, we identify this tension as a bias-precision paradox in causal representation learning and introduce sampling-based maximum mean discrepancy (sMMD), a stochastic alignment strategy that replaces global adversarial balancing with subset-level matching. We instantiate this approach in a framework for counterfactual outcome prediction with attribution-grounded interpretability. Across two large-scale ICU cohorts (n = 27,783), our framework improves accuracy under distribution shift, reducing error by up to 11.5% and substantially increasing recall in high-risk tasks. Mechanistic analyses show that sMMD selectively preserves clinically decisive variables. In human-AI evaluation, our method outperforms clinicians-in-training and large language models, and improves clinician accuracy by 14.7% while reducing decision time, enabling interpretable, real-time clinical decision support.

</details>


### [405] [Conceal, Reconstruct, Jailbreak: Exploiting the Reconstruction-Concealment Tradeoff in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05709)
*Md Farhamdur Reza,Richeng Jin,Tianfu Wu,Huaiyu Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reconstruction–concealment tradeoff governs intent-obfuscation attacks on multimodal LLMs. Character-removed variants plus concealment-aware, diverse variant construction with modality-aware prompts and keyword-related distractor images outperform baselines, revealing a model's reconstruction ability as a security vulnerability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and assess the vulnerability of multimodal LLMs to intent-obfuscation jailbreaks, quantify the tradeoffs in existing transformations, and explore more effective attack strategies (and, by implication, defense considerations).

Method: Reconstruction analysis of three black-box transformation methods; develop concealment-aware variant construction that greedily selects character-removed variants with low harmful-keyword alignment and high diversity; instantiate them via five modality-aware prompting strategies; introduce keyword-related distractor images; evaluate on closed-source and open-source MLLMs.

Result: Existing transformations struggle to balance concealment and recoverability; character-removed variants improve this balance; proposed strategies outperform strong baselines; demonstrated that a model's reconstruction ability can recover hidden harmful intent and produce unsafe responses.

Conclusion: Highlights an underexplored vulnerability where an MLLM’s own reconstruction ability can be exploited to reveal hidden intent and generate unsafe responses; emphasizes need for defenses that account for reconstruction risk in multimodal safety pipelines and prompt strategies.

Abstract: Intent-obfuscation-based jailbreak attacks on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) transform a harmful query into a concealed multimodal input to bypass safety mechanisms. We show that such attacks are governed by a \emph{reconstruction--concealment tradeoff}: the transformed input must hide harmful intent from safety filters while remaining recoverable enough for the victim model to reconstruct the original request. Through a reconstruction analysis of three representative black-box methods, we find that existing transformations struggle to balance this tradeoff, limiting their effectiveness. In contrast, we show that character-removed variants achieve a better balance. Building on this, we propose \emph{concealment-aware variant construction}, which greedily selects character-removed variants that are low in harmful-keyword alignment and mutually diverse, and instantiates them through five modality-aware prompting strategies. We further introduce \emph{keyword-related distractor images} that depict the harmful keyword in diverse contexts, providing more effective auxiliary visual context than generic distractor images. Experiments across closed-source and open-source MLLMs show the proposed strategies outperform strong baselines, revealing an underexplored vulnerability: a model's own reconstruction ability can be exploited to recover hidden harmful intent and produce unsafe responses.

</details>


### [406] [Decodable but Not Corrected by Fixed Residual-Stream Linear Steering: Evidence from Medical LLM Failure Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05715)
*Ming Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Linearly decodable failure signals in LLM hidden states (OT) are not easily exploitable for correction via fixed linear steering, though they support reliability estimation through abstention. OT shows strong linear decodability yet minimal impact from fixed steering across models/domains, suggesting representational entanglement with task computations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether linearly decodable failure signals in hidden representations can be leveraged to correct failures, by testing linear steering and evaluating the OT regime in medical QA and across architectures/domains.

Method: Quantify OT decodability (71.6% balanced accuracy, p<1e-16). Test five families of fixed linear steering (29 configurations, n=1,273). Assess cross-architecture (Qwen2.5-7B) and cross-domain (MMLU-STEM). Gather three lines of convergent evidence for entanglement (task-critical overlap 85–88%; detrimental effects of non-targeted steering; LEACE erasure harms accuracy) and compare with random erasures. Evaluate per-instance probe–steering correlation (r=-0.002, p=0.97). Assess selective abstention capability via held-out AUROC (0.610, p=0.009 vs baselines).

Result: Despite the OT direction being highly decodable, the five fixed linear steering configurations yield Delta ≈ 0 across models and domains. Representational analyses indicate substantial overlap with task-critical computation, and non-targeted steering degrades accuracy; LEACE erasure reduces accuracy while random erasures have negligible or positive effect. A per-instance probe–steering correlation is essentially null. However, the same probe enables selective abstention (AUROC 0.610), suggesting decodable failure signals can support reliability estimation rather than direct correction.

Conclusion: There is pronounced entanglement between OT failure signals and task representations. Fixed linear steering cannot exploit these signals for correction, but they can inform reliability estimation through abstention. Nonlinear or alternative intervention strategies may be required for corrective use of OT signals.

Abstract: Can linearly decodable failure signals in LLM hidden states be leveraged to correct those failures? We investigate this classification-correction gap via Overthinking (OT)--a stable behavioral regime (Jaccard >= 0.81, 94% inter-annotator agreement) in medical QA where models answer correctly under resampling yet fail in extended chain-of-thought. OT is linearly decodable at 71.6% balanced accuracy (p < 10^{-16}). Yet five families of fixed linear steering (29 configurations, n=1,273) all yield Delta ~= 0, with identical null results cross-architecture (Qwen2.5-7B) and cross-domain (MMLU-STEM). Three convergent lines of evidence suggest representational entanglement: the OT direction has 85-88% overlap with task-critical computation (specificity ratio <= 0.152); non-targeted shared-direction steering damages accuracy (-12.1pp); and LEACE concept erasure damages accuracy (-3.6pp, p=0.01), while 10 random erasures produce Delta=+0.3pp. The per-instance probe-steering correlation is r=-0.002 (p=0.97). Positively, the same probe enables selective abstention (held-out AUROC=0.610, exceeding all five uncertainty baselines, p=0.009): decodable failure structure supports post-generation reliability estimation even when the fixed linear steering family cannot exploit it for correction.

</details>


### [407] [Detecting Time Series Anomalies Like an Expert: A Multi-Agent LLM Framework with Specialized Analyzers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05725)
*Hyeongwon Kang,Jeongseob Kim,Jinwoo Park,Pilsung Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAGE is a multi-agent framework for univariate time-series anomaly detection, using four specialized Analyzers (point, structural, seasonal, pattern) plus an evidence-grounded Detector and a Supervisor to produce analyst-friendly reports. It uses synthetic in-context examples from normal data and shows strong performance across three benchmarks with ablations and human evaluation supporting reliability and usefulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of single general-purpose models that infer anomaly indices/intervals, which can limit controllability, interpretability, and reliability for complex anomaly patterns in time series.

Method: Decomposes anomaly analysis into four specialized Analyzers working with family-specific numerical tools and visualizations to generate evidence. An evidence-grounded Detector consolidates evidence into confidence-scored anomaly records with intervals and candidate types. A Supervisor converts structured records into analyst-facing diagnostic reports. Synthetic in-context examples are constructed from normal-reference segments without using real anomalies or anomaly-type labels.

Result: SAGE achieves the best average performance among strong ML/DL and language-model-based baselines across three benchmarks. Ablation studies and human evaluation indicate improved detection reliability and practical usefulness of outputs.

Conclusion: A modular, evidence-grounded, interpretable framework with synthetic in-context examples enhances controllability and reliability in time-series anomaly detection for univariate data, outperforming strong baselines.

Abstract: Recent studies have explored large language models for time-series anomaly detection, yet existing approaches often rely on a single general-purpose model to directly infer anomaly indices or intervals, limiting controllability, interpretability, and reliability for complex anomaly patterns. We propose SAGE (Specialized Analyzer Group for Expert-like Detection), a multi-agent framework for structured anomaly diagnosis in univariate time series. It decomposes anomaly analysis into four specialized Analyzers for point, structural, seasonal, and pattern anomalies. Each Analyzer applies family-specific numerical tools and diagnostic visualizations to generate evidence, while an evidence-grounded Detector consolidates the evidence into confidence-scored anomaly records with intervals and candidate types. A Supervisor then converts these structured records into analyst-facing diagnostic reports. SAGE further constructs synthetic in-context examples from normal-reference training segments, without using real anomalous segments or anomaly-type labels as in-context examples. Across three benchmarks, SAGE achieves the best average performance among strong ML/DL and language-model-based baselines. Ablation studies and human evaluation further show that the proposed framework improves detection reliability and the practical usefulness of diagnostic outputs.

</details>


### [408] [SkillRet: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Skill Retrieval in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05726)
*Hongcheol Cho,Ryangkyung Kang,Youngeun Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillRet introduces a large-scale skill retrieval benchmark for LLM agents, with 17,810 skills, a two-level taxonomy, 63,259 training samples, and 4,997 evaluation queries, enabling benchmarking and retrieval-focused training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents rely on large libraries of reusable skills, selecting the right skill under tight context and latency constraints is challenging. There is a lack of benchmarks and understanding of retrieval behavior on realistic, large skill libraries.

Method: SkillRet provides a two-level taxonomy (6 categories, 18 sub-categories) and 17,810 public agent skills with structured semantic tags. It offers 63,259 training samples and 4,997 evaluation queries with disjoint skill pools. The study benchmarks multiple retrievers and demonstrates that task-specific fine-tuning significantly improves performance.

Result: Off-the-shelf retrievers underperform on realistic large-scale libraries; prior retrieval models leave substantial headroom. Fine-tuning on SkillRet yields substantial gains, with NDCG@10 improving by 13.1 points over the strongest prior retriever and 16.9 points over the strongest off-the-shelf retriever, suggesting models leverage small skill-relevant signals within long, noisy queries.

Conclusion: SkillRet serves as a strong benchmark and foundation for future research on retrieval in large-scale agent systems, highlighting that retrieval is not yet solved and that task-specific fine-tuning can substantially boost performance.

Abstract: As LLM agents are increasingly deployed with large libraries of reusable skills, selecting the right skill for a user request has become a critical systems challenge. In small libraries, users may invoke skills explicitly by name, but this assumption breaks down as skill ecosystems grow under tight context and latency budgets. Despite its practical importance, skill retrieval remains underexplored, with limited benchmarks and little understanding of retrieval behavior on realistic skill libraries. To address this gap, we introduce SkillRet, a large-scale benchmark for skill retrieval in LLM agents. SkillRet contains 17,810 public agent skills, organized with structured semantic tags and a two-level taxonomy spanning 6 major categories and 18 sub-categories. It provides 63,259 training samples and 4,997 evaluation queries with disjoint skill pools, enabling both benchmarking and retrieval-oriented training. Across a diverse set of retrievers, we find that skill retrieval remains far from solved: off-the-shelf models struggle on realistic large-scale skill libraries, and prior skill-retrieval models still leave substantial headroom. Task-specific fine-tuning on SkillRet substantially improves performance, improving NDCG@10 by +13.1 points over the strongest prior retriever and by +16.9 points over the strongest off-the-shelf retriever. Our analysis further suggests that these gains arise because fine-tuned models better focus on the small skill-relevant signals within long and noisy queries. These results establish SkillRet as a strong benchmark and foundation for future research on retrieval in large-scale agent systems.

</details>


### [409] [Knee Osteoarthritis Severity Grading Using Optimized Deep Learning and LLM-Driven Intelligent AI on Computationally Limited Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05731)
*Dayam Nadeem,Neha,Safdar Mustafa,Adnan Alvi,Mohd Hussain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An automated KOA severity grader uses ResNet-18 CNN with transfer learning on public knee X-ray data, deployed on-device via TensorFlow Lite to classify Kellgren-Lawrence grades (KL 0–4); an LLM (Gemini-2.0-flash) provides interpretive outputs. Reported test accuracy ~94.5% with on-device operation and offline capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address subjectivity and inter-observer variability in KOA evaluation and enable timely, accessible diagnosis through on-device AI, reducing dependence on continuous connectivity.

Method: CNN-based model (ResNet-18) trained with transfer learning on a public knee dataset; five-class KL grading. Optimized and converted to TensorFlow Lite for lightweight on-device inference. An auxiliary LLM interface generates interpretable findings (symptoms, risk factors, preventive measures) without influencing classification.

Result: Test accuracy of 94.48% with stable convergence. Model converted to TensorFlow Lite enables operation on resource-constrained devices without internet. LLM provides structured interpretive outputs but does not affect the classifier.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of an on-device, interpretable decision-support tool for early KOA screening, combining high-performance CNN-based grading with offline deployment and user-facing interpretability.

Abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (KOA) is among the musculoskeletal disorders that considerably restrict joint mobility, cause severe chronic pain and impact negatively on quality life. It is one of the persistent health issues worldwide. Generally, subjectivity and inter-observer variability undermine conventional practices and evaluation process that are adopted to address such health issues. Hence precise and timely diagnosis would be one of the effective ways for the assessment of its severity. This paper proposes an automated diagnostic approach for severity grading of KOA by blending a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) with a device-based inference platform powered by TensorFlow Lite. It proposes a model based on the ResNet-18 convolutional neural network. The designed model is trained on publicly available database. Through a transfer learning approach obtained knee images are first classified into five Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) grades. Further the developed model is optimised. During the training of the model test accuracy of 94.48% with stable convergence has been achieved. Subsequently the optimised model transformed into a lightweight TensorFlow Lite format, facilitating seamless deployment on resource-constrained devices. The designed model is capable enough to operate in the environment having no continuous internet connectivity. Also, an auxiliary Large Language Model (Gemini-2.0-flash) is applied to generate structured interpretive findings like potential symptoms, risk factors, and preventive majors etc. The LLM component functions as interface without influencing the classification process. The proposed model articulates the feasibility of an on-device, interpretable decision-support tools for early diagnosis and improve accessibility to Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted knee screening tool.

</details>


### [410] [SDFlow: Similarity-Driven Flow Matching for Time Series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05736)
*Wei Li,Shibo Feng,Pengcheng Wu,Min Wu,Peilin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SDFlow introduces a non-autoregressive flow-matching approach in frozen VQ latent space to achieve exposure-bias–free, fast long-horizon sequence generation with state-of-the-art quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exposure bias in autoregressive VQ token modeling causes error accumulation and quality degradation in long-horizon generation; a non-autoregressive, latent-space method is needed for parallel generation and efficiency.

Method: Replace step-wise token prediction with a global transport map (flow matching); mitigates high-dimensional VQ tokens via low-rank manifold decomposition with a learned anchor prior over the latent space; integrate discrete supervision into continuous transport via a variational flow-matching formulation using a categorical posterior over codebook indices.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Discriminative Score and substantially reducing Context-FID, especially for long sequences; provides significant inference speedups over autoregressive baselines.

Conclusion: SDFlow delivers high fidelity and computational efficiency for long-sequence generation by combining global transport, latent-manifold modeling, and discrete-continuous supervision.

Abstract: Vector quantization (VQ) with autoregressive (AR) token modeling is a widely adopted and highly competitive paradigm for time-series generation. However, such models are fundamentally limited by exposure bias: during inference, errors can accumulate across sequential predictions, leading to pronounced quality degradation in long-horizon generation. To address this, we propose SDFlow ($\textbf{S}$imilarity-$\textbf{D}$riven $\textbf{Flow}$ Matching), a non-autoregressive framework that operates entirely in the frozen VQ latent space and enables parallel sequence generation via flow matching. We tackle three key challenges in making this transition: (1) eliminating exposure bias by replacing step-wise token prediction with a global transport map; (2) mitigating the high-dimensionality of VQ token spaces via a low-rank manifold decomposition with a learned anchor prior over the latent manifold; and (3) incorporating discrete supervision into continuous transport dynamics by introducing a categorical posterior over codebook indices within a variational flow-matching formulation. Extensive experiments show that SDFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Discriminative Score and substantially reducing Context-FID, particularly for challenging long-sequence generation. Moreover, SDFlow provides significant inference speedups over autoregressive baselines, offering both high fidelity and computational efficiency. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SDFlow-D6F3/

</details>


### [411] [ReFlect: An Effective Harness System for Complex Long-Horizon LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05737)
*Fan Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReFlect adds a deterministic inference-time error-detection and recovery wrapper around LLMs, yielding systematic gains over Direct CoT on long-horizon reasoning across multiple models and domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reasoning paradigms (chain-of-thought, ReAct, post-hoc self-critique) assume manageable error rates, but errors accumulate in long-horizon tasks. A standalone, detect-and-recover mechanism could robustly identify and recover from its own failures.

Method: A model-agnostic, training-free wrapper that installs standalone error-detection and recovery logic around the LLM at inference time. Evaluated across six reasoning domains and six models, comparing against Direct CoT and prompt-level self-critique. Measures include task success rates, per-model gains, and SWE-bench patch-structural quality.

Result: ReFlect yields task success rates from 41% (gpt-4o-mini) to 56% (Claude Sonnet 4.5). Per-model gains over Direct CoT range +7 to +29 percentage points. SWE-bench patch-structural quality rises from 0% (Direct CoT) to 82–87% depending on model (e.g., 82% for Qwen2.5-72B, 87% for GPT-4o). Harness gains are inversely related to baseline success (slope -1.69, r = -0.76). For some models (Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen2.5-72B), adding structured reasoning state yields only 15.0–18.7% pair-mean due to inability to populate required state. Overall, ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

Conclusion: A practical, model-agnostic approach to error detection and recovery in LLM reasoning that improves long-horizon task success without training. Gains are most pronounced when baseline Direct CoT performance is low, but not uniformly beneficial for all model scales; the framework impacts structured evaluation metrics (SWE) and demonstrates the feasibility of inference-time recoverability in reasoning pipelines.

Abstract: Current reasoning paradigms for LLMs include chain-of-thought, ReAct, and post-hoc self-critique. These paradigms rely on two assumptions that fail on long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. As a result, errors accumulate silently across reasoning steps, leaving an open question: can a reasoning system effectively detect and recover from its own failures? We present ReFlect, a \emph{harness} system for LLM reasoning that creates standalone error detection and recovery logic as a deterministic wrapper around the model. Controlled experiments across 6 reasoning domains show that prompt-level self-critique produces formulaic templates that flag no issues in 90 of 100 audited reflection blocks, and the investigated LLMs wrongly accept a wrong answer in at least 76\% of cases. Our ReFlect harness achieves task success rates ranging from 41\% on gpt-4o-mini to 56\% on Claude Sonnet 4.5 across six models spanning small and frontier scale, with per-model gains over Direct CoT ranging from +7 pp on Qwen2.5-72B to +29 pp on Claude Sonnet 4.5, and additionally raises SWE-bench patch-structural quality from 0\% (Direct CoT) to between 82\% (Qwen2.5-72B) and 87\% (GPT-4o). Notably, the harness gain is inversely proportional to the model's Direct CoT task success rate (the fitted slope is -1.69 with r=-0.76): each pp lost in baseline success rate is mechanically recovered by 1.69 pp of harness gain. We spot that adding structured reasoning state and operators yields only 15.0--18.7\% pair-mean on Llama-3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B because models at this scale cannot reliably populate the state its operators require. ReFlect is model-agnostic, training-free, and operates entirely at inference time.

</details>


### [412] [HyperLens: Quantifying Cognitive Effort in LLMs with Fine-grained Confidence Trajectory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05741)
*Chengda Lu,Xiaoyu Fan,Wei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HyperLens reveals that deeper transformer layers magnify small changes in confidence to produce high-resolution confidence trajectories; a cognitive effort metric differentiates simple vs. complex tasks, with SFT potentially reducing cognitive effort and degrading in-domain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand inference dynamics of LLMs beyond coarse metrics by mapping fine-grained confidence trajectories and quantifying cognitive effort.

Method: Introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe that traces layer-wise confidence trajectories across models and datasets, derives a quantitative cognitive effort metric, and diagnoses the impact of supervised fine-tuning.

Result: HyperLens uncovers consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks; establishes a robust cognitive effort metric; finds that complex tasks require higher cognitive effort; SFT tends to reduce cognitive effort and can degrade in-domain performance.

Conclusion: Confidence dynamics reveal a fundamental principle: complex tasks require higher cognitive effort. HyperLens provides a mechanistic diagnostic tool for inference dynamics and explains how SFT may harm performance on in-domain tasks.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks, their inference dynamics remain poorly understood because of the limited resolution of existing analysis tools. In this work, we identify an intrinsic magnification mechanism in transformer architectures: deeper layers inherently magnify the small changes of layer-wise confidence, providing a fine-grained confidence trajectory. Building on this insight, we introduce HyperLens, a high-resolution probe designed to trace confidence trajectories and quantify the cognitive effort during inference. Across LLMs and datasets, HyperLens reveals a consistent divergence in confidence trajectories that separates complex from simple tasks. We abstract this pattern into a quantitative cognitive effort metric. Our analysis reveals a fundamental principle: complex tasks consistently require higher cognitive effort. Finally, we provide a mechanistic diagnosis of a common side effect of standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): it can reduce cognitive effort and consequently degrade performance on in-domain tasks.

</details>


### [413] [Best Arm Identification in Generalized Linear Bandits via Hybrid Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05745)
*Qirun Zeng,Xuchuang Wang,Jiayi Shen,Xutong Liu,Fang Kong,Jinhang Zuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hybrid GLM bandits with fixed-confidence best-arm identification under mixed absolute and relative feedback; introduces likelihood-ratio confidence sequences and a Track-and-Stop design, plus a cost-aware extension and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify heterogeneous generalized linear observations (absolute reward and dueling feedback) under a common sequential design to improve sample efficiency in fixed-confidence best-arm identification.

Method: Develop a likelihood-ratio–based confidence sequence to accommodate heterogeneous GLM observations, yielding an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over the joint action space of arms and pairs. Extend to a cost-aware setting with heterogeneous acquisition costs. Establish δ-correctness and high-probability stopping-time bounds. Validate via experiments.

Result: The approach achieves provable δ-correctness and nontrivial high-probability bounds on stopping time; empirical results show improved sample efficiency over baselines.

Conclusion: The framework effectively integrates heterogeneous feedback in GLMs for efficient fixed-confidence best-arm identification, with a cost-aware extension and strong empirical support; it opens pathways for practical sequential design under mixed feedback modalities.

Abstract: We study fixed-confidence best arm identification in generalized linear bandits under a hybrid feedback model: at each round, the learner may query either (i) absolute reward feedback from a single arm or (ii) relative (dueling) feedback from an arm pair, both governed by generalized linear models. We introduce a likelihood-ratio--based confidence sequence that unifies heterogeneous generalized linear observations and yields an explicit ellipsoidal confidence set under a self-concordance assumption. Building on this confidence set, we propose a hybrid Track-and-Stop algorithm that adaptively allocates queries by tracking a minimax-optimal design over a joint action space of arms and pairs. We establish $δ$-correctness and provide high-probability upper bounds on the stopping time. We further extend the framework to a cost-aware setting that accounts for heterogeneous acquisition costs across feedback modalities. Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms significantly improve sample efficiency over baseline methods.

</details>


### [414] [Evaluating Explainability in Safety-Critical ATR Systems: Limitations of Post-Hoc Methods and Paths Toward Robust XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05748)
*Vanessa Buhrmester,David Muench,Dimitri Bulatov,Michael Arens*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Structured evaluation framework for XAI in safety-critical ATR; finds current post-hoc explanations are often unreliable for assurance and calls for robust, causally grounded, physically informed explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XAI is needed in safety-critical ATR to support validation, reliability, and verification beyond high predictive performance.

Method: Literature-based identification of XAI paradigms (saliency-based, attention-based, surrogate, and detection-aware extensions); formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment; develop a taxonomy; evaluate interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipulation, and validation/suitability; analyze failure modes.

Result: Systematic limitations identified for post-hoc XAI: spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust due to convincing visuals; current XAI methods may be insufficient for safety-critical ATR deployment.

Conclusion: Need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explainability; implications for ATR; call for approaches that support reliable decision-making and system-level assurance.

Abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is increasingly rec ognized as essential for deploying machine learning systems in safety critical environments. In Automatic Target Recognition (ATR), where models operate on image, video, radar, and multisensor data, high pre dictive performance alone is insufficient. Model decisions must also be interpretable, reliable, and suitable for validation. This paper presents a structured evaluation of explainability methods in the context of safety-critical ATR systems: We identify major XAI paradigms, including saliency-based, attention-based, and surrogate ap proaches, as well as recent detection-aware extensions. Based on this, we formalize explainability as an assurance-oriented assessment problem, introduce a taxonomy, and assess these methods with respect to four key dimensions: interpretability, robustness, vulnerability to manipula tion, and suitability for validation and verification. The analysis identifies systematic limitations of current post-hoc explanation methods. In par ticular, we derive critical failure modes such as spurious explanations, instability under perturbations, and overtrust induced by visually con vincing outputs. These findings indicate that widely used XAI techniques may be insufficient for safety-critical deployment. Finally, we discuss implications for ATR systems and outline directions toward more robust, causally grounded, and physically informed explain ability methods. Our results emphasize the need to move beyond visually plausible explanations toward approaches that support reliable decision making and system-level assurance.

</details>


### [415] [Confidence is the key: how conformal prediction enhances the generative design of permeable peptides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05770)
*Laura van Weesep,Sunay Chankeshwara,Leonardo De Maria,Florian David,Ola Engkvist,Gökçe Geylan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A reinforcement-learning–driven generative framework designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor, with conformal prediction to quantify uncertainty and constrain exploration within the predictor's applicability domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited domain applicability of predictive models in RL-guided design, particularly for cyclic peptides, by integrating uncertainty quantification to avoid high-reward but unreliable designs and improve efficiency in discovering permeable cyclic peptides.

Method: Combine RL-based generation (e.g., REINVENT/PepINVENT) with a permeability predictor augmented by conformal prediction (CP). CP calibrates predictive uncertainty at a user-defined confidence level, guiding the reward to CP-consistent designs and discouraging exploration outside the applicability domain.

Result: CP-informed reward improves reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization and reduces exploration into high-uncertainty chemical space; demonstrates, for the first time, an integration of conformal prediction with generative RL in this domain.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware RL with CP can bridge predictive uncertainty and exploration, yielding more reliable cyclic peptide designs with improved efficiency, and offering a general framework for uncertainty-aware generative design beyond small molecules.

Abstract: Generative models coupled with reinforcement learning (RL), such as REINVENT and PepINVENT, have emerged as a powerful framework for de novo molecular design. During the ideation process these generative frameworks utilize various predictive models as part of the optimization objectives. However, the utility of the predictive models can be limited by their domain of applicability. When RL is used to explore the chemical space with predictive models, it can suggest molecules that lie outside the predictor's domain of applicability. As a result, the predictions may become less reliable, potentially steering designs into high reward but also high uncertainty chemical spaces. This is particularly pronounced for cyclic peptides which show therapeutic promise due to their modifiability and large interaction surfaces but are understudied compared to small molecules. While passive membrane permeation in cyclic peptides has attracted interest, identifying optimal permeable designs remains challenging yet crucial for targeting intracellular sites. We present an RL-guided generative framework that designs permeable cyclic peptides using an uncertainty-aware permeability predictor as the scoring component. To address predictive uncertainty, especially impacted by novel chemistry, we integrate conformal prediction (CP) as our uncertainty quantification method. CP assesses designs based on the calibrated model under a user-defined confidence level. We demonstrate that rewarding generated peptides with CP-informed predictions improves both reliability and efficiency of peptide optimization process. This also discourages exploration outside the predictor's applicability domain. This approach bridges the gap between predictive uncertainty and RL-guided exploration, showing how generative modelling and conformal prediction can be combined for the first time.

</details>


### [416] [CircuitFormer: A Circuit Language Model for Analog Topology Design from Natural Language Prompt](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05773)
*Md Touhidul Islam,Sujan Kumar Saha,Farimah Farahmandi,Mark Tehranipoor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists with NL descriptions (31,341 pairs); introduces Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) with constant vocabulary, reducing sequence length by 57% and improving compression by 2.3x over BPE; trains CircuitFormer (511M) achieving 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across analog circuit classes, outperforming open-source LLMs with 10% and 14% gains and using 240x fewer parameters; dataset public.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap in analog design AI by addressing (a) scarcity of paired NL descriptions and circuit netlists, and (b) inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers for circuit graphs.

Method: 1) Curate a large dataset of 31,341 annotated analog circuit netlist–NL description pairs. 2) Develop Circuit Tokenizer (CKT) to encode netlist connectivity by mining frequent subcircuits, achieving constant vocabulary growth O(1) rather than O(n_max). 3) Train a circuit-specific encoder-decoder Transformer, CircuitFormer, with 511M parameters, on the dataset.

Result: CKT outperforms BPE in circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving 2.3x compression with a fixed vocabulary of 512. CircuitFormer achieves 100% syntactic correctness and 83% functional success across major analog circuit categories, surpassing state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% (syntactic) and 14% (functional) while using 240x fewer parameters.

Conclusion: The proposed CKT tokenization enables scalable, topology-aware encoding of circuit graphs, enabling effective analog circuit design with transformer models. The large annotated dataset demonstrates viability and provides a resource for future research; the dataset is publicly available.

Abstract: Automating analog circuit design remains a longstanding challenge in Electronic Design Automation (EDA). While Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized software code generation, their application to analog hardware design is hindered by two critical limitations: (i) the scarcity of analog design datasets containing natural language description of a design and its corresponding netlist, and (ii) the inefficiency of general-purpose tokenizers (e.g., Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)) in capturing the inherent graph structure of circuits. To bridge this gap, first, we curate the largest annotated dataset of analog circuit netlists to date, comprising 31,341 netlist-natural language description pairs across all major circuit classes. Furthermore, we propose Circuit Tokenizer (CKT), a novel circuit graph tokenizer designed to encode netlist connectivity by explicitly mining frequent subcircuits. In terms of scalability, CKT overcomes the bottleneck of prior circuit graph serialization methods where vocabulary size scales linearly with maximum number of components in the dataset, n_max, (O(n_max)); instead, CKT decouples vocabulary growth from circuit complexity, achieving a constant O(1) complexity. Empirically, CKT outperforms standard BPE on circuit topology representation, reducing sequence length by 57% and achieving a 2.3x superior compression ratio using a compact, fixed vocabulary of size 512. Leveraging this optimized tokenization, we train a circuit-specific language model, CircuitFormer, a 511M parameter encoder-decoder transformer. Our model achieves 100% syntactic correctness and an 83% functional success rate across all major analog circuit categories, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source LLMs by 10% and 14%, respectively, while requiring 240x fewer parameters. The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/touhid314/cktformer-dataset.

</details>


### [417] [HEDP: A Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt-based Framework for Domain Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05776)
*Yu Feng,Zhen Tian,Haoran Luo,Xie Yu,Diancheng Cheng,Haoyue Zheng,Shuai Lyu,Ping Zong,Lianyuan Li,Xin Ge,Yifan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP) for domain-incremental learning uses energy-based regularization and an energy-distance fusion mechanism to improve domain representation separability and domain selection, achieving better generalization to unseen domains; shows 2.57% accuracy gain on CORe50 and reduces catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain-incremental learning suffers from performance degradation due to domain shifts and catastrophic forgetting. There is a need for representations that are more separable across domains and for robust domain selection to maintain performance on unseen domains.

Method: Introduce Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt (HEDP) with an energy regularization loss to enhance domain-representation separability and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues for improved domain selection and generalization.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple benchmarks including CORe50 shows improved performance on unseen domains, with a 2.57 percentage point accuracy gain, indicating reduced forgetting and better open-world adaptability.

Conclusion: HEDP is effective for domain-incremental learning, leveraging energy-distance fusion to enhance domain generalization and mitigation of catastrophic forgetting; code is available online.

Abstract: Domain Incremental Learning is a critical scenario that requires models to continuously adapt to new data domains without retraining. However, domain shifts often cause severe performance degradation. To address this, we propose Hybrid Energy-Distance Prompt, a domain-incremental framework inspired by Helmholtz free energy. HEDP introduces an energy regularization loss to enhance the separability of domain representations and a hybrid energy-distance weighted mechanism that fuses energy-based and distance-based cues to improve domain selection and generalization. Experiments on multiple benchmarks, including CORe50, show that HEDP achieves superior performance on unseen domains with a 2.57\% accuracy gain, effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and enhancing open-world adaptability. Our code is \href{https://github.com/dannis97500/HEDP/}{available here}.

</details>


### [418] [Von Neumann Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05780)
*Shekhar S. Chandra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) are a diffusion-inspired, cellular-topology neural architecture with learnable, specialized neurons that self-design architectures based on input/output topology. Framed as an extension of neural operators via Green's functions, VNNs reside in the broader Cellular Machines framework and are claimed to be computationally universal. Empirically, VNN-based MLPs show parameter-efficient, superior performance on basic tasks, and can learn new task types, suggesting hardware-like Von Neumann concepts can be extended to cellular substrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To realize self-organizing neural systems whose architectures are dictated by the arrangement of inputs and outputs, drawing on von Neumann’s diffusion-based computation, and to connect cellular computation with neural operator theory and universal computation.

Method: Develop VNNs within a neural-operator framework, modeling diffusion on a cellular topology and introducing learnable Von Neumann neurons with specialized roles. Construct VNNs and prove they fit within Cellular Machines as a universal computational class. Compare VNN-based multilayer perceptrons to conventional deep nets on basic tasks to assess efficiency and adaptability.

Result: VNN-based MLPs outperform their equivalent deep learning variants on basic tasks while using fewer parameters. VNNs demonstrate the ability to learn new task types and produce architectures determined by input/output layout, effectively extending the Von Neumann hardware concept to cellular substrates.

Conclusion: VNNs offer a promising avenue for diffusion-based, hardware-inspired neural computation that is both universally powerful (within Cellular Machines) and regime-efficient, with practical gains in parameter efficiency and task adaptability, and they open opportunities for further integration of von Neumann-style hardware principles into cellular neural architectures.

Abstract: In the mid-twentieth century, mathematician and polymath John von Neumann created a computational system on an array of cells as a simple model of the human brain, where each cell had one of a finite set of roles or states that he predicted would be modelled by a diffusion process. In this work, we show that such a system, when developed in a modern deep learning setting, enables the construction of an artificial neuron having specialized roles that can be learnt. We refer to this neuron as the Von Neumann neuron, and the resulting neural network from such neurons result in a self-engineered design whose architecture is only dependent on the structure and locations of its inputs and outputs on this cellular array. The mathematical framework for these Von Neumann Networks (VNNs) is also constructed and shows that they are based on the extension of neural operators and the learning of Green's functions with convolutions on a cellular topology having a diffusion signature. We also prove that these VNNs are part of a more general computational system called Cellular Machines that are computationally universal. Initial experiments show that VNN based multi-layered perceptrons outperform their equivalent deep learning variant on basic tasks, while being more parameter efficient and are capable of learning new types of tasks. This includes the ability to solve for and construct an extension of the Von Neumann (hardware) architecture common to all modern computers to cells and suggests new opportunities that could be explored.

</details>


### [419] [Sheet as Token: A Graph-Enhanced Representation for Multi-Sheet Spreadsheet Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05811)
*Yiming Lei,Yiqi Wang,Yujia Zhang,Bo Guan,Depei Zhu,Chunhui Wang,Zhuonan Hao,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Sheet as Token proposes to treat each worksheet as a single semantic unit using sheet-level tokens, enabling graph-based cross-sheet retrieval for multi-sheet spreadsheets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-sheet spreadsheets with heterogeneous schemas and implicit inter-sheet relationships make row/column–level chunking insufficient and brittle for global understanding; a unified sheet-level representation could improve scalability and reasoning.

Method: Extract schema-aware records from sheet names, headers, representative values, and layout features; encode each worksheet into a dense token. A Graph Retriever builds a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, then a multi-stage graph transformer aggregates information to retrieve supporting sheet sets.

Result: Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet corpus indicate that sheet-level tokenization yields stable representations and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval compared with a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation.

Conclusion: Sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding, and graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning can leverage sheet tokens to enable effective retrieval across heterogeneous spreadsheets.

Abstract: Workbook-scale spreadsheet understanding is increasingly important for language-model-based data analysis agents, but remains challenging because relevant information is often distributed across multiple sheets with heterogeneous schemas, layouts, and implicit relationships. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches typically decompose spreadsheets into rows, columns, or blocks to improve scalability; however, such chunk-centric representations can fragment worksheets into isolated text spans and weaken global sheet-level semantics. We propose Sheet as Token, a graph-enhanced framework that treats each worksheet as a unified semantic unit for multi-sheet spreadsheet retrieval. Our method extracts schema-aware records from sheet names, column headers, representative values, and layout features, and encodes each worksheet into a compact dense token. Given a natural-language query, a Graph Retriever constructs a query-specific candidate graph over sheet tokens using semantic, query-conditioned, schema-consistency, and shape-compatibility relations, and composes these channels through a multi-stage graph transformer to retrieve supporting sheet sets. Experiments on a constructed multi-sheet spreadsheet corpus show that sheet-level tokenization learns stable representations, and that graph-enhanced cross-sheet reasoning improves listwise retrieval over a shallow graph baseline with limited additional graph-side computation. These results suggest that sheet-level tokenization is a promising abstraction for scalable multi-sheet spreadsheet understanding.

</details>


### [420] [Long-Horizon Q-Learning: Accurate Value Learning via n-Step Inequalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05812)
*Armaan A. Abraham,Lucy Xiaoyang Shi,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Long-horizon Q-learning (LQL) introduces a hinge-loss stabilization mechanism based on optimality-tightening bounds to curb error propagation in Q-learning; it uses existing TD-network outputs, requires no extra networks, and improves over 1-step and n-step TD on online and offline-to-online benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bootstrapping in Q-learning leads to compounding estimation errors across long horizons; a principled way to bound future value and enforce that early optimal actions need not be worse than observed actions for several steps.

Method: Translate the optimality-tightening inequality into hinge loss penalties that constrain Q-values along observed action sequences; compute penalties using network outputs already used for TD error; no auxiliary networks or extra forward passes; integrate with existing off-policy, value-based methods.

Result: Empirical results show LQL outperforms 1-step TD and n-step TD at similar runtime across multiple online and offline-to-online benchmarks when combined with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: LQL provides a practical, efficient stabilization mechanism for long-horizon Q-learning by enforcing favorable bounds on early actions, mitigating error propagation without increasing computational overhead.

Abstract: Off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning methods such as Q-learning are appealing because they can learn from arbitrary experience, including data collected by older policies or other agents. In practice, however, bootstrapping makes long-horizon learning brittle: estimation errors at later states propagate backward through temporal-difference (TD) updates and can compound over time. We propose long-horizon Q-learning (LQL), which introduces a principled backstop against compounding error when learning the optimal action-value function. LQL builds on a prior optimality tightening observation: any realized action sequence lower-bounds what the optimal policy can achieve in expectation, so acting optimally earlier should not be worse than following the observed actions for several steps before switching to optimal behavior. Our contribution is to turn this inequality into a practical stabilization mechanism for Q-learning by using a hinge loss to penalize violations of these bounds. Importantly, LQL computes these penalties using network outputs already produced for the TD error, requiring no auxiliary networks and no additional forward passes relative to Q-learning. When combined with multiple state-of-the-art methods on a range of online and offline-to-online benchmarks, LQL consistently outperforms both 1-step TD and n-step TD learning at similar runtime.

</details>


### [421] [AGPO: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization for Verifiable Reasoning and Search Ads Relevance at JD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05826)
*Yang Xu,Kun Yao,Yiming Deng,Zheng Fang,Kai Ming Ting,Ming Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AGPO introduces a negative-dominant RL objective plus a group-advantage positive update to counter boundary shrinkage in RLVR for LLM reasoning. It preserves base-model exploration while emphasizing rare correct paths, achieving state-of-the-art results on mathematical benchmarks and industrial data annotation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR often improves sampling efficiency but narrows the reasoning distribution, reducing exploration compared to base models. The aim is to maintain exploration while suppressing incorrect paths and to better emphasize rare correct reasoning paths.

Method: Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) combines a negative-dominant reinforcement signal to suppress incorrect reasoning paths with a group-advantage mechanism that scales positive updates by intra-group variance, amplifying updates for rare correct paths while dampening trivial ones.

Result: AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on five mathematical benchmarks and improves pass@k at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO enhances data annotation quality, yielding substantial gains for downstream student models.

Conclusion: AGPO mitigates boundary shrinkage observed in RLVR by maintaining exploration and focusing updates on informative, rare correct paths, yielding both benchmark-level improvements and practical industrial benefits.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated notable success in enhancing the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs). However, recent studies reveal that while current RLVR methods improve sampling efficiency towards correct paths, they do not elicit fundamentally new reasoning patterns. Instead, the reasoning capability boundary of trained models often narrows compared to their base models, with base models achieving higher coverage at large sample sizes. In this work, we propose Asymmetric Group Policy Optimization (AGPO) to counteract this boundary shrinkage. AGPO adopts a negative-dominant reinforcement strategy to suppress incorrect reasoning paths, maintaining the base model's exploration capacity. For positive reinforcement, AGPO adopts a group advantage mechanism, which scales positive updates based on intra-group variance, allowing the model to focus on rare correct paths while suppressing updates from trivial paths. Our experiments on five mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that AGPO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while consistently improving pass@$k$ performance at scale. In a large-scale industrial application for search ads relevance optimization, AGPO effectively enhances the quality of the data annotation, leading to substantial performance gains in downstream student models.

</details>


### [422] [On the Role of Language Representations in Auto-Bidding: Findings and Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05833)
*Guanyu Zhu,Jining Luan,Hanwen Du,Xinyu Fang,Sibo Xu,Ersheng Ni,Hongji Li,Jincheng Fang,Ronghao Chen,Huacan Wang,Xuanqi Lan,Yongxin Ni,Yiqi Sun,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemBid injects LLM-derived semantics as tokens into offline bidding trajectories to control auto-bidding, achieving better controllability, generalization, and constraint satisfaction without sacrificing numerical precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auto-bidding must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints. Traditional compact state representations struggle to encode high-level intent and evolving feedback. LLMs offer semantic cues but naive integration can harm numerical precision and performance.

Method: Introduce three semantic inputs—Task, History, Strategy—as tokens. Combine them with numerical trajectory tokens and process with self-attention to fuse semantics and numerics. Evaluate in offline bidding scenarios across budget regimes, comparing to offline RL and generative sequence baselines.

Result: SemBid outperforms competitive baselines across diverse scenarios, delivering more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Semantic embeddings improve controllability and generalization when integrated at the token level rather than by simple concatenation.

Conclusion: Careful semantic–numeric integration via token-level LLM semantics enhances auto-bidding performance and robustness. Naive concatenation is insufficient; SemBid demonstrates the value of embedding high-level semantics directly into bidding trajectories.

Abstract: Auto-bidding is a crucial task in real-time advertising markets, where policies must optimize long-horizon value under delivery constraints (e.g., budget and CPA). Existing methods for auto-bidding rely on compact numerical state representations: while they can implicitly capture delivery dynamics, they offer limited support for explicitly representing and controlling high-level intent, evolving feedback, and operator-style strategic guidance in real campaigns. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a powerful method for encoding semantic information, it remains unclear when LLMs help and how to integrate them without sacrificing numerical precision. Through systematic preliminary studies, we find that (1) LLM embeddings contain bidding-relevant cues yet cannot replace numerical features, and (2) gains emerge only with careful semantic--numeric integration rather than naive concatenation. Motivated by these findings, we propose \textit{SemBid}, a novel auto-bidding framework that injects LLM-encoded semantics into offline bidding trajectories at the token level. SemBid introduces three semantic inputs: \textit{Task}, \textit{History}, and \textit{Strategy}. It injects these semantics as tokens alongside numerical trajectory tokens and uses self-attention to integrate them, improving controllability and generalization across objectives. Across diverse scenarios and budget regimes, SemBid outperforms competitive baselines from offline RL and generative sequence modeling, with more consistent gains in overall performance, constraint satisfaction, and robustness. Our code is available at: \href{https://github.com/AlanYu04/SemBid-KDD2026}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

</details>


### [423] [Taklif.AI: LLM-Powered Platform for Interest-Based Personalized College Assignments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05842)
*Zaki Kurdya,Mohammed Zuqlam,Salem Amassi,Shady Telbany,Motaz Saad*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Taklif.AI uses LLMs to generate personalized assignments considering students' interests and cultural context, with guardrails and a serverless AWS stack; early testing shows favorable reception, with 84% finding personalization beneficial; rigorous empirical evaluation planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve student engagement and reduce misconduct by creating assignments aligned with individual interests and contexts, addressing limitations of performance-only personalization.

Method: Structured prompt engineering pipeline with input/output guardrails; serverless AWS Next.js app; Llama 3.3 70B via LiteLLM; LangChain for orchestration; multi-provider load balancing; description of system architecture, prompt design, guardrails; preliminary user testing with 68 participants.

Result: Positive reception: 84% participants rated personalization as beneficial; preliminary notes on capabilities and limitations; groundwork for future empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

Conclusion: Taklif.AI demonstrates potential for effective personalized assignments; need rigorous, controlled studies to assess impact on learning outcomes and address issues like privacy, bias, and scalability.

Abstract: Educators face significant challenges in creating engaging, personalized assignments that accommodate students' diverse interests and cognitive abilities. Traditional one-size-fits-all assignments frequently lead to decreased student engagement and increased reliance on unethical practices such as plagiarism. To address these challenges, we present Taklif.AI, a platform that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate personalized assignments tailored to individual student interests. Unlike existing AI-powered educational platforms that personalize based on academic performance metrics alone, Taklif.AI incorporates students' extracurricular interests and cultural contexts into the assignment generation process through a structured prompt engineering pipeline with input and output guardrails. The platform employs a serverless architecture on AWS with Next.js, using Llama 3.3 70B as the primary LLM via LiteLLM for multi-provider load balancing and LangChain for prompt orchestration. We describe the system architecture, the prompt design methodology, and the guardrails framework that ensures output quality. Preliminary user acceptance testing with 68 participants (65 students and 3 educators) indicates positive reception, with 84% of participants rating the personalization feature as beneficial. We discuss the platform's current capabilities and limitations, and outline directions for rigorous empirical evaluation of learning outcomes.

</details>


### [424] [AirQualityBench: A Realistic Evaluation Benchmark for Global Air Quality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05854)
*Xing Xu,Xu Wang,Yudong Zhang,Huilin Zhao,Zhengyang Zhou,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AirQualityBench is a global, multi-pollutant benchmark for realistic air-quality forecasting that preserves missingness patterns and evaluates models on fragmented, real-world monitoring streams; results show sanitized benchmarks overestimate transferability of performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world air-quality networks suffer uneven coverage, structured missingness, heterogenous pollutant scales, and deployment costs. Standard sanitized benchmarks fail to reflect these conditions.

Method: A global dataset of hourly observations from 3,720 stations (2021–2025) across six pollutants, preserving provider-native masks. Rather than imputing missing values, missingness is treated as part of forecasting. Models are evaluated on valid future observations after transforming back to physical concentration scales.

Result: Evaluation reveals that strong performance on sanitized, complete datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams.

Conclusion: AirQualityBench provides a realistic, mask-aware, physically interpretable testbed for scalable forecasting; all data, code, and baselines are available on GitHub.

Abstract: Air-quality forecasting models are commonly evaluated on regional, preprocessed, and normalized datasets, where missing observations are removed or artificially completed. Such protocols simplify comparison but hide the conditions that dominate real monitoring networks: uneven global coverage, structured missingness, heterogeneous pollutant scales, and deployment cost. We introduce \textbf{AirQualityBench}, a global multi-pollutant benchmark designed to evaluate forecasting models under these realistic conditions. The benchmark contains hourly observations from 3,720 monitoring stations over 2021--2025, covers six major pollutants, and preserves provider-native observation masks. Rather than imputing a dense data tensor, AirQualityBench exposes missingness as part of the forecasting problem and reports errors on valid future observations after inverse transformation to physical concentration scales. Evaluating representative spatio-temporal models under this unified protocol shows that strong performance on sanitized datasets does not reliably transfer to global, fragmented monitoring streams. AirQualityBench therefore serves as a realistic testbed for scalable, mask-aware, and physically interpretable air-quality forecasting. All benchmark data, code, evaluation scripts, and baseline implementations are available at \href{https://github.com/Star-Learning/AirQualityBench}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [425] [SANEmerg: An Emergent Communication Framework for Semantic-aware Agentic AI Networking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05861)
*Yong Xiao,Haoran Zhou,Yujie Zhou,Marwan Krunz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SANEmerg enables emergent, bandwidth-aware communication in semantic-aware AgentNet, using an MDL-based complexity regularizer and a bandwidth-adaptable importance filter to achieve higher task accuracy with lower bandwidth and compute in agent-based systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies from rigid separation of communication and computation in large-scale agentic AI networks and the need for autonomous, task-specific signaling protocols among many heterogeneous agents under bandwidth constraints.

Method: Proposes SANEmerg, a framework where communication protocols emerge among collaborative, computationally bounded agents. It includes a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter to prioritize high-contribution message dimensions and an MDL-based complexity-regularizer to foster computationally bounded signaling. Validated on an AgentNet prototype with extensive experiments.

Result: SANEmerg achieves superior task accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods while substantially reducing bandwidth and computational overhead in bandwidth-limited settings.

Conclusion: SANEmerg demonstrates that emergent communication within AgentNet can be effectively constrained by bandwidth and complexity considerations, enabling scalable, cooperative agent systems that balance performance with resource usage.

Abstract: Future networking systems are envisioned to become part of an agentic AI-native ecosystem in which a vast number of heterogeneous and specialized AI agents cooperate seamlessly to fulfill complex user requirements in real time. However, traditional networking paradigms are characterized by a rigid decoupling of communication and computation, which often leads to significant inefficiencies in large-scale agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems. Emergent communication offers a novel solution by enabling autonomous agents that support task-specific signaling protocols for information exchange and collaborative coordination. In this paper, we consider a multi-agent emergent communication framework, tailored for semantic-aware AgentNet systems in which the user's semantic intent can be automatically detected, inferred, and linked to a set of sub-tasks to be assigned to a set of agents. We investigate how communication and signaling protocols can emerge among collaborative agents with computationally bounded intelligence under stringent bandwidth constraints. Our proposed framework, called SANEmerg, is designed to facilitate the emergence of communication for collaborative task fulfillment while adhering to the physical limits of AgentNet. SANEmerg incorporates a bandwidth-adaptable importance-filter that dynamically prioritizes the transmission of higher-contribution message dimensions, ensuring robust performance in bandwidth-limited environments. Furthermore, SANEmerg integrates a complexity-regularizer grounded in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle to facilitate the emergence of computationally bounded signaling. Evaluated via an AgentNet prototype and extensive experimentation, SANEmerg demonstrates significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art solutions, achieving superior task accuracy while significantly reducing bandwidth and computational overhead.

</details>


### [426] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDecomposer is a prior-free, set-based framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns, enabling source-resolved phase retrieval without predefined phase lists or phase count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase PXRD analysis is a major bottleneck because real-world synthesis yields complex mixtures that are hard to disentangle. Existing methods typically assume single-phase inputs and need candidate phase lists or known phase numbers; there is a need for a prior-free, robust approach that can automatically identify multiple phases.

Method: Formulates multiphase diffraction analysis as a set-prediction problem. The model outputs an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and structural representations within a unified architecture. Introduces a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism and diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction to achieve accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Does not require candidate lists, templates, or prior knowledge of phase number.

Result: Extensive experiments on simulated and experimental datasets show substantial improvements in reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, with strong generalization to unseen mixtures and reduced dependence on prior-guided phase matching.

Conclusion: XDecomposer offers a practical, data-driven route for source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis, addressing a long-standing bottleneck and enabling robust multiphase identification without prior information. The approach is readily applicable and the code is openly available.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


### [427] [Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05878)
*Basel Magableh,OmniRisk Research*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IoV requires a composite risk primitive built from five engines (prediction, verification, sentiment fusion, agentic action under constraints, and API-risk scenario generation) to manage route, liquidity, sentiment, and policy risks in a partially-trusted, heterogeneous network. Empirical anchors include a Solana liquidity stress test and a long-run calibration of a prediction/router with class-imbalance honesty; findings indicate deployability and a falsifiable validator-loss decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the dominant marginal risk in the Internet of Value, which is composite and multi-domain (route health, price/liquidity/volatility, sentiment, and policy commitments). Current single-chain risk models are inadequate for a heterogeneous, partially-trusted ecosystem; a modular, verifiable risk primitive is needed to enable trustworthy forecasts, decisions, and actions across substrates.

Method: Propose an architecture comprising five engines: (1) a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; (2) a Bittensor verification subnet to decentralize and economically score prediction outputs; (3) a sentiment-fusion engine integrating textual signals, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; (4) an agentic engine enforcing constitutional, role-bound action constraints; (5) an API-risk and scenario engine converting forecasts into pre-committed action programs via Monte-Carlo scenario generation. Validation anchors include a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc with explicit class-imbalance honesty.

Result: The case study supports deployability of the architecture; the validator-loss decomposition is presented formally and is falsifiable, providing a concrete criterion for evaluation within the framework.

Conclusion: A modular, multi-engine risk-primitives approach appears viable for IoV, enabling integrated forecast-driven action under constitutional constraints and verifiable penalties (loss decomposition). The architecture is anchored in empirical evidence and offers a path to deployable, testable deployments in heterogeneous chain ecosystems.

Abstract: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

</details>


### [428] [Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05909)
*Yuhang Wang,Zhenxing Niu,Haoxuan Ji,Guangyu He,Linlin Zhang,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An MLLM unlearning framework that forgets targeted visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual and all textual knowledge by freezing the LLM backbone and fine-tuning the visual module, using Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to separate knowledge, a null-space constraint to protect retained knowledge, and an extension to continual unlearning with strong retention–forgetting balance across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the core problem of machine unlearning in multimodal LLMs: removing specific target knowledge while preserving non-target and textual knowledge, despite tight coupling between visual and textual modalities, and to extend capabilities to sequential forgetting scenarios.

Method: 1) Freeze the LLM backbone and fine-tune the visual module. 2) Propose Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) to push target visual concepts into appropriate regions of feature space while separating them from retained knowledge. 3) Identify a null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain unlearning within this space to mitigate loss of retained knowledge. 4) Extend the approach to continual unlearning with sequential forgetting requests.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks show that the method achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention, including in continual unlearning scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed CVF-based, null-space-constrained, continual unlearning framework effectively forgets target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual and all textual knowledge in MLLMs, achieving a favorable forgetting–retention trade-off.
