Abstract: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

</details>


### [429] [PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05911)
*Millend Roy,Agostino Capponi,Vineet Goyal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An online learning framework creates per-user summaries of product reviews by iteratively updating preferences from feedback, improving alignment with user interests while maintaining summary quality on Amazon Reviews'23 data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users face overwhelming review volume; static generic summaries fail to capture individual and evolving preferences. Unknown latent preferences necessitate adaptive, personalized summarization.

Method: Online learning loop per user: initialize a preference model, generate summaries guided by current preferences, collect feedback from generated summaries, update the user model, and regenerate summaries iteratively (simulated on Amazon Reviews'23).

Result: Controlled simulations show improved alignment with target user interests while preserving summary quality metrics.

Conclusion: Online, feedback-driven personalization is feasible for review summarization, enabling evolving, user-specific summaries without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

</details>


### [430] [Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05913)
*Weihua Wang,Haoji Li,Feilong Bao,Lei Yang,Guanglai Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Wisteria unifies local and global dependency modeling for DNA sequences by combining gated dilated convolutions for motif-level patterns, gated MLPs for global dependencies, and Fourier-based attention for frequency-domain modeling and length generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DNA language models emphasize long-range interactions but often neglect the interaction between local motifs and global dependencies, limiting multi-scale regulatory grammar capture.

Method: Enhances a Mamba-based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs, uses gated multilayer perceptrons to refine global dependencies, and introduces a Fourier-based attention mechanism to enable frequency-domain modeling, periodic extension, and length generalization.

Result: Across four experimental settings with short and long-range dependencies, Wisteria achieves strong performance on downstream benchmarks compared to competitive DNA language model baselines, indicating effective unification of local and global dependencies.

Conclusion: Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi-scale genomic sequence analysis.

Abstract: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

</details>


### [431] [Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05921)
*Alex Bäuerle,Adam Connors,Alexander Novikov,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Ngân Vũ,Fernanda Viegas,Martin Wattenberg,Lucas Dixon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formative study with 11 mathematicians using AlphaEvolve reveals 'intentmaking' as a goal-driven, iterative workflow that blends goal discovery with sensemaking, advocating for collaborative AI tools beyond Q/A.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand interaction paradigms for AI-assisted scientific discovery and how researchers interact with evolutionary coding agents; extend sensemaking to include goal management.

Method: Qualitative formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve; thematic analysis to identify workflow and design implications.

Result: Identifies 'intentmaking' as an iterative process of discovering, defining, refining experimental goals; describes interplay with sensemaking; proposes design of AI tools as collaborative instruments rather than opaque advisors.

Conclusion: Suggests design principles to support iterative goal-definition and interpretation cycles, enabling more effective human-AI collaboration in scientific discovery.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

</details>


### [432] [Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05929)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue,Pierre Monnin,Miguel Couceiro,Fabien Gandon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study proposes a methodology to quantify language distribution in multilingual LOD KGs and defines a preliminary low/medium/high-resource taxonomy using DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata to guide cross-lingual transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the Open Access Data divide between high- and low-resource languages in multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs; existing work lacks a quantitative definition of language-resource levels within LOD KGs.

Method: Analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and develop a multi-level categorization by integrating signals from DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata; formulate formal definitions for low-, medium-, and high-resource languages.

Result: A preliminary multi-level resource categorization with formal definitions, plus a framework to select cross-lingual transfer candidates based on language-resource level.

Conclusion: The proposed categorization provides a foundation to mitigate language-based OA data disparities via cross-lingual transfer; future work should validate and refine the scheme with empirical data and additional KG sources.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

</details>


### [433] [In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05931)
*Ndeye-Emilie Mbengue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A PhD proposal to quantify language coverage in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata) and to evaluate cross-lingual transfer strategies and analogical reasoning for improving multilingual KG completion and language coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the Open Access Data language divide, where high-resource languages dominate LOD. The work seeks to quantify language distribution across major multilingual LOD KGs and to develop methods that improve language coverage via cross-lingual transfer and analogical reasoning.

Method: 1) Define variables for language distribution: number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. 2) Analyze these variables across DBpedia, BabelNet, Wikidata. 3) Study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on multilingual KG completion, using strategies based on linguistic proximity and availability of curated alignments. 4) Explore analogical reasoning based on language similarities/dissimilarities to identify cross-language correspondences to improve KG completion.

Result: As a proposal, results are anticipated: (i) a characterization of language distribution in major LOD KGs, (ii) evaluation of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection strategies for multilingual KG completion, and (iii) assessment of analogical reasoning approaches in improving cross-language correspondences and language coverage.

Conclusion: The work targets reducing language bias in LOD by combining language-proximity guided cross-lingual transfer and analogical reasoning to enhance multilingual KG completion and language coverage in Open Access Data.

Abstract: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

</details>


### [434] [ICU-Bench:Benchmarking Continual Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05938)
*Yuhang Wang,Wenjie Mei,Junkai Zhang,Guangyu He,Zhenxing Niu,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ICU-Bench: a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark for privacy-sensitive documents, featuring 1k profiles, 9.5k images, 16k QA pairs, and 100 forget tasks; reveals current unlearning methods struggle in continual settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses privacy risks of training Multimodal LLMs on large multimodal data and the lack of benchmarks for continual privacy deletion; motivates evaluation of forgetting in realistic continual deployments.

Method: Proposes ICU-Bench dataset built from privacy-critical documents (medical reports and labor contracts) with multimodal data; introduces new continual unlearning metrics to assess forgetting, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability; conducts extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods.

Result: Found that existing unlearning methods struggle in continual settings and fail to balance forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences.

Conclusion: ICU-Bench provides a benchmark and metrics to drive development of multimodal unlearning methods tailored for continual privacy deletion in real-world deployments.

Abstract: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across many domains, their training on large-scale multimodal datasets raises serious privacy concerns, making effective machine unlearning increasingly necessary. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on static or short-sequence settings, offering limited support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments. To bridge this gap, we introduce ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical document data. ICU-Bench contains 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two document domains, medical reports and labor contracts, with 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Additionally, new continual unlearning metrics are introduced, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability throughout the continual unlearning process. Through extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench, we show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings and exhibit clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences. These findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

</details>


### [435] [MAS-Algorithm: A Workflow for Solving Algorithmic Programming Problems with a Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05949)
*Yuliang Xu,Xiang Xu,Yao Wan,Hu Wei,Tong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR:  MAS-Algorithm introduces a modular, multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving, achieving notable performance gains and interpretability over model-centric and prompting-based methods, with strong ablation analyses suggesting substantial contribution from individual agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fragmentation and limited interpretability of existing model-centric or prompting-based approaches for algorithmic problem solving by providing a unified, extensible, multi-agent framework that mirrors expert competitive programming workflows.

Method: Propose MAS-Algorithm: a systematic, modular multi-agent pipeline that decomposes end-to-end problem solving into stages, enables structured reasoning, tool integration, and coordinated action among agents. The design emphasizes rigor and extensibility. Empirical evaluation uses a self-constructed benchmark across Qwen-series models and LiveCodeBench-Pro, plus ablation studies and error analysis to understand reasoning patterns.

Result: MAS-Algorithm yields average acceptance-rate gains of 6.48% on Qwen-series models (vs 0.89% for parameter-efficient fine-tuning). A 4.72% gain is observed on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with improvements in accuracy and efficiency metrics. Ablation and customization studies indicate individual agents contributing up to 27.7% in certain settings, and analyses reveal error patterns and cross-scenario behavior that inform framework robustness.

Conclusion: MAS-Algorithm demonstrates the potential of a structured, multi-agent approach to AI-driven algorithmic reasoning, offering generalizability across diverse problem types, improved performance, and enhanced interpretability compared to traditional model-centric or prompting-based methods.

Abstract: Algorithmic problem solving serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating structured reasoning in AI coding systems, as it directly reflects a model's ability to perform structured reasoning in complex scenarios.Existing approaches predominantly rely on model-centric strategies, such as architectural modifications and data scaling, which are costly and offer limited interpretability. Alternative methods leveraging external tools or prompting techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) are often fragmented and lack a unified framework. In this paper, we propose MAS-Algorithm, a systematic multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving inspired by the practices of competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. Our framework decomposes the end-to-end solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among agents. The design emphasizes both rigor and extensibility, allowing it to generalize across diverse problem types.Experimental results on a self-constructed benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple Qwen series models, achieving an average gain of 6.48% in acceptance rate. In contrast, parameter-efficient fine-tuning on the same data yields only a marginal improvement of 0.89%. We further observe a 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with consistent improvements across additional accuracy and efficiency metrics.Beyond performance gains, we conduct comprehensive analyses to better understand the reasoning process within the workflow, including error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors. We further perform customized replacement and ablation studies to explore the upper bound of the framework, showing that individual agents can contribute improvements of up to 27.7%. These results highlight the strong potential of MAS-Algorithm for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning.

</details>


### [436] [HaM-World: Soft-Hamiltonian World Models with Selective Memory for Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05951)
*Haoyun Tang,Haodong Cui,Keyao Xu,Kun Wang,Zhandong Mei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HaM-World proposes a structured, Hamiltonian-inspired world model with a canonical (q,p) latent plus a context vector c, using Mamba memory; it improves long-horizon planning stability and OOD robustness on DM Control tasks, with diagnostics supporting soft-Hamiltonian dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imaginative rollouts in model-based planning degrade with horizon length and dynamics distribution shifts. The authors argue that planner-facing latents need (i) history-conditioned memory to approach Markov completeness and (ii) geometric separation of configuration, momentum, and task semantics.

Method: Decompose latent state into a canonical (q,p) subspace and a context subspace c; employ Mamba selective state-space memory as history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. The (q,p) subsystem evolves via an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This yields a single latent shared across dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search.

Result: On four DM Control Suite tasks, HaM-World achieves the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, it attains the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% (Finger Spin) and 13.6% (Reacher Easy). Diagnostics show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift and coherent energy transfer, supporting the Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

Conclusion: The structured, energy-based latent with history-conditioned memory yields stable, unified planning dynamics and robust generalization under dynamics shifts, aligning dynamics, value learning, and search within a single latent and validating the Soft-Hamiltonian design through diagnostic evidence.

Abstract: World models enable model-based planning through learned latent dynamics, but imagined rollouts become unstable as the planning horizon grows or the dynamics distribution shifts. We argue that this instability reflects two missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and geometric organization that separates configuration, momentum, and task semantics. We propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that decomposes the latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c, while using Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. Within this interface, (q, p) evolves through an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This gives the planner a single latent state shared by dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search. On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World reaches the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, HaM-World achieves the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics further show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift, structured energy variation under policy rollouts, and coherent control-induced energy transfer, supporting the intended Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

</details>


### [437] [Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05958)
*Peilin Zhan,Wei Chen,Weilin Chen,Shuyi Pan,Ruichu Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) for Knowledge Tracing to correct selection bias in observational logs by combining a propensity model with an error-imputation model, derives a variance-aware generalization bound, and shows improved performance across KT backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selection bias from non-random exercise recommendations and student choices contaminates KT training data. Standard empirical risk minimization on observed logs yields biased mastery estimates and unstable sequential learning.

Method: Introduce a doubly robust (DR) estimator that integrates a propensity model and an error imputation model. Derive a generalization bound showing variance-induced deviations in sequential KT. Propose Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) that jointly trains the KT predictor and imputor with a smoothness regularizer to reduce variance while maintaining DR unbiasedness.

Result: The TSDR framework yields unbiasedness under correct specification of either propensity or imputation model and, in practice, reduces variance-related instability. Empirical evaluations on real-world benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over various state-of-the-art KT backbones.

Conclusion: Principled bias correction via DR with temporal smoothness improves Knowledge Tracing accuracy and stability in the presence of selection bias, underscoring the importance of addressing bias and variance in sequential educational modeling.

Abstract: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is fundamental to intelligent education systems, yet relies on educational logs that are selectively observed. The non-random nature of exercise recommendations and student choices inevitably induces severe selection bias. Most existing KT methods neglect this issue, training on observed logs using standard empirical risk, which yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations. To address this, we introduce a doubly robust (DR) formulation for KT that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. Beyond unbiasedness, in the sequential setting of KT, we identify that the estimator's performance is compromised by variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, thereby causing training instability and limiting performance. To mitigate this, we derive a generalization bound that explicitly characterizes the impact of estimator variance and identifies temporal smoothness as a key factor in controlling it. Building on these theoretical insights, we propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework. TSDR jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputation model with a smoothness regularizer, effectively reducing variance while preserving the unbiasedness guarantee of DR. Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, underscoring the vital role of principled bias correction in KT.

</details>


### [438] [From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05959)
*Xinghao Wu,Jianwei Niu,Guogang Zhu,Xuefeng Liu,Shaojie Tang,Jiayuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FedSAF introduces structural (relational) alignment for prototype-based heterogeneous federated learning, replacing coordinate alignment to preserve client-specific feature subspaces; it outperforms prior methods by up to 3.52% across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In Heterogeneous FL, clients have different feature extractors, leading to distinct feature subspaces. Coordinate alignment forces a global subspace, which can impair learning. Aligning only inter-class relationships can preserve heterogeneity while still leveraging shared semantic structure.

Method: Replace absolute-coordinate alignment (MSE/cosine) with structural alignment on inter-class relations. Use class prototypes as representatives but align their relational structure (e.g., pairwise distances or relational graphs) rather than forcing embeddings to match globally. Applicable to prototype-based HtFL setups where clients share class semantics but have different feature spaces.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple benchmarks shows FedSAF outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52% in accuracy (or primary metric).

Conclusion: Structural alignment of inter-class relationships is more effective than coordinate alignment in heterogeneous federated settings, enabling better use of heterogeneous models without forcing a common feature basis.

Abstract: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

</details>


### [439] [TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05963)
*Junkai Li,Yunghwei Lai,Tianyi Zhu,Zheng Long Lee,Weizhi Ma,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TheraAgent introduces an iterative generate-judge-refine framework for treatment planning, using TheraJudge to enforce clinical standards during inference; achieves state-of-the-art on HealthBench, with 86% physician win rate and strong agreement between judges and HealthBench evaluations, indicating improved safety, targeting, and harm control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of one-shot LLM generation for treatment plans, which can be incomplete, unsafe, or inaccurate due to lack of explicit verification; emulate human expert reasoning through iterative refinement.

Method: Propose TheraAgent, an agentic loop that iterates: generate draft plans, judge their quality with an integrated TheraJudge module enforcing clinical standards, and refine plans accordingly. The framework is evaluated on HealthBench for quantitative metrics (Accuracy, Completeness) and via expert evaluations against physicians; TheraJudge's outputs are shown to align well with HealthBench evaluations.

Result: TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench (leading in Accuracy and Completeness); expert evaluations show an 86% win rate against physicians, with higher Targeting and Harm Control; strong agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations supports reliability of the framework.

Conclusion: Iterative generate-judge-refine systems with a domain-specific evaluator (TheraJudge) can substantially improve the quality, safety, and reliability of automated treatment planning, outperforming one-shot generation approaches and approaching or surpassing expert performance in certain metrics.

Abstract: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline. By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.

</details>


### [440] [BehaviorGuard: Online Backdoor Defense for Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05977)
*Yinbo Yu,Xueyu Yin,Jiadai Wang,Chunwei Tian,Sai Xu,Qi Zhu,Daoqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes BehaviorGuard, an online, behavior-based backdoor defense for DRL that detects and mitigates trigger-agnostic backdoor behavior by monitoring action-distribution drift, effective in single- and multi-agent DRL with improved efficiency over prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in DRL can rely on deceptive rewards or fine-tuning defenses, but complex triggers and high mitigation costs hinder robustness. There is a need for online, trigger-agnostic detection that remains effective across settings.

Method: Identify consistent shifts in action distributions induced by backdoors, focusing on high-quantile regions and distribution tails that persist without triggers. Develop a metric quantifying behavioral drift to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. Extend to online mitigation and apply to both single- and multi-agent DRL.

Result: BehaviorGuard consistently outperforms prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency across diverse backdoor attacks and benchmarks.

Conclusion: This work introduces the first online defense against backdoors in DRL that is effective for both single- and multi-agent settings, leveraging trigger-agnostic behavioral signatures for real-time detection and mitigation.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks pose a serious threat to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Current defenses typically rely on reward anomalies to reverse-engineer triggers and model finetuning to remove backdoors. However, complex trigger patterns undermine their robustness, and fine-tuning entails high costs, limiting practical utility. Therefore, we shift defense concerns to trigger-agnostic backdoor output behaviors and propose BehaviorGuard, an online behavior-based backdoor detection and mitigation framework for DRL. Specifically, we find that regardless of attacks, backdoored policies induce consistent shifts in action distributions to ensure reliable activation, leaving detectable traces in high-quantile regions and distribution tails, even in the absence of triggers. Based on this, we design a novel metric that captures behavioral drift in action distributions to identify and suppress backdoor actions at runtime. To our knowledge, this is the first online backdoor defense that counters attacks both in single- and multi-agent DRL. Evaluated across diverse benchmarks with different backdoor attacks, BehaviorGuard consistently surpasses prior methods in both efficacy and efficiency.

</details>


### [441] [TACT: Mitigating Overthinking and Overacting in Coding Agents via Activation Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05980)
*Yuan Sui,Yulin Chen,Yibo Li,Xue Jiang,Yufei He,Yihong Dong,Xiaoxin He,Tianyu Gao,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering) detects and mitigates agent drift in long-horizon LLM-agent tasks by classifying steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, discovering two drift axes in hidden states, and projecting activations onto these axes to steer drift back toward calibrated behavior, improving task resolve rates and reducing steps-to-resolve on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agent tasks suffer from drift where agents overthink or overact, leading to degraded performance over time. The work aims to identify drift signals within the residual stream and intervene to maintain reliability.

Method: Label each trajectory step as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated. Observe that hidden states separate along two drift axes. At test time, project each step's activation onto these axes and pull drifted steps toward the calibrated region to mitigate drift (residual-stream steering).

Result: TACT outperforms unsteered baselines on SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, increasing average resolve rate by +5.8 percentage points on Qwen3.5-27B and +4.8 percentage points on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it, while reducing steps-to-resolve by up to 26%.

Conclusion: Framing agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream yields a viable, effective mechanism (TACT) for improving reliability of long-horizon agents.

Abstract: When language model agents tackle complex software engineering tasks, they often degrade over long trajectories, which we define as *agent drift*. We focus on two recurring failure modes *overthinking* and *overacting*, i.e., where the agent repeatedly reasons over information it already has, and where it issues tool calls without integrating recent observations or acquiring new evidence. In this paper, we introduce TACT (Think-Act Calibration via activation Steering), to detect and mitigate agent drift in the residual stream before it surfaces as a behavioral failure. In specific, we label trajectory steps as overthinking, overacting, or calibrated, and find that their hidden states can separate linearly along two *drift axes*, pointing from calibrated behavior toward each failure mode (AUC $\approx$ 0.9). To mitigate agent drift, we project each step's activation onto these axes at test time and pull drifted ones back toward the calibrated region. Experiments show that TACT outperforms unsteered baselines across SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and CLAW-Eval, lifting average resolve rate by $+5.8$ pp on Qwen3.5-27B and $+4.8$ pp on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it while cutting steps-to-resolve by up to $26\%$. These gains frame agent drift as a steerable direction in the residual stream, and position TACT as a viable handle for reliable long-horizon agents.

</details>


### [442] [BioResearcher: Scenario-Guided Multi-Agent for Translational Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05985)
*Remigiusz Kinas,Joanna Krawczyk,Rafał Powalski,Przemysław Pietrzak,Agnieszka Kowalewska,Krzysztof Kolmus,Maciej Sypetkowski,Łukasz Smoliński,Tomasz Jetka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ingenix BioResearcher introduces a scenario-guided multi-agent system for translational medicine that ties queries to versioned research playbooks, leverages 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, and reconciles models before editorial assembly, achieving strong unit-, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose foundation models struggle with auditable, scenario-specific biomedical workflows and heterogeneous data integration; there is a need for auditable provenance, uncertainty handling, and reproducible analyses in translational medicine.

Method: Develop a scenario-guided multi-agent platform that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents across 30+ tools/ML endpoints, integrates structured database access with sandboxed genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation prior to editorial assembly.

Result: On 109 single-step tests: 83.49% pass; 0.892 average score. BixBench-Verified-50: 89.33%; BaisBench Scientific Discovery: mean score 0.758 (top). 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark: positive hit rate 74.7% ± 3.3%, negative clear rate 96.8% ± 0.2%.

Conclusion: BioResearcher demonstrates broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery benchmarks.

Abstract: Translational medicine turns underspecified development goals into evidence synthesis that must combine literature, trials, patents, and quantitative multi-omics analysis while preserving identifiers, uncertainty, and retrievable provenance. General-purpose foundation models and off-the-shelf tool-augmented or multi-agent systems are not built for this: they tend to produce single-shot answers or run open-endedly, and fall short on the auditable, scenario-specific workflows that heterogeneous biomedical sources demand.
  This paper introduces Ingenix BioResearcher, a scenario-guided multi-agent system that maps queries to versioned research playbooks, delegates to specialized subagents over 30+ tools and machine-learning endpoints, mixes structured database access with sandboxed code for genome-scale analyses, and applies claim-level multi-model reconciliation before editorial assembly.
  We evaluate BioResearcher across unit-level capabilities, open-ended biomedical reasoning, and end-to-end clinical discovery. It leads evaluated baselines on 109 single-step tests (83.49% pass rate; 0.892 average score), achieves strong biomedical benchmark performance (89.33% on BixBench-Verified-50 and the top 0.758 mean score on BaisBench Scientific Discovery), and leads on a 30-query clinical end-to-end benchmark with the highest positive hit rate (74.7% $\pm$ 3.3%) and negative clear rate (96.8% $\pm$ 0.2%). These results show broad, competitive performance across unit-level, open-ended, and end-to-end clinical evaluations.

</details>


### [443] [Strat-LLM: Stratified Strategy Alignment for LLM-based Stock Trading with Real-time Multi-Source Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06024)
*Wenliang Huang,Zengyi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Strat-LLM introduces Stratified Strategy Alignment for autonomous trading LLMs, showing mode- and scale-dependent alignment and performance across regimes; findings include: Free mode enables internal reasoning gains; Strict mode acts as risk anchor; alignment utility is regime-dependent; 35B models best under strict constraints; 122B models benefit in Guided mode; high win-rate trap mitigated by deep reasoning or guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency in live trading LLMs; reduce look-ahead bias by live-forward data integration; evaluate mode-based alignment across market regimes.

Method: Live-forward trading framework through 2025 using heterogeneous data (prices, news, annual reports) to avoid look-ahead; stress tests on A-share and US markets; compare reasoning-heavy vs standard LLMs; evaluate Free, Strict, Guided modes; analyze model scales around 35B and 122B; quantify alignment utility, drawdowns, total returns.

Result: Key findings: (1) reasoning-heavy models peak in Free Mode; standard models need Strict as a risk anchor; (2) alignment utility depends on regime; Free/Guided capture momentum in uptrends; Strict reduces drawdowns in downtrends; (3) 35B models achieve best fidelity under strict constraints; 122B models suffer alignment tax under rigid rules but improve in Guided; (4) standard LLMs exhibit high win-rate bias that hurts total returns; mitigation via deep reasoning or strict guardrails.

Conclusion: Stratified Strategy Alignment yields regime- and scale-aware insights for trading LLMs, highlighting mode selection as a critical design knob for balancing reasoning, risk management, and performance; guidance for future benchmarking and framework deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are evolving into autonomous trading agents, yet existing benchmarks often overlook the interplay between architectural reasoning and strategy consistency. We propose Strat-LLM, a framework grounded in Stratified Strategy Alignment. Operating in a live-forward setting throughout 2025, it integrates heterogeneous data including sequential prices, real-time news, and annual reports to eliminate look-ahead bias. Extensive stress tests on A-share and U.S. markets reveal: (1) reasoning-heavy models achieve peak utility in Free Mode via internal logic, whereas standard models require Strict Mode as a vital risk anchor; (2) alignment utility is regime-dependent, with Free and Guided modes capturing momentum in uptrending markets, while Strict Mode mitigates drawdowns in downtrends; (3) mid-scale models (35B) show optimal fidelity under strict constraints, whereas ultra-large models (122B) suffer an alignment tax under rigid rules but gain a performance premium in Guided Mode; (4) standard LLMs often fall into a high win-rate trap, optimizing for small gains at the expense of total returns, which can only be mitigated through deep reasoning or strict external guardrails. Project details are available at https://Strat-LLM.github.io.

</details>


### [444] [Pathways to AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06029)
*Gordon Fletcher,Saomai Vu Khan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A critical, socio-technical analysis of dominant generative AI pathways, identifying leverage points and divergent trajectories across frontier/proprietary, open-weight, and domain/sovereign models, mapping alternative branches and proposing governance-informed development programs toward AGI-adjacent capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the inevitability of current AI trajectories by examining how social, political, and economic conditions shape AI development and questioning AGI definitions, aiming for a conditional, critically informed perspective.

Method: Qualitative, historical-pathways analysis anchored in critical software studies; centers on five guiding questions to trace pathways, leverage points, alternative branches, and cross-trajectory differences, using comparative and scenario-based reasoning.

Result: A structured account of how decisions and pathways produced current generative AI dominance, a taxonomy of leverage points and dead ends, a cross-trajectory comparison, an inventory of alternative projects and their fates, and a blueprint of socio-technical development programs balancing transparency, moderation, wellbeing, and sustainable business models.

Conclusion: Provides a critical, conditional frame for evaluating AGI prospects, highlighting governance and socio-technical alignment as prerequisites for responsible progress and suggesting concrete, transparency-minded development directions.

Abstract: Our focus are five related questions that stem from a critical software studies perspective. Underpinning this view is the acknowledged need to avoid assumptions regarding the inevitability of the current situation relating to AI. What we need to see is the closeness of the linkage between current commercial AI development and our prevailing social, political and economic circumstances. This does mean that the perspectives presented here are done so critically and conditionally. Most importantly, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is seen as being problematic both conceptually and definitionally. This conditioning of any view regarding AGI does lead the discussion in specific directions and to certain conclusions regarding the future. However, adopting this perspective enables the work to offer some final recommendations. We set out to ask the following questions, 1. What are the critical pathways that produced the current dominant generative AI tools (capabilities, product forms, adoption patterns)? 2. Which decision points acted as leverage nodes (small changes that had large downstream effects), and which dead ends reveal alternative possibilities that did not become dominant? 3. How do pathways differ across three foundational-model trajectories such as the frontier proprietary models, open-weight models or specific domain and sovereign models? 4. Which alternative projects branched from key leverage nodes, what is their current state, and why did some succeed, stall, fail or become absorbed? 5. Based on this analysis, what socio-technical development programmes could plausibly move toward AGI-adjacent capability while meeting requirements for transparency, moderation, wellbeing and sustainable business models?

</details>


### [445] [Novelty-based Tree-of-Thought Search for LLM Reasoning and Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06040)
*Leon Hamm,Zlatan Ajanovic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces novelty-based pruning for tree-of-thought in LLM reasoning, borrowing width-based search ideas to prune low-novelty nodes; uses LLM prompts and pre-trained embeddings to quantify novelty, reducing overall token costs despite more prompts per state; validated on language-based planning and general reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness and high time/token costs of chain-of-thought/tree-of-thought and reinforcement learning in LLM reasoning; adapt width-based search to language domains to improve efficiency and scalability.

Method: Define a measurable novelty metric for each new node by comparing it to previously seen nodes using LLM prompts and embedding-based knowledge from pre-training; prune branches with low novelty to shrink the search tree; the approach trades additional prompts per state for overall reduction in tree size and token usage; evaluation on multiple language-based planning and general reasoning benchmarks.

Result: Preliminary experiments indicate that the novelty-based pruning reduces search space and token costs while maintaining competitive performance across several benchmarks; the method is tested and compared against baseline tree-of-thought approaches.

Conclusion: Novelty-based pruning is a viable, cost-efficient enhancement for tree-of-thought reasoning in language tasks, successfully integrating ideas from width-based search into LLM planning and reasoning.

Abstract: Although advances such as chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought or reinforcement learning have improved the performance of LLMs in reasoning and planning tasks, they are still brittle and have not achieved human-level performance in many domains, and often suffer from high time and token costs. Inspired by the success of width-based search in planning, we explore how the concept of novelty can be transferred to language domains and how it can improve tree-of-thought reasoning. A tree of thoughts relies on building possible "paths" of consecutive ideas or thoughts. These are generated by repeatedly prompting an LLM. In our paper, a measurable concept of novelty is proposed that describes the uniqueness of a new node (thought) in comparison to nodes previously seen in the search tree. Novelty is estimated by prompting an LLM and making use of embedded general knowledge from pre-training. This metric can then be used to prune branches and reduce the scope of the search. Although this method introduces more prompts per state, the overall token cost can be reduced by pruning and reducing the overall tree size. This procedure is tested and compared using several benchmarks in language-based planning and general reasoning.

</details>


### [446] [Visual Fingerprints for LLM Generation Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06054)
*Amal Alnouri,Andreas Hinterreiter,Christina Humer,Furui Cheng,Marc Streit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Visual fingerprints of LLM generation conditions by modeling linguistic choices and comparing distributions across samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture (generation conditions) bias outputs; traditional metrics miss distribution-level effects due to stochasticity.

Method: Extract linguistic choices (content, expression, and structure) from outputs via NLP pipelines; represent distributions over repeated samples; visualize as fingerprints for condition-level comparison.

Result: Demonstrates four usage scenarios; fingerprints reveal consistent condition-specific patterns not evident from individual responses or aggregate metrics; enables direct distribution-level comparisons.

Conclusion: Provides a practical framework for analyzing how generation conditions shape LLM behavior; supports prompt design and evaluation; fingerprints capture bias patterns and facilitate robust cross-condition comparisons.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) outputs arise from complex interactions among prompts, system instructions, model parameters, and architecture. We refer to specific configurations of these factors as generation conditions, each of which can bias outputs in various ways. Understanding how different generation conditions shape model behaviors is essential for tasks such as prompt design and model evaluation, yet it remains challenging due to the stochastic and open-ended nature of text generation. We present an approach to visually compare LLM outputs across generation conditions by modeling responses as collections of linguistic choices, including content, expression, and structure. We extract these choices using natural language processing pipelines and represent their distributions across repeated samples. We then visualize these distributions as visual fingerprints, enabling direct, distribution-level comparison of condition-specific tendencies. Through four usage scenarios, we demonstrate how visual fingerprints reveal consistent patterns in LLM behavior that are difficult to observe through individual responses or aggregate metrics.

</details>


### [447] [VibeServe: Can AI Agents Build Bespoke LLM Serving Systems?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06068)
*Keisuke Kamahori,Shihang Li,Simon Peter,Baris Kasikci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VibeServe introduces a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke LLM serving stacks for different usage scenarios, matching or surpassing generic systems (e.g., vLLM) and excelling in non-standard workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of a single, highly-optimized, general-purpose LLM serving stack by enabling generation-time specialization for diverse workloads and architectures.

Method: An outer loop plans and tracks the search over system designs; an inner loop implements candidates, verifies correctness, and measures performance on target benchmarks to build end-to-end serving stacks.

Result: In standard deployments, VibeServe is competitive with vLLM; in six non-standard scenarios it outperforms existing systems by exploiting workload/model/ hardware-specific opportunities.

Conclusion: Generation-time specialization offers a new design space for LLM infrastructure software, balancing performance with specialization; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: For years, we have built LLM serving systems like any other critical infrastructure: a single general-purpose stack, hand-tuned over many engineer-years, meant to support every model and workload. In this paper, we take the opposite bet: a multi-agent loop that automatically synthesizes bespoke serving systems for different usage scenarios. We propose VibeServe, the first agentic loop that generates entire LLM serving stacks end-to-end. VibeServe uses an outer loop to plan and track the search over system designs, and an inner loop to implement candidates, check correctness, and measure performance on the target benchmark. In the standard deployment setting, where existing stacks are highly optimized, VibeServe remains competitive with vLLM, showing that generation-time specialization need not come at the cost of performance. More interestingly, in non-standard scenarios, VibeServe outperforms existing systems by exploiting opportunities that generic systems miss in six scenarios involving non-standard model architectures, workload knowledge, and hardware-specific optimizations. Together, these results suggest a different point in the design space for infrastructure software: generation-time specialization rather than runtime generality. Code is available at https://github.com/uw-syfi/vibe-serve.

</details>


### [448] [Safety Certification is Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06087)
*Oliver Schön,Licio Romao,Sadegh Soudjani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Direct, non-recursive kernel-embedding estimator for T-step safety probability that bypasses DP recursion; remains stable with horizon and non-Markovian dynamics; subsumes barrier certificates and robust Markov models; validated on a neural-controlled quadrotor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP-based safety certification over trajectories suffers from compounding errors that yield vacuous lower bounds as the horizon grows; a horizon-insensitive method is needed, especially for non-Markovian dynamics.

Method: A kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a trajectory-data classification problem and directly estimates the T-step safety probability without recursion. It unifies existing approaches (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases.

Result: Direct estimators remain stable irrespective of the certification horizon and handle non-Markovian dynamics; DP-based certificates become unsound in comparison; demonstrated via simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

Conclusion: Direct, non-recursive, kernel-based safety certification provides horizon-independent guarantees and extends applicability beyond Markovian assumptions, offering a unified and potentially more robust alternative to DP-based methods.

Abstract: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons $T$. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the $T$-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.

</details>


### [449] [Shallow Prefill, Deep Decoding: Efficient Long-Context Inference via Layer-Asymmetric KV Visibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06105)
*Jungsuk Oh,Hyeseo Jeon,Hyunjune Ji,Kyongmin Kong,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPEED reduces long-context cost by making only lower layers materialize non-anchor prompt KV states in prefill; upper layers do not store prefill KV states, while decode-phase tokens remain full-depth, preserving performance with memory savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is expensive due to processing long prompts during prefill, KV caching at every layer, and repeated attention during autoregressive decode. A phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy aims to cut memory and compute without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Introduce Shallow Prefill, deep Decode (SPEED): a phase-asymmetric policy where non-anchor prompt-token KV states are materialized only in lower layers during prefill; upper-layer prefill KV states are removed from the Decode visibility set. Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth. Evaluate with a minimal BoS anchor on Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning, using 75% prefill layers; report OLMES-style scores, TTFT, TPOT, and active KV memory at 128K context.

Result: With 75% prefill layers, SPEED achieves 51.2 average on OLMES-style benchmarks versus 51.4 for full-depth baseline. It improves TTFT by 33% and TPOT by 22%. Active KV memory is reduced by 25.0% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics indicate the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions are preserved.

Conclusion: Long-context prompt tokens do not need to persist as full-depth KV-cache objects during Decode; a shallow prefill in lower layers with full-depth decode yields near-baseline performance while reducing memory costs.

Abstract: Long-context inference in decoder-only language models is costly because long prompts are processed during Prefill, cached at every layer, and repeatedly attended to during autoregressive Decode. We introduce \emph{Shallow Prefill, dEEp Decode} (SPEED), a phase-asymmetric KV-visibility policy that materializes non-anchor prompt-token KV states only in lower layers while keeping Decode-phase tokens full-depth. Unlike previous approaches that make upper-layer prompt KV states cheaper to store or construct, SPEED removes prefill tokens from the upper-layer Decode visibility set altogether. With a minimal BoS anchor, this simple change preserves broad benchmark quality while reducing long-context cost. In a controlled Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning study, SPEED using only 75\% of layers for prefill tokens reaches 51.2 average score on OLMES-style benchmarks, compared with 51.4 for the full-depth baseline, while improving TTFT by 33\%, TPOT by 22\%, and reducing active KV memory by 25.0\% at 128K context. Layer-wise diagnostics suggest that this cutoff retains the main prompt-selection and representation-stabilization regions of the full-depth model. These results show that long-context prompt tokens need not always persist as full-depth KV-cache objects when Decode-phase tokens remain full-depth.

</details>


### [450] [On Time, Within Budget: Constraint-Driven Online Resource Allocation for Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06110)
*Xinglin Wang,Zishen Liu,Shaoxiong Feng,Peiwen Yuan,Yiwei Li,Jiayi Shi,Yueqi Zhang,Chuyi Tan,Ji Zhang,Boyuan Pan,Yao Hu,Kan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows; introduces Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP) to maximize the probability that a workflow completes within budget and deadline by simulating executions and replanning; shows improved constrained completion probability on CodeFlow and ProofFlow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real deployments require guarantees that a workflow finishes within a given budget and deadline. Previous work focusing on average efficiency may fail to meet hard constraints; there is a need to maximize the probability of full completion under specified constraints.

Method: Formulates a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem for a dependency-structured workflow. Given subtask-model pair success rates and generation lengths, allocate models and parallel samples to executable subtasks while tracking remaining budget and time. Proposes Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning (MCPP), a closed-loop planner that estimates constrained completion probability via simulated workflow executions and replans after observing outcomes.

Result: Empirical results on CodeFlow and ProofFlow show that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget–deadline constraints.

Conclusion: MCPP provides a lightweight, principled approach to constraint-driven allocation in agentic workflows, enabling higher probability of end-to-end completion under budget and deadline constraints and beating strong baselines.

Abstract: Agentic systems increasingly solve complex user requests by executing orchestrated workflows, where subtasks are assigned to specialized models or tools and coordinated according to their dependencies. While recent work improves agent efficiency by optimizing the performance--cost--latency frontier, real deployments often impose concrete requirements: a workflow must be completed within a specified budget and before a specified deadline. This shifts the goal from average efficiency optimization to maximizing the probability that the entire workflow completes successfully under explicit budget and deadline constraints. We study \emph{constraint-driven online resource allocation for agentic workflows}. Given a dependency-structured workflow and estimates of success rates and generation lengths for each subtask--model pair, the executor allocates models and parallel samples across simultaneously executable subtasks while managing the remaining budget and time. We formulate this setting as a finite-horizon stochastic online allocation problem and propose \emph{Monte Carlo Portfolio Planning} (MCPP), a lightweight closed-loop planner that directly estimates constrained completion probability through simulated workflow executions and replans after observed outcomes. Experiments on CodeFlow and ProofFlow demonstrate that MCPP consistently improves constrained completion probability over strong baselines across a wide range of budget--deadline constraints.

</details>


### [451] [CrossCult-KIBench: A Benchmark for Cross-Cultural Knowledge Insertion in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06115)
*Zhen Zeng,Leijiang Gu,Feng Li,Jing Yu,Zenglin Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CrossCult-KIBench and MCKI to study cross-cultural knowledge insertion in Multimodal LLMs, evaluating adaptation vs behavior preservation across English, Chinese, and Arabic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs trained on English-centric data often produce culturally biased or misaligned responses in cross-cultural contexts; need benchmarks and methods to adapt models to specific cultures while preserving behavior in others.

Method: CrossCult-KIBench provides 9,800 image-grounded cases across 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios in English, Chinese, and Arabic, supporting single-insert and sequential-insert evaluation. MCKI (Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion) retrieves culture-relevant knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations and prepends matched entries as conditional prompts.

Result: Experimental results indicate current approaches struggle to achieve a balance between effective cultural adaptation and preservation of non-target cultural behavior.

Conclusion: The work highlights a key challenge in building culturally aware MLLMs and offers CrossCult-KIBench as a benchmark to drive future research toward more culturally adaptive and responsible models.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), trained primarily on English-centric data, frequently generate culturally inappropriate or misaligned responses in cross-cultural settings. To mitigate this, we introduce the task of cross-cultural knowledge insertion, which focuses on adapting models to specific cultural contexts while preserving their original behavior in other cultures. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce CrossCult-KIBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing both the effectiveness of knowledge insertion and its unintended side effects on non-target cultures. The benchmark includes 9,800 image-grounded cases covering 49 culturally relevant visual scenarios across English, Chinese, and Arabic language-culture groups. It supports evaluation in both single-insert and sequential-insert settings. We also propose Memory-Conditioned Knowledge Insertion (MCKI) as a baseline method. MCKI retrieves relevant cultural knowledge from an external memory using frozen MLLM representations, prepending matched entries as conditional prompts when applicable. Extensive experiments on CrossCult-KIBench reveal that current approaches struggle to balance effective cultural adaptation with behavioral preservation, highlighting a key challenge in developing culturally-aware MLLMs. Our work thus underscores an important research direction for developing more culturally adaptive and responsible MLLMs.

</details>


### [452] [Back to the Beginning of Heuristic Design: Bridging Code and Knowledge with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06123)
*Nguyen Viet Tuan Kiet,Bui Dinh Pham,Dao Van Tung,Tran Cong Dao,Huynh Thi Thanh Binh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A knowledge-first, top-down search paradigm for automatic heuristic design in combinatorial optimization, arguing that knowledge is the primary search object and code merely implements and tests it; formalizes a distortion–compression trade-off in a statistical-learning view; instantiated in both population-based and tree-based AHD, showing superior efficiency, transfer, and generalization, with gains from combining knowledge-first and code-centric strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bottom-up, code-centric design of heuristics struggles with transfer and reuse; there is a need for a top-down view where learned knowledge (hypotheses, priors) is the main search target and code serves to instantiate and test this knowledge.

Method: Proposes a statistical-learning framework that exposes a distortion–compression trade-off; formalizes knowledge-first search and implements it in population-based and tree-based AHD pipelines; compares with traditional code-centric approaches; evaluates across combinatorial optimization tasks and beyond.

Result: Knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization; often outperforming code-centric pipelines; combining both strategies yields additional gains.

Conclusion: Progress in automatic heuristic design depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses with value beyond a single search trajectory; knowledge-first approaches provide reusable insights and better generalization across problems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced automatic heuristic design (AHD) for combinatorial optimization (CO), where candidate heuristics are iteratively proposed, evaluated, and refined. Most existing approaches search over executable programs and distill insights from execution feedback to guide later iterations. Because this process moves from low-level implementations to high-level principles, we refer to it as a bottom-up paradigm. We argue that this view is incomplete and introduce a complementary top-down perspective: knowledge becomes the primary search object and code merely instantiates and tests it, making what is learned explicit and reusable across problems and trajectories. We formalize this shift through a statistical-learning view that exposes a distortion--compression trade-off, and instantiate it in both population-based and tree-based AHD frameworks. Across CO and tasks beyond it, knowledge-first search improves discovery efficiency, transfer, and generalization, often outperforming code-centric pipelines, while combining both strategies yields further gains. Our results suggest that progress in AHD depends on iteratively constructing and evolving interpretable hypotheses that retain value beyond a single search trajectory.

</details>


### [453] [P-Guide: Parameter-Efficient Prior Steering for Single-Pass CFG Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06124)
*Xin Peng,Ang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: P-Guide enables single-pass guidance for flow-based conditional generation, achieving roughly 50% faster inference by modulating only the initial latent state, while maintaining fidelity comparable to dual-pass CFG; also explores mean–variance priors (homoscedastic/heteroscedastic) for robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFG improves generation fidelity but requires dual forward passes per step, causing high computational overhead. There is a need for a single-pass guidance method that preserves quality and alignment.

Method: Propose P-Guide, a single-inference-pass framework that modulates the initial latent state to provide high-quality guidance. Under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG by steering generation from the prior space without velocity-field extrapolation. Analyze both homoscedastic and heteroscedastic priors and jointly model mean and variance to enable adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty.

Result: P-Guide reduces inference latency by ~50% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines. The first-order equivalence to CFG holds under the proposed approximation, and mean–variance modeling improves robustness to data uncertainty.

Conclusion: P-Guide presents a viable single-pass alternative to CFG for flow-based conditional generation, preserving quality while cutting compute; joint mean–variance priors further enhance robustness and adaptivity.

Abstract: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is essential for high-fidelity conditional generation in flow matching, yet it imposes significant computational overhead by requiring dual forward passes at each sampling step. In this work, we address this bottleneck by introducing \textbf{P-Guide}, a framework that achieves high-quality guidance through a single inference pass by modulating only the initial latent state. We further show that, under a first-order approximation, P-Guide is equivalent to CFG in the sense that it steers generation from the prior space, without requiring explicit velocity field extrapolation during sampling. We consider both homoscedastic and \textbf{heteroscedastic} priors, and find that jointly modeling the mean and variance enables adaptive loss attenuation and improved robustness to data uncertainty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P-Guide reduces inference latency by approximately 50\% while maintaining fidelity and prompt alignment competitive with standard dual-pass CFG baselines.

</details>


### [454] [Skill1: Unified Evolution of Skill-Augmented Agents via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06130)
*Yaorui Shi,Yuxin Chen,Zhengxi Lu,Yuchun Miao,Shugui Liu,Qi GU,Xunliang Cai,Xiang Wang,An Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skill1 is a unified framework that co-evolves skill selection, utilization, and distillation using a single task-outcome signal, via a single policy that queries a skill library, re-ranks candidates, executes with the chosen skill, and distills new skills from trajectory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome fragmented progress from optimizing skill selection, usage, and distillation separately; a persistent skill library can enable cross-task reuse if these capabilities co-evolve toward a unified objective.

Method: Train a single policy to (1) generate a query to search a persistent skill library, (2) re-rank candidates and select one, (3) solve the task conditioned on the selected skill, and (4) distill a new skill from the trajectory. All learning is driven by a single task-outcome signal, with low-frequency credit for selection and high-frequency credit for distillation.

Result: Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement-learning baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop. Training dynamics demonstrate co-evolution of selection, utilization, and distillation; ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades evolution.

Conclusion: Jointly co-evolving skill selection, utilization, and distillation under a single objective is feasible and beneficial, with evidence that credit signals must be preserved for all three capabilities; the approach scales to task domains like ALFWorld and WebShop.

Abstract: A persistent skill library allows language model agents to reuse successful strategies across tasks. Maintaining such a library requires three coupled capabilities. The agent selects a relevant skill, utilizes it during execution, and distills new skills from experience. Existing methods optimize these capabilities in isolation or with separate reward sources, resulting in partial and conflicting evolution. We propose Skill1, a framework that trains a single policy to co-evolve skill selection, utilization, and distillation toward a shared task-outcome objective. The policy generates a query to search the skill library, re-ranks candidates to select one, solves the task conditioned on it, and distills a new skill from the trajectory. All learning derives from a single task-outcome signal. Its low-frequency trend credits selection and its high-frequency variation credits distillation. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that Skill1 outperforms prior skill-based and reinforcement learning baselines. Training dynamics confirm the co-evolution of the three capabilities, and ablations show that removing any credit signal degrades the evolution.

</details>


### [455] [Graphlets as Building Blocks for Structural Vocabulary in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06154)
*Kossi Amouzouvi,Robert Wardenga,Jens Lehmann,Sahar Vahdati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graphlet-based vocabulary as universal structural tokens enables transferability in Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs), improving zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction across 51 heterogeneous KGs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KGs are discrete yet non-Euclidean; unlike text or images, there is no fixed grid to align tokens. This heterogeneity hampers cross-KG transfer in KGFMs, motivating a universal structural token set to capture recurring subgraph patterns.
