Method: Introduce a model-agnostic framework using a vocabulary of graphlets (closed/open 2- and 3-paths, and star graphs) to mine relations via pattern matching. Treat graphlets as structural tokens to learn invariances and enable transfer. Evaluate on 51 heterogeneous KGs for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction.

Result: Incorporating simple graphlets into the vocabulary yields improved performance over prior KGFMs, demonstrating robust invariances and enhanced transfer across unseen KGs.

Conclusion: Graphlet-based tokens offer a universal, discrete representation for KGs, bridging geometry gaps and enabling transferable knowledge. The approach is model-agnostic and scalable, with demonstrated gains across diverse datasets; further work could explore broader graphlet families and dynamic KGs.

Abstract: Foundation models excel at language, where sentences become tokens, and vision, where images become pixels, because both reduce to discrete symbols on a shared, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs share the discreteness, but not the geometry. Their entities and relations are discrete symbols, yet their arrangement is relational and lacks a common, fixed grid. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) share the discreteness, but not the geometry. They form irregular, non-Euclidean topologies whose local neighborhoods differ from graph to graph. Therefore, Knowledge Graph Foundation Models (KGFMs) rely on identifying structural invariances to produce transferable representations. Without a universal token set, KGFMs are limited in their ability to transfer representations across unseen KGs. We close this gap by treating graphlets, small connected graphs, as structural tokens that recur in heterogeneous KGs. In this paper, We introduce a model-agnostic framework based on a vocabulary of graphlets that mines a KG between relations via pattern matching. In particular, we considered closed and open 2- and 3-path, and star graphlets, to obtain robust invariances. The framework is evaluated on 51 KGs from a wide range of domains, for zero-shot inductive and transductive link prediction. Experiments show that adding simple graphlets to the vocabulary yields models that outperform prior KGFMs.

</details>


### [456] [Beyond Accuracy: Policy Invariance as a Reliability Test for LLM Safety Judges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06161)
*Shihao Weng,Yang Feng,Xiaofei Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Policy invariance for LLM-based safety judges: a stress-test protocol reveals judges’ verdicts depend on evaluation policy as much as agent behavior; introduces Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card; exposes unreliability in safety scoring and provides code for auditing evaluators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-as-a-Judge evaluators risk conflating agent behavior with evaluation prompts. Trustworthy safety judgments require invariance to policy rewrites and calibrated handling of ambiguity.

Method: Propose three principles: (1) rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, (2) rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, (3) ambiguity-aware calibration. Implement a stress-test protocol using four agent-class judges on ASSEBench and R-Judge trajectories; apply content-preserving rewrites to evaluate sensitivity; quantify verdict flips.

Result: Judges show sensitivity to meaningful normative shifts and structural rewrites; policy-preserving rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts; 18–43% of flips occur on unambiguous cases; wide inter-judge reliability gaps not visible to accuracy metrics.

Conclusion: Safety scores conflate agent behavior with evaluator prompting; introduces Policy Invariance Score and Judge Card reporting; provides protocol and code for auditing evaluators to ensure trustworthy benchmarking.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-Judge pipelines have become the de facto evaluator for agent safety, yet existing benchmarks treat their verdicts as ground-truth proxies without checking whether the verdicts depend on the agent's behavior or merely on how the evaluation policy happens to be worded. We argue that any trustworthy safety judge must satisfy a basic property we call policy invariance, and we operationalize it as three testable principles: rubric-semantics invariance under certified-equivalent rewrites, rubric-threshold invariance under intentional strict-to-lenient shifts, and ambiguity-aware calibration so that verdict instability concentrates on genuinely ambiguous cases. Instantiating these principles as a stress-test protocol with four agent-class judges on trajectories drawn from ASSEBench and R-Judge, we surface a previously unmeasured failure mode: today's judges respond to meaningful normative shifts and to meaningless structural rewrites with comparable strength, and cannot tell the two apart. Content-preserving policy rewrites flip up to 9.1% of verdicts above baseline jitter, and 18-43% of all observed flips occur on unambiguous cases under such rewrites, so existing safety scores conflate what the agent did with how the evaluator was prompted. Beyond the diagnosis, we contribute the Policy Invariance Score and the Judge Card reporting protocol, which expose an order-of-magnitude spread in judge reliability that is invisible to accuracy-only leaderboards. We release the protocol and code so that future agent-safety benchmarks can audit their own evaluators rather than trust them by default.

</details>


### [457] [BioMedArena: An Open-source Toolkit for Building and Evaluating Biomedical Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06177)
*Jinge Wu,Hongjian Zhou,Mingde Zeng,Jiayuan Zhu,Junde Wu,Jiazhen Pan,Sean Wu,Honghan Wu,Fenglin Liu,David A. Clifton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioMedArena is an open-source toolkit that decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation, enabling fair, scalable benchmarking with 147 benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 families; it reduces per-paper engineering tax and reports SOTA gains (+15.03 pp avg) on 8 biomedical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The development of deep biomedical research agents currently suffers from high per-paper engineering tax due to varying harnesses, tool registries, and model integration costs, hindering fair comparisons across models.

Method: Decouple evaluation into six layers (benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, scoring); provide 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 tools across 9 functional families; offer 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies (12 backbones total); supply provider adapters to register new models/benchmarks/tools; open-source toolkit with per-task traces.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks with an average improvement of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA; includes 12 backbones and 6 harnesses, enabling competitive research capabilities.

Conclusion: BioMedArena reduces the engineering tax per paper, enables fair cross-model comparisons, and supports scalable, reproducible benchmarking for biomedical foundation models; the project provides configurations and traces for replication.

Abstract: Building a deep research agent today is an exercise in glue code: the same backbone evaluated on the same benchmark can report different accuracies in different papers because harness and tool registry all differ, and integrating a new foundation model into a comparable evaluation surface costs weeks of model-specific engineering. We call this the per-paper engineering tax and release BioMedArena, an open-source toolkit that not only alleviates it but also provides an arena for fair comparison of different foundation models when evaluating them as deep-research agents. BioMedArena decouples six layers of biomedical agent evaluation -- benchmark loading, tool exposure, tool selection, execution mode, context management, and scoring -- and exposes 147 biomedical benchmarks and 75 biomedical tools across 9 functional families. Adding a new model, benchmark, or tool reduces to registering a few-line provider adapter. We further provide 6 agent harnesses with 6 context-management strategies, which provide 12 backbones with competitive research capabilities and significantly improved performance, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on 8 representative biomedical benchmarks, with an average lift of +15.03 percentage points over prior SOTA. The toolkit, configurations, and per-task traces are available at https://github.com/AI-in-Health/BioMedArena

</details>


### [458] [Rethinking Adapter Placement: A Dominant Adaptation Module Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06183)
*Suoxin Zhang,Run He,Di Fang,Xiang Tan,Kaixuan Chen,Huiping Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dominant adaptation module found for LoRA: a single adapter at the FFN down-projection yields strong performance with minimal parameters; PAGE identifies this module as the most gradient-energetic; DomLoRA places there and outperforms vanilla LoRA across tasks, and improves other LoRA variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA uses many adapters; optimal placement of a limited number of adapters is unclear. A data-driven method to identify where adapters contribute most could guide efficient fine-tuning.

Method: Introduce PAGE, a gradient-energy probe for each candidate LoRA adapter; analyze multiple model families and downstream tasks to locate a consistently dominant module (dominant adaptation module, often a shallow FFN down-projection). Propose DomLoRA: place a single adapter at that module; test across tasks; show it outperforms vanilla LoRA with ~0.7% of trainable params; also improves other LoRA variants.

Result: DomLoRA achieves higher average performance than vanilla LoRA across instruction following, math reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation; requires ~0.7% of LoRA parameters; PAGE's findings are task-stable and architecture-dependent.

Conclusion: A practical placement guideline for LoRA adapters: identify the dominant adaptation module with PAGE and deploy a single adapter there (DomLoRA); this improves efficiency and performance and generalizes to other LoRA variants.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that places trainable low-rank adapters into frozen pre-trained models. Recent studies show that using fewer LoRA adapters may still maintain or even improve performance, but existing methods still distribute adapters broadly, leaving where to place a limited number of adapters to maximize performance largely open. To investigate this, we introduce PAGE (Projected Adapter Gradient Energy), a gradient-based sensitivity probe that estimates the initial trainable gradient energy available to each candidate LoRA adapter. Surprisingly, we find that PAGE is highly concentrated on a single shallow FFN down-projection across two model families and four downstream tasks. We term this module the dominant adaptation module and show that its layer index is architecture-dependent but task-stable. Motivated by this finding, we propose DomLoRA, a placement method that places a single adapter at the dominant adaptation module. With only ~0.7% of vanilla LoRA's trainable parameters, DomLoRA outperforms it on average across various downstream tasks, including instruction following, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn conversation. This method also improves other LoRA variants, supporting the dominant adaptation module perspective as a practical placement guideline.

</details>


### [459] [Event-Causal RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Long Video Reasoning in Complex Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06185)
*Peizheng Yan,Yu Zhao,Liang Xie,Juntong Qi,Mingming Wang,Erwei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning using SES graphs and an Event Knowledge Graph to enable memory-efficient, causally-aware, streaming inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current long-video understanding struggles with ultra-long/infinite videos due to quadratic self-attention, fragmented clip-based memory, weak temporal/causal modeling, and high storage/inference costs; need scalable, memory-efficient approaches that preserve causal structure over long durations.

Method: Segment streaming videos into semantically coherent events; represent each event as a State-Event-State (SES) graph capturing the event and surrounding state transitions; merge graphs into a global Event Knowledge Graph stored in a dual-store memory enabling semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval; apply bidirectional retrieval to identify relevant event causal chains and associated video evidence; provide these to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation.

Result: On long-video benchmarks, the method consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, especially for questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps; also improves memory efficiency and streaming robustness.

Conclusion: Event-Causal RAG effectively addresses memory scalability and causal reasoning for ultra-long video understanding, enabling efficient streaming retrieval and improved multi-event reasoning across extended time horizons.

Abstract: Recent large vision-language models have achieved strong performance on short- and medium-length video understanding, yet they remain inadequate for ultra-long or even infinite video reasoning, where models must preserve coherent memory over extended durations and infer causal dependencies across temporally distant events. Existing end-to-end video understanding methods are fundamentally limited by the $O(n^2)$ complexity of self-attention, while recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches still suffer from fragmented clip-level memory, weak modeling of temporal and causal structure, and high storage and online inference costs. We present Event-Causal RAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for infinite long-video reasoning. Instead of indexing fixed-length clips, our method segments streaming videos into semantically coherent events and represents each event as a structured State-Event-State (SES) graph, capturing the event together with its surrounding state transitions. These graphs are merged into a global Event Knowledge Graph and stored in a dual-store memory that supports both semantic matching and causal-topological retrieval. On top of this memory, we design a bidirectional retrieval strategy to efficiently identify the most relevant event causal chains and provide them, together with the associated video evidence, to a backbone video foundation model for answer generation. Experiments on long-video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that Event-Causal RAG consistently outperforms strong clip-based retrieval baselines and long-context video models, particularly on questions requiring multi-event integration and causal inference across long temporal gaps, while also achieving improved memory efficiency and robust streaming performance.

</details>


### [460] [OPSD Compresses What RLVR Teaches: A Post-RL Compaction Stage for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06188)
*Jaehoon Kim,Dongha Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPSD in thinking-enabled math reasoning mainly acts as a compression mechanism; training on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while shortening outputs, whereas training on incorrect rollouts harms accuracy; a revised pipeline is SFT -> RLVR -> OPSD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why OPSD benefits in some settings but not in long-form thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, and to test whether hindsight supervision can provide better token-level guidance when using OPSD.

Method: Apply OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning to observe compression vs correction effects; evaluate impact on accuracy and response length; compare with baseline post-training configurations.

Result: OPSD behaves more like a compression mechanism in thinking-enabled reasoning: training on correct rollouts preserves accuracy and shortens responses; training on incorrect rollouts degrades accuracy. It does not reliably correct long-form reasoning.

Conclusion: Recommend a revised post-training pipeline: SFT followed by RLVR, then OPSD. Use OPSD primarily for compression via correct rollouts; avoid OPSD training on incorrect rollouts to prevent accuracy loss.

Abstract: On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) has recently emerged as an alternative to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), promising higher accuracy and shorter responses through token-level credit assignment from a self-teacher conditioned on privileged context. However, this promise does not carry over to thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, where reported accuracy gains shrink and sometimes turn negative. We hypothesize that hindsight supervision can specify better token-level alternatives in short thinking-disabled outputs, but in long thinking-enabled traces it more readily identifies redundancy than supplies better replacements. To test this, we applied OPSD separately to correct and incorrect rollout groups, so that compression and correction can be observed in isolation. Our results show that in thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning, OPSD behaves most reliably as a compression mechanism rather than a correction mechanism: training only on correct rollouts preserves accuracy while substantially shortening responses, whereas training only on incorrect rollouts damages accuracy. In light of these findings, we propose a revised post-training pipeline for thinking-enabled mathematical reasoning: SFT then RLVR then OPSD.

</details>


### [461] [Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models for Post-Discharge Clinical Action Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06191)
*Shivali Dalmia,Ananya Mantravadi,Prasanna Desikan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs perform well on binary actionability; but supervised models lead on fine-grained multi-label tasks; a two-stage prompting framework helps decompose narrative discharge notes into actionable steps; annotation conventions and absence of reasoning rationales limit evaluation; progress hinges on reasoning-annotated datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve safety-critical clinical action extraction from discharge notes under privacy constraints; compare general-purpose LLMs with task-specific models; assess annotation inconsistencies.

Method: Two-stage extraction framework with staged prompting to convert narrative discharge notes into fine-grained actionable tasks; zero-shot and few-shot LLM evaluations; compare generic LLMs vs BERT-based supervised models; analyze annotation inconsistencies; dataset: CLIP discharge-note.

Result: LLMs match or exceed supervised models on binary actionability detection; supervised models outperform on fine-grained multi-label classification; annotations inconsistency and lack of rationales limit interpretability; issues due to misalignment between model reasoning and annotation conventions; limited clinical reasoning in predictions.

Conclusion: Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why actions are actionable, not just where; evaluation should separate reasoning failures from annotation-convention mismatches; future work should incorporate rationale annotations to enable proper assessment of model understanding.

Abstract: The work in this paper evaluates zero-shot and few-shot large language models (LLMs) for safety-critical clinical action extraction using the CLIP discharge-note dataset, with particular emphasis on transitions of care and post-discharge patient safety. To manage the complexity of clinical documentation, we introduce a two-stage extraction framework that decomposes discharge notes, that are written in narrative form, into fine-grained, explicitly actionable clinical tasks through a staged prompting strategy. Our contributions include a systematic assessment of generative LLMs for clinical action extraction, a detailed comparison between general-purpose LLMs and task-specific supervised BERT-based models, and an analysis of annotation inconsistencies across different action categories. We show that contemporary LLMs achieve performance comparable to or exceeding supervised models on binary actionability detection, while supervised baselines retain a meaningful advantage on fine-grained multi-label category classification, despite the absence of task-specific fine-tuning and under strict data-privacy constraints. Qualitative error analysis reveals that many failures stem from misalignment between model reasoning and dataset annotation conventions, particularly in cases involving implicit clinical actions and rigid structural labeling rules. These results indicate that reported performance reflects model limitations due to lack of clinical reasoning, that is not captured by plain annotations. Labels without rationales make it impossible to distinguish clinical reasoning failures from annotation convention mismatches. Advancing clinical NLP requires reasoning-annotated datasets that document why specific spans are actionable, not merely which spans were labeled, enabling proper evaluation of model clinical understanding.

</details>


### [462] [The Granularity Axis: A Micro-to-Macro Latent Direction for Social Roles in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06196)
*Chonghan Qin,Xiachong Feng,Ziyun Song,Xiaocheng Feng,Jing Xiong,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dominant latent direction, the Granularity Axis, governs micro- to macro-level social-role responses in LLMs; it aligns with the first principal component, explains a substantial portion of variance, is stable and transferable across models, and is causally steerable to modulate response granularity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether LLMs encode social-role granularity as an intrinsic, manipulable latent structure and whether this structure is robust across models and prompt variations.

Method: Define 75 social roles across five granularity levels; collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses; extract role-level hidden states; project onto a Granularity Axis defined as the difference between macro- and micro-role mean hidden states; assess alignment with PC1 (cosine) and variance explained; evaluate monotonicity across levels; test stability across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets; test transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; examine causality via activation steering along the axis to modulate granularity.

Result: The Granularity Axis is the dominant geometric axis in role-conditioned space, with PC1 cosine ≈ 0.972 and explaining ≈52.6% of variance. Role projections rise monotonically with granularity and remain stable across layers, prompts, endpoint definitions, splits, and score-filtered subsets; axis transfers to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Positive steering along the axis causally increases granularity (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: 2.00→3.17 on a five-point macro scale for prompts that admit local responses); controllability differs by model, suggesting dependence on default operating regime.

Conclusion: Granularity is a structured, ordered, and manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned LLM behavior, indicating that social-role granularity is not merely stylistic but a core latent feature that can be steered and transferred across models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are routinely prompted to take on social roles ranging from individuals to institutions, yet it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode the granularity of such roles, from micro-level individual experience to macro-level organizational, institutional, or national reasoning. We show that they do. We define a contrast-based Granularity Axis as the difference between mean macro- and micro-role hidden states. In Qwen3-8B, this axis aligns with the principal axis (PC1) of the role representation space at cosine 0.972 and accounts for 52.6% of its variance, indicating that granularity is the dominant geometric axis organizing prompted social roles. We construct 75 social roles across five granularity levels and collect 91,200 role-conditioned responses over shared questions and prompt variants, then extract role-level hidden states and project them onto the axis. Role projections increase monotonically across all five levels, remain stable across layers, prompt variants, endpoint definitions, held-out splits, and score-filtered subsets, and transfer to Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. The axis is also causally relevant: activation steering along it shifts response granularity in the predicted direction, with Llama moving from 2.00 to 3.17 on a five-point macro scale under positive steering on prompts that admit local responses. The two models differ in controllability, suggesting that steering depends on each model's default operating regime. Overall, our findings suggest that social role granularity is not merely a stylistic surface feature, but a structured, ordered, and causally manipulable latent direction in role-conditioned language model behavior.

</details>


### [463] [Towards Annotation-Free Validation of MLLMs: A Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06201)
*Ying Gu,Mei Chee Leong,Hui Li Tan,Shangbo Mao,Liyuan Li,Nancy Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VL-LCM is a proposed framework to evaluate vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs, without ground-truth annotations, on MC-VQA and NaturalBench. It analyzes necessary and sufficient cause-effect relations, using MMMU and NaturalBench benchmarks across 11 open-source MLLMs. Key finding: logical consistency lags accuracy; VL-LCM correlates with ground-truth metrics, resists response distribution biases, and supports model selection and justification in novel tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dominant accuracy metrics can encourage unwarranted guessing and fail to assess logical consistency, especially on novel tasks lacking ground-truth. There is a need for an evaluation framework that measures the logical reliability of MLLMs with or without ground-truth annotations.

Method: Define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) based on basic logic principles for both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. Apply VL-LCM to traditional MC-VQA and NaturalBench tasks without requiring ground-truth annotations. Conduct systematic experiments across MMMU VL benchmark and recent VL challenges, evaluating 11 open-source MLLMs from four frontier families.

Result: VL-LCM reveals a gap: logical consistency trails behind accuracy improvements in recent MLLMs. Extensive analyses show VL-LCM correlates with GT-based metrics and demonstrates reliability and validity without GT. The metric remains applicable for MLLM selection, validation, and providing reliable justification in novel tasks where GT is unavailable.

Conclusion: Beyond accuracy, logical consistency can be a valuable criterion for evaluating and selecting MLLMs. VL-LCM offers a GT-free evaluation pathway, enabling more reliable responses and justification for novel tasks and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Dominant accuracy evaluation might reward unwarranted guessing of Large Language Models, and it might not be applicable to novel tasks for model validation without ground-truth (gt) annotation. Based on basic logic principle, we propose a novel framework to evaluate the vision-language logical consistency of MLLMs on both sufficient and necessary cause-effect relations. We define Vision-Language Logical Consistency Metric (VL-LCM) on traditional MC-VQA tests, and recent NaturalBench tests without the need for gt annotation. Through systematic experiments on representative VL benchmark MMMU and recent VL challenges like NaturalBench, we evaluated 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our findings reveal that, despite significant progress of recent MLLMs on accuracy, logical consistency lags behind significantly. Extensive evaluations on the correlations of VL-LCM with metrics on gt, the reliability of LCM, and the relation of VL-LCM with response distribution justify the validity and applicability of VL-LCM even without gt annotation. Our findings suggest that, beyond accuracy, logical consistency could be employed for both accuracy and reliability. VL-LCM can also be employed for MLLM selection, validation, and reliable answer justification in novel tasks without gt annotation.

</details>


### [464] [Beyond Fixed Benchmarks and Worst-Case Attacks: Dynamic Boundary Evaluation for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06213)
*Haoxiang Wang,Da Yu,Huishuai Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DBE shifts LLM evaluation from fixed benchmarks to boundary-driven, globally-scaled assessment using a calibrated item bank, boundary-search, and adaptive protocol; demonstrated across safety, capability, and truthfulness to avoid saturation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed benchmarks produce ceiling/floor effects that mask capability gaps; a boundary-based evaluation near 0.5 pass probability offers more informative signals and enables cross-model comparability on a unified difficulty scale; aims to grow the evaluation set adaptively.

Method: Introduce a calibrated item bank with per-item difficulty labels validated across 9 reference LLMs; develop Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS) to locate boundary items for a target model using only API-level queries; implement an evaluation protocol that maps a new LLM onto a unified ability scale and expands the evaluation set when coverage is insufficient.

Result: DBE yields three artifacts: (i) a calibrated item bank with per-item difficulties across safety, capability, and truthfulness; (ii) SGBS to locate boundary items using API access; (iii) an evaluation protocol to place a new LLM on a unified scale and grow the bank adaptively. Instantiated on four categories spanning harm refusal, constrained instruction following, and resistance to multi-turn sycophancy; broader model spectrum coverage without saturation and compatible with existing datasets.

Conclusion: DBE provides a more informative, scalable, and cross-model comparable evaluation framework for LLMs, reducing saturation and enabling adaptive growth while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) today rests on fixed benchmarks that apply the same set of items to any model, producing ceiling and floor effects that mask capability gaps. We argue that the most informative evaluation signal lies at the boundary, where the per-prompt pass probability is near $0.5$ under random-sampling decoding, and propose Dynamic Boundary Evaluation (DBE), which actively locates each model's boundary and places it on a globally comparable difficulty scale. DBE delivers three artifacts: (i) a calibrated item bank covering safety, capability, and truthfulness, with per-item difficulty labels validated across $9$ reference LLMs; (ii) Skill-Guided Boundary Search (SGBS), a search algorithm that finds boundary items for a given target LLM using only API-level query access; and (iii) an evaluation protocol that places a new LLM on a unified ability scale and grows the evaluation set adaptively when the target falls outside the bank's coverage. We instantiate DBE on four categories spanning safety (harmful request refusal and over-refusal), capability (constrained instruction following), and truthfulness (multi-turn sycophancy resistance). The resulting evaluation covers a broader model spectrum without saturation while remaining compatible with existing datasets.

</details>


### [465] [Joint Consistency: A Unified Test-Time Aggregation Framework via Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06219)
*Yunzhen Yao,Hongye Wang,Yahong Wang,Michael C. Gastpar,Bo Jiang,Lie He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Joint Consistency (JC): a unified, interaction-aware test-time aggregation framework for multiple reasoning traces, cast as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization; unifies voting and weighted aggregation; uses LLM-based judge comparisons; scalable via efficient approximation; achieves state-of-the-art on math/code reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trace-aggregation methods largely rely on per-trace evaluation signals in isolation or simple answer frequencies and ignore how traces compare with each other. There is a need for a framework that models interactions among candidate traces to improve final decisions.

Method: Formulate test-time aggregation as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization where external fields come from independent evaluation signals and pairwise interactions come from trace comparisons. Build the interaction matrix using LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, and provide a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. Develop an efficient approximation strategy to render interaction modeling scalable to many traces.

Result: JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across math and code reasoning benchmarks, across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

Conclusion: JC provides a unified framework that subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods and is practical for large-scale test-time aggregation, with theoretical grounding via answer-level homogeneity.

Abstract: This paper studies test-time aggregation, an approach that generates multiple reasoning traces and aggregates them into a final answer. Most existing methods rely on evaluation signals collected from candidate traces in isolation or answer frequencies, while ignoring comparative interactions among candidates. We propose Joint Consistency (JC), formulated as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem, where independent evaluation signals act as external fields and pairwise comparisons act as interactions. JC provides a unified framework for test-time aggregation that subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods as special cases. Our construction of the interaction matrix leverages LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, and admits a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. Moreover, we develop an efficient approximation strategy that makes interaction modeling practical for large-scale test-time aggregation. Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

</details>


### [466] [A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06226)
*Tianyu Liu,Wangjie Zheng,Rui Yang,Benny Kai Guo Loo,Hui Zhang,Jeffries Lauran,Jianlei Gu,Botao Yu,Weihao Xuan,Kexin Huang,Nan Liu,James Zou,Yonghui Jiang,Hua Xu,Hongyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-modal AI diagnostic assistant Hygieia integrates phenotypic, genomic, and clinical data with a router-based, knowledge-enhanced framework to reduce hallucinations, prioritize rare-disease genomic factors, and provide confidence scores; shows state-of-the-art performance and improves clinician performance in real-world validation, reducing workload.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address delays and inaccuracies in rare-disease diagnosis by leveraging diverse data sources and robust, interpretable AI guidance to support clinicians.

Method: Developed Hygieia's router-based architecture, knowledge-enhanced modules, multi-modal data integration, genomic risk prioritization for rare diseases, and confidence scoring; evaluated on benchmarks and via clinical collaboration with Yale and Duke-NUS.

Result: State-of-the-art diagnostic performance on benchmarks; improved physician performance by 12–60%; effective in real-world case handling and records analysis.

Conclusion: Hygieia holds promise as a clinical decision-support tool that improves accuracy, interpretability, and reduces clinician workload; further validation and real-world deployment would be beneficial.

Abstract: Accurate and timely diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, particularly in the context of rare diseases. However, current diagnostic workflows often lead to prolonged assessment times and low accuracy. To address these limitations, we introduce Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system designed to support precision disease diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources, including phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. Hygieia features a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that mitigates hallucination and tailors diagnostic strategies to different disease categories. Notably, it prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases and provides confidence scores to assist clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating that Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks. In collaboration with clinical experts from Yale School of Medicine and Duke-NUS Medical School, we further validated its practical utility by showing (1) Hygieia's superior diagnostic performance compared to physicians with an improvement from 12%-60% and (2) its effectiveness in assisting clinicians with medical records for handling real-world cases. Our findings indicate that Hygieia not only enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability but also significantly reduces clinician workload, highlighting its potential as a valuable tool in clinical decision support systems.

</details>


### [467] [Price of Fairness in Short-Term and Long-Term Algorithmic Selections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06227)
*Shahin Jabbari,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A sequential decision-making setting reveals a tension between fairness and utility: short-term fairness can incur a large Price of Fairness (PoF), while simple long-horizon investment policies can eliminate long-term disparities. The work provides theoretical characterizations of optimal and fair policies, analyzes PoF bounds, and validates findings empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between static fairness constraints and dynamic, long-run effects in algorithmic decision-making, showing that enforcing fairness in one period may worsen disparities over time.

Method: Introduce short-term and long-term group fairness notions, define the Price of Fairness (PoF), and theoretically analyze the trade-off between fairness and utility. Characterize optimal and fair policies in the short term, derive conditions under which PoF can be large, propose simple investment policies with low PoF, and validate both theoretically and empirically using synthetic and real datasets.

Result: The PoF can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical in the short term; long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF; the paper provides theoretical results and empirical validation supporting these claims.

Conclusion: Dynamic fairness requires accounting for long-run effects; static fairness constraints may be insufficient or even detrimental in sequential settings. Investment-oriented policies can reduce long-term disparities with low PoF, and the results are supported by experiments on synthetic and real data.

Abstract: Algorithmic decision-making in high-stakes settings can have profound impacts on individuals and populations. While much prior work studies fairness in static settings, recent results show that enforcing static fairness constraints may exacerbate long-run disparities. Motivated by this tension, we study a stylized sequential selection problem in which a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals, affecting both immediate utility and the population distribution over time. We introduce notions of group fairness for both the short and long term and theoretically analyze the trade-off between fairness and utility via the Price of Fairness (PoF). We characterize optimal and fair policies in the short term and show that the PoF can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical. In contrast, we show that long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF. We also empirically validate these theoretical observations using both synthetic and real datasets.

</details>


### [468] [Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06230)
*Xinquan Chen,Zhenyun Yin,Shan He,Bin Huang,Shanzhe Lei,Pengcheng Shi,Kun Cai,Bei Chen,Bangwei Liu,Zeyu Kang,Chao Huang,Yang Zhang,Wenjie Li,Ruijun Ge,Yajie Wang,Tianshun Fang,Tianyang Xu,Yiwen Cong,Meng Jin,Gaolei Li,Xuansheng Wu,Linhan Liu,Zijing He,An Li,Yan Teng,Xin Tan,ChaoChao Lu,Ji He,Jie Li,Chunfeng Song,Jinya Xu,Fan Song,Shujie Wang,Jianmin Qian,Jie Hou,Xuhong Wang,Yingchun Wang,Hui Wang,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Safactory, a scalable pipeline for trustworthy autonomous intelligence, integrating parallel simulation, trustworthy data management, and autonomous evolution to enable end-to-end, long-horizon agent development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fragmentation in agent infrastructure (evaluation, data management, and evolution) to systematically identify risks and close the loop on model improvement for long-horizon decision making.

Method: Introduce Safactory, composed of three tightly coupled platforms: (1) Parallel Simulation Platform for trajectory generation, (2) Trustworthy Data Platform for trajectory storage and experience extraction, (3) Autonomous Evolution Platform for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation; claims to be the first unified evolutionary pipeline for trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

Result: Conceptual framework; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Safactory offers a unified, scalable evolutionary pipeline aimed at trustworthy autonomous intelligence, enabling end-to-end development and continuous improvement of autonomous agents.

Abstract: As large models evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents, challenges increasingly arise from long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing agenticinfrastructure remain fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making it difficult to discover risks systematically and improve models in a continuous closed loop. In this report, we present \textbf{Safactory}, a scalable agent factory for trustworthy autonomous intelligence. Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: a \textbf{Parallel Simulation Platform} for trajectory generation, a \textbf{Trustworthy Data Platform} for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and an \textbf{Autonomous Evolution Platform} for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation. As far as we know, Safactory is the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

</details>


### [469] [Data Language Models: A New Foundation Model Class for Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06290)
*Eda Erol,Giuliano Pezzoli,Ozer Cem Kelahmet*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes the Data Language Model (DLM) to end the preprocessing bottleneck for tabular data by enabling native tabular understanding. Schema-1 (140M) learns from raw cell values and outperforms baselines on benchmarks, including missing-value imputation and sector identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular data is a crucial modality in real-world AI decisions but lacks a native foundation model. Current approaches rely on preprocessing pipelines and do not treat tables as a first-class modality, hindering integration with AI systems.

Method: Train a 140M parameter DLM (Schema-1) on over 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets using raw cell values without serialization or preprocessing. Evaluate on row-level prediction benchmarks, missing-value reconstruction tasks, and dataset-origin (industry sector) identification, comparing against gradient-boosted trees, AutoML stacks, and existing tabular foundation models.

Result: Schema-1 surpasses gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and evaluated tabular foundation models on standard benchmarks; achieves lower missing-value reconstruction error than classical methods and rival large language models; reliably identifies the industry sector of unseen datasets from raw values across domains.

Conclusion: A native tabular understanding layer (DLM) can enable AI systems without preprocessing pipelines, with structural geometry of data aiding imputation and domain identification, suggesting a new foundation model paradigm for tabular data.

Abstract: Every major data modality now has a foundation model that understands it natively: text has language models, images have vision models, audio has audio models. Tabular data, the modality on which many consequential real-world AI decisions are made, does not. Every approach to tabular AI today, from gradient-boosted trees to the latest tabular foundation models, requires a preprocessing pipeline before any model can consume the data. None of them understand tabular data as a modality. We introduce the Data Language Model (DLM), the missing foundation model for tabular data. A DLM understands tables the way a language model understands sentences: natively, without serialization or preprocessing, directly from raw cell values. It is the tabular data layer on which AI models, agents, and vertical AI applications can be built, eliminating the preprocessing pipelines that currently stand between raw data and every AI system that consumes it. We present Schema-1, the first DLM: a 140M parameter model trained on more than 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets. Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and the tabular foundation models we evaluate on established row-level prediction benchmarks. On missing value reconstruction it achieves lower reconstruction error than all classical statistical methods and frontier large language models on mean performance across conditions, establishing that structural understanding of a dataset's own distributional geometry is more useful for imputation than world knowledge encoded in language. It identifies the industry sector of any unseen dataset from raw cell values alone, reliably across any domain, a task no prior tabular model can perform. It is the native tabular understanding layer that has been missing from the AI stack.

</details>


### [470] [Addressing Labelled Data Scarcity: Taxonomy-Agnostic Annotation of PII Values in HTTP Traffic using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06305)
*Thomas Cory,Axel Küpper*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A taxonomy-agnostic, multi-stage LLM pipeline extracts and annotates explicit PII in HTTP bodies, using deterministic pre-processing, label-level classification, instance-level value annotation, and output validation, plus a synthetic HTTP traffic generator. Evaluated across three PII taxonomies, showing accurate type detection and value extraction; demonstrates LLMs as a flexible data-labeling backbone under evolving privacy taxonomies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy audit pipelines struggle with fixed and scarce label taxonomies and scarce labelled traffic. A taxonomy-agnostic approach could improve transferability of PII detectors and support evolving privacy definitions.

Method: A multi-stage LLM-based pipeline: (1) deterministic pre-processing of HTTP bodies, (2) label-level PII type classification, (3) targeted instance-level value annotation, (4) output validation. Additionally, an LLM-based generator creates synthetic HTTP traffic with taxonomy-derived, manually validated PII annotations for safer evaluation.

Result: Across three taxonomies with varying granularity, the pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values, demonstrating taxonomy-agnostic PII annotation and effective data labeling under evolving taxonomies.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as a flexible foundation for taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for generating labelled data aligned with evolving privacy taxonomies, enabling better transferability of privacy detectors and adaptable evaluation/data creation.

Abstract: Automated privacy audits of web and mobile applications often analyse outbound HTTP traffic to detect Personally Identifiable Information (PII) leakage. However, existing learning-based detectors typically depend on scarce, manually labelled traffic and are tightly coupled to fixed label taxonomies, limiting transferability across domains and evolving definitions of PII. This paper investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can support taxonomy-agnostic annotation of explicitly transmitted PII values in HTTP message bodies when the taxonomy is provided at runtime. We introduce a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline that combines deterministic pre-processing with label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. To enable controlled evaluation and exemplar-based prompting without relying on sensitive real-user captures, we further propose an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic with manually validated, taxonomy-derived PII annotations. We evaluate the approach across three taxonomies spanning different PII domains and granularity levels. Results show that the pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values for concrete PII taxonomies. Overall, our findings position LLMs as a promising foundation for flexible, taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for creating labelled data under evolving privacy taxonomies.

</details>


### [471] [Mind the Gap? A Distributional Comparison of Real and Synthetic Priors for Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06343)
*Alex O. Davies,Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho,Nirav Ajmeri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Examines three canonical tabular pretraining corpora (T4 web-scraped, TabFM Kaggle-curated, TabICL synthetic prior). Finds TabICL occupies a narrow real-table space; hyperparameter exploration (up to 86k configs) cannot close the gap; curated and web data are broadly interchangeable; the distributional gap's impact on performance is not mediated by feature proximity or TabICL representations, suggesting real-data coverage is not the primary driver of TabICL generalisation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how pretraining-data distributions relate to downstream performance in tabular foundation models, and to assess whether synthetic priors can approximate real data and what distributional distance implies for generalisation.

Method: Characterise each corpus with aggregate features over tables, columns, and correlations. Compare distributions using discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics. Assess whether extensive hyperparameter optimization of the TabICL synthetic prior can bridge the gap, and relate distributional proximity to downstream performance and internal representations.

Result: The TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of the space of real tables. This mismatch cannot be closed by optimising prior hyper-parameters across more than 86 thousand configurations. Curated (TabFM/Kaggle) and web-scraped (T4) corpora are broadly interchangeable on a distributional level in feature space. However, the distributional gap between synthetic pre-training data and real tables has a detectable effect on performance under neither feature-based proximity measures nor TabICL’s own internal representations, implying coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL generalisation.

Conclusion: Real-data distribution coverage is not the sole or primary determinant of TabICL generalisation. Synthetic priors remain insufficient to match real-table diversity, and despite measurable distributional gaps, this does not translate straightforwardly into performance gains or losses through the explored similarity metrics or representations. Other factors likely govern generalisation, suggesting a need for alternative data-generation approaches or training objectives.

Abstract: Tabular foundation models are pre-trained on one of three classes of corpus: curated datasets drawn from benchmark repositories, tables harvested at scale from the web, or synthetic tables sampled from a parametric generative prior. Despite the centrality of pre-training data to model performance, little is known about how these corpora relate to one another in distribution, and the impact this has on downstream performance. In this work we take three canonical, archetypal datasets used to train tabular foundation models; the T4 dataset represents web-scraped corpora, the TabFM dataset curated tables from Kaggle, and the TabICL dataset as the only well-used synthetic prior with publicly available parameters. We characterise each corpus using aggregate features over whole tables, columns and correlations, and compare them using discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics. We find that the TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of the space of real tables, that this mismatch cannot be closed by optimising prior hyper-parameters across more than 86 thousand configurations, and that curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable on a distributional level in feature space. Surprisingly, the distributional gap between synthetic pre-training data and real tables has a clearly detectable effect on performance under neither feature-based proximity measures or TabICL's own internal representations, suggesting that coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL's generalisation.

</details>


### [472] [More Than Can Be Said: A Benchmark and Framework for Pre-Question Scientific Ideation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06345)
*Jie Yu,Song Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: InciteResearch is a multi-agent framework that externalizes tacit research friction by structuring a five-dimensional profile, challenging assumptions with a 7-stage causal derivation, and validating Necessary consequences, complemented by TF-Bench benchmark showing substantial gains over prompt baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where AI research assistance relies on explicit initial questions, by making tacit, friction-driven understanding explicit and actionable, and by shifting value from surface prompt execution to architectural insight.

Method: Decompose Socratic questioning across the research pipeline; elicit a structured five-dimensional researcher profile anchored to friction points from vague inputs; maximize feasibility-novelty product with a 7-stage causal derivation trace; verify that proposed methods are Necessary consequences of reframed insights; introduce TF-Bench to benchmark tacit-to-explicit research assistance and distinguish domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations across four scientific modes.

Result: InciteResearch achieves leapfrogging gains over a prompt-based baseline (novelty/impact from 3.671/3.806 to 4.250/4.397), shifting generated proposals from recombination to architectural insight.

Conclusion: AI can serve as an extension of human thinking, enabling tacit understanding to be made explicit and actionable rather than merely automating downstream execution.

Abstract: AI research agents have shown strong potential in automating literature search and manuscript refinement, yet most assume a clear and actionable initial input, operating only after a research question has been made explicit. In contrast, human research often begins with tacit friction, a sense of misalignment before a question can be formed. We introduce InciteResearch, a multi-agent framework designed to make a researcher's implicit understanding explicit, inspectable, and actionable. InciteResearch decomposes the logical chain of Socratic questioning and distributes it across the entire pipeline that: (1) Elicits a structured five-dimensional researcher profile state anchored by specific friction points from vague, even domain-unrelated inputs; (2) Violates hidden assumptions by maximizing the feasibility-novelty product with enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace; and (3) check whether the proposed method is a Necessary consequence of the reframed insight. We further introduce TF-Bench, the first benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance that distinguishes domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations across four scientific modes. On TF-Bench, InciteResearch achieves leapfrogging gains over a prompt-based baseline (novelty/impact from 3.671/3.806 to 4.250/4.397), shifting generated proposals from recombination to architectural insight. Our work demonstrates that AI can serve as an extension of thinking itself, rather than merely automating downstream execution.

</details>


### [473] [Prediction and Empowerment: A Theory of Agency through Bridge Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06346)
*Richard Csaky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes agency under partial observability in deterministic environments using bridge interfaces that split agent-controlled and environment-controlled aspects, proving a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment; perfect prediction can be achieved via hidden-state identification or overwrite control; empowerment alone is insufficient; refinable interfaces with memory enable action-conditioned observation compression to reduce posterior uncertainty, and steering world-side conditions yields target-conditioned empowerment. With a conserved information budget, the tradeoffs between identification (internal capacity) and overwrite/terminal action capacity become explicit. The work advocates an interface-design perspective for AI alignment, emphasizing distinguishing identification, refinement, controllability, and distractor control—a design principle rather than an inevitability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the structure of sensing and actuation interfaces influences what an agent can predict, compress, and empower itself to do under uncertainty, and to inform AI alignment by clarifying when prediction or control is feasible given interface constraints.

Method: Theoretical framework: deterministic POMDPs built from latent microstates and coarse observations; define bridge interfaces separating agent vs environment control; prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment; analyze refinable interfaces, memory, and world-side channel steering; introduce a bit-string information-budget specialization to make tradeoffs explicit.

Result: Main findings include: (1) perfect prediction is achievable either by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the task or via overwrite control that fixes future actions; (2) high empowerment alone is not sufficient for prediction; (3) with refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation compression reduces posterior uncertainty about the latent quotient; (4) when refinement requires steering environment channel conditions, target-conditioned interface empowerment emerges; (5) with a conserved information budget, the cost of prediction via identification is at least the latent entropy, while overwrite control requires corresponding terminal action capacity; (6) these results advocate distinguishing hidden-state identification, interface refinement, and controllability in AI design.

Conclusion: Provides a design-principle framework: AI objectives should separate hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and overwrite/distractor control. Alignment is framed as an interface-design problem bridging human intent, agent internal state, tools, and world conditions. The paper is a working draft and invites feedback.

Abstract: We study agency under partial observability in deterministic physical or simulated worlds, where apparent randomness arises from uncertainty over initial conditions, fixed law bits, and unrolled exogenous noise. We model sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces split between agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state, inducing a deterministic POMDP through a prior over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Within this framework, we prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment. Perfect prediction can be achieved either by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the target family or by overwrite control that makes the future target action-determined; high empowerment alone is insufficient. Under refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation-compression progress reduces posterior uncertainty about the latent quotient, and when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this creates target-conditioned interface empowerment. A bit-string specialization with a conserved information budget makes the resulting tradeoff explicit: prediction by identification requires internal capacity at least the relevant latent entropy, whereas overwrite control requires terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient. For modern AI agents, the results suggest a design principle rather than a theorem of inevitability: objectives should distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite or distractor control. Human--AI alignment is partly an interface-design problem, where the relevant bridge is between human intent, agent internal state, external tools, and world-side channel conditions. This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome.

</details>


### [474] [From Agent Loops to Deterministic Graphs: Execution Lineage for Reproducible AI-Native Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06365)
*Josh Rosen,Seth Rosen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Execution lineage uses a DAG of artifact-producing computations to maintain stable work across revisions, outperforming loop-based replay on stateful AI-generated artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fix instability of implicit conversational state in AI agented workflows and preserve stable, reusable work products across revisions by making dependencies and lineage explicit.

Method: Introduce execution lineage as a directed acyclic graph of artifact-producing computations with explicit dependencies and identity-based replay; compare DAG replay against loop-centric baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks (unrelated-branch update and intermediate-artifact edit).

Result: DAG replay preserved the final memo exactly with zero churn and no unrelated-branch contamination in the unrelated-branch update; in the intermediate-artifact edit, DAG replay achieved perfect upstream preservation, downstream propagation, unaffected-artifact preservation, and cross-artifact consistency, whereas loop baselines regenerated or mispropagated elements. Final answer quality can diverge from maintained-state quality; strong loop baselines can suffice for bounded synthesis when context fits, but may mask state inconsistencies that compound over revisions.

Conclusion: Execution lineage provides stronger guarantees about what should change and what should remain stable, improving maintainability and traceability of evolving AI-generated work across revisions compared to loop-centric replay.

Abstract: Large language model systems are increasingly deployed as agentic workflows that interleave reasoning, tool use, memory, and iterative refinement. These systems are effective at producing answers, but they often rely on implicit conversational state, making it difficult to preserve stable work products, isolate irrelevant updates, or propagate changes through intermediate artifacts.
  We introduce execution lineage: an execution model in which AI-native work is represented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of artifact-producing computations with explicit dependencies, stable intermediate boundaries, and identity-based replay. The goal is not to make the model a better one-shot writer, but to make evolving AI-generated work maintainable under change.
  We compare execution-lineage replay against loop-centric update baselines on two controlled policy-memo update tasks. In an unrelated-branch update, DAG replay preserved the final memo exactly in all runs, with zero churn and zero unrelated-branch contamination, while loop baselines regenerated the memo and frequently imported unrelated context. In an intermediate-artifact edit, all systems reflected the new constraint in the final memo, but only DAG replay achieved perfect upstream preservation, downstream propagation, unaffected-artifact preservation, and cross-artifact consistency.
  These results show that final answer quality and maintained-state quality are distinct. Strong loop baselines can remain competitive at producing polished final outputs when the task is a bounded synthesis/update problem and all current sources fit in context, but immediate task success can mask partial state inconsistency that may compound over future revisions. Execution lineage provides stronger guarantees about what should change, what should remain stable, and how work evolves across revisions.

</details>


### [475] [Debiased Multimodal Personality Understanding through Dual Causal Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06371)
*Yangfu Zhu,Zitong Han,Nianwen Ning,Yuting Wei,Yuandong Wang,Hang Feng,Zhenzhou Shao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A causal framework for fair multimodal personality understanding; introduces DCAN with back-door and front-door adjustments; new DMSP dataset; shows improvements in accuracy and fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subject bias in multimodal personality understanding can lead to spurious associations and unfair predictions; a causal approach is needed to disentangle observable and latent biases.

Method: Construct a Structural Causal Model (SCM) and propose Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) with Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) using a prototype-based confounder dictionary and Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) via a learned mediator dictionary intervention; introduce the Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset; evaluate on CFI-V2 and DMSP with ablation and fairness metrics.

Result: DCAN improves prediction accuracy (CFI-V2: 92.11%; DMSP: 92.90%). Fairness metrics improve for equal opportunity (CFI-V2: +6.57%; DMSP: +15.38%) and demographic parity (CFI-V2: +7.97%; DMSP: +20.06%). Code and DMSP dataset released.

Conclusion: A principled causal framework (DCAN) effectively mitigates demographic and latent biases in multimodal personality understanding, yielding better accuracy and fairness; the DMSP dataset enables fairness-focused analysis and reproducibility.

Abstract: Multimodalpersonalityunderstandingplaysacriticalroleinhuman centered artificial intelligence. Previous work mainly focus on learn-ing rich multimodal representations for video personality under standing. However, they often suffer from potential harm caused by subject bias (e.g., observable age and unobservable mental states), as subjects originate from diverse demographic backgrounds. Learn ing such spurious associations between multimodal features and traits may lead to unfair personality understanding. In this work, weconstruct aStructural Causal Model (SCM)toanalyze theimpact of these biases from a causal perspective, and propose a novel Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) to mitigate the interference of subject attributes on personality understanding. Specifically, we design a Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) module to block spurious correlations from observable demographic factors via a prototype-based confounder dictionary, and subsequently ap ply a Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) module to ad dress latent and unobservable biases throughalearnedmediatordic tionary intervention, thereby achieving causal disentanglement of representations for deconfounded reasoning. Importantly, we con struct a Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset to support the analysis and discussion of fairness related factors. Extensive experiments on the benchmark dataset CFI-V2 and our DMSPdataset demonstrate that DCAN consistently improves prediction accuracy, reaching 92.11% and 92.90%, respec tively. Meanwhile, the improvementsinthefairnessmetricsofequal opportunity and demographic parity are 6.57% and 7.97% on CFI-V2, and 15.38% and 20.06% on the DMSP dataset. Our code and DMSP dataset are available at https://github.com/Sabrina-han/DCAN

</details>


### [476] [Rethinking Vacuity for OOD Detection in Evidential Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06382)
*Claire McNamara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Vacuity/UM as an OOD metric in Evidential Deep Learning is highly sensitive to the number of classes K; differences in K between ID and OOD can inflate AUROC/AUPR without changing predictions, revealing an evaluation artefact; the paper includes empirical/theoretical demonstrations and discussion for LLMs with MCQA datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To highlight a critical evaluation artefact in vacuity-based OOD detection for EDL, where the metric depends on K, potentially biasing comparisons between ID and OOD results.

Method: Empirical demonstrations comparing AUROC/AUPR under varying class cardinalities for standard EDL and IB-EDL, theoretical analysis of the dependence on K, and discussion of EDL-based OOD evaluation in causal LLMs using MCQA datasets.

Result: AUROC and AUPR are substantially affected by small differences in class cardinality between ID and OOD (differences up to ~0.3–0.68 in AUROC/AUPR when K changes by 1), revealing an artefact where OOD performance appears better without actual predictive change; the effect persists in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs.

Conclusion: Vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL-based models is highly sensitive to uncontrolled K differences; evaluations should ensure equal class cardinality or adopt K-invariant metrics and clearer ID/OOD definitions, especially in LLM MCQA contexts.

Abstract: Vacuity, or Uncertainty Mass (UM), is commonly used as a metric to evaluate Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection in Evidential Deep Learning (EDL). It generally involves dividing the number of classes ($K$) by the total strength of belief ($S$) of the model's predictions, where $S$ is derived from summing the Dirichlet parameters. As such, UM is sensitive to the cardinality of $K$. In particular, it is unlikely in practice that there is a linear relationship between $K$ and $S$ as $K$ and $S$ increase due to the nature of EDL (suppressing incorrectly assigned evidence). As a result, when comparing In Distribution (ID) and OOD results, it is important that $K_{\mathrm{ID}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{OOD}}$ are equal; something that is not always ensured in practice. We provide an empirical demonstration of how results for AUROC and AUPR can substantially differ when class cardinality between ID and OOD differs by 1, with AUROC differing by as much as 0.318 and AUPR by 0.613 for standard EDL, and AUROC by 0.360 and AUPR by 0.683 for IB-EDL. More concretely, our findings isolate an evaluation artefact: when K differs between ID and OOD, AUROC/AUPR can be artificially inflated without any change in model predictions. We further discuss the evaluation of EDL over causal language models using Multiple-Choice Question-Answer (MCQA) datasets and argue for clearer definitions of ID and OOD in this context. Our primary contribution is an empirical and theoretical demonstration that vacuity-based OOD detection in EDL-fine-tuned LLMs is highly sensitive to uncontrolled differences in evaluated class cardinality.

</details>


### [477] [Automated alignment is harder than you think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06390)
*Aleksandr Bowkis,Marie Davidsen Buhl,Jacob Pfau,Geoffrey Irving*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automating alignment research with AI agents risks systematic, hard-to-detect errors that can yield overconfident safety assessments and misaligned deployments; robust generalisation and scalable oversight are needed but face novel challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the viability and risks of the leading proposal to automate alignment research, and identify failure modes that could undermine safety as AI capabilities advance.

Method: Analytical argument identifying tasks that are hard to supervise, possible sources of error in AI-generated outputs, and comparison with human-led research; outlines four mechanisms by which automation could worsen safety assessments and evaluates remedies.

Result: Four risk factors amplify AI-generated mistakes: (1) optimization pressure concentrates errors missed by humans; (2) AI errors may differ from human mistakes; (3) AI-generated solutions may rely on arguments humans cannot evaluate; (4) shared data/training induce higher output correlation. These factors threaten reliable oversight and generalisation.

Conclusion: Automation of alignment research could backfire if not paired with advanced generalisation and scalable oversight, and addressing the unique challenges of hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks is essential prior to wide deployment.

Abstract: A leading proposal for aligning artificial superintelligence (ASI) is to use AI agents to automate an increasing fraction of alignment research as capabilities improve. We argue that, even when research agents are not scheming to deliberately sabotage alignment work, this plan could produce compelling but catastrophically misleading safety assessments resulting in the unintentional deployment of misaligned AI. This could happen because alignment research involves many hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks (tasks without clear evaluation criteria, for which human judgement is systematically flawed). Consequently, research outputs will contain systematic, undetected errors, and even correct outputs could be incorrectly aggregated into overconfident safety assessments. This problem is likely to be worse for automated alignment research than for human-generated alignment research for several reasons: 1) optimisation pressure means agent-generated mistakes are concentrated among those that human reviewers are least likely to catch; 2) agents are likely to produce errors that do not resemble human mistakes; 3) AI-generated alignment solutions may involve arguments humans cannot evaluate; and 4) shared weights, data and training processes may make AI outputs more correlated than human equivalents. Therefore, agents must be trained to reliably perform hard-to-supervise fuzzy tasks. Generalisation and scalable oversight are the leading candidates for achieving this but both face novel challenges in the context of automated alignment.

</details>


### [478] [Knowledge Graphs, the Missing Link in Agentic AI-based Formal Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06434)
*Vaisakh Naduvodi Viswambharan,Keerthan Kopparam Radhakrishna,Deepak Narayan Gadde,Aman Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KG-based, verification-centric synthesis of SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) from NL specs by grounding in RTL through a structured knowledge graph (KG) of IRs, enabling three refinement loops (syntax repair, CEX-guided correction, coverage-directed augmentation). Demonstrated on seven designs with 78.5–99.4% formal coverage and low syntax-repair overhead, but complex temporal/arithmetic reasoning remains challenging for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address ambiguity and incompleteness in natural-language specifications and the lack of RTL grounding, which leads to semantic misalignment and parse failures when generating SVAs from loose text; preserve design-grounded context to improve specification-to-RTL grounding.

Method: Construct a verification-centric Knowledge Graph (KG) from structured Intermediate Representations (IRs) extracted from the specification, RTL, and formal-tool feedback (syntax diagnostics, counterexamples, coverage). The KG links requirements, design hierarchy, signals, assumptions, and properties. A multi-agent workflow queries/updates the KG to generate SVAs and to drive three refinement loops: (1) syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, (2) CEX-guided correction using trace links, (3) coverage-directed property augmentation. Evaluated on seven benchmark designs, measuring grounding quality, compilability, syntax repair overhead, and formal coverage.

Result: KG-based context retrieval improves specification-to-RTL grounding, yielding compilable SVAs with low syntax-repair overhead. Formal coverage ranges from 78.5% to 99.4% across designs."

Conclusion: The approach enhances design-grounded SVA generation and formal coverage, but convergence is design-dependent, and current LLMs struggle with complex temporal and arithmetic reasoning required for some benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled workflows that generate SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) from natural-language specifications, with the potential to accelerate Formal Verification (FV). However, high-quality assertion synthesis remains challenging because specifications are often ambiguous or incomplete and critical micro-architectural details reside in the Register Transfer Level (RTL). Many existing approaches treat the specification and RTL as loosely structured text, which weakens specification-to-RTL grounding and leads to semantic mismatches and frequent syntax failures during formal parsing and elaboration. This work addresses these limitations with a verification-centric Knowledge Graph (KG) constructed from structured Intermediate Representations (IRs) extracted from the specification, RTL, and formal-tool feedback, including syntax diagnostics, Counterexamples (CEXs), and coverage reports. The KG links requirements, design hierarchy, signals, assumptions, and properties to provide traceable, design-grounded context for generation. A multi-agent workflow queries and updates this KG to generate SVAs and to drive three refinement loops: syntax repair guided by tool diagnostics, CEX-guided correction using trace links, and coverage-directed property augmentation. Evaluation across seven benchmark designs indicates that KG-based context retrieval improves specification-to-RTL grounding and consistently produces compilable SVAs with low syntax-repair overhead. The approach achieves formal coverage ranging from 78.5% to 99.4%, though convergence exhibits design dependence with complex temporal and arithmetic reasoning remaining challenging for current LLM capabilities.

</details>


### [479] [SCRuB: Social Concept Reasoning under Rubric-Based Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06444)
